DISS. ETH NO. 24828 
 
 
 
SOLÀ-MORALES’S TERRAIN VAGUE: TEXT AND CONTEXTS 
Formulation, Dissemination and Reception: Perception and Intervention

## S. 8

5 
 
Abstract 
Shaped by concern and curiosity for the state of indeterminacy in the public realm, 
the premise of this research is that questions of power, resistance and identity are mainly 
contested in undefined spaces within, as well as on the outskirts of, contemporary cities. The 
traditional concept of the city as a relatively contained and cohesive entity has been 
increasingly questioned by subsequent decentralization, suburbanization and fragmentation in 
the way the built-environment is perceived. The diversity inherent today in the concept of 
'city' is greater than the capacity of the available vocabulary to define new emerging spaces. 
Within the contemporary diffused urban fabric, new globalized landscapes merge city and 
nature into an uncontrollable condition. Some of these indeterminate spaces are neither 
properly defined nor well situated within the urban discourse. 
In this respect, a need surfaced in the 1990s for innovative and alternative tools to 
delineate, represent and intervene in such indefinite spaces. The notion of terrain vague, 
which defines not only physical aspects of indeterminate spaces, but also how we perceive 
and represent these, emerged out of this context. Such a notion, which appears to combine 
seemingly antagonist meanings of both vagueness and territoriality, and of both fear and hope, 
holds a strong potential for urban change. It is capable of responding to multiple issues posed 
by the contemporary urban fabric made of fragmented diversified elements. Accordingly, 
approaches and reflections on terrains vagues have become increasingly significant in the last 
twenty years, and the notion has evolved in marked urban approaches towards a discourse 
larger than a mere idea. 
The term was coined in the fields of architecture and urbanism by Catalan 
theoretician and architect Ignasi de Solà-Morales in the mid-1990s through his essay of the 
same name: "Terrain Vague". He described spaces that fall out of the normal urban 
organization due to their inefficiency to serve a primary function in the systems of 
production, consumption, infrastructure, or recreation. He argued that these spaces possess 
particular qualities making them notably attractive to photographers and architects. Inspired 
by the urban transformations of Berlin after the fall of the Wall in 1989 and those of post-
Olympics Barcelona after 1992, Solà-Morales first constructed and formulated the notion of 
terrain vague during the first half of the decade within the contexts of the Metropolis Master 
Program in Barcelona and his lectures of 1992-1993 at Princeton University. He then 
presented and published his essay, in 1994 and 1995, within a seminar at the Triennale di 
Milano and Anyplace at the CCA in Montreal, the fourth in a series of yearly conferences 
organized by the Anyone Corporation. After that, the essay and the notion were 
internationally influential, recurrently translated, re-presented, re-published, and received 
with great interest. 
From the notion’s history line, to the author’s trajectory and writings, to the urban 
transformations of the 1990s, many lines thus intersect through one essay that is consequently 
the central object and case study of this thesis. The present research seeks to understand how 
the notion of terrain vague thrived through the medium of Solà-Morales's essay. As both the

## S. 9

6 
essay and the notion developed in concert, they are jointly analyzed throughout this research 
in order to understand the connections between the historical, political and urban contexts of 
the terrain vague notion on the one hand, and the contexts of the essay and its author on the 
other. The purpose is hence to verify the hypothesis that Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague” essay 
is a political statement and a hidden manifesto whose formulation, presentation and diffusion 
led to its status as a core reference for the subject of terrain vague, and to the vibrancy of the 
discourse from the mid-1990s to the present day. This dissertation embraces both the historic 
details of the notion, as well as its position in the intellectual history of architectural debate of 
the period. 
In conclusion, the present dissertation exposes how the terrain vague marked, on the 
one hand, a climax and a reaction against modernist and postmodernist ideals prevalent in the 
contemporary city at the turn of the millennium, and how it also acted on the other hand as a 
catalyst for change. Solà-Morales gathered, within one essay, notions and impressions 
theoretically pertinent to an increasingly important type of urban spaces that caught the 
attention of his contemporaries. Through his articulation of the terrain vague and the way he 
presented the notion to the world, he also had a strong impact on urban and architectural 
practices. Accordingly, the double innuendo within his essay, alluding to a narrative on the 
one hand and a hidden manifesto on the other, reflects this oscillation between a synthesizing 
narrative of a period and a catalyzing call for action and for raising awareness to the 
indeterminacy of the public realm.

## S. 10

7 
 
Zusammenfassung 
Die Prämisse dieser Forschungsarbeit, geformt durch Sorge kombiniert mit Neugierde 
für den Zustand des Begriffs der Unbestimmtheit in der Öffentlichkeit, ist, dass die Fragen 
nach Macht, Widerstand und Identität hauptsächlich in den undefinierten Räumen innerhalb 
und am Rande zeitgenössischer Städte bestritten werden. Das traditionelle Konzept der Stadt 
als eine mehr oder weniger abgeschlossene und zusammenhängende Entität wird durch die 
seither eingetretene Dezentralisierung, Suburbanisierung und eine Fragmentierung der 
Wahrnehmung der bebauten Umwelt immer mehr in Frage gestellt. Die Diversität, die dem 
Konzept der 'Stadt' heute inneliegt, ist grösser als die Fähigkeit des existierenden Vokabulars, 
neu aufkommende Räume zu definieren. Innerhalb des kontemporären, diffusen Stadtgefüges 
vereinigen neue globalisierte Landschaften Stadt und Natur in einem unkontrollierbaren 
Zustand. Einige dieser unbestimmten Räume sind im städtischen Diskurs weder wohl 
definiert noch wohl platziert. 
In diesem Zusammenhang kam am Anfang der 1990er Jahren ein Bedarf nach 
innovativen und alternativen Mitteln zum Beschreiben und Darstellen von und Eingreifen in 
solchen undefinierten Räumen auf. Aus diesem Kontext heraus kam der Begriff vom Terrain 
Vague, welcher nicht nur die physischen Aspekte, sondern auch die Wahrnehmung und 
Darstellung solcher unbestimmten Räume definiert. Ein solcher Begriff, welcher scheinbar 
entgegengesetzte Bedeutungen wie Unbestimmtheit und Territorialität sowie Angst und 
Hoffnung vereint, hat grosses Potential für städtische Veränderung. Er ist fähig, viele 
Problemstellungen, die sich durch das gegenwärtige, aus fragmentierten und diversifizierten 
Elementen zusammengesetzte Stadtgefüge stellen, zu beantworten. Demzufolge sind Ansätze 
und Überlegungen zu Terrain Vague innerhalb der letzten zwanzig Jahre immer bedeutsamer 
geworden und der Begriff hat sich in ausgeprägten städtischen Herangehensweisen zu einem 
Diskurs, der über eine blosse Idee hinausgeht, entwickelt. 
Der Begriff Terrain Vague wurde in den Gebieten der Architektur und des 
Urbanismusses durch den katalanischen Theoretiker und Architekten Ignasi de Solà-Morales 
Mitte der 1990er Jahre in seiner Abhandlung gleichen Namens geprägt. Er beschrieb Räume, 
die aufgrund ihrer Ineffizienz, eine primäre Rolle in den Systemen Produktion, Verbrauch, 
Infrastruktur oder Entspannung einzunehmen, aus der normalen städtischen Organisation 
herausfallen. Er argumentierte, dass diese Räume besondere Eigenschaften haben, die sie für 
Fotografen und Architekten attraktiv machen. Inspiriert durch die städtische Transformation 
Berlins nach dem Mauerfall im Jahr 1989 und des post-olympischen Barcelonas nach 1992 hat 
Solà-Morales den Begriff des Terrain Vague erstmals in der ersten Hälfte des Jahrzehnts im 
Kontext des Metropolis Master Programms in Barcelona und seiner Vorlesungen an der 
Princeton Universität zwischen 1992 und 1993 konstruiert und formuliert. Er präsentierte 
und publizierte seine Abhandlung, in 1994 und 1995, in einem Seminar an der Triennale di 
Milano und in Anyplace am CCA in Montreal, die vierte in einer Serie jährlicher Konferenzen 
organisiert von der Anyone Corporation. Hiernach waren die Abhandlung und der Begriff

## S. 11

8 
international einflussreich, wurden immer wieder übersetzt, neu präsentiert, neu publiziert 
und mit grossem Interesse aufgenommen. 
Es kreuzen sich also viele Linien in einer Abhandlung, von der Herkunft des Begriffes 
über den Werdegang und die Schriften des Autors bis zu den städtischen Transformationen 
der 1990er. Die Abhandlung ist dementsprechend das Zentrum und die Fallstudie dieser 
Forschungsarbeit, die dem Gedeihens des Begriffs des Terrain Vague durch das Medium der 
Abhandlung von Solà-Morales nachgeht. Da die Abhandlung und der Begriff sich gemeinsam 
entwickelt haben, werden sie in dieser Arbeit durchgehend zusammen analysiert um die 
Verbindungen zwischen den historischen, politischen und städtischen Kontexten des Terrain 
Vague Begriffs auf der einen Seite und den Kontexten der Abhandlung und des Autors auf der 
anderen Seite zu verstehen. Das Ziel ist demnach, die Hypothese zu verifizieren, dass Solà-
Moraleses 'Terrain Vague' Abhandlung eine politische Aussage und ein verstecktes Manifest 
ist, dessen Formulierung, Darstellung und Verbreitung zu seinem Status als Kernreferenz zum 
Thema Terrain Vague und zu der Lebendigkeit des Diskurses seit der Mitte der 1990er Jahre 
bis zum heutigen Tage führte. Es umfasst sowohl die historischen Details des Begriffs als auch 
seine Position in der intellektuellen Geschichte der architektonischen Debatte dieses 
Zeitraums. 
Abschliessend sei gesagt, dass diese Forschungsarbeit aufzeigt, wie Terrain Vague auf 
der einen Seite ein Auslöser für Wandel war uns auf der anderen Seite einen Höhepunkt und 
eine Reaktion gegen die in der zeitgenössischen Stadt um die Jahrtausendwende 
vorherrschenden moderne und postmoderne Ideale markierte. Solà-Morales scheint in einer 
einzelnen Abhandlung Begriffe und Eindrücke gesammelt zu haben, die für eine immer 
wichtigere Art von städtischen Räumen relevant sind und die Aufmerksamkeit seiner 
Zeitgenossen auf sich gezogen hat. Durch das Prägen des Begriffs Terrain Vague und der Art 
und Weise wie er ihn der Welt vorgestellt hat scheint er auch einen Nerv in städtischen und 
architektonischen Methoden getroffen zu haben. Dementsprechend spiegelt die versteckte 
Andeutung in seiner Abhandlung, einerseits eine Erzählung und andererseits ein Manifest, 
das Hin und Her zwischen einer aufbauenden Schilderung eines Zeitraums und einem 
auslösenden Aufruf zum Handeln und Bewusstsein schaffen wieder.

## S. 12

9 
 
Acknowledgments 
A doctoral research is a reminder to everyone that the generosity and kindness of 
others have no limits and that humans are more than willing to help one another when 
invited to do so. I could have never imagined the dedication in time, attention, concern, 
intellect and even financial, that arose from so many people in the mere effort of helping this 
project, and me personally, grow into what we are today. I would sincerely like to 
acknowledge the encouragement and support I received from so many people, many of whom 
were by my side throughout the entire path of writing this dissertation. 
First of all, this work is greatly indebted to the encouragement and intellectual rigor 
of my advisors: Prof. Philip Ursprung and Prof. Xavier Costa. I am deeply grateful to Prof. 
Ursprung for his supervision (and endless patience), for pushing me towards difficult yet 
fruitful research paths, and for giving me the opportunity to explore and develop my ideas. 
Likewise, Prof. Costa was an immense source of guidance throughout this journey. I was 
floored by Prof. Costa's generosity the day we met in Barcelona and he accepted to be my coadvisor. I never took for granted the dedication he showed this work and the ocean of 
knowledge he provided for this research. 
Furthermore, I cannot thank enough the Institute of the History and Theory of 
Architecture at the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology, the doctoral program Pro*Doc 
Art&Science and the Swiss National Foundation (SNF) for providing a fantastic platform 
through which this Ph.D. thesis developed. It was truly a once in a lifetime chance to be part 
of this interdisciplinary exploration of the connections between various fields in art and 
science. Dr. Nina Zschocke, scientific coordinator of the Pro*Doc program, was constantly 
helping me explore the theoretical and disciplinary limits of our research subjects. This work 
also benefited greatly from the profound discussions with my colleagues at Universität Bern, 
Université de Fribourg, Université de Genève, Université de Lausanne, as well as my 
colleagues at ETH Zurich, among them: Elena Chestnova, Nicholas Drofiak, Marcela 
Hanáčková, Ronny Hardliz, Martin Hartung, Samia Henni, Andreas Kalpakci, Damjan 
Kokalevski, Marija Marić, Seraina Renz, Gabrielle Schaad, and many others. I would also 
particularly like to thank Daniela Ortiz dos Santos for always being my friend and for helping 
me, without fail and since day one, sail 'smoothly' through this doctoral journey. 
This dissertation also wouldn't have been possible if not for the memories, invaluable 
archives and enriching perspectives of the people involved at the Canadian Center for 
Architecture, including Phyllis Lambert, Natasha Leeman, Simon Pennec and Mariana 
Siracusa, where I was awarded a Collection Research Grant in 2014. Prof. Maristella Casciato 
was incredible in dedicating so much care and attention to the CCA Toolkit Seminar fellows, 
myself included; she became our intellectual guru during that period and I was very glad to 
meet her again on other occasions. And Renata Guttman was kind enough to make sure that 
some archival material at the CCA was made available for the first time on my request. 
I also wish to extend my sincerest gratitude to my professors, Rico Frances, Abdul-
Halim Jabr, Sylvia Shorto and Michael Stanton, who were always my intellectual 
correspondences and who triggered my fascination with architectural theory. I also cannot 
thank enough my professor George Arbid for his endless support and friendship and for 
always encouraging me to go beyond my limits; I owe you my professional achievements. 
I also would not have completed this research without the generosity of the artists, 
curators, architects and researchers who granted me interviews and constructive remarks on

## S. 13

10 
my writings, most notably: Stan Allen, Lara Almarcegui, Alex Axinte from StudioBasar, 
Patrick Barron, Stéphanie Bender, Friedrich von Borries, Eduard Bru, Peter Connolly, 
Cynthia Davidson, Davide Deriu, Peter Eisenman, Xavier Fabré, Shahira Fahmi, Ernst Ferré, 
Giulia Fiocca and Lorenzo Romito from Stalker, Kenneth Frampton, Christophe Girot, 
Philippe Grand, Krystallia Kamvasinou, Manolo Laguillo, Phyllis Lambert, Carole Lévesque, 
Luc Lévesque, Josep Lluis Mateo, Dara McGrath, Josep Maria Montaner, Francesc Muñoz, 
Jordi Oliveras, Ramon Paramon, Maurici Pla, William Priestley and the West 8 firm, 
Raumlabor, Felipe Lanuza Rilling, Carmen Rodriguez, Cristian Silva, Suzanne Strum, 
Anthony Vidler and Charles Waldheim. 
I wish to extend my gratitude also to the amazing people I met in Barcelona and who 
made me fall in love with the city and its culture, especially to my Catalan brother Albiol 
Sunyer who singlehandedly taught me both Castellano and Català and to his family who 
welcomed me in their house as one of them. I am also deeply indebted to the family of Ignasi 
de Solà-Morales, Eulàlia Serra, Clarà and Pau de Solà-Morales, who welcomed me to their 
homes and allowed me to spend endless hours in their office looking through their private 
archives. I cherish the memories of those months spent in their company, of our many 
conversations and of that wonderful Christmas dinner we shared together. 
Last but not least, this work is dedicated to my friends and family, their love and 
support is beyond words and thanks. To Gilles, for being my best friend, brother, therapist, 
editor, proofreader, translator, even at times landlord, my thanks is more of a promise to be 
there for him whenever he needs it; To Teo, for being my exceptional friend who at times 
woke up at 6am only to encourage me to move forward with this research; To Lídia, Lea and 
Vassili, who became my family in Zurich; To Gülru, Adriana, Aleksejs, Kaitlin, Lena and 
Julien, for being the greatest friends anyone could ever hope for. This work is also dedicated 
to my family; To my mother, Prof. May Abdallah, for her unconditional love and for raising 
me and for being everything for me all my life; To my second mother, amto Joumana, for 
going out of her way to be there for me and for showing me that you can accomplish 
whatever your heart desires no matter what stands in your way; To my late grandmother 
Salwa, for being one of the most amazing people that ever walked this planet, I miss you; To 
my grandmother Hoda, for setting the bar so high for all of us to try and follow on your 
footsteps; To my siblings, Abdel-Rahman and Kamel, and my quasi-siblings, Line and Faisal, 
for being the crazy awesome brothers and sister anyone could ever hope for; To my father, 
Mounir, who encouraged me and gave me the freedom to follow my dreams; To my aunt and 
uncle, Chadia and Mousbah, for being my moral compass and my non-judging confidants 
throughout my life. And finally, to Onur, for being the great man you are, for being my love 
and my life, for believing in me and for wandering this strange adventure called life together.

## S. 17

14 
Introduction 
   
The evolution of architecture and urbanism is a delicate interplay of theory and 
practice over time, affected by sociological, economic and political events. Clearly, it is 
not linear. Yet, one can differentiate between waves of interest dominating at one time or 
another over others. These do not occur without a theoretical and ideological production 
and a practice that evolves in parallel. However, since the beginning of the new 
millennium, architects and urbanists seem to have been disillusioned with this theoretical 
production in terms of its ability to define emerging urban conditions and, subsequently, 
generate a proper impact in practice. 
The concept of ‘city’ today encompasses more diversity than the available 
vocabulary related to these new emerging spaces is able to incorporate. The contemporary 
urban fabric is diffused and borderless, stretching beyond predefined urban spaces such as 
‘the historic center’ or ‘the periphery’. New globalized landscapes merge both city and 
nature into an uncontrollable condition where all is blurred into still-undefined forms. 
Some of these spaces, these in-betweens, interstices and wastelands, are neither properly 
defined nor well situated within the contemporary urban discourse. As a consequence, 
stakeholders and actors within the city seem to be in need of alternative tools and 
innovative ways to understand and intervene in this heterogeneous landscape. This need 
is reflected in the profusion of theoretical production on contemporary cities, whether as 
publications, lectures, journals, or even as artworks, photographs and exhibitions. 
Professionals, overwhelmed by this abundance, seem discouraged from delving in 
these theoretical discourses. In consequence, these actors have lost the means to intervene 
durably in the contemporary city; a city both continuous and fragmented. The city of 
today, as a result, appears to be a collection of individualized elements and miscellaneous 
forms without urban links, spreading without limits in the global landscape. 
Nevertheless, certain theoretical works are more successful than others in 
proposing innovative approaches which are essential in the face of this urbanity, where 
the built and the non-built are distributed with varied intensities. These contradict 
mainstream thought which still concentrates, strangely, on an urbanism that desperately 
seeks to define itself by its physical presence. According to Henri Lefebvre’s conception of 
urban moments, public space is as much about its representation and direct experience as 
its physical presence. Following such a mindset, new tools for representing urban spaces 
hold as much importance as the analysis of specific public places. By creating such tools, 
the center of interest is inverted, moving away from the constructed towards the nonconstructed, and from the full to the vacant. This counteractive approach proposes

## S. 18

15 
 
methods better tailored to the current urban context, shifting the focus of intervention 
towards landscapes, infrastructure and the spaces left behind by urban planning forces. 
These new methods thus often concentrate on fallows and indeterminate spaces. 
In order to better formulate an understanding of such alternative modes of 
perception and intervention, one must begin by discerning the elaborate array of terms 
and expressions that refer to such indefinite spaces and the connotations accompanying 
each term. As is the case for all words and humans, meaning is embedded in roots and in 
historical, political, economic and cultural contexts. Understanding the contexts, history 
and roots behind terminologies enables us to comprehend holistically a certain notion or 
approach and its position within the larger discourse. 
In this dissertation, attention will be given to one particular term within this 
range of terminologies and the chronology that underpins it: the terrain vague notion and 
the corresponding essay, "Terrain Vague"1. Written in 1994 by the renowned architect 
and theoretician Ignasi Rubió de Solà-Morales, the essay was the medium through which 
the terrain vague term was first presented to the fields of architecture and urbanism. This 
dissertation stems from a fascination with this essay so often translated, used and quoted 
in this context of inverted alternative urban approaches, yet never properly expounded, 
analyzed or contextualized. Hence, one of the main aims of this research is to 
contextualize and understand this essay. The objective is to solidify a theoretical tool for 
understanding and intervening in contemporary urban forms in new ways, the terrain 
vague being an intrinsic element. We will attempt to understand how the roots of the 
term are associated with its development into an architectural and urban discourse from 
the mid-1990s onward. 
The   terrain   vague   as   a   case   study   
“Where there is nothing, everything is possible. Where there is architecture, nothing 
(else) is possible.” 
 
 Rem Koolhaas in “Imagining Nothingness”2 
 
 
The definition of terrain vague often loosely refers to other terminologies which 
encompass “Wasteland” and “no man’s land”. It is a definition that puts many synonyms 
in one basket. The notion of terrain vague, however, does not carry the same undertones 
as others used to denote indefinite spaces. The pejorative connotations associated with 
terms such as ‘desolate space’, ‘wasteland’, ‘indeterminate’ or ‘derelict’ space, for instance, 
                                                                                                                                                                        
1 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: The 
MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
2 Koolhaas, Rem. “Imagining Nothingness.” Small, Medium, Large, Extra-Large: Office for Metropolitan 
Architecture, Rem Koolhaas, and Bruce Mau. Ed. Bruce Mau et al. 2d ed. New York, N.Y: Monacelli Press, 
1998. 199.

## S. 19

16 
are not the prime focus in the essence of ‘terrain vague’. In fact, the way the notion was 
introduced to the fields of architecture and urbanism is inseparable from a particular 
definition, a setting, various contexts and an interdisciplinary standing worthy of special 
attention. 
Moreover, although the notion of wasteland was a running theme decades before, 
the term, terrain vague, has been used recurrently mainly since the mid-1990s. This 
particular discourse, and the way it was shaped in the architectural and urban fields, finds 
its roots in Solà-Morales’s essay and urban strategy. Many, if not most, of the sources 
seeking to define the expression ‘terrain vague’ refer back to or directly quote Solà-
Morales’s text word for word. The essay indeed is of such strong standing that it could be 
considered the core reference for anyone interested in addressing the terrain vague 
subject. Its author is often regarded as the first to have coined the term, even though it 
was already in use as early as 18763. Adopted by architects and urbanists since his time as 
an alternative mode of intervention, Solà-Morales uses this term to describe spaces that 
fall out of the normal urban organization due to their inefficiency in serving a primary 
function in the urban systems of production, consumption, infrastructure, or recreation. 
He asserts that these spaces possess particular qualities that make them notably attractive 
to photographers and architects. 
The "Terrain Vague" essay is thus considered the centerpiece of this dissertation. 
We will try to demonstrate the highly innovative phenomena resulting from this written 
work. The strength of Solà-Morales’s essay lies, in particular, in his coining of the term 
“terrain vague”, but more importantly in the stimulating and intricate definition he 
confers on the term. He argues that the terrains vagues are spaces loaded with intensive 
potential that propel them out of their extensive borders. We will maintain that this 
potential became the catalyst of the series of developments that followed. During the 
same period spanning the 1980s and 1990s, a few other terminologies emerged describing 
fallows or adjacent notions. Among these, the terrain vague holds a special position as it 
allowed architects, urbanists, artists and theoreticians to become inspired and act on such 
spaces or raise awareness of their importance within the urban fabric. 
A chronology and analysis of the formulation, presentation, publication and 
diffusion of the "Terrain Vague" essay will make it possible to understand the origins of 
this theoretical work and relative ideas, as well as the reasons and forms through which it 
developed into an inspiring tool for both theoreticians and practitioners. Subsequently, 
the exploration of artistic, theoretical and practice-based domains inspired by the essay 
will verify and observe the influence of the "Terrain Vague" essay and notion on the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
3 Richepin, Jean. “Les terrains vagues.” Chanson des Gueux  : gueux des champs  ; gueux de Paris  ; nous autres 
gueux. Paris: Maurice Dreyfous, 1881(orig.ed.1876). 113.

## S. 20

17 
 
architectural and urban fields. Finally, the research will investigate contemporary 
protagonists of this urban approach through the terrain vague, as much in their projects as 
in their writings, to understand the forms that this approach can take today. Hence, this 
research seeks to illustrate how the terrain vague notion and essay inspires, through its 
formulation, presentation and reception, a new mode of perception and intervention in 
the urban fabric. 
Main research questions 
   
Keeping the above-mentioned in mind, certain questions arise which appear to 
have crucial significance for this thesis: How and with which tools and methods can one 
adequately deal with the contemporary urban condition? How can one bridge the 
dichotomy between theoretical practices and architectural professional practice? How can 
an architectural notion come to be created then transformed into application strategies? 
These questions are mainly related to defining theoretical tools that reflect the current 
urban fabric, its blurred limits, its new indefinite forms and its contemporary fragmented 
and hybrid condition. Setting these questions within the framework of one theoretical 
apparatus in particular, i.e. the terrain vague notion, these questions take on a contextual 
dimension: What is behind Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague” essay’s success and the 
development of the notion from the 1990s onward? In order to reply to this overarching 
question, other questions need to be initially answered. 
These are firstly related to understanding the essay itself, the formulation of the 
terrain vague notion, the essay’s composition and argumentation styles: What does the 
terrain vague actually entail and what does Solà-Morales’s notion imply? Other subquestions surface: Are his composition and his coining of the term behind the 
mythification of the terrain vague notion? Is Solà-Morales’s terrain vague a political 
statement? Is it a manifesto? What does the essay’s structure inform us about the 
formulation of the discourse on the terrain vague? 
Secondly, questions are related to the theoretical and urban context in which Solà-
Morales proposed and presented the notion of terrain vague: Which eras and spatial 
contexts are linked to the terrain vague? How was the essay presented and how was it 
disseminated throughout the world? 
Thirdly, questions arise regarding the reception of the essay: How and why did the 
essay and notion essentially inspire theoreticians and practitioners? In which directions 
could it still inspire prospective developments on the terrain vague discourse? And finally,

## S. 21

18 
how does the difference in context between the urban developments and conditions of 
the 1990s and those of today alter the terrain vague’s meaning and connotations? 
Hypothesis 
The "Terrain Vague" essay seems to contain enough theoretical basis and 
definition to inspire both practitioners and theoreticians and allow them to use the essay 
as their grounding tool. The establishment of the essay as the core reference behind the 
terrain vague discourse seems not only due to its inspirational writing style but also to its 
successful presentation and diffusion throughout the world. In this thesis, therefore, we 
are interested in understanding and demonstrating the following hypothesis: Solà-
Morales’s “Terrain Vague” essay is a political statement and a hidden manifesto whose 
formulation, presentation and diffusion have led to its strong theoretical standing as well 
as the vibrancy of the terrain vague discourse from the mid-1990s onward. 
The aim here is not to conjecture whether it was the essay’s formulation, 
presentation or diffusion that brought about its success, along with the accompanying 
notion. The objective is rather to understand how the combination of powerful 
formulation, prominent presentation and world-wide diffusion led to the significant 
position of the "Terrain Vague" essay and notion as both a synthesist and catalyst of an era 
and an inverted mode of thinking and intervening in the city, focused on its non-built 
and its non-form. 
Methods of investigation 
In order to reply to the aforementioned questions, the adopted methodology 
consists of five stages. The first chapter of the thesis is a historical and cultural 
contextualization of the terrain vague discussion within the larger scope. Hence, the first 
part of this chapter includes an analysis of the spectrum of terminologies related to 
indefinite spaces and where the terrain vague could be situated within this array. It also 
comprises historical and cultural contexts of the subject preceding the terrain vague essay 
and notion, mainly from the early 20th century onward. The second part of the chapter 
includes a closer contextualization of the "Terrain Vague" essay, thus offering a concise 
insight into its author and the Anyone Corporation, the international interdisciplinary 
platform from which the "Terrain Vague" essay was first presented. 
The second and third chapters of the thesis are an analysis of the "Terrain Vague" 
text per se and its contiguous connotations. We examine how Solà-Morales’s essay 
presents and coins the term “terrain vague”. We observe its composition, structure, and

## S. 22

19 
 
argumentation style, as well as its various published versions. We then proceed with an 
exploration of the references and mentions within the essay, written and visual, to 
understand the larger spectrum of meanings and signifiers linked to the terrain vague 
notion. 
Then the fourth chapter is a further in-depth exploration of the different 
circumstances and contexts in which the “Terrain Vague” essay and notion were created, 
presented and published. This includes an analysis of Solà-Morales’s presentations of the 
essay within different contexts: Barcelona, Princeton, Milano and Montreal, creating in 
parallel a critical chronology of the various events and how they impacted on the 
evolution of the terrain vague notion. A secondary inquiry is focused on Ignasi de Solà-
Morales, his written work, projects and theoretical influences. An analysis of the context 
surrounding him also proved necessary: his contemporaries, the socio-political setting, 
and the innovations in urban development of the time. We also observe in this chapter 
the many first direct impressions of the essay within these different contexts, in an 
attempt to understand its diffusion and corresponding reception from the 1990s onward. 
In the fifth chapter, we explore the main paths that the influence of the essay took 
beyond the 1990s. We define how the essay enabled these different tendencies. Finally, 
we propose directions supported by the terrain vague essay and notion for the 
contemporary architectural and urban discourse and for the role of the architect within 
the terrain vague. An added layer in this paradigm is thus a projection onto the 
contemporary relevance of the notion. 
Research scope 
The scope of this dissertation mainly includes the timeline of Solà-Morales’s essay, 
i.e. the context of the 1990s when the essay was formulated until the present day. In order 
to observe the impact of the terrain vague notion on the fields of architecture and 
urbanism, our time horizon stretches from the late 19th century, when the term was first 
used in an urban context, to our contemporary time, focusing mostly on the period from 
Solà-Morales’s coining and development of the terrain vague notion until its reception 
and influence on the architectural and urban fields. One could evoke endless examples of 
the notion’s current developments, yet the aim is not to be exhaustive but rather to reach 
a conclusive point from which one may have a global view of the subject under study. 
It is important to note that the adopted methodology is bipolar, as it moves in two 
directions and must keep in line with both. We are interested on the one hand in the 
potential of the notion of terrain vague as an approach for perceiving a system, be it a city 
or organism. On the other hand, we are equally interested in its potential to become an

## S. 23

20 
innovative way of intervening in the contemporary fragmented urban fabric. The 
intention is to fix a common lexicon and set an array of instruments between the different 
fields that could serve as a set of tools useful for contemplating the notion of terrain vague 
in its theoretical framework and its application in architectural and urban practices. In 
order to achieve this, a joint venture between theory and practice is needed to extract 
modes of perception and intervention. 
In addition, the setting of this research is necessarily interdisciplinary; which fits 
the inherent interdisciplinary character of both the terrain vague notion and the essay 
that attempts to merge the boundaries between different disciplines, be they architecture, 
art, photography, psychology, philosophy or urbanism. We aim to reach an understanding 
that is potentially useful for future application in the domain of architecture, as well as 
other fields; an understanding that also opens up new directions leading to the importance 
of this notion for urbanism in the 21st century. 
Main   research   tools   
Tools were divided between two complementary interests: the development of the 
terrain vague subject in general and an interest in Solà-Morales’s essay in particular, 
including the various contexts from which it emerged and on which it made an impact. 
Theoretical material thus went in these two directions: firstly, analyzing theories on 
urban developments, wastelands, landscape urbanism and the projects that have 
developed in this direction since the beginning of the 20th century, and secondly 
examining the theoretical work written particularly on the notion of terrain vague and on 
Solà-Morales’s essay and contexts. 
Furthermore, a good part of the research necessarily included the perusal of 
archives. These encompassed documented material in Barcelona, including the private 
archives and library of Ignasi de Solà-Morales, his lectures and archives at the Universitat 
Politècnica de Catalunya, the archives of the Metropolis Master program and the main 
archive of the Centre de Cultura Contemporània de Barcelona (CCCB), the private 
libraries4 and archives at the Col·legi Oficial d'Arquitectes de Catalunya (COAC). Other 
archival material was consulted at the Center for Canadian Architecture (CCA) in 
Montreal, including the photographic archives5, the Gene Summers fonds, the vertical 
                                                                                                                                                                        
4 Including the private library of Ignasi’s brother, Manuel de Solà-Morales, held at the COAC. 
5 Including original photographs of Olivo Barbieri, Thomas Struth, Gabriele Basilico, Gordon Matta-Clark, 
Louis Faurer, Lewis Baltz, Lucia Moholy-Nagy, and Arthur Köster’s Portfolio of photographs and a 
photocollage of models and drawings for the urban renewal of Potsdamer and Leipziger Platz and Mies van 
der Rohe’s (and Martin Wagner) competition entry for the urban renewal of Alexanderplatz of 1928-1929.

## S. 24

21 
 
files 6, and the archives of Peter Eisenman, as well as the archives of the Anyone 
Corporation Fonds, including the files and videos 7 regarding the CCA Anyplace 
conference of 1994 and the Lotus Magazine Project. Attention was thus given to the 
different contexts from which the subject emerged. Extensive research was conducted on 
the various references linked to Solà-Morales’s essay not only within Barcelona but also 
abroad; and research was also carried out on Solà-Morales the man himself, the contexts 
in which he lived and worked and his contemporaries. 
Part of the research also comprised meetings and interviews conducted with 
theoreticians and architects involved in this subject matter. One step in the methodology 
therefore involved seeking advice and input from researchers and professionals with 
expert knowledge of the subject of terrains vagues and analogous concepts. It was 
important to carry out such interviews and discussions in order to unfold the potential for 
negotiation between both theoretical and professional poles and bridge possible relations 
so as to support a sound analysis of the subject matter. These interviews are divided into 
two major categories: those related to Solà-Morales and the “Terrain Vague” essay, and 
others related to the notions of indeterminate spaces and the development of the terrain 
vague discourse beyond the essay. Accordingly, the first set of interviews and meetings 
were arranged with key figures with whom Solà-Morales was in contact or who affected 
the essay’s and notion’s developments: Cynthia Davidson, Peter Eisenman, Eulàlia Serra8, 
Clarà and Pau de Solà-Morales, Manolo Laguillo, Anthony Vidler, Phyllis Lambert, 
Suzanne Strum, Xavier Costa, Jordi Oliveras, Kenneth Frampton, Josep Lluis Mateo, 
Xavier Fabré, Maurici Pla, Josep Maria Montaner, Carmen Rodriguez, Eduard Bru, Ramon 
Paramon, Francesc Muñoz and Ernst Ferré. On the other hand, interviews were 
conducted and correspondence maintained with architects and scientists engaged in 
projects on the terrain vague subject, as well as theoreticians involved in the thoughtprocesses behind the notion’s development: Luc Lévesque, Patrick Barron, Lorenzo 
Romito and Giulia Fiocca from Stalker, Christophe Girot, Lara Almarcegui, Charles 
Waldheim, William Priestley from West 8 firm, Peter Connolly, Stan Allen, Stéphanie 
Bender, Raumlabor, Felipe Lanuza Rilling, Carole Lévesque, Alex Axinte from 
StudioBasar, Shahira Fahmi, Friedrich von Borries, Dara McGrath, Philippe Grand, 
Davide Deriu, Cristian Silva and Krystallia Kamvasinou. In this fashion, both the 
theoretical and practical dimensions of the discussion were tackled. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
6 CCA vertical files holding selected articles and photographs pertinent to Solà-Morales, the Anyone 
Corporation, documentation related to the 1990s and Berlin’s historical developments. 
7 These were made available for the first time by the CCA archives when we requested them for this 
dissertation in 2014. 
8 Interior architect Eulàlia Serra is Solà-Morales’s widow, while Clarà and Pau de Solà-Morales are his 
children, both architects.

## S. 25

22 
Hence, the terrain vague notion belongs to a larger discourse on indeterminate 
spaces, areas at the outskirts of cities and the development of a stream in urban thought 
since the 1950s onward. The terrain vague holds a particular position because of its 
neutrality between positive and negative connotations connected to such spaces. It is also 
a particular term due to its interdisciplinary character as affecting and being affected by a 
range of fields wider than architectural and urban discussions. Furthermore, with a 
changing political and economic configuration, the terrain vague notion has changed in 
meaning between the 1990s and the present day. The development of the notion hints at 
the trajectory of the larger framework around indefinite spaces and emerging urban 
configurations. Nonetheless, despite the notion’s relevance to this rising discourse, not 
many theoreticians, if any, have analyzed the terrain vague notion itself or the context 
from which it stemmed. Therefore, while certain researchers have settled on other 
notions within the discourse, such as Alain Berger’s “Drosscape”, Marc Augé’s “Non-lieu”, 
Peter and Alison Smithson’s “Charged Void”, Stéphanie Bender’s “Void” or Luc Lévesque’s 
“Interstitial”, the aim of this thesis is to contextualize and define the larger spectrum of 
connotations and nuances hidden in the notion of terrain vague. 
 
FIGURE 1: Files and Boxes of Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive.

## S. 27

24 
 
 
 
 
   
CHAPTER 1 – Historical and Cultural 
Contextualization

## S. 28

25 
 
Contexts and connotations before Solà-Morales’s terrain vague 
The terrain vague notion is linked with the larger discourse on indeterminate 
spaces such as wastelands, interstitial spaces, and post-industrial or abandoned cities. It 
also belongs to a tradition of resistance against regimenting and homogenizing forces in 
the city. The notion was mainly introduced to the fields of architecture and urbanism as a 
useful theoretical tool through Solà-Morales’s coining and diffusion of the term. Yet the 
lack of precise deﬁnition and contextualization of the concept has left this area somewhat 
insecure for architects, and this has prevented a clear evolution within this domain. 
In this chapter, we attempt to settle certain discrepancies as we examine how the 
terrain vague notion follows a tradition of resistance against the ordered city and how it 
hints at a desire to oppose preordered architectural, economic and political forms of 
thinking the city. Moreover, this section is about the contexts that preceded and set the 
tone for the terrain vague notion9. We acknowledge as premise that the discourse on 
indefinite spaces10 did not start with Solà-Morales and in fact took root before the 1990s. 
The terrain vague notion hence falls within a larger scope of urban void strategies11, 
encompassing the concept of “nothingness” 12 and alternative modes of architectural 
perception and intervention. This interest began in the early 20th century and developed 
into the 21st century. It is still growing in popularity, and projects such as the “High Line” 
in New York in 2003 and the more recent “High Line for London” competition in 201213 
have evoked its contemporary applicability.. This does not necessarily mean that this way 
of thinking emerged strictly from the 20th century, as no thought emerges from nothing. 
Yet, it was during this era that thought on void strategies and wastelands coagulated into 
some key concepts and terms, such as the terrain vague, which still echo in current 
thought. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
9 The development of the terrain vague notion’s more direct context is analyzed in the fourth chapter. 
10 It is important to mention that, for the rest of the thesis, we will sometimes use the terms “derelict”, 
“indeterminate” or “indefinite” to designate terrains vagues. Although they are used interchangeably in this 
thesis with the terms “terrain vague” and “interstitial”, these are actually different because they carry, as 
mentioned previously, negative connotations or are generally based on ‘vague’ nuances. We do not intend 
these meanings when we use these terms throughout this thesis. 
11 Stephanie Bender uses this term in the title of her thesis that revolves around exploring strategies of void 
in architectural and urban history. – Stephanie Bender. “Le Vide. Nouvelles Stratégies Urbaines.” EPFL, 2011. 
12 Rem Koolhaas and OMA, in the book SMLXL, employ this term in order to set out an alternative strategy 
on urban fabric. They also employ this thinking as the basis of the project “Ville Nouvelle Melun-Sénart”. 
13 A competition was held to find new 'green' designs for the development of London's spaces; an incentive 
by the Landscape Institute, Garden Museum and Mayor of London. The winner was chosen in October 2012.

## S. 29

26 
 
FIGURE 2: Terrain Vague before the High Line Project in New York. 2003. Photograph. – The High Line was 
the former 1930s West Side industrial railway that was abandoned in the 1980s. In 2003, a competition 
took place to regenerate and refurbish the infrastructure. 
 
FIGURE 3: Corner, James, Field Operations with Diller Scofidio + Renfro. High Line Project (2003) in New York. 
2010. Photograph. – The winning project of the High Line competition of 2003 was inspired by the terrain 
vague’s wild seeded landscape.

## S. 30

27 
 
 
FIGURE 4: Fletcher Priest Architects. Pop Down. Winning project of the Landscape Institute’s “High Line for 
London” Competition, 2012. Design Entry. – The winning project was an underground mushroom farm and a 
walk through the tunnels of the former Post Office Railway. This reinvented terrain vague capitalizes on 
London’s disused tunnels and lost waterways. 
 
By identifying the terminologies and respective connotations heralding and 
surrounding the terrain vague notion, we aim to construct a solid contextual framework. 
This can be divided into the following directions: “indeterminacy and vagueness”, 
“interstitiality and in-betweenness”, “liminality and borders”, and finally “emptiness and 
void strategies”. As we cannot be exhaustive, the different relevant terminologies have 
been joined within sets that follow the aforementioned connotations. The interest is on 
projecting characteristics of this larger discourse and understanding what meanings it may 
encompass. These sets designate the various facets of the terrain vague notion. The 
premises behind the selections are hence intimately correlated with Solà-Morales’s essay 
and notion. Furthermore, upon looking into the array of terminologies, each group of 
terms seems to refer to a certain episode in time and place that resulted in some 
formulation of ideas filtered through one term. When and from where they emerged 
affects their meaning and differences and changes in perspective; they thus lead to 
different perceptions and intervention processes. 
Indeterminate   spaces   
The first set of terminologies can be linked through connotations of 
indeterminacy: undecided spaces, indeterminate spaces, blurred spaces and loose spaces. 
These refer to the vagueness inherent in the terrain vague notion. This form of thinking

## S. 31

28 
on blurriness and undecidability is mainly embedded in Derrida’s theories14 of the 1970s, 
especially in his essay “La Double Séance” in which he blurs Mallarmé’s Mimique with his 
own essay and reintroduces the idea of mimesis as a form of self-presentation. Throughout 
the essay, Derrida also plays with typographic spacing in order to call attention towards 
the syntactical importance of spaces when reading a text. Derrida links this spacing with 
the notion of “undecidability” caused by the mere praxis of reading: 
“It is the “between” whether it names fusion or separation, that thus carries all the force 
of the operation. The hymen must be determined through the ‘entre’ and not the other way 
around. The hymen in the text (crime, sexual act, incest, suicide, simulacrum) is inscribed at 
the very tip of this indecision. This tip advances according to the irreducible excess of the 
syntactic over the semantic. The word "between" has no full meaning on its own.”15 
Derrida argues that the undecidable between a series of oppositions and 
contradictions produces an effect of in-betweenness. This could also be said of 
indeterminate spaces that draw their meaning from their surroundings and from the act of 
“reading”, i.e. dwelling in the city where the meaning of these spaces is provided by 
contrast. These ideas can be better understood in architectural terms through the work of 
architect Peter Eisenman in the 1980s and 1990s16, which is linked with deconstructivist 
ideas of perceiving the city. Similar to the importance Derrida attaches to space and to the 
undetermined in the act of reading, Eisenman 17 links the undetermined to creative 
practices. He states: 
“All individual processes […] result in embodied systems. They are already conditioned 
by significance. In this sense, the production of the interstitial can be seen as a critique of the 
operational idea of process.”18 
Eisenman thus associates interstitiality with a critique of preset methods. He 
creates interspaces, space and blurred zones in his projects in order to destabilize the 
experience of space and to question ideas about process and time. He later describes these 
blurred spaces as: 
“forms of architectural effects that displace our conventional or expected experience of 
space.”19 
Eisenman’s notion of indeterminate spaces is thus linked with spatial experience. 
On a more concrete level, in 1996 he applied these ideas to a project on the Aronoff 
Centre for Design and Art at the University of Cincinnati, where he created a new 
                                                                                                                                                                        
14 Derrida, Jacques. “The Double Session.” Dissemination. London: Athlone, 1981. 220–221. 
15 Derrida, Jacques. Ibid. 
16 Eisenman, Peter. “The Processes of the Interstitial: Spacing and the Arbitrary Text.” Blurred Zones: 
Investigations of the Interstitial  : Eisenman Architects, 1988-1998. New York: Monacelli Press, 2003. 94–101. 
17 It is important to mention here that Peter Eisenman was an influential figure in Solà-Morales’s work. 
Although the two architects did not follow similar theoretical routes, Solà-Morales was, during the 1980s 
and during a part of the 1990s, inspired by Eisenman’s thought on deconstructivism. More will be discussed 
later in this thesis regarding the collaboration between them. 
18 Eisenman, Peter. “Processes of the Interstitial.” Written into the Void: Selected Writings, 1990-2004. New 
Haven: Yale University Press, 2007. 69. 
19 Ibid. 109.

## S. 32

29 
 
addition to the building with the intent of blurring the boundaries between old and new; 
indeterminacy is thus key in his design. 
 
 
FIGURE 5: Eisenman, Peter. New Addition for the Aronoff Centre for Design and Art. University of Cincinnati, 
1996. Architectural drawings.

## S. 33

30 
The connotations of indeterminacy inherent to the notion of terrain vague are 
therefore associated in Derrida and Eisenman’s work with the idea that time and 
experience alters our expectations of space and redefines our position towards it. It is thus 
related to temporal variations and constantly affects urban spaces in transformation. 
Although such approaches towards the urban leave room for interpretation, they are 
generally set on a more negative rather than positive note. Aside from the negative “un-” 
and “in-” prefixes of the aforementioned terminologies, only nuances of “vague” are 
present in these terms. The territorial sense of “terrain” within the terrain vague seems 
lost, consequently tipping the notion towards a negatively-weighted polarization. 
Interstitial   spaces   
The second set of terminologies is related to ideas of in-betweenness: interstitial 
spaces, intermediate spaces, transmilieu, intermediary, interscape, and spaces in-between. 
These refer to the intermediary aspect of the terrain vague, both metaphorically and 
physically, between what is city and what is not, what is built and what is non-built. The 
term “interstitial” in particular derives from the Latin “interstit(ium)” (space between, 
interval), stemming from the verb “intersistere” meaning to stand (sistere) between 
(inter). Interstices form adequate indicators of evolving city morphologies. The term is 
associated with notions of time and territoriality. Characteristics such as ephemerality, 
informality and intensity jump to the foreground as well. A variety of artistic 
interventions, as well as literary works and photography, have made the topic of 
interstitiality relevant on a conceptual level, both projected and imagined. A good amount 
has been written on the notion of interstitiality in the last fifty to sixty years. 
Once again Peter Eisenman’s contribution was important in activating and 
qualifying the interstitial condition. The theme of interstitial emerged in Eisenman's 
practice between 1963 and 1988; his theoretical work also often tackled such ideas20. 
During this period, he valorized the image of in-between spaces, and more importantly he 
tackled the landscape and his architecture through interstitial approaches. These processes 
in Eisenman's work often focused on destabilizing the dweller, blurring thresholds, 
valorizing the relational aspects of in-between spaces and finally activating intensities and 
oppositions within these. 
Interstitiality is also explored in Eisenman’s narratives for the series of projects 
entitled Cities of Artificial Excavation. As architect and theoretician Luc Lévesque 
describes: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
20 One of his most prominent articles is, for instance, entitled “Processes of the Interstitial.”, while his book 
titles include Blurred Zones or Written into the Void.

## S. 34

31 
 
 “The project expresses the historical importance of Berlin's interstitial character, its 
suspension 'in time and space'. This choice is all the more significant since it is made in the 
context of a competition where the basic premises seem to be encouraging the opposite 
approach of obliterating the traces of indetermination characteristic of Berlin's history to 
make place for a literal and unbroken reconstitution of its urban fabric.”21 
He then continues by describing Eisenman's project as: 
"echoing the words of Homer, the old poet of Der Himmel über Berlin (1987) who, 
walking through the Potsdamer Platz wastelands, evokes the interstices - 'Why doesn't 
everyone see them...?' - giving access to the 'land of storytelling' and Berlin's hidden 
geography."22 
Thus, as Lévesque advocates, Eisenman’s interstices evoke the hidden and the 
invisible in the interstitial, whether physical manifestations, tensions or progressions in 
time. Eisenman's notion of the interstitial is understood as much on a spatial level as on 
experiential and temporal levels. More importantly, Eisenman’s attempts often provoke 
ruptures through these spatial and temporal conditions. In line with deconstructivist 
thought, Eisenman sees the interstitial as a means to deter, provoke and activate 
consciousness through fractures in time and space and the act of standing in-between. 
 
FIGURE 6: Wenders, Wim. Wings of Desire (Der Himmel über Berlin). 1987. Film. 
This dual aspect between spatial and temporal in thinking the interstice was 
already visible in the 1970s and 1980s in the French theorist and linguist Roland Barthes' 
understanding of intervals. Barthes' fascination with Japan led him to write about its 
culture, semiotics and symbolism. One of the concepts he seemed most fond of was the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
21 Lévesque, Luc. “Trajectories of Interstitial Landscapeness.” Urban Interstices: The Aesthetics and the 
Politics of the in-Between. Ed. Andrea Mubi Brighenti. Farnham, Surrey, England: Ashgate Pub. Limited, 
2013. 42. 
22 Ibid. 43.

## S. 35

32 
notion of "Ma" which refers to the Japanese understanding of both space and time 
simultaneously. Barthes considers Ma as the exemplary interval. He states: 
“Let us extend the Japanese attitude [...] which does not conceptualize either time or 
space, but only the interval, the relation of two moments, two places or objects ... try to 
conceive [...] the spacing between subjects.”23 
He thus promotes an approach to viewing our surrounding through an interstitial 
correlation between space and time inspired by Japanese culture. One could argue that 
the interval in this fashion is more important in Barthes’ reasoning than the moments or 
places themselves. Moreover, Barthes considers the city as a discourse of language, stating: 
“The city speaks to its inhabitants; we speak our city, the city where we are, simply by 
living in it, by wandering through it, by looking at it.” 24 
In the spirit of semiology, it therefore seems sensible that he attributes the 
interstitial to the term Ma and presents in turn a new form of perceiving and 
understanding the city through this temporal and spatial accord. This form of viewing the 
city is in line with Solà-Morales’s essay, as will be observed in the following chapter. 
Furthermore, Barthes describes Ma in relation to the technique of Haiku, the 
traditional seventeen-syllable Japanese poems that often evoke images of nature, praising 
the silence and the significations hidden in-between the verses. He remarks: 
 “If there was no space between matter, the human race would fit in a thimble.”25 
In this sense, Barthes recognizes the interstitial as a space to breathe and to simply 
be, both in space and time. In-betweenness becomes a prerequisite for mere existence. 
The relational becomes more important that the solitary entities. 
The link between Ma and interstitiality go beyond Barthes’ interest. In her book 
Roland Barthes at the Collège de France, Lucy O’Meara describes how the architect Arata 
Isozaki was inspired by the term to generate an exhibition in 1978 entitled 
“Ma. Espace/temps au Japon”, which took place in 1978 at the Musée des Arts Décoratifs 
in Paris26. According to O’Meara, Isozaki claimed that the exhibition in turn inspired 
Derrida and Foucault. In an interview with Hans Obrist, he described how Derrida was 
motivated by the exhibition to write his concept Espa[c]ement and how Foucault 
                                                                                                                                                                        
23 Our trans. from French: “Prolongeons l'attitude japonaise […] qui ne conceptuaIise ni le temps ni l'espace, 
mais seulement l'intervalle, le rapport de deux moments, de deux lieux ou objets […] essayons de concevoir 
[…] l'espacement entre les sujets” - Barthes, Roland. “Ma: Espace-temps du Japon (Paris: Festival d’automne 
à Paris, 1978).” Œuvres complètes. T. 3: 1974 - 1980. Ed. Éric Marty. Paris: Éd. du Seuil, 2002. 479–480. 
24 Barthes, Roland. “Semiology and the Urban.” The Semiotic Challenge. 1st ed. New York: Hill and Wang, 
1988. 200. 
25 Our trans. from French: “S'il n'y avait pas d'espace entre la matière, tout le genre humain tiendrait dans un 
dé à coudre.” - Barthes, Roland, and Nathalie Léger. “‘Mon’ Haïku.” La Préparation Du Roman. I et II, Notes 
de Cours et de Séminaires Au Collège de France, 1978-1979 et 1979-1980. Paris: Seuil  ; IMEC, 2003. 59. 
26 O’Meara, Lucy. “Japonisme and Minimal Existence in the Cours.” Roland Barthes at the Collège de France. 
Oxford University Press, 2012. 132–140.

## S. 36

33 
 
embarked on travels of Japan after the exhibition27. Barthes also took the opportunity of 
this exhibition to write an article about it entitled “L’intervalle” 28; his interest was 
evidently based on the interstitial. 
Similarly, ideas of interstitiality as a condition of space and time are also reflected 
in Michel De Certeau’s theories of the 1980s. He argues that understanding our 
relationships of space and time is a prerequisite to our understanding of everyday 
practices, especially those within the interstices where the discord between the marginal 
and the powerful occurs. He argues: 
“The surface of this order is everywhere punched and torn open by ellipses, drifts, and 
leaks of meaning: it is a sieve-order.”29 
Unlike Barthes, who finds relational links through Ma, De Certeau’s interest seems 
more focused, as in Eisenman’s case, on the dissonance that occurs at the interstice. De 
Certeau finds in these spaces a place for resistance and for temporal “tactics” that can, 
according to him, assist the powerless and the spaceless against the hegemonic structures 
of order: 
“I call a ‘tactic’ a calculus which cannot count on a ‘proper’ (a spatial or institutional 
localization)… The place of the tactic belongs to the other. A tactic insinuates itself into 
another’s place, fragmentarily, without ever taking over its entirety, without being able to 
keep it at a distance… The ‘proper’ is a victory of space over time. On the contrary, because it 
does not have a place, a tactic depends on time – it is always on the watch for opportunities 
that must be ‘seized on the wing’.”30 
De Certeau’s interest thus centers on the time factor within tactics on urban 
public spaces and particularly on the interstice. He does not describe the act of 
appropriating the interstitial, but rather explores the means by which such appropriations 
are possible. 
These ideas of resistance as related to the interstitial and the intermediary are 
furthermore recurrent in some of the theories of Gilles Deleuze and Felix Guattari. 
Deleuze describes the orderly city as the space for gods while demons dwell in the 
interstice: 
“A flight is a sort of delirium. To be delirious is exactly to go off the rails. There is 
something demoniacal or demonic in a line of flight. Demons are different from gods, because 
gods have fixed attributes, properties and functions, territories and codes: they have to do 
with rails, boundaries and surveys. What demons do is jump across intervals, and from one 
interval to another.”31 
                                                                                                                                                                        
27 Ibid. 160. 
28 Barthes, Roland. “L’Intervalle (in Le Nouvel Observateur, October 1978).” Œuvres complètes. T. 3: 1974 - 
1980. Ed. Éric Marty. Paris: Éd. du Seuil, 2002. 475–477. 
29 Certeau, Michel de. The Practice of Everyday Life. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1984. 108. 
30 Ibid. xix. 
31 Deleuze, Gilles, and Claire Parnet. “Un Entretien, Qu’est-Ce Que C’est, à Quoi Ça Sert.” Dialogues. Paris: 
Flammarion, 1977. 40.

## S. 37

34 
In this fashion, a contrast is created between codes and territories on the one hand 
and intervals and movement on the other. Deleuze nonetheless defines in his theory of 
consolidation a necessary consistency between the two poles: 
“This is not a contradiction, there must be an arrangement of intervals, a distribution of 
inequalities, such that it is sometimes necessary to make a hole in order to consolidate. […] 
Consolidation is not content to come after; it is creative. The fact is that the beginning always 
begins in-between, intermezzo. Consistency is the same as consolidation, it is the act that 
produces consolidated aggregates, of succession as well as of coexistence, by means of three 
factors just mentioned; intercalated elements, intervals, and articulations of superposition. 
[…] It is no longer a question of imposing a form upon a matter but of elaborating an 
increasingly rich and consistent material, the better to tap into increasingly intense forces.”32 
The accent is once again, similar to Barthes, on the idea of the interval as a 
connecting and harmonizing element that helps, albeit through resistance and creativity 
in time and movement, the consolidation and harmonization of the system in its entirety. 
In conclusion, through the notions of interstitiality visible in Eisenman’s theories 
from the 1970s to the 1990s, Barthes’ in the 1970s, De Certeau’s in the 1980s and Deleuze 
and Guattari’s theories in the 1970s and 1980s, one can delineate an interest in the mid to 
late 20th century in defining interstices. These are considered spaces of vital importance to 
an understanding of our surrounding environment. They are often defined through 
relational processes of time and space. More importantly, two directions seem to divide 
the theoreticians. On the one hand, resistance through the interstice is argued for by 
means of continuity and concord, such as in Barthes’ description of Ma and l’intervalle 
and in Deleuze and Guattari’s theory of consolidation. On the other hand, resistance 
through intermediary spaces is approached through methods of rupture and discord, such 
as in Eisenman’s work on interstitiality and in De Certeau’s theories of tactics and 
everyday practices. In this respect, Solà-Morales’s essay on the terrain vague notion 
appears to amend this duality between dissonance and continuity, as will be argued in the 
following chapter. 
Furthermore, interest in intermediary spaces entails questioning the relationship 
between the city and the experience of ‘in-betweenness’, i.e. the location of intermediary 
spaces in the experience of dwelling. When perceiving the terrain vague from an 
interstitial perspective, the focus is no longer on the architectural physical boundaries; 
                                                                                                                                                                        
32 Trans. from French: “Ce n'est pas le contraire, il faut qu'il y ait aménagement d'intervalles, répartition 
d'inégalités, au point que, pour consolider, il faut parfois faire un trou. […] La consolidation ne se contente pas de 
venir après, elle est créatrice. C'est que le commencement ne commence qu'entre deux, intermezzo. La consistance 
est précisément la consolidation, l'acte qui produit le consolidé, de succession comme de coexistence, avec les trois 
facteurs : intercales, intervalles et superpositions-articulations. […] Il ne s'agit plus d'imposer une forme à une 
matière, mais d'élaborer un matériau de plus en plus riche, de plus en plus consistant, apte dès lors à capter des 
forces de plus en plus intenses.” - Deleuze, Gilles, and Félix Guattari. “Devenir-Intense, Devenir-Animal, 
Devenir-Imperceptible.” Mille Plateaux. Capitalisme et Schizophrénie  ; Tome 2. Paris: Éditions de Minuit, 
1980. 328–9.

## S. 38

35 
 
form loses its importance as the focus shifts to the center of the space at hand. Attention is 
thus drawn to the event of transition through, in or out of the space. One can hence value 
the intensities and extensities of the spaces through such transitions. 
The concept of interstitiality is therefore close to the notion of terrain vague, 
although the former often refers to spaces within the city, and often on a smaller scale 
than the general conception of terrain vague. It could be said that Solà-Morales’s 
definition of terrain vague incorporates the interstitial in terms of intervention 33 . 
Nevertheless, what is most important in the idea of the interstitial is the condition of 
standing in-between; the condition of vagueness, associated more with the terrain vague 
notion, seems less important. The interstitial then, as well as other terms referring to this 
condition of in-betweenness or intermediacy, refers only to one nuance incorporated by 
the terrain vague. 
Liminal   spaces      
A third set within the range of terminologies refers to limits and urban borderline 
conditions: liminal space, threshold, edge land, no man’s land, marginal space and hybrid 
space. These terms are related to theories on territoriality, but also to theories of fracture, 
rupture and dislocation. Liminal space in particular is often used interchangeably with 
interstitial space, which is not accurate since “liminal” refers in its definition and 
etymology to the limits of space and ideas of becoming, while “interstitial” relates to ideas 
of being within or in-between spaces. 
It was probably in Athens that the first conceptualization of such liminal 
territorially-defined peripheral voids occurred as a natural outcome of the primary 
planning of cities. The idea of a void existing on the outskirts of the city, known as the 
chora (or khôra, χώρα), was linked with ideas of the city as an organism in continuous 
transformation and potential expansion that could ultimately lead to the annexation of 
neighboring cities. Such spaces in ancient Greece were in opposition, yet at the same time 
complementary, to the city itself (Astu or Asty). 
Referring to wastelands and terrains vagues, Plato, in Timaeus, uses chora to 
designate a metaphysical and hard-to-grasp concept that very roughly designates an 
interval, receptacle, a third space between the realm of ideas and that of the material 
world, or a radical otherness that gives place to being. Consequently, chora does not 
merely correspond to the empty space in which objects are inserted, but also to the 
property of senses and the instability of being, a neutral condition of possibility. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
33 As we will observe in the fifth chapter the influence of the essay and resulting intervention modes.

## S. 39

36 
Due to the potentiality, liminality and expectancy inherent in chora, the notion 
could indicate the beginning of thought about terrains vagues as expectant liminal spaces. 
These ideas lingered and rematerialized with the development of Modern, industrial and 
post-industrial cities. The notion of chora resurfaced in the work of Derrida and his 
collaboration with Peter Eisenman in 1985 on the Parc de la Villette “Chora L Works” 
project in Paris. In 199334 Derrida published a short text entitled Khôra35 in which he 
playfully reintroduced the term from Plato into deconstructivist thought. He stated: “One 
cannot even say of it (chora) that it is neither this nor that or that it is both this and that”, 
and “this paradoxical characteristic of chora constitutes a multiple ‘oscillation between 
two poles’” 36 Throughout the text, Derrida denies to chora any metaphor, logic, 
comparison or materialization that could help situate the term. Conclusively, it remains 
elusive, in a liminal state. 
Moreover, Derrida describes chora in terms of interval and spacing, thereby 
linking the notion with his concept of espacement. As Clare Blackburne states: 
 “Espacement also evokes the ambiguous figure of the interstice, and is related to the 
equally complex Derridean notions of chora.”37 
In fact, Derrida describes chora as an “unidentifiable excess that is the spacing which is 
the condition for everything to take place.”38 Then he describes spacing as “the impossibility for an 
identity to be closed on itself, on the inside of its proper interiority, or on its coincidence with itself. 
The irreducibility of spacing is the irreducibility of the other.”39 In this sense, chora presents a 
liminal state between both the becoming of the self and that of the other. It makes 
identity both possible and impossible. 
The concept of chora was almost materialized through the use of metaphors, 
interrupted spaces and interstices, in the project proposal “Chora L Works” (which was 
never realized), and the respective publication40 that presents the discussions of Peter 
Eisenman and Derrida between 1985 and 1987. These discussions, which continued until 
the beginning of the 1990s, would have considerable consequences on the development of 
deconstructivist thought in architecture, especially after the 1988 exhibition 
Deconstructivist Architecture at the MoMa in New York. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
34 Coincidentally, a year before the first publication of the "Terrain Vague" essay. 
35 Derrida, Jacques. Khôra. Paris: Galilée, 1993. 
36 Derrida, Jacques. “Chora.” Chora L Works: Jacques Derrida and Peter Eisenman. Ed. Peter Eisenman, 
Jeffrey Kipnis, and Thomas Leeser. New York: Monacelli Press, 1997. 15–32. 
37 Blackburne, Clare. “(Up) Against the (In) Between: Interstitial Spatiality in Genet and Derrida.” Parrhesia: 
a Journal of Critical Philosophy 3 (2007): 22–32. 
38 Kipnis, Jeffrey et al., eds. Chora L Works: Jacques Derrida and Peter Eisenman. New York: Monacelli Press, 
1997. 203. 
39 Derrida, Jacques, and Alan Bass. Positions. Chicago, III.: Univ. Chicago Press, 1998. 94. 
40 Kipnis, Jeffrey et al., eds. Chora L Works: Jacques Derrida and Peter Eisenman. New York: Monacelli Press, 
1997.

## S. 40

37 
 
The notion of chora also comes close to the terrain vague notion of present-day 
architecture due to its relevance to the Anyplace conference and publications in which 
Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay was first presented in 1994. Firstly, the critic and 
curator Akira Asada and the architect Arata Isosaki find parallels between the 
deconstructivist understanding of chora and the Asian notion Qi. They describe both as 
liminal spaces of in-between: 
 “…by means of deconstruction in the mode of Jacques Derrida, who deconstructed 
Western metaphysics and rediscovered chora as an abysmal base […] Qi will thereby appear 
neither as the place of Being nor of nothingness but as a placing/spacing in between them, 
passing through them.”41 
Once again, chora is the spacing in between, whether in Asian or Western 
understanding. Secondly, again in the Anyplace publication, architectural theorist Alberto 
Pérez-Gómez reintroduces the Platonic chora, the “ontological continuum between Being 
and Becoming” 42, to describe the dense relation between space and time and the 
importance of uncertainty to the urban experience. 
Nonetheless, the concept of chora only intersects partially with the notion of 
terrain vague since it is presently linked with deconstructivist ideas feebly relevant to the 
terrain vague and how it developed through Solà-Morales’s writing. His "Terrain Vague" 
essay, as will be witnessed in the following chapters, falls in between Derridian theories 
and Deleuzian notions of interstitiality, but never fully complies with either. The 
common point between the two philosophers that joins them both to the notion of terrain 
vague is the idea of liminal undecidability, as theorist Elizabeth Grosz describes: 
 “The space of the in-between is that which is not a space, a space without boundaries of 
its own, […] whose form is the outside of the identity, not just of an other but of others. […] 
The model of an in-betweenness, of an indeterminacy or undecidability, pervades the 
writings of contemporary philosophers, including Deleuze, Derrida, Serres, and Irigaray, 
where it goes under a number of different names: difference, repetition, iteration, the 
interval, among others. The space in between things is the space in which things are undone, 
the space to the side and around, which is the space of subversion and fraying, the edges of 
any identity’s limits. In short, it is the space of the bounding and undoing of the identities 
which constitute it.”43 
The terrain vague incorporates the idea of standing in between, which is related to 
both Derrida’s ideas of chora as the ideal in-between space as well as Deleuze’s notions of 
“milieu”44 and “interval”, as previously mentioned. In this sense, not only does the notion 
                                                                                                                                                                        
41 Isozaki, Arata, and Akira Asada. “Fabrication of Anyplace.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, 
Massachusetts: The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 206–210. 
42 Pérez-Gómez, Alberto. “Architecture as Science: Analogy or Disjunction?” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia 
Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 66–78. 
43 Grosz, Elizabeth. “In Between the Natural in Architecture and Culture.” Architecture from the Outside: 
Essays on Virtual and Real Space. MIT Press, 2001. 91–109.
