44 An interview between author and Peter Connolly, the landscape architect recurrently confronted ideas of 
Deleuze with the terrain vague notion, especially Deleuzian concepts of “milieu”, which he describes as the

## S. 41

38 
bridge an indirect link between the two streams of thought, it also evokes the positioning 
of identity and the “other” in relation to this space in between. 
The question is thus not only philosophical and theoretical, but also sociological. 
In this respect, the theories of sociologist Homi Bhabha on resistance through intervals, 
which emerged in the early 1990s, become pertinent. Derrida was influential on Bhabha’s 
postcolonial critique; one can find similarities between Derrida’s chora and Bhabha’s 
theorizing of “thirdspace” and “hybrid culture”: 
 “The template of the 'non-place' becomes the colonial space. The colonial space is the 
terra incognita or the terra nulla, the empty or wasted land whose history has to be begun.”45 
Bhabha is worthy of mentioning in this context because of the different political 
and sociological layers he introduces to the discussion. He is interested as much in 
interstices as in those inhabiting such spaces, which he denotes as “hybrid cultures”. He 
considers "thirdspace" as interstices that host hybrid cultures of in-between conditions, an 
eternal other, those in between identity, neither colonizing nor colonized. He states: 
 “The migrant culture of the ‘in-between’, the minority position, dramatizes the activity 
of culture’s untranslatability; and in so doing, it moves the question of culture’s appropriation 
beyond the assimilationist’s dream, or the racist’s nightmare, of a full transmittal of subject 
matter and towards an encounter with the ambivalent process of splitting and hybridity that 
marks the identification with culture’s difference.”46 
Moreover, "thirdspace" is not only a theoretical space; Bhabha's intent is also 
spatial. Third space is an attempt to spatialize where hybridization occurs. As Felipe 
Hernandez states: 
 “The Third Space offsets the theoretical operability of binary structures of cultural 
analysis […] It could also be argued that the Third Space is an attempt to ‘spatialize’ the 
liminal position it represents; in other words, to give a certain tangibility to the in-between 
space where hybridization occurs, and from where hybrid designations emerge.”47 
In this sense, Bhabha's theories of space also reflect ideas on representations of 
space as theorized by Henri Lefebvre. 
Furthermore, a similar duality echoed among other theoreticians also appears in 
Bhabha's understanding of the position of liminal space: on the one hand, there is its 
authority and places of manifestation, while on the other hand, there are hybrid cultures 
and the thirdspace they inhabit. Bhabha, however, in a similar vein to Barthes, finds a 
strong correlation and complementarity between the two poles. He states: 
“Strategies of hybridization reveal an estranging movement in the ‘authoritative’. [...] 
The precept attempts to objectify itself as generalized knowledge or a normalizing, 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
idea of immersing oneself in the terrain and becoming conscious of the affects and aesthetics conveyed by 
the terrain and the world taken as a whole. - Interview between author and Peter Connolly. 26 July 2017. 
45 Bhabha, Homi K. The Location of Culture. London, New York: Psychology Press, 1994. 302. 
46 Bhabha. Ibid. 224. 
47 Hernandez, Felipe. Bhabha for Architects. Routledge, 2010. 89.

## S. 42

39 
 
hegemonic practice, while the hybrid strategy or discourse opens up a space of negotiation 
where power is unequal but its articulation may be equivocal. Such negotiation is neither 
assimilation nor collaboration. It makes possible the emergence of an ‘interstitial’ agency that 
refuses the binary representation of social antagonism. […] Once the liminality of the nationspace is established, and its signifying difference is turned from the boundary ‘outside’ to its 
finitude ‘within’, the pedagogical and the performative threat of cultural difference is no 
longer a problem of ‘other’ people. The national subject splits in the ethnographic perspective 
of culture’s contemporaneity and provides both a theoretical position and a narrative 
authority for marginal voices or minority discourse.”48 
In this sense, marginalized cultures in Bhabha’s theories are a consequence and a 
part of a “nation-space” that have become too complex for a binary division between 
colonizing and colonized. In this respect, the terrain vague is the legitimate outcome and 
an integral part, neither colonizing nor colonized, of the regulating city and cannot be 
dissociated from it. Hence, Bhabha’s theorization of thirdspace also reflects some of 
Derrida's approach to chora. He considers thirdspace as neither within nor without: 
 “The hybrid is ‘neither one nor the other’, instead, it is always in-between, where it 
continually transforms itself according to the dynamics of cultural interaction.”49 
In addition, similar to Solà-Morales's terrain vague as will be observed in the 
following chapters, ideas of potentiality and an indirect call for action arise in Bhabha's 
writing. He states: 
“Traditional discourses on authority enables a form of subversion, founded on the 
undecidability that turns the discursive conditions of dominance into the grounds of 
intervention.”50 
Bhabha thus transforms this philosophical discourse of liminality, witnessed in 
Derridian terms and in Deleuzian terms, into a social discourse and an active platform for 
action. The liminal is no longer only a form of thinking identity, the other and space, it 
also becomes a form of territorial intermediation on the “thirdspace”. 
Nonetheless, in conclusion, this branch in terminologies offers, once more, only 
one form of comprehending the terrain vague. In fact, Solà-Morales’s outlining of the 
notion encompasses both the interstitial scale within the urban fabric and spaces on the 
outskirts of the city. The terrain vague helps in joining these two edges, interstitial and 
liminal, through ‘vague’ connotations of dwelling in the middle as well as ‘terrain’ 
connotations of delimiting such spaces. It is therefore restricting to define the terrain 
vague only through these borderline conditions, especially now that landscape, nature 
and city have blurred boundaries and urban discourses have evolved to include many 
more nuances. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
48 Bhabha, Homi K. The Location of Culture. London, New York: Psychology Press, 1994. 150. 
49 Bhabha, Homi K. “Third Space.” Identity: Community, Culture, Difference. Ed. Jonathan Rutherford. 
Lawrence & Wishart, 1990. 207–221. 
50 Bhabha, Homi K. The Location of Culture. London, New York: Psychology Press, 1994. 112.

## S. 43

40 
Derelict   spaces   
A fourth branch of terminologies is associated with connotations of decay and 
ruins: wasteland, derelict space, leftover space, drosscape, abandoned space, slag heap, 
trashed space, residual space, brown site and post-industrial ruins. These seem to be 
linked to a discourse on landscape rather than on architecture. Their connotations, in 
addition, prove to be fairly pejorative; they portray a negative vision of urban spaces. If 
they do not discourage us from dwelling on the subject, they at least portray a sort of 
nostalgic hopelessness towards contemporary urban transformations. Theoretician and 
landscape architect Christophe Girot is a strong advocate against this form of thinking. He 
states: 
 “In recent years there have been increasing efforts by artists and landscape architects to 
deal with these "new" landscapes and to point out the peculiar beauty, the special nature of 
these (non-) places by means of uncommitted, pure object-taking; artistic attempts at 
mediation are becoming increasingly accepted by the public.” 51 
Girot continues by arguing that the terrain vague should not simply be gazed upon 
in nostalgic longingness, as such an attitude is not fruitful for creating possible futures for 
such spaces and it counteracts efforts to maximize the terrain vague’s potential. These 
terms, in this perspective, do not provide a fresh outlook on the hidden promise of such 
spaces. 
Due to the associations with negative undertones of waste, ruin and nostalgia, this 
terminology does not show the integral image of the terrain vague, but rather only one 
side. It thus hinders opportunities to intervene in, experiment with and explore the 
potential hidden in the terrain vague. One could even add than such an understanding 
obstructs continuity and change, which are considered qualities rather than disadvantages 
in Solà-Morales’s terrain vague. 
Vacant   spaces   
A fifth branch is linked with images of ‘void’52: vacant space, dead zone, desolate 
space, abandoned spaces, and non-lieu. These terminologies have varied in meaning over 
the course of time as the place of the void in the city has also changed. This has affected 
the image of the city as an organism, for instance when the void was deemed part of the 
urban, or when it was viewed as part of the landscape. The history of theories on the void 
can be accordingly considered a prior timeline to the notion of terrain vague. Although 
the principal focus in this antecedent timeline was still on reintegrating the void within 
the urban project, the thought process and development does not fall far from the terrain 
                                                                                                                                                                        
51 Girot, Christophe. “Terrain Vague.” The Course of Landscape Architecture: A History of Our Designs on 
the Natural World, from Prehistory to the Present. London: Thames & Hudson, 2016. 282–304. 
52 Keeping in mind that ‘void’ in urban space does not imply that it is empty or physically devoid of content.

## S. 44

41 
 
vague discourse. It can be considered a precursor to the more contemporary inversion of 
urban perception through the terrain vague, i.e. thinking urban space beyond the 
limitations of urban planning and organizing. 
In fact, the notion of void in its broadest sense has been a matter of discussion 
since the 19th century and before. Architect Stéphanie Bender, in her doctoral thesis on 
the strategies of void53 at the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne, divides this 
timeline of void strategies into three stages. She considers the late 19th century54 to be the 
first period during which thinking emerged about the urban through the prism of the 
void. She then defines the phase of the early 20th century as including the main precursors 
to contemporary void strategies and the earliest examples in the Modern movement of 
such forms of thinking. Finally, she deems the phase starting from the mid-20th century as 
belonging to the pioneers of this inverted approach to the urban through the void rather 
than the full. The terrain vague’s history preceding Solà-Morales’s essay could be 
interpreted by such a timeline, since the notion was hitherto still not clearly defined. 
Firstly, the beginning of the 19th century witnessed waves of industrialization, 
rural exodus and the growth of cities. Concerns were expressed regarding the acceleration 
in urban development and the resulting hygiene problems in cities. Reflections on the 
status of the city triggered the first period of this ‘void timeline’. The notion of “urbanism” 
emerged from these concerns, formulated chiefly by Ildefons Cerdà in his treatise “Teoría 
General de la Urbanización”55 in 1867. According to Bender, from the earliest points of 
urbanism, pioneers and precursors of this approach through the void were inspired by 
methods that linked urban development closely with the landscape and infrastructure. 
The first phase of this timeline thus witnessed projects in which ideas of landscape were 
transposed onto urban projects and vice versa. Some cases can be denoted: the works of 
journalist Ebenezer Howard, architect-landscapist Frederick Law Olmsted, socio-urbanist 
Patrick Geddes and architect Camillo Sitte. Sitte for instance declared that the city is not 
only constituted of buildings, but also the interstitial between those buildings56, while 
Cerdà and Olmsted found the terrain vague within the urban environment; and Howard 
and Mata established urbanism within the landscape. The city was hence defined as a 
back and forth between landscape, infrastructure, the urban project, and the terrain 
vague. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
53 Bender, Stéphanie. “Le Vide. Nouvelles Stratégies Urbaines.” (PhD Thesis) EPFL, 2011. 19-63. 
54 Although these timelines are set out here as being sequential, they often overlap, and the time limits are 
therefore indicative. 
55 Cerdà, Ildefons. Teoría General de la Urbanización, y aplicación de sus principios y doctrinas a la reforma y 
ensanche de Barcelona. Madrid: Imprenta Española, 1867. 460-464. 
56 Sitte, Camillo. L’art de bâtir les villes: l’urbanisme selon ses fondements artistiques. Éditions du Seuil, 1996. 
92-93.

## S. 45

42 
The second phase, in the early 20th century, included projects on both built-up 
areas and terrains vagues. According to Bender, the intent was to connect both the 
landscape and the urban within a singular fabric, rather than transposing one onto the 
other. There was a stronger recognition of the importance of landscape and infrastructure 
as an essential part of the urban project among this period’s protagonists: architects Frank 
Lloyd Wright, Ludwig Hilberseimer, Ivan Leonidov, Bruno Taut and Mies van der Rohe. 
These figures often considered the terrain vague as a place of transition, a link between 
the urban and the landscape. Along these lines, Wright, for instance, created the 
“Broadacre City”, Hilberseimer the “Mid-West” and Taut the “Stadtkrone”, where nature 
structured the city and where cities were regarded as urban satellites within the 
landscape. As for Mies van der Rohe, he attempted to give importance within the urban 
plan to the infrastructure, landscape and ensuing terrains vagues. 
As for the third and final phase in this timeline, a new mode of intervening in the 
urban through its terrain vague found its precursors: mainly the architects Alison and 
Peter Smithson, Denise Scott Brown, Robert Venturi and Aldo Van Eyck. Again, 
according to Bender, in this phase, the void is not a by-product but rather the actual 
generator of the urban project, through a deeper understanding of the urban, the 
landscape and infrastructure. In fact, the end of the 1950s and the 1960s witnessed a 
critique of the CIAM’s concept of the functionalist city, its monotone urbanity and 
functionalist zoning. During the 1960s, the Modern project, with its reinvention of cities 
reduced to idealist visions and detached from the continuity of the urban and the 
landscape, was put into question. A new generation, Team 10, emerged to counterbalance 
the former: Peter and Alison Smithson, with their “charged voids”57, and van Eyck were 
the main protagonists. Projects were subsequently carried out with the terrain vague and 
the interstitial in the foreground. Van Eyck, for instance, reactivated 650 abandoned sites 
and voids within the city and transformed them into spaces reserved for children. 
Consequently, the evolution towards thinking the urban through its terrains 
vagues does not seem to follow an abrupt and brutal change in the discourse. It appears 
rather to be a slow development in understanding urbanism towards acknowledging the 
importance of the terrain vague as an integral and structuring element of the urban fabric. 
These ideas become even more relevant, as we will observe in what follows, when 
considering contemporary issues of urban sprawl, shrinking cities and disintegrating 
urban centers, where limits between landscape and city are increasingly blurred. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
57 Smithson, Alison Margaret, and Peter Smithson. The Charged Void: Architecture. Monacelli Press, 2001.

## S. 46

43 
 
Choosing   the   terrain   vague   notion   
Similar to Stéphanie Bender’s historical account of void strategies, there are 
several excellent general reviews on notions such as the “interstitial”, the “deadzone” or 
the “void”, by Luc Lévesque and Gil Doron; each reflects the author’s personal research 
interests and expertise. Of course, a truly comprehensive review is impossible, and 
certainly beyond the scope of this thesis. The preceding review of the various affiliated 
terminologies and notions briefly presents the properties inherent to each, but more 
importantly it reflects the characteristics inherent to the terrain vague notion. In 
conclusion, the different branches of the terminologies surrounding the terrain vague 
portray the variety of connotations and the magnitude that the discourse can embrace. 
Although these terms are erroneously used interchangeably, as we have begun to denote, 
differences between them lead to divergent processes of understanding and mediating the 
urban fabric. These mainly give clues as to how these notions affect the field of 
architecture and most importantly the urban experience of the terrain vague. They reveal 
many questions that will be revisited throughout this thesis from the perspective of the 
terrain vague and Solà-Morales’s essay on the notion. Furthermore, we are interested in 
this research in understanding how the discussion on terrains vagues developed between 
the 1990s and the present day, particularly in the fields of architecture and urbanism. 
For the purposes of this thesis, we have also chosen to focus on the terrain vague 
notion, among the spectrum of terms, because it appears on the one hand to lack hitherto 
the theoretical grounding that other terms have already received, and because it englobes 
on the other hand most of these inferences within its own discourse. The terrain vague 
notion seems evidently distinct from the aforesaid terminologies because of its capacity to 
encompass most of these connotations within its own definition. The different terms 
therefore show only one facet or another of the many angles the terrain vague notion 
contains. It proves even more interesting since it appears to portray the development of 
the discourse within its last phase into contemporary times, i.e. from the 1990s onward. A 
look at the contemporary situation as well as the future will be incorporated into our 
terrain vague timeline. 
Lastly, the terrain vague encompasses a great sense of potentiality and presents 
itself as a promising tool for both theoretical thought and professional architectural 
practice. This neutrality in approach and hidden potential makes the terrain vague notion 
attractive to tackle in its own right.

## S. 47

44 
Mentions of the term “terrain vague” before Solà-Morales 
Before delving into the contemporary uses of the terrain vague notion in 
architecture and urbanism, it is worth screening the history of the notion before Solà-
Morales’s essay appeared in the 1990s. Although the term was not yet theorized in 
architectural and urban terms, it was a commonly used expression in the French language 
since at least as early as the 18th century. 
Firstly, the earliest written mention of “terrain vague” in literature probably dates 
back to 1802 when the writer François-René de Chateaubriand wrote in relation to 
Christianity: 
“Most of the concessions made to the monasteries in the first centuries of the Church 
were terrains vagues, which the monks cultivated with their own hands. Wild forests, 
impassable marshes, and vast heaths were the source of those riches for which we have 
reproached the clergy so much.”58 
Secondly, the wider historical context of the terrain vague discourse in relation to 
urban transformations should probably begin with the French novelist Jean Richepin, 
who wrote a poem in 1876 entitled “Les Terrains Vagues”. His poem was a critique against 
the modernization of Paris under Napoleon III and the planning carried out by Georges-
Eugène Haussmann: 
“What do you care? With tired feet and weary legs, 
You feel no disgust at using as a mattress 
The basin into which the giant city vomits. 
A bed is always soft, even when it’s filthy.” 59 
In 19th-century Paris, Haussmann’s urban renewal plan produced desolate spaces 
on the peripheries of new developments and around railway stations. French flâneur 
Eugène Atget documented these wastelands between 1910 and 1913, whilst Jean-François 
Raffaëlli sketched and painted the suburban landscape60; one of his etchings is even 
entitled Terrain Vague. And Richepin described in his poem these terrains vagues as the 
peaceful refuge for the homeless barred from wandering the properly controlled 
boulevards and public parks of Haussmann’s plan. According to James Cannon in Paris 
Zone, Richepin received a one-month prison sentence and a large fine for ‘affront to 
                                                                                                                                                                        
58 Our trans. from French: «La plupart des concessions faites aux monastères dans les premiers siècles de 
l'Eglise étaient des terres vagues, que les moines cultivaient de leurs propres mains. Des forêts sauvages, des 
marais impraticables, de vastes landes furent la source de ces richesses que nous avons tant reprochées au 
clergé.» - Chateaubriand, François René. Génie Du Christianisme, Ou Beautés De La Religion Chrétienne. 
Migneret, 1802. 1054. 
59 Trans. from French: “Que t’importe? Les pieds fourbus, les membres las, - Tu ne sens nul dégoût d’avoir 
pour matelas - La cuvette où vomit la cité colossale. - Un lit est toujours doux, même quand il est sale.” 
- Richepin, Jean. “Les Terrains Vagues.” Chanson des Gueux  : gueux des champs  ; gueux de Paris  ; nous autres 
gueux. Paris: Maurice Dreyfous, 1881(orig.1876). 113. 
60 Alexandre, Arsène, and Jean François Raffaëlli. Jean-François Raffaëlli: peintre, graveur et sculpteur. H. 
Floury, 1909.

## S. 48

45 
 
public decency’ because of his “irreverent attitude towards hygiene.”61 The terrain vague 
was in this manner associated, even at these earliest stages, with resilient defiance to 
controlling and regulating urban forces. 
 
FIGURE 7: Raffaëlli, Jean-François. Terrain Vague. 18.7 × 21.7 cm, 1894. Color Etching. 
 
The term terrain vague was thus already commonly used before the 20th century62. 
It also appeared as the title of a Man Ray photograph63 as early as 1932, of which Solà-
Morales was probably aware as he even refers to the artist among other photographers in 
his "Terrain Vague" essay. The term terrain vague was recurrent in other instances within 
the arts and literature scene, but we will recount only the most prominent: the publishing 
house "Le Terrain Vague" founded in 1955 in Paris, which became a hub for intellectual 
                                                                                                                                                                        
61 Cannon, James. “The Emergence of the Zone as a Metaphor, 1870–1889.” The Paris Zone: A Cultural 
History, 1840-1944. Routledge, 2016. 43-46. 
62 However, it gained popularity from the 1990s in the architectural and urban fields thanks to Solà-Morales’s 
essay. 
63 Man Ray (1932) Terrain Vague. Scala (for MoMa in New York). Rayograph. – We will elaborate on this 
artwork in the third chapter.

## S. 49

46 
encounters, a novel by Jean Forton in 195164, an author who has been rediscovered in the 
last fifteen years, and the title of a film directed by Marcel Carné in 196065. One of the 
earliest written forms in which the terrain vague term appears could also be traced back 
to a 1902 literary fiction entitled Yasmina by Isabelle Eberhardt, in which the author 
describes a space situated between the gates of the city and the first traces of mountain 
slopes, and in which the main protagonist wanders in the dark, escaping her oppressive 
life66.

style et sans grâce de jour, dans la lumière magique de la lune, elle apparaissait diaphane et presque 
translucide, baignée d'un rayonnement imprécis."

## S. 50

47 
 
Context of the 1990s surrounding the "Terrain Vague" 
Having considered other references, contexts, connotations and preceding 
historical contexts, we focus in this section on the period and contexts surrounding the 
terrain vague notion during and since the 1990s. The aforementioned array of 
terminologies portrayed the various facets of the terrain vague and the noteworthy 
elements of interest that emerge from these contexts: interest in resistance to 
hegemonizing forces, interest in alternative spaces, in attention to intensities and forces of 
the city, in alternative interpretations focused on landscape and infrastructure, in 
adapting to new urban transformations and challenges, and finally an interest in placing 
the terrain vague in the globalized urban landscape. 
The different terms also portrayed various phases in the larger timeline of the 
subject. Accordingly, the period of the 1990s onward, relative to the terrain vague in 
particular, presents a new context following on from the previous phases of the subject, 
yet different from them. Contemporary urban challenges affect the terrain vague 
discourse; from the worsening urban sprawl to shrinking cities and the voiding of city 
centers, to the environmental crisis, the fragmentation of the urban fabric, theories of 
urban blight and the effect of globalization in the 21st century. 
Urban blight, for instance, is a contemporary concept contiguous to the terrain 
vague and usually associated with the context of American cities to describe the process of 
urban decay and areas falling into decrepitude. The term became mostly relevant in the 
1970s as authorities often used it to control urban development by designating one part of 
a city or another as "urban blight" and consequently gentrifying the region and imposing, 
often top-down, regeneration regulations. As landscape architect Alan Berger describes: 
"Cities are increasingly designating property as "blight" not because it exhibits the 
aforementioned conditions such as toxicity, but because the city views the property as 
unproductive (from a tax-revenue perspective or an aesthetic one). Each city and local 
government opportunistically defines blight according to its own needs for redevelopment. 
There is no single definition for the actual composition of blight. Hundreds of abusive 
eminent-domain cases are reported as the result of this redevelopment strategy."67 
In this respect, the terrain vague plays the counter-part to this negative 
designation of indeterminate spaces. 
Since the 1990s, the terrain vague concept has been a relevant topic to the subject 
of shrinking cities, i.e. cities, often post-industrial, that have suffered significant 
depopulation. Two events in this respect were significant at the beginning of the new 
millennium: the EU funded the "Shrinking Cities Project" between 2002-2008, directed by 
Philipp Oswalt, Klaus Overmeyer (the founders of Urban Catalyst in 2003) and the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
67 Berger, Alan. Drosscape Wasting Land in Urban America. New York: Princeton Arch. Press, 2006. 75.

## S. 51

48 
Federal Cultural Foundation in Berlin, and the founding of the International Center for 
Urban Ecology, ICUE, by the architect Kyong Park. These helped to put a spotlight on the 
consequences of deindustrialization in urban contexts. Those cities analyzed were also 
cities in which the notion of terrain vague often received a large audience: American 
cities such as Detroit, the German cities of Berlin, Dresden, Halle and Leipzig, post-Soviet 
cities in Russia, and Manchester and Liverpool in England68. As urban theorist Helen 
Armstrong writes: 
"The Shrinking Cities movement focuses on a new form of urban ecology, both people 
and systems, suggesting that ‘in the fault lines of the Modernist city, fresh and rebellious 
ideas and designs are emerging as an “architecture of resistance”’ (Park, 2005:176). Project 
Shrinking Cities actively encourages new ideas for temporary land-uses for urban 
wasteland."69 
Along similar lines, Armstrong also argues that the subject of terrain vague is 
equally relevant to growing cities, or sprawling cities, as she states: 
"The most regrettable loss for growing cities, however, is the loss of the voids. As 
recently as the mid 1990s, Ignasi de Sola-Morales called for those involved in urban design to 
respect existing urban voids or Terrains Vagues. While his eloquent arguments have been 
heeded in shrinking cities, in growing cities they have been ignored, unrelenting 
development occupying these spaces with the urban form of Late Capitalism."70 
Consequently, there seems to be an emerging sensitivity in the last few decades 
towards the urban environment focused on conditions of interstitial spaces and 
wastelands. It is not merely sensitivity to the physical presence and the physiognomy of a 
place; it encompasses a condition of space where representation, image, imagination and 
experience of urban space are reformulated away from form and towards an inverted 
focus on non-form. 
The notion of terrain vague could thus be considered as a point of intersection 
between various lines and a gateway for different contemporary urban discourses. The 
notion is moreover multinational and Solà-Morales developed theories with clear 
influences from both North American and European thought. In addition, the notion falls 
naturally between disciplines; it is present in the sciences as an intervention method, in 
photography within the “New Topographics” movement since the 1970s, and in urban 
and architectural discussions since Solà-Morales’s essay. An interdisciplinary approach is 
an important prerequisite for tackling the phenomenon of terrain vague and 
understanding the entire significance behind its various manifestations. In fact, it is a 
prerequisite greatly supported by the description and definition given by Solà-Morales. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
68 Oswalt, Philipp, and Kulturstiftung des Bundes, eds. Shrinking Cities. Ostfildern-Ruit [Germany]  : New 
York: Hatje Cantz; Distributed by D.A.P./Distributed Art Publishers, 2005. 
69 Armstrong, Helen. “Time, Dereliction and Beauty: An Argument for ‘Landscapes of Contempt.’” IFLA 
Eastern Region Conference Proceeding Book (2006): 125. 
70 Ibid. 116.

## S. 52

49 
 
 
FIGURE 9: Urban Peripheries, Where the City Lost Its Name - Barcelona 1947-1985. MUHBA exhibition, 
2013. Photographs. 
In this respect, the photographic context of the 1970s until now is an important 
precedent to the terrain vague, as we will elaborate in the third chapter. William Jenkins 
coined the term "New Topographics" in 1975 in order to describe the work of eight 
American photographers, including Robert Adams, Nicholas Nixon, Joe Deal and Lewis 
Baltz, and two Germans, Bernd and Hilla Becher. In October 1975, he curated the 
exhibition "New Topographics: Photographs of a Man-Altered Landscape” at the 
International Museum of Photography at the George Eastman House in New York, which 
greatly altered the practice of landscape photography. It signaled an important shift from 
romanticized depictions of nature and landscapes towards an attempt at objective 
documentation. The terrains vagues, as we will observe in the third chapter, were among 
the most common subjects of this innovative form of photography. As Davide Deriu and 
Krystallia Kamvasinou state: 
"Focused on the sprawling commercial and residential development that increasingly 
characterized the edges of American cities, the work in the exhibition dissolved ‘the integrity 
of landscape as a distinct experience, setting in its place a liminal place of exchange between 
nature and urbanity.’ From the late 1960s onwards, photographers in Western Europe had 
posed similar questions about the necessary relation between landscape representation and 
external nature."71 
The exhibition was a turning point towards a new approach in photography and 
landscape which since then has been named as 'new topographic' photography. Moreover, 
in an interview between the author and architect Suzanne Strum, a former student of 
Solà-Morales and current co-director of the Metropolis Master Program in Barcelona, the 
subject of the New Topographics kept recurring in relation to the terrain vague notion. As 
she describes: 
"The images [in the exhibition] were linked to sensibilities of land art. […] This 
reemergence of the topographic tradition is fully evident in Robert Smithson’s ironic tour de 
force of picturesque travel genres, “ A Tour of the Monuments of Passaic” (1967) a record of a 
                                                                                                                                                                        
71 Deriu, Davide, and Krystallia Kamvasinou. “Picturing Global Change.” Emerging Landscapes: Between 
Production and Representation. Routledge, 2016. 11–12.

## S. 53

50 
voyage to the entropic landscape of his home town in New Jersey as “memory traces of an 
abandoned set of futures.” […] Featured in the “New Topographics” exhibition, the critical 
eye of Robert Adams, also a New Jersey native but resident of the American west, revealed 
landscape as real estate, just another consumer product, to be cut into subdivisions."72 
One can thus even associate some of the artistic productions of land art of the 
1960s and 1970s with the New Topographics movement that took place around the same 
time. And, in turn, both can be associated with the terrain vague notion that became 
more pertinent a few decades later. In fact, a new imagery resurfaced in the 1990s in line 
with the new topography codes. Large format antiheroic photographs of Humberto Rivas 
and Manolo Laguillo documented the urban developments of Barcelona, as well as others 
in the photographic documentation of the city in the journal of Quaderns. As Jorge 
Ribalta writes: 
"The Quaderns survey adopted the stylistic codes of the new topographics, and was 
based on analysis of the geographic conditions of the metropolitan areas, establishing a 
methodological strategy of the division of territory and distribution of the commissions to the 
photographers according to that geographic division. Thus, John Davies was responsible for 
the Llobregat area; Gabriele Basilico for the seafront; Joan Fontcuberta for Ciutat Vella; 
Ferran Freixa for the Eixample; Ana Muller for the foothills; Manolo Laguillo for the 
mountain strip; and Gilbert Fastenaekens for the Besòs area."73 
As a result, Solà-Morales wrote his "Terrain Vague" essay in a period during which 
a heightened awareness of a new aesthetic perspective on the urban surrounding was 
gaining momentum and when new emerging urban challenges were compelling architects 
and urbanists to find new forms of comprehending and mediating the globalized urban 
fabric. 
 
FIGURE 10: Mir, Joaquim. La Catedral dels Pobres / The Cathedral of the Poor. Barcelona, 1989. Oil on 
Canvas. Exhibition in MNAC, Barcelona, 2013. - In 1989, Antoni Gaudi’s Sagrada Familia stood in the middle of 
a terrain vague. Popularly known as the “Cathedral of the poor”, Mir chose to portray the people begging near 
the scaffolding. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
72 Strum, Suzanne. “Pencil of Nature/Mirror of Culture: Photography in the Topographic Tradition.” SCE 
Journal - Scapes 5 (2006): 22–26. 
73 Ribalta, Jorge. “The Photographic Construction of Barcelona.” Universal Archive. The Condition of the 
Document and the Modern Photographic Utopia. Barcelona: MACBA, 2008. 83–86.

## S. 54

51 
 
Conditions   behind   the   creation   of   wastelands   
In a similar attempt to situate the terrain vague, it is worth mentioning which 
circumstances lead to the creation of wastelands in the city. More often than not, these 
conditions are related to limits dissecting the urban fabric. One can make a list of the 
different types of such limits: Building shells, brown fields, differences in urban scale, 
industry borderlines, urban planning mishaps, demarcation lines and transport lines. Each 
of these limits ends up stimulating different interventions and projects, aside from other 
influencing factors that contribute to this undertaking. 
The first limit is the most common one, usually seen in dense urban systems. 
Whether city or suburbs, building codes specify plots and areas in which one can 
construct under strict rules and within borderlines. The outcome is surplus space around 
these that is left untouched. From barren fields and economic blanks, to demolished lots 
and desolate buildings, the examples could be numerous, but the reasons are the same: 
disorganized urban planning or simply the work of time on the city. It is important to 
take into consideration the context of such spaces if a program needs to be initiated. A 
second limit is related to scale as a caliber of difference. As the city grows, dimensions 
tend to change and become diverse. Taking Beirut as an example, the lack of urban 
control has created a proliferation of towers that have accumulated in the city and 
gradually replaced the traditional small brick-roofed houses. The result is the creation of 
smaller empty cavities devoid of ‘breathing space’ where it is almost impossible to build in 
a humane way. Such spaces end up forgotten, even hidden. The occupation of such spaces 
becomes ephemeral and mobile. On a different level, the fall of industry has produced 
major changes in cities that in turn have produced forgotten wastelands from desolate 
industrial sites. Such spaces have a certain air of rust and steel that seems to appeal to 
artists and architects; the outcome is often an adoption of such sites for artistic, ‘greening’, 
or ‘fill-in’ purposes. 
On another note, the city of New York includes many vague spaces due to the 
superimposition of the old Manhattan plan that goes diagonally against the newer 
rectilinear grid of the city. The intersection of the old and new plans forms triangular 
interstices often occupied by unofficial illegal street sellers and artists74. This is once again 
a form of urban limitation, this time due to massive planning decisions. Such limits are 
sometimes complex to recognize and understand as they fuse into the city. On a different 
scale, socio-political conflicts can also be major catalysts of borderlines within the city 
and around them, ranging from demarcation lines to historical gates. One only needs to 
contemplate the ‘no man’s land’ created by the Berlin Wall to understand how frequently 
such types of limits produce wastelands, often dictating their future functions. Finally, 
                                                                                                                                                                        
74 Bedou, Dominique. Chimères. Paris: ERES, 2003. 134-142.

## S. 55

52 
desolate transport systems present a similar type of limit. Once again, the presence of such 
limits is crucial for future interventions in surrounding indeterminate spaces. 
Therefore, through studies of their contexts, the limits75 within which they are 
situated, the historical reasons behind their creation, and the interventions implemented 
on them, terrains vagues form an alternative reading of the city’s narration. They become 
fragments of history written on urban fabric. 
 
FIGURE 11: Border Tower Comes Toppling Down. Montreal Daily News. 13 December, 1989. Newspaper 
article. - CCA Archives, Vertical Files: Berlin. 
 
FIGURE 12: Kinzer, Stephen. Retrieve the Lurid Past? (Some Germans Recoil). The New York Times. 19 
February, 1992. Newspaper article. - CCA Archives, Vertical Files: Berlin. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
75 Evidently, we cannot be exhaustive in defining the various causes, yet those mentioned are the most 
prominent examples of conditions and limits behind the creation of terrains vagues in the contemporary city.

## S. 56

53 
 
 
FIGURE 13: Koring, Paul. Wasteland: City Struggles to Get Over the Wall. The Globe and Mail. 30 May, 1992. 
Newspaper article. - CCA Archives, Vertical Files: Berlin. 
 
FIGURE 14: Vey, P.C.. Beyond Words, Recollections of 1989. The New York Times. 31 December, 1989. 
Newspaper vignette. - CCA Archives, Vertical Files: Berlin.

## S. 57

54 
Economic, political and sociological contexts 
“The problems of this postmodern organization in the landscape became obvious in 
the 1990s with the proliferation of sprawling cities, gated enclaves, residential communities, 
megamalls, and theme parks.”   76                           
Grahame   Shane,   “The   Emergence   of   ‘Landscape   Urbanism’” 
 
Today’s cities are growing throughout the world at a dizzying pace. In developing 
countries, this expansion is accompanied by infrastructure and urban development 
problems, while in Europe the shifts in demographics often leave behind desolate urban 
centers and ill-managed peripheries. Facing these challenges, architects since the 1990s 
have begun to reevaluate their role in these changes and question the breadth of their 
capabilities, which has changed since the last century. In fact, at a time when urban 
transformations developed at a slower pace, the role of architecture was better defined 
and gauged in order to guide these transformations. At the current speed, architects are 
confronted with decisions made by other professionals, whether real estate forces for 
economic reasons, engineers for technical reasons or politicians due to power relations. 
The outcome is that both architectural and urban scales are hindered: urban sprawl is far 
from resolved and, more importantly for this thesis, indefinite landscapes are left 
underappreciated. Whether through partial projects or comprehensive planning, urban 
development needs to find alternatives to adapt to the transforming field. Urban planning 
thus mainly faces challenges related to a discipline’s reevaluation and a hindrance to the 
scope of action due to political, economic and technical pressures. 
When the “Terrain Vague” essay was first published in the mid-1990s, the context 
of the period and the particular development of certain cities allowed for such themes to 
develop and take the foreground in architectural and urban theory. The wastelands of the 
1990s were principally large urban territories that resulted from massive, often 
traumatizing, events which altered both the center and the peripheries of cities. These 
were the problems that started to emerge in the mid-1990s, and from which alternative 
topics such as the terrain vague were beginning to materialize. Coincidently or not, cities 
in which Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay was first presented, published or celebrated 
came under these transformations: chiefly Barcelona, Berlin, Montreal, Milan, and 
Detroit. 
The Olympic Games, for instance, in Barcelona in 1992 accelerated the city’s 
transformations. The focus of new urban plans towards hosting the games was on bringing 
value to the periphery, as well as regenerating the center. The terrains vagues on the 
peripheries were hence among the main topics of urban discussion. Barcelona had already 
witnessed an interest in the city-landscape-countryside opposition with Ildefons Cerdà. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
76 Shane, David Grahame. “The Emergence of ‘Landscape Urbanism’ - Reflections on Stalking Detroit.” 
Harvard Design Magazine Fall2003/ Winter2004: 1–8.

## S. 58

55 
 
The engineer-architect was the first to introduce ideas on the potential of the void and 
the indeterminate through his treatise in 1867 and through his design of the urban 
expansion plan of Barcelona in 1859 where each urban block was conceived with an 
empty space in its center where opportunities for landscape and terrain vague were 
created. Interviews conducted between the author and Spanish intellectuals, such as 
Ernest Ferré, Maurici Pla and Jordi Oliveras, uncover how Cerdà’s plan is intimately 
linked in the minds of locals with the notion of terrain vague. 
 
FIGURE 15: PobleNou Barcelona 2004. MUHBA exhibition (Museu d’Història de Barcelona), 2014. Model. - 
Fragment of a model-map of Barcelona in 2004, showing the terrains vagues of the Cerdà plan and their 
transformations at the opening of via Diagonal and the transformations of PobleNou Area. 
On a similar note, during the same period, Berlin also witnessed massive 
transformations after 1989 in its post-wall phase. The terrain vague was once again at the 
core of many urban discussions. Wim Wenders’ film Wings of Desire released in 1987 
portrays the magnitude of the terrains vagues around the wall just before its fall. The 
poetic imagery is powerful in foreseeing the potential that such spaces crossing the urban 
heart would bring to the city from the 1990s until the present time. Such films and 
photographs about the terrains vagues of Berlin remained strong in the collective

## S. 59

56 
imagination of the period77. Solà-Morales presented his essay there on several occasions in 
the mid-1990s, which reverberated with the city’s transformations. The same could be 
said of the powerful images of a 1990s desolate Detroit in crisis. The post-industrial 
history of the city generated massive terrains vagues in its center and brought 
international attention to its development. Photography then was also a medium to 
propagate these powerful urban impressions and incite discussion. A similar progression 
was witnessed in other post-industrial cities too, such as Milano and Montreal, and this 
went hand in hand with the rising importance of their terrains vagues. 
Among these urban and theoretical transformations, Solà-Morales’s “Terrain 
Vague” essay appeared both as a synthesist of these city developments and a catalyst of 
interventions and interpretations. It helped with coming to terms with these 
developments. As Helen Armstrong states: 
 “Since the early 1990s, designers have been exhorted to understand and respect the 
qualities of such left over spaces. […] In Europe sensitivity to terrains vagues continues.”78 
 
Indefinite urban spaces can also be considered as an outcome of the diversity of 
limits and fragments in the contemporary city. In this regard, architect Carlos Leite 
underlined the abandoned and wedged areas in cities, too, to describe problems of urban 
fragmentation: 
 “A discussion of some urban projects work on these new challenges presented on the 
contemporary city. These urban projects work on the bordering conditions of the fragmented 
metropolitan territory: urban voids, wastelands, brownfields, terrain vague.”79 
This contemporary interest in ‘fragments’ seems to be rooted in the period in 
which we are interested, i.e. the final decades of the 20th century. The development of 
the notion of terrain vague is pertinent to our contemporary worldview because it defines 
a shift in perception from the element to the fragment and from the momentous to what 
is in-between. It sets out a new paradigm for understanding these ‘in between’ spaces of 
the 21st-century urban context. New layers within the urban fabric start to emerge, which 
were vaguely described in the previous century and which have repercussions today on 
how we perceive and intervene in the contemporary city and its formal and informal 
contexts. 
Hence, since the 1980s, spaces on the periphery have been of increasing interest in 
urban discussion, since new cities grow continually into these peripheral areas, especially 
                                                                                                                                                                        
77 These photographs even found their way into the “Terrain Vague” essay, as we will observe in the third 
chapter. 
78 Armstrong, Helen. “Time, Dereliction and Beauty: An Argument for ‘Landscapes of Contempt.’” IFLA 
Eastern Region Conference Proceeding Book (2006). 116–127. 
79 Leite, Carlos. “Economic Modeling Techniques Applied to Regional and Urban Issues, ECOMOD´2006 
International Conference on Regional and Urban Modeling.” Conference Paper. Free University of Brussels, 
2006. 4-5.

## S. 60

57 
 
with globalization and an international economic crisis lurking on the political and 
capitalist agenda. The wasteland inevitably becomes a symbol of hope and positive 
change. Slowly, in the new millennium, the scale of the discussion has become massive 
and globalized. Urban planner Oliver Gillham writes, for instance, about the sprawling 
city80 and its peripheries. In this respect, terrains vagues are not only indeterminate spaces 
within the city; they can also be considered a resource for alternative forms of expansion. 
The subject took on a wider, more crucial role in the contemporary urban debates of the 
1990s and 2000s, with discussions on shrinking and expanding cities growing in mass 
media. 
Relevant   environmental   apprehensions   
According to Christophe Girot, the terrain vague became a focal subject for 
landscape architects following the first signs of environmental degradation observed by 
Georg Aemi81. Environmental problems in the last forty years have become more alarming 
and more global than ever. Be it climate change, global warming, or environmental 
dilapidation, understanding our world’s limits has created a significant change in the way 
one perceives nature. As a consequence, Girot presents two possible attitudes towards 
nature and landscape architecture: one that reaches into the realms of nostalgia and 
romantic abandonment, and one that seeks adaptation and a regenerative approach to the 
landscape. Although the terrain vague could be perceived from both perspectives, Girot 
advocates that the former bears few solutions for the future while the latter, although far 
more challenging, may open up directions for future exploration. 
According to the landscape architect, the first attitude offers a comfortable preestablished way of thinking, linked with notions of ruins and decay, and the aesthetic 
pleasure these afford, calling it “laisser faire nostalgia.” In this mindset, the terrain vague 
is a reflection of a self-loathing, depraved society and therefore falls short in providing 
opportunities for the future. Girot states: 
 “Terrain vague is a place of modern brutality and environmental irresponsibility, but 
one also where the truth and consequence of such abandonment can be found […] 
particularly with respect to large-scale landscape designs spanning over extended periods of 
time”82   
What is still missing, he suggests, is an exploration of the terrain vague in the 
other direction, where opportunities are still uncharted. From a similar viewpoint, Solà-
Morales’s terrain vague belongs to these changing attitudes towards environmental 
                                                                                                                                                                        
80 Gillham, Oliver. The Limitless City: A Primer on the Urban Sprawl Debate. Island Press, 2002. 
81 Girot, Christophe. “Terrain Vague.” The Course of Landscape Architecture: A History of Our Designs on 
the Natural World, from Prehistory to the Present. London: Thames & Hudson, 2016. 282–304. 
82 Girot, Ibid.

## S. 61

58 
problems, trying to seek new forms of dealing with different challenges and transcending 
our predetermined approaches to architecture, landscape and urbanism. Whether looking 
towards the past or facing future problems, the terrain vague seems to reflect these 
environmental realities in mutation. 
Waves of resistance, social engagement and experimentation 
Aside from the array of terminologies linked to the terrain vague, Solà-Morales’s 
discourse on the subject can be encompassed in a wave of resistance witnessed mainly in 
the architectural and urban fields. As we began to notice with the aforementioned 
precursors, undertones of resistance and escape are important for describing how the 
terrain vague is embedded within a historical and cultural contextualization of the 
tradition of wandering and opposition. This is pertinent when trying to identify the forms 
the notion has adopted in contemporary society (and will most probably be reflected in 
the future). 
Connotations   of   “vague”   
Since the beginning of the 20th century, many theoreticians and practitioners have 
contested the homogenizing planning forces of the city and their overarching utopian 
visions. They have favored instead standpoints where the mundane, general happenings 
and improvised encounters are celebrated. Accordingly, fueled by philosophy and theory, 
architecture activist movements have arisen challenging the established discipline. 
These movements of resistance are linked to connotations of “vague”, i.e. 
vagueness, vagaries and wandering. They are often associated with viewing and 
understanding the city through experience and intensities, rather than organized built 
forms and urban grids. This mode of perceiving the city through wandering and firsthand 
experience finds its origins decades before Solà-Morales’s essay, from Dadaists and 
Surrealists, to the Situationist International and more recently in Peter Handke’s work 
and a movement in urban and architectural thought that finds its theoretical origins in 
the 1950s. 
Firstly, in the artistic fields, the act of wandering as anti-art is visible in the Dada 
movement from 1921,which organized outings to what were labeled as the “ordinary 
places” of Paris. These excursions, or déambulations, were artistic statements that rejected 
the institutionalized places of art and sought new artistic frontiers within the city spaces. 
Similarly, a few years later (in the 1930s), Walter Benjamin began to embark on his urban 
explorations, or flâneries. Without a given purpose or target, and with a fragmented city

## S. 62

59 
 
image in mind, his approach to the urban was to inspire, a few decades later, not only 
Solà-Morales’s urban concepts and his approach to the city, but also his style of writing, 
which was fragmented and eternally open-ended. 
Secondly, Solà-Morales’s theories, including his approach to the terrain vague, 
were likewise close to pioneers of contemporary art83, especially the Surrealists of the 
1930s. Then, between 1957 and 1972, theories on wandering were to take another course 
with the Situationist International. 
Following   Situationist   methods   
The Situationist International was an artistic and political collective, an 
interdisciplinary organization that was initiated in 1957 and dissolved in 1972. It united a 
multitude of international figures including politicians, artists and theoreticians with the 
shared purpose of socially revolutionizing the then status quo of urbanism. Over the 
course of fifteen years, Situationists formulated a theoretical critique and practice of 
everyday life, which translated in spatial terms into a critique of isolation and constraint. 
The Situationists adopted Marxist concepts, asserting that the misery of contemporary 
society comes from social alienation and consumerist fetishism. They were involved in 
analyzing and creating transitory micro-environments focused on the elements of play, 
strolling and accidental encounters. Their urban strategies consisted mainly of direct 
intervention methods: psychogeography, spectacle, détournement, situations, dérive and 
unitary urbanism. Behind all of these strategies, the aim was to allow citizens to inhabit 
and dwell in space freely. Their struggle was against the alienating practices of the 
functionalist urbanism of Modernism and the consumerism of capitalist society. 
Situationists used both theoretical publications as well as artistic forms to present their 
ideas, including journals, graphical documents, articles, topographic images, pamphlets, 
models, reused maps and photographs. 
The psychogeographic concepts started in the 1950s with the Letterist 
International and then the Situationists proposed, among other endeavors, new 
intervention and experimentation practices on ephemeral urban spaces. They considered 
that there is potential for innovation when one creates a state of indeterminacy, through 
exchanging the regulated for the pervasive. These practices were interdisciplinary as they 
involved aesthetic, political and cultural layers. The proclivity in Solà-Morales’s terrain 
vague is similar to this approach since it permeates a potential for innovation solely by 
existing. In this respect, the mere presence of terrains vagues within the urban fabric, 
when made conscious and visible, challenges the regulated strata of the city with the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
83 Moreover, the photographs and photographers mentioned in Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague” essay are also 
linked with this form of resistance, as we will examine in the third chapter.

## S. 63

60 
indefinite and the pervasive; a state of indeterminacy, promoted by the Situationists, is 
thus inevitably created. 
The Situationists took inspiration from the early 20th century avant-garde 
movements, notably Surrealism and Dadaism, especially in their habits of wandering the 
streets of Paris aimlessly. The Situationists’ techniques also seemed to be a return to a 
surrealist approach of celebrating the quotidian over the aesthetic, especially evident in 
their readymades. In the context of the terrain vague, one "urban" readymade in 
particular is worth mentioning: the "Readymade City"84 in Saint-Julien-le-Pauvre, which 
the Surrealists visited in 1921 and which was "the first operation that assigns a symbolic 
aesthetic value to a space instead of an object."85 As Rui Mendes asserts: 
"It is not clear who proposed that place among artists Dada – “an abandoned church, 
little known and poorly, at that time surrounded by a kind of Terrain Vague contained by 
one fence.” That little garden that surrounded the church was chosen precisely because it 
seemed an abandoned garden: a place to inquire for being familiar, although unknown, so 
little frequented as obvious a space banal and useless, that like many others, would not have 
really any reason to exist.”86 
This readymade ties together the works of Dadaism, the Situationists’ intentions 
and finally Solà-Morales's terrain vague, through an attentiveness to the forgotten faces of 
the city, the banal, the hidden, the everyday, the non-productive, and the indeterminate, 
all joined as a form of resistance, through continuity and subversion of the everyday, 
against the capitalist and the regulatory systems of the city. 
One could argue that the terrain vague notion was already subtly embedded in the 
ideas of the Situationist International from its outset. As architect Francesco Careri 
describes, spaces that interested the collective were "places forgotten by the dominant 
culture, off the map of the tourist itinerary."87 Although these spaces were not primarily 
only terrains vagues, the latent interest was in rediscovering the city and its suppressed 
and hidden spaces "often far from the gaze of the society."88 In fact, some of the news in 
Potlatch, the information bulletin of the Letterist International, included reports about 
the destruction or the status quo of terrains vagues. For instance, Potlatch n.7 included an 
article about the destruction of La Rue Sauvage and its wastelands: 
“(La Rue Sauvage) located between the railway tracks of the station of Austerlitz and a 
district of terrains vagues on the banks of the Seine, is - since last winter - framed by a few of 
those debilitating constructions that get lined up in our suburbs to house the sad people. We 
deplore the disappearance of an artery that is little known, and yet more alive than the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
84 Careri, Francesco. Walkscapes: Walking as an Aesthetic Practice. Editorial Gustavo Gili, S.L., 2002. 75. 
85 Ibid. 76. 
86 Mendes, Rui. “Shedding Light on the Still-Not-Happened: Dérive, Terrain Vague, Áreas de Impunidad.” 
Rethinking Urban Inclusion: Spaces, Mobilizations, Interventions. Coimbra, Portugal: CES Contexto Debates, 
2012. 527–530. 
87 Careri, Francesco. Walkscapes: Walking as an Aesthetic Practice. Editorial Gustavo Gili, S.L., 2002. 187. 
88 Ibid. 189.

## S. 64

61 
 
Champs-Elysees and its lights. [...] The civil barracks that rise in their place have a free 
ugliness that calls for the blasters.”89 
Likewise, Potlatch n.16 included an article by Michèle Bernstein describing the 
terrain vague and beckoning people to benefit from such spaces: 
“However, the square of Missions Étrangères is isolated from the street by a strange 
terrain vague that is separated from the square itself by very thick hedges. In this vague 
square, closed on all sides, and whose only use seems to be the creation of a distance between 
the square and the passers-by of the Rue du Bac, stands a 2-meter high bust of 
Chateaubriand90, in the form of the god Terme, overlooking soil of clinker. The Missions 
Étrangères Square can be used to welcome friends from afar, to be stormed at night, and for 
various other psychogeographic purposes." 91 
Through such ideas, the Situationists jump-started discussion on urban activism92 
and a dialogue on how to defy and transgress elitist architecture and the 
“commercialization of popular culture”93 which prevailed at the time. 
The sense of potential inherent to the terrain vague was then similarly inferred by 
the Situationist International. As Gil Doron describes: 
"As non-utilitarian spaces, they opposed the capitalist society and even more so the 
architectural profession […] The Situationists reproduced such spaces by cutting out parts in 
the Naked City map. [...] They cut out from the map the places they had been drifting 
through; they erased their own traces; they sabotaged the cartographic project and the 
production of knowledge."94 
This approach of erasure, escape and freeing up space is prototypical of the 
Situationist International, and thus similar to Solà-Morales's view of the terrains vagues as 
spaces of resistance. As architect Emma Fraser explains: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
89 Our trans. from French: “placée entre les voies ferrées de la gare d’Austerlitz et un quartier de terrains vagues 
au bord de la Seine, est – depuis l’hiver dernier – encadrée de quelques-unes de ces constructions débilitantes que 
l’on aligne dans nos banlieues pour loger les gens tristes. Nous déplorons la disparition d’une artère peu connue, et 
cependant plus vivante que les Champs-Élysées et leurs lumières. [...] Les casernes civiles qui s’élèvent à leur place 
ont une laideur gratuite qui appelle les dynamiteurs.” - International, Situationist. “Potlatch n.7 (3 August 
1954).” Potlatch (1954-1957). Paris: Allia, 1996. 33. 
90 It is curious to point out that the bust depicts Chateaubriand, probably the first writer who ever mentioned 
the terrain vague in a literary text. - Chateaubriand, François René. Génie Du Christianisme, Ou Beautés De 
La Religion Chrétienne. Migneret, 1802. 1054. 
91 Our trans. from French: “Toutefois le square des Missions Étrangères se trouve isolé de cette rue par un curieux 
terrain vague que des haies très épaisses séparent du square proprement dit. Dans ce square vague, fermé de toutes 
parts, et dont le seul emploi semble être de créer une distance entre le square et les passants de la rue du Bac, 
s’élève à deux mètres un buste de Chateaubriand en forme de dieu Terme, dominant un sol de mâchefer. Le square 
des Missions Étrangères peut servir à recevoir des amis venant de loin, à être pris d’assaut la nuit, et à diverses 
autres fins psychogéographiques.” - International, Situationist. “Potlatch n.16 (26 January 1955).” Potlatch 
(1954-1957). Paris: Allia, 1996. 65. 
92 These activist practices are also reminiscent of Heidegger’s notions of poetic dwelling and the lived 
experience and imagination of space through practical engagement. 
93 Dehaene, Michiel, and Lieven de Cauter. “Heterotopia in a Postcivil Society.” Heterotopia and the City: 
Public Space in a Postcivil Society. London; New York: Routledge, 2008. 3–11. 
94 Doron, Gil. “‘...those Marvelous Empty Zones at the Edge of Cities’: Heterotopia and the ‘Dead Zone.’” 
Heterotopia and the City: Public Space in a Postcivil Society. London; New York: Routledge, 2008. 203–215.

## S. 65

62 
"the Situationist International sought to counter hegemonic control of urban space, 
which reduces our capacity to experience diversity in the built environment of the city."95 
Situationists can thus be perceived as precursors to Solà-Morales in understanding 
wandering as a form of resistance. Their project Paris Peasant is one such example of 
exploring the terrain vague (Aragon 1994) The Situationists' "revolution of everyday life"96 
could, moreover, be traced back to the flâneries in Charles Baudelaire's poems and in 
Walter Benjamin's walks in the Parisian arcades. 
On a similar note, Guy Debord was one of the main figures of the Situationist 
International connected with ideas on wanderings and wastelands. He was a theorist, 
writer and filmmaker, and the original founder of the Letterist International (which ran 
between 1952 and 1956) and subsequently the Situationist International in 1957. He 
edited the magazines and bulletins of both the Situationist International and the Letterist 
International; one of these was Potlatch. In 1956, Debord also published his theory of 
Dérive97, a method of drifting aimlessly in urban spaces, which can be associated with the 
terrain vague’s connotations of wandering. It was initially a feature of the Letterist 
International before it became a tenet of the Situationist International. It is a technique of 
drifting through urban spaces, merged with a tendency towards playful-constructive 
behavior and psychogeographical effects. 
More interestingly, Debord's first film entitled Hurlement en Faveur de Sade98 
predicts in 1952 already the collective’s interest in the fragmentary experience of the city 
and its loose spaces and terrains vagues. The entire film could be compared to a long 
epileptic episode. There are many missing images, a succession of white, pieces of 
dialogue, and alternate black and white projections; the sequence of frames in the film is 
repetitively interrupted. The void, the terrain vague, is always present. Debord's Film, as 
well 
as 
his 
psychogeographical 
maps 
in 
1957, 
"Naked 
City" 
and 
"Guide 
psychogéographique Paris", accentuate a fragmented structure that increases “the 
discontinuity characteristic of a productive social geography.”99 These techniques hence seem to 
be Debord's form of resistance. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
95 Fraser, Emma. “Interrupting Progress: Ruins, Rubble and Catastrophe in Walter Benjamin’s History.” 
Master of Arts in Humanities and Social Sciences. University of Technology, 2012. 43. 
96 Sadler, Simon. The Situationist City. Cambridge, Mass.;London: MIT Press, 1999. 161. 
97 It was published again in 1958 in the second edition of the Situationist International journal. 
98 Debord, Guy. Hurlements En Faveur de Sade (Howlings in Favor of De Sade). Films Letterists, 1952. 
99 Levin, Thomas. “Geopolitics of Hibernation: The Drift of Situationist Urbanism.” Situacionistas: Arte, 
Politica, Urbanismo = Situationists  : Art, Politics, Urbanism. Barcelona: Actar, 1996. 118.

## S. 66

63 
 
 
FIGURE 16: Poster for the Internationale Situationniste. Section Française de l’IS, 1958. Poster. 
By the same token, cross-movements and blurring between different disciplines, 
seen in Debord’s projects but also in the Situationists’ works in general, contributed to a 
new sense of experimentalism and resistance, and the development of a counter-urban 
theory which the Situationists named “Urbanisme Unitaire” 100 (Unitary Urbanism), a 
unifying tendency to join together various fields under the same objective of finding 
alternative modes for living and communicating the urban. Francesco Careri argues that 
the Situationists attempted through this form of urbanism to create a "unifying 
interdisciplinary approach"101 He writes: 
"Many artists have attempted not to fall into the abyss of negation consciously opened 
by Dada but to leap beyond it. […] the Situationists, starting again from Dada, attempted to 
transform anti-art into a unified discipline (urbanisme unitaire) through the expansion of the 
field toward politics."102 
Along these lines, the disciplinary limits of urbanism, art and architecture were 
increasingly blurred and other disciplines were also introduced into the equation: 
geography, politics and psychology took on a vital role in living, imagining and recording 
the city. 
The expression "Unity Urbanism" actually dates back even earlier, to 1953, and 
was first used at the end of 1956 in a short treatise circulated in Turin by Italian 
                                                                                                                                                                        
100 Brun, Éric, ed. Les Situationnistes: Une Avant-Garde Totale (1950-1972). Paris: CNRS Éditions, 2014. 288. 
101 Ibid. 126. 
102 Careri, Francesco. Walkscapes: Walking as an Aesthetic Practice. Editorial Gustavo Gili, S.L., 2002. 25-26.

## S. 67

64 
Situationists entitled "Manifestate a Favore Dell'Urbanismo Unitario"103. Around the same 
time, a key text written by Gilles Ivain (otherwise known as Ivan Chtcheglov) entitled 
"Unitary Urbanism and the Critique of Functionalism" was published in the first 
periodical of the Situationist International in 1958. In this text, according to Hilde 
Heynen, the author decried boredom and utilitarian urbanism and opposed it to "strange 
images of symbolically charged urban scenes and magic sites where, he argued, 
imagination is stimulated."104 The terrain vague can be considered a kind of “magic site”, 
antagonistic towards functional urbanism. Moreover, in 1958, for the third Situationist 
International Conference, Debord and artist Constant Nieuwenhuys 105 wrote the 
"Amsterdam Declaration" in which they defined Unitary Urbanism as "the complex, 
ongoing activity, which consciously recreates man’s environment according to the most 
advanced conception in every domain."106 In this respect, Mark Wigley states how this 
new form of urbanism is temporal and non-static: 
"it is the activation of the temporary, the emergent and the transitory, the changeable, 
the variable, the immediately fulfilling and satisfying."107 
Whether this unitary urbanism was ever achieved is debatable, yet the limits of 
each discipline were pushed or at least challenged by this wave of thought. It was also in 
this direction that the Situationists implemented their efforts in destabilizing the 
preordained use of space at planned sites and empowering a clandestine social life. In this 
sense, the Situationists’ intentions were along similar lines as Solà-Morales’s later 
approach to the terrain vague. However, they “upset everyday use of space at strategic 
sites”108; in other words, they often invested in main sites as well, rather than just focusing 
on wastelands and desolate spaces. 
Constant   Nieuwenhuys:   a   special   case   
One of the important Situationist artists worthy of mentioning in relation to the 
terrain vague subject is Constant Nieuwenhuys, better known simply as Constant. The 
Dutch painter was already well known as a member of the Cobra group when he decided 
to join the Situationist International. He was present when a group of avant-garde artists 
                                                                                                                                                                        
103 Trans.: Act (or manifesto) in Favor of Unitary Urbanism. 
104 Heynen, Hilde. “New Babylon: The Antinomies of Utopia.” Architecture and Modernity: A Critique. 
Second printing. Cambridge, Massachusetts: MIT Press, 1999. 153. 
105 Constant also gave a lecture in 1960 entitled "Unitary Urbanism" at the Stedelijk Museum in Amsterdam. 
- Nieuwenhuys, Constant. “Unitary Urbanism.” Constant’s New Babylon: The Hyper-Architecture of Desire. 
Rotterdam: Witte de With, Center for Contemporary Art  : 010 Publishers, 1998. 131–135. 
106 Nieuwenhuys, Constant, and Debord, Guy. “The Amsterdam Declaration.” Theory of the Dérive and 
Other Situationist Writings on the City. Barcelona: Museu d’Art Contemporani de Barcelona, 1996. 80. 
107 Wigley, Mark. “The Great Urbanism Game.” Architectural Design: New Babylonians, v. 71, No. 3. Ed. Iain 
Borden and Sandy McCreery. London: Wiley-Academy, 2001. 9–10. 
108 Carney, Phil, and Miller, Vincent. “Vague Spaces.” Strange Spaces: Explorations into Mediated Obscurity. 
Ed. André Jansson and Amanda Lagerkvist. Farnham, England; Burlington, VT: Ashgate Pub., 2009. 33–57.

## S. 68

65 
 
met in Alba, Italy, in 1956, an event that greatly helped to establish the Situationist 
International a year after in London. He was then a key figure in the Amsterdam branch 
of the Situationist International established in 1958. In 1959, in the Situationist 
International Journal, Constant published an article entitled "A Different City for a 
Different Life"109 in which he described Unitary Urbanism as an urbanism of pleasure, 
arguing that through collective play and spatial continuum one can overcome alienation 
and self-expropriation. 
Two of his creations are pertinent to the discourse on terrains vagues: the New 
Babylon series and the Terrain Vague paintings. New Babylon was a utopian design of a 
futuristic complex urban system envisioned as a horizontal skeleton, elevated 
approximately 20 meters above the ground and stretching ten to twenty hectares over a 
terrain vague. Climatic conditions and the different areas of the project were imagined by 
Constant as controlled through hyper-advanced fully-automated technical systems, 
perfected telecommunications and audiovisual media. He depicted in his drawings, 
collages, and models a vision of New Babylon as almost floating above a large-scale terrain 
vague. 
 
FIGURE 17: Nieuwenhuys, Constant. View of New Babylonian Sectors. Collection Gemeentemuseum Den Haag, 
1971. Pencil photomontage watercolor. 
In the same spirit of situationist drifting and Debord’s Naked City, Constant's New 
Babylon also indirectly tackles notions of the void and terrain vague. Constant's vision 
                                                                                                                                                                        
109 Nieuwenhuys, Constant. “A Different City for a Different Life (Orig: Internationale Situationniste 3 
(December 1959), 37–40.).” Guy Debord and the Situationist International: Texts and Documents. Trans. 
John Shepley. Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, 2004. 95–101.

## S. 69

66 
hovers over vacant ashen spaces, this "white surface [was] no longer a neutral ground for a 
drawing. It [was] the drawn ground itself."110 Since Constant’s Homo Ludens is freed from 
all obligations, attachment to one specific location is no longer necessary. Thus, in full 
contradiction with the idea of a sedentary city, Constant imagines the dwellers of New 
Babylon as nomadic. He envisions a continuous flow and a "spatial continuum"111 which 
resonates with Solà-Morales's terrain vague, as we will ascertain in the next chapter. 
Constant's New Babylon was, furthermore, an attempt to expound, in the artist's 
own interpretation, on the expectations set out for Unitary Urbanism in the "Declaration 
of Amsterdam"112 manifesto. Over a decade and a half, he envisioned it as an environment 
that facilitates collective play and interventions, and social interactions, and in which one
