of the aims is to create affective micro-environments of emotions and psychogeographical 
maps. As theoretician Hilde Heynen describes: 
“New Babylon is a world in which all that is fleeting and transient has acquired the force 
of law. It is also a world of collective creation and absolute transparency; everything is 
exposed to the public gaze. In New Babylon imagination is in power and homo ludens is 
sovereign.” 113 
Constant's New Babylon is a celebration of the transitory and a utopian vision of a 
world where the homo faber is forever freed, thanks to a perfected technological system, 
from the labors of work to become a homo ludens, seeking play and pleasure. Ideas of 
wandering and resistance thus resurface once again. 
Along similar lines, Francesco Careri describes New Babylon as: 
"a system of enormous empty corridors extending across the territory, permitting the 
continuous migration of the multicultural populations. Empty corridors for nomadic 
wandering took the place of the consolidated city, superimposing themselves on the land like 
a formless, continuous, communicating spiderweb, in which life would be an adventure. But 
if we venture today into the empty wrinkles of Zonzo we get the impression that New 
Babylon has finally been realized. The seas of Zonzo are like a New Babylon without any 
megastructural or hypertechnological aspects. They are empty spaces like deserts, but like 
deserts they are not so empty after all. […] New Babylon lives inside the amnesias of the 
contemporary city like an enormous desert system ready to be inhabited by nomadic 
transurbance."114 
Careri's Zonzo corresponds greatly to Solà-Morales's terrain vague. In this sense, 
Careri recognizes New Babylon in the contemporary city as materialized in the terrain 
                                                                                                                                                                        
110 Zegher, M. Catherine de, and Mark Wigley, eds. The Activist Drawing: Retracing Situationist 
Architectures from Constant’s New Babylon to beyond. Cambridge, Mass: MIT Press, 2001. 45. 
111 Nieuwenhuys, Constant. “A Different City for a Different Life (Orig: Internationale Situationniste 3 
(December 1959), 37–40.).” Guy Debord and the Situationist International: Texts and Documents. Trans. 
John Shepley. Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, 2004. 95–101. 
112 Nieuwenhuys, Constant, and Guy Debord. “The Amsterdam Declaration.” Theory of the Dérive and Other 
Situationist Writings on the City. Barcelona: Museu d’Art Contemporani de Barcelona, 1996. 80. 
113 Heynen, Hilde. “New Babylon: The Antinomies of Utopia.” Assemblage, No. 29 (1996): 24–39. 
114 Careri, Francesco. Walkscapes: Walking as an Aesthetic Practice. Editorial Gustavo Gili, S.L., 2002. 187.

## S. 70

67 
 
vague. In fact, various aforementioned connotations associated with the terrain vague 
throughout this chapter are recurrent here: "empty corridors", "nomadic wandering", 
"formless continuous spiderweb"; New Babylon indeed lives through the terrain vague. 
Careri transcends the Situationists’ period and projects New Babylon onto the 
contemporary city: 
"New Babylon has emigrated, it has left the outskirts of Passaic, crossed the oceans and 
reached culturally different, ancient climes, raising interesting issues of identity. Venturing 
into New Babylon can be a useful method for the interpretation and transformation of those 
zones of Zonzo that, in recent years, have thrown the disciplines of architecture and urban 
planning into crisis."115 
Thus, Constant's vision, according to Careri, extends beyond the Situationist 
International, to Robert Smithson’s monument of Passaic and Freud's oceanic 
impressions116, to finally reach the contemporary importance of the terrain vague for the 
city and how such spaces are challenging the current status quo of architectural and urban 
disciplines. Moreover, Constant’s vision is probably best understood through his plastic 
representations rather than in his writing. His deconstructed landscapes and labyrinthine 
Piranesian constructions not only appeal to the continuous rhizomatic potentiallycharged flow of a terrain vague landscape, as advocated in Solà-Morales's essay, but also to 
the ideals of the city as labyrinth according to Walter Benjamin and the surrealists. 
It is also important to mention, in relation to the terrain vague discourse, that 
Constant's artistic long-term series New Babylon stems originally from the 
aforementioned meeting in Alba of 1956 and from Constant's encounters there with the 
Gypsy community settled on the land of the artist Pinot Gallizio. The nomadic culture in 
New Babylon was probably inspired by these encounters with the Gypsies of Alba. In fact, 
Constant launched his New Babylon series with a model, Ruimte Circus (Space Circus) 
which he initiated on his return from Alba. The model is composed of interweaving 
spirals that barely connect to a few structures on which the model rests on the ground. It 
is an allegory of a microcosm of the state of playfulness, periodically floating over and 
occupying the terrains vagues of the sedentary city. It represented the nomadic culture 
through ideas of the circus, dynamic movement and restless changing of place. This model 
is the first representation in Constant's New Babylon of a connection between the 
structures he imagines and the terrain vague below that constitutes the only grounding in 
the landscape or even reality. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
115 Ibid. 189. 
116 As we will describe in the following chapter in relation to Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay.

## S. 71

68 
 
FIGURE 18: Pinot Gallizio and the Gypsy Camp of Alba. Italy, 1956. Photograph. 
 
FIGURE 19: Nieuwenhuys, Constant. RuimteCircus (Space Circus). 1956. Soldered Iron Wire Model. 
Photograph by author at the Collection Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam.

## S. 72

69 
 
 
Constant's New Babylon generated controversy, especially among the Situationist 
International. Some accused him of succumbing to the instrumentalization of late 
capitalist consumption: 
“In attempting to design a utopia, a no-place, Constant inadvertently prefigured our 
contemporary non-places: the airports, auto routes, shopping centers, and generally that 
whole pseudo-architecture which has come increasingly to define our everyday lives at the 
end of the century [...] We might say that these drawings remain mired in contradictory 
language rather than embodying the language of contradiction.” 117 
Due to Constant's structural approach and his fascination in and conviction of the 
social efficacy of architecture, the Dutch artist quit the Situationist International in 1960. 
In "Critique of Urbanism" in 1961, the editorial board wrote in criticism of Constant, a 
year after he left: 
"The former situationist Constant, whose Dutch collaborators had been excluded from 
the SI for having agreed to build a church, frankly offers himself, along with two or three 
plagiarized and misconstrued situationist ideas, [...] as the only one still concerned with the 
urban milieu. Under such conditions, yes! Moreover, one might do well to recall that in April 
1959 this same group of former members of the Dutch section of the SI was firmly opposed to 
the adoption by the SI of an “Appeal to Revolutionary Artists and Intellectuals,” and stated: 
“For us, these perspectives do not depend on a revolutionary overthrow of present-day 
society, for which the conditions are lacking” (for this debate, see Internationale 
Situationniste no. 3, pp. 23 and 24)."118 
The Situationist International, especially Debord, were in fact very critical of 
Constant's New Babylon, which they believed to be too concerned with technical and 
architectural instrumentalization to be successfully subversive in keeping with 
Situationist International ideals: "the future revolutionary organization will not be able to 
rely on instruments less complete."119 One must not forget, nevertheless, that Constant's 
New Babylon is only a utopia, an idealized representation of an architectural and 
technical system, which the artist never actually intended to be realized. 
Keeping this criticism of Constant in mind, the artist proved a few years later his 
incisive foresight regarding the urban conundrum when he painted the Terrain Vague 
series, one of the last pieces of New Babylon in the early 1970s. These paintings are 
perhaps not the artist's most well-known projects but they are evidently the most relevant 
to our subject. They are often composed of a large seemingly monotonous yellow 
foreground surface, subtly constructed out of a collage of newspapers and old imagery, i.e. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
117 McDonough, Tom. “Fluid Spaces: Constant and the Situationist Critique of Architecture.” The Activist 
Drawing: Retracing Situationist Architectures from Constant’s New Babylon to beyond. Ed. M. Catherine de 
Zegher and Mark Wigley. Cambridge, Mass: MIT Press, 2001. 100. 
118 “Editorial Notes: Critique of Urbanism (Originally from Internationale Situationniste 6 (August 1961), 3–
11.).” Guy Debord and the Situationist International: Texts and Documents. Trans. John Shepley. Cambridge, 
Mass.: MIT Press, 2004. 103–115. 
119 Ibid.

## S. 73

70 
what one might interpret as traces of history in the yellow vast terrain vague. The strong 
black color of the background of these paintings contrasts sharply with the yellow and 
vague elements of what seem to depict his earlier forms and structures of New Babylon 
hidden in the distance. The stark focus in the painting is clearly on terrains vagues, as one 
can guess from the title; they are the most important element of this 'new' New Babylon. 
 
FIGURE 20: Nieuwenhuys, Constant. Terrain Vague. Collection Gemeentemuseum Den Haag, 1973. Linen oil 
paint. 
One might argue that these paintings portray a destruction of New Babylon, or 
Constant’s farewell to the series120. Constant himself, however, seemed to disagree. When 
Francesco Careri met the artist in 2000, the topic of the terrain vague was raised and 
Constant affirmed that the Terrain Vague series was for him more a continuation of the 
New Babylon project, rather than its eradication121. In the interview with Careri, Constant 
declares how he deemed the series a tribute to the Letterists. He describes how he 
                                                                                                                                                                        
120 Such thinkers are Mark Wigley, Jean Clarence Lambert and Hilde Heynen (Heynen, Hilde. “New 
Babylon: The Antinomies of Utopia.” Assemblage, No. 29 (1996): 24–39.). 
121 Careri, Francesco. Constant: New Babylon, Una Città Nomade. Torino: Testo & immagine, 2001. 80-81.

## S. 74

71 
 
received from Debord, before he even met him, a particular edition of Potlatch in which 
the Letterists opposed the Festival de l'Unité d'Abitation in Marseille. Constant states: 
"I hated Le Corbusier because he was a functionalist. His functions of living, working, 
circulating and recreating were the opposite of what I wanted: it was the State of Workers, 
slavery, I was thinking of the Homo Ludens, the State of Creators. In this edition of Potlatch, 
Letterists railed against the Marseille festival, saying: ‘We favor the terrain vague over the 
Unité d'Abitation.’ […] I did four or five other paintings, which I called Terrain Vague, as a 
tribute to the Letterists. This is why I started painting. [...] I made the terrains vagues and I 
dedicated them to the Letterists."122 
Thus, articles such as this, published by Potlatch, inspired Constant to paint the 
Terrain Vague series. These paintings consequently portray a progression in Constant’s 
thought, from the beginning of his involvement with the Situationist International, and 
reveal how he was motivated by ideas on the terrain vague, just as Solà-Morales a few 
decades later was inspired to write his essay. In effect, Constant viewed the terrain vague 
as a formula for contesting Modernist ideals, in a similar way to how Solà-Morales 
regarded the terrain vague as challenging the colonizing aspect of architectural practice. 
Careri’s interview with Constant indicates, moreover, a strong correlation 
between the notion of terrain vague and how Constant conceived New Babylon in general 
and what this utopia means to artists in terms more contemporary to the 1990s and 
nowadays. As Constant describes: 
"I wanted to continue New Babylon through painting on. When I started painting again 
it was not a break with New Babylon. With maquettes I did everything I could do, but I am a 
painter after all. […] When I made the picture I thought of the Letterists against Le 
Corbusier. In the terrain vague everyone can get to do what he or she likes. It is a Neo-
Babylonian space."123 
The terrain vague was hence interpreted by Constant as the new Babylonian 
space. 
Furthermore, the interview discloses how the Letterists, and Potlatch, were not 
the only inspiration driving the artist’s interest in the terrain vague. Aside from his 
correspondence with Debord, Constant also evokes his interaction with many gypsies and 
nomads, the music and instruments he played during these interactions, and the 
inspirational impact these had on his work and life. Aside from the gypsies that he 
                                                                                                                                                                        
122 Our trans from Italian: “Io detestavo Le Corbusier perché era un funzionalista. Le sue funzioni di abitare, 
lavorare, circolare e ricrearsi erano il contrario di quello che volevo io: era lo Stato dei Lavoratori, lo schiavismo, io 
pensavo all'Homo Ludens, allo Stato dei Creatori. Nel numero di Potlatch i lettristi si scagliavano contro il festival 
di Marsiglia dicendo: ‘Noi opponiamo all'Unitè d'Abitation il terrain vague.’ […] ho fatto altri quattro o cinque 
quadri che ho chiamato Terrain Vague come omaggio ai lettristi. È per questo che ho ripreso la pittura. […] ho 
fatto i terrains vagues e li ho dedicati ai lettristi.” - Ibid. 
123 Our trans. from Italian: “Io volevo continuare New Babylon nella pittura. Quando ho ricominciato a dipingere 
non era una rottura con New Babylon. Con le maquettes ho fatto tutto quello che potevo fare, ma io dopo tutto 
sono pittore. […] Quando ho fatto il quadro ho pensato ai lettristi contro Le Corbusier. Nel terrain vague tutti 
possono mettersi a fare quello che gli piace. È uno spazio neobabilonese.” - Ibid.

## S. 75

72 
encountered in Alba, as previously mentioned, Constant's atelier in Amsterdam once 
faced a terrain vague where nomads lived, which the artist describes: 
"Do you know what the terrain vague is? Have you seen the neighborhood here in front? 
It was built recently, but before that, when I had just come here to work, there was a large 
terrain vague with twigs, sand... There were many tents, nomads who made fires, who sang ... 
I watched them for ten years from this window, now I've covered it (with a panel), but 
before it was open because I liked watching it all."124 
Constant’s interest in the terrain vague hence goes deeper than initially 
anticipated; it is a lingering long-term personal involvement. Several days after Constant's 
death in 2005, Careri visited the gypsies' camp at Piedmont Sinti, which the painter had 
discovered in 1956, and found them still living there: 
"It was for them that Constant had drawn up his first architectural design, the Camp of 
the Alba Gypsies, which was the beginning of that concrete utopia developed in the 
following twenty years as New Babylon: the nomad city which, after the Situationist 
revolution, would have inhabited a land without borders, by ramifying itself out in a 
continuous flux, creating a new itinerant multicultural mankind - the wandering New 
Babylonians."125 
The terrain vague in Constant’s narrative is in this manner a social concern similar 
to Bhabha’s theorization of “thirdspace”. One can thus establish a connecting line, despite 
the variants in theoretical directions, joining Derrida’s chora, Constant’s New Babylon, 
Bhabha’s thirdspace, Barthes’ Ma and, finally, Solà-Morales’s terrain vague, in a quest to 
find the place of the “other”. 
Finally, Situationist International and Constant are still relevant today to notions 
of wandering and a new perception of urbanism through resistant practices. Quoting 
Thomas Levin: 
"One of the most important movements of the twentieth century, the Situationist 
International, introduces a thought around urbanity and its collective fruition. It is since 
1989, with the exhibition devoted to SI by the Centre Georges Pompidou in Paris, that many 
of its actions can now be revisited to come up with highly topical discussions concerning 
contemporary art, architecture and urbanism."126 
Far from being forgotten, the Situationist International methods inspired many 
interdisciplinary and artistic groups in the 1990s and beyond, some of which were also 
inspired by Solà-Morales to create new approaches more suited to their time. Stalker, 
Stalking Detroit and Atelier SYN- are three such examples that we will visit in the fifth 
chapter of this dissertation. Constant's paintings, for instance, are mentioned in the book 
                                                                                                                                                                        
124 Our trans. from Italian: “Tu lo sai cos'è il terrain vague? Hai visto il quartiere qui davanti? È stato costruito 
da poco, ma prima quando io venivo già qui a lavorare, là c'era un grande terrain vague con sterpi, sabbia… 
C'erano molte tende, i nomadi che facevano i fuochi, che cantavano… li ho visti per dieci anni da questa 
finestra, ora l'ho coperta, ma prima era aperta perché a me piaceva vedere tutto ciò.” - Ibid. 
125 Careri, Francesco. “Is New Babylon Here?” lo Squaderno no.18 The value of places / Il valore dei luoghi 
(2010): 61–65. 
126 Levin, Thomas. “Geopolitics of Hibernation: The Drift of Situationist Urbanism.” Situacionistas: Arte, 
Politica, Urbanismo = Situationists  : Art, Politics, Urbanism. Barcelona: Actar, 1996. 526-529.

## S. 76

73 
 
Stalking Detroit, by the same landscape urbanists who were also inspired by Solà-
Morales's terrain vague. It includes a description of Constant's 1973 painting: 
 “Beyond the simple depiction of a wasteland, it illuminates the complex dynamics 
between presence and absence, between erasure or destruction and construction. A 
predominantly barren, emptied white space traced with a few lines recognizable as planes 
and volumes recedes toward a strongly marked horizon."127 
Constant's terrains vagues are thus depictions of the process of becoming, the 
fleeting moment between absence and presence. Constant's terrain vague could indeed 
refer to the terrain vague's subversive, marginal and suspended qualities in opposition to 
the dynamism of the functional city. As a consequence, the terrain vague could be 
understood as a fertile land for Situationist approaches. As Francesco Marullo argues: 
"Situationists postulated vagueness as an active principle, necessary to subvert the 
functional order of the city. […] the precariousness of the terrain vague offered an 
inexhaustible fertility for new tactics of struggle, stimulating a series of illegal activities, 
misalignments, strategies of appropriation and use beyond the functionalist predicament of 
urban planning. To state the terrain vague as essential substratum of urban settlements meant 
to reverse the whole apparatus of capitalist production against itself, dismantling its cultural 
schemes of integration and reassembling them into new unexpected forms."128 
 
In this fashion, similar to the ideas promoted in Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague” 
essay, the Situationist strategies aimed at creating an alternative urban realm where 
consumerism and planning are not the controlling forces, thus transgressing preestablished urban formulas. However, unlike the Situationists, the terrains vagues of Solà-
Morales are spaces where this transgression happens naturally, almost as an unavoidable 
consequence of the ordered city; a natural glitch in the system. Therefore, in the terrain 
vague, the resistance is natural and inevitable. What is needed, nonetheless, and what 
both Situationists and Solà-Morales point towards, is the necessity to raise awareness 
towards such spaces in order to benefit from and not lose this given potential. 
Following a similar line of thought, the philosopher and sociologist Henri 
Lefebvre promoted in the 1970s moments in space differentiated through practice, 
production, mapping and experience. His mode of thinking space proliferated new 
approaches to understanding the urban realm beyond its physical presence, including the 
experience of space as well as its representation. As he affirmed: “(Social) space is a (social) 
process.”129 He described how space is modified through people’s occupation of place, how 
appropriation, regardless of planning and architectural efforts, alters and transforms space. 
According to Sophie Bøggild, from this perspective “(urban) space is never stable, empty 
or Euclidian, but develops organically as an open process with the social in various 
                                                                                                                                                                        
127 Daskalakis, Georgia, Charles Waldheim, and Jason Young. Stalking Detroit. Actar, 2001. 81. 
128 Marullo, Francesco. “Productive Uncertainty. Notes on Terrain Vague.” lo Squaderno no. 34 | Stand-by 
(2014): 19–24. 
129 Lefebvre, Henri. The Production of Space. Malden, Mass. Blackwell, 2011(1974). 26.

## S. 77

74 
guises.” 130 In this context, the terrain vague is created not only through cracks in the 
planning but also through people’s intervention in urban spaces. They activate the 
terrains vagues in function and meaning, and make them visible; or they transform, 
through mutations and rupture, the controlled spaces into terrains vagues. Lefebvre 
would denominate the terrain vague as the “dominated (and dominant) space, which is to 
say a space transformed—and mediated—by technology, by practice.”131 In this sense, the 
terrain vague is a form of resistance to what Lefebvre argues is the killing of spaces of 
representation in the modern era by representations of space. As Phil Carney & Vincent 
Miller put it: “‛colonization’ occurs when conceptions or representations precede space 
that he calls ‘fully lived’ and space itself becomes a representation.”132 Terrain vague is a 
resistance against this colonization where “abstracted forces and representation assert 
order on the imagined and the perceived.”133 
In Lefebvre’s Critique of the Everyday Life, the systems produced by everyday life 
are linked through the use of space. Consequently, a struggle takes place against the 
planned space with its preordained rationale. Three layered moments arise from this 
struggle between capitalist rationality, the production of space and everyday life. In this 
framework, the terrains vagues seem to simultaneously incorporate all three of Lefebvre’s 
“urban moments”134, but in a dissident form. They subvert conforming “spatial practices” 
that attempt to strengthen pre-given spatial models and induce alternative practices. They 
reveal the cracks within the design and planning of “representations of space”. They 
finally form alternative resistant “spaces of representation”, spaces of the other where 
spatial and historical layers overlap in unexpected forms. 
Hence, the promise of vagueness and wandering inherent to the terrain vague is 
apparent in these various precedent movements inspired to understand and act differently 
on urban spaces in order to forge new spaces of representation and subversive 
representations of space. Along comparable lines of activist practice linked to the 
Situationists and Lefebvre’s sociology 135, the terrain vague discourse emerged a few 
decades later in the context of late capitalism. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
130 Bøggild, Signe Sophie. “Happy Ever After? The Welfare City in between the Freetown and the New 
Town.” Space for Urban Alternatives? Christiania 1971-2011. Ed. Håkan Thörn. Hedemora: Gidlunds Förlag, 
2011. 114–115. 
131 Lefebvre, Henri. The Production of Space. Malden, Mass.: Blackwell, 2011(1974). 164. 
132 Carney, Phil, and Vincent Miller. “Vague Spaces.” Strange Spaces: Explorations into Mediated Obscurity. 
Ed. André Jansson and Amanda Lagerkvist. Farnham, England: Ashgate Pub., 2009. 33–57. 
133 Carney, Phil, and Vincent Miller. Ibid. 43. 
134 Lefebvre, Henri. Critique of Everyday Life. Verso, 1991. 340-356. 
135 One can also mention Georges Bataille’s notion of transgression and the social critique of Michel De 
Certeau on the practice of everyday life.

## S. 78

75 
 
Peter   Handke:   a   reference   contemporaneous   with   Solà-­‐Morales      
Following Dadaist thinking, Surrealist déambulations, Situationist methods and 
Constant’s paintings, the terrain vague could be understood in part as a constellation of 
nomadic spaces in constant transformation, disappearing and reappearing, in spite of the 
imposed order of the city. In this respect, the notion can be associated in the 1980s and 
1990s with the work of novelist and playwright, Peter Handke. According to film-maker 
Philip Grand, in an interviewed with the author136, Handke is an inspiration for artists 
currently working on the terrain vague from this perspective of escape and opposition. 
Handke describes, for instance, various spaces, suburbs and “bays” in his novel 
My Year in the No-Man's-Bay137 in the same spirit of fragmented transforming nomadic 
movements. He also wrote a novel entitled Absence138 in 1990, which he made into a film 
two years later and in which the protagonists set off to drift into a desolate wasteland. The 
storyline, much like the desert-like space described, is composed of a series of fragmented 
and dislocated events and monologues. The nomadic flair of the script and the desolate 
environment are reminiscent of Wenders’ Wings of Desire139, which Handke also cowrote a few years earlier. Through such instances and descriptions, Handke’s inner self 
and outer world often merge to delineate a sense of wandering, alienation and banality 
that stretch between an inner unrest and outer fragmented transformation. Handke 
describes this in an interview: 
“I live only through these intermediate spaces, where history is as when two planecarriers come closer and leave between them only a thin slit... It is of these slits, of these 
looks passing through the interstices that I live and that I write; all that I have done lives 
through these narrowing intermediate spaces, and it is also defined by history. So I look 
towards where I can still escape, but while escaping, which is also very important, and 
towards where I can generate a movement that will produce a permanence or a project.”140 
Handke thus connects the interstice with his urge to escape. More importantly, 
similar to the Situationists and Constant, Handke’s approach is not passive either; he seeks 
forms of production and intervention. In this fashion, terrains vagues are perceived as 
active connectors between the inner and the outer conflicts that the urban dweller faces. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
136 Grand himself was inspired by Handke’s work to make a film-documentary entitled Terrain Vague. 
- Interview between author and Philippe Grand. 5 Dec. 2013. 
137 Handke, Peter, and Winston, Krishna. My Year in the No-Man’s-Bay. 1st American ed. New York: Farrar, 
Straus and Giroux, 1998. 
138 Handke, Peter. Absence. London: Methuen, 1991. 
139 Wenders, Wim. and Handke, Peter (screenplay). Wings of Desire (Der Himmel über Berlin). Basis-Film-
Verleih GmbH, 1987. Film. 
140 Our trans. from French: “Je ne vis que de ces espaces intermédiaires, où l’histoire est comme lorsque deux 
porte-avions se rapprochent et ne laissent entre eux qu’une mince fente… C’est de ces fentes, de ces regards 
passant par les interstices que je vis et que j’écris; tout ce que j’ai fait vit de ces espaces intermédiaires qui se 
rétrécissent, et c’est aussi défini par l’histoire. Je regarde donc par où puis-je encore m’échapper, mais tout en 
m’échappant, ce qui est aussi très important, où puis-je susciter un mouvement producteur d’une 
permanence ou d’un projet.” - Gamper, Herbert, Peter Handke, and Nicole Casanova. Espaces intermédiaires: 
Entretiens. Paris: C. Bourgois, 1992. 125.

## S. 79

76 
The act of wandering is a consequence and a symptom of a resistance against these 
conflicts, created by the discrepancy between what the aspirations of the self are and 
what the city offers. 
In addition, such acts of meandering are often denigrated. As sociologist Vincent 
Miller argues: “two main forms of power have confronted wandering practices: first, the 
state and, secondly, capital.” 141 Meanwhile sociologist William Chambliss sets forth 
examples in his book Social Problems demonstrating that, according to established 
powers, the traveller, the vagrant and the dweller of the interstitial are regarded as 
somehow criminal simply due to their wandering practices, regardless of their other 
actions142. In our laws, even up until today, ‘vagrancy’ often remains illegal, for the simple 
reason that wandering itself is considered an activity of the suspicious kind and that the 
act of errancy is sufficient to be the object of criminal prosecution. All of this brings the 
tension to an even higher level between the different stakeholders on the terrain vague. 
 
FIGURE 21: Handke, Peter. The Absence. Gemini Films, 1995. Film poster. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
141 Carney, Phil, and Miller, Vincent. “Vague Spaces.” Strange Spaces: Explorations into Mediated Obscurity. 
Ed. André Jansson and Amanda Lagerkvist. Farnham, England: Ashgate Pub., 2009. 52. 
142 Chambliss, William J. “A Sociological Analysis of the Law of Vagrancy.” Social Problems 12 (1964): 67–77.

## S. 80

77 
 
Connotations   of   “terrain”   
On a similar note, the connotations inherent in “terrain” can also be associated 
with precedents sharing Solà-Morales’s idea of the city as a constellation of fragments 
measured in intensities and tensions. For instance, in the 1980s, the city as a constellation 
of diverse fragments is already present in the Swiss historian of urbanism André Corboz’s 
theory of palimpsests143; the city as a harmonious entity has since then, and probably even 
earlier, been contested. Corboz however, unlike Solà-Morales, tackles this fragmentation 
from a negative perspective. Corboz also focused more on the built form rather than the 
non-built in the writing of his theories. Nonetheless, through its transformations and 
mutations, the terrain vague could be considered as the ultimate palimpsest, a collection 
of memories and signifiers often in decomposition and recomposition that reveal the city’s 
constant rewriting. In Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague”, these intensities and forgotten 
fragments form a resource and a quality for the city. 
Solà-Morales’s terrain vague hence encourages an alternative interpretation of 
Western cities with trends of fragmentation and separation. It represents both the 
ruptures in a preconceived whole, as well as the liberating elements within efforts of 
order and conformity. On a more contemporary level, his essay subtly addresses a political 
concern towards the 1990s onward. It gains a new significance in late capitalist politics 
since, as landscape architect Helen Armstrong argues, “space’s exchange value becomes far 
more important than its social and cultural value.”144 Therefore, the terrain vague, including 
every forgotten urban spot and roadside, gains a higher exchange value and makes public 
urban appropriation ever harder and unwelcome by capitalist forces. 
In conclusion, we have tried to situate in this part of the thesis the terrain vague 
notion within a larger context from the 1950s onward, thus attempting to formulate a 
preliminary understanding of the urban and architectural challenges that this period has 
faced and which have triggered interest in the terrain vague discourse. On a second note, 
the set of precursors and terminologies to which the terrain vague belongs defines the 
notion’s historical cultural layer, its connotations and ultimately its layered meaning. 
Considering these two contextualizations, the notion hence becomes more than a mere 
philosophical linguistic interest; this setting sets the background for a new discourse and, 
as Solà-Morales advocates, a potential new approach to the fields of architecture and 
urbanism. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
143 Corboz, André. “Le Territoire Comme Palimpseste.” Diogène n°121, Paris. (1983): 14–35. 
144 Armstrong, Helen. “Time, Dereliction and Beauty: An Argument for ‘Landscapes of Contempt.’” IFLA 
Eastern Region Conference Proceeding Book (2006): 116–127.

## S. 81

78 
 
FIGURE 22: Photograph of Ignasi de Solà-Morales from his library in Barcelona. (Photograph by Author).

## S. 82

79 
 
Biographical introduction to Solà-Morales 
Although the terrain vague notion is becoming increasingly recognized in 
contemporary urban discussions, its origins are blurred. Not much has been written on 
how it came to be coined by Solà-Morales, or how it was received, interpreted and put 
into practice by his contemporaries. In order to fully comprehend the notion, one must 
look into its creation and the contexts in which it was devised, as well as the applications 
that followed. An important factor in this pursuit is to investigate how the former is 
linked to the latter, i.e. how the process of theoretical formulation influenced the process 
of application. In order to succeed in this back-and-forth quest, one should first 
understand the position of Ignasi de Solà-Morales (1942-2001) as a philosopher, 
theoretician, architect, writer, intellectual and member of different theoretical circles, 
such as the Anyone Corporation, the Institute for Architecture and Urban Studies in New 
York and the Triennale di Milano, as well as the academic realms of Princeton University 
and the Catalan universities and institutes, mainly the University of Barcelona, the Centre 
de Cultura Contemporània de Barcelona (CCCB) and the Collegi Oficial d'Arquitectes de 
Catalunya (COAC). 
Solà-Morales is often described as a versatile multifaceted figure. He could be 
considered both a traditionalist and a revolutionary. He was closely linked with 
architectural traditions found in the academic circles of Barcelona, characterized by a 
commitment to the historical, architectural and artistic heritage of the city. He was at the 
same time attentive to more contemporary urban changes and transformations on a local 
and global scale. In addition, an astute sense of curiosity accompanied him throughout his 
intellectual journey. A sensitivity to various professional fields, different theories and 
numerous intellectual voices, mature and young, led to his innovative essays and notions, 
such as “liquid architecture”, “terrain vague”, “building on the built”, “untimeliness” and 
“theatricality”. 
Born in Barcelona into a family of architects, Solà-Morales was one of the most 
prominent Catalan thinkers, his theoretical works on contemporary urban issues spanning 
more than 20 years from the 1980s to the beginning of the new millennium. His diverse 
interests reflect his interdisciplinary education between philosophy and architecture. He 
studied both architecture145 and philosophy at the University of Barcelona, obtaining a 
degree in philosophy and literature in 1968 and a doctorate in architecture146 in 1973. This 
                                                                                                                                                                        
145 At the ETSAB, Escola Tècnica Superior d’Arquitectura de Barcelona. 
146 His doctoral research entitled “Rubió i Bellver y la fortuna del Gaudinismo”, signaled the beginning of his 
interest in the history of Catalan architecture, which was truncated by the Franco regime. His thesis, later 
published in 1975, was dedicated to Joan Rubió i Bellver, Antoni Gaudi’s assistant.

## S. 83

80 
double training allowed him to build his theoretical work on solid aesthetic, scientific, 
technical, as well as philosophical grounds. 
In 1978, Solà-Morales became a professor of Architectural Composition at the 
Higher Technical School of Architecture of Barcelona, and was often invited to lecture in 
other universities worldwide. He was, for instance, a visiting scholar at Columbia 
University in 1980, a professor of the International Laboratory of Architecture, Urbanism 
and Design (I.L.A.U.D.) in Urbino-Siena in 1982, a visiting professor at the Art History 
Department of Bologna University in 1984-85, at the School of Architecture of Torino 
Politechnical University in 1986-87, at Princeton University in 1993-94, at the 
Architecture and Art History Department of the University of Cambridge in 2000, and a 
visiting professor of the Accademia di Architettura at the Università della Svizzera 
Italiana in 2000147. His teachings and theoretical work thus helped him become an 
internationally known figure in the fields of architectural and urban theory148. 
Solà-Morales also played a role in many intellectual groups and magazines in 
various countries, such as the Editorial Gustavo Gili where he edited the Architecture and 
Criticism collection, as well as the Board of the Fondation Musée Olympique de 
Lausanne, among others. These involvements reveal Solà-Morales’s commitment to 
society and social matters. He was likewise a member of the Patronage of the Mies van 
der Rohe Foundation and the Jury of the European Architecture Prize Mies van der Rohe. 
Accordingly, he was the architect behind the construction of a replica of Ludwig Mies van 
der Rohe's German Pavilion of the 1929 International Exposition in Barcelona. This 
reconstruction of the Barcelona Pavilion, one of the most celebrated architectural works 
worldwide and one of the biggest projects in Spain in the 20th century, was his most 
notable architectural project which also propelled his name to an international level. 
However, we will not explore the project in this dissertation, as it is not really relevant to 
the subject under study149. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
147 Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archives - Documents - Letters. 
148 Solà-Morales was even invited to Zurich as a member of the Scientific Advisory Board of the Stiftung 
Bibliothek Werner Oechslin in Zurich in 1999. He also gave a lecture entitled “The Play of Interpretations” 
in 1999 at the Institut für Geschichte und Theorie der Architektur (gta), at ETH Zurich (1999). Previously, in 
1987, Solà-Morales had already given a conference at ETH entitled “Rekonstruktion Des Barcelona 
Pavillons”, as well as another in 1988 entitled “Costruzione Dello Spazio Urbano. “L’american Vitrubius” di 
Werner Hegemann”. He also intended to give a conference at the gta in 1994 entitled “Colonization and 
Violence in Architecture”, which he could not attend due to the destruction of the Liceu operahouse caused 
by a fire that year. 
149 We will examine instead, in the following chapters, Solà-Morales’s reconstruction of the Liceu Theater 
because the project is intimately linked with the narrative of Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay.

## S. 84

81 
 
 
FIGURE 23: Foundation Mies van der Rohe. Memorial Plate for Ignasi de Solà-Morales and Arthur Drexler. “In 
recognition of those who have made possible the reconstruction of the German Pavilion of the International 
exhibition of Barcelona in 1929 by the architect Ludwig Mies van der Rohe.” (Photograph by Author). 
Solà-Morales was, moreover, both a theoretician and an architect; one can even 
say that he was more of the former than the latter. His architectural projects were 
therefore endowed with theoretical grounding, while his written work was in turn 
supported by a more profound knowledge and experience of architectural practice. One 
particular project consumed his energy in the last decade of his life: the enlargement and 
reconstruction of the Gran Teatre del Liceu in Barcelona after it suffered a dreadful fire in 
1994. Many of his other projects included the restoration of ancient theatres such as those 
of Torelló and Valls. He restored many historical buildings and town centers as well, for 
instance in Tarragona, Marseille and some areas of Barcelona. 
 
FIGURE 24: Ruins of the Liceu Theater after the Fire. Barcelona, 1994. Photograph.

## S. 85

82 
 
FIGURE 25: Article in El Pais on the Liceu Theater Fire. Edicion de Madrid, 1994. Journal article. 
Solà-Morales’s theoretical work developed beyond the local scale and limits of 
architectural thought. Disciplines such as literature, photography and art are recurrent in 
his work, interpreted into the field of urban and architecture theory. Accordingly, his 
intellectual journey developed through various phases. Solà-Morales’s position, in fact, 
seemed to change significantly throughout his career, and mainly around three episodes: 
an initial formative phase focused on architectural history, phenomenology and typology; 
a second phase fixed on architectural theory with inputs from deconstructivism, Peter 
Eisenman and Manfredo Tafuri; and finally a third more experimental phase stimulated 
by metropolitan concerns and global urban issues. The "Terrain Vague" essay and some of 
his most inspiring work belong to this phase. 
In the first period, Solà-Morales began to teach at the ETSAB. His classes were 
inspired by his study of urban typologies and his interactions with the Institute of 
Architecture of Venice (IUAV) where Manfredo Tafuri was teaching at the time. During 
the 1970s and the 1980s his work was inspired by waves of critical and typological 
thinking in Italy. Conforming to ideas about restoration and historic preservation, his 
attention was drawn to preserving Catalan artistic and architectural heritage. In parallel, 
he became close to the Gustavo Gili publishing house with which he would maintain a 
theoretically and professionally fruitful friendship throughout his life. This allowed him 
to translate works from Peter Collins for inclusion in the Gili collection of Architecture 
and Criticism. He also worked during this phase on texts of influential contemporary

## S. 86

83 
 
critics such as Colin Rowe, Aldo Rossi and Alan Colquhoun. Furthermore, he became 
interested in phenomenological thought with the philosophies of Merleau-Ponty and 
Christian Norberg-Schulz. This phenomenological and existentialist inspiration would 
prove useful around a decade later for his formulation of the terrain vague notion150. 
The second period of Solà-Morales’s itinerary, which was gradually expounded 
during the early 1980s, was focused even more on architectural theory and on insights 
from Lewis Mumford, Colin Rowe, Manfredo Tafuri151 and Sigfried Giedion. It was also 
during this period that the professor became more involved in American intellectual 
spheres and was in contact with Peter Eisenman who joined as a fellow at the Institute for 
Architecture and Urban Studies in 1980/1981152. With one foot in history and the other in 
contemporaneity, he began to oppose in this phase pre-established ideas of architectural 
discipline, history and criticism. Based on Derridian concepts, but more importantly 
following the traditions of the Frankfurt School and Walter Benjamin, this phase would, 
moreover, prove useful in building Solà-Morales’s vision of contemporary architecture 
and urbanism as a condition of fragmentation and diffusion; this later became visible in 
his theorization of the terrain vague notion. 
What is most important for this thesis is Solà-Morales’s third phase. In the 1990s, 
the theoretician dedicated most of his time to North American theories, manifested in his 
published essay during this period and his co-founding of the Anyone Corporation and 
the Master program Metropolis in Barcelona following an experimental approach to 
architecture and urbanism. He came very close to the visions of Zygmunt Bauman, and to 
deconstructivist Derridian ideas similar to Eisenman’s interests, while maintaining an 
ongoing interest in Deleuzian post-structuralist thought, as observed in the "Terrain 
Vague" essay153. His travels to New York and his involvements with Princeton University 
during this phase enabled him to distance himself from European ideologies. He adhered 
neither to Modernist nor Postmodernist ideologies, hence refusing certainties or utopian 
interpretations imposed on the urban and the sociological. Congruously, one of his last 
                                                                                                                                                                        
150 As we will explain in the second chapter. 
151 However, although Manfredo Tafuri was his teacher, one cannot really state that Solà-Morales was a 
‘tafurian’151 as his work differed from the former’s and their interests diverged. Peter Eisenman, in an 
interview with the author, agrees with this statement and argues that one cannot even consider Solà-Morales 
as following Derrida or others; Eisenman describes Solà-Morales as a “lone wolf” following his own 
theoretical trajectory. - Interview between author and Peter Eisenman in Venice. 6 May 2014. 
152 Kim Forester points out, in his doctoral thesis, that Solà-Morales was a visiting fellow at the Institute in 
1980/81 (together with Lars Lerup and Aldo Rossi). According to the students’ bulletin no 7, 1980, he taught 
a course on urban development in the Undergraduate Program in Architecture. Solà-Morales was also 
presented as a visiting fellow at the fellows evening on May 5, 1981. Apparently, Eisenman proposed that the 
exhibition New Wave of Spanish Architecture should be curated by Ignasi Solà-Morales in the year 1982/83, 
which did not happen. These are probably the earliest encounters between Eisenman and Solà-Morales, 
which prompted, a decade later, the creation of the Anyone Corporation. 
153 As we will examine in the following chapter.

## S. 87

84 
lectures was aptly entitled “After Postmodern Architecture”, held at the MACBA on 15 
November 2000. This is also visible in the "Terrain Vague" essay in which predefined 
forms of interpreting and planning urban spaces are repudiated. Moreover, in this last 
period, Solà-Morales’s interest in a fragmented urban philosophy, originating from his 
fascination with Walter Benjamin’s writings and approach, found its consolidation in his 
conviction that only a fragmented form of writing, i.e. in essays and articles, can reflect 
the contemporary urban experience, stating: “It is impossible today to write a treatise on 
architecture”154. Accordingly, while his body of work includes numerous books, it is in his 
published essays, such as the "Terrain Vague", that one finds most of his innovative 
visions on the contemporary architectural and urban condition. 
 
FIGURE 26: Passport. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive. The Professor’s passport shows the dates of his 
many travels to the American continent in the 1980s and 1990s. 
Solà-Morales was also significant in playing the role of intellectual ambassador of 
Spain and Catalonia to the world. Rooted in Barcelona and its universities, his lectures 
created a bridge between the local and global spheres. He contributed to promoting 
innovative approaches in Barcelona after the Franco regime and then again after the 
Olympic games. His international connections, facilitated by the debates at Triennale di 
Milano and the conferences of the Anyone Corporation, helped to elevate the national 
critical stance beyond its borders. Another important event was his involvement as 
general organizer and rapporteur of the nineteenth Congress of the International Union 
of Architects (UIA) in 1996 which took place in Barcelona, and general moderator of the 
Present and Future International Symposium, the terrain vague being one of these events’ 
main themes. Correspondingly, the "Terrain Vague" essay reflects both a local scale, 
relevant to Barcelona and Europe, and a global one, relevant to the American sphere, to 
developing cities and cities in sprawl. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
154 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Sado-Masochism: Criticism and Architectural Practice.” Differences: 
Topographies of Contemporary Architecture. Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, 1997. 139–140.

## S. 88

85 
 
 
FIGURE 27: Anyone Conference Announcement. CCA Archives - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001, 
1991. Poster.

## S. 89

86 
The "Terrain Vague" essay within the Anyone corporation 
In relation to the "Terrain Vague" essay, one of the most important events in this 
biography is Solà-Morales’s co-founding of the Anyone Corporation initiated in 1990. It is 
one of the most acknowledged contexts in which he presented and published the essay. It 
is also one of his most important networks that received the greatest attention and 
propelled him from a local figure to an internationally recognized intellectual reference. 
It is therefore worthy to elaborate first on this event before burrowing into the essay 
itself. 
Solà-Morales was the only Spanish figure among the founding group that 
constituted an American architect from New York, Peter Eisenman, and a Japanese 
architect from Tokyo, Arata Isozaki. The corporation generated ten main conferences 
throughout the decade 1991-2000, based on the ten compounds of the word “any” in the 
English dictionary. The intention was to incorporate the state of architectural and urban 
thought at the end of the millennium into international interdisciplinary discussions, first 
on actors and stakeholders (Anyone conference, Los Angeles, 1991), then on location and 
geopolitics (Anywhere conference, Japan, 1992), on methods and approaches (Anyway, 
Barcelona 1993, which Solà-Morales directed at the Center for Contemporary Culture of 
Barcelona), on place and field positioning (Anyplace, Montreal, 1994, in which the 
"Terrain Vague" essay was presented), on knowledge and information (Anywise, Korea, 
1995), on body and form (Anybody, Buenos Aires, 1996), on means and processes 
(Anyhow, Rotterdam, 1997), on time and history (Anytime, Ankara 1998), on excess and 
production (Anymore, Paris, 1999), and finally on a general closing theme under the title 
Anything in New York in 2000. Solà-Morales attended all of these conferences and was 
published in the proceedings publications, as well as the related Any magazine. 
Anyplace was thus the fourth in this series of conferences and published volumes 
compiled annually by the Anyone Corporation. These ten cross-cultural and 
multidisciplinary conferences were held to reflect on the condition of architecture at the 
end of the millennium. The corporation was an architectural think tank and discussion 
platform that has been referred to as “one of the most ambitious and adventurous projects in 
the history of architecture.”155 
                                                                                                                                                                        
155 Pascucci, Ernest. “ANY Magazine to Release ‘Whiteness’.” 14 Nov. 1999. Web. 9 Oct. 2013. 
 – More on the Anyone Corporation will be discussed in the fourth chapter.

## S. 90

87 
 
 
FIGURE 28: Anyplace Conference Announcement. CCA Archives - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001, 
1994. Poster. 
By tracing the author’s network, some clarifications will be given regarding the 
most acknowledged issuing of the essay within the book (and conference proceeding) 
Anyplace. The "Terrain Vague" essay, as previously mentioned, owes its renown and 
vibrancy partly to its position in a publication whose back cover reads as: 
 “What if you held an architecture conference and Fredric Jameson came, and Peter 
Eisenman and Arata Isozaki, Rem Koolhaas and Daniel Libeskind, Bernard Tschumi and 
Elizabeth Diller, Toyo Ito and Seok Chul Kim, and Sylviane Agacinski, Elizabeth Grosz, John 
Rajchman, Jean-Louis Déotte, Sanford Kwinter, Peter Galison and Caroline Jones, Alberto 
Pérez-Gómez, Jean-Claude Guédon, Brian Boigon, Ignasi de Solà-Morales, Johanne 
Lamoureux, Douglas Cooper, Melvin Charney, Sylvia Lavin, Michel Iorio, Daniel Latouche, 
Kojin Karatani, Bruce Trigger, Rebecca Comay, and Akira Asada? The result would be, and is, 
‹Anyplace›, a stimulating collection of essays, images, and dialogue that probes architecture's 
relationships with philosophy, science, art, technology, and politics.”156 
Indeed, the importance of the corporation comes from the fact that it involved, 
from its very establishment, many prominent and influential figures in architecture and 
other disciplines157. Since the group's initiation, prominent members took up a base in this 
corporation to discuss the field of architecture and exchange thoughts on contemporary 
                                                                                                                                                                        
156 Davidson, Cynthia., ed. Anyplace. Cambridge, Mass.: Anyone Corp.; MIT Press, 1995. Cover. 
157 It is not a simple matter of creating a corporation whose base is international and whose members are 
dispersed. The main requirements are good organization, willingness to focus on productive outcomes from 
time-limited meetings, and individual single-task assignments. The necessary prerequisite is that all members 
agree on one general profile of the corporation and work accordingly towards reaching the same goals. The 
Anyone corporation integrated all of the above in order to become an influential thought-generating force in 
the work of each of its members, including Solà-Morales.

## S. 91

88 
theoretical issues. These characters integrated their various international backgrounds 
into their inputs within the corporation, which gave the latter a universal character and 
its influence a worldly recognition. This globalized vision was intently portrayed in the 
corporation and one of the capital reasons behind the locations of the conferences, hosted 
each time by a different city around the world. 
In credit to this placeless initiative, the Anyone corporation soon gained the 
support of other international institutions, such as the Shimizu Corporation of Japan, the 
Rizzoli International Publications and the Getty Center for the History of Art and the 
Humanities. These were equally supportive of comprehensive and interdisciplinary 
approaches towards future research. The "Terrain Vague" essay and notion were thus well 
introduced into a global interdisciplinary context, where intellectuals from around the 
globe congregated to select, activate and propagate certain aspects of architectural and 
urban thought. 
 
FIGURE 29: AnyTIME, AnyPLACE, AnyWEAR. 1993. CCA Archives - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001. 
Advertisement.

## S. 92

89 
 
Anyplace,   an   interdisciplinary   book   
Hence, the essay "Terrain Vague" first gained universal recognition within the 
context of the Anyplace publication. Looking back at this book, it is worth mentioning 
that the editing was undertaken by Cynthia Davidson, the main organizer of both the 
book and the corporation. Although the Anyone Corporation was co-founded by her 
husband, Peter Eisenman, it was her efforts as chief editor that really shaped the 
corporation and its publications, books and the ANY magazine series. In an interview 
with the author158, Eisenman describes how he was involved with other interests at the 
time and how it was Davidson, and her editing team, who really took charge of the 
corporation, made sure the yearly conferences took place and that the yearly publications 
kept pace. Accordingly, Davidson had a strong effect on the general orientation of the 
book; her role in choosing the various writers and in directing the course of the 
conversation is apparent, since she herself is in between domains. As Davidson puts it: 
"The cross-cultural and multidisciplinary "asylum" both sustains and problematizes 
ANY’s indeterminacy. This is especially apparent in the shared problem of the city. […] In 
what way architecture is able to deal with the repressed and with indeterminacy as a way to 
open this condition is still not clear, because architecture's foundations are often built on 
such repressions. But the past six years of the ANY conferences show that architecture can 
move and is moving. To record and understand this movement we turn, in part, to the letter 
(hence the publications of Anyone)."159 
The main objective seems to be an invitation to celebrate indeterminacy as an 
occasion to explore possibilities. One proposed approach is to blur the boundaries 
between what was perceived as separate domains contained within clear distinctions. In 
this sense, at the turn of the millennium, the very definition of architecture was under 
debate. During this period, questions such as 'What could be considered as architecture?' 
and 'Who does architecture address?' made people feel uneasy about the consequences of 
global networks and new technology. This state of mind was again not far from the 
structuralists’ mindset; it was also an alternative continuum to Koolhaas’s aspiring grid. 
The divisions of the book thus equally refer to the potential of this multidisciplinarity and 
its effect on architecture and, vice versa, the insurgence of architecture within other 
domains. We have already noticed the multidisciplinary aspect of architecture and 
urbanism during this period, when notions apparent in diverse domains were linked and 
commons paths deciphered between them. This had a promoting impact on the terrain 
vague notion, because it was referred to within photography, architecture, the arts and 
urbanism. Solà-Morales himself relies on this multidisciplinarity to formulate his 
arguments and evoke the elusive character of the notion. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
158 Interview between author and Peter Eisenman in Venice. 6 May 2014. 
159 Davidson, Cynthia. “Any(Story).” Lotus  : rivista trimestrale di architettura. Milano, 1997. 94–131.

## S. 93

90 
 
FIGURE 30: Anyplace Conference T-shirt. 1994. CCA Archives - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001. 
Accordingly, the different chapters of the book read as: “The province of 
architecture in thought”, “The installation of Architecture in science and technology”, 
“The charge of architecture in the arts” (containing the essay of Solà-Morales), 
“Establishing place in cyberspace”, “The residence of architecture in politics” and finally a 
conclusive section entitled “Locating the Architecture of Anyplace”. The introduction and 
ending of the book, written by Davidson, summarize the intentions behind the text and 
set the tone for the terrain vague discourse. The chapters in the book move between the 
various fields in a sequential manner. Architecture is first described in the thinking realm, 
i.e. relating it to philosophy and sociological fields. Then the discourse moves from this 
thought-based level to connect it to the technological advancements of the century and 
their inputs on the discussion. It then goes on to include other fields within the 
discussion, such as the changing role of the arts and their influence on architecture (and 
vice versa), or the political aspect of the architectural discourse of the era. As previously 
mentioned, Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague” essay was published within the third chapter 
of the book, which touches on architectural affiliations within the artistic domain. In 
contrast with other chapters, this latter tends to lure the reader’s attention to the artistic 
attributes of architecture and to the aesthetic sensibility of the domain. Yet, far from a 
stereotyped approach, this chapter seems to successfully set new trends in perceiving the 
link, and interdisciplinarity in general. 
After the Anyplace conference, one of the main criticisms expressed regarded this 
very question of theorizing architecture and the philosopher-architect link. In this 
respect, Phyllis Lambert, director of the CCA and member of the board of directors of the 
Anyone corporation, wrote about Anyplace in the Lotus magazine:

## S. 94

91 
 
"Overall I observed the difficulty architects have in speaking to philosophers and the 
hesitation with which philosophers approach architects. From the failure to formulate a 
dialogue, however, we learn to bridge concepts."160 
Along similar lines, two years earlier, writing to Anywhere, the architect Daniel 
Libeskind stated: 
"It struck me that there was a tremendous split between those architects and artists who 
are actually practicing... as opposed to the theoretical and philosophical side, which, though 
profound and extremely relevant, seemed to be totally disembodied from the stream of 
making and creating the city."161 
The controversy was thus based on the difficulty to communicate and connect 
between the different disciplines as well as the theory-practice dichotomy within those 
fields. The first section in the book Anyplace is representative of such debates within the 
Anyone corporation, and the architectural discourse of the 1990s in general. Solà-
Morales’s theorizing of the terrain vague is also considerably affected by these discussions. 
His approach seems conversely sounder since he dedicates a final section of his essay to 
the practical role of the architect in intervening in wastelands; he hence tried to bend the 
schism between architectural theory and the practice behind it. 
In conclusion, the publication of the "Terrain Vague" essay within the book 
Anyplace is befitting. Firstly, the reference to ‘any’ infers undertones of indeterminacy 
surrounding both the publication and the corporation. Secondly, the references to place 
equally suggest the territoriality of the terrain vague and the positioning of the discipline 
of architecture, both of which are pertinent ideas in the essay. The debates behind the 
corporation and the Anyplace book therefore provide the ideal backdrop to Solà-Morales’s 
essay and his discourse on the terrain vague. This relevance will become clearer in the 
following chapter as we carefully examine the structure, nuances, writing styles and other 
aspects of the "Terrain Vague" essay. 
 
 
 
 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
160 Davidson, Cynthia. “Caro Lotus” Lotus: Rivista Trimestrale di Architettura. Milano, 1997. 130–131. 
161 Davidson, Ibid. 131.

## S. 95

92 
 
 
 
 
   
CHAPTER 2 – Analyzing the "Terrain Vague" 
essay

93

## S. 99

96 
FIGURE 32: Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, 
Massachusetts: The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123.

## S. 102

99 
 
FIGURE 33: Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua 
(1996): 34–44.

## S. 107

104 
Applied method towards analyzing the essay 
The "Terrain Vague" essay is conceivably formulated in a way that leads in part to 
its consequent mythification. One aim of this chapter is to understand how the very 
composition of the essay is partially responsible for the consequent fascination, 
sublimation and mythification of the terrain vague notion. Different elements composing 
the essay provide ground for such a hypothesis. This chapter focuses thus on the essay’s 
structure and formulation. Building a resonance and a vibrant notion requires a set of 
characteristics related to its composition and its consequent diffusion; a greatly written 
essay stirs a great reception. In what follows, the composition of the essay will be 
dismantled in order to analyze how it contributes to the overall intentions behind the 
essay’s production. A tall tale at heart, a manifesto in modus operandi, the essay is 
particular in various ways. Aside from the context in which it was produced, the very 
composition of the latter sheds light on the acclaim it subsequently received and how this 
reception occurred from quoting, repetition, reflection on, rejection or celebration. 
By dismantling the essay, the attempt is to demonstrate the tale and manifesto 
hidden within the text’s structure. The essay elements hereby discussed are linked with 
the succession of thoughts followed by the author, with the argumentation throughout 
different conceptual divisions, and finally with the format in which this argumentation is 
presented. These various elements combined possibly enabled the empowerment of the 
discourse on the terrain vague and its settlement within the fields of architectural and 
urban studies. 
Another aim of the chapter is to recognize how many versions of the “Terrain 
Vague” essay exist, and which version is the most acknowledged and quoted. Taken often 
as sole origin of the discourse, repeatedly quoted word by word, the piece by Solà-Morales 
is nonetheless an amalgam of different versions. Between the years 1994 and 1996, but 
also in the following decades, the excerpt appeared in a variety of publications and places, 
under several formats and languages. And, while these versions of the essay are similar, 
the variations between them hold clues to understand the text and discourse. There is no 
one version considered the ultimate format, yet two publications come close: the 
publication162 in Anyplace163 in 1995 and the following publication in the Catalan journal 
Quaderns in 1996 164. These will be analyzed in depth and subsequently confronted 
                                                                                                                                                                        
162 This publication could in some way be considered the most recognized of the text's versions even though 
it was neither the first, nor the last, nor the most edited. It is simply acknowledged, even erroneously by 
Solà-Morales, as the primal version of the text. 
163 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 118–123. 
164 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Agua (1996): 
34–44.

## S. 108

105 
 
throughout the chapter in order to understand how the differences between the two shed 
light on differences in connotations of the terrain vague notion. The analysis is thus 
between discursive and literary, confronting it with other writings and more importantly 
with itself through its different versions, while in parallel dissecting the text into its basic 
elements. 
A third underlying thread of this analysis constitutes in defining the ongoing 
notions presented, explicitly or not, within the excerpt. These form the theoretical 
support and background of the terrain vague discourse. As no discourse is self-standing; 
several complying notions are conceived and defined by the author so as to base the 
foundation of the proposed discourse. The various sections of the essay are linked with 
different disciplines and foundations, forming a network of notions and subsequent 
connotations that help build the author's arguments and coining of the terrain vague 
term. Moreover, these notions are linked with the contexts in which the terrain vague is 
situated and therefore the dismantling of this network will lead to relations between the 
given written piece, the different notions and the contexts from which they emerged. 
Various versions of the same essay 
Timeline   of   the   essay      
Before discussing the variations within the essays’ versions, one must set a 
chronology of the publications and when they emerged. The most important year in this 
timeline is 1994. Firstly, the essay was published in November, preceding the exhibition 
and seminar of the Triennale di Milano double-entitled Identità e Differenze - I Racconti 
dell'Abitare165. This version of the essay was published in Italian, translated from Solà-
Morales’s draft in Spanish, as mentioned at the end of the essay. No other future 
publication seems to mention this version. 
Secondly, Solà-Morales also presented the essay, that same year, in English at the 
Anyplace conference. The event took place in Montreal in the Canadian Center for 
Architecture during two days, June 16th to 18th 1994. The essay became thus his lecture 
theme in the 4th annual conference of the Anyone corporation. The essay was then 
published in 1995 in the Anyplace166 book, under the MIT Press167 in Cambridge. This 
                                                                                                                                                                        
165 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Triennale di Milano I Racconti dell’Abitare un Seminario. una 
Mostra.Abitare Segesta cataloghi (1994): 74–82. 
166 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
167 The MIT Press was in strong contact with the ANY group; it published many of the books that the 
members wrote throughout their lives.

## S. 109

106 
version is often considered the original publication168. That same year, the architect Luc 
Lévesque also published a re-edited version of the essay in the Inter journal under the 
title “Urbanité Interstitielle”169. It was however altered significantly from the original text 
after correspondence between Lévesque and Solà-Morales170. 
Thirdly, in 1996, Spanish, French, Catalan and English versions171 were published 
in the Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme in Barcelona172. After this point, the essay and 
notion became widely recognized. Solà-Morales was being quoted around the world and 
the notion inspired practitioners and thinkers. Accordingly, the essay was also 
republished in 1996 in a Japanese version of the book Anyplace173 published by NTT 
Publishing174. It was also the core text within the 1996 third edition175 of the Kerb Journal 
at the Royal Melbourne Institute of Technology (RMIT), Australia. 
Few years later, in 2001, the essay was again published in the book Habitats, 
Tectonics, Landscapes: Contemporary Spanish Architecture176. After Solà-Morales died in 
2001, posthumous versions of the essay appeared in various publications. In 2002, Gustavo 
Gili editorial house republished many of Solà-Morales’s essays, including the “Terrain 
Vague”, as part of a book entitled Territorios177. The latter was intended as a recollection 
of Solà-Morales’s main work within themes, one of which was ‘territories’. Then in 2007, 
Manolo Laguillo republished the "Terrain Vague" essay, in his book Barcelona 1978-
1997178, as a theoretical grounding for his photographic work on the terrains vagues of

## S. 110

107 
 
Barcelona. Letters in the archives179 show how Laguillo requested from Eulàlia Serra the 
permission to republish the essay in the book that accompanied two of his exhibitions180. 
Many of the artists' works depicted the terrains vagues of Barcelona and other cities. 
Titles within the exhibition were also Solà-Morales inspired: "From the City to the 
Metropolis", "The City of the People", "The Urban Question"181. 
In 2017, the essay was republished in the journal Center 14 On Landscape 
Urbanism182, edited by Dean Almy. In 2009, a collection of Solà-Morales’s essays, included 
the "Terrain Vague" was issued within the ANY publications titled Los Artículos de 
Any183, published by the Fundación Caja de Arquitectos under the suggestion of Solà-
Morales’s family. That same year, another book, entitled Naturaleza y Artificio, edited 
by Iñaki Ábalos184 also included the essay. Then in 2012, Igor Fracalossi translated the text 
into Portuguese185. Finally, in September 2013, the "Terrain Vague" was rereleased in the 
prelude of the book Terrain Vague: Interstices at the Edge of the Pale 186 , edited 
by Manuela Mariani and Patrick Barron; the authors took the essay as the canonical 
reference of the terrain vague notion and included it, in its entirety, at the beginning of 
their book. 
All these recurrent publications consider the essay in the book Anyplace as the 
original text and republish it as is. Yet some did not comply with it and republished, 
instead, the version of the essay published in 1996 in Quaderns, which had been 
translated into English, Catalan, Spanish and French, and was therefore better propagated 
within Spanish or French speaking areas and finally around the world. These Quaderns 
versions were thus not mirroring exactly the ‘original’ text in Anyplace, or the initially 
published essay in the Triennale di Milano. An analysis of the differences between these 
re-editions by Solà-Morales himself will be given in the following section. These

periods, put in contrast with the 1980s/1990s.
