## S. 111

108 
alterations shed light on connotations and intentions behind the essay and help clarify 
some of the ambiguities held in the excerpt. 
Comparing the essay’s versions 
The combination of three versions synthesizes thus what we know of Solà-
Morales’s “Terrain Vague” essay and notion. All other publications take as source one of 
these three. The first one, often unknown, published in 1994 in the Triennale di Milano, 
could be almost considered a first draft of the essay. The second is the original, most 
acknowledged and bold version in Anyplace in 1995. The third appears, in contrast, as an 
undertoned revision in Quaderns in Barcelona in 1996. 
 
Since the first and third are essays in journals, the format is accordingly less 
reserved, with more layout design or visual illustrations. They are both situated within a 
larger series of articles under a certain theme: the Triennale di Milano subject of “identity 
and difference”, and the Quaderns theme of “Tierra-Aigua”, Land-Water. The second 
version in Anyplace is in contrast presented in a cleaner format within a book, under the 
general theme of “anyplace”, with only one photograph as opposed to the various 
photographs of both city and terrains vagues seen in the Quaderns version. 
By the same token, unlike the book excerpt, both journal essays include preludes 
linking the article to the journal's intent. The first, in the Triennale di Milano, is preceded 
by a presentation by Solà-Morales of Marcel Duchamp's art piece “Trois Stoppages-
Étalon”, linking the temporality of this readymade to the temporality of the terrain vague 
notion, which was part of the requirements of the Triennale committee187. The third, in 
Quaderns, links the essay's ideas with the XIX Congress of the International Union of 
Architects [UIA] in Barcelona. In so doing, this last version placed the concept of terrain 
vague within an important context particularly relative to the Catalan capital and to the 
architectural and urban transformations of late 1990s. Indeed, Solà-Morales states in the 
prelude in the Quaderns version that the terrain vague is "one of the most relevant 
problematics in the relationship between architecture and the city in large metropolitan 
areas."188 
                                                                                                                                                                        
187 The artwork and its significance to the terrain vague will be discussed in the third and fourth chapters. 
188 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua (1996): 
34–44.

## S. 112

109 
 
Analyzing the essay 
Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay is constituted of several parts to which could 
be attributed different functions, enveloping membranes, and a general locus in the 
organism of his argumentation. The proposed approach to deal with the essay is thus to 
analyze how the arguments flow through the various membranes of the excerpt. Special 
attention is set on the type of connectors used to link the segments. In order to do so, a 
first step would be to dissect the essay into its different parts, and decipher objectives, 
intentions, possible hidden connotations and the objects of study. The essay version 
considered in the following analysis is mainly the one in Quaderns, as it is the last revised 
version written by the author. At any rate, the differences between the Anyplace version 
and the one in Quaderns, that concern us in what follows, appear mainly in the last two 
sections of the essay. Differences present in the first three sections do not affect the 
following text analysis. 
Then comes the question of audience; to whom are the various sections of the 
essay addressed? To which purposes? What implications could a potential audience have 
on the argumentation and style adopted in the piece? In order to answers these questions, 
to each section will be attributed a codifying number and a title, if need be. A set of 
examinations will be run on each unit of text, defining thus the subject, object, main 
argument, supporting elements and even contradictory elements if any. A brief summary 
of each section will also be helpful, so as to highlight the essential points of the essay, 
including how these parts are enveloped, how they nurture the main argument and 
whether or not they are meant to inform, influence or destabilize the readers, or to simply 
affect their standpoint. 
First section: a new metropolitan gaze - relation to photography 
In the first section of the essay, which spans from “The representation of the 
metropolis…” to “…about these seen or imagined places”, Solà-Morales approaches his 
subject as though from a side entry point. His core theme is the terrain vague, yet he 
tackles the matter with safety gloves, analyzing first photography's intake on urban and 
architectural spaces. Instead of opting to address wastelands, vague spaces or interstices 
directly, he contemplates on the shifts in perception introduced into the urban and 
architectural fields through the medium of the collage, the photomontage and the 
photograph. This approach allows the author to take safe distance from the main subject 
of terrain vague through another discipline that seems to have been until then more 
anchored in the treatment of the given topic. Solà-Morales ends this section of his essay 
with:

## S. 113

110 
“As does any other aesthetic product, photography communicates not only the 
perceptions we may accumulate of these kinds of spaces but also the affects, experiences that 
pass from the physical to the psychic, converting the vehicle of the photographic image into 
the medium through which we form value judgments about these seen or imagined places.”189 
This last sentence is particularly important as it summarizes the first set of lenses 
through which Solà-Morales intends to analyze the terrain vague. These lenses could be 
categorized into three variables: ‘aesthetic perception’, ‘affective experience’ and 
‘judgmental imagination’. ‘Aesthetic perception’ is linked with the transformations in the 
ways we perceive the urban fabric through photography and other media and the 
aesthetic values that accompany these perception modes. ‘Affective experience’ is linked 
with other forms of experiencing the city through affects and intensities. ‘Judgmental 
imagination’ is linked with experiencing the urban fabric through imagination and our 
own judgments, needs and expectations. Respectively, Solà-Morales approaches the first 
unit of the article through the first variable, moves on to the third and redraws 
connection finally back to the second. 
The first section is thus linked with aesthetic perception. Solà-Morales analyzes in 
sum the shifts in perceiving the metropolis due to a then newly acquired photographic 
sensibility. This sensibility, as he claims, was set as early as the 1970s on the non-material 
and the non-monumental within the urban, which included "empty, abandoned spaces in 
which a series of occurrences have taken place".190 Finally, deriving from this claim, he 
concludes the section by proposing the term “terrain vague”. 
We notice here a discrepancy of few decades between the publication of Solà-
Morales’s essay in 1995 and the photography he presents as being conscious of the terrain 
vague phenomenon, at least from an aesthetic perceptive point of view191. One could 
deduce a precedent-current relation. The author is in fact positioning the notion of 
terrain vague as having precedents within the artistic field of photography, and projecting 
it onto the field of architecture and urbanism. He does not merely state that the terrain 
vague is a phenomenon visible in the urban fabric; rather, he confers to it an already 
acquired position within artistic fields going more than twenty years back. Furthermore, 
considering the much more powerful position photography had acquired during this 
period within the artistic field and especially in relation to architecture, one can state that 
what Solà-Morales is interested in achieving in this first instance is the positioning of the 
notion within a time continuum, ratifying he is not the 'inventor' of the terrain vague, but 
                                                                                                                                                                        
189 Ibid. 
190 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 118–119. 
191 This delay could seem natural, as architecture is often offset in relation to the development of other 
artistic fields, mainly due to its practical physical and professional aspects. To put it simply, it takes often 
long to construct a building and modify the field of architecture and urbanism, while many artworks and 
photographs could already be circulating.

## S. 114

111 
 
rather a guide, projecting the notion onto new fields. Photography thus, as Solà-Morales is 
employing it, is mainly a tool; a metaphor allowing him to describe the new metropolitan 
gaze and the newly acquired aesthetic perceptive sensibility that followed this gaze, hence 
proving the latter to be his true object of study for this section and an entry-point for the 
second. 
A relevant aspect to be drawn from this first excerpt is the way the author 
approaches the readers and the extent of his personal presence in the essay. Initially, the 
audience of this part seems to be the urban dweller, with whom the author identifies as 
he constantly employs the form 'we' as subject of many of his sentences. This 
identification moreover is not objective; it is not a neutral analytical setting such as 'we 
conclude' or 'we observe'. On the contrary, Solà-Morales addresses in his essay the 
collective memory, the collective imagination and the collective perception of urban 
dwellers, inclusive of himself. He states, for example, how "our gaze has been constructed" 
or "our imagination shaped". Therefore, the author seeks to fully address the readers, 
engaging not only their logic in the discussion, but also their emotions, memories, 
experiences and imagination. 
Subsequently, and setting himself further away from a pure academic or analytical 
format, Solà-Morales soon retracts the 'we' in two instances into a 'me' and an 'I'. Once 
the reader is fully called upon, the author seeks detached awareness as an all-knowing 
master. The stage is set on the monologue: "yet the phenomenon that interests me 
here…", and then one sentence below: "I will denote".192 Solà-Morales could have simply 
stated: 'the phenomenon that is interesting here…' or he could have announced: 'such 
urban space which could be denoted by…'; yet he explicitly chose the personal crediting 
"such urban space, which I will denote by…" and again "phenomenon that interests 
me…"193, and in so doing claimed rights on the term, its definition, usage and relative 
characteristics and attributes. 
Solà-Morales’s contribution is most significant when seen as a re-invention of the 
term terrain vague. The phenomenon that interests us here is precisely this claim of 
authorship and its consequent hold on the notion of terrain vague. Such an authorship, 
which I will denote by the English expression 'claiming rights', assumes a significant 
status in this thesis, the most solvent sign of any authorship proclaimed on the notion and 
on what our experience of it is.194 Thus, by using the first pronoun, Solà-Morales declares 
                                                                                                                                                                        
192 Solà-Morales, Ibid. 118-119. 
193 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 119. 
194 On a more objective note, such moments of 'I' in written form that attempts at sounding analytical and 
neutral are worthy of attention; they often allude to a hidden agenda, strong statements and often-violent 
positions. As an example, Bruno Latour's moment of 'I' in “The Sociology of a Door-Closer”, a fictional 
author-in-text Jim Johnson, represents a perfect example in which the personal pronoun take on a life on its

## S. 115

112 
authorship. The patent is drawn on the nomination “terrain vague”, which becomes the 
cheer focus of the second part of the essay. 
Second section: etymological connotations 
The second section in the “Terrain Vague” essay, which extends from “It is 
impossible to capture in a single…” to “…free time, liberty.”, is relatively shorter than 
others. It constitutes nonetheless the core argument. Solà-Morales herewith succeeds in 
bringing legitimacy to the main discourse. He adopts a more scientific tone and addresses 
hence a larger audience of professionals. The adopted lens is one of a linguist or 
semiologist. Solà-Morales goes deep in an etymological analysis of the coupled terms he 
proposes. The focus in this part is on a micro-scale rather than the first macro analysis of 
urban and photographic transformations. The main topic of study in this part is finally the 
core subject of the essay. The author proceeds by analyzing the origins of both terms, 
relating them to their possible connotations, linking these back to the characteristics he 
emphasizes in the spaces he is observing. His aim is thus to coin the term terrain vague he 
had introduced, by rooting it this time within etymological origins, as opposed to 
precedents of the concept, as suggested in the first section. 
Accordingly, the author begins by denoting the languages referenced: 
"It is impossible to capture in a single English word or phrase the meaning of terrain 
vague. The French term terrain connotes a more urban quality than the English land;…".195 
He evokes both the English and French languages, situating the term in between 
the two. By so doing, he introduces a term that could be read in both languages, even if he 
accentuates its untranslatability and the importance of the French connotations. 
This bilingual adoption moreover is evidently linked to the weight of French 
theories and literature on the person of Solà-Morales and his work. When 
interviewed, Eulàlia Serra, his wife and an interior architect herself, affirms that the 
French influence on her and Solà-Morales has been a great one, as from 1970s through to 
the 1990s the French intellectual scene was central and for decades many of the learned 
Spanish would make educated excursions to the French capital in order to be inspired by 
new cultural and intellectual advances and bring them back to the Spanish spheres196. 
Solà-Morales was thus well knowledgeable in the language and the French literary 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
own, let alone a significant presence in the text. [Latour, Bruno. “Mixing Humans and Nonhumans Together: 
The Sociology of a Door-Closer.” Ecologies of Knowledge: Work and Politics in Science and Technology. Ed. 
Susan Leigh Star. Binghamton: State University of New York Press, 1995. 257–279.] 
195 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 119. 
196 Interview between author and Eulàlia Serra and Clarà de Solà-Morales in Barcelona. Spring 2013.

## S. 116

113 
 
references197, historical and contemporary. The relevance of the French influence, also 
according to Serra, had lessened in his work with a reorientation of his thought towards 
the American stream by the end of the 1980s. That said, it is evident that the French 
influence persisted on inspiring the author beyond this period. 
Another more interesting reflection here is perhaps the absence of Spanish and 
Catalan within this etymological sorting. As seen in Quaderns' republishing of the 
"Terrain Vague" essay, the author mentions explicitly under the English, French and 
Spanish published versions of the essay that the latter was “originally written in English. 
For this reason the semantic translations are between French and English.”198 One of the 
main reasons to this explicit English orientation is due to Solà-Morales’s aim of addressing 
the largest audience possible.199 Be it for Anyplace or for Quaderns, the intended audience 
is never solely the Spanish or Catalan spheres; his target is international for both 
publications. 
Had the essay been written in other circumstances, the chances it would have 
been originally in Spanish are rather high. One could imagine the notion's alternative 
future would have been fairly different. The adoption of both English and French as main 
attributed languages to the term “terrain vague”, intensified in part its importance and 
spreading. 
Referring back to the etymology of words falls thus within the quest for reaching 
the largest audience, including both the random urban dweller as well as professionals in 
relative fields. The scientific tone adopted in this segment appears quite different from the 
rather reader-identification strategy visible in the first excerpt of the essay. "We" has no 
place in this section, except for two instances: "we receive…" and "we have noted…"200 In 
these two references, what "we" represents is significantly distinct from previous 
connotations; the accent is on an anonymous identification, a neutralized form towards 
objectivity. Emotions are evidently not targeted. This section's objectivity is exceptional 
in the essay as it seems to go astray from the general emotional or poetic tone often visible 
in all other sections. The most subjective judgment here seems to be the preferential 
                                                                                                                                                                        
197 As a side note, mining for etymological rooting in itself is also often an academically appraised form of 
analysis in French academic research methods. 
198 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua (1996): 
34–44. 
199 As will be mentioned in detail in the fourth chapter, the essay was in fact first published in Italian, 
translated from Spanish. This information had been marginalized, even by the author himself, yet holds 
truth in supporting the argument that the intended audience was projected as international, and therefore 
the Italian and Spanish etymological references were probably considered less suited. 
200 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 120.

## S. 117

114 
accentuation of one meaning of the words over another, as Solà-Morales for example 
clearly favors the French references of the word 'vague' over the German ones. 
In response to addressing the larger audience, this segment of the essay seems to 
be in turn the most quoted. Both practitioners and theoreticians adopt excerpts of this 
section to support their approaches. Yet, the high rate of quoting is not only due to the 
larger addressed audience, it is also because this section presents the clearest legitimizing 
form of definition attributed to the terrain vague notion. It includes as well the array of 
connotations, negative and positive, that Solà-Morales conferred on the coined notion. 
These etymological associations could be perhaps summarized into five points: land 
affiliation, undefined territory, evocative potential, unstable movement, and blurriness or 
vagueness. A sixth connotation is also introduced in this section, yet not supported by 
direct etymological attribution. This final inference refers to the metaphoric location of 
terrain vague as being "out of the city" in the many forms,    physical and conceptual, in 
which this could be understood. 
All these attributes set by Solà-Morales allowed the notion of terrain vague to 
strive differently from other terms presented before or around the same period of time of 
the publication: "non-lieu", "dérive", "non-site", "interstitial", to count only few of the 
aforementioned terminologies201. The strength of the terrain vague notion comes on the 
one hand from this intentional duality between positive and negative positioning as 
opposed to the many negative undertones visible in other assimilated notions. On the 
other hand, the author loaded the notion with associations that proved useful for both 
theoretical reasoning on such discourse as well as practical multidisciplinary 
interventions; unlike some other coined terms falling almost entirely within just one 
discipline. 
Finally, the author ends this section by stating: 
"While the analogous terms that we have noted are generally preceded by negative 
particles (in-determinate, im-precise, un-certain), this absence of limit precisely contains the 
expectations of mobility, vagrant roving, free time, liberty."202 
Aside from reasserting the positive light shed on the notion, Solà-Morales presents 
an important final characteristic of the terrain vague, which is the 'absence of limits', thus 
linking firmly the discourse with notions such as borderlines, transition, and the like. By 
doing so, he is setting the stage for the coming fragment of the essay that deals with this 
particular loss in relation to other aspects of the terrain vague. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
201 As mentioned in the first chapter. 
202 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 120.

## S. 118

115 
 
Third section: the estranged experience of the Metropolis 
The most visible and important notion in this section of the essay, spanning from 
“The triple signification…” to “…before our city, before ourselves.”, is “strangeness”. It 
relates to the experience of dwelling in the city, from perceptual to physical. Solà-Morales 
describes this notion and its relation to the main subject, terrain vague, by exposing it to 
various interdisciplinary fields and referenced authors. He establishes thus links between 
the subject of terrain vague and the fields of philosophy and psychology through 
referencing notions of strangeness in the philosopher Odo Marquard’s work and ideas of 
uncanniness and strangeness to one’s own self in the neurologist Sigmund Freud’s 
theories, as will be described more in detail in the following chapter. Solà-Morales then 
links the terrain vague notion to the fields of social, economic, political and urban studies 
through references of alienation and estrangement in the work and theories of Françoise 
Choay, Gilles Deleuze and Julia Kristeva. 
These references seem to serve more metaphorical purposes rather than acute 
engagement with each of the referenced fields, works and authors. One reference for 
instance, Odo Marquard, was even mentioned several times and in the essay's various 
publications after that of Anyplace under the false name ‘Marquand’. Neither Solà-
Morales, nor any following publication referring to his essay, have marked the error. The 
premise here is that the relevance of 'Marquard', even if referenced on more than one 
occasion within the essay, is secondary and is merely to prove legitimacy to the notion of 
strangeness and its significance in relation to the terrain vague. 203 What applies to 
Marquard seems to also be the case of other references mentioned. None was given 
lengthy attention; the only ongoing intent in relation to all these references is how each 
handled the idea of 'feeling (not) at home in the city'204. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
203 It is almost anecdotal to mention the false name of Marquard/Marquand, yet this error proved vital in 
realizing that the original text of terrain vague was actually published in the year 1994 within the 
proceedings of the Triennale di Milano, a year before the publication of Anyplace (1995) In this original 
version of the text, in Italian, the name is spelled correctly Marquard, alluding to the fact that Solà-Morales 
indeed knew the right name of the Freud Prize winning author, yet did not know him well enough to realize 
the mistake on the other occasions in which the excerpt was republished and reedited. 
204 When speaking of notions of 'homeliness' in the terrain vague, a suggested image to visualize is an 
important scene in the movie Stalker in which the main character lies intimately and peacefully within his 
own terrain vague, the 'zone'. Stalker will be referenced in the fifth chapter as related to the architectureactivist team from Rome under the same name.

## S. 119

116 
 
FIGURE 34: “Terrain Vague” draft. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 27. Document with corrections 
(probably after the G.A.Thomson translation company). 
 
Moreover, these references were mainly used due to their interdisciplinary 
approaches; Marquard's ideas were between philosophy and psychology, Kristeva's were 
between political, economic, psychological and urban. This proves essential once again in 
understanding the approach and aim of the essay, and links the latter to its context: the 
transdisciplinary scope of the Anyplace publication and the Anyone corporation. In 
addition, a connection is set in this section between this interdisciplinarity and new 
theories regarding technological advances and globalization205. Nonetheless, the most 
apparent shift in this section becomes clear in the sentence: 
"This theme of estrangement, from the political perspective of an increasingly 
multicultural Europe with its conflicting nationalisms, with the rebirth of particularisms of 
                                                                                                                                                                        
205 We acknowledge here the negative sensitivity the notion of 'globalization' often generates among 
academic and theoretical circles in contemporary times. Yet a revision of the notion of 'globalization' should 
better be considered, as advocated by Robert Reich in his lectures and economic documentary "Inequality for 
All".[Kornbluth, Jacob. Inequality for All. 2013. Documentary Film.] According to Reich, the notion of 
globalization was "moved too fast from unknown to banality". Even though we will not go in depth of this 
issue in this thesis, one could argue that the notion of “globalization” should be constantly revised for its 
important relevance on a socio-technological level.

## S. 120

117 
 
all kinds, also ultimately leads from the political to the urban discourse. From the polis to the 
urbs."206 
These two intermittent poles of urbs and polis are the drive force of the section207. It is 
important to mention here how this section is the first moment in the essay in which the 
fields of urban and architectural studies are directly addressed. The author moves from 
the urban dweller’s own experience of strangeness to the city’s urban configuration and 
policies to situate the terrain vague as spaces of refuge from this tension between the 
dweller and the city. 
The linking paragraph between this section and the previous alludes to the urbanpolitical positioning of the terrain vague: 
"In these apparently forgotten places, the memory of the past seems to predominate over 
the present. Here only a few residual values survive, despite the total disaffection from the 
activity of the city. These strange places exist outside the city's effective circuits and 
productive structures."208 
The terrain vague is thus out of the city's active systems and in a timeline between its past 
and present with a tendency towards the past. It’s a space that recollects within its terrain 
the essence of the past and opens wide the possibilities for the present and future. Hence, 
the time element is introduced and consecutive temporal conditions of the terrain vague 
are defined: ephemerality, positioning within the present, predomination of the past on 
the overall status of the space. A running intention through the essay is to take these 
spaces as a moment in the city's evolution to be evaluated in their own terms, detached 
from the past and partially detached from the future. In a globalizing and interdisciplinary 
spirit, the causes leading to the existence of terrains vagues in the city are not particularly 
described; the latter are almost taken as given spaces, regardless of original causation. 
On another level, Solà-Morales turns back in this section into emotional 
evocations in regard to the audience. “Strangers in our own land, strangers in our city, we 
inhabitants of the metropolis…”209 Identification is once again between the author, the 
essay and the metropolis inhabitant. Even more so, Solà-Morales in this section attempts a 
more suggestive tone. He asks questions, only to answer them a moment later: the 
engagement of the audience is evident, the reader is addressed, questioned, recounted; an 
approach comparable to a play or a novel. It is no more the didactic linguist empowering 
voice of the former section. Moreover, what is sought to be provoked by the narrative of 
                                                                                                                                                                        
206 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 122. 
207 Solà-Morales links the shift to the 'rebirth of particularisms'. Although he does not elaborate on this point, 
it is probably useful to keep in mind this thought within later stages as an indicator of certain contemporary 
changes within the period in which the text was written: What are 'particularisms' and what is the author 
particularly referring to? 
208 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 120. 
209 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 121.

## S. 121

118 
this section is the emotional experience of living the estrangement of the city, mainly 
through an economic and political persona confronted, often negatively, with the city. By 
doing so, the terrain vague becomes the antidote of the ordered city. In this respect, Solà-
Morales presents a consequence: aesthetic and(or) ethical problems. This will prove to be 
a vital point for this thesis: Solà-Morales, adopting Marquard's position, identifies a 
duality between the aesthetic, which he links to experience and perception, and the ethic, 
which he links to action and intervention. Yet, he seems to identify two sides of the same 
coin. Combined, the terrain vague and the orderly city form a wholesome picture. 
Considering the time continuum, the terrain vague and the orderly city become one 
network, one fabric. 
Same could be said of another set: the alleged ‘new dweller' and the ‘historical 
dweller'210. The historical dweller, according to the author, is enlightened and democratic, 
while the new dweller is estranged and bewildered. The new dweller is torn between the 
experience of the city and the experience of the terrain vague; the two are implied by 
Solà-Morales as contradictory. The experience of the terrain vague is also presented as a 
consequence of this estrangement to one's city and one's own self, and hence the increase 
and decrease of this estrangement seem to modify the degree of importance and intensity 
of the terrain vague within the city. 
Finally, this section ends with: 
"The enthusiasm for these vacant spaces – expectant, imprecise, fluctuating – transposed 
to the urban key, reflects our strangeness in front of the world, in front of our city, before 
ourselves." 
By evoking the enthusiasm towards the terrains vague 'transposed to the urban key', Solà-
Morales hints at this enthusiasm translated into action, and hence insinuates towards the 
following section. 
Fourth section: the problematic role of the architect 
The fourth section, from “In this situation the role…” to “…loss of limits have 
established.”, presents several dilemmas that are then partially tackled in the fifth and 
final section. Both sections are related to the architect's role in the transformations of and 
on the terrain vague. The most interesting to mention here is the disquieting chasm 
between the "magic of the obsolete" Solà-Morales attributes to vacant spaces and the 
"projection of (architect's) desires" onto these spaces211. He creates two extreme poles: on 
                                                                                                                                                                        
210 The particular term "historical" is reintroduced in re-publications and renditions of the text after the 1995 
Anyplace publication where it does not appear explicitly, but is rather implied. 
211 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 122-123.

## S. 122

119 
 
the one hand the characteristics and attributes of the terrain vague, i.e. its potentiality for 
freedom and dwelling beyond the ordered urban fabric, and, on the other hand, the 
architect's empowered and colonizing actions. Solà-Morales initiates this dichotomy since 
the first sentence: 
"In this situation the role of the architect is inevitably problematic. Architecture’s 
destiny has always been colonization, the imposing of limits, order, and form."212 
The architect is hereby finally introduced into the discussion. By proposing the 
'role of the architect' as the subject under scrutiny in this section, Solà-Morales comes 
near to introducing a definition all to him of what this 'role' entails. The latter is 
denounced as 'inevitably problematic'. The negativity of the tone persists throughout the 
section, to the extent of sounding almost insulting to the architecture's profession. 
"Incapable of doing anything other than introducing violent transformations"213, the 
architect is so far presented to be as pejorative to the metropolis inhabitant as is the 
orderly city214. He or she is, according to Solà-Morales’s description, the representative of 
urban powers manifestations, of the controlling stakeholders such as the capitalist state, 
and of the real estate market. 
The audience addressed in this section is both the city inhabitants and the 
professionals and practitioners in the fields of architecture and urbanism; the former are 
being pointed towards the latter, who are in turn being accused and denounced. The 
pronoun 'we' disappears from this section, replaced by a 'they'; no identification is made 
between the author, although an architect himself, and the profession of architects; hence 
another shift in identification takes place, different once again from the second or third 
section. 
Another disparity worthy of mentioning in this regard is the attribution of 
material implementations by the architect onto the city and the terrain vague, versus the 
immaterial forces attributed to the terrain vague dweller; accentuating thus even more 
the chasm between the two opposing realms. Therefore, while this section proves smaller 
than other sections, its importance is essential as the author directs the discussion finally 
to the material and architectural problem at hand. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
212 Ibid. 122. 
213 Ibid. 122. 
214 It is important to mention here that all these terms, discourses and denominations reflect Solà-Morales’s 
own take on the fields at hand and are related and similarly supported, as will be shown in the following, in 
others of his writings such as the text "Weak Architecture" or "Present and Futures: architecture in cities" in 
which the position of architecture and the architect are represented under similar light.

## S. 123

120 
Fifth section: presenting the notion of continuity as remedy 
The final section of the essay goes from “Our culture detests the monument…” to 
“…nomadism to eroticism.” As in the previous sections, the first paragraph holds the key; 
particularly the second sentence: 
"Only an architecture of dualism, of the difference of discontinuity installed within the 
continuity of time, can stand up against the anguished aggression of technological reason, 
telematic universalism, cybernetic totalitarianism, and egalitarian and homogenizing 
terror.”215 
The author introduces the object of study of this section: proposing a solution for the 
“architect versus terrain vague” dilemma: "an architecture of dualism". Through the 
section, the proposed solution appears immaterial. "Continuity", "energies", "flows", even 
"dualism", all allude to a non-material approach to resolving the dilemma. This approach 
is similar to Deleuze and Guattari’s theories of intensities and rhizomes, such as their 
concept of striated and smooth spaces. This immateriality is both vague and open for 
interpretation. It goes, as implied in the segment, in two directions. On the one hand, a 
call for continuity is apparent, linked with transition, the 'passing of time' and 
perception216. On the other hand, the second proposed direction is one of 'difference of 
discontinuity', linked with proposing circumstances in which limits are lost and various 
dichotomies appear through attempts of preserving disturbances. Both resolution 
directions defined by Solà-Morales in relation to the terrain vague are set in contradiction 
with the clarity and distinctiveness of the ordered city, enhancing further their 
immaterial qualities. 
In contrast with this immateriality, Solà-Morales presents for the first and only 
time a tangible case study, Alexanderplatz in Berlin217. The author presents three stages in 
the history of Alexanderplatz and defines one of them as a transitional moment in which 
Alexanderplatz becomes a terrain vague. The first moment he begins describing is the 
newest of the three: a moment of “post-Stalinist”218 Alexanderplatz in times, quoting Solà-
Morales, where “the primacy of the abstractly political is converted into absolute 
                                                                                                                                                                        
215 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 123. 
216 Unlike the version of Anyplace, Solà-Morales had decided in future publications to re-write 'perceptive' in 
the sentence "the treatment of the residual city should be undertaken from a contradictory complicity that 
would not shatter the [perceptive] elements which maintain its continuity in time and space." [Solà-Morales, 
Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua (1996): 34–44.], a decision 
to clearly allude to perceptive connotations of terrain vague. This proves important as the notion itself is 
constantly debated between perception, aesthetics, and intervention in the terrain vague. 
217 This 'case study' appears only in the essay’s versions in Anyplace and at the Triennale di Milano. It was 
later on deleted from future versions, published in Quaderns for example or in the proceedings of the XIX 
International Congress in Barcelona. As lack of word count was not an issue for Quaderns, this omission 
could refer to the predicaments and growing connotations that the case of Berlin was gaining and with 
which Solà-Morales perhaps did not want to be directly connected. 
218 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 123.

## S. 124

121 
 
dominion.”219 Solà-Morales is probably here referring at the new masterplan for the square 
designed by the firm of Hans Kollhoff and Helga Timmermann published in 1993, the 
same year that Solà-Morales was writing the essay. The plan portrays indeed a high-rise, 
rather capitalist and generic, vision for the square where the economic dominion of real 
estate is evident: “Big Brother's rendering of the modern utopia.”220 
In contrast, the second moment Solà-Morales describes is an earlier phase of 
Alexanderplatz in 1945, where the square is transformed into a terrain vague due to the 
Second World War. He accentuates through his description how the war brought to light 
some of the hidden intensities, “indescribable” and “strange”221, of the square. 
The third and chronologically earliest moment is finally Mies van der Rohe’s 
photomontage of Alexanderplatz in 1928. He presents van der Rohe’s idea as exemplary of 
‘good intervention’ on a terrain vague, yet he hardly elaborates on the reasoning behind 
such a statement, other than declaring: 
“This silent artificial landscape touches the historical time of the city, yet neither cancels 
nor imitates it. Flow, force, incorporation, independence of forms, expression of the lines that 
cross the city- all find expression in Mies's visionary plan. It is beyond art that unveils new 
freedoms.”222 
Van der Rohe’s design is described by Solà-Morales as an imagined void imposed 
on an actual void, a space where forces and tensions and the invisible lines of the city are 
expressed in van der Rohe’s plan. He remains however vague on his intent in this 
description223. The only premise he postulates is that the modern movement’s “efficient 
model”224 can no longer be applied in present-day interventions on the city or in its 
liminal spaces and terrains vagues.

122

## S. 126

123

## S. 127

124 
An interesting question that comes to mind is: why is Berlin evoked when the 
author comes from Spain and the conference takes place in Canada? Clearly, the audience 
Solà-Morales is addressing, and the context of the notion to which he refers, go beyond 
the local contribution, even beyond Europe225. Berlin's urban transformations were very 
relevant to the discourse at hand in the period of the 1990s in which Solà-Morales was 
writing the given work226. The author is thus intently alluding to the contemporary urban 
discussions of his age when he proposes Berlin as a relevant case. 
Moreover, another issue appears once again in the chosen example: 
interdisciplinarity and the role of photography in the architectural discourse. Mies van 
der Rohe’s photomontage of Alexanderplatz is put on equal levels with actual real 
historical moments of the place, presented as the resolution, as an example of 
“architecture of dualism” as Solà-Morales would imagine it. It is however not the only 
moment in the text where he reestablishes the link architecture-interdisciplinarity. From 
the very beginning of the section, a polarization is set between the proposed 'architecture 
of 
dualism' 
versus 
the 
“aggression 
of 
technological 
reason 
[…] 
cybernetic 
totalitarianism.” 227 In addition, Solà-Morales alludes to the given solution as initially 
adopted from Marquard: “Marquand [SIC] proposes the notion of continuity in contrast to 
clarity […] In the same way we should treat the residual city.”228 This interdisciplinarity 
seems so anchored in his approach that it becomes ambiguous to differentiate where 
architecture begins and other disciplines dissolve, or where it is Solà-Morales’s voice or 
another philosopher's or psychologist's, especially considering he was himself 
interdisciplinary in training229. 
The relation between the notion of terrain vague and the contemporary debates 
on globalization and cyber-techno-developments is once again exposed in Solà-Morales’s 
essay; referring thus back to the context of the Anyone corporation and its conceptual 
objectives for the Anyplace conference. One would wonder how much this context 
altered the description of the notion under study, and how much it had an impact on the 
formulation of the discourse on the terrain vague. On another note, another form of 
contextualization appears more explicitly in this section, one related to cities’ histories 
and the terrains vagues’ raison d'être. As seen in the case on Alexanderplatz, by situating 
the terrain vague as a transitional moment in the timeline of cities, the reasons behind the 
existence of such spaces emerge from this very timeline. For Berlin, this meant the war 
                                                                                                                                                                        
225 As will be clarified in the fourth and fifth chapter. 
226 These urban transformations will be central in the following chapter; they hold significance in the making 
of the architectural notion of terrain vague. 
227 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 123. 
228 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 123. 
229 Solà-Morales had studied philosophy in parallel with his architecture education, he was moreover both an 
academic teacher, a practitioner, an art enthusiast, following as well the advances of scientific discoveries.

## S. 128

125 
 
and then the city’s disunion and the ultimate fall of the wall; for other cases, as mentioned 
in the previous chapter, it was diverse events altering the urban fabric, leaving leftovers 
that potentially turn into terrains vagues. Solà-Morales cites in the essay few ‘terrain 
vague 
types’: 
“industrial 
areas, 
railway 
stations, 
ports, 
unsafe 
residential 
neighborhoods…” 230 All are linked with different transformations of the city, some 
punctual and dramatic such as a war, some lengthy and seemingly more natural such as 
decay. In all cases, the terrain vague is embedded within a narrative of the city, and is 
somehow materializing its unresolved tensions. 
 
FIGURE 40: The New Face of Berlin. Newsweek v.CXIX, n.5, 3 February 1992. Newspaper article. - CCA 
Archives, Vertical Files: Berlin West. 
Referring back to the audiences whom Solà-Morales might be addressing, one 
notices once again a shift in identification. Solà-Morales writes this section and proposes 
his 'solutions' with professionals and regular dwellers in mind. He initiates the section by 
addressing the dwellers: "our culture", only to turn at the end of the essay into finally 
admitting his architect-self: "we should treat the residual city with a contradictory 
complicity…"231 The cause of this shift in audience appears to be another accompanying 
shift in Solà-Morales’s argumentation: that of the ethical position of architects and their 
                                                                                                                                                                        
230 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 120. 
231 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 123.

## S. 129

126 
role in the terrain vague. By inviting architects to give up their empowering abilities for a 
less-authoritarian role of 'accomplice', the architect is finally redeemed and is set on equal 
level as the dweller. He or she is thus no longer the representative of power structures but 
rather the bringer of hope. Solà-Morales shows here almost a change of heart in regard to 
the profession of architects, in contradiction with his use of radical words such as 
"undoubtedly" and "inevitably". In a similar attempt to reach the audience, the author 
applies rhetoric questioning: luring the reader into his reasoning, the author asks the 
important question: "How can architecture act in the terrain vague...", only to reply to it 
one sentence later "Undoubtedly, through attention to continuity...". 
One could argue that this final note of the architect's new position seems sudden 
to the essay's narrative as it appears on the one hand as a repetition of the previously 
stated architecture's potential, and on the other hand fairly detached from the preceding 
case study where no actual architect's intervention is visible beyond van der Rohe's 
photomontage. This brings questions of relevance. In this double-sided ethical positioning 
of the architect, combined with the various shifts in identification and voices in the essay, 
as well as the attributes of vagueness advocated to the terrain vague, what is the author 
really referring to when he proposes "a contradictory complicity that will not shatter the 
elements that maintain its continuity in time and space"? What are the mentioned 
elements to be maintained? How can one become the accomplice of the residual city? 
Even more so, a more important questioning revolves around the intently designed 
ambiguity of the author's articulation; to what extent was this ambiguity intentional? 
What are the roles of clarity and vagueness within the actual essay, let alone the 
presented notions? This ambiguity will prove to have important impact on the reception 
of the essay. 
Synthesizing the thread lines 
Some thread lines compose the underlying mesh of the essay. These could be 
translated into three dichotomies: clarity-vagueness, materiality-immateriality, and 
finally a dichotomy of perception-intervention. 
 
An important point to mention regarding the entire essay is the subliminal accent 
on the notion of vagueness, a notion that will prove vital for the reception of the essay 
and discourse. The theme of vagueness appears to be the essential running theme, as it 
defines the subject as well as the argumentation approach; it reflects the overall flair of 
the article. On the one hand, the author attempts at ‘vague-ing’ the terrain by introducing 
the notion of “vague” as the most important attribute of the spaces he is attempting at 
defining. Moreover, to this notion are linked all other attributes given to the terrain

## S. 130

127 
 
vague: movement, potential, form and limits. Even the attributes of the term “terrain” 
refer back to this vague-ing, denoting it thus as being “less precisely defined.”232 As seen 
above, the solutions to architects’ role in the terrain vague, proposed at the end of the 
essay, instead of clarifying, seem to allude again to the parallel vagueness of both essay 
and topic. Yet, far from being a weakness, this vague-ing appears to be working for the 
benefit of the essay, linking hence all presented notions under this tension between 
vagueness and clarity. 'Vague' could be hence claimed as the innovation brought by this 
essay. It implies potential by stating that one is not in full knowledge of the urban terrain, 
and that there is a notion of terrain still to be discovered, perceived, interpreted and 
intervened on. The potential allowed by this given vagueness is the running attraction of 
the essay. Its impact is visible on how and how much the essay was consequently received 
and acclaimed; and this on two levels: variations in interpretations and usages of the essay 
by theoreticians and practitioners, and the recurrence of 'vague' connotations within the 
reception of the essay. The notion is furthermore important in order to understand how 
the essay fits within the context of the Anyone corporation whose main argument 
embraced, as previously noted, strong interest in notions of 'undecidability'. 
A second running thread binding the essay is the dichotomy materialityimmateriality. This is particularly important in setting the relation between material 
subjects and immaterial notions. The author seems to be linking for instance ideas of 
setting scenery with issues regarding stakeholders and actors. As with vagueness, Solà-
Morales uses the tension between two opposites to set an in-between for the terrain vague 
notion. Neither material nor immaterial, he reorients our vision towards a non-empty 
perception of the terrain vague while at the same time providing a certain non-materiality 
to the experience of being in such spaces. By referring to various abstract notions, such as 
‘estrangement’ ‘elusiveness’ and ‘intensities’, and merging them with more palpable urban 
experiences and photographs, the outcome is neither on one side nor on the other, 
augmenting thus the tension previously staged rather than reaching a balanced 
compromise. Even the proposed conclusion on the essay “attention to continuity… from 
nomadism to eroticism” falls within this material-immaterial dichotomy, hence 
preserving the tension. 
Another dichotomy goes under the opposition perception-intervention: the essay 
questions the validity of intervening on a moment that should be valued through its 
perceptive qualities. The dichotomy goes on further as the author puts in contradiction 
the city's general interventions of order and control versus the perceptive and sensual 
experience of dwellers. The estranged dweller is clearly the longest addressed subject and 
audience of the essay. While shifting between several domains and perspectives on the 
discourse, the identification within the essay runs mostly in accordance with the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
232 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 119.

## S. 131

128 
metropolis dweller's standpoint. The latter is taken by the hand and guided through the 
different disciplines and notions, through a subtle narrative of spaces and experiences. 
Leaving room for imagination, inciting poetics, emotions, immateriality and magic, the 
essay addresses the child within the dweller, presenting thus a dichotomized as well as 
polarized and judgmental view, be it on the city, the architect or the terrain vague. This 
view is divided hence morally between what is sought to be the positive side, or the 
negative one. This staging sets the tone for the coming section of this chapter: the plot. 
On   interdisciplinarity   
On a final note, the entire essay is running on an interdisciplinary approach and 
intent. It is omnipresent; be it in the way different fields and professionals are involved in 
the description and analysis of the 'terrain vague phenomenon', or in the compromise 
given to the dilemma of terrain vague versus architect. These disciplines however do not 
serve similar or equal positions in relation to the discourse, as Solà-Morales sets it. 
According to him, some disciplines are opposed, such as architecture and art, politics and 
psychology, economy and sociology, while others are coupled, such as ecology and art233. 
This opposition seems often based on a controlling versus assisting relation, where one 
dominates the urban experience while the other assists and enriches it. However, in the 
case of Alexanderplatz, architects fulfill a positive position when they act as an artist: van 
der Rohe's photomontage is praised as an “art that unveils new freedoms [...] it goes from 
nomadism to eroticism.”234 Hence, architecture is redeemed in Solà-Morales’s example, 
from controlling to assisting, when merged with art or photography, i.e. through 
interdisciplinary approaches. 
This interdisciplinarity is moreover coupled with changes in scale. The author 
moves between the macro scale of urban transformations up to the micro scale of certain 
vacant spaces and their appearance within the city. These changes in scale help Solà-
Morales stage a discourse. Accordingly, scales and interdisciplinarity are necessary in 
order to relate this discourse to the period and context in which it is inscribed. With the 
transformations of the 1990s in mind, as well as the Anyone corporation's objectives and 
Solà-Morales’s own standpoint, the given essay was indeed meant to address various 
professions, incorporate different viewpoints, and be propagated in many regions235. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
233 These disciplines follow the ‘love-hate’ relation between the orderly city and the terrain vague. 
234 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 122. 
235 We will tackle context, reception and influence more in depth in the following chapters.

## S. 132

129 
 
One specification to mention here is how this essay's original audiences were of 
the Triennale di Milano236 and the Montreal Anyplace conference in the same year. The 
two vary significantly, which alludes to the effect on the essay's orientation, in the same 
lines as the interdisciplinary globalized sense presented here. Nonetheless, the audience of 
the Anyplace conference was, as mentioned before, both local and global. Cynthia 
Davidson describes in the Lotus magazine in relation to the ANY conferences' audience: 
"ANY's conferences and seminars are attended by a larger group of people, of variable 
composition, that can include representatives of the "local" culture, of the city, region, 
nation, and continent where the meeting is held."237 
Then in relation to the Anyone Corporation, she states: 
"ANY is in a position to involve local groups and situations in a process of cultural 
cosmopolitanism ‘that had already come to characterize much of the other human sciences,’ 
as Sanford Kwinter has observed […] the constitution of a ‘world-culture’ in architecture as 
well […] a melting pot for different cultures, where the research, tensions and reflections 
that emerge in particular places are brought together and can confront one another."238 
In fact, according to the Anyone corporation aims, the ANY conferences were 
destined to include professionals from various fields who would commute to the 
conferences' locations from around the world, as well as interested locals, from Canada for 
example, who would find affinities between their local conditions and the discourses 
presented in the conference. Hence, the target audience was global with a tint of locality, 
which might have conceivably had impact on the “Terrain Vague” essay and its 
argumentation. 
The author’s conscious/unconscious omissions 
Omissions are one of the essay's most important aspects; its prominence is large 
enough to be considered in many ways intentional on behalf of the author and editors. 
The most evident act of excision in the text is the bibliography; it is inexistent. None of 
the versions of the essay holds any bibliographical reference to the many exhibitions and 
authors mentioned in the essay. Be it in Quaderns, Anyplace or more recent versions, the 
essay is self-standing, almost self-referential, functioning arguably as a tall tale, i.e. a tieddown written form of an originally mouth-to-mouth storytelling, or as a manifesto, i.e. a 
self-standing published verbal declaration of intent, motives and modes of action. 
The essay also fails to mention the Mies van der Rohe Symposium that took place 
in Berlin on the 27th of March 1992, which Solà-Morales attended and which is probably 
                                                                                                                                                                        
236 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Triennale di Milano I Racconti dell’Abitare un Seminario. una 
Mostra.Abitare Segesta cataloghi (1994): 74–82. 
237 Davidson, Cynthia. “Anyone Anywhere Anytime.” Lotus  : rivista trimestrale di architettura 1997: 94–95. 
238 Ibid.

## S. 133

130 
one of the main reasons why he became fascinated during the early 1990s with notions of 
disappearance, emptiness, terrains vagues and urban transformations. In fact, the 
symposium included a good section on the presence of van der Rohe in Berlin, including 
his photomontages for Alexanderplatz, which Solà-Morales would later describe in the 
"Terrain Vague" essay. The symposium also included a presentation by Hans Kollhoff on 
the developments projected for the city, which Solà-Morales would also indirectly refer to 
in his essay. The symposium moreover was intended as a reflection on the urban future of 
Berlin and its terrains vagues due to the fall of the wall. As the organizer Fritz Neumeyer 
described, in a letter to Solà-Morales on the 5th of March 1992: 
“Today, after the reunion of Berlin, the question of density becomes an urban issue 
again. In this context, the question of a realization of Mies’s vision emerges at the place of its 
origin.”239 
Beside the urban transformations of Barcelona due to the 1992 Olympic games, as 
we will describe in the fourth chapter, it was probably Berlin’s transformations that 
propelled Solà-Morales to write on the terrain vague. The symposium, although not 
mentioned in the essay, was also of strong impact on Solà-Morales’s theoretical 
preoccupations. 
 
FIGURE 41: Mies van Der Rohe Symposium. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 22, 1992. Program 
pamphlet, handouts. 
 
In a similar fashion, the author also intently distances his essay from other 
architectural texts and projects of a previous or contemporary period dealing with similar 
                                                                                                                                                                        
239 Letter: Fritz Neumeyer to Solà-Morales - 5 March 1992. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 22.

## S. 134

131 
 
topics, such as Koolhaas’s "Imagine Nothingness"240 related to notions of void for the 
Melun-Sénart project, or Peter et Alison Smithson's "The Space Between"241 published in 
1974 and related to notions of interstitiality, or Paul Virilio's Esthétique de la 
Disparition242 in 1989 related to notions of disappearance and the dimensions of space as 
fugitive momentary apparitions of speed and light, or even the article of Eduard Bru 
entitled "The Urban Void"243 of the same year related to the unresolved urban tensions 
that create voids in the city. 
This distancing creates a self-standing article, which Solà-Morales seems to have 
intended; yet it also presents the notion of terrain vague as disengaged from other notions 
of homologous resonance regarding urban vacancy, interstitiality and nihility. Perhaps 
the attempt is one of detaching the discourse from undesirable connotations, in order to 
freely choreograph it within an unmarked set perspective. In so doing, the networks of 
ideas leading to the essay become hardly traceable, as though they do not exist. 
Accordingly, a question arises: Was Solà-Morales the 'inventor' of the term? The question 
is put in debate in length through this analysis, yet it is important to remind of the 
recurrence of the expression “terrain vague” in some precedent moments, as described in 
the first chapter. Nonetheless, while these earlier allusions to the terrain vague provide 
some indices of the term's connotations, none attempted at theorizing its meaning beyond 
its aesthetic significance or projected purport. Solà-Morales does not mention any of these 
references and instead ‘coins’ the term within the theoretical architectural and urban 
domains aforementioned; perhaps due to a lack of previous architectural references and 
usages within the essay, but more probably, and more importantly, to accentuate his own 
authorship. 
The terrain vague notion is thus portrayed unsupported by any other structure 
except the qualities and characteristics presented within the essay. These however 
constitute only one side of the coin: a rather promising outlook on such spaces and their 
essential status as place of refuge from the orderly metropolis. The notion is hence exempt 
from most negative overtones, only rather neutral ones are still tolerated such as "terrain”, 
“void" or "absence"; i.e. lacking both evident positivity or negativity. The essay is kept in 
close proximity with the given term terrain vague in order to dismiss all other possible 
translations that hold certain pessimism within their very definition: [waste]land, 
[derelict] space, [un]determined space, [non]-lieu, [non]-site, to count but the most 
                                                                                                                                                                        
240 Originally published in: Koolhaas, Rem. “To Imagine Nothingness.” L’architecture d’Aujourd’hui Apr. 
1985 : LXVII. 
241 Smithson, Peter, and Alison Smithson. “The Space Between.” Oppositions 4 (1974): 76–78. 
242 Virilio, Paul. Esthétique de la Disparition. Paris: Éditions Galilée, 1989. 
243 Bru, Eduard. “The Urban Void.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 183 Ciutat I projecte/ City and 
project (1989): 50–53.

## S. 135

132 
prominent. The terrain vague is thus set within a rather neutral to positive and potentialvibrant scope. 
By the same token, Solà-Morales also abstains from mentioning cinematic 
references that confronted themes of wastelands and desolate terrains mainly from the 
1960s to the 1980s, such as Michelangelo Antonioni’s Il Deserto Rosso (1964), Marcel 
Carné’s Terrain Vague (1960), Andrei Tarkovsky’s Stalker (1979), Pier Paolo Pasolini’s 
Uccellacci E Uccellini (1969), Federico Fellini’s Le notti di Cabiria (1957), or Wim 
Wenders’ Der Himmel über Berlin (1987). These films often depict terrains vagues by 
revealing their inner processes, by exaggerating their essence, or illuminating their 
transformations. From Carné’s delinquents, to Pasolini’s wandering man and son, to 
Antonioni’s isolated housewife, to Wenders’ invisible strolling angels, these films also 
reveal how these spaces interact and affect the characters who dwell in and around them. 
Although Solà-Morales refers to photography and cinema as the precursors to the subject 
of the terrain vague within architecture, he does not name any film in particular, 
probably for the same aforesaid reasons of claiming ownership on the notion. These films 
also often shed a melancholic and forsaken air on the spaces they expose, which is again a 
negativity that Solà-Morales is essentially stirring away from in his account of the terrain 
vague. 
Nonetheless, by keeping one eye shut on the negative side of such spaces, the risk 
of these turning into a threat rather than an opportunity becomes unexplored, hidden 
behind layers of sublimation and fascination. The danger of the 'story untold' becomes 
then imminent, as is the case with any radically positive or negative positioning of an 
ideology or a manifesto, as the risks of the dissented image of the declared perspective 
remains understudied. To put it simply, the terrain vague could turn into the space of 
danger and mayhem where a trespasser is heavily punished244. 
Even the solution given in the last section is wrapped within this stream of 
positive undecidability. Its ambiguity and lack of precision have consequences on the 
notion’s reception. The essay stands without a clear conclusive point except for implying 
potential for an alternative mode of perception and intervention. Hence, rather than 
radically answering questions as might seem the case at first glance, the author poses 
questions to be debated on. The rhetoric questioning, as we have seen previously, guides 
the reader erroneously into what seems to be clear-cut answers to the terrain vague 
dilemma, yet it soon becomes evident that answers given to the questions, such as 
"undoubtedly through an architecture of dualism", are far from being concrete or 
satisfying. Thus the ambiguity of the essay's impact on the architect's role might possibly 
                                                                                                                                                                        
244 West Side Story is an appropriate example of terrains vagues gone bad, where the vacant lot is the location 
of crisis and the 'wasteland' is a space of conflict and violent dispute.

## S. 136

133 
 
be a purposely-orchestrated position, leaving room for interpretation and future 
renderings. 
Solà-Morales’s style of writing is moreover beyond this essay’s limits. His openended questioning on a theoretical discourse is a recurrent approach in his essays. This 
style is plausibly influenced by the author’s affinity, as heretofore mentioned, to Walter 
Benjamin’s open-ended style of writing. His aim is to open the debate and create a 
discourse, leaving to others and their own interpretations the indulgence of closure. 
 
FIGURE 42: Carné, Marcel. Terrain Vague. Cinédis, 1960. Film. 
 
FIGURE 43: Antonioni, Michelangelo. Il Deserto Rosso. Rizzoli, 1964. Film.

## S. 137

134 
 
FIGURE 44: Pasolini, Pier Paolo. Uccellacci E Uccellini. Arco Film, 1969. Film. 
 
The presence of a narrative construct in the essay 
‘Once upon a time in a faraway land...’, this is how one could perhaps introduce 
the plot adopted by Ignasi de Solà-Morales in his essay “Terrain Vague”. Far from being a 
literary text, and certainly not attempting consciously to be one, the essay nonetheless 
includes various narrative components. These seem to allude with refined subtlety to 
folktales, vacillating between a legend, i.e. historically grounded, and a tall tale, i.e. a 
fictional tale yet set in familiar settings. The reference here is to the essay's plot and its 
elements, from characters to setting description, writing style and a narrative sequence of 
events. In respect, both definitions of the word “plot” are significant, as on the one hand 
referring to 'a secretive plan of action' and on the other hand evoking 'the main elements 
and sequence of interrelated events devised for a play or similar work'245. In this sense, a 
double layer is corresponding to the essay as both 'plotting' a course of action and 
'plotting' a narrative; the author recounts a ‘tale’ that ends with a manifesto-like call for 
action. In what follows, both connotations will serve as the underlying thread linking all 
the elements of the essay together, proving hence in retrospect the legitimacy of their 
presence while disseminating their composition. The second definition, ‘plotting a 
narrative’, holds nonetheless high priority in the unfolding of the essay’s synopsis as it is 
                                                                                                                                                                        
245 “Plot: Definition of Plot in Oxford Dictionary (British & World English).” Oxford dictionary. Oxford 
University Press. Web. 1 Nov. 2013. <http://www.oxforddictionaries.com/definition/english/plot>

## S. 138

135 
 
directly linked with the element of time crucial in Solà-Morales’s essay and 
argumentation. Moreover, the notion of “narrative” is one of the main interests in this 
chapter as pertaining to certain qualities that had repercussions of how the notion of 
terrain vague was introduced and how it developed into a concept and discourse in the 
architectural and urban fields. 
Similar thus to a folktale, the plot in Solà-Morales’s essay is visible through many 
aspects traditionally indicative of tales. These could be summarized under six elements 
essential for the underlying narrative of the essay, as well as for Solà-Morales’s reasoning. 
They can be labeled as such: “the ethical layer”, “symbolic characters”, “enchanted 
settings”, “the narrative sequence of events”, “the element of enchantment” and finally 
“the positive disentanglement”. Each of these characteristics holds a certain key to 
decipher the essay, and as a consequence, how the notion of terrain vague was introduced 
in the field of urban studies and architecture. The embedded 'tale' undertones hold hence 
repercussions on the reception of the essay. In fact, as will be elaborated further on, the 
essay was often directly quoted, constantly repeated among publications of theoreticians 
as well as practicing architects. The terrain vague notion became intimately linked with 
Solà-Morales’s essay that received, as did the notion, a status of fascination. 
Ethical narrative layer 
Just as the tale sets divisions in storytelling between the main characters and the 
threats they face, Solà-Morales equally divides his essay into a righteous side and a sinful 
one. As previously mentioned, polarization is key to understanding his argumentation. 
This matter has repercussions on the ethical layering that alludes to the tall tale. The 
ethical ambiguity is visible when the threat becomes inherent within the dweller's psyche 
and salvation becomes linked with the architect. On this account, the dweller is 'stranger 
to his or her own self' and seeks refuge from this self-estrangement, whereas the architect 
is urged to work against his or her own demons and towards salvation. 
The tone is thus set on narration, i.e. the disposition and respective sequel of 
events. Once the characters defined and the context staged, comes the actual plot, the 
storyline. The time sequel in this respect is important. We can mention here two 
important layers: the events sequence within the essay's narration, and the sequence of 
events proposed by the essay as leading to the existence of the main character, i.e. the 
terrain vague. 
On the one hand, the essay follows a structure in which at first the metropolis is 
analyzed, followed by a presentation of the terrain vague, then an analysis of the 
metropolis dweller, an analysis of the threats the dweller is going through, including the

## S. 139

136 
problematic role of the architect in these threats, to finally reach a conclusion linked with 
a possible resolution of these threats. Evidently, this sequel corresponds a great deal to the 
sequence of a tall tale where a descriptive section is followed by the development of the 
characters, abruptly disturbed by a threat situation, to be finally resolved into an oftenpositive culmination. 
On the other hand, Solà-Morales proposed the terrain vague as an outcome 'after a 
sequel of events'. The causes behind the creation of terrains vagues, according to Solà-
Morales’s essay, are often linked with various forms of crisis. From the crisis comes the 
need for escape; and in parallel the terrain vague emerges and is taken as an answer to the 
crisis that holds potential for resolving the tensions created by it. This parallel duality 
between the essay's plot and that of the terrain vague’s formation presents an indicator to 
the importance of narrative in defining the terrain vague and in presenting the notion as 
intimately linked with time and transition246. 
The polarization process in the essay is also better substantiated when discussed in 
terms of binding the different notions of the essay together. Inherent dichotomies seem to 
represent the driving force behind the argumentation and the approach adopted to 
describe then formulate a new urban vision. By denoting and analyzing divisions between 
order and chaos, the self and the strange, and most importantly the vague and the distinct, 
Solà-Morales approaches the ideas he presents from clean-cut positions. Whether 
discussing the 'new metropolis', the 'role of the architect' or suggesting possible 
interventions in terrain vague, the author seems to be constantly with a certain position, 
against another. This approach delegates to the essay a certain power in the positions held 
and make it distinctively appealing to the reader, as it assimilates this attitude of 'plotting' 
merged with a judgmental positioning that moves the essay away from objectivity. 
Nonetheless, an intently designed vagary still appears in this strong positioning. Ethics are 
for instance coupled with aesthetics, continuity is opposed to distinctiveness; 
contradictions are not always legitimized as clear oppositions. Solà-Morales sets first clear 
positions towards the various notions he presents to then almost propose a contradiction 
to his own position going hand in hand with a constant vagueness visible throughout the 
essay. The storyline keeps large room for imagination: Who is the weak dweller? What is 
in effect the terrain vague? Who is trying to save it? More importantly: the architects' 
ethical position is intently fluctuating between both poles, they are condemned, then 
redeemed, staged as the evil, then sent to purging. Architects are particularly compelled 
to read Solà-Morales’s essay due to this overlapping ambiguity and are henceforth 
                                                                                                                                                                        
246 In fact, the author presents the 'problem' of terrain vague as pertaining to a three-fold dilemma, a 
trilemma. Notions of transition, limit, and in-betweenness are constituents of the discourse246. Enough 
attention should be thus set on these in order to fully grasp the potential and position of terrain vague in 
relation to the urban experience. Many interventions are respectively putting attention on one or the other 
of these aspects, as seen in the chapter on influences.

## S. 140

137 
 
attracted by this vague double-attribution they are presented with. The only constants in 
the essay are hence the main character and the main stage: the dweller and his or her 
terrain vague. All other notions seem to inherit a certain ethical fluidity visible in other 
texts of Solà-Morales247. 
Fictional characters 
The storyline set in Solà-Morales’s essay could be caricatured in the following tale. 
It proves simplistic yet essential to propose such an abstraction in order to make the tall 
tale connotation more accessible. By this token, the suggested tale reads as such: 
Once upon a time, in a faraway land, existed a dungeon in a castle where lied a 
prince(ss) in distress. It was not until one day this prince(ss) found a loophole in this prison 
that (s)he was able to escape and enjoy freedom in a hidden enchanted land. Little did the 
prince(ss) know that this enchanted escape was under threat, as the evil sorcerer(ss), who had 
initially created the dungeon and locked the prince(ss), knew all about it. Soon the threat
