became imminent. The sorcerer(ss) almost succeeded in annihilating the enchanted land, 
when suddenly, out of spellbound remembrance, the sorcerer(ss) turned into the long 
awaited hero(ine) who finally rescued the prince(ss) and the entranced land, transforming it 
into an even more wonderful land. 
 
For a tale plot to function, some ingredients are necessary. A role-play takes place, 
creating polarized relations between various characters that are often simplified into a 
main character in distress, another causing the distress and finally a third solving the 
accumulated tension. Solà-Morales’s essay accordingly revolves around such three poles. 
In the third section of the essay, ‘The Estranged Experience of Metropolis’, the first 
character is staged. One could fairly state that the role of the 'prince(ss) in distress' is 
attributed to the Metropolitan dweller, while the ‘evil sorcerer(ss)’ becomes 'the 
controlling architect' whose intent is to "colonize" and "order" as Solà-Morales describes. 
Finally, the role of the ‘hero(ine)’ is also allocated to the architect on whom hope lies in 
preserving the enchanted characteristics of the enchanted land. These elements of 
plotting are adopted by Solà-Morales respectively in the third, fourth and fifth sections of 
the essay. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
247 One author even reviews this particular fluidity in Solà-Morales’s work to write an article under the title 
"L'architettura liquida di Ignasi de Solà-Morales"[Sassi, Enrico. “L’architettura Liquida di Ignasi de Solà-
Morales.” Archi: rivista svissera di architettura, ingegneria e urbanistica = Swiss review of architecture, 
engineering and urban planning (2002): 36–39.], linking notions of liquidity in Solà-Morales’s theories 
within material conditions of fluidity, connecting then this condition to a notion of time: "lo spazio è 
percepito attraverso il tempo e il tempo può essere considerato come un'esperienza spaziale", fluidity 
becomes a characteristic linked with the experience of space and of moving in space as visible in Solà-
Morales’s thought. In the given text, the fluidity attributed to definitions is thus an attribute of the 
experience of the terrain vague and the following characteristics of this experience, as no characteristic or 
definition seems stable, except for the overall vague-ing.

## S. 141

138 
The Metropolitan inhabitant is first presented in an attempt to elaborate on the 
sociocultural scope of the discourse. Connotations of uncanniness, strangeness and 
alienation are predominant. The author in this section attempts a direct connection with 
the reader, hence the use of “we”, which he evokes again few sentences further by 
adopting the voice of Julia Kristeva: “‘étrangers à nous-mêmes’ reveals individual as 
carrier…"248 The reader is thus invited to associate with the helpless anxious metropolitan 
individual. This emotional stirring seems to indicate a narrative style where the reader 
identifies with the main characters of a tale, rather than a neutral analytical style. Solà-
Morales evokes thus the sociocultural, political as well as economic layers of the terrain 
vague discourse, starting with the intimate scale of the single individual, positioning him 
or her through his or her emotional and psychological state within the larger scope, and 
the sequence of events pushing to seek the terrain vague as an alternative to the 
predominant ordered and alienating system of the city: 
"The main characteristic of the contemporary individual is anxiety regarding all that 
protects him from anxiety, the need to assimilate the negativity whose eradication is 
seemingly the social objective of political activity."249 
 
In contrast to the helpless Metropolitan dweller, the writer presents in the fourth 
section of the essay the character of the much powerful architect. While thus projecting 
the discourse on the terrain vague onto the professional practice of architects and urban 
planners, the author is compelled to subject the role of the architect to direct criticism. He 
presents the latter as "inevitably problematic", whose destiny is forever "colonization" and 
who is "striving at all costs to dissolve uncontaminated magic of obsolete". It is interesting 
here to spend a moment reflecting on the choice of the word "magic". It is the unique 
moment in the essay where reference to an intangible ethereal layer is directly stated, an 
incident that appears in other parts of the essay under more earthly notions of 'out of the 
system', 'vague' or even 'strange' or 'alternative'. By referring to such a 'magical' realm, 
the author once again speaks on a more personal level with the reader, evoking less the 
intelligible self and more the affectionate fearful dweller who is at risk in regard to the 
architect's implementations and other powers external to his or her influence. Once more, 
Solà-Morales sets the tone on self-identification. Thus the architect is portrayed in this 
fourth section of the essay as the potential ambassador of power, control and oppression. 
Nonetheless, the character of the architect is inverted in this final staging of 'the 
tale on terrain vague'. In contrast with the controlling architect, Solà-Morales introduces 
a twist to the apparently doomed fate of the terrain vague. He suggests thus a dual 
identity to the architect in which the latter would finally 'rescue', through alleged 
                                                                                                                                                                        
248 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 122. 
249 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 121.

## S. 142

139 
 
strategies of “dualism” and “continuity” as he describes, the potential of the terrain vague 
and the alternative prospect these spaces offer to the distressed dweller. Once again the 
reader and the author are joined, yet this time, it is an architect-reader who is addressed 
under a subtle 'call for action': "We should treat residual city..."250 In so doing, the first 
meaning of the term 'plot' is in motion, i.e. 'a secretive plan of action'. The subtlety and 
low profile of the proposal of intervention proposed by the writer often goes unnoticed by 
those looking into the subject of terrain vague. It also seems to be addressing only a 
fraction of the essay's audience: a selection of professionals capable and willing to 
overturn the course of the predominant system of order. Their aim would be to provide 
space for the survival of the terrain vague's qualities as described by the author and to 
adopt his alternative course of action. Far from presenting a bold conclusion, the essay's 
finale resembles though an important footnote, squeezed after yet another final reference 
to Odo Marquard. The essay as manifesto is nonetheless palpable in this call for action. 
The subtle proposal for intervention turns the essay into a subtle manifesto. 
Enchanted settings 
Tales function best when inscribed within a staging that follows the enchantment 
of the plotting; polarized spaces accompany the polarized characters. In the essay's 
context, the stage itself is the main focus. Between the “new Metropolis” on one end, as 
Solà-Morales names it, and the terrain vague on the other, the characters abovementioned are pushed to choose sides. Evidently, the controlling character is compelled 
to control, whereas the weak character is pushed to seek refuge. In respect, Solà-Morales 
presents the contemporary city in a negative light. On the one hand, it is by nature a 
space where power dominates, where control and order are keys; on the other hand, it is 
under the control of political and economic forces external to the individual dweller. In 
contrast, the terrain vague is presented as the place of refuge by excellence. It is not the 
place where one would be intimidated or urged to respect rules. The terrain vague in 
contrast presents features of positive enchantment, where being 'out of the system' 
responds to the needs for freedom and alternative refuge. It is almost a character on its 
own. One could even argue that the terrain vague is evidently the main character in the 
fairy tale, not merely a background for the others. It is the main title of the tale, and 
around it revolve other characters. In so doing, positioning it in contrast with the other 
'backdrops' is essential. 
It is important at this point to emphasize the polarization these delegated 'stages' 
encompass. They are certainly not neutral. They are also not referred to in their overall 
picture. More likely, they are presented as caricatures, as ideals, be it of negativity or 
                                                                                                                                                                        
250 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 123.

## S. 143

140 
positivity, as utopias or dystopias. From this black and white staging, the narrative tale is 
empowered. Places are no more to be viewed as literal places, rather ideals of places to be 
judged and reflected upon emotionally. Here, once again the author addresses the 
emotional and psychological state of the reader, convincing of 'fearing' certain spaces and 
urging him or her to be attracted and mesmerized by others. Whether the terrain vague is 
mesmerizing on its own becomes a question to be asked, of which the answer is: perhaps 
not; referring back to the traditional existentialist question: 'If a tree falls in a forest, and 
no one is there to see it, has it really fallen?'. The question becomes: If a terrain vague has 
no audience, no flâneurs, no spectators and no users, is it really a terrain vague or merely 
an empty space? Solà-Morales’s attempts to address directly the reader and to raise 
awareness of the terrain vague’s existence are conceivably what give this “fascination” to 
the term. 
Moreover, in this dichotomy “metropolis-terrain vague”, the second definition of 
‘plot’, i.e. as scheme or plan, reemerges. In this mobile invitation to flee the orderly city 
and run towards the disorderly wastelands, an escape plot seems to be in the making. This 
plot is intently counteracting and disrupting the system, resembling with acute subtlety a 
'revolutionary plot'. In this sense, Solà-Morales’s essay features some elements of a 
manifesto: targeted speech, polarized judgments, and invitation for action. Nonetheless, 
the narrative and sequel of positions set by the author keep rather his essay carefully out 
of the 'manifesto range' and more into a ‘talltale’ range, as he portrays the state of matter 
in a descriptive manner and renders the action proposal to a minimum. 
Enchantment and imagination 
As previously mentioned, one driving force of Solà-Morales’s essay is vagueness, a 
notion that will reappear in the thesis under a more practical light. Another force is 
possibly that of fascination, which goes hand in hand with the former. The author at 
various moments in the essay appeals directly to the imagination of the reader, referring 
to immaterial allusions of the terrain vague, the experience linked to it and the emotional 
states associated with wandering in such spaces. By alluding to the psyche, to magic, to 
fascination and to vagueness, an atmosphere of the immaterial, even the ethereal, is 
established. The terrain vague is thus portrayed within a network of immaterial 
references: in relation to the 'unheimlich' (uncanny) by Sigmund Freud when speaking of 
“estrangement”, or to the wanderings of Benjamin's flâneur when speaking of the urban 
experience, or more interestingly in relation to photography of architectural and urban 
spaces rather than direct references to architectural or urban projects. These theoretical, 
psychological or artistic depictions of the urban experience develop the reader's own 
understanding and interpretation of what the terrain vague can encompass. All this mood

## S. 144

141 
 
setting becomes more convincing when the essay is paralleled to a tale. Emotional at base, 
memories and dreams are kindled. The strange, the homely, the fascination; all lead to a 
certain understanding of 'out of the system' as being similarly 'out of the material'. The 
locution 'out' is crucial in this depiction, linked with aspiration and wonder but also 
rejection and threat, as if composing, as Solà-Morales intended, a parallel world of 
disorder, fluxes and confronted energies. 
Positive disentanglement 
Curiously, the fields of urbanism and certainly architecture are never explicitly 
mentioned in the essay until the author finally evokes the problematic role of the 
architect. Devoid from this final section, the essay could almost pass for a discussion on 
social experience, or a photographic account of contemporary transformations, or even an 
account of urban experience rather than a discourse on the derelict and its corresponding 
architectural trade, as the essay finally claims to be. The ending seems thus sudden. More 
importantly, it ends on a positive note, an unexpected turn of events251 after the author 
had radically shed a negative light on the architectural practice. This final note adds on to 
the theatricality of the essay, while preserving the sense of vagueness and immateriality 
omnipresent throughout. Solà-Morales does not mention how exactly the architect is 
expected to perform and intervene in the terrain vague, he just stops at one term: 
“continuity”. He describes it simply as an attention to the 'passing of time' and 'the 
destruction of limits'. This imprecise proposal becomes the recipe that would shift the 
ethical role of the architect. It contributes also to what makes the essay so appealing to 
practitioners, as it promises a positive end and turns the architect into the ‘hero’ who will 
preserve the endangered species that is the terrain vague. 
The catalyzing effect of vagueness proves to be present till the very end of Solà-
Morales’s argumentation. It is precisely what makes the essay so appealing, even if it is 
misunderstood at times as its frailty. One could accuse the ending of being impractical, yet 
it undeniably leaves room for the practitioner's imagination and an interval for possible 
interpretations based on immaterial criteria such as flow and energy. In this respect, the 
essay refers back to the period in which it was written. The late 20th century was 
particularly marked by such immaterial notions fed on the one hand by the rise in 
popularity of theory and debates such as globalization, networking and advances in 
technology and virtual realities, and on the other hand by corresponding theories such as 
                                                                                                                                                                        
251 The essay could thus be divided in two parts. A pre-architect and post-architect phases, the first part being 
a preamble to the crisis staged by the appearance of the architect, which is finally resolved by transgressing 
the architect's traditional role, as described by Solà-Morales.

## S. 145

142 
the rhizomatic networks of Deleuze and Guattari, which Solà-Morales even references in 
the “Terrain Vague” essay, as will be described in the third chapter. 
Notions and terms within the essay 
Dramatization appears on more than one level when discussing the terminologies 
used in the essay. Strong connotations and radical insinuations seem to be the running 
intent behind the words choices. Solà-Morales uses adjectives and adverbs falling mostly 
within this dramatization and the consecutive polarization previously mentioned. 
Photography is described for instance with adverbs such as "decisively influenced" or 
"impossible to imagine (without)" or "primordial". Similarly, urban 'reality' is described 
with adverbs such as "inevitably", "fundamentally", "permanently" or even "radically". No 
room is left for discussion in the various positions; arguments are given as de facto. Terms 
are explicitly used such as "negativity", "insecurity", "fear", "loss", "helplessness" on one 
side, in contrast with "liberty", "free", "possible", "refuge" on the other. This polarization 
paved the way towards opposing connotations as we have seen in the plot and following 
argumentation. The essay's strength emerges moreover from this polarization. 
On a different level, the essay not only portrays the coining of the term terrain 
vague, it also provides a network of other ideas from which the term emerges. In order to 
describe the notion of terrain vague, Solà-Morales introduces various new inputs: a 
specific perspective on the metropolitan gaze, the role of photography in forming this 
gaze, a specific condition of dwelling in the metropolis through its desolate spaces, the 
role of architects in the latter, and finally, a new proposal for architectural intervention 
that he defines as 'architecture of dualism'. One could safely state that what Solà-Morales 
presents was not merely a new interpretation of certain urban spaces, but rather an 
amalgam of viewpoints on art, architecture, the urban fabric, psychology, politics, and 
economy, all reinscribed within a worldview he composes, in which the terrain vague is 
the center. The motor force of the essay and of the notion derives from this network. 
Furthermore, this network, when inscribed within Solà-Morales’s thought, can be 
considered self-standing, but it can also be visualized within the larger framework of his 
writings. In fact, some of the presented ideas in the "Terrain Vague" essay are recurrent in 
Solà-Morales’s publications. For instance, the colonizing role of architecture is visible in 
his essay "Making Architecture Today"252 or his book Present and Futures: Architecture in 
Cities253, in which the author elaborates more in length on the effects of the architect's 
                                                                                                                                                                        
252 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Fer arquitectura avui = Making architecture today.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i 
urbanisme 207-209 Tríptic d’arquitectura Catalana = Triptych of Catalan architecture (1995): 180–181. 
253 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de, Xavier Costa, and Susana Landrove, eds. “Present and Futures: Architecture in 
Cities.” XIX Congress of the International Union of Architects UIA. Barcelona: Actar, 1996. 10–23.

## S. 146

143 
 
position within the ordering and control of the urban fabric, as he describes in the 
"Terrain Vague" essay in a more summarized version. Hence, the network of notions 
established in this essay can be expanded onto other essays of the author, which 
contributes to understanding each of these notions and how they define the term of 
interest. The terrain vague is in this sense not only an essay but also a conclusive point 
within a continuum and Solà-Morales’s lifespan of work. 
On an equally important note, the adoption of both English and French as main 
attributed languages to the term terrain vague, intensified in part its importance and 
spreading. One should not forget that the notion was even first introduced in the 
Anyplace conference in Canada, a country itself shared between English and French 
speakers; a coincidence that might have had influence on the serendipitous adoption of 
both languages for the introduction of the new notion. Solà-Morales might have chosen 
the etymologies with the Canadian audience of Montreal in mind. The term and notion 
ultimately became referenced almost uniquely to the given essay, the latter becoming 
thus accredited as the sole original reference to any architect, urbanist or theoretician 
interested in the notion of terrain vague254. The essay was later translated, first by Solà-
Morales then by others, in various languages aside from the 'original' English version. 
It is additionally significant to recall in this section the interplay of various scales 
in the formulation of the given discourse. Moving from a macro-scale of urban and artistic 
transformations, onto a micro-scale of etymological references and urban characteristics 
of certain urban 'space-types', then readjusting the lens towards the dual micro-macro 
scale of urban dwelling experiences, these 'zoom-in-zoom-out' schemes appear to be yet 
another strategy of empowerment through 'vague-ing' the boundaries between the 
different scales and the boundaries between space and dweller; the terrain vague and the 
escaping dweller become one entity, sharing the same tale and succumbing to the same 
fate. 
One final note on the notions and terms should be the importance of coining. As 
Christophe Girot, when interviewed, accurately puts it: 
"What is important in Solà-Morales’s article is that he explicitly names the terrain vague. 
Naming the terrain vague is probably what makes this text so important"255. 
Similarly, in an interview with theoretician and professor of Architecture 
Anthony Vidler, also colleague and friend of Solà-Morales, he describes this importance 
of coining of a term, confirming how this could be linked with Solà-Morales “picking up 
                                                                                                                                                                        
254 Interestingly, the author himself could not seemingly escape his own plot, as he republished the same text 
in various locations and in more than one occasion through the rest of his life, inducing mere minor changes 
on the actual text. 
255 Interview between author and Christophe Girot in Zurich. 11 Apr. 2013.

## S. 147

144 
what is in the air and synthesizing it into writing… He has an effect and then people say I 
thought about it.”256 
Then again, in an interview with Jordi Oliveras, Solà-Morales’s colleague and 
friend at the Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya (UPC), the professor of architecture 
describes how the "Terrain Vague" essay can be considered a result of many events 
culminating into one essay, and one term as a “kind of communication”257 to synthesize 
and hence validate these events. 
In fact, another earlier article entitled "The Urban Void" by Eduard Bru258 in 1989 
seems to come close in spirit to the given essay. Bru even quotes another earlier essay of 
Solà-Morales, yet fails to denominate the spaces he attempts at describing except in the 
elementary title: "Urban Void". The described phenomenon thus falls almost nameless. In 
contrast, the coining of terrain vague in Solà-Morales’s essay and the network of relative 
notions defined around it are the core of the essay's skeleton. 
Multiplicity of argumentation styles 
Regarding argumentation, the author does not follow a linear thread of 
hypothesis-arguments-analysis-conclusion. Instead, the author moves between several 
observations that turn around the main subject matter in order to create a realm in which 
the center subject is inscribed. The subject is not initially stated; it is rather slowly 
uncovered until the very end. It culminates by the third section of the essay into a crisis 
point, where the problematic questions are finally raised and the main problem finally 
stated: the discourse on the terrain vague and the role of the architect with respect to this 
discourse. The essay appears as a fragmented narrative in which the link between the 
different sections is only subtly implied in the text. It is not until the second section of the 
essay that the notion of terrain vague is presented, and not until the last two sections that 
it becomes directly affiliated with the urban and architectural professions. This 
multilayered argumentation setting seems to challenge a linear mono-disciplinary 
approach to the notion of terrain vague, and it rather builds up an interdisciplinary realm 
undetachable from the notion's definition and characteristics. 
If one would compare the essay to a dictionary entry, these different perspectives 
seem to be various entries defining a single term. In contrast however, a dictionary 
definition would have started with the etymological layer rather than encircling the term 
from other reference points before diving into its etymology; a standard dictionary entry 
                                                                                                                                                                        
256 Interview between author and Anthony Vidler in Zurich. 10 Mar. 2015. 
257 Interview between author and Oliveras Jordi in Barcelona. 18 Dec. 2013. 
258 Bru, Eduard. “The Urban Void.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 183 Ciutat i projecte/City and 
project (1989): 50–53.

## S. 148

145 
 
would also not have changed writing styles. Naturally, emotional involvements would be 
avoided and a consistent neutral tone would be opted for. This comparison sheds better 
light on how the writing style of the essay could not be categorized as academic or 
neutral, yet could not be that further apart either. Never explicitly stated and perhaps not 
consciously intended either, the plotting of the essay, as formerly described, falls within 
the category of literary works. Evidently, the essay's style is not deliberately literary, yet 
it is neither analytical nor technical. It thus seems to hold characteristics of both forms 
and remains in between. 
As previously indicated, far from being purely analytical, the approach in the 
essay is closer to a narrative or even a manifesto. Emotions are stirred in relation to the 
city, identification is established between author and reader, rhetoric questions are 
adopted to lure the audience in; and finally judgments and opinions are stated strongly. 
All of these indications do not situate the essay within a purely academic setting, yet it is 
not entirely out of it. References are often academic and analytical observation is still 
considered, a system of argumentation still takes place, and finally the subject itself is 
fairly field- and profession-specific. 
Upon closer examination, the text sustains changes in writing modes throughout 
the various sections. One witnesses a shift from emotional evocation, followed by a 
neutral tone in the second section, then moving back to an emotional resonance in the 
writing style of the rest of the sections. In parallel, the accent is on observation in the first 
section, then it turns analytical in the second, shifts back to observation in the third, and 
finally appears critical and preaching in the fourth and fifth section, discerning an almost 
ideology-driven approach by the end of the text. These shifts in styles of writing and 
approaches appear often sudden, with no introduction. As in a tale, the intention seems 
set on evoking surprise and fascination in the reader through these shifts in questioning 
the subject and identifying with it. 
Moreover, by comparing the essay to other texts of Solà-Morales, one realizes that 
these adopted writing styles are intentional. As we can see in similar essays written by 
Solà-Morales, such as in "Architettura Debole", the theoretician usually reflects on 
contemporary architecture in a more critical and form-oriented manner259. He often 
discussed architects and architectural projects from a theoretician and historian point of 
view. It does not seem to be Solà-Morales’s regular habit to make changes between 
different voices. These shifts appear rather as something particular to the given essay and 
were adopted intently for the latter260 in order to tackle the terrain vague from different 
                                                                                                                                                                        
259 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Weak Architecture.” Differences: Topographies of Contemporary Architecture. 
Ed. Sarah Whiting. Trans. Graham Thompson. Cambridge: MIT Press, 1999. 57–73. 
260 Moreover, the fact that the text did not change drastically between the various versions, and that it was 
published even before the Anyplace version, give clues on the fact that the writing style was mainly Solà-

## S. 149

146 
views expanding the subject’s scope. This accumulation of tones seems provocative, 
underlying consequently a manifesto-intent in Solà-Morales’s argumentation. 
The aim of the essay does not seem to be simply the elucidation of the terrain 
vague subject, but rather an intensification of it. By accumulating various references, 
styles of writing, fields of study, material and immaterial references as well as numerous 
points of view towards the subject, the discourse on the terrain vague is presented 
through this multiplicity of layers, which renders the essay dense and the notion of 
terrain vague intrinsically complex. This intensification does not blur the discourse, 
rather it employs this vagueness in orientation to the notion's benefit; various audiences 
get addressed and several arguments form a network that builds the discourse beyond a 
single viewpoint. 
 
FIGURE 45: “Terrain Vague” draft. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 27. Document with corrections 
(probably after the Thomson translation company). 
 
Variations in inflection between the essay’s versions 
Adding to the differences in argumentation styles throughout the essay, the 
author’s intonation also changes within the essay, and more importantly it changes 
between the three versions of the essay. Solà-Morales’s standpoint in the Triennale di 
Milano and in Anyplace takes on authoritative tones. In contrast, the version in Quaderns 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
Morales’s decision rather than a compromise with the Anyone corporation for instance, where the editors' 
voice was reputed for its strong impact on the orientation of the publications and conferences that emerged 
from the corporation.

## S. 150

147 
 
is markedly subdued. This is perhaps due to growing prominence of this essay’s version as 
Solà-Morales’s contribution to the UIA 1996 Congress, merged with a wiser more 
nuanced after-thought of the author. Many elements within the essay point at this 
moderation and restraint in opinion. First, the identification approach is partially 
eliminated, "we have" comes exchanged for "there is", and “we could" by "this was". 
Second, the writing style lacks in the Quaderns publication some of the zest of the 
previous, as for instance expressions are introduced such as "that is to say" and "which 
means”. Third, the affirmative 'is' and 'will' are replaced by 'seems' and 'would be' or 
'would become'. Fourth, the tone in some instances appears hesitant in the Quaderns 
publication: "If I am not mistaken, however, the evolution…"; more details are given to 
the German etymological origins under such a subtle hesitant tone as well. 
On similar grounds, the authorship established by Solà-Morales towards the 
coined notion is equally toned down in the Quaderns edition of the essay. As "we" is 
downgraded to "this", the pronoun "I" is downgraded to "we". Thus, "interests me" is 
replaced with "in which we are interested"; while the coining of the term introduced by 
“which I will denote” is rephrased into "which I should like to denote". Seemingly due to 
a rise in consciousness in Solà-Morales’s tone, he perhaps addresses the audience within 
his own context, i.e. Catalonia and Spain, with greater caution and subtlety. Nonetheless, 
even through these changes, Solà-Morales keeps hold of authorship: the "I" is not fully 
omitted. 
Another significant change occurring in the revised version within Quaderns is 
also the change in targeted audience. In the aim of addressing more broadly the UIA 
Congress in Barcelona, as well as the growing audience of the essay itself, inferences 
related to intervention and professional fields were set in the foreground, while the 
specific case study related to Alexanderplatz was omitted. Moreover, in what seems to be 
an attempt towards a manifesto within the essay, the sections of the essay were reshuffled; 
the third and fourth sections were fragmented into a larger portion of text, with more 
paragraphs and a slower pace of thought-flow. More connotations were also attributed to 
the defining section. New introduced words, such as "Margins", "leftovers", "perceptive (of 
the city's flows)", helped frame better Solà-Morales’s vision and answer some dilemmas: Is 
the terrain vague within or on the limit of the city? or Are they the grayscale of the city? 
Finally, the revised essay in Quaderns does not end with the abrupt solution 
witnessed in the former version. Instead, it concludes on a theoretically charged note 
"From nomadism to eroticism", opening thus even wider the doors for freedom of 
interpretation and the level of vagueness and enthrallment inherent in Solà-Morales’s 
discourse.

## S. 151

148 
A tale, a concealed manifesto and a political statement 
The "Terrain Vague" essay seems to be the essence, derivative and conclusion of 
a cumulus of discussions and thoughts embedded in a time and context very specific to the 
subject under study. Solà-Morales wrote the interdisciplinary and critical essay in order to 
unravel paradoxes or provoke new discussion and questions through these paradoxes. He 
thus converses, narrates, communicates a multitude of ideas and finally links ideas in 
controversial manners so as to stir reactions and raise awareness to urban dichotomies. 
The result is an essay that falls between a tale and a concealed urban and political 
manifesto. 
The "Terrain Vague" essay is seemingly written in a form that also triggered its 
wide interdisciplinary reception and its popularity among theoreticians and practitioners 
alike. The writing style throughout the essay seems to oscillate between an academic 
paper and a narrative. On the one hand, the essay provides a good theoretical basis and a 
proper definition to the terrain vague notion. It introduces the term from the field of 
photography to the fields of architecture and urbanism. It also provides an etymological, 
psychological and philosophical grounding to the notion that alters the negative 
connotations often associated with desolate spaces to a more neutral perception. The 
different sections of the essay show thus an interdisciplinarity inherent to the terrain 
vague notion as presented by the author. They also portray the diverse lenses adopted by 
Solà-Morales to present the terrain vague notion. From perceiving, to experiencing, to 
imagining the urban fabric, the idea of a tale is already visible in the use of these lenses. 
Similarly, the tale-like style also helped diffuse the essay as it provided 
simultaneously vagueness and simplicity to the ideas evoked by the author. Moreover, 
Solà-Morales’s style of writing is open-ended. Especially in his ANY essays such as the 
"Terrain Vague", this style keeps an intended window for diverse interpretations and 
developments on the notions, which allowed for the various directions that the terrain 
vague took after the essay. 
On a different yet complementary note, the subtle manifesto style hidden in Solà-
Morales’s essay also helped trigger a larger audience reaction. The essay’s argumentation 
style involves directly the reader in a manifesto-like manner that incites for action on the 
terrain vague. The word manifesto comes from the Latin manifestus meaning literally that 
may be laid hold of by the hand. Manus means hand and Festus comes from fendo: to dash 
against. Eventually it came to stand for a document, signed by the master of a vessel at the 
place of lading, which was to be exhibited. Accordingly the word manifest means 'to 
show plainly, to display, or clear to view'. The word manifesto is therefore also used for a 
public declaration of intent. Architects have used the manifesto to declare an opinion

## S. 152

149 
 
about an issue and to project that opinion into a strategy for practice. As such it gives the 
architect the opportunity to investigate on the one hand broader issues affecting 
architecture and to begin to mold a set of values and attitudes towards the field, and to 
deepen on the other hand the implications of choices specifically in regard to how those 
choices affect the perception and experience of architecture and cities. 
Accordingly, the essay acts as a subtle manifesto through dramatization, emotional 
engagement, the intimate commitment with various target audiences, identification with 
the reader, the imperative mode, an action plot, a polarization of ideas, writing in small 
prose, with a rhythm and cadence in crescendo, towards asking questions only to be 
briskly answered, and other techniques. In this fashion, Solà-Morales turns the essay into 
a tool useful to perceive the terrain vague differently, but also to intervene in such spaces. 
The author’s style moreover gives us the keys to understand the manifesto and political 
intent behind his writing:. The style of prose is sometimes anecdotal, sometimes 
professional and often moving: emotions are stirred towards urban and economic change. 
A subtle call for action and for raising awareness of the terrain vague’s importance is in 
the making. The first parts of the essay present the problem, the second parts the cause, 
and the final parts include some solutions for those looking to intervene in the terrain 
vague. The incitement to act is political. Solà-Morales stands against the given orderly city 
and raises awareness to the potential hidden in the terrain vague as an alternative to the 
hegemony of the preordained urban and political systems. The essay brings hope; hence 
its inspirational status. Additionally, the ambiguity of its conclusion allows a vast 
understanding of what ‘intervening’ entails. This vagueness is clearly reflected in the 
essay’s reception that proves as interdisciplinary and diverse in attitude as the text. The 
subtle manifesto style hidden in Solà-Morales’s essay is thus important as it helped trigger 
a larger audience reaction. The tale-like style on the other hand helped diffuse the essay 
in interdisciplinary forms as it provided simultaneously vagueness and simplicity to the 
ideas evoked by the author. Especially in his ANY essays such as the "Terrain Vague", this 
style kept an intended window for different interpretations and enabled various 
directions the terrain vague took after the essay. The author moreover presents a solution 
at the end of the essay, which helped the idea of manifesto and the vibrancy of the 
notion. Furthermore, the fact that the author coined the term is very important in 
understanding the idea of manifesto. Potential and positivity as well. Call for action was 
through these tools accentuated and secured. 
In summary, Solà-Morales’s essay seems to be a text functioning between a tale 
and a manifesto. It is from this style that the terrain vague discourse took its vibrancy. 
Various points point towards the tale, others point towards the manifesto. The 
combination of both created a vibrant essay and a vibrant discourse on the terrain vague. 
Furthermore, Solà-Morales’s form of writing between a tale and a manifesto takes on

## S. 153

150 
another meaning when considered within the tradition of hermeneutics, as well as 
Kristeva’s understanding of intertextuality. On the one hand, in hermeneutical thought, 
Solà-Morales’s ‘hidden manifesto’ could be considered as one piece of communication 
within the larger spectrum including both verbal, i.e. the essay, and non-verbal, i.e. 
actions and the context of the 1990s. Solà-Morales’s form of writing could be considered a 
subversion of traditional scientific methods in order to create this manifesto-tale structure 
that actively incites action. On the other hand, Julia Kristeva not only appears in the essay 
as a reference on urban estrangement. Her notion of intertextuality can also be key in 
understanding how the "Terrain Vague" essay is formulated, i.e. a text being an interstitial 
between numberless other texts from the past, present and even future. In fact, many of 
the notions evoked by Solà-Morales are left ajar and open for interpretation, such as the 
notions of otherness, uncanniness towards urban dwelling, rhizomatic systems and 
intensities in the urban experience. In order to close the gaps, one must take the essay 
within a continuum of Solà-Morales’s essays during this period. These links provide clues 
on how to understand and elaborate on each of the notions to finally form Solà-Morales’s 
unique theoretical urban discourse beyond the terrain vague notion, incorporating 
different viewpoints on a fluid intertextual urbanity forged by intensities and experiences 
and rhizomes. In this sense, the "Terrain Vague" essay as a tale and a manifesto gains 
importance in its ability to connect, interconnect, and to induce actions and effects. 
On similar grounds, the next chapter is about the references, written and visual, 
within the essay that develop as well a range of connotations and contexts expounding on 
what the terrain vague notion encompasses; which builds again on the essay being a 
vibrant contribution to the urban and architectural field. 
 
FIGURE 46: Solà-Morales Logo. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private library and archives. - Signature mark on many, 
if not most, of Solà-Morales’s books.

## S. 155

152 
 
 
CHAPTER 3 – Analyzing References within the 
"Terrain Vague" Essay 
 
 
 
FIGURE 47: André Malraux selecting photographs for his book Le Musée Imaginaire in his house of Boulogne 
near Paris, by Maurice Jarnoux. Paris Match, 1947. Photograph.

## S. 156

153 
 
An essay’s musée imaginaire - a thousand plateaus 
As the previous chapter was a close reading of the “Terrain Vague” essay, the 
following is an attempt to finish it and to complete the essay by developing on its 
branching references and their terrain vague nuances. The essay is construed as a 
Benjaminian flânerie strolling through references. Doors are thus open without a direct 
clear statement of the reason for their presence in the essay, or how they relate to the 
notion of terrain vague. Hence, in the same spirit of demystification, it becomes necessary 
to attempt to close the open loops, analyzing the various references, written or visual, and 
the discernible hidden voices. In so doing, the author’s musée imaginaire261 leading him to 
writing “Terrain Vague” is displayed. This step is necessary in order to understand what 
he really intends when defining the terrain vague and the different facets his essay entails. 
His portrayal of the terrain vague through visual references and written mentioning of 
theoreticians and photographers’ works is correlated with the inspirational potential of 
the essay. This parallel theoretical and visual formulation of the terrain vague partially led 
to its wide resonance among architects, intellectuals and artists of the time, and even 
today. These references give also more clues on how to understand the essay as a hidden 
political call for action on these spaces and a call to raise awareness on their value. 
Much can be deduced of the rationale behind Solà-Morales’s thought when one 
looks at the immense library in his house and his office, at the structured categories into 
which it is divided, and at the authors of whom some seem more recurrent than others. 
Gilles Deleuze and Félix Guattari seem to be an appropriate reference here; their 
philosophy greatly inspired Solà-Morales’s thought262 and style of writing263. Similar to the 
"Terrain Vague" essay, the library follows a strategy and seemingly a hierarchy of 
Deleuzian milles plateaux. One can amble through sections such as photography, cinema, 
graphic design, as well as categories such as events and publications. Likewise, the author 
adopted in the “Terrain Vague” essay the same idea of a thousand plateaus as an 
                                                                                                                                                                        
261 Usually translated into English as "the museum without walls", it is a concept introduced by André 
Malraux in mid-1940s that freed visualizing art from the confines of museum grouping. It also expanded to 
an idea of arts and literature interconnected through mind-maps. In this chapter, the aim is to identify this 
mind-map in relation to Solà-Morales’s visualization of the notion of terrain vague. 
262 When interviewed, Eulàlia Serra as well as Ernst Ferré and Xavier Costa and other scholars close to Solà-
Morales attested to the influence some French philosophers, especially Gilles Deleuze, and German 
philosophers, especially Walter Benjamin and Heidegger, had on Solà-Morales’s work. Benjamin was 
repeatedly mentioned as a major influence, his writing is also one of the most recurrent references in Solà-
Morales’s publications. 
263 Even more so, Deleuze is directly quoted in the essay: “To employ a terminology current in the aesthetics 
underlying Deleuze’s thinking, architecture would forever be on the side of the forms, of the distant, of the 
optical and the figurative, while the divided individual of the contemporary city would look for forces 
instead of forms, for the incorporated instead of the distant, for the aptic instead of the optic, the rhizomatic 
instead of the figurative.” The author presents Deleuze’s philosophy as the alternative approach architects 
could have to the ongoing fascination with form and colonization.

## S. 157

154 
organizing element, a system for presenting thoughts and interconnecting ideas, hence a 
different form for writing. Subsequently, through flânerie leading from one section, or 
plateau, of the essay to another, various references are evoked while other silent or 
hidden voices seem to linger in the background of this dense piece. Solà-Morales wanders 
through, hoping for the courtesy of the reader to connect the fragmented elements and 
their invisible ties. 
 
FIGURE 48: One of the rooms of Ignasi de Solà-Morales’s private library, 2013. 
 
A   narrative   of   accumulated   references   
The references in Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay can be categorized into 
two main types. On the one hand, the essay includes references to particular 
photographers, photomontages, exhibitions of photography, and photographs. Even the 
illustrations in the essay moved from a photograph of a space, Potsdamer Platz, to 
photographs referring to themselves, as seen in subsequent publications of the essay. On 
the other hand, another type of referencing is that of theoretical mentioning: 
philosophers, psychologists, thinkers and others, affiliated through their ideas to the 
terrain vague notion. The author deliberately links many photographers' names together, 
whilst many theoretical notions are equally tackled as an interconnected mesh. The first 
section of the essay is linked with the first type of referencing, while the third, fourth,

## S. 158

155 
 
and fifth are mainly linked with the second. This multiple referencing is indicative of the 
approach and argumentation style of the author, enthused by a Walter Benjamin 
spasmodic style of evoking and writing. The author does not follow one reference all 
through his argumentation; instead, he pays little attention to each of the references, be it 
photography or theoretical notions, and opts in contrast to merge and accumulate various 
references together. This approach seems useful in order to present the subject as a 
general phenomenon rather than simply linked to x or y. The accent is on what binds a 
variety of different references together and how one can generate meaning from this 
networking. In this sense, the essay seems close in spirit to van der Rohe’s photomontage: 
“a void in a void”264. It seems as if the author is transforming the text itself into a terrain 
vague, or one thousand and one nights of terrains vagues through one thousand and one 
photographs and one thousand and one theories. 
In a similar fashion, this accumulative approach, aside from asserting a general 
position, is closely linked to the purpose these references serve. They are used in order to 
endorse the author's argumentation while creating a narrative. They do so by setting 
metaphors. The hypothesis is: the overlay of many assimilated references, the indifferent 
shifts between them, plus the usage of various reference types and origins, all these 
strategies serve to formulate metaphors that illustrate the author's narrative of the terrain 
vague. No special attention is given to any; even some of the names are seen erroneously 
referenced. For instance, the photographer Thomas Struth is incorrectly spelled265 in the 
following publication versions within Quaderns. These errors in references' names seem 
to hint thus at their secondary position in the essay's narrative as merely illustrating 
metaphors rather than being the foreground interest of the author, or the interest of the 
essay's reception since these errors keep on being repeated without fixing 266 . The 
metaphors we are referring to are those of 'the role of photography', 'the architect' and 
'the uncanny experience of the city'. The various relative fields of these metaphors are 
mainly addressed to stage the discourse of the terrain vague, rather than being tackled in 
depth. The essay for instance does not make a study on photography and its evolution, but 
rather points at these changes as relevant to the changes in perception of terrains vagues. 
A final important point to also mention regarding referencing is, as hitherto 
observed, the lack of a bibliography. Only the used illustrations have indications of 
origins267. This seems to be once more an attempt to stray away from an academic essay 
                                                                                                                                                                        
264 Using Solà-Morales’s words. - Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. 
Cambridge, Massachusetts: The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
265 So are the names of Odo Marquard and Olivo Barbieri. 
266 Even the new publication of the text in the book Terrain Vague Interstices at the Edge of Pale published 
in 2013 includes still the incorrect names. - Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Terrain Vague: 
Interstices at the Edge of the Pale. New York: Routledge, 2013. 24–31. 
267 Probably for copyright reasons.

## S. 159

156 
and more towards a narration or even a manifesto. Freed from ideas' origins, the concept 
of terrain vague is presented as a declaration revolving around the rendered-absolute 
authorship of Solà-Morales, setting hence the references solely within the realm of 
metaphor and argumentation support. 
Theoretical references 
The theoretical references in Solà-Morales’s essay reveal the key notions 
surrounding his terrain vague discourse, mainly those of otherness, uncanniness towards 
urban dwelling, rhizomatic systems and intensities in the urban experience. 
The approach is interdisciplinary as many of the references in the essay are not 
from urbanism or architecture, even though tangent to the disciplines268. Françoise Choay, 
Gilles Deleuze and Julia Kristeva may be considered adherent to the urban field, yet their 
mentioned ideas seem to refer more to their understanding of psychosocial and perceptual 
experiences of space; none however is addressed specifically in relation to spaces such as 
vacant spaces or non-sites269. These references and notions are nonetheless reminiscent of 
the grounding, previously mentioned in the first chapter, of Solà-Morales’s terrain vague 
within the discourse on indeterminacy, architecture resistance and activism of the 1920s-
30s Dadaists and Surrealists, the 1950s-70s Situationists and 1980s Lefebvre. 
On a first level, many of the authors mentioned in Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague” 
essay could be joined through their interest in notions of otherness and uncanniness. This 
includes the theoreticians and philosophers Julia Kristeva, Odo Marquard, Sigmund Freud 
and Françoise Choay. Marquard for instance is described in the context of post-Heidegger 
hermeneutics, where a call is in due to surpass the division between experience and action 
on the urban space, and between aesthetics and ethics. This call follows the 
aforementioned Situationists’ approach to the urban experience. On a similar note, Solà-
Morales links the polis to urbs, referring to Gilles Deleuze’s work on such notions. He 
thus links the dwellers’ conflict to the outside world with their own internal conflict, 
these being put to the limelight in the indeterminacy and strangeness of spaces such as the 
terrain vague. Moreover, similar to Marquard’s “epoch of strangeness in front of the world”270, 
Kristeva is also a recurrent reference in Solà-Morales’s essay for understanding this 
uncanniness experienced by the contemporary urban dweller, both internally and 
                                                                                                                                                                        
268 In contrast, during Solà-Morales’s period, similar ideas to those presented in his essay were already 
circulating within the urban and architectural fields; none are taken into consideration in the essay. 
269 Even Mies van der Rohe is presented as a 'photomontage artist'. 
270 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123.

## S. 160

157 
 
externally. This experience is confronted in the "Terrain Vague" essay with the dual 
feelings of anxiety and liberation produced by one’s immersion in the terrain vague. 
In an analogous fashion, Françoise Choay’s work, also a reference in the "Terrain 
Vague" essay, often refers to the notion of otherness. Her work tackled a vision of a 
fragmentary globalized urbanity. Her 1970s notion of “post-urbain” 271 (post-urban) 
illustrates traditional cities in disintegration in favor or a generalized and diffused 
urbanization. Solà-Morales’s terrain vague fits in this fragmented vision where, according 
to both authors, flux takes over place. Solà-Morales’s approach seems however more 
positive than Choay’s, as he sees in these fragments potential for innovation and 
alternative communication. 
As for Freud’s theories in Civilization and its Discontents, he describes one’s 
unease to one’s own self and how feelings of uncanniness and alienation are linked. In 
this book, Freud also describes an oceanic feeling that some of his patients experienced 
which makes them feel linked to something larger than their own ego. Solà-Morales 
evokes this oceanic sentiment to describe the impression of being in the terrain vague. He 
also describes the place of the terrain vague in the city by evoking Freud’s argument that 
“the uncanny is something which ought to have remained hidden but has come to light.”272 This 
uncanniness (unheimlich) makes the terrain vague an appealing wistful space that invites 
one in and blurs the boundaries of space and linearity of time. According to Solà-Morales, 
this uncanniness is linked to our fascination with the world and our urban sense of 
dislocation and displacement witnessed since the 1990s. 
Moreover, Solà-Morales also mentions Julia Kristeva in his essay “Place: 
Permanence or Production” in 1992. He repeats his interest in urban feelings of profound 
estrangement and otherness: 
“we are strangers in our own land, as Julia Kristeva has recently written, recognizing the 
paradox that our modern ubiquity results in expulsion and exile. We are barbarians in the 
places where we are supposed to be citizens, and our art and literature return time and time 
again to the experiences of isolation and loneliness of the contemporary individual.”273 
The interest in Kristeva and such notions of estrangement and otherness are not 
momentary in Solà-Morales’s work on mid to late 1990s; they keep recurring in his work 
and constitute a good part of his urban theorizing of the contemporary city that he seems 
to envision as full of complementary dichotomies and fragments. His solution, as in his 
“Terrain Vague” essay, is to use these dichotomies, schisms, fragments and intensities 
towards beneficial innovative alternative forms of visualizing and affecting urban spaces. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
271 Merlin, Pierre, and Françoise Choay. Dictionnaire de l’urbanisme et de l’aménagement. PUF, 2010. 136. 
272 Freud, Sigmund. Civilization and Its Discontents. Great Books Foundation, 1966. 241. 
273 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Place: Permanence or Production.” Anywhere. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. New 
York: Rizzoli, 1992. 108–115.

## S. 161

158 
And on a more economic note, Solà-Morales links these theoretical and psychological 
feelings with the wave of urbanity of late capitalism where globalization is valued more 
than the local and where real estate and exchange value are higher than social and 
personal value. 
On a second level, Solà-Morales discusses the theories of Gilles Deleuze 274 in 
relation to notions of rhizomes and the forces developing the urban surrounding. As 
Deleuze and Guattari write: 
“'Unlike trees or their roots, the rhizome connects any point to any other point ... It has 
neither beginning nor end, but always a middle (milieu) from which it grows and overspills, 
[constituting] linear multiplicities.” 275 
Following Guattari and Deleuze’s theories, Solà-Morales opposes in the "Terrain 
Vague" essay the rhizomatic to the figurative, the haptic to the optic, forces to forms, the 
incorporated to the distant. He links the first to the individual and to the terrain vague, 
opposing it to the orderly system of the contemporary city. In this context, Deleuze’s 
concepts of smooth and striated spaces also come to mind. It seems that Solà-Morales’s 
terrain vague falls under smooth spaces while the ordered city is formed of striated spaces. 
The difference seems moreover to be a political and economic matter. The terrain vague 
inhabits the minorities, the refugees, the aliens, often illegal, and the nomads; while the 
controlled city holds the productive, the majority and with it the decision-making forces, 
the money, the capital. Furthermore, in Solà-Morales’s 1990s, an economic period of late 
capitalism, the problems of the millennium were starting to take form. Globalized and 
large-scale urban planning appeared on an international scale, and with it the large-scale 
forces of urban space colonization through spatial divisions. And, as the large-scale 
planning induces large-scale terrains vagues, these are hence undeniable emerging parts of 
urban planning. 
On a third level, the authors referenced in Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay 
reflect in their theories a similar sense of resistance as the one witnessed in the larger 
discourse on indeterminacy and indefinite spaces276. Françoise Choay for instance wrote 
often about the resistance of the individual against the prevailing fluxes in places, or of 
utopias and realities and resisting the problems of planning in the 19th century. 
The theoretical references in the "Terrain Vague" essay are therefore 
interconnected in the essay to portray certain impressions and ideas of the contemporary 
urban experience, fueled by estrangement and alienation. They also help situate the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
274 In fact, Deleuze was a recurrent inspiration to Solà-Morales since his early work phases, especially in his 
theorization of the interaction between repetition and difference and how he analyzed the architecture of 
Mies van der Rohe, including in the "Terrain Vague" essay. 
275 Deleuze, Gilles, and Félix Guattari. A Thousand Plateaus: Capitalism and Schizophrenia. University of 
Minnesota Press, 1987. 21-22. 
276 Mentioned in the first chapter.

## S. 162

159 
 
terrain vague within this urban imprint as a resistive fragment among the collection of 
fragments that is the city. Solà-Morales therefore proposes a theoretical interdisciplinary 
grounding for the notion and for an alternative form of understanding the urban fabric as 
a rhizome of potentially charged fragments. 
Photographic references 
When Solà-Morales presented orally for the first time the notion of terrain vague 
within the second session of the Anyplace conference at the CCA in 1994, he initiated the 
lecture by stating: 
“The title of this presentation is the French expression “Terrain Vague” and it has to do 
with the relation between cities, photography and architecture. […] I prepared some slides, 
but since there were ‘technically complication’, I think you can imagine the kind of slides 
that should be displayed.”277 
Architecture, urbanism and the art of photography form the tripod of interest. 
References to photography take hence an important role in both the definition of terrain 
vague and Solà-Morales’s argumentation. Who are the photographers mentioned in the 
“Terrain Vague” essay? What photographs are presented in the various publications of the 
essay? What do these written and visual references tell us about the notion of terrain 
vague? What roles do they play in illustrating and gradually producing the notion under 
study? What are finally the missing links between the different photographers, the notion 
and the author? 
As establishing a new discourse must necessarily be based on precedents of ideas 
and other manifestations. In this respect, the art and photography references mentioned 
in the essay are presented as precedents to the reformulated notion of terrain vague in the 
fields of architecture and urbanism. These references are situated few decades earlier than 
the given text. Some cited photographers belong even to the 20th century while the cited 
exhibitions date as early as 1955278, yet the focus is on the 1970s' 'new' photographer's gaze 
on the metropolis, as defined by the author, that portrays arguably one of the first 
intended depictions of the terrain vague in photography. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
277 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. Anyplace Conference Recordings. CCA Archives Collection - Anyone 
Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001: 1994. Video Recording. 
278 Family of Man, an exhibition organized by Edward Steichen at the Museum of Modern Art in New York.

## S. 163

160 
Quaderns   edition   versus   Anyplace   edition:   Harf   Zimmerman’s   photograph   
 
FIGURE 49: Photograph by Harf Zimmermann. Extract from “Terrain Vague” in Anyplace, 1995. 
The visual references accompanying Solà-Morales’s essay varied between one 
publication version and another. As heretofore mentioned, two publications could be 
considered the original publications that all other publications refer to: the essay within 
Anyplace279 and the one in the Quaderns 280 journal. The focus of this section will be on 
Quaderns’ version. Although Anyplace precedes in time the journal, the “Terrain Vague” 
essay in the latter is a more elaborate version with a greater emphasis on the visual. Only 
one photograph by the German photographer Harf Zimmermann accompanies the essay 
in Anyplace, while eleven photographs and artworks by American and European artists 
appear in the Quaderns edition. Moreover, as seen in Solà-Morales’s archives, these 
                                                                                                                                                                        
279 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
280 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua (1996): 
34–44.

## S. 164

161 
 
photographs were recurrent in other future publications of the essay; the theoretician also 
used them in the various lectures on terrain vague that he presented worldwide. In 
contrast, Zimmermann’s photograph was never used after Anyplace where the choice of 
the photograph was, in fact, not entirely in Solà-Morales’s hands. 
As seen in the archives of the Anyone Corporation at the CCA in Montreal, the 
author had sent slides in 1994 to the Anyplace editorial board, upon request by Cynthia 
Davidson. These slides correspond chiefly to the photographs present in the Quaderns 
version of the “Terrain Vague” essay. The editors281 opted however to request the rights to 
publish a photograph from Harf Zimmermann. In an interview with the author 282, 
Cynthia Davidson described how she was inspired to ask for this photograph from the 
New York Times and from its owner. Davidson recounted how the decision was brought 
up after noticing Zimmermann’s panoramic photographs of Berlin, published in the New 
York Times Magazine that same year. The Anyone Corporation Fonds at the CCA 
archives283 enclose correspondence from Miranda Robbins representing the editorial board 
to the Ostkreuz Agency representing Harf Zimmermann, showing interest in the artist’s 
photographs that appeared in the New York Times Magazine on February 5, 1995. These 
depictions seemed to match Solà-Morales’s discourse on the terrain vague. 
Zimmermann’s photograph in Anyplace depicts the terrains vagues around 
Potsdamer Platz. The bird’s-eye view and the panoramic span across four pages of the 
article seem to portray the terrain vague as being a wide-ranging occurring phenomenon, 
in post-wall Berlin, in post-industrial Europe, in the post-war western hemisphere, in 
general. Nonetheless it is probably the strong affiliation between Berlin and the 
photograph that triggered its disappearance from future mentions in later publications of 
the “Terrain Vague” essay, as was the case of the example in the "Terrain Vague" essay of 
Berlin’s Alexanderplatz, again present only in the Anyplace edition. Perhaps this double 
omission in future publications helped positioning Solà-Morales’s terrain vague beyond a 
mere post-wall Berlin discourse. The photograph nonetheless presents the image engraved 
in the collective memory of the period of mid-1990s, when the transformations of Berlin 
were a highlight in urban, architectural and political discussions. 
The photograph of Potsdamer Platz works moreover as an intensifier of the 
production of fascination that the author is attempting. It serves as visual definition of 
what a terrain vague could be, while at the same time presenting one terrain vague 
variety of many, portraying the only visual case study in the Anyplace edition, in black 
and white, and attempting at provoking emotional tension in the viewer. The publications 
                                                                                                                                                                        
281 Mainly Cynthia Davidson, chief editor, Miranda Robbins, assistant editor in 1995, and John Hendrix, 
editorial intern. 
282 Interview between author and Cynthia Davidson in Venice. 6 May 2014. 
283 Anyplace Files. CCA Archives - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001. Documents.

## S. 165

162 
of the essay in Quaderns follow more the system of metaphors accumulation; the adopted 
illustrations are as equally accumulated as the written mentionings within the text, to 
portray on the one hand the uncanny metropolis and to present on the other hand 
photographs of vacant spaces284. 
 
 
 
FIGURE 50: Zimmermann, Harf. Berlin 2005. in STERN - CCA Archives - Vertical Files: Berlin, 2005. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
284 As mentioned earlier, the panoramic photograph of Potsdamer Platz is omitted in these later versions of 
the essay.

## S. 166

163 
 
Three lists of photographers - Three historical contexts 
Similar to reciting ingredients in a recipe, photographers are enumerated by Solà-
Morales in a seemingly loose and unbiased manner. The following could appear at first 
glance as an extensive enumeration of photographers and artists randomly presented. 
Three organizing layers are nevertheless inherent to the enumeration. These lists of 
photographers seem to present three instances in what one could consider a terrainvague-based timeline: a prehistory, a transitory phase and an initiation phase. 
The first list, the prehistory, incorporates artists who produce photomontages and 
artworks pertaining to a ‘pre-terrain vague’ period. This list appears in the second 
paragraph of the essay: Paul Citroen, Man Ray, George Grosz and John Heartfield. Then 
the third paragraph of the essay revolves around the second list, the transitory phase: 
Edward Steichen, Magnum Photos and Cartier-Bresson, and Robert Frank. Finally, the 
third list is more specific to the description of a purported photographic new gaze that led 
to the terrain vague notion, as advocated by the author. This initiation phase appears 
further in the essay, in the 9th paragraph: John Davies, David Plowden, Thomas Struth, 
Jannes Linders, Manolo Laguillo and Olivo Barbieri. These artists were interconnected as 
well as linked in various modes to Solà-Morales. The networks, as will be presented 
below, expand the notion of terrain vague and shed different lights on the scope it can 
take and on its characteristics as described by Solà-Morales. 
Second, the visual references accompanying the essay in Quaderns follow the 
same timeline. Those were carefully chosen by the author to portray in images what 
cannot be proven with words. The first instance, prehistory, is covered by artworks of 
Kazimierz Podsadecki and Janusz Maria Brzeski. The transitory phase is illustrated with 
two photographs of Louis Faurer. While the third instance, or initiation phase, is 
represented through the photographs of Jordi Bernadó, one of which is used as the essay’s 
cover page, and of John Davies, Gilbert Fastenaekens, Alex Jordan, David Plowden, Lewis 
Baltz, Gus Blaisdell, Thomas Struth and Olivo Barbieri285. These visuals are moreover put 
in dialogue in the essay through supporting positioning or through confrontation, giving 
thus further clues into this augmented visual reality of terrains vagues. 
Third, the various publications of Solà-Morales could be conceivably read as a 
continuum. On one axis, the different essays, written by the author for the Anyone yearly 
conferences over a decade, constitute a gamut in which each essay feeds information into 
                                                                                                                                                                        
285 Many names are written wrong in the essay, such as Olivo Barbieri written Olivio or Blaisdell written 
Blaisdele, seemingly due to Solà-Morales’s flâneur attitude towards the references he chooses and due to the 
fact that the essay was mainly translated to English by G.A.Thomson from HOLOGRAF, a Catalan 
translation agency with which the author often collaborated. This is visible in the archival original typed 
drafts of many of Solà-Morales essays: the style of paper, typefont and writing layouts correspond always to 
the same agency’s corporate identity.

## S. 167

164 
the other, complementary and clarifying the different notions presented and the terms 
used. The end product appears finally as a coherent puzzle. On a second axis, Solà-
Morales wrote several essays in parallel with “Terrain Vague”. These essays provide also 
clues and clarifications to the dense essay. This double axis is hence recurrent as a tool to 
defragment the essay’s references and visuals. In the following sections, the focus is on the 
photographic references that also recur repeatedly along these two axes. Moreover, this 
given array of essays brings out hidden voices in Solà-Morales’s musée imaginaire that 
supported the “Terrain Vague” essay. Other related names thus come forth: Marcel 
Duchamp, Gabriele Basilico and El Lissitzky. These artists also follow the same timeline 
that Solà-Morales seems to stage in the essay for the terrain vague notion and the 
photographic influences on it. 
FIGURE 51: Original article to be sent to G.A.Thomson for translation. - Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - 
Box 23. Documents.

## S. 168

165 
 
First context and list of references: a politically loaded prehistory 
Photographers enumerated by Solà-Morales in the first list are recurrent in several 
other essays the author had published between the mid 1970s, with the essay “Barcelona: 
Construir Sobre lo ya Construido”286, and the year 2000 in which he published “Surface 
Inscriptions”, his last essay in the ANY publications287. Paul Citroen is for instance in the 
foreground of the references in the “Terrain Vague” essay and appears again as a visual 
reference in Solà-Morales’s essay in the publication Present and Futures  : Architecture in 
Cities288 for the UIA Congress in Barcelona in 1996. John Heartfield on the other hand is 
repeatedly evoked by Solà-Morales as key photographer within his discourse on 
transformations of bodies in time in his essay “Absent Bodies”289. Upon analyzing carefully 
the various mentioned artists and how Solà-Morales quotes them, a historical and political 
context becomes conceivably staged as preluding and embedding the discourse on the 
terrain vague. Several common points linking the artists become indicative of what the 
author is attempting to state in the first section of the “Terrain Vague” essay. 
In fact, this first list of photographers and photomontages seem to be intimately 
linked to a German historical and political context. Each of George Grosz, John Heartfield 
and Paul Citroen belong to a phase in which photomontages took on German political 
connotations, mainly anti-Nazi. They contributed in elevating the value of photography 
and collage beyond mere aesthetic considerations. One of Solà-Morales’s essays is 
particularly significant in revealing the position that the photomontages of the 1920s and 
1930s hold in the author's reasoning. In 1994, while touring in conferences in Europe and 
the United States for the promotion of the recently presented essay “Terrain Vague”, Solà-
Morales was in parallel publishing and lecturing on his second essay entitled 
“Representations: From the City Capital to the Metropolis”290. He presented the essay in 
two lectures291 of the same name at the school of architecture in Copenhagen in November 
                                                                                                                                                                        
286 Trans.: “ Barcelona, Constructing over the already constructed ”. 
Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Barcelona: Construir Sobre lo ya Construido.” Ed. José Ortega y Gasset. Revista de 
Occidente n. 97 (1971): 25–45. 
287 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Surface Inscriptions.” Anything. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Anyone Corporation, 
2001. 233–238. 
288 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de, Xavier Costa, and Susana Landrove, eds. “Present and Futures: Architecture in 
Cities.” XIX Congress of the International Union of Architects UIA. Barcelona: Actar, 1996. 10–23. 
289 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Absent Bodies.” Anybody. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, MA: The MIT 
Press, 1997. 16–26. 
290 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Representations: From the City Capital to the Metropolis.” Cities: from the 
Balloon to the Satellite. Barcelona: Centre de Arte Contemporània, Angle Editorial, 1994. 42–46. 
291 Upon invitation by Helle Juul, head of Ass.ofArch.Cph. at the Royal Academy, Sch. of Architecture. 
Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Representations: From the City Capital to the Metropolis.” School of architecture. 
Copenhagen: 1994. Conference Paper – Solà-Morales Archives.

## S. 169

166 
1994 and at Columbia University in New York in October 292. In this essay some 
photographers referenced in “Terrain Vague” are mentioned, with more detailed 
explanation of the author’s interest in them. Once more, Paul Citroen is in the foreground 
of a list of photographers followed by Man Ray and George Grosz, among other artists 
who could have been mentioned in the essay on terrain vague yet were not. 
Forgotten by most in the Spanish influential circles surrounding Solà-Morales, 
these artists are never mentioned during any of the interviews conducted with his 
colleagues and contemporaries. With the exception of Suzanne Strum 293 , neither 
contemporary professors nor disciples of Solà-Morales in Spain seem to show particular 
interest in them; an indicator of the influences behind the “Terrain Vague” essay reaching 
beyond local stimulation. 
Various depictions of terrain vague: each artist’s own imprint 
The joining of names seems to be an ongoing trend in Solà-Morales’s referencing 
when the author points at a trend or a movement in artistic and architectural 
developments rather than relying on a sole example or case study. This could also partially 
explain the lack of a single case study depicting the terrain vague notion in the Quaderns
