edition. These names expand the information given in the dense essay; a larger context 
comes to play. In this particular section, it involves a mainly German context of inbetween World Wars. 
First artist on the list, Paul Citroen was a German-born Dutch artist who lived at 
the turn of the 20th century. He studied and taught art between Berlin and Amsterdam. 
He was involved with the Bauhaus, during which period he started the collage 
“Metropolis”, in 1923, which influenced Fritz Lang to make his classic film Metropolis. 
Solà-Morales chose this collage to include in his introductory essay “Present and Futures. 
Architecture in Cities” for the UIA publication294 in 1996, an essay that turned as well very 
influential and was propagated and republished in several instances295. The same collage is 
                                                                                                                                                                        
292 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Representations: From the City Capital to the Metropolis.” Colombia University. 
New York: 1994. Conference Paper – Solà-Morales Archives. 
293 Interview between author and Suzanne Strum in Barcelona. 23 Dec. 2013. 
294 UIA96 is the XIX Congress of the International Union of Architects held from Wednesday 3 to Saturday 6 
July 1996 on the theme: Present and Futures. Architecture in Cities. in Barcelona. It was organized by Solà-
Morales and the five main themes included Terrain Vague. We will elaborate on the congress and its 
importance at the time in the following chapter. For now, it is important to mention that the publication 
linked to the UIA is largely revealing what the notion of terrain vague entailed for Solà-Morales and the link 
terrain vague-Barcelona. 
295 One of Solà-Morales’s approaches was the re-publishing of his worthy essays. This process is essential in 
understanding the reception of the notion of terrain vague, we will be discussing this in the fifth chapter on 
influence and propagation.

## S. 170

167 
 
also recurrent, retrieved from the archives296 of Solà-Morales, in the foreground of a list of 
illustrations related to terrains vagues. Citroen's piece, used repeatedly by the author, 
represents a collage of a generic Metropolis, produced in 1923, rather avant-garde for the 
period of mid-20th century of which Solà-Morales is attempting to describe the 
photography. In this collage, what interests the author, as he describes in the article 
“Capital Ciudad”297 (City Capital), is the relation between abstraction and figuration of that 
period. The artwork is nothing but innocent. Similar to the brutality seen in Picasso’s 
Guernica, the photomontage depicts an overloaded and almost brutal portrayal of a 
completely dense metropolis. The latter is presented lacking a figure-ground ratio; only a 
sepia-vs.-grayscale layering creates depth. It is also a symbolically loaded abstraction of 
urban life. Citroen was evidently an important photography reference for Solà-Morales, 
even if he did not arguably sustain a timely renowned standing298. Moreover, the fact that 
he is consistently in the foreground of the artists' enumerations, and is also referenced 
visually, puts him in a privileged position as representative of what Solà-Morales intends 
in his first enumeration: a political and historical contextualization of post-war 
photomontages preluding the 'new urban gaze' of the 1960s and 1970s that he advocates 
later on in the essay. 
On a similar note, George Grosz, the second artist on Solà-Morales’s list, was also a 
German artist at the turn of the 20th century who was best known for his artwork critical 
of Berlin life and politics in the 1920s. Painter, draftsman and illustrator, he studied at the 
Dresden Academy of Fine Arts, was involved with the Dada-Berlin movement, and took 
part in the Erste Internationale Dada-Messe. He finally developed, along with his 
colleague and friend John Heartfield who is also mentioned in the essay, the technique of 
photomontage in order to criticize the contemporary art world and to have a political 
standing. In fact, both Grosz and Heartfield changed their names during the same period 
as a political statement against German nationalism and out of a romantic enthusiasm for 
America299. Grosz ultimately left Germany and emigrated with his family to the United 
States where he became a naturalized citizen in 1938. Similar to Paul Citroen, Grosz did 
not sustain the same world renown as his colleague Heartfield or Man Ray, who were as 
well more referenced in Solà-Morales’s publications. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
296 The list of imagery is affiliated to a series of lectures conducted by Solà-Morales in Rhodes, Greece. The 
document is representative of a list of illustrations recurrent in some of Solà-Morales articles. 
297 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Representations: From the City Capital to the Metropolis.” Cities: from the 
Balloon to the Satellite. Barcelona: Centre de Arte Contemporània, Angle Editorial, 1994. 42–46. 
298 Unlike some of the other mentioned artists, Paul Citroen does not figure for instance in Spanish libraries 
nor Swiss Libraries; the artist's influence and recognition is not as spread in our subject's context as other 
mentioned artists such as Man Ray. 
299 Georg Groß became George Grosz while Helmut Herzfeld turned into the more British version John 
Heartfield. - It is interesting to mention that Solà-Morales himself, almost a century later, shared the artists' 
romantic enthusiasm for America. An enthusiasm, as will be elaborated further on, that deeply affected his 
writing and theories.

## S. 171

168 
 
 
 
FIGURE 52: Citroen, Paul. Metropolis. 1923. Photomontage.

## S. 172

169 
 
 
FIGURE 53: Grosz, George, and John Heartfield. Life and Work in Universal City, 12:05 Noon. 1919.

## S. 173

170 
As for John Heartfield, contemporary to the hitherto mentioned artists, he was 
also a pioneer in the use of art as a political weapon. In the book entitled Anyway, Solà-
Morales describes: 
“The photomontages of Heartfield or Grosz, the presentation of the urban design 
projects for Berlin by Mies van der Rohe or Hilberseimer, the relationship between the 
possibility of developing the ideas of modern architecture and authoritarian political regimes 
in Germany, Italy, the USSR, are equally indicative of the aggressiveness to be seen in 
the deployment of the new architectonic ideas.” 300 
Clearly, in Solà-Morales’s thought, violence, urban political decisions and photomontages 
are linked subjects. In his reasoning, the orderly city is constructed through violent 
colonizing of space by architects and urbanists, the urban fabric is thus solely dedicated to 
forces of production and consumption that devoid the city from spaces for free 
uncontrolled expression. Heartfield in this sense is one of his most used examples. In 
Cities: from the Balloon to the Satellite, Solà-Morales describes: 
“The photomontages by John Heartfield are, as Louis Aragon said, knives that pierce all 
hearts.”301 
The author links Heartfield's techniques of photomontage with urban destructive 
violence and means of conflict and collision. The dramatic essence inherent in the 
artworks emits emotional resonance within the observer, stirring reaction towards the 
implicit political agenda. 
The artist Heartfield was perhaps more present in the author’s thought due to one 
event contemporary to Solà-Morales’s related writings. Being famous for his anti-Nazi 
anti-Fascist statements and as a pioneer of modern photomontages, Heartfield exceeded in 
his influence beyond his initial German Berlin-based circle to reach the United Kingdom 
and the United States. Consequently, from April 15 to July 6, 1993, the second floor of the 
Museum of Modern Art in New York was the American venue for an exhibition of 
Heartfield's original montages. Being an interdisciplinary character linked with the 
American academic circles, Solà-Morales was most likely aware and particularly 
interested in this exhibition that coincides with the years he was writing articles 
referencing Heartfield's work. 
A post-war fragmented gaze 
The notion of fragmentation seems to be the link between all these artists and 
Solà-Morales’s theories on urban fabric. As he explains: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
300 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Colonization, Violence, Resistance.” Anyway. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. New York: 
Anyone Corporation, 1994. 116–123. 
301 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Representations: From the City Capital to the Metropolis.” Cities: from the 
Balloon to the Satellite. Barcelona: Centre de Arte Contemporània, Angle Editorial, 1994. 42–46.

## S. 174

171 
 
“The photomontage technique developed by Moholy-Nagy, El Lissitzky, Man Ray, Grosz 
and many other artists during the period between the Wars has the freeing of the fragmented 
image in common.”302 
 
The artistic contextualization presented by the theoretician at the beginning of his 
“Terrain Vague” essay sets political and economic values to a particular urban viewpoint, 
or the lack thereof. The notion of terrain vague is thus presented as an aftermath of the 
fragmentation of the urban gaze and becomes important as a consequence of the post-war 
political and economic agenda. As Solà-Morales states in “Representations: From the City 
Capital to the Metropolis”, 
“The view of the greater city does not have privileged points of view or emblematic 
places that represent power in an unequivocal way. Power is everywhere and the greater city 
starts to become an accumulation of unconnected parts, innovations and waste in which the 
sense of totality is only found negatively.” 303 
Solà-Morales does not perceive the city as homogeneous, but rather as a 
fragmented mosaic composition of which the terrains vagues are integral pieces. 
In this essay preluding the “Terrain Vague”, the author sets the stage to a new 
worldview where degrees of importance are inverted. Almost a critique to the destructive 
qualities of the modernization period, the latter is thought to have paced posthumously 
the way to an inversion of monumentality. The accumulation of urban elements, this 
“negatively” found sense of totality, is put in the foreground, while architectural 
monumentality and clearly defined spaces are rendered trivial. Moreover, this connection, 
between art, architecture and political urban agendas, is thought to be closely linked with 
the economy of advanced capitalism. When political power itself is ruled over by the 
power of the post-war economic market, a sense of globalized fragmentation is even more 
so plausible. This is how Citroen's photomontages become interesting, where buildings 
are stacked like products in a flee-market, merging into a dissected vision of a metropolis, 
a “fragmented condition, spread out over a territory which can only be captured from 
points of view that are way beyond conservative sight.”304 Moreover, this same notion of 
fragments seems to be an ongoing theme in Solà-Morales’s writings as it links for instance 
the “Terrain Vague” essay to the other essays he wrote for the ANY conferences. For 
instance, in the conference and book Anybody, Heartfield is once again referenced, along 
with other photomontages of László Moholy-Nagy and Raoul Hausmann in order to 
describe the freeing of the relationship between bodies and their surroundings. The 
fragmentation of bodies seems in parallel with the fragmentation of the urban fabric, 
                                                                                                                                                                        
302 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 
303 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Representations: From the City Capital to the Metropolis.” Cities: from the 
Balloon to the Satellite. Barcelona: Centre de Arte Contemporània, Angle Editorial, 1994. 42–46. 
304 Solà-Morales. Ibid.

## S. 175

172 
denoting a common wave towards a changing urban gaze in favor of a photomontaged 
metropolis. 
Most of the mentioned artists in this first list are hence German affiliated. Postwar Germany takes on front-row importance in Solà-Morales’s description. Berlin and its 
urban and artistic developments become a key example both in the essay on terrain vague 
and other publications of the author. This is already visible since 1975 when he published 
“La Arquitectura Del Expresionismo”305 in which he mentions for the first time the new 
Großstadtarchitektur and describes the Bauhaus movement in relation to the same 
photographers. The relative political and economic contexts are accordingly closely linked 
with the staging of the notion of terrain vague, validating Solà-Morales’s contemporary 
interest in this shift in urban gaze in the 1990s. As war and conflict are among the 
significant reasons for the creation of fragmentation, one particular ‘fragment’ being the 
terrain vague, the history of Berlin is particularly exemplary. It comes as no surprise 
therefore that Grosz and Heartfield took part in the city’s Dada movement, or that 
Citroen studied at the Bauhaus and Grosz at the Berlin College of Arts and Crafts. 
Moreover, in the same spirit of conflict, all these mentioned artists are entangled with the 
American-British-German confrontation in the period between the two world wars. Some 
had changed their German names to more English versions, some had immigrated to New 
York. Going back to Solà-Morales’s essay in Anyway in 1994, as referenced previously, 
the author links, as in his "Terrain Vague" essay, the photomontages to the Mies van der 
Rohe design projects for Berlin in order to prove correlation between violence, urban 
colonization, and the creation of opportunity: 
“When the ashes of Europe’s destruction invited the architects to intervene on a massive 
scale in the historic cities, the opportunity for a perfectly legitimated colonization was the 
prevailing perception of the time.” 306 
In his essay in Anybody, few years later in 1997, the message becomes clear: 
“Mechanic/organic dismembering produced the degenerate art and architecture that for 
some still represent the origin of the modern movement.” 307 
These essays seem to call hence for a turn against this ‘degenerate’ period of the 
Modern movement. Opportunity is to be seized as the outcome of conflict, fragmentation 
and discontinuity, as is equally advocated at the end of the “Terrain Vague” essay; and this 
conclusion is proposed to be widespread rather than merely connected to central 
Europe. It thus comes as expected that Solà-Morales chose to omit the direct example of 
Berlin and Mies van der Rohe's photomontages from the “Terrain Vague” essay as 
                                                                                                                                                                        
305 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “La Arquitectura Del Expresionismo.” Colegio de Arquitectos de Cataluña y 
Baleares, Barcelona: 1975. 1–24. 
306 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Colonization, Violence, Resistance.” Anyway. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. New York: 
Anyone Corporation, 1994. 116–123. 
307 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Absent Bodies.” Anybody. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, MA: The MIT 
Press, 1997. 16–26.

## S. 176

173 
 
published in the Spanish journal Quaderns. Probably aiming at a different audience from 
the one of Anyplace, Berlin's case study is omitted only to be replaced by visual references 
to more American affiliated artists or, more importantly, Spanish related imagery, such as 
Jordi Bernadó who figures in front page. Solà-Morales attempts in this manner a more 
local link and plays on an American-European fascination308. 
On   Moholy-­‐Nagy   and   other   unmentioned   artists   
As a side yet important note, László Moholy-Nagy is one of the photographers 
who could have been equally mentioned. Often referenced by Solà-Morales in some of his 
other essays such as “Ciudad Capital” or “Absent Bodies”, the artist is equally Germanbased and of the same period as Citroen or Grosz. He ultimately also fled Germany for the 
United States. The photographer and painter was as well a professor at the Bauhaus, 
involved in technological advancements within the arts. Respectively, he pushed the 
Bauhaus beyond their expressionistic trend towards more industrial inspirations and the 
use of diverse media, including photomontage and photography. Along with El 
Lissitzky309, the two artists could have easily been on Solà-Morales’s list, as they were 
previously in other essays due to their affiliation with the same context of post-industrial 
in-between wars setting that the author is attributing to a pre-terrain vague period. 
The link between the German in-between wars context and the development of 
photomontage techniques is not serendipitous. The history of photography and 
particularly photomontages has also been intrinsically connected to the history of 
Germany of the same era. In his book Photography Calling! 310, Ulrich Krempel, director 
of the Sprengel Museum Hannover, describes a 'new seeing' that arose from the Weimar 
period311. He explains that photography gained wide public resonance as a “seemingly 
objective medium”312 while at the same time gaining territory in the artistic discourse 
                                                                                                                                                                        
308 Solà-Morales was repeatedly described in interviews as a theoretician architect who adopted an academic 
profile between the Spanish and the American circles, which allowed him eventually to acquire an 
international amplitude both in his thoughts and public reception. (As will be described in the following 
chapter.) 
309 El Lissitzky, friend of Moholy-Nagy was a Russian artist and photographer, also involved in the German 
scene where he studied and later returned to Berlin in the 1920s to initiate collaborations between Russian 
and German artists and made his first solo-exhibition in the Hanover Gallery Kestnergesellschaft. Later in 
1927, he also conducted the design team “Photography and Photomechanics” (i.e. photomontages) for one of 
his most noteworthy exhibitions, the All-Union Polygraphic Exhibit in Moscow. 
310 Schube, Inka, and Thomas Weski. Photography Calling! Gerhard Steidl Druckerei und Verlag, 2011. 
311 Weimar Period is the period in which the photographers at hand mainly arose. It is the name set by 
historians to the federal republic and semi-presidential representative democracy between 1919 and 1933. It 
emerged from the German Revolution in November 1918 and was succeeded by the Nazi era. The official 
name 'Deutsches Reich' remained, continuing from the pre-1918 Imperial period. 
312 Krempel, Ulrich. “Forward Sprengel Museum Hannover.” Photography Calling! Ed. Inka Schube and 
Thomas Weski. Gerhard Steidl Druckerei und Verlag, 2011. 12–18.

## S. 177

174 
where “professionalization of photographic art took the place of somewhat anonymous art 
photography.”313 That same book is a continuation in the German spirit of photographic 
professionalism. Various photographers from the last century as well as current are 
confronted with recorded imagery of terrains vagues linked to Germany as well as the 
United States. For instance, Robert Adam's photographs from “The New West” depicted 
American 'Western terrains vagues', and Hilla and Bernd Becher “21-Teilige Typologie”314 
depicted post-industrial ruins from Central Europe. This phenomenon emerged from 
several well renowned exhibitions that took place in Germany315. These exhibitions caused 
the German artistic circles to be avant-garde in their dealing with photography. Some 
significant publications point to that same intense context, such as the text of Walter 
Dexel’s “Neue Wege der Photographie”316 or Moholy-Nagy’s “Malerei, Fotografie, Film”317. 
It is moreover particularly interesting to follow on this parallel evolution between the 
developments in photography and the historical urban developments of German cities 
such as Berlin. This parallel is what Solà-Morales was aiming for when he first tackled the 
subject of terrain vague from that German photography perspective. German cities, due to 
processes of deindustrialization, post-war traumas, shifting photographic aesthetics prove 
hence fruitful for both staging a historical and political context for the terrain vague while 
in parallel providing visual case studies for this very visually based notion. 
A shifting gaze 
Based on these variations in photographic approaches and respective changes in 
representation of space, the first two pages of Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague” essay 
revolve around main shifts in urban gaze in the 20th century. The shift debated here is 
mainly the one between the 1920-30s gaze and that of the 1960-70s. It comes as a 
consequence of the political and economic contexts previously mentioned, and paves the 
way to the “new gaze” of mid-20th century, presented by Solà-Morales as intimately 
linked to the notion of terrain vague. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
313 Krempel, Ulrich. Ibid. 
314 Trans.: “21-Part Typology”. 
315 “Pressa” took place for instance in Cologne in 1928, and “Neue Wege der Photographie” was held in Jena 
that same year where works by Albert Renger-Patzsch, Laszlo Moholy-Nagy and Umbo, were exhibited. 
Then in Stuttgart, the International Exhibition of the Deutscher Werkbund was presented in 1929, 
introduced by Moholy-Nagy and revolving solely around Photography and Film. Essen was fourth in hosting 
such an exhibition entitled Fotografie der Gegenwart in 1929, later shown in Hanover. 
316 Trans.: “New Ways of Photography”, and Moholy-Nagy’s “Painting, Photography, Film”. 
317 Ulrich Krempel in the book Photography Calling! presents a more extensive list of publications held in 
that same period, most of which are German affiliated and most referring to a new wave in photography: 
“Neue Topographie” and “Neue Wege”, referring thus to that same shift in photography significance in the 
arts.

## S. 178

175 
 
As advocated by Heartfield in the book entitled Fotomontagen 1930-1938 318, 
images from different takes can produce new impressions and landscapes; their meaning is 
no longer resting on realism. In the same spirit, photomontage techniques of the 
aforementioned artists presented new worldviews often charged politically and 
economically. Accordingly, the context in which the notion of terrain vague was first 
conceived in spatial and urban form through photomontages before it became a 
photographic trend among photographers of the 1960s, 1970s and onward. Moreover, all 
this discourse on the gaze and photographic techniques allowed Solà-Morales to situate 
the terrain vague within a larger discussion on artistic and architectural development, as 
well as an even larger debate on aesthetics. Furthermore, since page one, Solà-Morales’s 
debate on terrain vague aesthetics is charged with historical, political and economic 
implications. He introduces the notion not as a mere reverse in urban aesthetics, but as a 
natural consequence of historical changes that found their expression in new ways of 
comprehending and representing space. By introducing changes in photography and a 
repeated list of photographers, not only does Solà-Morales link the essay to the saga of 
ANY books, he also manages to stage a prehistory to the notion of terrain vague itself. 
The special case of Man Ray 
“The manipulation of the objects captured by the camera, their framing, the 
composition, the detail, has a decisive influence on our perception of the works of 
architecture photographed. […] physical impulses that steer in a particular direction the 
construction of an imaginary which we establish as that of a specific place or city.”319 
   
In this play of name changing and unspoken affiliations, one artist stands out from 
the rest of the enumerated photographers yet still fits within the list. Born Emmanuel 
Radnitzky, Man Ray differs from the others listed in both places of origin as well as 
influence and significance to the subject of terrain vague. The question imposes: what 
weight does Man Ray hold in Solà-Morales’s enumeration and his theorization of the 
topic of terrain vague? Unlike Grosz or Heartfield, Man Ray was of American origins yet 
considered his home to be the city of Paris where he spent most of his career. He moved 
there in 1921, where he became the well-acknowledged artist and photographer we read 
about. In this sense, Man Ray had a trajectory inverse to the others mentioned, coming 
from the United States, from a family of Russian Jewish immigrants, and returning back to 
Europe to establish relations, although informal, with the Dada and Surrealist movements. 
Man Ray's change of name however did not result from anti-Nazi or anti-nationalist 
                                                                                                                                                                        
318 John Heartfield, Photograph, Schriftsteller Wieland Herzfelde, and Friedrich Pfäfflin. Krieg im Frieden  : 
Fotomontagen zur Zeit 1930-1938. Vol. 103. München: Hanser, 1972. 101-112. 
319 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass.: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 118–123.

## S. 179

176 
statements, as in Grosz’s case, but rather from a reaction to the anti-Semite discrimination 
widespread during the period; the politically and culturally charged context thus still 
prevails. Even more so, Man Ray's trajectory was also deeply affected by the in-between 
wars era as he was forced for example to return to the United States between 1940 and 
1951 due to the Second World War circumstances, making consequently his art and 
photography relevant in American as well as European spheres. Nonetheless, it was in 
Paris in 1921 when Man Ray devoted himself to the photographic profession, learning its 
craft and more importantly adding on to it. Indeed, Man Ray, as the others in Solà-
Morales’s first list, did also belong to the in-between wars era, yet witnessing interwar 
Paris rather than the German counterparts; therefore the political influence did differ. 
Same could be said of his mostly French and American Dada affiliations, rather than 
German, even though the various Dada artists did indeed commute often320. It is in this 
Parisian Dada and Surrealist surroundings, where he stayed between 1921 and 1940, that 
he developed his piece entitled “Terrain Vague”. 
It comes as no surprise that a subject such as terrain vague finds one of its main 
origins in the Dada movement and their love of the everyday translated in readymades or 
distorted out of context. The terrain vague found its way then to Surrealism and Pop art, 
and finally the notion’s first urban and architectural evocation by Solà-Morales in the 
context of the Triennale di Milano321 where, adequately enough, Solà-Morales links the 
notion of terrain vague with the Surrealist artist Marcel Duchamp. 
Fearing to be confined in the mere role of 'photographer of art', probably what 
best distinguishes Man Ray in our context is the experimental nature of his work and his 
avant-garde subjects; one of these subjects happening to be the terrain vague. Man Ray is 
indeed strikingly avant-garde to his period in both artistic work and thought; which sets 
his artworks not within his period but rather in an initial timeframe few decades deferred 
from his. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
320 For example, previously mentioned El Lissitzky, another one of Solà-Morales’s frequently mentioned 
artists in the discourse, was inspired by the Dada photomontages. Solà-Morales followed closely the Dada 
and the Surrealist movements in their evolution and influence and was himself largely inspired by these 
artists in his ANY essays saga. 
321 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “‘Seminario internazionale: Identità e Differenza. I racconti dell’Abitare’ - From 
Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive.”

## S. 180

177 
 
 
FIGURE 55: Man Ray, Terrain Vague, 1929/1932. Gelatin-silver print, 30.2 x 22.6 cm. - The Museum of

## S. 181

178 
What separates moreover Man Ray from the rest of the writers is his larger 
prominence as artist and photographer beyond a specific array of artistic belonging322. A 
common affiliation between Solà-Morales and Man Ray is also their interdisciplinary 
character. While Solà-Morales was theoretician, philosopher, architect, and art critic, 
Man Ray is considered one of the most versatile artists of his time. Painter, sculptor, 
photographer and filmmaker, the artist was best known for his innovative 
experimentation with the various fields, mixing techniques and subjects of study. In the 
context of the terrain vague, the presence of Man Ray on Solà-Morales’s list of references 
is particularly crucial due to the artist’s prominence as key-figure within the pre-1960s 
photography in relation to a particular photographic “gaze”, as the author describes it. “I 
do not photograph nature. I photograph my visions” 323, a quote Man Ray is known for. 
Expanding the limits of body, space and art, he aimed consciously at expanding the limits 
of reality. In accord with the Dada movement and later with the Surrealists, his work 
staged new visions of reality, or rather altered senses of what could be considered real, 
unreal, surreal. 
Terrain Vague, one of his photomontages324, falls in one of those rims between the 
observed and the envisioned. The technique used in this masterpiece, made in 1929, is 
archetypal of Man Ray's work of that period, even if it seems at first hand less controlled 
or intently staged. Ray called his signature technique ‘Rayograph’, in reference to 
himself325; this so-called 'camera-less' method of making pictures, among other forms of 
experimentation with photography, collage and photomontage allowed the artist to reach 
the sensitivity found in the terrain vague. As his friend Marcel Duchamp once said of 
him: 
“It was his achievement to treat the camera as he treated the paint brush, as a mere 
instrument at the service of the mind.” 326 
Indeed, Man Ray's Terrain Vague appears as an odd sloped wasteland in which lies a 
strange seemingly decaying piece of ironwork and a seemingly decaying tree. The space 
generates the same oddness one could sense in Beckett's scenographic staging for his En 
Attendant Godot play: “A country road. A tree. Evening.”327 One unique decaying tree 
(“The tree has four or five leaves.”) alludes to the timelessness of the setting, giving an 
overall sense of uncanniness, absurdity, but also a sense of materiality to the stage. Even 
                                                                                                                                                                        
322 Man Ray's acclaim resisted the weight of time and is even named by the ARTnews magazine in 1999 as 
one of the most influential artists of the 20th century 
323 “Man Ray - Prophet of the Avant-Garde | American Masters | PBS.” Web. 7 May 2014. 
324 The photomontage is part of a collection at the Museum of Modern Art. Man Ray, Terrain Vague, 
1929/1932. Gelatin-silver print, 30.2 x 22.6 cm. The Museum of Modern Art, New York. Gift of James Thrall 
Soby. - Man Ray Trust ADAGP, Paris and DACS, London, 2001. 
325 Which he described as “pure Dadaism”. 
326 Sanouillet, Michel, and Elmer Peterson, eds. The Writings of Marcel Duchamp. New York: Da Capo Press, 
1973. 152. 
327 Samuel Beckett, Schriftsteller. En attendant Godot. Paris: Les Editions de Minuit, 1952. 2-3.

## S. 182

179 
 
more so, imprinted steps visible in Man Ray's photomontage imply a previous human 
presence. The illustrated terrain is hence not devoid, at least in past tense, of human 
activity328. One would imagine this carefully 'staged' or simply 'observed' space being the 
perfect scenography for a hypothetical third act of Beckett's play: Estragon and Vladimir, 
giving up on Godot, leave; all that is left is this space on hold, in a waiting essence of 
actions to come, i.e. a terrain vague. 
According to Phil Carney and Vincent Miller in their article “Vague Spaces”: 
 “All the time Surrealists sought to interrupt the rationalized world of modernity with 
the dream. They also found the dream in photographed reality, including the dream of 
certain spaces of representation existing in the midst of the hegemonic representation of 
space.” 329 
The authors in this article link Man Ray's works with Cartier-Bresson, bridging thus the 
artist of Solà-Morales’s first list with one of the second context described in the author’s 
staging of a ‘terrain vague photographic history’. In so doing, their argument is based on 
setting a linkage between the surreal, the strange and photographers' constant attraction 
to these found possibilities in space. What is also interesting in the given discussion on the 
terrain vague is how Man Ray is recurrent as the first reference in photography, in 
relation to discussing vagueness and strangeness in space. 
Man Ray's photomontage is arguably the first visual and analytical origin of the 
notion of terrain vague, especially as Solà-Morales defines it. Linked to a fleeting sense of 
time and continuity while staying faithful to a particular sense of space and urban gaze, 
Man Ray's piece could not have gone unnoticed by Solà-Morales. In a previous essay of 
the author titled “Weak Architecture”, he once again evokes Marcel Duchamp and Man 
Ray in a discussion on photomontage and architectural expression. He states in reference 
to the collaborative artwork “Dust Farm” between Marcel Duchamp and Man Ray,: 
“…the idea is evidently recreated of an apprehended temporality, trapped in an instant, 
and in which it is accepted that the work should age and be affected by the passage of 
time.”330 
Once again, the narrative (spatial or time-bound) in Solà-Morales’s thought is fragmented, 
yet 'accumulative', conjuring into a cohesive sum, very much like Man Ray's urban vision 
of terrain vague and “affected by the passage of time”, as Solà-Morales describes Man 
Ray's work in the same essay. 
Man Ray's montage sets in fact the perfect example of this first section of Solà-
Morales’s discourse. The ironwork, the decaying tree-nature, the remnant presence of 
                                                                                                                                                                        
328 This theme, i.e. presence and absence of human intervention in the terrain vague, will be recurrent in 
many of the examples of terrain vague photography that will be observed throughout this chapter. 
329 Carney, Phil, and Vincent Miller. “Vague Spaces.” Strange Spaces: Explorations into Mediated Obscurity. 
Ed. André Jansson and Amanda Lagerkvist. Farnham, England; Burlington, VT: Ashgate Pub., 2009. 33–57. 
330 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Weak Architecture.” Differences: Topographies of Contemporary Architecture. 
Ed. Sarah Whiting. Trans. Graham Thompson. Cambridge: MIT Press, 1999. 57–73.

## S. 183

180 
human interference, all stage a post-industrial polemic contest of laissez-faire versus 
human colonization. Solà-Morales could not have asked for a better visual description of 
his notion's preliminary vision. Moreover, the photograph, or rather photomontage, 
inflicts a sense of instability, making one wonder how deep Ray's understanding of the 
notion of terrain vague was and how spatial. Much is thus left to be clarified by the 
photographer, as is the case in many of his artworks; Nonetheless, it stays evident where 
the artist's interest lies. The use of accessories in a Surrealist movement, the diversity in 
illuminations, the carefully chosen montage, hazard and improvisation are only a facade. 
Man Ray seeks to eliminate the boundaries of the real, the disorderly, the surreal and the 
ordered; and succeeds in doing so. Even the framing of the work is carefully and intently 
controlled in function of a subsequent expansion as seen in the various trials of the same 
photographs and rayographs. Even more so, the artist, as described by conservator and art 
historian Alain Sayag331, appropriates some classical techniques from painting such as the 
background canvas or the roman column and includes them in innovative forms in his 
work. The tree in Man Ray’s Terrain Vague could very well have worked to the benefit of 
Man Ray's organization as a roman column worked for a classical portrait: aesthetic 
pleasance merged with reminiscence of a glorious past. Is this not one of the dominant 
ongoing discussions on the terrain vague? 
Furthermore, in the same exploration on order and staging, Neil Baldwin 
describes: 
“His (Man Ray) unerring eye could pick out a constellation of details and frame them so 
that they appeared to have arranged themselves. […] Man Ray's photography is great because 
it embraces variety. It represents a virtual new order of reality; it is its own world. It is 
restless and omnivorous, taking on all the possibilities of perception as its territory.” 332 
Once again, as Solà-Morales describes in the first part of his “Terrain Vague” essay, 
perception and representation of space are the subject of debate. In this context, Man Ray 
fits graciously: a new order founded from juxtaposed elements on vague grounds; or in 
other words, a new urban vision within familiar grounds. The author and the 
photographer in this sense share a similar peculiar approach to their respective 
production, at first based on perception and moving towards the creation or rather 
accentuation of a new order of reality; in the interest of this thesis: the terrain vague. This 
order is advocated moreover by McGrane, when discussing Solà-Morales’s terrain vague, 
as flexible enough to “produce an indeterminate design, capable of responding to the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
331 Sayag, Alain, and Emmanuelle de L’Ecotais. Man Ray. La Photographie à l’Envers. Paris: Seuil, 1998. 257. 
332 Baldwin, Neil. Man Ray: American Artist. New York: Da Capo Press, 2000. XIII-XIV. 
1932: Photomontages these years had a particular impact and an intense political resonance. It was in the 
year of 1932 that John Heartfield produced the satiric “Adolf, the Superman: Swallows Gold and Spouts 
Junk”, depicting a Hitler greedy for fortune and power. The following year, Hitler would become chancellor. 
Also in 1932, Man Ray would make his photography “Terrain Vague”.

## S. 184

181 
 
changing growth patterns and perceptions of the future city.”333 Indeed, Man Ray's concise 
piece, very much like Solà-Morales’s essay, sustains a greater potential than it seems at 
first, and, as we have described, quite evocative of future developments of the urban gaze. 
One can hence safely state that, even though other works in art and literature had, 
previous to Man Ray, tackled the subject of terrain vague, it was this versatile artist who 
initiated a deeper visionary discourse on the notion through this falsely-random 
photomontage. It is precisely in this concocted order of few objects in space that the 
mystification and problematization of the terrain vague sets sail. McGrane again describes: 
“Originally based on Man Ray's 1934 photograph, the urban design concept of terrain 
vague has been developed to describe areas of indeterminate use or identity. It is the leftover 
material; unplanned, or forgotten space within the urban environment. These spaces can be 
seen as either a stain on the city and in need of an architectural order, or as a sort of urban 
wilderness, which holds meaning and value in its own right.”334 
Solà-Morales’s discourse holds origins here, where perception, vision, reality and 
value become debatable within one notion. 
First list of visual references 
Marcel   Duchamp’s   Cosa   
“The material thread and the randomness of gravity and times are the producing agents 
of a form, an object that does not appear by transposition, but emerges from its slight physical 
substance to reach the fullness of a fragile meaning.” 335 
 
Man Ray and Marcel Duchamp seem to be linked both in their personal artistic 
trajectories and in their relation to the notion of terrain vague. First, they were friends 
and collaborated on many now-famous artworks and photographs. Duchamp, even more 
than Man Ray, is a recurrent inspiration in Solà-Morales’s thought, as described by 
Manolo Laguillo in an interview with the author336. Man Ray and Duchamp are joined as 
well in their experimental and innovative approach. It is therefore not a sheer 
coincidence that when Solà-Morales evokes the former in his “Terrain Vague” essay he 
also evokes the latter. The sense of readymade closely linked to Duchamp's artistic work, 
is visible in the ironwork of the terrain vague photography; almost a re-enactment of 
                                                                                                                                                                        
333 McGrane, Kurt Ryan. “Landscapes, Architecture.” Thesis. Montana State University - Bozeman, College of 
Arts & Architecture, 2010. 19-20. 
334 McGrane, Kurt Ryan. Ibid. 27. 
335 Trans. from Italian: “Il filo materiale e la casualità della gravitazione e del tempo sono gli agenti produttori 
di una forma, di un oggetto che non nasce per trasposizione, ma emerge dalla sua esile consistenza fisica fino 
a raggiungere la pienezza di un significato fragile.” - Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Triennale di 
Milano I Racconti dell’Abitare un Seminario. una Mostra.Abitare Segesta cataloghi (1994): 74–82. 
336 Interview between author and Manolo Laguillo in Barcelona. 21 Jan. 2014.

## S. 185

182 
Duchamp's Pissoir moment337. In Solà-Morales’s essay “Arquitectura Debole”338, the two 
artists are tackled side by side in an attempt once again to understand a certain experience 
of architecture and space. Written few years before the "Terrain Vague", the former essay 
could be considered as a prelude to the more urban discourse of the latter. Transparency, 
temporality, narrative, and energy already surface as key characteristics and as key 
criteria to analyze both the artists' works and the experience of space. These same 
elements would form few years later the basis for the terrain vague discourse. 
At this point, it is noteworthy to address the artist Marcel Duchamp's relation to 
the "Terrain Vague" essay. Evidently, he was not referenced directly in the integral text. 
Solà-Morales however adopted one of his art pieces as a prologue to his “Terrain Vague” 
essay as it first appeared in the Triennale di Milano339. In the Triennale Seminar and 
exhibition preceding the publication, each of the contributing writers were requested to 
chose one art object, “la cosa”340, to accompany their texts on various urban matters. Solà-
Morales chose Duchamp's “Trois Stoppages-Étalon” as his selected 'object'. One can find 
in his archives a detailed description341 of the piece. In the same archived document, a 
description could also be found of his intentions behind choosing this artwork. Being a 
readymade, Duchamp’s piece induces, according to Solà-Morales, a discussion between 
the specific and the universal; it simulates a condition of uniqueness removing it thus 
from its everyday use342. On another level, since the piece is presented as an artwork while 
at the same time evoking memories of past uses, the generated feeling is one of 
decontextualization and estrangement. Solà-Morales puts thus the object in the context of 
estrangement towards one's environment, advocating this estrangement is maintained 
                                                                                                                                                                        
337 In M. Sanouillet's text, researcher on the Dada movement, “Duchamp and Man Ray, Regards Croisés”, the 
author confronts the two artists, simply labeling Duchamp's art as “eclectic and legitimized” in its position in 
the artistic world, while confining Man Ray's art to a “disparate and illegitimate” status. Beyond criticism, 
this judgment on Man Ray's work could be also understood as referring to the side-status to which Man Ray 
adhered. According to Sanouillet, Man ray stayed within a ‘darker underground side of art’, the artist's work 
was not as clearly analyzed as Duchamp's. This could partially explain why works such as “Terrain Vague” 
did not acquire yet the attention they deserve. - Sayag, Alain, and Emmanuelle de L’Ecotais. Man Ray. La 
Photographie à l’Envers. Paris: Seuil, 1998. 257. 
338 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Weak Architecture.” Architecture Theory Since 1968. Ed. K. Michael Hays. 
Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, 2000. 614–642.

Mostra.Abitare Segesta cataloghi (1994): 74–82.

through the thread of memory 343 . Many notions hence, found in Solà-Morales’s 
interpretation of Duchamp's artwork, are also found in his essay: estrangement, conveying 
of meaning, universality, memory, and time.

## S. 187

184 
One could also interpret Solà-Morales’s choice of the art piece and its respective 
author, Duchamp, as an attempt at linking once again the art of Dadaists and Surrealists, 
their aspirations and their fascination with the everyday, to the terrain vague notion. In 
this sense, a readymade is the appropriate choice to accompany the essay. The artist 
Marcel Duchamp was not himself close to the Dadaists and the Surrealists; he did not join 
these movements, yet he was involved and collaborated with many of these artists, such 
as Max Ernst and André Breton. Thus in spirit, Duchamp does indirectly belong to the 
first set of artists, i.e. to the historical context of the terrain vague notion as set in the 
essay. 
Moreover, another reason could be behind Solà-Morales’s choice. The author 
describes Duchamp's work as attempts at creating meaning ex-nihilo, rather than 
manipulating existing significations. Creating meaning ex-nihilo from the available 
everyday material seems to be what the terrain vague is also about. Being both an already 
existing term before Solà-Morales, as well as spaces physically present in the urban fabric, 
the "Terrain Vague" essay conveys a refreshing new mode of interpreting these spaces in 
the city, which is where the strength of the essay lies and why Solà-Morales is considered 
to have coined the term terrain vague. 
Podsadecki   and   Brzeski’s   photomontages   
Two other artists represented in visuals in the Quaderns version of the "Terrain 
Vague" essay are the Russian Kazimierz Podsadecki and the Polish Janusz Maria Brzeski. 
These artists were not well known in Solà-Morales’s context as the other photographers 
mentioned by name in the essay, and they are still not a contemporary common 
reference. The author nonetheless chose to include their photographs, as they are 
representative of the photomontages of the first list of photographers. Podsadecki’s 
photomontage “Modern City” for instance, shown in the essay, represents, as the title 
predicts, a fragmented condensed view of the contemporary urban fabric. Both humans 
and machines are portrayed as volatile elements of the urban fabric composed mainly of 
high-rise structures. Development, technology and urbanity are put in focus. Same could 
be said of Brzeski’s photomontage, where the “Birth of the Robot”344 is depicted through a 
female body floating over a collection of bridges and skyscrapers. Both artists share the 
same in-between World Wars context. They represented similar political and artistic 
takes on the city and the toll of historical events and technological advances on the urban 
domain. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
344 Trans. from the original title: “ Naissance du Robot ”.

## S. 188

185 
 
Podsadecki’s montage in particular constitutes a link between Solà-Morales’s 
essays “Terrain Vague” and “Absent Bodies” 345. The piece is represented in both; it is a 
visual illustration of Solà-Morales’s description of “the freeing of the relationship between 
the body and its physical surroundings.”346 Yet, this relationship is taken on an architectural 
level in the second essay, whereas in “Terrain Vague” attention is on the urban scale. 
Ergonomic architecture is thus discussed in the former while the fragmented metropolis is 
advocated in the latter, where the human body becomes a vulnerable piece of the larger 
urban puzzle. 
 
Second context and list of references: a humanist in-transition gaze 
The contrast between the illustrations of the third and fourth page of the 
Quaderns publication of the “Terrain Vague” essay illustrates well the shift in 
photographic systems and styles described in the essay. The photomontages of Podsadecki 
from 1928 and Brzeski from 1933 present a completely different approach to photography 
and to the reading of the city than the photographs of Louis Faurer of 1942 and 1948 on 
the opposite page. The first two seem to portray a similar period and style to that of Solà-
Morales’s first list of photographers previously described, such as Paul Citroen’s or 
Heartfield's. Faurer's photographs on the other hand portray an “existentialist”, 
“humanist” narrative of urban dwellers in their ‘natural habitat’, somewhat similar in 
spirit to Robert Frank's The Americans. Both phases do not portray terrains vagues, as 
readers might expect. Yet, both will be then contrasted in the following pages of Solà-
Morales’s essay with the post-1960s and 1970s eras in which, as he argues, the focus 
turned to “those empty, abandoned spaces, in which a series of occurrences have taken 
place, seem to subjugate the eye of the urban photographer.”347 
Except for the first photograph by Jordi Bernadó on the front page of Quaderns' 
version of the essay, Solà-Morales’s illustrating references seem to follow a chronological 
logic in accordance with the timeline of historical and photographic contexts Solà-
Morales attempts at staging for the terrain vague notion in the first two pages of the essay. 
Not all the photographs in his essay should be thus considered as examples of the terrain 
vague. Only the ones on the fifth page onward, including evidently the representative 
cover photograph by Bernadó, are depictions of terrains vagues. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
345 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Absent Bodies.” Anybody. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, MA: The MIT 
Press, 1997. 16–26. 
346 Ibid. 
347 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua (1996): 
34–44.

## S. 189

186 
Family of Man and other exhibitions 
Solà-Morales mentions briefly the period right after the World War II putting it in 
contrast with the photomontages of the 1920s/30s. In this sense, he presents this period as 
a second layer of historical context to the notion of terrain vague. He introduces a system 
of signs developed in this period in opposition with the density of photomontages of the 
previous period. Attention seems set on humanist urban narratives in which anonymous 
people were depicted with a background lacking architectural grandiloquence. The 
exhibition Family of Man348 is an example of this, quoted in fact by Solà-Morales in the 
essay. It was also between the American and European contexts, held in the United States 
but also exhibited in Europe, mainly France. Curated by Edward Steichen, the exhibition 
displays an existentialist read of the city and the landscape, developed or not; this read 
reached its apotheosis with the artist Robert Frank's exhibition The Americans349. Critic 
and theoretician Roland Barthes in his book Mythologies describes more in detail the 
Family of Man exhibition. He puts under scrutiny the entire idea of a ‘homogenized 
humanity’ that lies behind the exhibition. He presents under negative light the 
naturalizing intent behind what Solà-Morales calls a “humanist existentialist reading.”350 
Possibly the terrain vague gaze is a counter-reaction to this homogenizing “naturalizing” 
about which Barthes warns already in 1957. The terrain vague in this light belongs to an 
undeclared struggle against the homogenization of the city, through artistic 
representations, but also through economic and political means. 
In the same spirit, one can address Robert Frank's The Americans and its position 
in the "Terrain Vague" essay. In the book on American photography Street Seen, the 
curator Lisa Hostetler approaches photography of mid-20th century in a similar fashion to 
Solà-Morales. She begins her article by stating: 
“During and immediately after World War II, a distinctly subjective edge sliced through 
the work of a number of artists. The photographs of Lisette Model, Louis Faurer, Ted Croner, 
Saul Leiter, William Klein, and Robert Frank are particularly powerful manifestations of this 
more personal approach...”351 
The author explains this phase in the history of photography as being a reaction 
against the previous period of World Wars, the deployment of atomic bombs and the 
onset of the Cold War. In contrast, Robert Frank, Faurer and others, as she describes, 
                                                                                                                                                                        
348 NY, Museum of Modern Art New York. The Family of Man: Exhibition  : the 30th Anniversary Edition of 
the Classic Book of Photography Created by Edward Steichen for the Museum of Modern Art, New York. 
MoMa, 1994. 
349 Day, Jonathan. Robert Frank’s The Americans: The Art of Documentary Photography. Intellect Books, 
2011. 
350 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass.: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 120. 
351 Hostetler, Lisa. Street Seen: The Psychological Gesture in American Photography, 1940-1959. Munich; 
New York: DelMonico Books - Prestel, 2010. 25.

## S. 190

187 
 
“embraced photography as an "act of living" - an exploration of identity rather than a 
tool for telling a story, a personal journey rather than a technique for graphic 
experimentation or documentation […] exploiting and undermining the presumption of 
objectivity attached to photographic imagery, they explored the nature of individuality in a 
rapidly changing, impersonal social environment.” 352 
Thus in this episode of photography history, one can already perceive proclivity to 
the uncanny “estrangement to the world and ultimately to one-self”353 that Solà-Morales 
attributes to the urban experience at the turn of the millennium and to the experience of 
the terrain vague as refuge from this estrangement. 
Second list of visual references: Louis Faurer 
“We are talking here of a politics for the individual in conflict with himself, desolately 
despairing at the rapidity with which the whole world is transformed…”354 
 
Same as others in the Solà-Morales’s second list of photographers, Louis Faurer 
belongs to the post-World War II period. The artist was related to creative photography 
in the United States in the 1940s and 1950s. He spent his non-commercial hours 
photographing New York's Times Square and the Union Square355. Times Square was a 
particular choice as it symbolized for him the decline of the West, where castoffs of the 
bourgeois society wandered; a sense of primitivism thus ruled “final scenes of 
disintegrative decay.”356 The two photographs by Faurer appearing in Solà-Morales’s essay 
are representative of this dichotomy personal-estranged. In these almost-portrait 
photographs, the existence of some persons is attested through the photograph, isolated 
from the crowds in which they would otherwise have blended. Tattered, vulnerable, this 
is the soul of modern urbanity. This is the soul that will find itself eventually reflected 
and empowered in the terrain vague photographs in which potential is key. 
Similar to Robert Frank, Faurer attempts at depicting the “act of living” and uses 
photography as a tool for the “exploration of identity rather than a tool for story 
                                                                                                                                                                        
352 Ibid. 26. 
353 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass.: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 122. 
354 Ibid. 118–123. 
355 He free-lanced otherwise, for monetary reasons, as a fashion photographer for Flair, Junior Bazar and 
other magazines and journals. 
356 Hostetler writes so, quoting Allen Ginsberg, in order to describe the correspondence between the latter’s 
writings and thought to Faurer’s photographs and attitude towards decaying urban characters. Hostetler, 
Lisa. Street Seen: The Psychological Gesture in American Photography, 1940-1959. Munich; New York: 
DelMonico Books - Prestel, 2010. 76-77.

## S. 191

188 
telling.”357 Thus the interest falls on the personal journey rather than documentation 
photography. The photographer in this form is openly involved in his pictures; 
subjectivity is elementary. Faurer perhaps identified with these characters, an American 
born from Polish immigrants, he had gone through the World War II, which he 
photographed, then survived the post-war through photographing fashion, leading 
ultimately to an early retirement from the commercial realm in order to focus on more 
independent creative projects. Nonetheless this mode of photographing is both supportive 
of and against objectivity, whereas the photograph falls between narrative and reality. 
The relation is even more active for the viewer of the photographs whose subjectivity is 
equally at stake. 
In the essay “Faurer: Finding the Self in the Other”358 the idea of humanity is 
followed in order to start a confrontation between the ‘self’ and the ‘other’ within a larger 
perspective of urbanity. As is the case in most of Faurer's work, emotion and observation 
are merged, implying uncertainty and duration, in opposition to artist Cartier-Bresson359 
whose work seem more a recreation of a reality with great intent. 
In this sense, the period right after the World War II and before the 1960s 
functions as a buffer zone in which the authenticity of firsthand observation of the 1930s 
is combined with the emotional World War II, yet not aware still of the massive 
emotional and physical void generated by the war and the subsequent potential of this 
void. The result hence is an identity of singular characters lost in the urban experience, 
working as a preamble to the “self versus other” discourse in Solà-Morales’s essay, in 
which urban identity goes into conflict with the other, the city and the self. Similar to the 
Surrealists, Faurer was interested in Freud and placed importance on the role of the 
subconscious. Yet, rather than searching to reveal the dreamy and the surreal, he 
considered the subconscious as the major player in everyday activity in urban public 
realms such as in Times Square, as shown in the photos of Solà-Morales’s essay. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
357 Hostetler. Ibid. - Hostetler again puts in question Faurer’s photography as “ an act of living ”, i.e. “ an 
exploration of identity rather than a tool for telling a story, a personal journey rather than a technique for 
graphic experimentation or documentation.” 
358 Hostetler. Ibid. 75. 
359 Cartier-Bresson had influenced Faurer; it is therefore not by coincidence that both are put in connection. 
We will tackle Cartier-Bresson in the following section.

## S. 192

189 
 
 
FIGURE 58: Faurer, Louis. Fifth Avenue. New York, 1948. Photograph. 
 
FIGURE 59: Faurer, Louis. Atlantic City. 1942. Photograph.

## S. 193

190 
Magnum and Henri Cartier-Bresson’s photographs 
“…a contradictory complicity that would not shatter the perceptive elements which 
maintain its continuity in the time and space.”360 
 
The group Magnum Photos was also mentioned in the "Terrain Vague" essay 
within the post-World War II period. Cartier-Bresson, one of the main characters of the 
group, was mentioned in the Quaderns publication of the essay. He had co-founded this 
cooperative photography agency based between New York and Paris in 1947. One 
common feature between Cartier-Bresson and the others on Solà-Morales’s list is once 
again the pre-established periods, the artist having lived between the WWII phase and 
the one following. Prisoner of war in 1940 after serving with the French Army, Cartier-
Bresson had managed to escape and photographed in 1945 the liberation of Paris. He later 
was the first foreign photographer allowed to take pictures in post-war USSR. Similar to 
Solà-Morales, the artist was an influential figure bridging between Europe and America. 
For instance, he first exhibited his work in Mexico and in the United States in 1933 and 
1935, but had started his career as a painter in Chanteloup in France. 
Another common feature between Cartier-Bresson and others on Solà-Morales’s 
list is the interest in Surrealism. His work often displayed strange scenes of wastelands in 
which the rationalized city and dream are in fusion. Phil Carney and Vincent Miller 
describe his photographs as “dreams of certain spaces of representation existing in midst 
of hegemonic representation of spaces.”361 They discuss for instance in the article “Vague 
Spaces”362 one particular photograph of the Saint-Lazare station, relating it directly with 
Solà-Morales’s essay on the one hand and with photographers' infatuation with 
Surrealism on the other. 
Cartier-Bresson's photography is moreover meant to merge interior and external 
visions into one image made at a “decisive moment”. Time was indeed an important factor 
in Cartier-Bresson's photography, as it shows in his book rightly entitled Decisive 
Moment363 from 1952 in which, as in the photograph of Saint-Lazare, moments are caught 
in their transitory fleeting motion. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
360 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
361 Carney, Phil, and Vincent Miller. “Vague Spaces.” Strange Spaces: Explorations into Mediated Obscurity. 
Ed. André Jansson and Amanda Lagerkvist. Farnham, England: Ashgate Pub., 2009. 43-44. 
362 Carney, Phil, and Vincent Miller. Ibid. 
363 Cartier-Bresson, Henri. The Decisive Moment: (diaporama). Scholastic Book Services, 1973.

## S. 194

191 
 
Furthermore, the photographer-artist is adequately described in Street Seen as 
“writing with emotional power […] without lapsing into sentimentality.”364 Indeed motion, 
time, strangeness and dream, all elements in Cartier-Bresson are carefully studied, 
recreated. The outcome is different from other photographers of his period in which 
traces of the real are on equal grounds as subjectivity. This dichotomy between reality and 
dream is pushed further in the third list of photographers in Solà-Morales’s essay as will 
be described in what follows. These would address this dichotomy from a more focused 
perspective directed mainly towards urban spaces. In this sense, Cartier-Bresson forms a 
prelude in the terrain vague text to the third context staged by Solà-Morales’s following 
list of photographers. 
 
FIGURE 60: Cartier-Bresson, Henri. Behind the Gare Saint-Lazare. Paris, 1932. Photograph. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
364 Hostetler, Lisa. “Louis Faurer: Finding the Self in Others.” Street Seen: The Psychological Gesture in 
American Photography, 1940-1959. Munich; New York: DelMonico Books - Prestel, 2010. 75.

## S. 195

192 
Third context and list of references: initial photographic gaze 
The third set of photographers comes in direct relation with Solà-Morales’s 
analysis of the terrain vague etymology. Solà-Morales states: 
“The triple signification of the French word vague as wave, vacant and vague is present 
in a multitude of photographic images, in which the most recent photography, from John 
Davies to David Plowden, from Thomas Strath [SIC] to Jannes Linders, from Manolo Laguillo 
to Olivio Barbieri [SIC], has captured the condition of these spaces as internal to the city yet 
at the same time external to its everyday utilization.”365 
He moves on to describe these spaces within three main points: “memory of the 
past predominate the present”, then “obsolete spaces in which only a few residual values 
seem to manage to survive” and finally “external spaces, strange spaces left outside the 
city’s effective circuits and productive structures.”366 These three characters coupled with 
the triple signification of “vague” seem to be the cause of the photographers’ enumeration. 
Yet, the artists mentioned by Solà-Morales represent more than mere examples of terrain 
vague photography from a certain period, as it seems at first glance. Each of the artists 
opens a gateway to a network of relations and inferences between the notion terrain 
vague, its characteristics as presented in the essay, and the author of the context. The 
outcome is a notion empowered by a rhizomatic interchange of visual references and their 
connotations. 
John Davies – first reference, first visual 
Solà-Morales’s third list of photographers begins with a noted photographer: John 
Davies. The artist photographer, other than being an important reference among 
photographers of the terrain vague, is also present in various links to the author Solà-
Morales himself. More importantly, he is directly linked to a text and an author, Ian 
Walker, which could be considered, as described in what follows, a direct precedent to 
Solà-Morales’s essay from the point of view of significations and notions. 
 First, John Davies was a contemporary artist to Solà-Morales. Born in 1949, he 
lived and worked in Great Britain while touring Western Europe for his photography, 
including Barcelona and its suburbs. He gained an international reputation for his black 
and white photography of high vantage points with wide-angle lens, depicting in still-like 
photographs the changes in European mainly post-industrial landscapes. His most famous 
works that involve scenery of terrains vagues were published under the peculiar title A 
                                                                                                                                                                        
365 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
366 Solà-Morales. Ibid.

## S. 196

193 
 
Green and Pleasant Land367 where he focused on sceneries of industrial sites in the United 
Kingdom in 1987, then a second publication entitled Cross Currents368 in 1992 where he 
focused on other cities and regions in Europe, only a couple of years before the 
publication of the “Terrain Vague” essay. Barcelona and Llobregat appear in this 
publication, alluding to possible direct contact between the author and the photographer 
when the former was writing the essay. 
On another note, Davies’s photograph of Rotterdam in 1970 in Urban Landscapes 
was chosen to represent the notion of terrain vague in the post-mortem publication Los 
Artículos de Any369, as it was the only photograph accompanying the "Terrain Vague" 
essay in this publication. The same photograph was also published in the Quaderns 
version of the essay in 1996. In Urban Landscapes, Davies is described as a 
“master of landscape-like views with a wealth of details and a composition that clarifies 
functional connections between various (architectonic) visual elements.”370 
It was perhaps this ability to clarify connections that earned him eventually the 
status of visual representative of the terrain vague in the before-mentioned publications. 
All the elements of his photograph are interlinked; be it human and car, landscape and 
factory, or even solids and fluids, these compositions allude to larger frameworks of 
relations. Another photograph of Davies, “Ciudad de Vigo” from 1992, is also recurrent in 
the archives of Solà-Morales. A slide of it was sent within a set of ten slides to the 1994 
Triennale di Milano seminar. Davies’s photograph comes in first place in the slides list371. 
Pietro Derossi from the Triennale di Milano had requested from Solà-Morales, for 
instance, visual references for his lecture and for possible post-conference publications372. 
The slides were thus sent to Milano in 1994 along with a letter by Solà-Morales 
describing, for the first time, his intentions for his coming lecture there: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
367 A title as ironic as the meanings charged in the photographs contained in the book. Davies’s gaze oscillates 
between a strong fascination and a deep sense of loss towards these large plains and gothic-like agriculture 
structures. - Davies, John. A Green and Pleasant Land. First edition. Manchester: Cornerhouse Publications, 
1987. 
368 Davies, John, and Ian Walker. Cross Currents John Davies. Cardiff: Ffotogallery Cornerhouse publ., 1992. 
369 Trans.: “The articles of Any”, a collection of all the essays of Solà-Morales in the ANY publications. - Solà-
Morales, Ignasi de. Los Artículos de Any. Barcelona: Fundación Caja de Arquitectos, 2009. 
N.b. According to Eulàlia Serra and Clarà de Solà-Morales, the Fundación Caja de Arquitectos requested their 
assistance when they planned to make re-publications of some of Solà-Morales’s essays as a commemoration 
of his achievements. The agreement finally was to republish in Spanish the essays Solà-Morales wrote within 
the Anyone corporation in order to make them more visible and accessible to the Spanish audience. 
370 Cusveller, Sjoerd, Gabriele Basilico, and John Davies, eds. Stadslandschappen - Urban Landscapes. 
Rotterdam: Uitgeverij 010 Publishers, 1986. 22. 
371 The slide was also sent to a conference in Rhodes given by Solà-Morales the following year. 
372 The diapositives were eventually returned to Solà-Morales without being used in the publication of the 
Triennale conference proceedings. In fact, no visuals were used in the Triennale edition of “Terrain Vague”

## S. 197

194 
“It is a reflection, both urban and political, on the residual spaces of the city. It 
corresponds to a line of work that I'm developing and in which, as you will see, the tradition 
of hermeneutic thought has an important weight.”373 
Note the accent on political intent present in Solà-Morales’s description of the 
terrain vague as spaces escaping the order and dominion of the city’s productive system. 
This is also apparent in his manifesto-like subtle call for action at the end of his essay. In 
this context, Davies’s photographs are far from innocent, where estrangement is put in 
focus to expose the contrast of the orderly city versus the ambiguous terrain vague374. 
 
FIGURE 61: Davies, John. Urban Landscapes. Europoort, Rotterdam, 1970. 
 
FIGURE 62: Davies, John. Goats and Sheep. Barcelona - Assiciation of Catalan Architects, 1990. Photograph. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
373 Our trans. from Spanish: “Se trata de una reflexión a la vez urbana y política sobre los espacios residuales 
de la ciudad. Corresponde a una línea de trabajo que estoy desarrollando y en la que, como podrás 
comprobar, tiene un peso importante la tradición del pensamiento hermenéutico.” - Letter: Ignasi de Solà-
Morales to Pietro Derossi. 3 Nov. 1995. Letter-fax. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archives. 
374 Notice also Solà-Morales’s interest in linking hermeneutic thought with the terrain vague notion.

## S. 198

195 
 
Both of his photographs, “Rotterdam” and “Ciudad de Vigo”, are representative of 
Davies’s photography of terrains vagues. Mainly devoid of humans, almost sterilized, one 
could sense a dramatization of non-place no man’s land where the sky weighs in 
consistency as much as the vast terrains depicted; “obsolete, external, strange spaces”, as 
Solà-Morales describes. Be it Rotterdam or Barcelona, the context is always within a 
Europe diversified through its terrains vagues. As Christopher Coppock, director of 
Ffotogallery in Wales, puts it in the foreword of the book Cross Currents: 
“a timely and evocative set of photographic prints from across the twelve member states 
of the European Community […] the nuances of an innocent slag heap take on a different 
profile from country to country.”375 
John Davies’s photographs portray thus a characteristic of terrain vague as 
intimately linked to the context of city and country to which they adhere. In so doing, 
these photographs are perhaps the best representative of Solà-Morales’s observation: 
“mentally exterior to the physical interior of the city, appearing as its negative image as 
much in the sense of critique as in that of possible alternative.”376 This negative image of 
the city is what Davies’s photographs often portray. He was one of the few who could 
clearly depict these nuances. Having endeavored a journey in Europe from 1984 up to the 
summer of 1992, he was exposed to various regions and recorded terrains from his first 
continental commission in Berlin up to photographs from Cork in Ireland. Remnants of 
wars, a long-gone coalfield, forgotten memorials of veterans, and a traditional windmill, 
only Davies could depict peculiarities in seemingly trifling terrains. As the artist himself 
puts it: 
“...the thing that is most significant for me is the landscape that is evolving and 
changing. Through the actions of people rather than the changes in the seasons… I am 
attracted to areas where symbols of our modern life-styles merge with the symbols of our past 
… where old meets new; urban meets rural; 'natural' meets artificial.” 377 
Once again a direct relation is to be noticed between the photographer's intent 
and Solà-Morales’s description of terrain vague: “forgotten places, (where) the memory of 
the past seems to predominate over the present.”378 
On another note, the description of “otherness” and “strangeness before 
ourselves”379 present in Solà-Morales’s essay does not appear in an equal fashion in Davies’s 
photographs, yet the questioning is present: how could a terrain vague in France be 
                                                                                                                                                                        
375 Coppock, Christopher. “Foreword.” Cross Currents John Davies. Cardiff Wales: Ffotogallery, 1992. 1–2. 
376 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass.: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 118–123. 
377 From the artist's statement, in: Durand, Régis, Claude Caroly, and Alain Buttard. Les Quatres Saisons Du 
Territoire: Le Printemps. 2. CAC de Belfort, 1989. 125. 
378 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Mass.: Anyone 
Corp.  ; MIT Press, 1995. 118–123. 
379 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 121.

## S. 199

196 
different from that in Britain? What human action altered the space? Who are the 
stakeholders? What impact do these actions have on the terrain and its corresponding 
political and economic systems? Davies’s photograph of Rotterdam, constantly referenced 
by Solà-Morales, is a good depiction of these questions. A car and a partially hidden 
person in the foreground are foreshadowed by large industrial scenery in the horizon, 
separated from the human by a vast body of water. A triple-presence of terrain vague 
hence divides the photograph into layers, and questions arise regarding possible relations, 
and the lack thereof, between these layers: effects of industry on the land, effects on the 
water, on the human, and vice versa. The actual human presence is minimal, yet he or she 
equally appears as though a mastermind contemplating his or her work. A network of 
politically charged relations is thus set in motion. 
Perhaps Davies, more than others on Solà-Morales’s list, was the closest at 
depicting this political value inherent within the terrains he photographed. Through 
juxtapositions of present, future prospects and past remnants, Davies derives cultural 
meaning from the landscapes he photographed, which are very politically loaded. In the 
photographer's words:
