“I thought I was committing a political act in making pictures in which I wanted people 
to experience this respect for the land.”380 
The artist advocates that this respect applies for both the land and the dwellers. In 
the case of Britain, the artist's homeland, it is a respect for the people who live in the land; 
in the case of central Europe, it is the respect for people who live elsewhere, the other. 
Questions of identity in Davies’s work not only dispute the 'identity' of the terrain vague 
he depicts, but also the identity of dwellers of such places, of those who alter these spaces 
and the political charge such human interferences on the terrain vague hold. 
On another level, the presence of John Davies in the foreground of the second set 
of photographers in Solà-Morales’s essay is quite adequate as he permeates a link between 
the first and second list of photographers. We notice once more a recurrent fascination 
with the everyday and what could at first glance read as banal; a fascination inherited 
from Surrealism, from which Davies was much inspired. Again, as though in accordance 
with Solà-Morales’s interests, Davies’s influences also include the Surrealist movement. As 
Ian Walker describes: 
“Surrealism has meant much to John Davies in the past, and a 'surrealism of the real' 
continues to give added pungency to his views of other people's everyday reality. To view the 
lives of others as surreal can seem condescending, if one does so only in order to confirm 
one's own normality. In fact, it should make us question our apparent 'normality'.”381 
                                                                                                                                                                        
380 Davies, John, and Ian Walker. Cross Currents John Davies. Cardiff: Ffotogallery Cornerhouse publ., 1992. 
Butler, Susan. “Landscapes in Transition: John Davies Interviewed by Susan Butler.” Creative Camera 
231.231 (1985). 16. 
381 Ibid.

## S. 200

197 
 
Reading the terrain vague from this point of view can prove paradoxical. Places 
commonly viewed as disturbing and polluted are gazed upon in awe. Cultural, political 
and social concerns are married with a quest for beauty and aesthetic pleasance. In this 
sense, Davies’s photographs fit perfectly in Solà-Morales’s dichotomized description of the 
terrain vague as being both positive and negative, internal and external to the city, a 
threat as well as an opportunity. It makes thus sense that the photographer is present in 
Solà-Morales’s references often as the main visual representative of his essay and his 
interpretation of the terrain vague notion. One must keep in mind, when looking into 
these photographs, that the attention and the final note are on the idea of change rather 
than a picturesque depiction of nostalgia. Be it the evolution of the industrial north of the 
UK as in A Green and Pleasant Land382 in 1987, or the “Return of Nature” in the “Littoral 
Sud-Ouest of France” in 2001, the running theme is always the dynamic transformations 
of land. In this sense, the terrain vague is never static, always in motion, in transition, as 
advocated in the last section of Solà-Morales’s essay where the accent is on time and 
transformation. 
Moreover, the link between Solà-Morales and John Davies stretches beyond a 
mere author-reference relationship: Davies’s particular interest in Barcelona had set the 
right stage for more connections between Solà-Morales’s terrain vague and Davies’s work. 
It comes as no surprise that Ian Walker initiates his article on Davies with a description of 
his photograph of a 1990 Barcelona: 
“Near Barcelona, 1990. In the foreground, a man is digging in a field. Behind him, a train 
passes. Displayed for its passengers are two billboards: one for Kodak film proclaims Color 
Olimpico, the other for Chesterfield cigarettes features a man, a woman and a car. Next to 
that is an old farm. Across the flat fields is a large car park next to industrial or commercial 
buildings. Beyond is the city and, beyond that, the mountains.” 383 
He describes an image of Barcelona as portrayed by Davies: a city surrounded by 
mountains and terrains vagues. Walker puts in foreground Barcelona as a crucial city in 
such discussions and in relation to depictions. 
Walker again describes in his same publication yet another photograph of Davies 
depicting the outskirts of Barcelona: 
“In the background, rows of tower block apartments all face the same direction. In the 
foreground is an area of rough ground, across which passes a flock of goats.”384 
These photographs and depictions seem well embedded in Solà-Morales’s musée 
imaginaire when he states: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
382 Davies, John. A Green and Pleasant Land. First edition. Manchester: Cornerhouse Publications, 1987. 
383 Davies, John, and Ian Walker. Cross Currents John Davies. Cardiff: Ffotogallery publ., 1992. 2-3. 
384 This photograph is so representative of Davies’s work that it is chosen to appear on the book front cover.

## S. 201

198 
“Why this landscape sensibility, as such unlimited, with regard to this artificial nature 
populated by surprises, its limits imprecise, devoid of strong forms representing the 
power?”385 
Indeed, Davies was also interested particularly in Barcelona. He publishes in 1990 
in an edition of Quaderns dedicated to the city: “Barcelona: una Geografia Virtual.”386 
Along with Enric Miralles and Marcià Codinachs, the three take on the task of analyzing 
the area of Llobregat. Solà-Morales was of course involved with the journal Quaderns 
where he was invited often to contribute. Whether Solà-Morales knew Davies in person 
does not thus change the fact that his photography and publications were well known to 
the author. 
In return, such a triangular connection Solà-Morales-Davies-Barcelona legitimizes 
a bond between Solà-Morales’s terrain vague and his city, Barcelona. Unlike most of his 
colleagues, Solà-Morales was a very international figure inspired by a variety of places 
where he taught, lectured, or simply visited. Barcelona's direct influence on the notion of 
terrain vague cannot therefore be considered as a mere given. Yet, the aforementioned 
triangular connection gives an indicator of another possible connection, especially that 
the Quaderns edition in which Davies appears is in 1990, only a few years before the 
publication of the “Terrain Vague” essay. Davies’s interest in Barcelona is likewise 
entangled with what the artist was seeking at the time: the effect of change and humanmade transformations on the land. Retaining in parallel a transforming periphery and a 
drastic transformation of the entire city in preparation for the 1992 Olympic games a 
couple of years later, Davies found in Barcelona a perfect field to observe, photograph, 
experiment. Solà-Morales, in turn, found in Davies’s depictions a prosperous example of 
his urban visions of that time including the terrain vague. 
In parallel, not only was John Davies connected with Solà-Morales’s work, his 
network of collaborations includes the other artists mentioned by the author in the 
“Terrain Vague” essay and other related essays. The book Urban Landscapes387, from 
which Solà-Morales derives his photograph of Rotterdam 388, is an account of such 
collaborations. Joined under the same title of Stadslandschappen, photographs of both 
Davies and Jannes Linders are confronted, as well as Gabriele Basilico and Gilbert 
Fastenaekens, two artists who could have readily been referenced by Solà-Morales as he 
did in other publications. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
385 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
386 Davies, John, Marcià Codinachs, and Enric Miralles. “A Llobregat.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 
186 - Barcelona: una Geografia Virtual (1990): 16–23. 
387 Cusveller, Sjoerd, ed. Stadslandschappen - Urban Landscapes. Rotterdam: Uitgeverij 010 Publishers, 1986. 
388 Used as hitherto mentioned in the “Terrain Vague” essay as published in Quaderns.

## S. 202

199 
 
 
FIGURE 63: Davies, John. Llobregat. Barcelona - Assiciation of Catalan Architects, 1990. Photograph. 
 
FIGURE 64: Davies, John. Martin Gropius Bau, Kreuzberg, Berlin. - Internationale Bauausstellung Berlin, 1984. 
Photograph. 
This network of artistic collaborations proves closer than it seems at first. It also 
sets the stage for interactions that lead to the creation of the notion of terrain vague 
during this period. Perhaps what Solà-Morales fails to accentuate in his essay is the 
importance of these various collaborations between artists at the time in the formulation 
of new modes of understanding the urban fabric. The publication of Stadslandschappen is 
an example of artists cooperating towards a new merging of urban and landscape 
formulations, through the medium of photography. Their attention is stirring away from 
the normalized city to more uncanny urban spaces, such as terrains vagues. 
Another very important publication in this context is the above-mentioned Cross 
Currents389. Out of this affiliation between John Davies and the sociologist theoretician Ian 
Walker, the essay “transitions”, present in this publication, proves notable as a 
                                                                                                                                                                        
389 Davies, John, and Ian Walker. Cross Currents John Davies. Cardiff: Ffotogallery Cornerhouse publ., 1992.

## S. 203

200 
prerequisite for understanding the essay of Solà-Morales. From descriptions of Davies’s 
photography, Walker derives various notions that appeared to be reinterpreted a couple of 
years later by Solà-Morales in order to formulate the notion of terrain vague. Accordingly, 
there seems to be a dialogue back and forth between Solà-Morales’s essay and that of 
Walker. Notions are shared, as well as references. Both authors tackle the urban realm 
from the point of view of photography in a way that allows discussion over “continuity”, 
“otherness” and “cultivation of meaning”. In parallel, both adopt the case study of Berlin 
and Barcelona390. Third, both take on the work of Davies and the exhibition Family of 
Man as legitimate examples towards demonstrating a specific urban vision focused on 
terrain vague, or “slagheap” as named by Walker. 
Solà-Morales was moreover aware of Walker's writings. Being interdisciplinary 
himself, the theoretician-philosopher-architect was probably aware of Walker who was at 
the same time photographer, writer on photography and lecturer in cultural studies at 
Gwent College, Newport. Just as Solà-Morales does in his essay, Walker tackles the 
subject of “slagheaps” first through photographs and what photography could signify. He 
states: 
“…the exploitation of the very factuality of photography to create political and social 
meanings that extend far beyond the specific subject of the specific photograph. The sense we 
have of the neutrality of the camera is in fact being used to make us reflect upon the meaning 
of what it is we are seeing.”391 
Walker continues on to describe the relation of photography's signification in 
relation to the sense of place, and lack thereof, which photography can convey of spaces 
photographed. His approach to the subject comes quite similar to Solà-Morales’s 
interpretation of Krauss’s writing about signifying and codes392. More importantly, it offers 
a layer of understanding of how the signification of placelessness is linked with 
photography, as Solà-Morales densely explains. Walker's focus however is less about the 
actual “slagheap” and more about the “cultivation of land and culture” within these 
spaces. As Christopher Coppock393 describes in the foreword of the same publication “Ian 
Walker observes […] that even the nuances of an innocent slag heap take on a different 
profile from country to country.”394 The focus of Walker's article is similar to that of Solà-
Morales, yet the accent is set on identity and transition as the title (“Transitions”) 
declares. Walker derives meaning from Davies’s photograph in order to convey the 
importance of these photographs: raising public awareness of the importance of the spaces 
conveyed. In so doing, the discussion on otherness and strangeness in Solà-Morales’s essay 
                                                                                                                                                                        
390 Although Solà-Morales’s reference to Barcelona is indirect, mainly through visuals. 
391 Walker, Ian. “Transitions.” Cross Currents John Davies. Cardiff: FFotogallery and Cornerhouse publ., 
1992. 5-16. 
392 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
393 Director of Ffotogallery Wales. 
394 Coppock, Christopher. “Foreword.” Cross Currents John Davies. Cardiff Wales: Ffotogallery, 1992. 1–2.

## S. 204

201 
 
takes on a more cultural nuance. As Solà-Morales explains the sense of strangeness to our 
own self, triggered in the terrain vague, Walker refers to the same sense of strangeness as 
linked with the dangerous attempt to tame such spaces within one identity: 
“Identity is never very secure and is, in one sense, defined against what we are not; it 
slips and slides. […] to have one safe, secure identity is limiting and potentially dangerous - 
certainly to 'others' and ultimately to oneself.”395 
Walker in addition closes the loop to Solà-Morales’s essay. He reconnects the 
notion of otherness back to Surrealism. As previously discussed, Solà-Morales’s first set of 
photographers refer to a mostly German context, consisting of artists who adhere to the 
Surrealist and Dada movements of the period. Walker similarly states clearly the link 
between the fascination with everyday life found in Davies’s photographs and the 
Surrealists' fascination with the everyday; all within the context of describing Davies’s 
photograph of Barcelona's periphery: 
“Would the shepherd see the striking juxtaposition of his animals and the tower blocks 
of Barcelona? Surrealism has meant much to John Davies in the past, and a 'surrealism of the 
real' continues to give added pungency to his views of other people's everyday reality.”396 
Moreover, he continues further by linking these to the notion of estrangement, 
bridging thus the section on photography in Solà-Morales’s essay with his section on 
otherness. 
 
On another level, a couple of references presented by Solà-Morales in the “Terrain 
Vague” essay seem to have an ambiguous relation to the discourse on the notion. 
Correspondingly, Walker provides in his essay clarifications on the relation between these 
same references and the notion. On the one hand, both Walker and Solà-Morales adopt as 
reference the exhibition Family of Man. Their interpretations seem complementary in 
defining a relation between the notion of terrain vague and the sense of place and 
belonging. On the other hand, both authors adopt the city of Berlin and its evolution as 
case study to discuss the problematic notion of continuity. Both describe the city; one 
through the photograph of Davies of 1984397, the other through three instances in the 
timeline of Alexanderplatz398. Both accentuate the presence of large areas of terrains 
vagues in one instance of Berlin’s evolution. Yet, small nuances in the interpretation of 
these observations set the intent of the authors on two different, yet complementary, 
tones. Walker, the predecessor, deduces from the description of Davies’s photograph a 
                                                                                                                                                                        
395 Walker, Ian. “Transitions.” Cross Currents John Davies. Cardiff: FFotogallery and Cornerhouse publ., 
1992. 5-16. 
396 Ibid. 
397 Walker writes in Cross Currents: “Berlin, 1984. The Berlin Wall cuts between two nineteenth century 
buildings designed to face each other. […] In front of these buildings, next to the wall, is a large area of 
empty land. Something is going on there, earth is being moved, but it's hard to tell why.” – Ibid. 
398 As aforesaid, the Alexanderplatz case is present in the “ Terrain Vague ” essay in the Anyplace edition, 
absent from future publications of the essay.

## S. 205

202 
juxtaposed layering of time and identity that renders the landscape into a “fabric of 
meanings”: 
“The meshing of cultural meanings in the landscape does not happen only in the present 
- 'horizontally', the local existing next to the national next to the multinational. It also works 
'vertically' - in history, as the past continues to signify, through layer upon layer of sites and 
artifacts.”399 
By confronting three moments in the history line of Berlin, Solà-Morales seems to 
build on the same approach of layering. Yet he pushes the argument further in order to 
demonstrate the importance of “continuity” in future implementations on the terrain 
vague, giving the latter a particular status as an important moment to observe carefully 
within this layering of time and meanings. 
Finally, even though the focus of Walker's article is elsewhere, he himself 
mentions peculiarly the term terrain vague. Far from attempting a definition, he casually 
employs the term, two years before Solà-Morales’s essay, in order to pinpoint the 
importance of the no man’s land in the dialogue city-countryside. Walker states: 
“As urban features spread across the rural landscape, the main battleground between the 
values of the city and those of the country is in that ill-defined area where the two comingle, in the 'no mans land', the 'terrain vague', between them.”400 
Walker further in the article reminds that this battleground, i.e. the terrain vague, 
is closely linked to a question of diversity in cultures and, more importantly, the 
connection between them. Conceivably, Walker emphasized only one part of the terrain 
vague discourse related to notions of cultural diversity, otherness and the passage in 
landscape, which in turn alters culture: 
“…bodily or imaginatively, we pass through the landscape - for business, for leisure, to 
see the world, to be part of the world. And the landscape is marked by our passage.”401 
In so doing, Walker defines the terrain vague less as an actual space and more as 
an element of time and transitions, and as a dynamic between city and country where 
connections are key. This position thus comes close to the last part of Solà-Morales’s essay 
in which terrain vague is explained in relation to the effective system of the city and to 
the notion of continuity. The idea promoted here is that Walker's article clarifies some 
ambiguous points of Solà-Morales’s essay, mainly what the latter means by continuity and 
how the terrain vague is a temporal element and a space where strangeness strikes the 
psyche. And in turn, Solà-Morales’s essay is seen under a condensed light, as though the 
essay was a summary of Walker's position among others of that era. The essay 
“Transitions” thus takes on the position of predecessor to that of terrain vague. In fact, 
Solà-Morales tackles similar notions of that article yet brings them forward under clear 
light, more effectively focused around the main discourse, the terrain vague. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
399 Ibid. 
400 Ibid. 
401 Ibid.

## S. 206

203 
 
 
FIGURE 65: Slides on “Terrain Vague.” - Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box Territorios. Projection 
Slides. 
 
FIGURE 66: Linders, Jannes. Urban Landscapes. Rotterdam, 1970.

## S. 207

204 
Jannes Linders – a gaze on time and change 
The photographer Jannes Linders is cited in the “Terrain Vague” essay, referenced 
in the visuals present in the Quaderns publication of the essay, as well as recurrent in 
Solà-Morales’s archives. His photograph is included in the list of slides sent to the seminar 
of the Triennale di Milano, as well as another conference titled Building on the Built402 in 
Rhodes. The photograph entitled “Rotterdam” from 1970 was taken from the publication 
Urban Landscapes403. Solà-Morales had chosen the right photograph when he opted for 
Linders’ to include in his essay. The city, the publication, the commission behind it and 
the photographers involved, all create the perfect setting to describe what Solà-Morales 
defined as a “shift in gaze” 404 towards the terrain vague. The book entitled both 
Stadslandschappen and Urban Landscapes is the outcome of a commission given to four 
photographers to portray different areas in Rotterdam405. The aim was to provide a 
reinterpretation of Rotterdam's “present cacophony of architectural objects and spaces, 
which, heaped together arbitrarily, constitute the city scene.”406 By inviting contemporary 
photographers specialized in documenting the changing urban fabric, the hope was to 
alter the common given image of the city of Rotterdam of that given period. As explained 
by Mariëtte Haveman in the same publication: 
“The photos in this book belong to a different category: that of documentary photo 
assignments, in which the environment is used as a test for the interpretative capacity of the 
photographers.”407 
Haveman goes on by pinpointing that it was not the “objective beauty of the 
surrounding” that was sought after, but rather the photographers' ability to “find his own 
harmony” in his photographic representation of the city. Out of this collaboration 
between four photographers, a “new gaze” upon the city of Rotterdam was tested, one in 
which semi-industrial often-abandoned areas are in the foreground. Inciting discussion 
since its postwar phase, inspiring movies in and about Rotterdam in the 1930s such as 
Ivens and Kiljan’s, the city was finally captured in this given publication in its strength as 
                                                                                                                                                                        
402 Trans. from French : “Construire sur le Construit.” 
403 The photograph is wrongly referenced as taken from Urban Landscape. It was repeated and quoted, again 
in the same spirit of godly-sent referencing, which is how the original text of Solà-Morales seems to be 
treated. 
404 From “Terrain Vague”: “the phenomenon in which we are interested is subsequent to this, and dates from 
the seventies, with the inauguration of quite another sensibility that began to direct a different gaze on the 
big cities.” - Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, 
Massachusetts: The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
405 The photographers, as aforementioned, were Linders, Basilico, Davies and Fastenaekens. 
406 As stated in the book's introductory article written by Umberto Barbieri and Evert Rodrigo. - Barbieri, 
Umberto, and Evert Rodrigo. “Architecture Photography: Image and Imagination.” Stadslandschappen - 
Urban Landscapes. Ed. Sjoerd Cusveller. Rotterdam: Uitgeverij 010 Publishers, 1986. 7–8. 
407 Haveman, Mariëtte. “Whal Turns a Photo Inlo a Town View?” Stadslandschappen - Urban Landscapes. Ed. 
Sjoerd Cusveller. Rotterdam: Uitgeverij 010 Publishers, 1986. 11–12.

## S. 208

205 
 
the exemplary city of “Urban Landscapes”. This 'new' representation of the city shaped 
thus a new identity and a changing gaze focused on its terrains vagues. 
Linders was adequately chosen to participate in this endeavor. Having previously 
photographed Rotterdam's harbor and some town views, his 'gaze' was rightly fitting the 
publication's apparent aim: a photographer who recurrently directs his gaze at the terrain 
vague and similar situations in the urban environment “in which formal and informal 
structures are juxtaposed and mixed up.”408 The terrain vague in Linders’ photograph is 
hence a composition of juxtaposed layers of time and signifiers. Moreover, Linders’ terrain 
vague is anything but static. As seen in another commission entitled “The Dutch 
Landscape”, Linders and André-Pierre Lamoth portray spaces in the process of being 
changed, where developments are constantly lurking in the horizon. The transient aspect 
of the terrain vague is evoked and put in the foreground. Thus, in this quest for a new 
urban gaze initiated through photography, Linders’ photographs and approach are 
particularly refreshing as they accentuate the notion of time and human-induced change 
on the urban landscape, and particularly on its terrains vagues. 
David Plowden – an expectant gaze on absence 
In a correspondence recorded in Solà-Morales’s private archives, the author writes 
to Evelina Calvi for the Triennale di Milano: 
“Photographs for my article "Terrain Vague". You can choose any three photos of artists, 
such as Olivo Barbieri, Manolo Laguillo, David Plowden or others cited in the text. The 
previous Triennale published beautiful photographs of this type but I do not think it's logical 
to repeat. The only matter that should be retained is that the photographs are "signed" by 
contemporary photographers, on urban landscapes whose common characteristic are the 
vacant, obsolete and expectant spaces. I fully trust in your selection capabilities.”409 
Hence, the three main photographers who seem to first come to Solà-Morales’s 
mind when evoking the subject of terrain vague are two Europeans, Manolo Laguillo and 
Olivo Barbieri, and one American, David Plowden410. 
Plowden's photographs appear in several instances in Solà-Morales’s archives and 
publications linked with the subject of terrain vague. Other than being referenced within 
                                                                                                                                                                        
408 Ibid. 
409 Our trans. from Spanish: “Fotografias para mi articulo “Terrain Vague”. Podeis escoger 3 fotos cualquiera 
de autores del tipo Olivio [SIC] Barbieri, Manolo Laguillo, David Plowden, tales como las que se citan en el 
texto. La Triennale anterior publicó en su catalogo final bellas fotografias de este tipo, pero no creo que sea 
lógico repetirlas. Lo unico que deben tener en común ha de ser que se trate de fotografías “firrnadas” por 
fotógrafos actuales sobre paisajes urbanos cuya característica común sean los espacios vacios, obsoletes y 
expectantes. Confio plenamente en vuestra capacidad de selección.” 
 - Letter: Ignasi de Solà-Morales to Evelina Calvi. 24 Oct. 1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive. 
410 Focus is once more on a European-American context.

## S. 209

206 
the text, one of his photographs of Chicago in 1985 “Industrial Landscapes” appears in the 
two publications of the “Terrain Vague” essay in Quaderns in 1996. The same photograph 
also appears in the XIX UIA Congress publication illustrating Solà-Morales’s essay entitled 
“Present and Futures”411. As with Linders and Davies, a slide of the photo could be found 
in Solà-Morales’s archives. It was also used in various occasions: the Triennale di Milano 
seminar, the Anyplace conference, as well as sent to Luc Lévesque as possible visual 
reference for the publication of “Urbanité Interstitielle” in Inter 412 . A post-mortem 
publication of the “Terrain Vague” essay in the book Naturaleza y Artificio413 displays as 
well the same photograph in the foreground of the essay, as though it were the most 
representative of Solà-Morales’s thought. Moreover, again in the archives, Plowden's 
photograph as listed for a Rhode Island conference on terrains vagues, is the only 
photograph accompanied with a brief description: “Abandoned grain elevator west of the 
loop.”414 
Similar to Linders, the photographer David Plowden, also figuring in Solà-
Morales’s list, captured landscapes and wastelands in transformation. His approach 
however was quite different from the former as both his intent and his subjects of interest 
diverged noticeably from Linders’, giving thus a character of terrain vague distinct from 
others on Solà-Morales’s list. Plowden published many books displaying his photography, 
of which the titles are quite revealing of his interests: End of an Era, Vanishing Point, 
Requiem for Steam. Plowden's black and white photography is known to represent sides 
of American culture that are now long gone. Accordingly, David McCullough writes: 
“Plowden has produced some of the most powerful photographs we have of man-made 
America. He is propelled, driven, by a sense of time running out and the feeling that he must 
not just make a record, but confer a kind of immortality on certain aspects of American 
civilization before they vanish.”415 
The steam railroad, the steamboat, the farm barn or other national creatures; the 
depiction of these American disappearing landscapes are what gave the photographer a 
place in Solà-Morales’s list. 
In his photographic book Heartland, Plowden makes the eulogy of grain elevators 
describing them as the cathedrals of vast plains, as testifiers of human presence and action 
on land. Quoting the photographer: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
411 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de, Xavier Costa, and Susana Landrove, eds. “Present and Futures: Architecture in 
Cities.” XIX Congress of the International Union of Architects UIA. Barcelona: Actar, 1996. 10–23. 
412 a variation on the “Terrain Vague” essay as an outcome of correspondence between Solà-Morales and 
Lévesque. - Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Urbanité Interstitielle.” Trans. Luc Lévesque. Inter: art actuel 61 
(1995): 27-28. 
413 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Naturaleza y Artificio el ideal pintoresco en la arquitectura y el 
paisajismo contemporáneos. Ed. Iñaki Ábalos. Barcelona: Gustavo Gili, 2009. 123–133. 
414 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Rhodes” - From Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive. 
415 Steve Edwards. David Plowden: Vanishing Point: Fifty Years of Photography. New York: W Norton & 
Company Incorporated, 2007. 17.

## S. 210

207 
 
“In many farmers' eyes they are not considered ‘prairie icons’, ‘prairie cathedrals’, or 
‘prairie sentinels’, as they have been called by outlanders like me, who appreciate them as 
more than simply storage bins. […] it reminds a man the place is still inhabited.”416 
Once again, Plowden's interests seem similar to Linders’. They both display manmade marks on the terrain vague, evidence of human intervention in seemingly 
abandoned spaces. Plowden's terrain vague is however strikingly American. Born in 
Boston, Massachusetts in 1932, Plowden was also a contemporary (though a little older in 
generation) of Solà-Morales, but lived and worked in the United States. His transient 
transforming lands are not devoid of a national sense; on the contrary their depiction of 
disappearing cultural features accentuates even more the 'American identity' of the land 
and of the photographer's gaze. 
 
FIGURE 67: Plowden, David. Abandoned Grain Elevator. West of Chicago, 1983. 
 
FIGURE 68: Plowden, David. Grain Elevator. Russell, Kansas, 1983. Photograph. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
416 Plowden, David. “Heartland.” Heartland: The Plains and the Prairie. First edition. New York: W.W. 
Norton & Company, 2013. 9–10.

## S. 211

208 
Plowden's affiliation with the American terrains vagues goes however beyond a 
mere nationalist feel. Poetic, nostalgic, his terrains vagues are charged with emotional 
attachment, wonder and childhood memories. The poetic flair is equally visible in his 
writing about the places and terrains vagues he photographed: 
“If people were going to come here they'd say, 'My God, what an awful place. How 
hideous this is!' But if you look quickly-look now with the sun pouring down on some of 
these great silos and cranes-it's really very, very impressive.”417 
The poetics in description also influenced the critics writing about Plowden's 
photography; one finds it hard not to be marveled by the photographer's depictions. Steve 
Edwards writes a dozen pages of what feels to be poetry in prose 418 serving as an 
introduction to Plowden's person and work in the photography book Vanishing Point. 
This particular charm in Plowden's wastelands adds to the mystification of the terrain 
vague. His depictions are far less negative in spirit than what may appear at first glance. 
The amalgam of bitter sweetness seems to adhere perfectly with Solà-Morales’s definition 
of terrain vague as neither negative nor positive419, which could explain in retrospect the 
author's recurrent use of the photographer's work to illustrate his point of view. 
Finally, as a side note, it is interesting to note how Solà-Morales casually shifts 
between European and American artists to illustrate his essay, which sets his discourse on 
the terrain vague in a rather western context, one foot in the American and one in the 
European, mainly Western Europe420. 
Olivo Barbieri – a gaze on human presence and disappearance 
As is the case of Plowden, the photographer Olivo Barbieri figures as well in the 
“Terrain Vague” essay both as a written reference and a visual one. The photographer, 
born in Emilia Romagna, Italy in 1954 is also a contemporary of Solà-Morales whom he 
might have personally met in one of his exhibitions in Europe or North America, or 
during the Venice Biennale in which Barbieri participated few times in a row between 
1993 and 1997. The photographer, as previously mentioned, is cited in Solà-Morales’s 
letter to Evelina Calvi421. Unlike the other two photographers named in the letter-fax to 
Milano, Barbieri himself emerged from this northern Italian context. One would expect 
                                                                                                                                                                        
417 Steve Edwards. David Plowden: Vanishing Point  : Fifty Years of Photography. New York: W Norton & 
Company Incorporated, 2007. 8. 
418 He states for instance: “I felt as if I were gazing into America's industrial stomach. Where I saw decay and 
detritus, Plowden saw something else…" - Ibid. 
419 In Solà-Morales’s words (from “Terrain Vague“) : “It certainly appears that the analogous terms we have 
remarked are generally preceded by a negative particle in-determinate, im-precise, un-certain, but it is no 
less the case that this absence of limit, this almost oceanic sentiment, to use Freud’s expression, is precisely 
the message which contains the expectations of mobility, vagrant roving, free time, liberty.” 
420 A common characteristic to Solà-Morales’s own trajectory, as we will see in the following chapter. 
421 Letter: Ignasi de Solà-Morales to Evelina Calvi. 24 Oct. 1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive.

## S. 212

209 
 
thus that Pietro Derossi and the team behind the Triennale di Milano, Saverio Monno and 
Pierantonio Bertè, were familiar with the photographer's work. 
Regardless of his acclaim, the photographer's name is nonetheless misspelled in 
several instances in Solà-Morales’s publications in Anyplace and Quaderns as well as later 
publications of the “Terrain Vague” essay422. These misspellings seem almost deliberate in 
their nonchalance. In Solà-Morales’s fragmentary Benjaminian style such misspellings 
seem to happen frequently. The author himself appears as a rhetoric flâneur, promenading 
between the various references without delving long in any of them, rather taking 
strength in their cumulative juxtaposition. 
Nonetheless, regardless of the misspelling, Barbieri is a well-known artist and 
photographer in Europe and particularly Barcelona 423. The connection Barbieri-Solà-
Morales was moreover strengthened in 1996 by another of Solà-Morales’s publications of 
the same year, aside from Quaderns’ publication of the “Terrain Vague” essay in which 
Barbieri's photograph appears. In Milano, where Solà-Morales was member of the 
editorial board of Lotus, a seminar in the format of a bus journey took place in January 
1996424 entitled Highway, Multiplicity. Barbieri was the designated photographer who 
documented the seminar and what was observed through the journey. The result of the 
seminar was the publication of a book, Paesaggi Ibridi425, which came out the same year, 
i.e. two years after the first publication of the “Terrain Vague” essay within the Triennale 
di Milano. What makes this publication particularly relevant is the fact that it included 
the first published citing of Solà-Morales’s essay. The latter is referenced in large in the 
publication, explicitly cited by Mirko Zardini in order to illustrate the argument that 
values of liberty and indeterminacy of the contemporary city are represented through its 
terrains vagues. Furthermore, similar ideas to Solà-Morales’s regarding urbanity appear in 
this publication: discussions on the Großstadt, the edge-city, the fragmented 'New 
Metropolis', it seems evident that those involved in the seminar were aware of Solà-
Morales’s writings and that a wave of mutual interests was circulating during this period 
in these architectural and urban spheres. This adds one more layer to the "Terrain Vague" 
essay as being a synthesist and catalyst of an era’s interests. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
422 The name was never corrected which alludes to the same approach of ‘word-for-word’ direct copying of 
Solà-Morales’s essay without more extensive look into references and detail. 
423 One can still find many of his books in Spanish and European libraries, more than Linders’ for instance. 
Even Manuel de Solà-Morales, brother of Ignasi, held in his private library some of Barbieri's books, which 
could be found now in his fonds at the COAC archives in Barcelona. 
424 The seminar took place on 26 January 1996, few months before the Quaderns 212 publication of “ Terrain 
Vague ” in June. That same year, the UIA XIX congress directed by Solà-Morales took place in Barcelona and, 
almost in parallel, the XIX esposizione international 'Identità e Differenze' was inaugurated in Milano in 
February 1996. 
425 Trans.: “ Hybrid Passages ” - Zardini, Mirko, Barbieri, Olivo, Aldo Aymonino, and Stefano, Ciorra, Pippo, 
Desideri, Paolo, Zucchi, Cino, Highway, Multiplicity Boeri, eds. Paesaggi Ibridi: Highway, Multiplicity. 
Milano: Skira, 1999.

## S. 213

210 
Another publication entitled I Volti della Città426 from 1998 sheds light on the 
collaborations between photographers and sometimes architects in the quest for a new 
urban understanding out of which emerged the interest and discourse on the terrain 
vague. The photography book is the result of collaboration between two known urban 
photographers of the period, Gabriele Basilico and Olivo Barbieri. The aim was to depict 
the various faces of a city, Bolzano in Italy, at the turn of the millennium. The 
photographs depict the different phases of construction, as well as the successive 
transformations of the city. In this publication, as in the photograph chosen by Solà-
Morales for his essay, Barbieri's urban sensitivity is at its best. His signature is a play on 
light and scale. From a technique of long exposure to night-lights to a miniature still 
photography created by shallow depths of field, Barbieri's urban depictions come very 
close to the attempts of magical realism at accentuating the marvelous in the ordinary. 
Barbieri transforms what one would see as banal or unnoticeable into a mysterious 
compelling stage of an everyday urban experience. In this sense, the model-like 
photographs seem unreal, hypothetical places of wonder, revealing aspects of the urban 
that were unseen, reaching a “new objectivity” as Enrico Ghezzi describes in Artificial 
Illuminations when referring to Barbieri’s night stills: 
“Despite the new objectivity that the night reaches in these long exposures, which X-ray 
and dry it up till it gets dissolved, the imagery convey the nocturnal fever […] Night, 
kingdom of the subjective, of the darkness to imagine or to fill, become a hallucination of 
objectivity and of precision.   From the blurred and from the ambiguous, one moves to the 
spectral and to the transparent.”427 
 Through these techniques, Barbieri's work is also aimed towards seeking new 
urban identities, “identità non ancora compiuta”428 as Aurelio Natali puts it. Barbieri's 
terrain vague is thus bound to context; hence much less hypothetical than one would 
imagine. His depictions were mainly of European urban realms, such as the terrains 
vagues of Emilia Romagna or the no man’s land between France and Belgium for the 
M.Ph.Transmanche commission in 1993429. This latter commission is also an indicator of 
the intimate network of collaborations between the different photographers mentioned 
on Solà-Morales’s list, as well as their collective unconscious interested in subjects of 
urban realm, particularly the terrain vague. M.Ph.Transmanche was a collection of 
various commissions and consequent publications called Cahiers430. These were dedicated 
                                                                                                                                                                        
426 Trans.: “ The Faces of the City ” - Natali, Aurelio. “I Volti della Città.” Bolzano - Città e Contrasti: il 
Centro e la Zona  : fotografie di Olivo Barbieri e Gabriele Basilico. Milano: Leonardo arte, 1998. 10–13. 
427 Barbieri, Olivo, and Enrico Ghezzi. Olivo Barbieri: Artificial Illuminations. Washington, DC: Smithsonian 
Institution Press, 1998. Motta Fotografia. VI-VII. 
428 Trans. “Identity not yet Accomplished/Accounted for.” 
Natali, Aurelio. “I Volti della Città.” Bolzano - Città e Contrasti: il Centro e la Zona  : fotografie di Olivo 
Barbieri e Gabriele Basilico. Milano: Leonardo arte, 1998. 10–13. 
429 Barbieri also photographed extensively the Far East, mainly China, yet this part of his photography is not 
much related to this thesis’ subject. 
430 Trans.: “Notebooks”

## S. 214

211 
 
to the photographic documentation of the artificial borders between France and Belgium 
and the communities that live there in the period of disappearing European frontiers. 
Barbieri depicted in his Cahier 12431 an area filled with terrains vagues, a particular kind of 
borderline terrain vague. Few years earlier, Davies had photographed his own 
interpretation of terrains vagues on the frontier in Cahier 2 of the same collection. The 
notion of disappearance is particularly representative of these works; it is also an ongoing 
thread in Barbieri's photography. The same element of disappearance is for instance 
visible in one of his earlier works, “Linea di Confine”432 in 1990, where he once again 
depicts marginal and transitional spaces. 
 
FIGURE 69: Barbieri, Olivo. Mantova. Italy, 1986. Photograph. 
 
FIGURE 70: Barbieri, Olivo. Artificial Illuminations. Motteggiana, Mantova Italy, 1986. 
The photograph in Solà-Morales’s essay in Quaderns is a long exposure of a terrain 
vague where human presence and activities on a terrain vague are visible through changes 
in light exposure. It exemplifies Barbieri's marginal ephemeral terrains vagues in which 
disappearance takes the foreground over any signs of presence, except for the light 
                                                                                                                                                                        
431 Barbieri, Olivo. Frontière Franco-Belge. Cahier 12. Douchy: Centre Régional de la Photographie du Nord-
Pas-de-Calais, 1993. M.Ph.Transmanche. 
432 Guerrieri, William. Via Emilia: fotografie luoghi e non luoghi 2. Rubiera (Reggio Emilia): Comune di 
Rubiera  : Linea di confine per la fotografia contemporanea, 2000.

## S. 215

212 
exposure433. In Ghezzi's words, “the appearance of the fantastic, bound not to the fullness 
of the image but rather to darkness and, even more, to the void.”434 In this same spirit of 
play on scale and light, a terrain vague is presented in a dichotic dialogue between what is 
real and what is out of this world. 
Thomas Struth – a sterile gaze on occupying 
“It is not only anonymous and generic space between buildings, but the psychological 
and subjective space between people, close and distant, which Struth's looking describes” 
- Richard Sennett, in Thomas Struth, Strangers and Friends435 
 
One more reference on Solà-Morales’s list is the photographer Thomas Struth436. 
Born in 1954, Struth was also a contemporary to the other photographers on the list and 
to Solà-Morales. German himself, the artist is still more largely recognized in German 
contexts. He lived and worked in Berlin, Düsseldorf and New York, being thus, as Solà-
Morales, a character bridging between Europe and the United States. Moreover, similar to 
Barbieri, the photographer received many awards for his work. One of which was the 
Werner Mantz Prize for Photography in the Netherlands in 1992, which could have 
triggered Solà-Morales’s awareness of the photographer's work since he was in the same 
period researching into the notion of terrain vague and photography. 
While still a student at the Düsseldorf Academy, Struth began already 
photographing deserted streets and decayed spaces. His earliest photos shown in public 
depicted garbage-strewn streets in New York. He continued to represent uncanny empty 
urban spaces that recall Anthony Vidler's definition of the architectural uncanny or 
Freud's unheimlich. Struth's photography also attempts at pinpointing the relation 
between people and their urban context. Many of his photographs are of cityscapes and 
urban environments, such as the photograph of Geneva titled “Le Lignon (horizontal) 
Genf” of 1984 figuring in the Quaderns publication of Solà-Morales’s essay. His recurring 
themes pertain to transition, identity and its abandonment, as put in evidence in the book 
                                                                                                                                                                        
433 “Adopting time itself as set” - Barbieri, Olivo, and Enrico Ghezzi. Olivo Barbieri: Artificial Illuminations. 
Washington, DC: Smithsonian Institution Press, 1998. Motta Fotografia. VI-VII. 
434 Ibid. VII-VIII. 
435 Sennett, Richard, Thomas Struth, and Art Gallery of Ontario. Thomas Struth, Strangers and Friends, 
Photographs, 1986-1992. Cambridge, Mass: MIT Press, 1994. 12. 
436 The name of the artist, although spelled right in Anyplace, was spelled wrong in the text of Quaderns, 
although the artist's photographed was rightly annotated, referring perhaps to Solà-Morales’s knowledge of 
the right spelling, unlike the other misspellings, where the line is blurred between nonchalant disinterest 
and simple unfamiliarity. Interestingly, later republications of the essay in Spanish include again the same 
misspelling, while English republications do not.

## S. 216

213 
 
Strangers and Friends437. His photographs could be often described as depicting backside 
elevations of cities, where terrain vagues are set in the foreground; these depictions seem 
to tell more about the city than front-side elevations could. A photograph of Berlin's 
terrain vague of 1992 is exemplary of this premise. It is also similar in rationale to 
Zimmermann’s photograph of Potsdamer Platz, the only illustration in the Anyplace 
edition of the "Terrain Vague": a seemingly objective representation of the city’s terrains 
vagues as a general phenomenon devoid of emotional value. In fact, between 1984 and 
1995, the photographer seems to have been at peak interest in terrains vagues. In his book 
Unconscious Places438 as well as in Strassen, many of his photographs adhere to Solà-
Morales’s description of the terrain vague: 
 “Places that are foreign to the urban system [...] appearing as its negative image as much 
in the sense of critique as in that of possible alternative.”439 
This explains the recurrent referencing of the photographer in Solà-Morales’s essays and 
archive sections related to the terrain vague notion. At first glance however, his 
photograph of a 1984 Geneva in Quaderns is arguably not the most suited among Struth's 
photographs to depict the terrain vague. The space represented is an interstitial space 
linked with a specific building rather than an indeterminate terrain out of the cities 
functioning circuits. The photograph in Quaderns is coupled with Barbieri's photograph 
appearing right underneath, also depicting a rather interstitial space of smaller scale than 
other artists’ photographs. Nonetheless, perhaps Solà-Morales’s intent through such 
examples is to portray the various scales and shapes the notion of terrain vague can 
embrace, from vast fields of derelict industrial emptiness as is the case of Plowden, to 
liminal spaces within the city, as is the case in this photograph of Thomas Struth. 
Moreover, perhaps Struth's photograph was placed at the end of the essay as a visual 
reference to Solà-Morales’s argument of the architect's role in the terrain vague. The 
author problematizes the colonizing character of the architectural practice presenting it as 
paradoxical with the terrain vague’s essence. Struth’s representation of architectural 
edifices daunting the adjacent interstitial space exposes such a discord. 
Struth's style is hence adequate. On the one hand, he exposes the visible as is, 
avoiding sentimentality. On the other hand, he portrays spaces and terrain vagues that are 
inhabited. In contrast, in Barbieri's photography, the visible is interpreted, condensed and 
manipulated towards evoking emotions and a sense of disappearance. From this 
perspective, the two artists' terrain vagues are thus quite different. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
437 Struth's photographs portray the relationships, conscious and unconscious, through which we build and 
abandon our identities in a world of transitory physical and social structures. 
438 Struth, Thomas, and Richard Sennett. Unconscious Places. München: Schirmer Mosel, 2012. 
439 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123.
