## S. 218

215 
 
Manolo Laguillo – a sagacious gaze 
Manolo Laguillo is also one of the photographers mentioned in Solà-Morales’s 
third list of photographers. He is a professor of photography closely linked to Barcelona 
and the author. In interviews conducted Solà-Morales’s contemporaries, such as Josep 
Montaner, Suzanne Strum, Ramon Paramon and Xavier Fabré, Manolo Laguillo was 
recurrently mentioned as having a prominent position in regard to the subject of terrain 
vague as well as in the evolution of Barcelona, and being more than an artist and a 
photographer within this context440. 
Laguillo photographed Barcelona's urban transformations and the creation of a 
new model of representation of Barcelona, more complex and critical, from 1978 until 
1997. The exhibition that took place in MACBA entitled Nacimiento Diagonal recollected 
these photographs with a focus on Barcelona's terrains vagues441. Many of the photographs 
are building sites, ruins, abandoned spaces and new peripheral areas, areas seldom 
represented that do not fit into the touristic image of Barcelona. Similar to Davies in his 
article in Quaderns 442 , Laguillo also photographed the Catalan peripheral areas of 
Llobregat and Muntanya, areas between the rural and the urban443. Concomitant to Solà-
Morales’s theorization of the alienated self in the city, these photographs emit signs of 
strangeness. His work was particularly important for the 1980s as it was different from the 
already mentioned typical work following the “Barcelona Model”444. He depicted suburbs, 
derelict sites and spaces far from the touristic radar. According to Laguillo, in an 
interview with the author445, throughout the thirty years in which he photographed 
Barcelona, his attention was on movement, change and mainly the element of time, 
instead of space as he had initially used to advocate. He describes with passion how he is 
interested in Solà-Morales’s terrain vague notion and how he participated with him in the 
project of the Liceu446 Opera Theatre, by taking photographs of the evolution of the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
440 Suzanne Strum for instance mentions, in an interview with the author, how the photographer is very 
important in the given context, while Josep Montaner, in an interview with the author, accentuates the 
necessity to include Laguillo as a reference in relation to the photographic standpoint on the terrain vague. - 
Interview between author and Suzanne Strum in Barcelona. 23 Dec. 2013. 
441 Laguillo, Manolo, Museo Colecciones I.C.O, and PhotoEspaña (Festival). Manolo Laguillo: Razón Y 
Ciudad. Madrid, Spain: Fundación ICO  : La Fábrica, 2013. 
442 Davies, John, Marcià Codinachs, and Enric Miralles. “A Llobregat.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 
186 - Barcelona: una Geografia Virtual (1990): 16–23. 
443 At least when the photos were taken. - Bru, Eduard, and Manolo Laguillo. “F Muntanya.” Quaderns 
d’arquitectura i urbanisme 186 - Barcelona: una Geografia Virtual (1990): 50–57. 
444 An image of Barcelona developed mainly for touristic and consuming purposes, based on landmarks and 
picturesque sights, which happened in parallel with the 1992 Olympic Games that took place in Barcelona. 
445 Interview between author and Manolo Laguillo in Barcelona. 21 Jan. 2014. 
446 The Gran Teatre del Liceu opera house was one of the main few architectural projects undertaken by Solà-
Morales. Laguillo was commissioned by Solà-Morales to photograph the various steps of the reconstruction 
after the January 1994 fire. The photographer states, when interviewed, that even in this project, which 
coincided the same year Ignasi was working on “Terrain Vague”, notions of vagueness, void, modern ruins

## S. 219

216 
project and the different construction phases. He found this documentation approach 
fascinating. His photographs, and terrains vagues, should not be viewed as static images of 
certain spaces; one should rather focus on the elements of transformations within the 
depiction, and take into consideration the represented space within its historical and 
projected timelines. 
Similar to Struth and less like Barbieri's photographs, his intention was to show 
the fabric of the city, a fabric of the living and the surrounding environment. On a larger 
scale, his work fits well in a period where the “New Topographics” movement in Spain 
was emerging, which renewed globally the interest in documentary photography, 
modernizing topographic traditions that were thought to be abandoned since the 19th 
century. This movement was also linked with the establishment of the urban landscape 
discipline; thus large format photographs became in vogue depicting transformations and 
emerging spaces in cities. Many other photographers were involved; some, such as Lewis 
Baltz, are referenced as well in the "Terrain Vague" essay. The “New Topographics” 
movement is directly linked with the evolution of Catalan photography during this period 
and with the subject of terrain vague that, in the context of Solà-Morales’s interpretation, 
partially emerged from this movement. Respectively, many of this movement’s 
photographs depict terrains vagues adherent to Solà-Morales’s definition. 
 
FIGURE 73: Laguillo, Manolo. Nacimiento de La Diagonal. Barcelona, 1979. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
(as the Liceu) were on Solà-Morales’s mind and his own. - Interview between author and Manolo Laguillo. 
21 Jan. 2014.

## S. 220

217 
 
 
FIGURE 74: Laguillo, Manolo. Berlin. 1992. Photograph. 
The 183rd edition of Quaderns in 1989 could be considered a supporting document 
to the movement's intentions and to Solà-Morales’s reflections. Laguillo in this edition 
publishes an article entitled “The City of Photography”447, while Hans Kollhoff writes 
about “Architecture versus Urban Design”448, Roger Diener discusses “On the Uncertainty 
of the Individual”, Josep Quetglas elaborates on “Clouds, Angels, Cities” alluding to the 
film by Wim Wenders Der Himmel über Berlin (Wings of Desire), Fritz Neumeyer spoke 
of “Metropolitan Architecture and Urban Identity”, and finally Eduard Bru wrote on “The 
Urban Void”. Some of the notions advocated in Solà-Morales’s essay are already present: 
photography’s toll on the urban gaze, the role of the architect in urban transformations, 
the ‘void’, the urban identity and the urban feeling of estrangement. Moreover, on a side 
note, the reference to Wim Wenders is particularly important. The filmmaker and 
playwright affected greatly the discourse on the terrain vague. He is often a reference 
when discussing derelict spaces of the 1990s. His work, as aforesaid, is engraved in the 
collective memory of the period as the main imagery of what the terrain vague could 
visually be. His 1987 film Wings of Desire for instance is almost entirely played in the 
terrains vagues of Berlin449. In an interview with the author450, Manolo Laguillo recalls a 
discussion about the film where he and his colleagues compared the first black and white 
                                                                                                                                                                        
447 The city in this article is Barcelona, of which Laguillo photographed the terrains vagues for decades. 
- Laguillo, Manolo. “The City of Photography.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 183 - City and Project 
(1989): 70–77. 
448 Kollhoff, Hans. “Architecture versus Urban Design.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 183 - City and 
Project (1989): 13–23. 
449 Wenders, Wim. Wings of Desire. 1987. Film. - It is also worth mentioning again here the artist-authordirector Peter Handke who co-wrote the film; he was himself a famous traveler and wanderer and even 
wrote poems and made films on such notions as the terrain vague and wandering such as Walk About the 
Villages : A Dramatic Poem (1996, Farrar Straus & Giroux). 
450 Interview between author and Manolo Laguillo in Barcelona. 21 Jan. 2014.

## S. 221

218 
section of the film with the colored second part. He declares a personal preference and 
strong fondness and inspiration from the first part that depicts an angel wandering in the 
terrains vagues reading the thoughts of the living dwellers in Berlin. In fact, some of 
Laguillo’s photography seems directly inspired from Wenders’s movies, especially his 
black and white photographs of Berlin and Barcelona. In these photographs, urban 
dwellings are often present in the background, a testimony of human presence. The 
terrains vagues in Laguillo’s work are never really empty, as if the thoughts and whispers 
of the nearby effective city are constantly telling stories about the past, present and 
futures of these terrains vagues. 
Third list of visual references 
In parallel with citing photographers by name, Solà-Morales continues his list 
through visual references in the Quaderns version of his essay in 1995 and in the set of 
slides he used in his various lectures on the subject. These, as much as the written 
referenced artists, reflect certain aspects of the terrain vague that Solà-Morales achieved 
at depicting through visual impact; mainly the vagrant aura inherent in the terrain vague 
and respectively in photographic representations of such spaces. 
Gilbert   Fastenaekens   –   a   vagrant   post-­‐industrial   gaze      
Gilbert Fastenaekens appears quite adequately in the foreground of the list of 
visual references. Not only is he a photographer of such spaces as terrains vagues, he is 
also a writer on similar subjects. The artist-author is considered one of the main European 
authors in photography research on urban and industrial territories. He collaborated on 
projects such as DATAR451 and Linea di confine 452 in which he worked with Olivo 
Barbieri. These allowed him to address indirectly some aspects of the terrain vague that 
Solà-Morales later clearly described and put in context. For instance, Fastenaekens 
addresses first hand the issue of “identity” in urban realm describing the term as 
reductive. According to him, by limiting urban territory to perceived visions and a limited 
set of tools to work with, “identity” turns into a challenging element to utilize for the 
benefit of undetermined spaces such as terrains vagues. 
His photograph of Lloydstraat figuring in the “Terrain Vague” essay, taken from 
Urban Landscapes, seems to represent this same reasoning. The space depicted oscillates 
                                                                                                                                                                        
451 Délégation à l'Aménagement du Territoire et à l'Action Régionale (Trans. English: “Interministerial 
Delegation of Land Planning and Regional Attractiveness”). 
452 Guerrieri, William. Via Emilia: fotografie luoghi e non luoghi 2. Rubiera (Reggio Emilia): Comune di 
Rubiera  : Linea di confine per la fotografia contemporanea, 2000.

## S. 222

219 
 
between a generic derelict space and a post-industrial space representative of Rotterdam’s 
character. The rail lines extended beyond the photograph’s limits and the repetitive 
element of the two closed doors emphasize as well the incongruity of the terrain vague. 
The obscure night vision accentuates even more this schizophrenic aloof feel of the space. 
These visual characteristics that put in question the safety, sanity and particularity of the 
terrain vague are recurrent elements in Fastenaekens’ work. His photographs are 
therefore more about an aesthetic approach to the terrain vague, or about a post-industrial 
vagrant gaze on such spaces where accent is on their nature and their identity and on how 
they fit in the urban fabric. 
Alex   Jordan   –   an   interstitial   gaze   
Barely anything is known on Alex Jordan in relation to the terrain vague topic and 
Solà-Morales. No substantial information explains how the author used his photograph as 
a visual reference within Quaderns’ publication of his essay. The artist has no evident 
relation to Solà-Morales’s other essays either. The photograph moreover does not seem to 
refer to the contextualization that the author first set with the previously mentioned 
photographers, nor does it comply effectively with his definition of the terrain vague. As 
if it landed in the essay by error, the photograph seems to only refer by contrast to the 
extent Solà-Morales’s definition can take. Representing a desolate urban street where a 
mendicant is sleeping on the sidewalk, the urban scale represented is evidently smaller 
than the urban scale depicted in the other photographs. 
 
FIGURE 75: Fastenaekens, Gilbert. Lloydstraat - Urban Landscapes. Rotterdam, 1970.

## S. 223

220 
 
FIGURE 76: Jordan, Alex. Sans Abri À Belleville. 1987. 
Moreover, the space seems to be within the city rather than on the outskirts; Solà-
Morales’s description of “the condition of these spaces as internal to the city yet at the 
same time external to its everyday utilization”453 is thus justly represented. By selecting 
this photograph to illustrate his essay, the author seems to be stating: the scale of the 
terrain vague is not always large, the interstitial and the liminal within the city is 
inherent in the definition. The contrast with the other photographs in the "Terrain 
Vague" essay reveals hence the interstitial and smaller scale the notion of terrain vague 
can encompass; somewhat similar to the discourse on the interstitial explored later on by 
Luc Lévesque454. Coincidentally, it is the only photograph that appears in the Inter455 
edition of the essay entitled “Urbanité Interstitielle”, a title chosen by Luc Lévesque who 
translated the "Terrain Vague" essay to French and reedited it towards a more interstitial 
perspective on the notion. 
Jordi   Bernadó   –   a   peripheral   liminal   gaze   
Similar to Laguillo, Jordi Bernadó was and still is a prominent figure in Catalan 
photography focused on peripheries and wastelands. He collaborated and published often 
in Quaderns, where he was commissioned for instance to photograph Valdemingómez for 
the edition on factories, or Tenerife, on the coastal fringe456. The photographer even 
depicted Barcelona’s concentric rings in Quaderns 186, the same number featuring work 
                                                                                                                                                                        
453 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
454 As will be observed in the fifth chapter. 
455 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Urbanité Interstitielle.” Trans. Luc Lévesque. Inter: art actuel 61 (1995): 27-28. 
456 Respectively: Quaderns 230 Fum de fàbrica, and Quaderns 216 Form and Place.

## S. 224

221 
 
of Davies, Basilico, Laguillo and Fastenaekens. This strong affiliation with the journal and 
Solà-Morales’s Catalan circles could be the reason for his photograph figuring as front 
cover of the “Terrain Vague” essay in Quaderns. Nonetheless, it was mainly the 
photographer’s subjects of interest that made him relevant to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague. 
On the one hand, Bernadó depicted since the 1980s wastelands of cities 
noteworthy to Solà-Morales’s discourse: Barcelona and its peripheral satellites, post-wall 
Berlin, post-industrial Detroit, and other European and American scapes. His work 
portrayed ghostly abandoned areas and buildings, empty lots in state of expectancy, or 
even large vacant territories in European or American suburbs. Similar to Laguillo and 
Solà-Morales, the work of Bernadó gives in this manner insight on the connections 
between the Catalan intellectual spheres and some European cities such as Berlin. His 
work often alludes to these links, such as in his publication entitled Paris, Texas, referring 
to Wim Wenders’s film of the same name where the background of the story and plot are 
the terrains vagues of Texas. The photographer also collaborated with Charles Waldheim, 
Georgia Daskalakis and Jason Young on the book and initiative Stalking Detroit457 whose 
authors were largely inspired by the “Terrain Vague” essay, and who were in contact with 
Solà-Morales in 2001 since he was supposed to write the introduction for the book458. 
 
FIGURE 77: Bernadó, Jordi. Detroit. USA, 1981. Photograph. 
 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
457 Daskalakis, Georgia, Charles Waldheim, and Jason Young. Stalking Detroit. Barcelona: Actar, 2001. 
458 Sadly, Solà-Morales died unexpectedly in 2001.

## S. 225

222 
 
FIGURE 78: Bernadó, Jordi. Berlin Bis. 1999. Photograph. 
 
FIGURE 79: Bernadó, Jordi. Welcome to Espaiñ. Editor Actar Barcelona, 2009.

## S. 226

223 
 
 
FIGURE 80: Bernadó, Jordi. Welcome to Espaiñ. Editor Actar Barcelona, 2009. 
On the other hand, the accent in Bernadó’s terrains vagues is on similar 
characteristics as Solà-Morales’s description. The relation between such spaces and the 
effective circuits of the city is pushed to the foreground. His photographs often depict 
sarcastic views of terrains vagues where human presence is only visible through naïve 
graffiti and ludicrous writings on surrounding urban structures, often the blank walls of 
buildings. In Bernadó’s work, the artificiality of these, often-American, very 
scenographic, urban spaces seem to overspill on the rest of the presumably functional city. 
The break between reality and simulation is moreover welded. In his Quaderns article 
“Welcome Utopia”, the ideal of the city is equally ridiculed; the terrain vague in his work 
seems hence more real than the city’s alleged productive systems constantly consumed. 
Accordingly, his photograph figuring in the “Terrain Vague” essay is particularly 
interesting. It depicts in the foreground a terrain vague limited by an illustration painted 
and collaged on a wall of a boat floating on sea waves. Perhaps the photograph is chosen 
for its caricaturist depiction of both Freud’s oceanic sentiment, and the triple etymological 
significance of “vague” advocated by Solà-Morales in his essay: “wave, vacant and vague.” 
Furthermore, the artificiality of this presumed publicity imposed on the terrain vague 
neutralizes the charge of the latter as neither negative nor positive, but rather simply 
enigmatic.

## S. 227

224 
 
FIGURE 81: Blaisdell, Gus, and Lewis Baltz. Park City. Suburb of Salk Lake City, 1980. 
Baltz   and   Blaisdell   –   a   singular   American   gaze   
Lewis Baltz and Gus Blaisdell are only referenced in one of the visuals in 
Quaderns’ edition of Solà-Morales’s essay. The photograph entitled “ Suburb of Salt 
Lake City “ from 1980 represents a desolate terrain vague on the periphery with a hilly 
landscape in the background. The photograph figures in greenish sepia monochrome, 
projecting an uncanny flair to the depiction. A text, extract from the essay in a larger type 
font, accompanies adequately the photograph: 
“The enthusiasm for these vacant spaces – expectant, imprecise, fluctuating – is, 
transposed to the urban key, the response to our strangeness before the world, before our 
city, before ourselves.”459 
Indeed, the photograph seems to be chosen precisely to portray this feeling of 
strangeness. This impression is recurrent in some of the photographers’ work mainly 
focused on the American urban landscape. For instance, Baltz’s photographs are published 
in Photography Calling!460, where Thomas Struth’s work is also present, portray similar 
eerie terrains vagues on the outskirts of 1977 Nevada. Solà-Morales probably became 
familiar with the two photographers due to his trips to the United States, as they do not 
                                                                                                                                                                        
459Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua (1996): 
34–44. 
460 Schube, Inka, and Thomas Weski, eds. Photography Calling! Göttingen: Gerhard Steidl Druckerei und 
Verlag, 2011.

## S. 228

225 
 
seem to be a familiar reference in the European spheres461. They however do not recur in 
any of the author’s publications or anywhere in the archives, alluding thus to the 
probability that the photographers only served a localized purpose; with no deeper 
interest in their work from the author’s account. 
Nonetheless, their presence in the essay’s visuals is necessary. The connection to 
the American landscape and its terrains vagues becomes clear. The notion thus takes on a 
European-American scope, with a focus on peripheral areas where landscape, urban fabric 
and ‘nature’ fuse. Furthermore, the uncanny essence of Baltz photographs put in question 
the positivity of this unequal fusion. In fact, nature seems to be at loss; the augmented 
vagueness of the depicted terrains is intrusive and invasive at heart. The landscapes in the 
background are more desolate than lively. 
 
FIGURE 82: Basilico, Gabriele. Beirut 1991. Lebanon, 1991. Photograph. 
Gabriele Basilico: An unmentioned yet implicit gaze 
One important photographer was never mentioned by Solà-Morales explicitly in 
his essay, or in the visual references of the essay: Gabriele Basilico. Nonetheless, he is 
                                                                                                                                                                        
461 Hardly any references to the two artists seem to be available in Spanish or Swiss libraries.

## S. 229

226 
considered one of the main photography references in relation to the subject of terrain 
vague462. Many of those interviewed regarding Solà-Morales’s terrain vague, such as 
Francesc Muñoz, Suzanne Strum and Jordi Oliveras, mention Basilico as key figure to look 
into when observing the period of the 1990s in relation to Barcelona’s developments and 
Solà-Morales. The author does mention him moreover in the letters463 addressed to Pietro 
Derossi regarding visual support for the essay in the Triennale di Milano. At this stage, he 
chose simply one of Basilico’s 1970 photographs from Urban Landscapes464 as potential 
visual, but the photograph was not used in any of the final published versions of the essay. 
Gabriele Basilico was largely interested in spaces on the cities' outskirts, in large 
territories and successive incursions, or “leopard spots” as Basilico describes them465. The 
photographer's approach is that of underscoring the fragmentary aspect of the urban 
fabric, looking into urban “extractions”466 taken from observing the 20th century city and 
the places bordering historical city centers; accentuating a shared sense of coherence 
without merging the various parts. According to the artist, the city is complex and one 
cannot encompass it all in one exhibit; otherwise the outcome is an “ambitious 
unmanageable mosaic of fragments.”467 To this, he prefers partial narrations. He approached 
for instance Milano in Interrupted City by depicting the larger portrait of the city through 
photographs of three separate areas representative of the entirety of the city. Having 
studied architecture, Basilico was sensitive to intents behind urban interventions. More 
than mere observations, his photographs are a visual critique of architecture and 
urbanism, 
“an opportunity to read the urban-scale interventions of this great apartment that is the 
city we live in; interventions that will remain for centuries to determine the future behavior 
of populations and contradictions of the urban environment and the surrounding area.”468 
Basilico’s architectural background is evident. It is the reason behind his strong 
relation to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague since most of his work is a photographic take on 
architectural and urban discourses. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
462 In conducted interviews, Basilico is certainly in the collective unconscious of the Spanish spheres. Perhaps 
both Laguillo and Basilico are not as heavily mentioned in the essay’s editions as the others referenced due to 
a semi-conscious attempt on behalf of Solà-Morales at keeping the intended audience of the lectures broader 
than the closer circle of Spanish and Italian contexts. 
463 Letter: Ignasi de Solà-Morales to Pietro Derossi. 3 Nov. 1995. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive. 
464 Cusveller, Sjoerd, ed. Stadslandschappen - Urban Landscapes. Rotterdam: Uitgeverij 010 Publishers, 1986. 
465 Basilico, Gabriele, and Jordi Bernadó. [Gabriele] Basilico Interrupted City: Gabriele Basilico  : La Ciudad 
Interrumpida. Barcelona: Actar, 1999. 2-4. 
466 Basilico, Gabriele. Ibid. 
467 Basilico, Gabriele. Ibid. 
468 Our trans. from Italian: “Un'occasione per leggere gli interventi a scala urbana di questo grande 
appartamento comune che è la citta in cui viviamo, interventi che rimarranno ancora per secoli a 
determinare i comportamenti futuri delle popolazioni e le contraddizioni dell'ambiente urbano e del 
territorio circostante.” - Azzolini, Carlo. “Appunti per una Lettura Urbana delle Immagini.” Bolzano - Città e 
Contrasti: il Centro e la Zona  : fotografie di Olivo Barbieri e Gabriele Basilico. Milano: Leonardo arte, 1998. 
16–21.

## S. 230

227 
 
The artist is moreover one of the most known photographers among Solà-
Morales’s references. His influence in contemporary photography and architecture is 
worldwide; partly due to his photographs of post-war and of post-industrial terrains 
vagues that capture the attention in quite a brutal yet poetic evocation469. His work with 
Boeri on “empty sites of possibility” in Cross Sections of a Country, depicting the terrains 
vagues of Italy in 1998, is representative of such evocations. Solà-Morales also probably 
personally met Basilico in one of the photographer’s many travels and works in Italy, 
Berlin, Madrid, or most probably when he worked on Barcelona and Bilbao, or even 
when he was photographing the industrial and urban landscapes of Milano for his book 
Milano, Ritratti di Fabbriche470 in 1983 which got him one of his first large scaled acclaim. 
Basilico’s link to those on Solà-Morales’s second list is also strong. Aside from 
participating in the previously mentioned Urban Landscapes with Linders, Davies, 
Fastenaekens and Barbieri, Basilico collaborated as well with the latter on Bolzano - Città 
e Contrasti: il Centro e la Zona 471 photographing the Italian city of Bolzano at the turn of 
the millennium. Evidently, attention was on urban spaces, on the terrains vagues of a 
Bolzano in perpetual transformation. 
Moreover, Basilico was chosen to participate in Urban Landscapes due to his 
photographs within the initiative of DATAR472. In fact, between 1984 and 1985, the 
photographer participated in this initiative, supported by the French government. It was 
one of the largest photography missions of the 20th century, which included 36 
photographers commissioned to photograph different regions of France between 1984 and 
1989, these regions being mainly landscapes of transformation, hence the prominent 
recurrences of terrains vagues depictions; as Suzanne Strum describes, 
“The DATAR mission’s point of departure was the fact that most people mentally 
conjured the “landscape” as that of their grandparent’s youth or a picturesque postcard cliché. 
Instead the DATAR photographers recorded the shock of an artificialized territory, nature 
yielding to power lines, technological artifacts and infrastructures across the land.”473 
                                                                                                                                                                        
469 The photographs of post-war destroyed Beirut and of Milano Garibaldi are a good example. - 
In appreciation of similar photographs, the artist won the “Prix Mois de la Photographie” in Paris for “Porti 
di Mare” in 1990; and in 1994, same year as Solà-Morales’s first lecture on the terrain vague, a retrospective 
of his work was staged in Fondazione Galleria Gottardo in Lugano under the title “L’Esperienza dei Luoghi 
1978-1993”. 
470 Basilico, Gabriele, Marco Romano, and Carlo Bertelli. Milano: Ritratti Di Fabbriche. Milano: Federico 
Motta Editore, 2009. 
471 Basilico, Gabriele, Olivo Barbieri, and Aurelio Natali. Bolzano - Città e Contrasti: il Centro e la Zona  : 
fotografie di Olivo Barbieri e Gabriele Basilico. Milano: Leonardo arte, 1998. 
472 Délégation à l'Aménagement du Territoire et à l'Action Régionale (Trans. English: “Interministerial 
Delegation of Land Planning and Regional Attractiveness”). 
473 Strum, Suzanne. “Pencil of Nature/Mirror of Culture: Photography in the Topographic Tradition.” SCE 
Journal - Scapes 5 (2006): 22–26.

## S. 231

228 
Thus, similar to M.Ph.Transmanche and other collaborations, DATAR also 
promoted new urban imagery focused on spaces such as terrains vagues. It contributed as 
well in the “shift of gaze” promoted by Solà-Morales from the picturesque to the 
indeterminate. 
DATAR in its turn inspired other top projects and planning. Coming out of the 
“New Topographics” movement that had inspired in this period many other European 
Public Commissions, it was one of the initiatives that positively affected the development 
of urban notions, such as the terrain vague, within the realm of photography. In so doing, 
this movement had a major role on the evolution of urban perceptions in photography, 
and consequently on the depictions of the terrain vague, spilling finally into the new 
millennium from urban perception to urban intervention. Quoting DATAR: 
“(the) task is to throw light on the organization and shapes of national territory at the 
end of what has been a period of profound change, to give individual expression to the first 
signs on the horizon of future shifts in modes of production, urbanization, information, and, 
lastly, to highlight the components of our national landscape that are dying, 
disappearing…”474 
Accent was on the disappearing and transforming layers of the urban fabric. Thus, 
in mid-1980s, the public role of photography was accentuated by such initiatives. On 
another note, the projects turned increasingly political in Europe, which was reflected 
finally in movements such as Landscape Urbanism with James Corner and Jordi Bernadó 
with whom notions of urban aesthetics and politically loaded intentions and ideas such as 
terrain vague are still an ongoing relevant matter of discussion. 
 
FIGURE 83: Basilico, Gabriele. Place Des Martyres. Beirut - Domus 862, 1991. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
474 Mission photographique de la DATAR (France). Paysages: Photographies: Travaux En Cours 1984/1985. 
Paris: Hazan, 1985. 32.

## S. 232

229 
 
 
FIGURE 84: Basilico, Gabriele. Quartiere Isola. Milano, 1978. Photograph. 
Basilico can be considered one of the most interesting artists who collaborated in 
DATAR, an opinion also upheld by Suzanne Strum475. Basilico’s work is nonetheless 
perhaps as poetic as polemic. The terrain vague as expression of absence, both in a 
provocative and an aesthetic sense, seems to be a running theme in Basilico’s 
photography. Similar to Plowden, the accent is set on loss. In 1991 for instance, the 
essence of a post-war Beirut is captured by the photographer as part of collaboration 
between Basilico, Robert Frank and Fouad El Khoury476. A lifeless city is represented, only 
hunted by the shadow of its past glory. A chaotic nonsensical emptiness invades every 
frame. A particular relation to time is presented. Similar to Solà-Morales’s description of 
terrain vague as having the “past dominant over present and future”477, Basilico himself 
states: 
“Time-wise, here is the site of maximum urban ‘coexistence’: awaiting transformation 
for more than twenty years, here lie, side by side, those vast, disused areas of the ‘city of 
factories’ […] the new legions of buildings clustering in ordered groups along the redefined 
borders, and other installations occupying the access roads of the city.”478 
                                                                                                                                                                        
475 Strum, Suzanne. “Pencil of Nature/Mirror of Culture: Photography in the Topographic Tradition.” SCE 
Journal - Scapes 5 (2006): 22–26. 
476 Depardon, Raymond, Gabriele Basilico, and Robert Frank. Beyrouth Centre Ville. First edition. Beirut: 
Éditions du Cyprès, 1992. 
477 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
478 Basilico, Gabriele, and Jordi Bernadó. [Gabriele] Basilico Interrupted City: Gabriele Basilico  : La Ciudad 
Interrumpida. Barcelona: Actar, 1999. 2-4.

## S. 233

230 
Basilico further continues: 
“Here you have the great metropolis, living its last metamorphosis before the arrival of 
the third millennium”479 
This complexity in the urban time perception is coupled in Basilico’s photography 
with a spatial complexity from which the terrain vague is finally produced. Basilico thus 
argues in Interrupted City that marginal spaces are the most complex and cosmopolitan 
territories: 
“where cities break up along their own boundaries and where their ‘scarred’ fabrics, 
abandoned for years to creeping lethargy, grow new and unforeseen layers of skin.”480 
This description comes very close to Solà-Morales’s understanding of terrain vague: 
 “in this absence of limit, this almost oceanic sentiment, to use Freud’s expression, is 
precisely the message which contains the expectations of mobility, vagrant roving, free time, 
liberty”481; 
which, in turn, explains why the artist’s name keeps emerging when describing Solà-
Morales’s terrain vague: both raise awareness to the promises that lay expectantly in the 
terrain vague. 
 
FIGURE 85: Basilico, Gabriele. Quartiere Isola. Milano, 1978. Photograph. 
 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
479 Ibid. 
480 Ibid. 
481 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123.

## S. 234

231 
 
Recapitulating: an "ambitious unmanageable mosaic of fragments" 
This chapter’s list of references and photographers linked to the terrain vague 
notion is evidently not exhaustive. It is mainly an elaboration on the references directly 
linked to the text. Important silent voices that are recurrent, either in the conducted 
interviews or in others of Solà-Morales’s essays related to the notion of terrain vague, are 
also presented. 
Moreover, many photographers and art events mentioned are based in a European 
or American context: "The Americans", "Family of Man"482; many of the references to 
thoughts and concepts are also of Western origins: Deleuze, Freud, Kristeva or Choay. 
The only case study in the essay also refers to the developments of a European city, 
Berlin. The focus on Europe and the United States seems to position the experience of the 
'new Metropolis' and the new photographer's gaze within this developed western world. 
By the same token, the etymological referencing of the notion revolves around French, 
English and German roots. The coined discourse is thus situated within mostly western 
urban context that attempts to be validated on a larger context for any given metropolis. 
This enumeration of both theoretical and visual references helps moreover 
understand how Solà-Morales establishes in his essay the terrain vague notion beyond a 
mere terminology. First, he sets a theoretical grounding within different disciplines 
through the works of different theorists. Second, the three staged photographic references 
form a context within which the terrain vague notion is embedded: a prehistory to the 
notion, a political and historical grounding, and a mostly Western context; second, a 
psychological and urban grounding; and third, a starting point in time and space for the 
terrain vague notion, and a diversity of imagery affiliated to the notion. These different 
sensibilities tackling a common phenomenon in the urban fabric were thus joined by 
Solà-Morales towards the creation of a discourse and of an architectural and urban tool. 
Solà-Morales thus theorized what was embedded till then in observation. 
Accordingly, on a first level, the initial section of the "Terrain Vague" essay483 
situates the sociopolitical context from which the terrain vague notion develops. The 
accompanying photographs are equally representative of this setting: a fragmented view 
on a post-war urban fabric in which the human is fragile in form of the high-rise 
metropolis. A German-American tension is the underlying political agenda; France also 
comes into play. Moreover, from a pool of mainly German artists, Man Ray emerges as an 
                                                                                                                                                                        
482 Day, Jonathan. Robert Frank’s The Americans: The Art of Documentary Photography. Bristol: Intellect 
Books, 2011. 
483 Until “…a way of explaining the experience of the big city” - Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” 
Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 
118–123.

## S. 235

232 
avant-garde exception that predicts the shift of gaze towards the terrain vague. Each of 
the other artists mentioned provide a nuance on this setting, be it Podsadecki’s 
fragmented photomontages or Man Ray’s futuristic vision. It is important to note that 
Solà-Morales did not attempt to embed the notion within a Spanish or Catalan prehistory, 
but rather adopted for a dialectic between Europe and the United States to situate the 
terrain vague. The notion in turn becomes charged with a cross-continental discourse on 
public space, its colonization and the human position in the metropolis. Such a first 
setting frames the aspect of the terrain vague as belonging to a need to resist outer forces 
on the urban, a position already evoked in the previous chapter. 
Second, the “Terrain Vague” essay’s middle section484 portrays a transitory phase in 
which shifts in gaze are the focal interest. From the fearful postwar fascination with highrise and density, this phase in photography history accentuates the lack of consideration 
of the humane level in the city. Be it Bresson’s photographs or Faurer’s uncanny portraits, 
alienation, estrangement, as well as humanist utopias and homogenizing hopeful 
scenarios, are the circulating concepts in the various exhibitions that established an even 
stronger position to photography within the arts. These feelings were already evoked in 
the first chapter485 and are equally conjured by the theoretical references in the essay. 
They reflect a new form of comprehending the urban landscape with a focus on the 
terrain vague; an approach crucial for tackling contemporary urban concerns. 
Third, the photographic gaze on terrain vague is directly tackled after the second 
section of the essay, and is recurrent throughout the rest of the essay. The photographs 
and photographers mentioned represent various forms486 that this gaze can assume from 
Linders’ attention to time and change, to Barbieri’s traces of human presence, and to 
Bernadó’s photographs on the liminal. These terrain vague forms also provide the limits of 
the notion’s definition. Through these photographs, the characteristics derived by Solà-
Morales from etymology, sociology and psychology are demonstrated through powerful 
visual forms. 
Finally, these visual references refer to the similar, previously evoked, ideas of 
resistance, vagrancy, and blurriness. These photographs and photomontages show as well 
how the terrain vague notion falls between negative and positive connotations and how 
the notion of potential, whether aesthetic or action-oriented, is the most important 
characteristic of terrains vagues. These mentions therefore hint also at the subtle 
manifesto agenda of Solà-Morales and they create a powerful and vibrant array that feeds 
and incites the collective urban imagination beyond the "Terrain Vague" essay. Moreover, 
                                                                                                                                                                        
484 Until “…yet another gaze on the big cities” – Ibid. 
485 As one of the main branches of the terminologies and preceding contexts surrounding the terrain vague. 
486 As we will discuss in the fifth chapter, these forms are linked with the ways the essay’s influence and its 
resulting interventions took.

## S. 236

233 
 
the different depictions prove how versatile the interpretations of the terrain vague can 
be, and respectively how relevant the essay is to a late 20th century urban context that will 
be discussed in the following chapter.

## S. 237

234 
 
 
 
CHAPTER 4 – Construction, Presentation and 
Diffusion: Diverse Contexts behind Solà-Morales’s 
"Terrain Vague"

## S. 239

236 
Timeline of Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague” essay 
 
FIGURE 86: Muntadas, Antoni. Entre / In Between. Exhibition at the Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina 
Sofía, 2011. The show exhibits the artist’s work under thematic boxes; one of which, “Public territories”, 
includes a good section on the terrain vague. 
 
Framing   the   “Terrain   Vague”   within   a   continuum   of   essays   and   contexts   
The timeline of Solà-Morales’s essay, including its initial formulation and its first 
presentations and publications, hints at its success and effective diffusion. One can argue a 
correlation between this history and the success of the notion itself. In order to 
understand the assumptions and derivatives leading to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague, one 
must first understand the phase in Solà-Morales’s theoretical production when he wrote 
the essay. Both author and context lead to a better grasp of the importance of the “Terrain 
Vague” essay as a theoretical interdisciplinary construct that sets forth a new set of 
relations between the urban fabric and the architect’s role. Moreover, empathizing the 
origins and contiguous contexts of the essay ultimately leads to a better positioning of the 
terrain vague notion within the larger context of architectural discourse. The hypothesis 
is that the relevance of the given notion comes from it being a product of an author’s life 
work and the synthesis of an era’s collective inkling. 
When analyzing Solà-Morales’s work, the “Terrain Vague” essay seems to belong 
to a continuum of essays written by the theoretician for the Anyone conferences where 
one feeds into another. It also belongs to a larger set of essays the author wrote 
throughout the late 1980s and the 1990s. His theories become wholesome when looking 
at all these texts as one. This period, as previously mentioned, could be considered the

## S. 240

237 
 
second phase of the author’s intellectual endeavor, in which the “Terrain Vague” essay 
constitutes a culminating point. In fact, since the late 1980s, Solà-Morales’s theoretical 
work seemed to witness a shift from a relatively historical architectural focus to an 
emphasis on more contemporary urban concerns, the terrain vague being one of them. 
This shift was allegedly triggered by the author’s many travels during these years, and his 
theories evidently developed along his journey. As an exemplary case of this process, Solà-
Morales’s notion of the terrain vague moved from an educational tool at Princeton 
University, to an artistic experiment at the Triennale di Milano, to a theoretical manifesto 
at the CCA in Montreal, to a foundation pillar at the UIA Congress in Barcelona, until it 
finally exploded into a wide-reaching acknowledged notion in the field of architecture 
and urbanism. This timeline is evidently not detached from its various respective contexts 
that influenced and were influenced by this “travelling concept”487. 
In this section, the period in which the “Terrain Vague” essay was first written 
and presented is put under scrutiny. On the one hand, the aim is to situate the essay in its 
most direct context and to create a chronology of the first presentations, exhibitions and 
publications of the “Terrain Vague”, creating thus a timeline as reference for future 
mentioning. On the other hand, the context and events surrounding the author would be 
put in parallel to this timeline, drawing possible impetuses leading to the terrain vague 
notion’s development. 
First   essay’s   audiences   
As an intrinsic part of the given study, the influence and legacy of the “Terrain 
Vague” essay is analyzed closely in the fifth chapter. First, however, we will look at the 
initial direct receptions of the essay within the period of 1992 to 1996 and the original 
contexts in the four locations where the “Terrain Vague” essay was first conceived, 
written, published and presented: Barcelona, Princeton, Milano and Montreal. Various 
architects, students and intellectuals were present at the events; they were inspired by 
Solà-Morales’s essay and presentations. Some adopted the notion in their own work, 
experimenting with its potential and its subordinate themes. Some even pushed the 
notion in later stages to its inevitable development from Solà-Morales’s coining to its 
contemporary connotations of interstitiality, temporality and redefined vagueness. The 
first receptions of the essay predict already some of the notion’s transformations. They 
show how, since its inception, Solà-Morales’s terrain vague had influential standing 
within the theoretical precepts of the mid-1990s. They also project how the notion was 
                                                                                                                                                                        
487 Idea adopted from Mieke Bal’s theory of travelling concepts and their affiliations with cultural and 
interdisciplinary constructs. - Bal, Mieke. Travelling Concepts in the Humanities: A Rough Guide. Toronto: 
University of Toronto Press, 2002.

## S. 241

238 
connected to an interdisciplinary positioning between the arts, architecture and 
urbanism. 
It is perhaps worth restating here that in the mid-1990s Solà-Morales was at the 
peak of his career, a prominent figure both on local and international scales. His work was 
strongly affected by his various involvements with universities in the United States, 
including mainly Princeton, Harvard and Columbia, and his collaborations with 
influential American figures in the field, such as Peter Eisenman and Anthony Vidler. 
The result was hence a mild interest in deconstructivist ideas, enthused by Jacques 
Derrida and Peter Eisenman, a keener interest in Anthony Vidler's work such as “the 
Architectural Uncanny”, a growing interest in hermeneutic thought, and finally an 
interest in contemporary French and German philosophy with an architectural bearing, 
such as Gilles Deleuze and Walter Benjamin. 
All these stimuli are reflected in the terrain vague, and in its reception and then 
influence on the contemporary scene. First, Solà-Morales’s effect in Spain was mainly 
visible as a projection of interdisciplinary international links between Spain and the 
United States and then the world, due to overlapping common dilemmas pertaining to the 
metropolis, be it within Spanish cities, American cities or on an international scale. 
Second, the audience to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague at Princeton was inspired by the 
notion's description of a contemporary urban condition and the potential in this notion to 
expand beyond its definition. Third, the audience at the Triennale di Milano seemed more 
in tune with the notion's link to hermeneutics and to Solà-Morales’s ability of bridging 
philosophy, art and architecture into a comprehensive understanding of the urban fabric. 
Fourth, the CCA audience was particularly interested in Solà-Morales’s link to the 
American schools of thought and to his ability to coin a term in tune with the 
contemporary urban condition from an interdisciplinary point of view, coupled with a 
particular interest in Mies van der Rohe's presence as case study within the terrain vague. 
1990-1993: Experimenting and generating a notion 
The first phase in Solà-Morales’s terrain vague timeline is between 1990 and 1993 
when the author generated the essay through his involvements within a Spanish Catalan 
context and within an American context revolving mainly around Princeton University. 
At the beginning of the 1990s, Solà-Morales was still interested in a historicized view on 
architecture, faithful to his academic training and his position as art and architecture 
historian, writing articles such as “The discovery of the Walls of Barcelona” or “Matters of 
Style”. He was nonetheless moving at fast pace towards more contemporary issues, 
analyzing current architects and theoretical issues of the present-day cities. Some of the

## S. 242

239 
 
most prominent theoretical influences on him also started to become evident in his 
published work. He wrote for instance “The capital cities of Walter Benjamin” and “A 
Benjaminian Approach to the Architectural Project”, in which he expresses reverence 
towards Benjamin’s fragmentary depictions of the urban experience. He wrote as well “A 
Thousand Platforms” in which he expresses equal regard to Deleuze and Guattari’s 
rhizomatic accounts. Mies van der Rohe was as well a recurrent reference and subject of 
many of his essays during this period and he was directly involved in the reconstruction 
of the Mies van der Rohe Pavilion in Barcelona and was collaborating with the Mies van 
der Rohe Foundation on different levels. It is thus not a happenstance that all these 
references are encompassed in the terrain vague, be it directly as is the case of van der 
Rohe and Deleuze, or indirectly as in Benjamin’s case. 
Within the context of Barcelona 
Solà-Morales’s first context was evidently Barcelona; it is evident that his 
theoretical work was first motivated by the city’s developments and by his involvements 
in it. Aside from the Catalan conferences in which he was engaged in this period, the 
philosopher was also actively implicated as a professor in the city, his main location. He 
taught at the Escuela Tecnica Superior de Arquitectura (ETSAB) at the Universitat 
Politècnica de Catalunya (UPC) and was involved in many committees. He also 
collaborated and published in some of the city’s most prominent architecture magazines, 
notably in Quaderns. All these activities allowed the professor to be quite influential and 
in turn influenced by the Catalan scene488, despite his many travels and external stimuli489. 
One can find correlations between his writing and subjects of interest and the 
transformations and events occurring in Spain and in the Catalan capital, such as the pre- 
and post-Olympic games transformations. 
Out of many cities in which he worked or contributed, Barcelona is perhaps the 
most notable as it is the birthplace of his terrain vague theories and implementations. 
Within this oriented biography of the notion, one should also shed some light on the 
developments of the Catalan capital: a post-Franco era of major urban developments that 
had various repercussions on those of European cities since the 1990s, to the extent that 
French architect David Mangin could speak of Barcelona as a "Mecca for urban 
practices."490 Accordingly, the essay's notions seem to be intimately connected to the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
488 His influence ranged also beyond Catalonia in Spain, where he was even member of the Reial Patronat del 
Museu Nacional Centre d’Art Reina Sofia de Madrid, one of the most significant museums in Spain. 
489 Unlike what some have debated, when interviewed, the Catalan influence on Solà-Morales’s work seems 
evident and we will try to elucidate some aspects of it in this section, mainly in relation to the terrain vague 
notion. 
490 Mangin, David. “Une Mecque des Pratiques Urbaines.” L’Architecture d’Aujourd’hui Oct. 1992: 30.

## S. 243

240 
transformations occurring between the late 19th century and the 1990s on the metropolis 
and on relative fields, such as the art sphere and the urban realm. 
In this respect, the urban transformations of Barcelona showed a high diversity of 
left-over and interstitial spaces during this period, exceeding other cities due to a 
conscious awareness of its urban stakeholders towards an interstitial thinking following 
the evolution of theories and architectural works witnessed since the urban innovations 
of Cerdà. Paul Morand even describes the city as the "homeland of melodious rhythm […] 
not of disjointed sounds and discordant tumult, it is the realm of that often hidden style that 
commands the mass of rough cyclopean constructions and the infinite smallness of mosaics."491 
The city has had a steady theoretical and architectural framework since the 1990s 
that followed a line of thought oriented towards public space. Its urban evolution had 
hinted to this theoretical revolution since the mid-19th century. From Cerdà, the 
‘founder of Urbanism’, who wrote and worked on Barcelona, and his 1859 Eixample plan 
of the extension of Barcelona, passing by the works of the politician and functionary 
Arturo Soria y Mata in late 19th century, to the Via Diagonal projects, the more recent 
project of Herzog and de Meuron, the Diagonal Mar, and the Olympic games projects of 
1992, the theories of Ignasi de Solà-Morales are inherently part of this urban path. Key 
references in this analysis of Barcelona are thus the rise of urbanism with Ildefons Cerdà, 
the fall of Urbanism with the postmodernist urban wave, and the more contemporary 
gentrification movements that followed this wave. 
 
FIGURE 87: Savoia, Stephen. Olympic Games. Associated Press. Barcelona, 1992. Photograph. - Urban 
transformations for the promotion of the city: China’s Fu Mingxia competed in the women’s 10-meter Platform 
Diving, with the city of Barcelona always present in the background. 
 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
491 Morand, Paul. Méditerranée: Mer des Surprises. Paris: Maison Mame, 1938. 26.

## S. 244

241 
 
 
FIGURE 88: Olympic Games. Barcelona, 1992. Brochures, Maps and Pamphlets for the games and the city. 
Firstly, the Cerdà period in Barcelona’s development is one of the historical 
moments in which the importance of voids in the city is accentuated and where an urban 
plan’s strength is in its shared interstitial spaces rather than the built form, thus a possible 
antecedent to the terrain vague discourse492. Solà-Morales had often written about the 
city’s transformations. Being a historian, philosopher and a theoretician, the professor had 
spent his earlier years focused, among other subjects, on the urban and architectural 
history of Catalonia. He reanalyzed the Cerdà Plan; the latter being probably a latent 
inspiration to the terrain vague notion. 
Secondly, during Solà-Morales’s period, Barcelona beheld the creation of the 
Olympic village for the 1992 Summer Olympics as well as the controversial Diagonal Mar 
urban project in 1997, which polarized and gentrified the city and added another 
dimension to the discussion on vacant lands. According to Jordi Oliveras, in an interview 
with the author, Barcelona witnessed in the 1980s a “kind of urban revolution converting all of 
the public space such as interstitial spaces.”493 He argues that this ‘revolution’ was accelerated 
later with the works towards hosting the Olympic games. Thus, as Jean-Louis Cohen 
summarizes in his Learning from Barcelona in 1998: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
492 Exhibitions and conferences from this period also portrayed this shift and perhaps influenced Solà-
Morales since he was in 1993 member of the “Comissió del Patrimoni Cultural de Catalunya, Generalitat de 
Catalunya”. In the same spirit, Solà-Morales was also member of the Patrimonat Fondacio Mies and on the 
Jury for the Mies van der Rohe Competition. 
493 Interview between author and Oliveras Jordi in Barcelona. 18 Dec. 2013.

## S. 245

242 
“From Cerdà's Eixample to Léon Jaussely's plans and the Macià plan, Barcelona is a 
leading place in the formulation of modern theories of town planning, including pioneering 
projects.”494 
What also speaks for Barcelona is its central location: belonging to the 
Mediterranean and still part of Europe, the city is situated in the zone where discussion 
on terrain vague takes on a local dimension: European cities having a pattern of old 
nucleus vs. emerging spaces vs. spaces on the edge. 
Thirdly, after 1992, a post-Olympics Barcelona was witnessing a thrive in 
architectural and urban development, both physically and theoretically, followed by a 
stasis. The transitions witnessed by the city had an impact on the local architectural and 
urban discourses. During this phase, Solà-Morales was particularly interested in Barcelona 
pre- and post-Olympics, writing articles such as “Barcellona alla Fine del 1992”, 
“Barcelona después del 92” and “Dieci Anni Interventi Urbani a Barcellona”. Attention 
was gradually shifted away from the city that seemed over-designed and ‘complete’ to the 
peripheral zones and the becoming city, mainly the Llobregat, Badalona, but also the Sant 
Andreu495 and Sant Marti areas. With an overbuilt and overdesigned city, interest in 
recycle, reuse and the redevelopment of peripheries became the alternative. Agendax 21, 
in 1993, is one such initiative that intended to sustain and provide support for smaller 
peripheral cities; and Manuel Gausa created as well the workshop “Urbanism 
Contemporani” in 1993. The terrain vague notion falls within this wave. These initiatives 
could have been in Solà-Morales’s receptive background when he wrote: 
“…casual unfolding of a particular proposal which is superimposed on the already 
existing; repeated void on the void of the city; silent artificial landscape touching the 
historical time of the city yet neither cancelling it nor imitating it.”496 
Accordingly, notions in Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay are projected onto 
the future as the author presents solutions for future implementation on the terrain 
vague's and the city's transformations. 
These different instances in the city’s urban history share a common interest in 
voids, wastelands and the intimate interaction between the urban and architectural scales 
of the city as a utopic remedy for a healthy metropolis. Barcelona has thus undergone 
physical urban changes as well as transformations of theoretical caliber, which helps 
illustrate both folds of our paradigm: perception and intervention. The juxtaposition of 
different urban layers as well as the accumulation of theoretical urban data related to the 
subject of the terrain vague transformed Barcelona into a fertile case to dwell into. It is 
                                                                                                                                                                        
494 Cohen, Jean-Louis. “Learning from Barcelona  ; Vingt Ans de Projets Urbains et Leur Réception.” Ciutat 
Real, Ciutat Ideal: Significat I Funció a L’espai Urbà Modern. (Urbanitats 7). Barcelona: Centre de Cultura 
Contemporània, 1998. 
495 Even a new university section of ETSA opened in one the developing area: in the Vallès. 
496 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua (1996): 
34–44.

## S. 246

243 
 
hence not surprising that a theoretician such as Solà-Morales emerged from Barcelona 
since the history of the city is in itself inviting to think of such notions as the terrain 
vague. 
It was also during the same period of early 1990s when the author initiated his 
work on the Gran Teatre del Liceu in Barcelona, a project that consumed much of his 
time and energy497 for almost a decade. The Liceu had burned on the 31st of January 1994 
and Solà-Morales was the commissioned architect for the reconstruction498. The burning 
of the Liceu had caused a new terrain vague in the city. In this line of thought, it is no 
surprise that Solà-Morales valued the terrain vague ephemeral phase of the Liceu’s 
reconstruction and sought to capture it through photography, choosing just the right 
Catalan photographer to do so, Manolo Laguillo, a photographer equally interested in the 
urban qualities of the city and its terrains vagues. The terrain vague was here considered 
as a segment of a continuum within a temporal representation of the city, rather than an 
actual physical space. 
 
FIGURE 89: Projecto de Reconstrucción Y Ampliación Del Gran Teatro Del Liceu. Revista Opera Actual n.16, 
Barcelona, 1995. Article. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
497 A letter from Solà-Morales to Seyda Arguner describes how he was consumed by the Liceu reconstruction 
project, yet still wanted to continue his commitment with the Metropolis Master. – In: Correspondence 
1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 1994. 
498 The Liceu reopened in 1999; and in 2000 it won the Cultural Heritage National Prize handed by the 
Catalan Government (Generalitat).

## S. 247

244 
When asked about the terrain vague notion, Eduard Bru for instance, an architect, 
professor, colleague and friend of Solà-Morales, conveys an anecdotal account of an 
incident499; a lunch he had with Solà-Morales in 1994. They had met unexpectedly on the 
Rambla where Solà-Morales was working on the Liceu opera house, which had burned 
down the same year. While the architects were discussing the new matters regarding 
Barcelona’s architectural and urban scene, Bru spoke of “terrains vagues”, much to Solà-
Morales’s surprise since the essay then was written but not yet presented. His instant 
reaction was to ask where Bru had heard the term from, to which the latter replied that 
“it just seems to be the correct term to use” for what he intended to denote. More 
interesting than the content of this anecdote is its timing. It portrays the “Terrain Vague” 
essay’s nature as a synthesist of preexisting ideas of the time; the notion and its discourse 
emerged from and were linked to a specific period and to Barcelona’s architectural 
discourses and transformations to which Solà-Morales was an active witness. Barcelona is 
here the first context from which Solà-Morales’s terrain vague notion emerged, a city 
interested in its own terrains vagues that helped its development beyond a notion. 
Context   of   the   Metropolis   Master   program   
In 1993, when not visiting Princeton University, Solà-Morales was teaching at 
ETSAB. But between 1992 and 1993, the professor decided to initiate a Master program 
entitled “Metropolis” partially detached from the university. The program was based at 
the Centre de Cultura Contemporània de Barcelona (CCCB)500. It became a locus to test 
and experiment with new ideas and notions, one of which was the terrain vague. It was 
focused on connections between architecture, urbanism, theory and the arts, with an 
emphasis on the metropolis of the present and of the future from a contemporary 
standpoint. Solà-Morales used both the more established ETSAB and the Metropolis 
program to test strategies on the city and deduce new perspectives. Accordingly, the first 
curriculum of 1993-1994 included eight lessons: Metropolis, Flows/Fluxus, Containers, 
Scopes, Exchangers, Megastructures, Monuments and, evidently, Terrain Vague501. The 
initial program found in Solà-Morales’s archives includes an explanation section for every 
lesson except “Metropolis” and “Terrain Vague”. Perhaps this section was not finalized yet 
in 1993 since the terrain vague was indeed a work in progress for Solà-Morales 
throughout the early 1990s and even afterwards. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
499 Interview between author and Eduard Bru in Barcelona. 24 Dec. 2013. 
500 The program, still running, is intended as a collaboration between the IDEC/Universitat Pompeu Fabra 
and the CCCB. Solà-Morales was also a member of the scientific committee at the CCCB for the exhibition El 
Noucentisme. Un Projecte de Modernitat in 1994. 
501 Trans. from Spanish: “Metrópolis, Flujos, Contenedores, Visores, Intercambiadores, Megaestructuras, 
Monumentos, "Terrain Vague".” – In: Metropolis Master. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 25.

## S. 248

245 
 
The Metropolis Master moreover was, and still is, as international and 
interdisciplinary as its professors and students, experts from various backgrounds tackling 
the field of architecture from unexpected directions. The original founding academic 
committee also shows academics related to Princeton and the American circles: Georges 
Teyssot, Carl Schorske and Anthony Vidler among others. As a consequence, outcomes of 
the Metropolis program were research theses as versatile as the students, which were a 
pool of inspiration for Solà-Morales. According to Jordi Oliveras502, colleague of Solà-
Morales at the UPC, the professor shifted his attention during this period from reading 
historical references to listening to fresh ideas from the youth, hence his interest in the 
Metropolis program. His attention was since then set on the future of contemporary cities, 
rather than their history to which he had dedicated most of his earlier work; an approach 
evident in the “Terrain Vague” essay in which history is mainly in the service of the 
present and future. 
 
FIGURE 90: Metropolis Master Program. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box Correspondence, 1994. 
Document. 
First reactions within the Metropolis Master Program 
After 1994, and still ongoing, the terrain vague became a recurrent notion within 
the Metropolis program. It was and still is mentioned in lecture series and experimented 
with by both professors and students. Some students directly adopted the notion as their 
own, making it the core subject of their theses. Many of the concepts advocated by Solà-
Morales in his “Terrain Vague” were reflected in the theses from the mid-1990s to the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
502 Interview between author and Oliveras Jordi in Barcelona. 18 Dec. 2013.

## S. 249

246 
2000s: such as Jorge Salinas’s “The construction of the identity in the Metropolis: 
Dispersion and fragmentation”503 (1996), José Cabrujas’s “Atopía, Otros Lugares”504 (1999) 
and Florian Hoyndorf’s “Invading the Dead Zone” (2001). Others experimented with 
notions within the essay, such as Sanjeev Joshi’s “Urban Montage” (1996), Dany Izbitzki’s 
“Traces” (2000), Jennifer Shepard’s “The Clandestine in the City” (2000) and Eduardo 
Chirinos’s “Cities in Ruins - The Uses of the Past in the Contemporary City” (2003). In all 
these theses, the notion of terrain vague is persistently quoted. Some of these students 
became themselves teachers, reenacting Solà-Morales’s teachings and re-evoking the 
terrain vague as one of the celebrated notions; Solà-Morales’s influence is still ongoing. 
In some theses, attempts at developing the notion beyond Solà-Morales’s ideas 
were conducted. For instance, the urban theoretician Francesc Muñoz Ramirez wrote his 
thesis on “Barcelona, Mil Territories en Deconstrucció” (1994), linking the notion directly 
with Barcelona and with deconstructivist ideas505. The notion evolved moreover to smaller 
more ephemeral scales, first within Metropolis but also in the world: Heike Elser’s thesis 
was on “Transitions” (1996), Mónika Sanchez Rosado’s thesis was on “Ephemeral Cities” 
(1999), María Bertuzzi’s on “Intermediate Landscapes” (2000), Diego Bari’s was on 
“Interstices” (2001). All these share a common interest in the non-city, the spaces that fall 
out of the productive system and more importantly looking in depth into what Solà-
Morales had intended by “continuity” as the solution for intervening on such spaces. The 
transformations of the terrain vague to more interstitial definitions seem to follow such 
intent for active intervention, rather than passive observation of the terrain vagues of the 
metropolis. Future developments on the architectural practice level within the beginning 
of the new millennium portray this change in scale. 
Moreover, even at the ETSAB, Solà-Morales engaged in classes where he spoke of 
the terrain vague since as early as 1994. One can still find at the UPC the slides he 
presented during his class506 about the same photographers mentioned in the previous 
chapter. And, as a professor at the UPC, at the Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona (UAB), 
and at the Universitat de Barcelona (UB), Solà-Morales also supervised many doctoral 
theses in Spain between 1980 and 2001. These theses mirror Solà-Morales’s pivot at the 
beginning of the 1990s from, as previously mentioned, a rather conventional Catalan 
historiographer’s approach to a theoretician’s approach focused more on contemporary 
urban and architectural questions. Titles of theses he supervised in the 1980s included 
                                                                                                                                                                        
503 Metropolis Master Program. “Theses and Archives Material.” CCCB Archives. 
504 Trans.: “Atopia, other places”. 
505 These were already subtly hinted in the essay. - 1994, trans.: “Thousand Territories in Deconstruction” - 
Muñoz describes himself how he is very influenced by the notion of terrain vague since Solà-Morales was his 
teacher. - Interview between author and Francesc Muñoz in Barcelona. 7 June 2013. 
506 Solà-Morales Archives at the UPC ETSAB. Barcelona.

## S. 250

247 
 
“The Evolution of the Facade in the Italian Renaissance Palace 1450-1537”507 or “The 
Catalan Architecture of Modernity (1901-1951)” 508 . The theses through the 1990s 
gradually involved more contemporary subjects. They became also closer in spirit to the 
terrain vague after the mid-1990s. They even included occasionally the same case studies, 
Mies van der Rohe and Berlin: “Fragments of a Critique: Revising IBA Berlin”509, “The 
culture of the transformation of the contemporary city: Berlin of the reunification of 
Germany”510; or an adjacent subject: “The residential production of low intensity at the 
province of Barcelona 1985-2001”511, Francesc Muñoz’s doctoral thesis in which Solà-
Morales’s work around similar topics is quoted. The impact of Solà-Morales’s terrain 
vague can be noticed within these doctoral theses, whether through direct mentioning or 
through the adopted approach or study subjects. 
The   Anyway   conference      
Solà-Morales was also involved in 1993 in organizing the Anyway conference in 
Barcelona, the third conference of the Anyone Corporation held on the 5th of June at the 
CCCB. During this conference, Solà-Morales presented his essay “Colonization, Violence, 
Resistance”512 on the same lines as the “Terrain Vague” essay. It evoked notions that 
appeared to occupy the author already in the early 1990s, such as montage, continuum, 
otherness, void, utopias, the colonizing role of the architect and destruction. 
Photomontages and Mies van der Rohe’s project for Alexanderplatz in Berlin are once 
again references as in the terrain vague essay: 
“The photomontages of Heartfield or Grosz, the presentation of the urban design 
projects for Berlin by Mies van der Rohe or Hilberseimer, the relationship between the 
possibility of developing the ideas of modern architecture and authoritarian political regimes, 
are equally indicative of the aggressiveness to be seen in the deployment of the new 
architectonic ideas. When the ashes of Europe’s destruction, in the aftermath of the Second 
World War (1939-45), invited the architects to intervene on a massive scale in the historic 
cities, the opportunity for a perfectly legitimated colonization was the prevailing perception 
of the time."513 
                                                                                                                                                                        
507 Trans. from Spanish: “la Evolución de la Fachada en el Palacio Renacentista Italiano 1450-1537”. Gonzáles 
Raventós, Aquiles, 1984, UPC. 
508 Trans. from Spanish: “La Arquitectura Catalana de la Modernidad (1901-1951)”, Josep Maria Rovira 
Gimeno, 1982, UPC. 
509 Trans. Spanish: “Fragmentos de una critica: revisando IBA de Berlin”, Lais Bronstein Passaro, 2002, UPC 
510 Trans. from Italian: “La cultura de la trasformazione della città contemporánea: Berlino della 
riunificazione delle due Germanie: idee di città per la progettazzione del "centro" di una nuova metrópoli 
europea” - Michaela Minen – 2002 - UPC 
511 Trans. from Catalan: “La producció residencial de baìxa intensitat a la provincia de Barcelona 1985-2001” - 
Francesc Muñoz – 2006 - UAB 
512 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Colonization, Violence, Resistance.” Anyway. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. New York: 
Anyone Corporation, 1994. 116–123. 
513 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 117.

## S. 251

248 
As in the "Terrain Vague" essay, Solà-Morales seems to attack already in this essay 
the role of the architect as colonizer of space, especially after the Second World War, and 
to politicize this role, deprecating its aggressiveness. The essay moreover evokes already 
spaces such as the terrain vague: 
“The empty spaces of Beckett’s theatre are spaces neither of freedom nor of 
enclosedness. They are the manifestation of the limit that surrounds us on all sides, impeding 
any escape to some other world. Architecture as utopia is thus devoid of meaning, and 
constitutes an unacceptable game of mystification. It may present itself as dream or vision, as 
the fantasies of memory or the celibate machine producing useless forms or spaces, but the 
design of the alternative, of the perfect reality to come as opposed to the hateful reality that 
holds us permanently captive would seem to be wholly lacking in credibility.”514 
However, unlike in the “Terrain Vague” essay, the intonation here appears far 
more negative and bolder in confronting the delineated predicaments. The artists’ escapist 
strategies in relation to indefinite spaces evoked in the "Terrain Vague" are negated here. 
Solà-Morales seems more pessimistic than he appears a year later in the “Terrain Vague” 
essay. There is no utopia here, there is no escape, or so it seems. Adequately, the 
conclusive point of the essay is equally unwavering in its manifesto approach: 
“An architecture and an art of resistance go beyond the formalism of the thousand 
reflected images of deconstructivism’s spaces, go beyond the silent voids of minimalism. In 
these, in both, it is from the outside, from the text or the object, that worlds that might be 
conceived as alternative are generated. In a culture of resistance it must be the subject, 
individual or collective, who takes the initiative and the risk, using all its intelligence to 
escape, to break, to punish, even if only momentarily, the permanently watchful eye of 
Polyphemus.”515 
This essay could be thus considered a bolder gaudier prelude to “Terrain Vague”. 
Where the latter is subtle and cautiously written in awareness of the period’s aversion to 
manifestos, the former is indifferent and bold in its call for action. As a consequence, it is 
perhaps the positive and inspiring intonation sensed in the "Terrain Vague" that allowed 
this essay and notion to be Solà-Morales’s most famous contribution. 
On a different note, the vibrancy of the terrain vague notion is at least partly an 
outcome of Solà-Morales’s influential standing in Barcelona and abroad. A very similar 
audience attended both 1994 Anyplace and 1993 Anyway conferences. PierLuigi 
Nicolin516, Rem Koolhaas, Sanford Kwinter, Rafael Moneo, Daniel Libeskind, Bernard 
Tschumi and others attended both conferences. Solà-Morales was thus knowingly
