addressing the same theoreticians and architects in his presentations.517 With him being 
                                                                                                                                                                        
514 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 120. 
515 Solà-Morales. Ibid. 123. 
516 Nicolin being related to the Triennale di Milano: audiences of both Triennale and the ANY conferences 
are equally similar. 
517 Perhaps Julia Schulz-Dornburg also attended the conference since she had moved to live in Barcelona in 
1993. She is a contemporary important figure within the terrain vague discourse since her interests and work 
relate to the subject, as seen in her book Arte y Arquitectura: Nuevas Afinidades.

## S. 252

249 
 
the director and delegate for the Anyway conference518, he developed contacts with the 
international attendees, which allowed him to promote the XIX UIA Congress, of which 
the terrain vague also forms an intrinsic part. In fact, Solà-Morales’s archives clearly 
reflect the professor’s initiatives at organizing and working towards the UIA Congress that 
would take place in Barcelona in 1996519. The audience of the terrain vague was therefore 
as local as international, the two often merging. Through these consecutive events, Solà-
Morales had stabilized a strong influential status, which had in turn put his work equally 
in the limelight, promoting thus the “Terrain Vague” essay and notion within Barcelona 
and beyond. 
Other   relevant   publications   in   Barcelona   
Aside from teaching in Barcelona and other cities, Solà-Morales was hectically 
involved with Catalan journals and publishing houses. Firstly, he was in close contact 
with Gustavo Gili and his family who hold the renowned G.Gili publishing house; this 
friendship allowed him to publish numerous books and be involved in editing others. It 
also helped him and his work reach an prominent local standing. Secondly, he was 
influential in Quaderns; he was a colleague and friend to many of the members and 
writers of the journal. Some of his most notable essays within the mid-1990s were 
published in this journal, or republished such as the “Terrain Vague” essay in 1996. On 
another level, the essays published by Solà-Morales within Quaderns echo each other and 
provide corresponding clarifications and nuances to the same notions tackled frequently 
by Solà-Morales from different viewpoints. 
Furthermore, aside from the books and essays published or edited, one can find in 
the archives of Solà-Morales some of the books he had ordered in Barcelona to read 
within the same period he was conceiving the “Terrain Vague” essay: Lewis Mumford’s 
The City in History and Technics and Civilization520, and Paul Zucker’s Fascination of 
Decay521. As expected, one finds interests relative to the terrain vague; the subjects are 
interdisciplinary, notions of decay and ruins are in play, and evidently the element of 
                                                                                                                                                                        
518 Important note: When interviewed, Phyllis Lambert states how this conference was the latent cause to 
her sensitivity regarding the use of imagery in architectural debates and to the decision of prohibiting visual 
material and presentations from the Anyplace conference private debates. 
 - Interview between author and Phyllis Lambert in Montreal. 22 July 2014. 
519 Many such letters addressed by Solà-Morales in 1993 can be found in his private archives. Many 
conferences he conducted in the world between 1993 and 1996 were also intently promoting the XIX UIA. 
520 Mumford, Lewis. The City in History: Its Origins, Its Transformations, and Its Prospects. Harcourt, Brace 
& World, 1961. – and: Mumford, Lewis, and Langdon Winner. Technics and Civilization. University of 
Chicago Press, 2010. 
521 Zucker, Paul. Fascination of Decay: Ruins: Relic, Symbol, Ornament. Gregg Press, 1968.

## S. 253

250 
history, which is often the driving force behind his reflections on the contemporary city. 
The author even used these same books within the reading list for his 1993 Princeton 
lectures. The terrain vague was thus equally a connecting line between these various 
interests of Solà-Morales during this phase and helped bind his thoughts into a cohesive 
urban vision. 
Invitation to Princeton and writing the "Terrain Vague" essay 
Perhaps the most concrete starting point of Solà-Morales’s work on the terrain 
vague was when he wrote the first draft of the "Terrain Vague" essay during his visit, as 
guest lecturer and teacher, to Princeton University in 1993. That year, Solà-Morales was 
mainly occupied with four matters: being a visiting professor at Princeton, initiating the 
Metropolis program, attending and organizing the Anyway conference in Barcelona, and 
finally writing the "Terrain Vague" for Anyplace, the fourth conference of the Anyone 
Corporation. His attention was thus divided between Metropolis, Princeton and the 
Anyone Corporation. 
Princeton provided the perfect environment to focus and be able to write free of 
disturbances. In fact, and according to his wife and interior architect Eulàlia Serra522, this 
was not his first visit to Princeton523 and after every visit the author would come back 
with an essay ready to be published. The 1994 visit was not an exception as it was 
probably in Princeton that he took time and concentration to write the "Terrain Vague". 
Solà-Morales was invited to Princeton to be a visiting professor in 1993524. He had 
met the dean Prof. Lerner, in the autumn semester 1992 when he gave a lecture at 
Princeton on the 30th of September entitled "Open Heart Surgery: Building Architectural 
History". Lerner offered him an academic position that he ultimately had to reject due to 
his obligations and family in Barcelona. Solà-Morales did however propose to be a visiting 
professor at Princeton for one or two semesters in 1993. Accordingly, Lerner and Mario 
Gandelsonas invited him as visiting professor and requested he teaches two classes during 
his stay for the fall semester, one for the Master’s level and one for doctoral students. 
Solà-Morales took great inspiration from these classes to write his "Terrain Vague" 
essay. In fact, traces of the notion and the ideas present in the essay already appear in the 
classes' curricula, and more interestingly in the students' work, kept on record in Solà-
                                                                                                                                                                        
522 Interview between author and Eulàlia Serra in Barcelona. 23 Dec. 2013. 
523 Solà-Morales had a visiting card for Princeton since August 1980 when he was a visitor for the first time. 
The passport of Solà-Morales of 1994 shows also six previous visits (visas) to the United States. The author in 
fact was strongly connected during this period to the American circles. In: Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private 
Archive - Box Princeton. Documents. 
524 According to a letter from Gregotti in Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive.

## S. 254

251 
 
Morales’s archives in Barcelona525. The bibliography of the Metropolis program in 1993 
was also comparable to that of the professor’s lectures at Princeton of that same year, in 
addition to an extensive list of books recommended for the program, by authors relevant, 
in his view, to the terrain vague subject such as Walter Benjamin, Gilles Deleuze and Paul 
Virilio. One could consider that Solà-Morales’s Princeton lectures were in this fashion a 
preamble and groundwork towards Metropolis. Accordingly, the students who attended 
Solà-Morales’s courses at Princeton presented final projects equally relevant to the terrain 
vague notion. 
 
FIGURE 91: Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. Open Heart Surgery: Building Architectural History. Ignasi de Solà-Morales 
Private Archive, Box 23. Princeton University, 30 September 1992. Poster showing also other lectures at 
Princeton during this period, including Anthony Vidler and Brian Boigon. 
Inspirational   academics   and   colleagues   
Other academics teaching simultaneously at Princeton during the same years were 
also inspirational to Solà-Morales’s theories. As seen in his archives, Lerner invited Solà-
Morales to a dinner upon his arrival to Princeton in 1993, along with Alan Colquhoun, 
Georges Teyssot and Alessandra Ponte; those were to become the pertinent figures for 
Solà-Morales during his stay. And, accordingly, Solà-Morales was keen in Princeton on 
knowing and documenting particularly the lessons of Teyssot, Ponte, Colquhoun and 
Christine Boyer. As seen in his archives, he highlighted notions and marked with asterisks 
the literary references in which he was interested; most marks and notes set by the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
525 Princeton Lectures, Students Theses. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box Princeton. Documents.

## S. 255

252 
professor seem linked to the notions used in the "Terrain Vague" essay. Even Solà-
Morales’s own set reading list526 for the classes at Princeton displays the same interests: a 
concern for public space through articles such as "Architecture of the Public Space”, 
interest in art through Goldberg’s book Performance Art, interest in place and void 
through Augé’s Non-Lieux and Ilardi’s La Città Senza Luoghi, otherness through Segalen’s 
L'Autre et le Semblable, and even fluxus and the historicizing of urbanism through 
Sohm’s Happening and Fluxus. The terrain vague seems to emerge thus partly from this 
back and forth between the author and urban concepts circulating around Princeton. 
Teyssot, Ponte and Colquhoun, all gave lectures on themes related to the abovementioned as well as other notions present in the "Terrain Vague" essay. Teyssot527 tackled 
disembodiment and displacement, analyzing the "edification" of an architecture related to 
"everyday life". Ponte lectured on the natural and artificial landscapes of the 16th to 20th 
century, proposing readings such as "Images of Decay: Photography in the Picturesque 
Tradition" by Wolfgang Kemp and "Illiterate Monuments. The Ruin as Dialect and 
Broken Classic" by Barbara Stafford, both of which Solà-Morales highlighted in 
his notes as important. And Alan Colquhoun's class on European Modern Architects of 
the 19th and 20th century also included readings worthy of mentioning: "The Space 
Between" by Hubert Damisch, "Architecture and Nihilism: on the Philosophy of Modern 
Architecture" by Massimo Cacciari, and "Spoken into the Void" by Adolf Loos. One more 
teacher falls as well within this list: Christine Boyer, whose class "Modernism and the 
Historical Industrial City" referred to cultural assumptions embedded within urban forms 
of Modern European and American cities and notions such as destruction, collective 
memory and Walter Benjamin's Fragments of Modernity. Some of the general theoretical 
interests circulating around Princeton during the early 1990s seemed to be the fate of 
cities in transformation, be it post-industrial cities, shrinking cities 528 , or more 
importantly, cities in sprawl. These called for new forms of understanding the 
configurations architecture-landscape; the terrain vague notion partly comes out of these 
reflections. 
It thus seems that Solà-Morales’s contribution at Princeton and his ideas of 
contemporary urban concerns, including concepts of decay, voids, wastelands, interstitial 
spaces, indeterminate space, photographic references, American and European urban 
fabrics and the fascination with the everyday life, all corresponded well to his Princeton 
colleagues' interests and the research they were conducting. These connections seem 
                                                                                                                                                                        
526 The list had to eventually be modified slightly due to unavailability of all the books at Princeton. 
527 Solà-Morales even assigned Georges Teyssot as member of the teaching staff in his newly established 
Master Metropolis Program along with Carl E.Schorske, also professor at Princeton (Emeritus at the time). 
528 When interviewed, Anthony Vidler even states that the American circles were quite attentive to the topic 
of shrinking cities, and that it was through Solà-Morales that the terrain vague notion was made visible. - 
Interview between author and Anthony Vidler in Zurich. 10 Mar. 2015.

## S. 256

253 
 
during that period stronger than Solà-Morales’s link to the Catalan professors and the 
interests of the Spanish academic realm in the mid-1990s. 
Solà-Morales’s own classes in Princeton were furthermore in the same spirit of 
interdisciplinary and urban questions. One for instance was entitled "Categories of the 
Metropolitan Experience" and was an approach to the urban experience derived from 
thought, the arts and architecture, much similar to the author's approach to the terrain 
vague. Even more so, similar to the UIA Congress he was planning for 1996, six notions 
were proposed during his classes as contemporary exploration paths traceable to different 
currents in the arts and in philosophy: Metropolis, Fluxus, Containers, Structures, Visors 
and Terrain Vague. Perhaps the roots of Solà-Morales’s interdisciplinary approach to the 
terrain vague notion can be found here. Once again, similar notions can be traced to other 
seminars of Solà-Morales and finally to the "Terrain Vague" essay: interdisciplinarity, 
voids, photography, otherness and alienation, fluxus, continuity and the metropolis. 
In the same spirit, Solà-Morales gave another seminar at the university that 
revolved mainly around public space. The terrain vague seemed to represent the 
culminating conclusion to the seminar's questions: 
"Are there experiences and repertoires which reveal other possibilities? Are there still 
architectures with the capacity to give form to the experience of the public dimension as 
something other than a residual space?"529 
The various sections of the seminar included titles such as "Absence and Nostalgia 
for the Public Space in the contemporary city" or "Architectures of Retrieval". The class’s 
reading list included titles such as Collage City by Colin Rowe and The City in History by 
Lewis Mumford. Requestioning the definition of urban and public space, its position and 
value in the city, its presence and more importantly its absence; these appear to be the 
general directions the seminars took and from which the terrain vague notion was first 
put to test by the theoretician. The notion hence developed originally from this 
questioning on the definition, place, value and materiality of urban spaces at the turn of 
the century. 
Other   inspirations   in   the   United   States   
Solà-Morales’s connections in the United States during his visit, and its consequent 
effect on his terrain vague formulation, can also be noticed through his commitments 
there between 1992 and 1995. As observed in his archives530, including a couple of private 
address books and numerous letters, the professor was linked to many professionals from 
                                                                                                                                                                        
529 Princeton Lectures, Students Theses. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box Princeton. Documents. 
530 Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box Princeton. Documents – Letters – Personal address book.

## S. 257

254 
both the practicing architects and the academics531 of Princeton, Columbia, the University 
of Pennsylvania, the Rhode Island School of Design, the Massachusetts Institute of 
Technology (MIT), Harvard and Philadelphia University, and finally with professionals 
from the arts such as the Philadelphia Museum of Modern Art, Hacker Art Books and 
MOMA. The network is vast, including interactions with diverse American circles. He 
was hence aware of the art exhibitions of the time and artists and photographers 
previously mentioned. 
The theoretician was also aware of the wave of architectural interest in 
deconstructivist ideas, as he was in contact with many fellows of the AIA American 
Institute for Architecture of which he was himself a member532 through his connection 
with Peter Eisenman. Clarà de Solà-Morales, architect and daughter of Ignasi, indicates533 
Anthony Vidler and Peter Eisenman as the two most prominent inspirational figures on 
the author’s theoretical work in the 1990s. 
Peter Eisenman was an important presence, evidently, in the team making, 
directing and productions of the Anyone corporation. Eisenman534 was the one who 
suggested to Solà-Morales and Isozaki to found the corporation, although his wife Cynthia 
Davidson was, and still is, the real force behind the corporation and the one who chiefly 
edited, planned and organized, the conferences, publications and meetings. The effect of 
Eisenman's theories on the discourse of terrain vague was in good part a reference to the 
discourse of interstices and disappearance535, as aforesaid in the first chapter. Eisenman for 
instance supported536 the idea that building a ‘new architecture’ is a political task that 
carries an immense challenge that is not individual, but rather a communal thing, and 
                                                                                                                                                                        
531 Solà-Morales directed for instance Beatriz Colomina’s doctoral thesis in 1991 at the ETSAB, and she had 
been at Princeton when Ignasi was there. His archives in fact show letters, between him and the dean Ralph 
Lerner, in which he supported her reappointment in 1993 at the rank of Assistant Professor at Princeton. - 
Correspondence between Ralph Lerner and Solà-Morales, October-November 1993. Ignasi de Solà-Morales 
Private Archive - Box Princeton. 
532 In Solà-Morales’s archives one can find an entire address book dedicated to his contacts in the United 
States. One can find also a large number of letters and faxes from this period between the professor and 
these contacts, yet some names are more recurrent than others such as Cynthia Davidson, Alan Colquhoun, 
Xavier Costa, Anthony Vidler...). The author was also in contact with Beatriz Colomina, whom he had 
helped in her tenure-track at Princeton. When interviewed, Anthony Vidler describes how Kenneth 
Frampton helped Solà-Morales get a visiting position at Columbia and how he probably was able to go to the 
AIA through this invite since the AIA could not afford his invite. 
533 Interview between Author and Clarà de Solà-Morales in Barcelona. 23 Jan. 2014. 
534 Eisenman was also well connected to the Spanish context before Solà-Morales. In an interview with the 
author, he described how he was in contact with many of the Spanish architects and theoreticians of the 
period through congresses titled Pequeños Congresos to which he was invited in 1968 in Vitoria, Spain. 
Eisenman was then already in contact with Federico Correa, Oriol Bohigas, Rafael Moneo, Manuel de Solà-
Morales and others. 
535 Eisenman, Peter. Written into the Void: Selected Writings, 1990-2004. New Haven: Yale University Press, 
2007. 
536 Eisenman, Peter. “The Processes of the Interstitial: Spacing and the Arbitrary Text.” Blurred Zones: 
Investigations of the Interstitial  : Eisenman Architects, 1988-1998. New York: Monacelli Press, 2003. 94–101.

## S. 258

255 
 
hence cannot be done by a single human being. In this respect, Eisenman's thought can be 
linked to Solà-Morales’s interest in the disruptive character of terrains vagues and of the 
architect's role in bringing new charged interventions in the city through the interstitial. 
Vidler is similarly evoked by Solà-Morales’s wife Eulàlia Serra as having a 
continuous impact on Solà-Morales’s work, unlike other characters with whom the 
theoretician’s work diverged at one point or another. Indeed, in an interview with the 
author, Vidler describes his friendship to Solà-Morales and how he was first to invite him 
to Princeton537, years before 1993 during his Sabbatical year538. The two theoreticians 
shared many common theoretical interests and had a similar standpoint towards 
architecture and urbanism, as well as a friendship; even though in 1993, Vidler had 
unfortunately already left Princeton for UCLA and therefore could not meet Solà-Morales 
during that year, to the latter's dismay539. In relation to the terrain vague, it was perhaps 
Vidler's notion of the architectural uncanny that gets the closest in the latter’s written 
work. His alternative approach to the field of architecture and urbanism through the 
fields of psychology, art and even semiology could have abetted Solà-Morales in his own 
interdisciplinary approach to the terrain vague. 
Solà-Morales had moreover other affiliations in the United States. He was for 
instance member of the advisory council of the School of Architecture at Princeton540. He 
also gave a lecture as part of the Harvard lecture series of 1993, and he participated at a 
round table “focusing on the post-World War II period and its significance for 
architecture today” 541 at Columbia University, upon invitation by then dean Bernard 
Tschumi, along with Alan Colquhoun, Jean-Louis Cohen, Jacques Gubler and Joan 
Ockman. The events in the United States had, in turn, their own bearing on the author’s 
work. David Plowden for instance, the photographer referenced in the “Terrain Vague” 
essay, moved in 1993 his artworks to the Yale University Archives, which could have 
prompted the presence of the artist as reference in the essay. It is also noteworthy to 
                                                                                                                                                                        
537 As Vidler puts it: “No else was there to invite him.” - Interview between author and Anthony Vidler in 
Zurich. 10 Mar. 2015. 
538 Although Vidler does not specify which year this was, the archives of Solà-Morales show that the earliest 
conference he gave at Princeton University was probably in 1990, entitled “Architecture at the Borders: 
J.Mª.Jujol”. 
539 Solà-Morales even visited Vidler's office and left him a letter declaring his disappointment for not finding 
him "after all these years". – In: Princeton Lectures, Students Theses. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive 
- Box Princeton. Documents. 
540 Appropriately, that same year, Princeton celebrated its 250th anniversary with a speech by its president 
then Harold Shapiro stating the university’s “commitment to civic engagement” and at the “service of all 
nations”. Perhaps Solà-Morales invitation to Princeton falls under the same intent, as the theoretician’s 
interests were equally involved with civic and international engagement. 
541 Letter: Bernard Tschumi to Solà-Morales - 13 September 1993. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - 
Box Princeton.

## S. 259

256 
mention that the Anyone corporation's base was in the United States, in New York and 
indirectly linked to Princeton due to its main members’ affiliations to the university542. 
All these connections brought thus a strong imprint on Solà-Morales, as well as 
supported his mounting to a worldwide recognition. Respectively his work in the 1990s 
reflected concepts inherently universal, such as 'vagueness' or 'difference and 
repetition'543, focused often on the western cities in Europe and the United States, though 
unbound to a specific location or context. Thus inspired and inspiring, Solà-Morales wrote 
his essay in this stimulating environment. 
First reception of the terrain vague notion at Princeton 
In relation to Princeton, many of Solà-Morales’s students’ theses took the notion 
of terrain vague or adjacent ideas as core subject of research. For instance, in Solà-
Morales’s fall term 1994, his notes in the archives include a list of the graduate program 
thesis titles: Kimberly Starr’s “Parking Lots as Terrain Vague”, Mark Naden’s “Urban 
Flows”, Karen Pollock’s “Interstitial Space of Graffiti”, Heidi Liebes’s “Metrópolis II”, 
Mikesch Mücke’s “A tale of Knots”544. An explanation of the grades given by Solà-Morales 
to the students, found in his archives545, portray the strong impact of the notion of terrain 
vague and the methodology used in the essay on the students’ dissertations. On the one 
hand, terrain vague was recurrent as a source of inspiration, as Solà-Morales explains in 
relation to Starr’s546, Pollock’s and Bucholz’s theses. On the other hand, the inherent ideas 
within “Terrain Vague” were used in various theses. Mücke for instance used for her 
thesis hermeneutical thought and the multiplication of references as a theoretical 
methodology to approach her case study of OMA’s Euralille547, while Bucholz took as a 
theoretical base the complexity of urban mobility and the indetermination of urban form; 
he also used a similar methodology of references accumulation as Solà-Morales used in the 
“Terrain Vague” essay. The latter ended his notes with this comment on Bucholz’s thesis: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
542 He also gave lectures in 1994 in Vancouver, MIT and Columbia, taking advantage of his journey to 
Montreal for the Anyplace conference. These lectures were in part a re-presentation of the ''Terrain Vague" 
essay. 
543 According to some of Solà-Morales’s colleagues in Barcelona, it is arguably misfortunate that the professor 
concentrated a lot on his international relations, as it allegedly did not allow him to create a strong base to be 
followed within the Spanish and the Catalan academic realms, with the exception of the Metropolis Master 
Program. This position is debatable. 
544 Princeton Lectures, Students Theses. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box Princeton. Documents. 
545 Comments of Theses by Ignasi de Solà-Morales - Reference: ARCH595. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private 
Archive - Digital Archive. 
546 Comments of Solà-Morales: “Kim Starr has developed a commendable effort at a fairly unprecedented 
approach: understanding the great spaces of intermittent use of the parking lots of down town American 
cities as active and significant parts of urban mutability itself.” - Ibid. 
547 Comments of Solà-Morales on the thesis: “The multiplicity of hermeneutical levels is here a starting point 
that leads to a rainbow of references according to the methodology proposed in the seminar itself.” – Ibid.

## S. 260

257 
 
“It seems to be a legitimate exercise of trying a wide and multifocal reflection even from 
a ‘case study’ that only to some extent materializes what you are trying to view. ‘Terrain 
Vague’ or motion picture? One could deepen and discuss these ideas...”548 
These theses hint hence at attempts to define the terrain vague and what it can 
encompass and they can be considered first attempts at approaching the subject through 
Solà-Morales’s vision, i.e. linked with ideas of urban fluxus, hermeneutical thought and 
other sub-notions present a year later in his "Terrain Vague" essay. 
On another note, Solà-Morales’s notion became linked with some American 
publications. In April 1997, James O’Brien and Robert Clocker asked for instance to 
republish the essay "Present and Futures. Architecture in Cities" in the MIT journal 
Thresholds 14, where the terrain vague notion takes on an important role. In fact, other 
articles’ titles in the same journal edition read as such: “Consolidated Periphery”, 
“Passages”, “Learning Where Not to Build”, “Gray Areas in Green Politics”, “Industrial 
Nocturnes” and “Against Projects”. This journal’s edition had thus themes fluctuating 
between different types of terrains vagues, from brown spaces to industrial ruins to 
leftover spaces and peripheral wastelands. They portray once again a tendency during the 
1990s to define terrains vagues and what they entail, especially after the "Terrain Vague" 
essay. As seen in Solà-Morales’s private archives, the editors had insisted for this 
publication on visual references for the essay, asking especially for visual representation of 
the terrain vague. They also requested a refocus of the essay in order to detach it from the 
UIA Congress and to consider it as self-standing in its understanding of the contemporary 
architectural and urban condition: 
“The focus on the urban condition ending with the terrain vague is good for us. […] The 
rest of the article holds together well with or without reference to the UIA Congress. We 
know that the Congress was the origin of the paper, but the paper’s ideas are valid on their 
own, without it.”549 
Another letter from the editors also stated: 
“Our Thresholds issue is on landscape, building and urbanism. Much inspiration for our 
interest in this issue’s topic came from your ideas about terrain vague.” 550 
Thus, one can see a resonance at MIT of Solà-Morales’s terrain vague being 
propagated after 1994; a resonance linked to an interdisciplinary approach towards 
architecture, bordering other fields and creating new modes of perception through the 
fusion. This created various attempts at joining the urban, architectural and landscape 
layers in new combination befitting contemporary global transformations. That same 
year, Michael Hays also contacted Solà-Morales to express his interest in his 
presentations, at Harvard and Cambridge and elsewhere, and to request to publish some of 
                                                                                                                                                                        
548 Comments of Solà-Morales. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Digital Archive. 
549 Letter: James O’Brien to Solà-Morales, April 1997. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 32. 
550 Correspondence between UIA editors and Solà-Morales, April 1997. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private 
Archive - Box 32.

## S. 261

258 
his recent work as a contribution to the journal Assemblage. Hays moreover included 
Solà-Morales’s article “Weak Architecture” 551 in 1998 within his renowned book 
Architecture Theory since 1968. The choice of article was probably due to the fact that 
the “Terrain Vague” had been repeatedly published since 1994, that “Weak Architecture” 
was more relevant to the architectural historical line drawn in Hays’ ontology, and finally 
that this ontology only reaches in the book so far as 1993 while the "Terrain Vague" was 
first published a year later. 
Thus, Solà-Morales’s influence is apparent through these connections during the 
period of mid-1990s within American universities and among intellectuals of the 
American spheres interested in understanding the contemporary urban condition, which 
includes the terrain vague. In this context, Solà-Morales’s terrain vague seems well 
connected to the circulating concerns about the urban experience and the fragmented 
mosaic of the city, forming a synthesis of these interests. 
 
FIGURE 92: Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. Program for the Articles of 1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive, 
Box 27, 1993. - Notes for the Triennale di Milano Seminar “Identità e Differenze” and for the ANY 
conferences. They show Solà-Morales considering Wim Wenders as his “cosa” for the 
Triennale seminar. They also show his thoughts during this period: mentioning of Deleuze, ideas 
of semiology, theory versus practice, political and technical layers, deconstructivism and 
narrative. It also shows how he was thinking of a paradigm shift from language, to words and 
finally to action. Solà-Morales was clearly preparing his “Terrain Vague” essay. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
551 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Weak Architecture.” Architecture Theory Since 1968. Ed. K. Michael Hays. 
Cambridge, Mass.: MIT Press, 1998. 614–642.

## S. 262

259 
 
1994-1995: First presentations and publications of the essay 
Triennale di Milano and the Italian influence 
The year 1994 is probably the most crucial period within this chronology. In June, 
Solà-Morales presented for the first time, as previously mentioned, a lecture entitled 
“Terrain Vague” within the Anyplace conference in Montreal. From November 24th to 
27th of the same year, the author also published for the first time the essay “Terrain 
Vague” and presented it within the Triennale di Milano XIX seminar, exhibition and 
‘preceding’ 552 publication entitled Identità e Differenze, I Racconti dell’Abitare un 
Seminario, una Mostra, at the Palazzo dell'Arte. Solà-Morales had sent the first version of 
the essay to Pietro Derossi, the scientific coordinator and curator of the XIX International 
Exposition, on the 30th of August, signed June 1994553, along with a set of slides to be 
potentially used for publication, coinciding thus with the same time of his lecture in 
Montreal. He was also a coordinator of a session within the seminar entitled Evento, 
Monumento, Decorazione554, while he gave his "Terrain Vague" lecture within the session 
"Progetto, Contesto, Legittimazione" 555 along with the lecturers Paolo Fabbri, Mark 
Wigley, Rosalind Krauss556, coordinated by Jean François Lyotard557 and commented by 
Antonio Monestiroli and Stefano Boeri558. This was thus the first direct audience of the 
initial "Terrain Vague" publication. These probably took interest in various aspects of the 
essay and notion. Some were already interested, such as Krauss, in the link between art, 
photography, psychology and urbanism, while others, such as the semiologist Paolo 
Fabbri, were probably more interested in the ontological character of the essay. 
In return, the influence of the Triennale di Milano and the Italian intellectual 
spheres on Solà-Morales’s terrain vague differ from the American one. The latter 
presented a field to research, brainstorm, keep up to date with the trends of the period 
and to write, while the former presented a field to experiment with new forms and ideas 
and test the freshly drafted essay “Terrain Vague”. According to an interview with Eulàlia 
Serra559, writing for the Anyplace conference was a highlight in Solà-Morales’s year and a 
                                                                                                                                                                        
552 The organizers decided to publish before the seminar and two years after the seminar in a sequel event. 
553 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “‘Seminario Internazionale: Identità e Differenze. I Racconti dell’Abitare’ - From 
Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive.” 
554 Trans.: “Event, Monument, Decoration”. Initially, in the first reunion of the scientific committee in 
October 1993, Solà-Morales was expected to coordinate the session "Abitare l'Amore" (inhabiting love). 
555 Trans.: “Project, Context, Legitimization.” 
556 Rosalind Krauss, the American art critic and theorist was an audience member but also herself a reference 
in the "Terrain Vague" essay. 
557 Both Lyotard, Wigley were also part of the scientific committee organized by Pietro Derossi. 
558 Initially, during the first reunion of the Triennale scientific committee in October 1993, Jacques Derrida 
and Margarethe von Trotta were expected to be included in this section. 
559 Interview between author and Eulàlia Serra in Barcelona. 23 Dec. 2013.

## S. 263

260 
synthesizing point in his theoretical production. One could almost consider that the 
publication within the Triennale preceding the one within the Anyone corporation 
presented a field to test the essay first before submitting it within this overwhelming 
international scope of Anyplace. In fact, this publication of the essay in Italian560 was 
disregarded from future mentioning, while the publication of the essay within Anyplace 
is often considered the original publication of the essay, even by the author himself561. 
What is more, the theme of the entire Triennale di Milano of 1994 seems to have included 
a great deal of experimentation. In the reunion of the scientific committee prior to the 
seminar, in January 1994, it was agreed that the lecturers and narrators of the exhibition 
would be given a "discrete autonomy"562 as to how their sections of the exhibit would be 
designed and implemented. During the same meeting, it was decided that the theme of 
the Triennale would be about difference, identity and the narratives of the city. In a letter 
from Saverio Monno, the general director of the Triennale, to Solà-Morales563, the curators 
set the intent: 
“The activity of knowing, in the journey of interpretation, aimed at revealing identity 
and differences, may well refer to the consistency and structure of a narrative.”564 
Identity, differences, narrative and knowledge were thus the rotation wheels of 
the 1994 Triennale, which was reflected in the “Terrain Vague” essay in which narrative 
and knowledge are debated in relation to residual allegedly ‘identity-less’ spaces. In fact, 
these seem to be running themes during this phase in the Italian spheres and can be 
considered reactions to Portoghesi’s 1980 Venice Biennale in search for new definitions 
away from the aesthetic fascination, and the overarching proclaimed ‘postmodern’ ideas. 
Interest was rerouted back to questioning rather than finding answers, and towards less 
permanent endeavors; the terrain vague being one such less overarching scenario. 
According to the description set in the program565, the exhibition was meant to be 
a "place of encounter, information, consultation and of permanent discussion: four spaces, 
eighteen "cose" (items), eighteen "Librerie d'affezione" (Libraries of affection), and, among 
other exhibited items, televisions displaying the interviews conducted with the 
theoreticians, including Solà-Morales with his "Terrain Vague" essay and his chosen 
‘object’ and book list. The same spirit of experimentality was also visible in the magazine 
                                                                                                                                                                        
560 Translated to Italian by Alessandra Gambino and Tonino Griffero from the original Spanish version 
written by the author. 
561 Solà-Morales writes at the end of the Quaderns edition of the “Terrain Vague” essay: “This text was 
originally written in English. For that reason the semantic translations are between French and English. 
(Author's note)”. {Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-
Aigua (1996): 34–44.} The essay was however first written in Spanish, translated and first published in 
Italian, then first presented in English. 
562 The Triennale Di Milano Seminar, Exhibit. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 27. Documents. 
563 Ibid. - Letter on 12 September 1995. 
564 Our trans. from Spanish: "La actividad del conocer en el viaje de Ia interpretación orientada a revelar 
identidad y diferencias bien se puede remitir a Ia consistencia y a la estructura de una narración." 
565 The Triennale Di Milano Seminar, Exhibit. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 27. Documents.

## S. 264

261 
 
of the Triennale with the main headline: "Milano in Laboratorio"566. The resonance of this 
exhibition reached at least until 1996 in which an exhibition of the Triennale di Milano 
was once again named Identità e Differenze and which was about the integration and 
plurality of contemporary forms and culture between the ephemeral and the durable. The 
notion of terrain vague is still visible in this subject where the temporary and the 
permanent spaces are analyzed, the terrain vague being one of those contested forms in 
the contemporary urban fabric. Similar notions to the 1994 exhibit were visible in the 
1996 exhibition summary: identity, urban forms, diversity, fragmentation, and most 
importantly urban narratives and temporality567. Solà-Morales was not unconcerned with 
the choices of themes for the Triennale exhibits since he was himself member of the 
scientific committee of the Triennale XIX and attended for instance the meeting in 
January 1994 deciding on these themes. In fact, as it shows in his archives, Solà-Morales 
was involved since the Triennale’s reopening568 in December 1979, already with ideas 
visible in the "Terrain Vague" essay. He also was on various other occasions implicated; he 
participated for example in the Triennale of 1988 with "La Città del Mondo e il Futuro 
della Metropoli", and in February 1990 with "Manifesto e Poetica dell'Architettura"569. 
Evidently, the themes already revolved around urban narratives570 and the future of cities 
and their fragments; even the idea of manifesto was already evoked. 
This experimental focus on the city through the Triennale’s experimental 
framework seemed to nurture the discourse of linking architecture and urbanism. The 
separation between these two scales is blurred depending on the size of the urban 
fragment that comes to define finally what is urban and what is architectural. A notion of 
urban fragments seems to put in a different dialogue the architectural scale and the urban 
one. Perhaps this is linked with the Italian discourses at the end of the millennium in 
                                                                                                                                                                        
566 Triennale notizie 4, Sept.Dec. 1995 - describing three exhibits within the Triennale: “Giuseppe Terragni”, 
“Il Centro Altrove”, and “La Città Temporanea”, the temporary city. In these exhibition occurring between 
1994 and 1996, notions such as the terrain vague are again noticeable, such as in the presented paintings of 
Milano's desolate spaces of Umberto Boccioni or the photographs of a desolate Monte Stella by Paolo Monti. 
567 The Triennale Di Milano Seminar, Exhibit. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 27. Documents. 
568 He wrote already in 1980 in Saber an article entitled "La Reobertura de la Triennale di Milà” (The 
reopening of the Triennale di Milano.) - Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “La Reobertura de La Triennale Di Milà.” 
Saber no1 L’Avenç, Barcelona (1980): 8–9. 
569 Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box Triennale. 
570 Even Pietro Derossi was interested in the narrative of architecture and the urban realm as seen from his 
publication list of that period such as "Progettare nella Città" in Torino in 1988, "Un'architettura per 
Raccontare", an article in Ottagono in 1990; and from his exhibitions then: Le Città immaginate at the 
Triennale of 1987 and Architettura e Urbanistica a Torino in 1991.

## S. 265

262 
which a renewed interest was set on looking at the urban scale as an essential part of the 
architectural discourse571. 
Solà-Morales’s   exhibit   at   the   Triennale   (1994-­‐1995)   
As a consequence to the experimental and interdisciplinary theme, the design of 
the Triennale seminar and exhibit of 1994 was meant to follow the same intent. The 
exhibit was thus divided in different sections following the theoreticians and main 
speakers taking part in the exhibit. Each of the theoreticians' sections was in turn divided 
in two parts: one displaying the "cosa" and one displaying the list of books and essay. Both 
were chosen by the theoreticians and both displays were orchestrated by an artist or 
architect joining forces with the former, such as Zaha Hadid collaborating with Rosalind 
Krauss, or Pete Sans collaborating with Solà-Morales. This seems to accentuate the 
interest in collaborative efforts between artists and architects to redefine the urban 
dwelling experience through this interchange art-architecture572. 
 
FIGURE 93: Sans, Pete. Multifaristol. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive, Box 27, 1994. Final design of the 
stand holding the books chosen by Solà-Morales for his “Library of Affection” at the Triennale di Milano 
Seminar. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
571 The link between the terrain vague notion, the perspective of viewing the city through its fragmented 
state, Abitare la Città571, and finally Colin Rowe’s Collage City571, is evident within the Italian circles. This is 
also visible in Solà-Morales’s vision of a city made of fragments, as advocated in some of his essays, within 
which the terrain vague constitutes one of the fragments. Hence, his perspective on a collaged fragmented 
city pieced together does not go too far away from the discourse of Colin Rowe, Tafuri, the Surrealists, 
Benjamin or Deleuze, all of which were influential characters within the Italian spheres. 
572 An interest recurrent also in Solà-Morales’s work.

## S. 266

263 
 
 
FIGURE 94: Multifaristol and Pete Sans. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive, Box 27, 1994. Photograph 
showing Peter Sans behind his designed stands at the Triennale Seminar. 
Solà-Morales had requested a Spanish designer among Pete Sans, Carlos Riart, and 
Santi Roqueta to be the designated artist to follow his intentions for his part of the 
exhibition. The curators chose Pete Sans to collaborate with him on his display of Marcel 
Duchamp’s artwork "Trois Stoppages-Étalon"573 and his ‘library of affection’. The display 
named "Multifaristol"574 designed by Pete Sans was meant to be simple and rather austere, 
giving higher importance to the content. Thus the "Terrain Vague" essay and the list of 
books chosen by Solà-Morales were literally put under the spotlight. This list was 
adequately very significant to Solà-Morales and in good part representative of his 
interests, especially in relation to the terrain vague and the Triennale’s subject, i.e. the 
experience of dwelling in and out of the city. A text in Solà-Morales’s archives lists the 
books: 
 
1. Homero. La Odisea. 
2. Emile Zola. Le Ventre de Paris. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
573 Marcel Duchamp: Trois Stoppages-Étalon, Paris 1913-14. En una caja de madera (in a wooden box) de 
129,2 x 28,2 x 22,7cms. 
574 One can still find it now in the hall of Solà-Morales’s office and private archives, a lingering memory of 
the Triennale.

264 
3. James Joyce. Ulyses.
