10. André Malraux. Les Voix du Silence. 
11. John Cage. Silence. 
12. R.M. Rilke. Cartas a un Joven Poeta. 
13. Julio Cortazar. Rayuela. 
14. Vargas Llosa. La Ciudad y los Perros. 
15. Juan Marsé. Ultimas Tardes con Teresa. 
16. Roberto Longhi. Piero della Francesca. 
17. Eugène Viollet-le-Duc. Histoire d'une Maison. 
18. John Ruskin. Modern Painters. 
 
The list of books unveils hidden links created through an overarching position 
towards the subject of terrain vague. As will be explained herewith, be they Deleuze, 
Trías or Merleau-Ponty, all are joined in this attempt to redefine the contemporary 
theoretical scene directly or indirectly viewed from a terrain vague perspective. Many of 
these authors are also recurrent in Solà-Morales’s writing and shed light on different 
points related to the terrain vague subject but also his architectural and urban ideas: 
1. Homero. La Odisea: wandering and resistance 
Roland Barthes in his book Mythologies evokes Homer’s Greek epic poem the 
Odyssey, so does Deleuze in Desert Islands575. Both link the book to the idea of erring, 
wandering and the ambiguous relation of man to nature. Barthes writes: “…man strongly 
personifies Nature in order to confront it more readily and to free himself more 
completely.”576 In this sense, Homer’s Odyssey fits well the terrain vague subject. He 
represents the wandering dweller of the terrain vague, similar to Tarkovsky’s Stalker. 
Moreover, Solà-Morales mentions the character as well in his essay “Architectonic 
Heritage or Theme Park?”: 
“…Like Odysseus against the giant Polyphemus, makes it possible on occasion to beat 
the supposed totality of facts and processes. A cunning position with respect to the 
commercialization of the images of mass architecture is the production of other images, of a 
contrary character, capable of stimulating other gazes, interpretative and critical as opposed 
to the established typification.” 577 
In this sense, the author seems to present Odysseus as the metaphor to the terrain 
vague: the reverse image of the productive city obsessed with its images; a gaze of 
                                                                                                                                                                        
575 Deleuze, Gilles. Desert Islands and Other Texts, 1953-1974. Semiotext(e), 2004. 169-170. 
576 Our trans. from French: “L'homme personnifie fortement la Nature pour la prendre plus facilement à 
partie et mieux s'en libérer.” – In: Barthes, Roland. Mythologies. Paris: Seuil, 1957. 106. 
577 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Architectonic Heritage or Theme Park?” (1996): 125–128.

## S. 268

265 
 
resistance. Perhaps this approach of resistance in Milano is also addressed against 
Portoghesi’s 1980 Venice Biennale and against the postmodern obsession with image and 
form and aesthetic consumption. It is a resistance formed by the production of alternative 
counteracting urban images, the terrain vague being one of them. 
2. Emile Zola. Le Ventre de Paris: terrain vague narratives 
The French novelist Emile Zola’s naturalist writing evokes often scenes where 
characters erred in terrains vagues: 
“There, between a mechanical sawmill and a button works, was a strip of meadow still 
remaining, with patches of scorched yellow grass; a goat, tied to a post, walked round in 
circles bleating; further on a dead tree crumbled in the hot sun. … they looked into the 
distance, lost in thought, on the pallid slopes of Montmartre, surrounded by the great forest 
of factory chimneys blocking out the horizon, in that chalky and desolate banlieue, where 
the green trees shading the cheap taverns moved them to the edge of tears.”578 
Poetics and a sense of narrative are also apparent in Solà-Morales’s description of 
terrains vagues. Zola expresses in words a type of terrain vague similar to what the 
photographers in the essay expressed in images. Zola also belongs to a phase in French 
literature where description of urban life was promoted through the naturalist movement. 
Literary naturalism also involves a degree of existentialism, detachment and determinism, 
reflected in the lives of urban dwellers in cities ruled by consumption, as describes Zola. 
For instance in his novel “Aux Bonheur des Dames”, women in European galleries are 
described as lost in consumption frenzy and the power of the market takes over personal 
will. A particular point of view towards modern urban life is hence presented and once 
again a sense of resistance is called for through narrative, similar to the call for resistance 
in the "Terrain Vague" essay against the economic and capitalist forces of the city. 
3. James Joyce. Ulysses: alienation and otherness 
In Irish novelist James Joyce’s Ulysses, one reads: 
“I hear the ruin of all space, shattered glass and toppling masonry, and time one livid 
final flame. What’s left us then?”579 
Such ‘stream-of-consciousness’ mode of narrative was exemplary of Joyce’s 
Ulysses. This author used methods like these to establish a detachment that serves as an 
implicit critique or a ‘negative knowledge’ of the dehumanizing institutions and of society 
under capitalism. Joyce’s presence in this list is thus related to the terrain vague’s image as 
a reverse to feelings of alienation and otherness in the productive city. It is hence linked 
to the theoretical references in the essay such as Kristeva, Choay and Freud, and to an 
alternative urban imagery against the homogenizing city. 
4. Franz Kafka. América: nomad 
                                                                                                                                                                        
578 Zola, Emile. L’assommoir. Paris: Charpentier, 1877. Les Rougon-Macquart. 213. 
579 Joyce, James. Ulysses. Courier Corporation, 1922. 24.

## S. 269

266 
The author is often cited in relation to nomadic notions in Gilles Deleuze’s work. 
He is however not a recurrent reference in Solà-Morales’s work. Similar ideas of 
alienation, realism and existentialist anxiety are yet present in Kafka’s work, which links 
the novelist’s to others in this book list and to the themes mentioned in Solà-Morales’s 
essays. Socio-political and economic powers are also often deprecated in Kafka’s work 
when linked to bureaucratic and controlling processes, in accordance with Solà-Morales’s 
standpoint in the "Terrain Vague". 
5. J.C.F. Schiller. Cartas Sobre la Educación Estética del Hombre: resistance 
Perhaps Schiller is on this list because he is a recurrent reference in Solà-Morales’s 
work, even if not directly related to the terrain vague. The professor represents 
nonetheless in his essays the “Schillerian Project” as the opposite of productivity, of the 
industrial city and as the “reconciliation of man with himself”580. He seems to thus view a 
sense of resistance in Schiller’s work, as he did in others on this list. Schiller’s treatise 
aforementioned is besides related to the manifesto-like approach of Solà-Morales as it 
denounces the disenchantment with the French revolution gone wrong, with political 
powers and with the pejorative consequences behind the human drive to impose order on 
the world. Some of the followers of Schiller’s treatise, such as Herbert Marcuse, found in 
it a resolution to the crisis of social contemporary alienation. In this sense, even Solà-
Morales could be considered a follower of Schiller’s ideas. 
6. Maurice Merleau-Ponty. Le Visible et l'Invisible: perception shift and alienation 
Merleau-Ponty relates to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague also through notions of 
alienation, existentialism and the experience of dwelling in the city. He wrote for 
instance: 
The image at the same time it renders self-knowledge possible, enables a kind of 
alienation: I am no longer who I felt myself to be immediately, I am the image of me that the 
mirror gives me. […] In this sense I am torn from myself, and the image of the mirror 
prepares me to another even more serious alienation, which will be the alienation by 
others.”581 
Again, similar ideas of alienation and estrangement, as in Kristeva’s or in Choay’s 
work, seem to appear. Solà-Morales also wrote at various instances about the link between 
hermeneutics and Merleau-Ponty. He proposed to derive from this philosophy a new 
approach to architecture from an ontological and interdisciplinary perspective; terrain 
vague being one of these perception shifts. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
580 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. La Arquitectura del Expresionismo. Barcelona: Escola Tècnica Superior 
d’Arquitectura de Barcelona, 1982. 5. 
581 Our trans. from French: “L’image en même temps qu’elle rend possible la connaissance de soi, rend 
possible une sorte d’aliénation: je ne suis plus ce que je me sentais être immédiatement, je suis cette image de 
moi que m’offre le miroir. […] En ce sens je suis arraché à moi-même, et l’image du miroir me prépare à une 
autre aliénation encore plus grave, qui sera l’aliénation par autrui.” – In: Merleau-Ponty, Maurice. Parcours 
deux: 1951-1961. Verdier, 2000. 203.

## S. 270

267 
 
7. Félix Guattari - Gilles Deleuze. Mille Plateaux: a thousand reason 
From among the mentioned references, Solà-Morales only included Deleuze’s 
Milles Plateaux in his 1993 Princeton classes’ reading list, alluding to the book’s 
significance for the professor in this period. Perhaps, the reference to Milles Plateaux is 
the most relevant in relation to the terrain vague. The book and the authors are often 
cited in the conducted interviews, they are also a recurrent reference in Solà-Morales’s 
publications and presentations. Deleuze is even cited in the “Terrain Vague” essay as the 
author utilizes the terminologies of rhizomes, striated spaces, forces and the haptic to 
describe residual spaces582. 
8. René Girard. La Violence et le Sacré: the colonizing architect 
“Difference that exists outside the system is terrifying because it reveals the truth of the 
system, its relativity, its fragility, and its mortality.” 583, writes René Girard. The French 
philosopher was indeed interested in the relation between violence, the imposing of rules 
and religion, and the beginning of language and representation. In his philosophical 
approach, much inspired by Claude Lévi-Strauss, difference induces acts of violence. In 
this sense, the terrain vague reveals the fragility of the orderly city and induces aggressive 
action against it. Solà-Morales moreover mentions Girard in his essay “Colonization, 
Violence, Resistance”584 in relation to the violence inherent in the architect’s colonizing 
role, as he later describes in the “Terrain Vague” essay. In this sense, the architect is the 
one protagonist of violence against the existence of terrains vagues. Girard alludes to the 
religious sanctity intrinsic to the action of making a house, a property, a homeland and 
every act of building. It is against this violence where the “Terrain Vague” essay’s last 
point stands. Girard is hence once again a reference to the resistance against the 
hegemonizing and controlling city, as advocated in the "Terrain Vague". 
9. Eugenio Trías. Lógica del Límite: borderlines and art 
Trías was not only a reference but also a contemporary and colleague to Solà-
Morales. He even wrote an entry in Solà-Morales’s memorial book585. On a few occasions, 
Solà-Morales based himself on his book Lógica del Límite to describe a relation between 
the indefinite act of defining limits and modern artworks such as Duchamp’s “The Bride 
Stripped Bare by Her Bachelors, Even”586. Thus Duchamp and Trías are linked in the 
author’s work. On the one hand, art and the urban layer are linked through defining the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
582 Deleuze is also often evoked throughout this thesis in relation to the subnotions in Solà-Morales’s essay. 
583 Girard, René. The Scapegoat. Johns Hopkins University Press, 1989. 21. 
584 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Colonization, Violence, Resistance.” Anyway. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. New York: 
Anyone Corporation, 1994. 116–123. 
585 Trías, Eugenio. “La Última Generación Humanística.” Teorías de La Arquitectura. Memorial Ignasi de 
Solà-Morales. Ed. Josep M. Montaner and Fabián Gabriel Pérez. Barcelona: Edicions UPC, 2003. 69–71. 
586Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Weak Architecture.” Differences: Topographies of Contemporary Architecture. 
Ed. Sarah Whiting. Trans. Graham Thompson. Cambridge: MIT Press, 1999. 57–73.

## S. 271

268 
borderlines of each in respect to the other. On the other hand, the connotations of 
vagueness linked to defining the terrain vague is put in focus. 
10. André Malraux. Les Voix du Silence: artistic gaze 
 In a similar interdisciplinary approach between art and urbanism, Solà-Morales states: 
“Malraux's imaginary museum is not only the beginning of an enlightened cultural 
populism, but with it the sacred precincts of the artistic are opened up to the multiplication 
of gazes, of books of pictures. […] The canon of aesthetic values drawn up by high culture has 
lost its relevance, immersed and manipulated as it is in the eclectic universe of a thousand 
and one visual proposals.”587 
French novelist André Malraux also falls within this category of authors that, 
according to Solà-Morales, best described the contemporary condition of artwork. He 
describes a status-quo of the artistic condition as subjected to an abundance of viewpoints 
and a profusion of “gazes” and information. Solà-Morales’s own essay reflects, as 
mentioned in the previous chapter, this ‘thousand and one visual’. The "Terrain Vague" 
essay is in this sense Solà-Morales’s musée imaginaire in relation to emerging urban 
spaces. Malraux seems to be one of the silent voices in the "Terrain Vague" essay and a 
necessary prelude to understanding Solà-Morales’s perspective on the terrain vague. 
Malraux’s work seems to be as well a resistance against the wave of aesthetic fascination 
where “the canon of aesthetic values drawn up by high culture has lost its relevance”. 
14. Vargas Llosa. La Ciudad y los Perros: fragmented temporality 
The only apparent reference to the Peruvian writer Vargas Llosa in Solà-Morales’s 
work is in his essay “Weak Architecture”588. In this article, he describes the multiplication 
and juxtaposition of time in contemporary literary and art works where the traditional 
linearity of time is fragmented, meddled with, discontinued. These new time formulae are 
intrinsic to the equally fragmented urban fabric to which the terrain vague discourse 
adheres. 
17. Eugène Viollet-le-Duc. Histoire d'une Maison: photomontaged continuum 
Solà-Morales writes also in his article “Weak Architecture”: 
“Viollet-le-Duc, the greatest theoretician of this transition process in his “Entretiens sur 
l'Architecture”, issued in a definitive way, through a meticulous deconstruction of the 
historic architecture, the new building paradigms. The elements of the Roman, Byzantine 
and Gothic architecture elaborately drawn in axonometry, identified themselves as 
autonomous, able to be treated separately independent pieces and, of course, be the subject of 
combinatory montages, of assemblages, which had nothing to do with the organic continuity 
of elements and ornaments of the walls and vaults of classical architecture.” 
                                                                                                                                                                        
587 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Architectonic Heritage or Theme Park?” (1996). – e-pub.uni-weimar.de. Web. 23 
May 2015. 
588Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Weak Architecture.” Differences: Topographies of Contemporary Architecture. 
Ed. Sarah Whiting. Trans. Graham Thompson. Cambridge: MIT Press, 1999. 57–73.

## S. 272

269 
 
In Solà-Morales’s constant attempt in learning from the past to reflect on the 
present and futures of cities, he finds in Viollet-le-Duc an exemplary case, similar to 
Llosa, that equally sets emphasis on the fragmentary quality of montage techniques. The 
notion of continuity is thus shed under yet another light, linked also to history of 
architecture. 
18. John Ruskin. Modern Painters: continuum 
Both Viollet-le-Duc and art critic John Ruskin are considered by Solà-Morales589 
one of the most prominent examples of theoreticians who tried to understand and loosen 
the disparity between the present and the past. The important notion of continuity 
promoted in the “Terrain Vague” essay is also relevant to Ruskin’s work as related to art 
history and to the Gothic style in particular; a continuity Solà-Morales advocates for in 
interventions in contemporary architecture. 
In summary, the various books on Solà-Morales’s list can be joined in this attempt 
to redefine the contemporary theoretical scene directly or indirectly viewed from a 
terrain vague perspective. Accordingly, they all share similar connotations as those 
mentioned in the first chapter of this thesis, i.e. as related to the array of terminologies, as 
well as the third chapter’s themes of photographs linked to the terrain vague: wandering, 
resistance, borderlines, continuity, fragmentation and alienation come to mind. Evidently, 
these books are perhaps some of the most influential on Solà-Morales’s thinking, hence 
the choice. The same connotations also predict the directions the reception of the 
"Terrain Vague" essay took and how the notion developed590. 
Solà-­‐Morales   and   Duchamp:   “La   Cosa”   
Aside from the books list, Solà-Morales’s essay at the Triennale was accompanied 
with Duchamp’s artwork “Trois Stoppages-Étalon”591. The link between Duchamp’s piece, 
the list of books and the “Terrain Vague” essay is seemingly subtle. Even Solà-Morales’s 
notes592 for the Triennale in 1993 show another option for the "cosa" for which he was 
considering opting before finally deciding on Duchamp: Wim Wenders’s photographs. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
589 Moneo, José Rafael, and Ignasi de Solà-Morales Rubió. Apuntes sobre: Pugin, Ruskin y Viollet le Duc. 
Escuela Técnica Superior de Arquitectura de Barcelona, 1975. 
590 As we will observe in the fifth chapter on influences. 
591 Described in the previous chapter. Moreover, while the excerpt in the first publication in Milano includes 
no visual illustration, it holds instead a prelude linking the text to “Trois Stoppages-Étalon”, an art piece of 
Marcel Duchamp. - [Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Triennale di Milano I Racconti dell’Abitare un 
Seminario. una Mostra.Abitare Segesta cataloghi (1994): 74–82.] - The essay does not refer moreover to the 
art piece except in the prelude introductory note. This incident is not reflected in the reception of the terrain 
vague discourse, as not many seem to be acquainted with this first publication of the text in 1994. 
592 The Triennale Di Milano Seminar, Exhibit. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 27. Documents.

## S. 273

270 
The theoretician did not specify which photographs he was considering. Perhaps he did 
not opt for the photographs because they are not an ‘object’ as the Triennale organizers 
requested593. It is nonetheless no surprise that Solà-Morales was considering Wenders as a 
reference; even if this is the only visible reference to the filmmaker within Solà-Morales’s 
terrain vague. In fact, in some of the conducted interviews, mainly with Manolo Laguillo, 
Eduard Bru and Maurici Pla, the reference to Wenders is recurrent as an exemplary case 
of the terrain vague being a ‘major’ topic of interest during this period. 
Yet, Duchamp’s artwork was conceivably the adequate choice as it describes 
through art what Solà-Morales’s essay describes for the urban field. It presents a piece of 
everyday life under a reinstated viewpoint that brings out a novel value hidden in that 
piece. In Solà-Morales’s notes, a description of the choice can be read as follows in a letter 
addressed to the organizers of the Triennale, mainly Derossi and Monno: 
"In the work of Marcel Duchamp, at the boundary between the intellectual and the 
perceptive, there is an exploration of the universal affiliation of objects. 
Things are singled, made unique, in the operation of displacement that removes them 
from their everyday condition to a simulating unique state. It is the readymade. A procedure 
that seems to be the invitation of the seminars’ organizers, asking us to deliver separately 
displayed objects of affection that inhabit our environment. 
It is through the thread of memory that this operation of estrangement is maintained. 
But in Duchamp there is another line of research in the reverse direction, not as much in 
manipulating the existing meaning but rather produce it ex-novo. 
Trois Stoppages-Étalon is randomness preserved, pure production, gratuitous, of 
meaning from the non-existent, the minimal. The material thread and the fortuity of 
gravitation and of time are the producing agents of a form, of an object, that is not born by 
transferring but emerges from its poor physical consistency to the fullness of a fragile 
meaning. 
Light baggage, as the poet Antonio Machado wrote, meaning and art are not in the back 
and forth of everyday world but are found in the thin edge that we can trap between 
thinking and feeling."594 
                                                                                                                                                                        
593 Derossi had specified in two letters to Solà-Morales in December 1993 and January 1994 that the "cosa" 
should be an existing object or made ad-hoc and should be contained within a parallelepiped of 1.5m by 2m. 
The materiality of the object was thus of significance. 
594 The letter was never published. It was written by Solà-Morales in Spanish: "En la obra de Marcel Duchamp, 
en el límite entre lo intelectual y lo perceptivo, hay una exploración de la pertenencia universal de los objetos. Las 
cosas se singularizan, se hacen únicas, en la operación de desplazamiento que las remueve de su condición cotidiana 
a la simulación de lo único. Es el ready made. Un procedimiento al que parecen invitarnos los organizadores de este 
seminario y exposición cuando nos piden que les entreguemos para ser aisladamente mostrado uno de los objetos de 
afección que habitan en nuestro entorno. Es a través del hilo de la memoria como se mantiene esta operación de 
extrañamiento. Pero en Duchamp hay otra línea de investigación en sentido contrario. No tanto manipular el 
significado existente cuanto producirlo ex-novo. Trois stoppages-étalon es azar en conserva, producción 
pura, gratuita, de un significado desde lo no existente, lo mínimo. El hilo material y la casualidad de la gravitación y 
del tiempo son los agentes productores de una forma, de un objeto, que no nace por transferencia sino que emerge 
desde su escasa consistencia física hacia la plenitud de un significado frágil. Ligero de equipage, como escribia el 
poeta Antonio Machado, el significado y el arte no estan en el ir y venir por el universo cotidiano sino que se 
encuentran en la tenue orilla que podemos atrapar entre el pensar y el sentir." – In: Digitalized Items. Ignasi de 
Solà-Morales Private Archive.

## S. 274

271 
 
Solà-Morales thus evoked the operation of displacement related to readymades in 
art. Perhaps he is equally displacing the terrain vague from its forgotten place in the 
urban everyday and putting it in focus. In this sense, the terrain vague is the object of 
affection that inhabits our environment. As Solà-Morales describes for the object, 
memory is also what maintains “this act of estrangement”. Also, as Duchamp creates 
meaning “ex-novo” through his readymade, so does Solà-Morales with the terrain vague, 
by producing meaning through coining a term. The terrain vague being potentially a 
different form of perception and intervention, the beginning of this description is 
interesting as it situates equally Duchamp’s piece between the perceptive and the 
theoretical. The readymade is discussed as being an everyday object made unique, distinct 
through displacement and the “operation of estrangement”, much like the terrain vague 
and its wandering dwellers. The similarity is even more accentuated by the description of 
the object as “randomness preserved, pure production, gratuitous, of meaning from the 
non-existent”; the terrain vague is similarly described by Solà-Morales as an outcome of 
urban and historical randomness and thus falling out of the productive circuits of the city. 
Even the productive forces, i.e. “the fortuity of gravitation and of time”, match those of 
the terrain vague as seen in the last section of the essay where these spaces are portrayed 
as situations in time rather than in place: 
“Only an architecture of dualism, of the difference of discontinuity installed within the 
continuity of time can stand up against the anguished aggression of technological reason, of 
telematic universalism, of cybernetic totalitarianism, of the egalitarian and homogenizing 
terror.”595 
In compliance with Solà-Morales’s description, one could read Duchamp’s own 
explanation of his art piece in 1964: 
“This experiment was made in 1913 to imprison and preserve forms obtained through 
chance, through my chance. At the same time, the unit of length, one meter, was changed 
from a straight line to a curved line without actually losing its identity [as] the meter, and yet 
casting a pataphysical doubt on the concept of a straight edge as being the shortest route from 
one point to another.”596 
The subtlety thus lies in the element of chance: the terrain vague notion is 
introduced as an imprisonment and preservation of forms obtained through chance and 
where traditional givens are put to question through these new forms within the Racconti 
dell’Abitare exhibition. As Solà-Morales describes in a letter to Derossi regarding his 
“Terrain Vague” essay: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
595 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
596 Anne d'Harnoncourt and Kynaston McShine, eds., Marcel Duchamp, exhibition catalogue, Philadelphia 
Museum of Art, 1973. 273-274.

## S. 275

272 
"This is a reflection both urban and political on the residual spaces of the city. It 
corresponds to a line of work I am developing and in which, as you will see, the tradition of 
hermeneutical thought plays a major role." 597 
A political intent is here clearly stated, denying some future descriptions of Solà-
Morales’s essay as devoid from a political position598. Hermeneutics also form part of the 
author’s theoretical and methodological approach. A parenthesis could be opened here 
regarding hermeneutical thought since Solà-Morales often alludes to it, in this letter for 
instance, but also in the “Terrain Vague” essay: 
“…thinking along general lines of radical post-Heideggerian hermeneutics […] in 
simultaneously confronting the perception of the messages that reach us through our 
openness to the world and the behaviors produced in this situation, in Marquard’s thinking, 
in common with radical post-Heideggerian hermeneutics in general, points towards the 
transcending of the split between aesthetics and ethics, between interpretation of the world 
and action on the world." 599 
Solà-Morales connects thus hermeneutics with the need to transcend the split 
between interpretation and action, or more simply between thinking and perceiving the 
urban and intervening on it. The author is perhaps referring here to the German 
philosopher Hans-Georg Gadamer’s position on hermeneutical aesthetics philosophy in 
which the dynamics and processes through which art acquires quality and value take on 
an important role. Gadamer for instance suggests the metaphor of “the play” to illustrate 
the anthropological phenomena from which art emerges 600 , emphasizing on the 
importance of imagination and perception shifts behind the German word “Bildung” 
(shaping, forming, enlightenment), preferring it over “Bild” (image). Solà-Morales, being a 
philosophy graduate, was well acquainted with these notions that lie behind relating the 
artwork and readymade of Duchamp with the city’s readymades, the terrains vagues, and 
how both attain value through processes of perception and intervention in time. There is 
hence a link, through a hermeneutical thread, between the terrain vague and Duchamp’s 
readymades, as well as the Surrealists movement that lead to different promises of 
perception and intervention, and in general a link to various forms of understanding the 
urban through resistance to the hegemony of urban space. 
On another note, Marcel Duchamp’s art is particularly relevant to Solà-Morales’s 
work and region in the mid-1990s: the theoretician had organized in Spain, together with 
                                                                                                                                                                        
597 Solà-Morales sent this letter along with the original "Terrain Vague" essay both in English and Spanish for 
the catalogue of the final Triennale exhibition, as well as ten slides of illustrations that were eventually not 
used for the catalogue. The letter was in Spanish: "Se trata de una reflexíon a la vez urbana y politica sobre 
los espacios residuales de la ciudad. Corresponde a una linea de trabajo que estoy desarrollando y en la que, 
como podrás comprobar, tiene un peso importante la tradición del pensamiento hermenéutico." – In: Letter: 
Solà-Morales to Pietro Derossi, November 1995. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 27. 
598 Solà-Morales formed as well part of the "Comitato Scientifico del Dizionario dell’Architettura del 20e 
Secolo", showing thus even more interest in definitions and hermeneutics. 
599 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123. 
600 Unlike Immanuel Kant whose arguments in Hermeneutics seem more ahistorical.

## S. 276

273 
 
Eulàlia Serra, an exhibition dedicated solely to the artist’s work. The exhibition in the 
artist’s name took place in 1980 in Barcelona, Madrid and Cologne601. This retrospective 
account was a distinctive chapter in the Solà-Morales’s engagement with contemporary 
art. It gained numerous endorsements and positive criticism in the media, partially due to 
the full-scale reconstruction by Solà-Morales of Duchamp’s Le Grand Verre. The interest 
in the artist within the Spanish spheres was then a rather scarce one and still at its 
beginning. This interest slowly grew, aided by Solà-Morales’s avant-garde initiatives 
reintroducing Duchamp on various levels to the intellectuals of his region. The artist 
appeared often as a reference in the author’s writings, such as in “Liquid Architecture”602 
or “Absent Bodies”603. Marcel Duchamp can be hence considered a connecting element 
between Solà-Morales’s essays for the Anyone conferences. In the Anyone conference in 
1991 for example, Solà-Morales notes that Duchamp opposes through sarcasm the “retinal 
value”604 of art to its unrepeatability. From a similar point of view, architects are invited to 
perceive urban spaces as case-specific unrepeatable phenomena whose value must be 
preserved and on which one intervenes in a corresponding unrepeatable fashion. In the 
Anywhere conference in 1992, on the same lines of gaze shifting, the author describes 
Duchamp as avant-garde in exploiting extreme conditions of perception, such as 
artificially stimulated sleeplessness, to exploit new aesthetic effects, and encourages 
architects to seek spatial innovation through the exploration of the “psychological 
pathways of the subject”605. And in the Anybody conference in 1996, Duchamp is again 
cited in relation to a psychoanalytic reflection on the body, where the artist’s passage 
from painting to readymade is described in relation to an abstraction of the body through 
its fragments, similar to the Deleuzian abstraction of the subject in relation to the 
Lacanian subject, in turn similar to the abstraction of the urban fabric through its terrain 
vague fragments. Duchamp is hence repeatedly cited by Solà-Morales as an example for 
architects towards innovation in the domain of perception and reflection on the self and 
the urban environment. 
The same array of ideas from the previous chapters are thus recurrent: interest in 
fragments, interest in hermeneutics, in readymades and in a way of understanding the 
urban through experience and forces. Solà-Morales’s approach seems thus related to a 
wave of resistance against postmodernist currents of the time and the obsession with 
image and architectural objects. Duchamp’s piece is hence interesting as it creates for the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
601 At the Joan Miró Foundation in Barcelona, the Madrid Pension Fund Foundation and the Museum Ludwig 
in Cologne in 1984. 
602 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Liquid Architecture.” Anyhow. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. 1998. 36–43. 
603 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Absent Bodies.” Anybody. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. MIT Press, 1997. 16–26. 
604 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “From Autonomy to Untimeliness.” Anyone. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. New York: 
Rizzoli International Publications, 1991. 185–190. 
605 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Place: Permanence or Production.” Anywhere. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. New 
York: Rizzoli, 1992. 108–115.

## S. 277

274 
“Terrain Vague” essay a first base in art, an interdisciplinary staging and a theoretical 
inspiration, three elements that Solà-Morales attempts at staging for the essay and notion. 
Similar to Solà-Morales’s approach to Duchamp, the terrain vague is put under this dual 
light between the academic and the practitioner; hence the effect on reception: both 
academics and practicing artists and architects were inspired. 
Italian   references   in   the   essay   
In a letter addressed to Evelina Calvi, then scientific coordinator of the internal 
session of the Triennale, Solà-Morales requests: 
"Photographs for my "Terrain Vague" essay. You can choose three photos from any 
author such as Olivia [SIC] Barbieri, Manolo Laguillo, David Plowden, and those cited in the 
text. The last Triennale published in its final catalog beautiful photographs of this type, but I 
do not see it logical to repeat. The only matter to keep in common concerns "signed" 
photographs by contemporary photographers of cityscapes whose common feature are the 
voids, obsolete and expectant spaces. I fully trust in your selection capability."606 
The letter shows two eminent matters. Firstly, the author's essay and the context 
of the Triennale are not separate. In fact, the audience in Italy was familiar with his 
references and the author relied on the Triennale team for support in making his exhibit 
and publication. Secondly, Solà-Morales’s essay did not fall out of context. In fact, the 
Triennale publications preceding the one in 1994 were already depicting similar spaces 
and notions such as the terrain vague. Solà-Morales’s essay is thus once again a 
synthesizing piece within intellectual circles already prone to the discourse of the terrain 
vague from an interdisciplinary 607 point of view; specifically here between art, 
photography and architecture. In fact, aside from the Triennale event and the influential 
figures from the Italian spheres on Solà-Morales’s work and theories, some of the direct 
references in the "Terrain Vague" essay are themselves Italian. Some artistic events 
                                                                                                                                                                        
606 Our trans. from Spanish: "Fotografias para mi articulo "Terrain Vague". Podeis escoger 3 fotos cualquiera 
de autores del tipo Olivia [SIC] Barbieri, Manolo Laguillo, David Plowden, tales como las que se citan en el 
texto. La Triennale anterior publicó en su catalogo final bellas fotografias de este tipo, pero no creo que sea 
lógico repetirlas. Lo unico que deben tener en comun ha de ser que se trate de fotografias "firmadas" por 
fotógrafos actuales sobre paisajes urbanos cuya caracteristica comun sean los espacios vacios, obsoletes y 
expectantes. Confio plenamente en vuestra capacidad de selección." – In: Letter: Ignasi de Solà-Morales to 
Evelina Calvi. 24 Oct. 1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 27. 
607 In the same spirit of interdisciplinarity, an event directly following the Triennale seminar and exhibit was 
curated by Stefano Boeri entitled L'Opera e i suoi Racconti from 28 November to 21 December 1994. It was 
meant to be a continuation of the initial exhibit just few days earlier. Various sections within the exhibit 
program were dedicated to a mix of disciplines: a section on art, another on cinema with Silvio Soldini, and 
finally one on photography entitled "Tre Racconti sulla Città" where Basilico, Chiaramonte and Rosselli 
exhibited photographs of urban spaces. This event proved once again how the Italian scene was prone and 
interested in interdisciplinary approaches and characters, and how art, photography, architecture and other 
domains were interlinked with artists such as Basilico who studied architecture and photographed cities. The 
section on photography in this exhibit also complements the original Triennale’s intent on accentuating 
narrative, particularly that of urban experiences of city dwelling.

## S. 278

275 
 
happened as well in Italy at the end of the millennium, which could be linked with Solà-
Morales work on the terrain vague, be it inspired or inspiring. Thomas Struth for instance, 
also a reference in the “Terrain Vague” essay, exhibited his photographic work in 
Milano in 1993, while Olivo Barbieri was exhibiting his photographic work at the Venice 
Biennale of 1993, 1995 and 1997. Both exhibitions were not far in spirit from the "Terrain 
Vague" essay. A third reference in the essay, Gabriele Basilico, along with the architect 
Stefano Boeri, contributed also in 1994 to the very same Triennale. The photographer was 
taking as well pictures that were to be published in Interrupted City. These events are 
perhaps behind Solà-Morales opting for these artists as references in his essay or behind 
his familiarity with their work. In the short yet beautiful text that Basilico writes 
accompanying his photographs of Milano and its terrains vagues, he describes, as 
previously mentioned, these spaces as fragments of an “unmanageable mosaic”608, elements 
belonging to the tense narrative of the city. The terrain vague is not denounced as such, 
yet it is implicit: 
“The zone in which the cities crumble over their own limits and where tissues 'heal', 
abandoned for many years to a slow lethargy, reproducing new skin of unexpected features. 
It is the largest station of urban 'coexistence'”609 
The terrain vague is here described by Basilico as if it were “urban soil” in a 
regeneration phase. A similar perspective is reflected on the city as an amalgam of the past 
and the present. The approach is therefore similar to that noticed within the essay’s 
references as seen in the previous chapter. 
Around   the   Triennale   and   the   Italian   influence   
Evidently, those involved in the Triennale exhibit were not only artists and 
certainly not only Italian. Peter Eisenman for instance was participating, and so were Jean 
Nouvel and Juan Navarro Baldeweg, among other architects invited to narrate their 
projects. Solà-Morales knew some of them quite well and had collaborated with them on 
several occasions. In fact, Solà-Morales had sent letters on March 1994 to Rafael Moneo 
and to Peter Eisenman urging them to take part in the Triennale610. Solà-Morales’s efforts 
to promote the Triennale events indicate his interest in the Triennale experiment and a 
will to endorse its interdisciplinary approach taking increasingly place in Italy, especially 
after the 1980 Venice Biennale. The presence of Eisenman611 at the Triennale indicates 
furthermore how the audiences of the Triennale and the Anyone Corporation are far from 
being separated, even if distinctions in approaches to the architectural field are evident. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
608 Basilico, Gabriele. Interrupted City. Barcelona: Actar, 1998. 2-5. 
609 Ibid. 
610 The latter was finally one of the narrating architects in the exhibit, upon the additional request of Mark 
Wigley, but did not attend the entire seminar. 
611 Peter Eisenman participated as one of the architecture narrators and exhibited his work.

## S. 279

276 
This overlap hints at how the stakeholders in architectural theory were and are often well 
connected and how the global interests in architectural theory are generated by this 
fascinating network of people and ideas, which in due course gave also birth to the terrain 
vague. 
On a similar note, in interviews with the author, Anthony Vidler and Peter 
Eisenman both reflected on the great influence of some Italian intellectuals on the 
Spanish scene, including Solà-Morales. Vidler for instance describes how Manfredo Tafuri 
and Aldo Rossi were friends and theoretical guide to both Solà-Morales and himself, even 
if they did not completely agree. While Eisenman describes how the Italian architect 
Vittorio Gregotti was the only non-Spanish person, other than him, to be invited in Spain 
to the Pequeños Congresos. Even Josep Lluis Mateo, when interviewed612, describes, as 
does Vidler, Paolo Portoghesi’s 1980 Venice Biennale as a crucial event to consider in 
relation to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague. They both view in his essay the same kind of 
resistance against the alleged postmodern wave that swept through many intellectual 
hubs during this phase. 
Then, aside from references and the Triennale di Milano event, Solà-Morales’s 
work was in reverse relevant during this period to Italy due to the country’s terrains 
vagues. The professor was also involved throughout his career with other Italian cities. 
Jordi Oliveras even hints, in an interview with the author, to Solà-Morales being a 
theoretician not fully immersed in Spain, but rather always having a foot in Milano613. 
In Siena for example, he attended in 1992 the international convention of 
urbanism in March under the title Murallas y Ciudad Historica. Many important 
theoreticians were present such as Carlo Olmo, Andre Corboz from Zurich and Bernardo 
Secchi. In the convention, Solà-Morales wrote "Los Locos Arquitectos de una Ciudad 
Soñada", discussing the visionary plans and projects that overwhelm cities, similar to how 
he describes the violent colonizing role of architects in the “Terrain Vague” essay. 
Other   relevant   Solà-­‐Morales   publications   in   Italy   
In that same period, Solà-Morales was also involved in several Italian publishing 
houses and magazines such as Lotus and Lotus International, with Mirko Zardini and 
PierLuigi Nicolin, and Domus with Vittorio Lampugnani. He wrote various articles in the 
same period that, as aforesaid, feed into each other; the "Terrain Vague" essay belongs to 
this mesh of essays. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
612 Interview between author and Josep Lluís Mateo in Zurich. 11 Apr. 2013. 
613 Interview between author and Oliveras Jordi in Barcelona. 18 Dec. 2013.

## S. 280

277 
 
The link between Solà-Morales’s work on the terrain vague and his involvement 
with Lotus and the publishing house Electa dates back to 1986 when the author wrote 
"Minimal Architecture in Barcelona. Building on the Built City"614 as one of the Electa 
Documents series. This monograph was about the development of Barcelona, and about 
monuments and the technique of collage. Solà-Morales links, in the article, technology 
and violence with the transformations at the periphery. But more importantly, it was in 
this text that the author first mentions the term “terrain vague” in a publication. Even 
references to ruins, interstitial spaces, fragments and other adjacent notions are visible. 
One can recognize moreover that the terrain vague notion in Solà-Morales’s thought was 
in the process of making since 1986 and was not detached from Milano and Barcelona, but 
is rather very linked to these cities and to the transformations of these cities since the 
1980s. 
Then, in December 1992, Solà-Morales also wrote a letter to PierLuigi Nicolin 
along with an article entitled "La Arquitectura de Barcelona al final del 1992" for the 
Forum of Lotus International. Already in this article, notions present in the "Terrain 
Vague" essay are addressed. The forum was entitled Contestualismo (contextualization) 
and involved a number of important theoreticians such as Anthony Vidler. The author 
presents for instance the idea of the “coordinating architect” (architetto coordinatore) that 
seems to be the final note in the "Terrain Vague" essay; he also discusses architecture on 
the margins of the city, Benjaminian reflections and the art of montage and collage, all 
linked in a similar hermeneutic fashion as in “Terrain Vague”615. 
On a similar note, one edition of Lotus616 in 1997 was entirely dedicated to the 
Anyone Corporation, with various essays and opinions from its members. Solà-Morales 
was most probably behind this edition where Cynthia Davidson appears as main editor. 
This edition helped better connect the Anyone Corporation to the Italian theoreticians. It, 
at the same time, reveals in reverse an acute interest by the magazine to hold a special 
edition focused on the corporation. 
Thus, be it in his choice of elements to exhibit, the “Terrain Vague” essay itself, or 
the mere fact that he contributed to this specific Triennale di Milano, Solà-Morales’s 
character as interdisciplinary theoretician of multifaceted notions appears to be the 
underlying tone. One could almost debate the interdisciplinary character of the terrain 
vague once separated from the author, if it were not for the notion’s close link with this 
very same interdisciplinarity. Solà-Morales’s success nonetheless in bringing forward this 
                                                                                                                                                                        
614 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. Architettura Minimale a Barcelona: Costruire Sulla Città Costruita = Minimal 
Architecture in Barcelona  : Building on the Built Citty. Milano: Electa, 1986. 
615 Membre Del Comite de Lotus Internacio (Milano). Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Blue File. 
Documents - Letters. 
616 Davidson, Cynthia, ed. Lotus 92: rivista trimestrale di architettura (1997).

## S. 281

278 
characteristic of the notion is not to be taken lightly, for his importance is in his role as 
synthesist of an interest and approach reflecting an entire era and generation at the turn 
of the millennium. 
 
FIGURE 95: Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. Notes during the First Reunion of the Scientific Committee for the 
Triennale Seminar. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive, Box 27, 1993. - Notes showing Solà-Morales’s 
interest in notions of narrative, humanity and monumentality.

## S. 282

279 
 
First   essay’s   reception   at   the   Triennale   di   Milano   
It is difficult to assess the reaction to Solà-Morales’s presentation of the terrain 
vague at the Triennale di Milano due to a lack of archived records. However, the audience 
of the Triennale overlaps well with the audiences of the UIA Congress and partially with 
that of the Anyplace conference at the CCA. Their voices can thus be heard there617. In 
conclusion, Solà-Morales’s presentation at the Triennale was more a prelude to the events 
that would follow and a first attempt by the professor to test his newly drafted essay in 
front of a critical audience. 
Presenting the essay at Anyplace in Montreal 
 
FIGURE 96: Anyplace Conference 1994 - CCA Collection-Anyone Corporation Fonds. 
Considered formally the event in which Solà-Morales presented the notion of 
terrain vague to a worldwide public, the Anyplace conference took place at the Canadian 
Center for Architecture, in Montreal in June 1994, i.e. few months before the publishing 
of the Triennale Proceedings618, although the Anyplace book was published a year after619. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
617 For instance, a letter from Vittorio Savi from Florence and Milano to Solà-Morales in October 1994 shows 
how he took part in the UIA Congress. - Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 1994. 
618 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Triennale di Milano I Racconti dell’Abitare un Seminario. una 
Mostra.Abitare Segesta cataloghi (1994): 74–82.

## S. 283

280 
The team of the CCA, mainly Allan Penning, and the director Phyllis Lambert hosted the 
event. As the Anyone corporation was gaining by then international renown, many 
influential figures in the international architectural scene attended the conference, such 
as Arata Isozaki and Peter Eisenman, the co-founders of the corporation, Jeffrey Kipnis 
and Rem Koolhaas, who were even on the board of directors, as well as others from 
various nationalities and fields of expertise, of whom some were particularly interesting 
for the terrain vague subject and had a significant role to play later in the development of 
the notion620. This diversity of people was due to the interdisciplinary character of the 
Anyone Corporation and this particular conference in which the aim was stated as such: 
 “Anyplace sees architecture as placing, relocating, and rethinking science, technology, 
art, politics and philosophy rather than the more commonplace view of architecture as being 
reformulated by these disciplines. Anyplace thus asks the question: Do the conceptual and 
physical constructs of architecture affect other disciplines in a way that is not readily evident 
or even acknowledged? How, for example, does architecture affect Singapore or Bosnia or 
Inuit territories as emerging political entities? How does architecture "civilize" art? How does 
architecture potentially help to reconceptualize late-20th century thought? How does 
architecture inform the inhabitation of cyberspace? As place becomes identified more by its 
function than its geography, the notion of place is changing from the static or the stationary 
to the dynamic and conceptual. Likewise our understanding of architecture is changing from 
that of traditional place bound practices to one of movement, mediation, and anyplace. The 
Anyplace meeting, in a program of roundtable presentations and discussions will address the 
place of architecture in thought, in politics, in art, and in science, and the ability of 
architecture to occupy any place.”621 
The premise of the conference was thus on the interdisciplinarity within 
architecture, and on the place of other disciplines in the architectural theory and practice. 
It was also about emerging political conditions and the ability of architecture to occupy 
place, physical or theoretical. And, behind the direct interest in architectural practice, 
was a concern regarding globalization at the turn of the century and the loss of scale 
between local and global. The question was thus how to intervene suitably on the urban, 
including the terrain vague, and how to situate the urban within and without predefined 
“places”, i.e. within Anyplace. 
In the same interdisciplinary spirit, Solà-Morales’s section in the conference was 
entitled The Charge of Architecture in the Arts and involved Melvin Charney, Douglas 
Cooper, and Johanne Lamoureux, i.e. characters between art architecture and history, and 
an architectural project: the new pavilion of the Republic of Korea in the Venice Biennale 
Gardens by Seok Chul Kim. The terrain vague notion, especially how Solà-Morales 
tackled it, fits perfectly within this session and discourse. Feeding art, and in Solà-
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
619 Davidson, Cynthia., and Anyone Corporation, eds. Anyplace. Cambridge, Mass.: Anyone Corp.; MIT 
Press, 1995. 
620 Luc Lévesque is among those who were inspired by the “ Terrain Vague ” lecture and became later 
influential for this discourse. Delfy was also present at the CCA lecture. 
621 Anyplace Files. CCA Archives - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001. Documents.

## S. 284

281 
 
Morales’s case photography, into architecture, and vice versa, was conforming in good 
part to Solà-Morales’s interests. Others in this session and in the conference in general 
spoke and wrote about the correlations between the field of architecture and other 
domains such as science, philosophy, technology, “cyberspace” and politics, finding 
common ground between these fields and architectural thought and practice, as well as 
perceiving architecture through a multitude of binoculars. 
Ideas of a rather global, transdisciplinary appreciation of architectural and urban 
notions are thus visible in the Anyplace conference and in Solà-Morales’s approach. 
Moving from an artistic and photographic perspective to a rather scientific and 
semiological analysis then to a psychological economic and political layering, the essay 
shares the objectives of the Anyplace publication in which it is situated, and the group to 
which the publication belongs, i.e. the Anyone Corporation. In this regard, Cynthia 
Davidson evokes this approach by stating: 
"The problem of site becomes newly defined within the idea of globalization. 
Representatives from different countries and different disciplines come not to explain but to 
challenge the theme of global dialogue. This movement also mirrors the nomadic condition 
of late-20th-century culture, where everything from people to information is constantly 
crossing borders."622 
The book Anyplace was indeed generated in order to reflect on these nomadic 
interdisciplinary links between architecture and other fields such as the artistic realm, the 
political and the scientific ones. On the one hand, the publication, including the essay at 
hand, aimed at understanding the influence on and by architecture in regard to this 
interdisciplinarity, and at generating new perspectives on Architecture and Urbanism 
based on these interconnections. On the other hand, the Anyone Corporation falls equally 
within these interdisciplinary aspirations, coupled with objectives revolving around the 
notions of globalization and undecidability623. 
 
FIGURE 97: Anyplace Conference Poster. CCA Collection - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001, 1994. 
Photograph. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
622 Davidson, Cynthia. “Anyone Anywhere Anytime.” Lotus  : rivista trimestrale 1997 : 94–95. 
623 Davidson again mentions in relation to this attempt for undecidability: "All of these issues of the 
undecidable relate to architecture directly, but not to architecture as host. In fact, the attempt of the placebound discipline of architecture to host an investigation of the undecidable is one of the risks of the Anyone 
project." [Davidson, Cynthia. “Anyone Anywhere Anytime.” Lotus  : rivista trimestrale 1997 : 94–95.]

## S. 285

282 
Conditions   around   the   CCA   presentations   
As did Peter Eisenman in his own lecture, Solà-Morales initiated his lecture by 
apologizing for the lack of visuals 624. He urged the audience however to use their 
imagination and consider the lack of visuals unimportant. This position does not 
seemingly fall far from Solà-Morales’s original intent: photographs and slides appear to be 
an added process in Solà-Morales’s production as seen in the author’s notes. Letters back 
and forth in 1993 and 1994, between him, Cynthia Davidson and Pietro Derossi, suggest 
that the author was first interested in the written essay and was later urged in postscriptum by the organizers to submit visual substantiation625. However, at the Anyplace 
conference, the decision to prevent the partakers in the conference from using visual 
support came from Phyllis Lambert. The CCA director and member of the Anyone board 
of directors, in an interview with the author626, states that she had grown intolerant to the 
strengthening sway of the image in the architectural discourse and to the increasing 
reliance of architects on visual presentation, especially after the 1994 Anyway conference 
in Barcelona. Such a position is noteworthy in this period and for such a notion as the 
terrain vague. Firstly, in an architectural realm at the turn of the millennium where the 
image was gaining a dominant place, the act of banning the image and relying on the 
word and the imagination could be considered a strong political statement and reaction 
against627, in Josep Lluís Mateo’s words, “the overruling postmodern movement of the 
period.”628 Secondly, Solà-Morales used this to his advantage as an opportunity to discuss a 
notion that could easily be haunted by specific imagery, and to rely solely on theory, 
empowering thus the imagination of the audience; a rather bold move to coin a term and 
introduce a new discourse. The author in this manner kept faithful to the vagueness of the 
terrain vague, i.e. preserved a visual formlessness to the notion, similar to Andrei 
Tarkovsky’s film Stalker in which the depiction of the Zone is never wholesome, always 
fragmented and based on the protagonists’ experience of the area in time. The terrain 
vague depiction appears equally fragmented in Solà-Morales’s essay, with a multitude of 
references and a play on a triple-signification of the term “vague”. The approach is more 
compliant to the hermeneutic approach where meaning is in focus and signification is 
debated and historicized. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
624 Anyplace Conference Recordings. CCA Archives - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001. Video. 
625 This argument is however not fully valid considering how the author around that period taught at various 
universities and relied heavily on slides at the Polytechnic University of Catalonia to give his lectures. 
626 Interview between author and Phyllis Lambert in Montreal. 22 July 2014. 
627 Even Cynthia Davidson describes, in an interview with the author, how she later decided to initiate after 
this phase, a journal entitled Log that was conceived as a resistive force against the seductive power of the 
image. The journal is still ongoing. 
628 Interview between author and Josep Lluís Mateo in Zurich. 11 Apr. 2013. Oral record,

## S. 286

283 
 
 
 
 
FIGURE 98: Anyplace Conference Stills. CCA Collection - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990 – 2001, 1994. 
Photographs.

## S. 287

284 
On another note, Solà-Morales’s presentation was in English. As shown in video 
recordings, the theoretician was reading from his pages. He alluded to the importance of 
nuances within his speech by weighing vocally every word he read, pausing after every 
important term. Rather than an incapability to improvise 629, the lecture seemed to 
accentuate how condensed and elaborately constructed the essay was. Solà-Morales’s 
presentation could almost be considered a theoretical performance in its own merits so 
much the lecture was carefully displayed. Each paragraph of the essay could conceivably 
form a chapter with its lengthy enumeration of references in a multitude of domains from 
philosophy to photography, art and psychology. Furthermore, along with the 
international edge to the Anyplace and the other ANY conferences, the initial audience of 
the CCA in Montreal played perhaps a role in the approach adopted by Solà-Morales: 
bilingual terminologies, French and English, different fields and mainly cross-continental 
references between the North American continent and Europe. Montreal’s history and 
character is also probably relevant in this regard: a bilingual post-industrial city whose 
development was facing similar threats to that of Barcelona. Architecture and urbanism 
were entangled in Montreal; the city is thus linked to Solà-Morales’s questions on the role 
of the architect in the development of the city and its wastelands. Montreal’s own terrains 
vagues were, and still are, a matter of controversial debate. 
In addition, one could say that Solà-Morales’s presentation was more prescient 
than other lectures witnessed at the Anyplace conference. The approach was fresh to set 
focus on urbanism from an architectural point of view, as well as trying to merge 
influence from the arts into architecture, an ongoing interest particular to Solà-Morales’s 
work. This was similar to the professor’s contribution in establishing the Master 
Metropolis program he envisioned as dedicated to the interdisciplinary mission of feeding 
art into architecture and vice versa. One could also restate that Solà-Morales’s relation to 
the conference in Montreal was fairly different from his involvement at the Triennale di 
Milano. The Anyplace conference took on a much more formal note. On a yearly basis, 
Solà-Morales’s essays for the Anyone Corporation were considered culminating and 
conclusive points in the theoretician’s work. Being a co-founder of the corporation along 
with the pressure of international exposure and the importance of these conferences’ 
contribution to the field, all these factors set high standards for Solà-Morales who 
dedicated much attention and time to his work for the Anyone Corporation630. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
629 Solà-Morales’s fluency in English was indeed not his best asset, as visible in Audio recordings in the CCA 
archives. One finds also in Solà-Morales’s archives that a translation agency based in Spain was responsible of 
translating many of the author’s essays from Spanish or Catalan to English. 
630 The Interview between author and Clarà de Solà-Morales, daughter of Ignasi and architect herself at the 
Solà-Morales office, confirms this significance. - Interview between author and Clarà de Solà-Morales in 
Barcelona. 23 Jan. 2014.

## S. 288

285 
 
The “Terrain Vague” essay holds thus heavy significance within the author’s 
production, especially in 1994 when the Anyone Corporation was at the peak of its 
prominent standing within the field. In 1993 moreover, the first edition of the ANY 
Magazine had been published, which points again at the expanding presence of the 
Anyone in this period and to its growing public. 
 
FIGURE 99: Anyplace Conference Participants. CCA Collection - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001, 
1994. Photograph. 
First reactions to the essay at the CCA 
The ANY conferences were an annual phenomenon; internationally recognized 
with an audience ranging from the most renowned names in various fields such as 
architecture, urbanism and art history, to local architecture students and people merely 
interested in the given theme. In fact, a caricature in 1992 under the title “The Seven 
Lucky Gods of Architecture”, accompanying an article631 in Telescope 8 on the ANY 
conferences, portrayed certain conferences participants and some of the ANY board of 
directors, including Rem Koolhaas, Peter Eisenman, Arata Isozaki, Sanford Kwinter, 
Jacques Derrida, Madeline Arakawa Gins and Akira Asada, on a boat seemingly pulled 
away by a dragon on the way to the Anywhere conference which took place in Yufuin on 
the Kyushu island in Japan. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
631 Telescope n.8 (autumn 1992) pp.34-37. CCA Archives - Vertical Files: Anyplace CCA. Article.

## S. 289

286 
 
FIGURE 100: ‘The Seven Lucky Gods of Architecture’ Last seen off Kyushu... in Telescope 8 - (autumn 1992) 
37. CCA Archives - Vertical Files: Anyplace. – Caricature portraying from left to right: Akira Asada, Jacques 
Derrida, Sanford Kwinter, Madeline Arakawa Gins, Arata Isozaki, Peter Eisenman, Rem Koolhaas. 
The role of the ANY lectures in the theoretical architectural and urban 
development was as a consequence wide-ranging, and consequently elusive to determine. 
The Anyplace conference at the CCA in Montreal was no exception. Nonetheless, in the 
videos of the conference, it is apparent that Solà-Morales’s presentation had already made

## S. 290

287 
 
an impact within the conference itself: even if set in the enclosed session, Solà-Morales’s 
words were quoted after his lecture in other presentations and his thoughts were reevoked by others throughout the rest of the conference632. 
On a similar note, for each of the sections of the conference, a debate session 
followed. In Solà-Morales’s section however, the debate session was minimal and was 
therefore not mentioned in the proceeding publication633. Moreover, since Solà-Morales’s 
lecture was part of a closed session of the conference, only a select number of participants 
were involved in the roundtable discussions and lectures in the research center of the 
CCA, among which was the “Terrain Vague” lecture. The remaining larger public and 
audience followed the session through live streaming in other antechambers of the center. 
In this regard, in a letter634 in 1994 to the board of directors635, Cynthia Davidson puts in 
summary the minutes636 of their third Anyone board meeting held in Barcelona in 1993 
with the aim of preparing the forthcoming Anyplace conference. She confirms Phyllis 
Lambert’s suggestion of making only the evening session of the Anyplace conference open 
to public, while keeping the rest of the conference exclusive to private audiences. This 
could have purportedly meant to keep the discussion at a higher more serious professional 
level, beyond public interference. It could have also been due to the experimental 
innovative aspect of the notions presented and therefore was meant as a roundtable 
workshop rather than final presentations. This secretive exclusive aura accentuated 
furthermore the performative character of the lectures and conference, and the 
dramatization and impact these lectures had. This complemented Solà-Morales’s already 
dramatic presentation and gave the "Terrain Vague" lecture an air of prophecy. What is 
more, this overall attitude towards the ANY conferences hints at how such intellectual 
groups in the 1990s created circles that affected to a great extent the course of 
architecture theory. And despite the apparent ‘openness’ of the Anyone Corporation and 
its agenda and endeavors, these circles seemed often exclusive to who is involved in the 
theoretical ‘roundtable’637. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
632 Anyplace Conference Recordings. CCA Archives - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001. Video. 
633 The CCA archives of the Anyone Corporation include videos of the various sessions, made accessible to 
the public in 2014, in addition to many audio files and pictures of “Anyplace” and the other conferences. - 
Anyplace Files. - CCA Archives - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001. Documents. 
634 Letter: Cynthia Davidson to Ignasi de Solà-Morales, 30 July 1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - 
Box Correspondence 1994. 
635 Of which Solà-Morales is a member (since he is co-founder). 
636 Part of these minutes was a declaration to appoint Cynthia Davidson as the treasurer and vice-president of 
the Anyone Corporation in 1994. This shows the great influence Davidson had in the development and 
ongoing of the corporation. 
637 This is also apparent in Solà-Morales’s archive, in the correspondence regarding the collective’s choice of 
where the conferences took place and who was chosen to handle the various events.

## S. 291

288 
 
FIGURE 101: Anyplace Conference Stills. CCA Collection - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001, 1994. 
Video Recording. 
In Solà-Morales’s notes from the conference, the author wrote down some of the 
comments given to him after his lecture, either publicly or in private. Mario Goldman for 
instance, architect and professor from Argentina, spoke to him in person after the 
presentations. He expressed his content to the fact that Solà-Morales’s presentation, 
unlike others, examined the role of picture, and eventually film, to specifically address 
contemporary arts in the city and the urban condition. He liked that the arts were finally 
directly addressed in relation to the urban space. He also asked the theoretician for 
clarification regarding the role of motion picture in his definition of terrain vague, and 
finally inquired on whether the idea of “Anyplace” is necessarily urban areas, and what 
the role of other disciplines is, such as poetry, literature, sculpture, painting and other 
arts, in their link to place. One recurrent interest was indeed in questioning the 
interdisciplinary aspect of the theoretician’s approach to the terrain vague notion, and 
asking why photography is the main mentioned art form and not others. Goldman finally 
suggested not to discriminate the different forms of art in the perception and construction 
of the contemporary city638. This is perhaps the most direct and noteworthy firsthand 
reception of the “Terrain Vague” essay. Goldman’s observation puts in foreground some of 
the important predicaments of the Anyplace conference: the discriminating character of 
the conference focused primarily on certain disciplines and not others, and the 
controversy behind the term “Anyplace” and what it encompasses or not of the 
contemporary architectural and urban fabric. In this sense, Solà-Morales’s lecture was 
                                                                                                                                                                        
638 File “Anyplace” - Involvements with Anyone in 1994. Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 26. Documents.

## S. 292

289 
 
refreshing in requestioning the original premises of what was already considered as urban 
space or not. 
Similarly, other critics had also seen his presentation and expressed their opinions 
shortly after639. For instance, a letter in January 1994 from Michael Speaks,   member of the 
Anyone Corporation and senior editor of the Anyplace book and the ANY series editor, 
describes the latter's impressions of Solà-Morales’s lecture and work: 
"I have always thought that your work (especially the essays in the ANY books) was 
informed by an understanding of intellectual history that is often lacking in the American 
situation."640 
Speaks praised in general Solà-Morales’s analysis of intellectual history, and the 
European edge he brought to the American spheres641. He hinted moreover at some of the 
inspirational figures behind Solà-Morales’s theoretical work, mainly Gilles Deleuze, Félix 
Guattari, Manfredo Tafuri and Walter Benjamin. He described Solà-Morales’s approach as 
similar to Deleuzian plateaux or Benjaminian fragments; then in relation to Tafuri, he 
stated: 
"Since Tafuri is an important undercurrent in your essays, I wonder if you would be 
interested in writing a new essay on Tafuri and the problem of ideology and contemporary 
architecture. This would, I think, make an excellent concluding essay to your book."642 
Michael Speaks was indeed assigned to work with Solà-Morales on some of his 
publications of the period, including editing his ANY Magazine 25/26 edition "Essere 
Manfredo Tafuri" 643. In this sense, Solà-Morales brought to the American circles a 
European edge inspired by French and German philosophers, and by a historical 
grounding of his ideas but also by contemporary figures such as Tafuri644. The "Terrain 
Vague" was probably also influential for the same reasons: contemporary ideas grounded 
in history and artistic analysis. As Jordi Oliveras describes, in an interview with the 
author, Solà-Morales brought to the American discussion a “very graphical topic dedicated to 
civic space […] because the American people at the beginning of the twentieth century like that, 
like to see how was the spaces of imperial Rome and the Paris and the big spaces of Berlin. They 
                                                                                                                                                                        
639 Moreover, in Solà-Morales archives in Barcelona, the Anyplace minutes are preserved, including letters 
between Solà-Morales, Cynthia Davidson and Peter Eisenman. 
640 Letter: Michael Speaks to Solà-Morales, 1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 1994. 
641 Correspondence 1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 1994. Documents - Letters. 
642 Correspondence 1994. Ibid. 
643 Correspondence 1994. Ibid. - Solà-Morales was indeed heavily involved in 1994 in an issue of the ANY 
Magazine dedicated to Tafuri, more than he was involved in any of the other editions of the magazine. Solà-
Morales took this edition in charge due to his strong interest in Tafuri’s work. 
644 When interviewed, Peter Eisenman describes Tafuri as having “an enormous influence on the Spanish 
scene” especially from the 1970s to the 1990s. He however does not consider Tafuri to be the main influence 
on Solà-Morales’s work and his "Terrain Vague" essay; nor does he consider the terrain vague a notion 
adherent to Jacques Derrida’s ideas or Rafael Moneo’s or even Aldo Rossi’s, even though they were 
intermittently influential on Solà-Morales’s theories.

290 
knew the spaces of London and they knew the spaces of maybe Scandinavian countries, but they 
didn't know the spaces of Central Europe and Mediterranean towns.”645 
 
FIGURE 102: Anyplace Conference Participants. CCA Collection - Anyone Corporation Fonds, 1990-2001, 
1994. Photograph. - Front row left to right: Akira Asada, Ignasi de Solà-Morales, Johanne Lamoureux, Peter 
Eisenman, Alberto Pérez-Gómez, Cynthia Davidson, Jean-Louis Déotte, Melvin Charney, Daniel Libeskind,
