Moreover, Solà-Morales’s archives show letters exchanged between him and Luc 
Lévesque, Canadian architect and professor, who became also inspired by his 
presentation. Lévesque requested in these letters in 1994 the permission to publish the 
“Terrain Vague” essay within the journal Inter646. He had also spoken in person to Solà-
Morales after his lecture at the CCA and asked for visual material that could be published 
with the essay as well as bibliographical references that were not defined within the 
essay647. Lévesque asked as well for the permission to edit and translate the essay to 
French. The result was an essay entitled “Urbanité Interstitielle”, with an introduction by 
Lévesque: 
“The Terrain Vague appears as the counter-image of the city, as much in the sense of its 
critique as in the index of its possible overtaking. Why does urbanity seem to be embodied in 
a privileged manner in this type of landscape? Why are photographers often more drawn to 
these places rather than the apotheosis of objects or the geometric layout of major 
infrastructures constituting the fabric of cities? Why is there a landscape sensitivity for this 
                                                                                                                                                                        
645 Interview between author and Oliveras Jordi in Barcelona. 18 Dec. 2013. 
646 Folder “Terrain Vague” - 1995. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Brown File. Documents - Letters. 
647 Solà-Morales also gave Lévesque the slides that were supposed to be used for the Anyplace publication and 
presentation, yet were not. One of the slides made its way finally to the Inter publication.

## S. 294

291 
 
artificial naturalization with imprecise boundaries, populated with surprises but devoid of 
shapes representing power?”648 
This introduction evokes already a more practice-oriented focus. In fact, the 
interests of Lévesque turned since the 1990s towards interstitial spaces where he applied 
some of Solà-Morales’s thoughts into practice in architectural and urban projects, often 
temporary and ephemeral, around Canada649. 
The reception and later influence of Solà-Morales’s presentation on the locals from 
Montreal is moreover not limited to Lévesque. In fact, some of Montreal’s projects, even 
until now, were situated in or focused on the terrains vagues of the city and were 
developed very much in the spirit of Solà-Morales’s essay. One such project is Village 
Éphémère on the waterfront, or Flora Urbana or Sentier Urbain. A Research and Creation 
Symposium was even organized in May 2016, by ACFAS and by Élise Lepage from the 
University of Waterloo and Isabelle Miron from the Université du Québec à Montréal 
(UQAM), on the topic of the imaginary of the terrain vague. The symposium650 was 
interdisciplinary and included lectures on the subject from various domains including 
architecture, literature, psychology and even poetry. It also included artistic workshops 
and interventions in few terrains vagues around the city. The symposium also began with 
Solà-Morales’s essay and took a similar approach of questioning what the terrain vague 
could entail, rather than finding solutions or answers to the questions651. The influence of 
the terrain vague notion on the city’s urban theories is thus still in progress. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
648 Our trans. from French: “Le terrain vague apparaît comme la contre-image de la cité, autant dans le sens 
de sa critique que dans celui de l'indice de son possible dépassement. Pourquoi l'urbanité paraît-elle 
s'incarner de façon privilégiée dans ce type de paysage? Pourquoi les photographes sont souvent plus attirés 
par ces lieux que par l'apothéose des objets ou par le tracé géométrique des grandes infrastructures 
constituant le tissu des métropoles? Pourquoi y a-t-il une sensibilité paysagiste pour cette naturalité 
artificielle aux limites imprécises, peuplée de surprises mais dépourvue de formes représentant le pouvoir?” 
– Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Urbanité Interstitielle.” Trans. Luc Lévesque. Inter: art actuel 61 (1995): 27–28. 
649 More of Lévesque and other reactions to the terrain vague notion will be detailed in the fifth chapter. 
650 The author of this dissertation participated and contributed to this symposium. 
651 For more information: https://uwaterloo.ca/pays-paysage/realisations.

## S. 295

292 
 
FIGURE 103: Village Ephémère. Montréal, 2016. Photograph. 
 
FIGURE 104: “Imaginaire Du Terrain Vague” Conference at the Université Du Québec À Montréal (UQAM). 
Montréal, 2016. Photograph.

## S. 296

293 
 
Montreal was effectively a germane environment in which to present Solà-
Morales’s terrain vague due to its historical baggage as a post-industrial city with singular 
terrains vagues as witnesses of the city’s development. Perhaps Solà-Morales had the 
bilingual city in mind when he wrote his bilingual essay. Around the same period, other 
events were also taking place in Montreal linked to the notion of terrain vague: a 
collective for instance entitled Argué took place between 1993 and 2000, interested in 
such urban notions as desolate spaces. Other ANY events happened as well in October 
1993, entitled Electrotecture and Warchitecture, incessantly expanding the limits of the 
architectural field and its theoretical domain throughout the 1990s. The terrain vague 
notion belongs to these attempts at expounding this limit of what is the urban scale and 
what is architecture. 
Nevertheless, not all reactions were purely positive. On a more critical note, as a 
response to Anyplace, curator and writer Hans van Dijk wrote an article entitled 
“Montreal Anyplace” putting in question the interdisciplinary intent of the conference, 
arguing, similar to Goldman and Speaks, that a strict division of roles had been imposed at 
the conference between architects and non-architects and how this had not helped the 
cause for interdisciplinary exchange. Nonetheless, aside from being very critical of all the 
sessions of the Anyplace conference, including the one on arts and architecture in which 
Solà-Morales was involved, the critic was positively stricken by Solà-Morales’s input and 
lecture. After condemning the pessimism emergent from the “American East-Coast” 
debate, he contrasted it with the lectures of Sanford Kwinter and Solà-Morales stating 
that these brought a refreshing perspective towards empowering architecture, rather than 
merely succumbing under the weight of economic and political constraints652. Solà-
Morales’s approach to architecture practice in the essay and his call for continuity were 
thus once again inspirational. It is also interesting to mention a letter van Dijk wrote in 
1997 to Solà-Morales asking him for an interview for the Archis magazine653 on the subject 
of “Architectural Criticism in the Mid-Nineties”, showing once more his admiration of 
Solà-Morales’s thought. Solà-Morales thus gained acclaim due to his presentation, and the 
terrain vague notion was positively received. 
In addition, another essay on August 1994 is preserved in the CCA vertical 
archives, entitled “Review – The Academy Speaks (to the Academy)” in the Canadian 
Architect magazine, in which architecture critic Beth Kapusta gives an account of the 
Anyplace conference: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
652 Dijk finally describes Solà-Morales’s essay as exposing the “post-Marxist impasse in which the American 
debate is in danger of falling into”. – Van Dijk, Hans. Montreal Anyplace. CCA Archives - Vertical Files: 
Anyplace CCA. Article. 
653 Letter: Hans van Dijk to Solà-Morales, 1997. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 32. 
Van Dijk was also involved in the Anyhow conference, taking place in Rotterdam in 1997, and even gave a 
lecture himself. He also wrote other articles on the ANY conferences.

## S. 297

294 
“This self-described “institution without walls” has an interdisciplinary conference (and 
subsequent publication) each year in order to ponder architecture in relation to 
contemporary culture. As with previous Anys, the speakers at the conference held in June at 
the Canadian Center for Architecture in Montreal were mostly Others: academics, 
philosophers, artists, scientists etc.” 654 
The most intriguing point of the conference is once again the relation between 
architects and non-architects at the conference. The discrepancy between the approaches 
of the various fields feeding into “the big A”655 is criticized, including Solà-Morales’s 
section of arts within architecture. Yet one exception is singled out: 
“the parallel paths of means of production of architecture and science, following the 
evolution of the studio out of the solitary-artist-as alchemist model, through the blurring of 
authorship. […] I found myself wondering if there was some coincidence in the fact that the 
ANY conference took place on June 16, the selfsame day spanned by James Joyce's Ulysses.”656 
The dispersion of authorship and the changing role of the architect, as also 
described in Solà-Morales’s last intent in the “Terrain Vague” essay, was thus positively 
received, even by Kapusta who found in this matter a constructive interdisciplinary 
connecting point657. 
Architecture theoretician Detlef Mertins from the University of Toronto was also 
among those who expressed their fascination with Solà-Morales’s theories. He had come 
in contact with him after his presentation658 at the CCA and expressed his interest in 
working with him on a book related to Mies van der Rohe, later titled The Presence of 
Mies, due to Solà-Morales’s interest in van der Rohe, displayed also by the latter being a 
core example within the "Terrain Vague" essay. Similarly, Luc Lévesque, apart from 
contacting Solà-Morales to publish his essay within Inter, had requested from the author a 
detailed explanation on his interest in van der Rohe, accentuating the fact that the latter 
is the only tangible architectural example within the essay659. As expected, Solà-Morales’s 
lecture echoed in various directions in the audience, especially since the essay itself is 
multidirectional and multidisciplinary. For instance, in a letter to Solà-Morales, Mertins660 
expresses his gratitude for his lecture at the CCA and attaches to his letter a text about 
"Benjamin, Scheerbart, Dada and play" that he had mentioned to Solà-Morales after his 
                                                                                                                                                                        
654 Kapusta, Beth. “Review – The Academy Speaks (to the Academy)” in Canadian Architect - August 1994, 
pp.8-11. CCA Archives - Vertical Files: Anyplace CCA. Article. 
655 Kapusta, Beth. Ibid. 
656 Kapusta, Beth. Ibid. 
657 Moreover, the reference to James Joyce’s Ulysses seems also in this direction: a reference also in Solà-
Morales’s presentation at the Triennale di Milano, Ulysses is the vagrant character who is lost in the terrain 
vague, out in search for new horizons or a new home; architecture in this context seems to follow a similar 
path through the Anyplace’s interdisciplinary dream and through Solà-Morales’s interdisciplinary terrain 
vague. 
658 Correspondence 1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive. 
659 Correspondence between Luc Lévesque and Solà-Morales, 1994-1995. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private 
Archive - Free Files. Letters. 
660 Letter: Detlef Mertins to Solà-Morales, 1994. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 1994.

## S. 298

295 
 
lecture. Moreover, the interest in Mies van der Rohe was not a surprise considering his 
presence in Montreal and his relevance to the Canadian architecture, to the CCA founder 
Phyllis Lambert and to the CCA circles, the 1994 context in which the “Terrain Vague” 
essay was presented to the world. 
In conclusion, in the context of the CCA, Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" belonged 
to a conference and an interdisciplinary audience gathered in the aim of pushing the 
fields of architecture and urbanism beyond predefined conceptions of what these 
disciplines are. A general concern with new urban transformations and with globalization 
was behind Anyplace and behind the Anyone Corporation in general. The terrain vague 
notion was thus one of the innovative contributions towards delineating new definitions 
for emerging configurations of urban-landscape-architecture. 
1996-1998: Dissemination of the essay, re-presentations and 
republications 
The Catalan context: the XIX UIA Congress and Quaderns significance 
Between 1995 and 1996, two events relevant to Solà-Morales’s development of the 
terrain vague notion occurred in Barcelona. The professor organized, promoted and 
directed the nineteenth Congress of the UIA International Union of Architects; one of its 
main themes was the terrain vague. In parallel, he published the “Terrain Vague” essay in 
the journal Quaderns 212. Side projects, exhibitions and publications in Barcelona were 
also of interest during this period, both inspiring on and inspired by Solà-Morales’s terrain 
vague. In this period, Solà-Morales was absorbed in promoting his already well-received 
terrain vague notion. He aimed at developing it beyond a mere essay, transforming it into 
a core discourse and one of the five pillars of his urban contemporary vision. He shared 
this vision with the world through two main gateways during this phase: the XIX UIA 
Congress and his Quaderns publications. 
The   terrain   vague   within   the   UIA   Congress   
The UIA Congress entitled “Present and Futures: Architecture in Cities” occurred 
at various centers in Barcelona in July 1996. The International Union of Architects was 
first founded in Lausanne, Switzerland in 1948; its General Secretariat is located in Paris. 
Every three years a member section and its respective country hold the World Congress 
of Architecture. It is therefore an internationally recognized event representing over a 
million architects in 124 countries. From the 3rd to the 6th of June 1996, the XIX UIA

## S. 299

296 
Congress held in Barcelona was enacted as if it were the ‘architectural Olympics’661. The 
event reflected also a then-rising phenomenon of the “Star Architect” 662 , a growing 
fascination with image and an architecture beguiled with its own objects. The attendees 
were far larger in number than expected, probably due to the Congress’s program: 28 
exhibitions, 35 venues, 300 papers, thirty world famous architects, and finally a 335-page 
guide in five languages; An entire section was dedicated to the terrain vague notion. The 
10300 registrants and the congress’s media coverage were enough to ensure broad display 
and reception of the terrain vague notion beyond the professor’s personal efforts to 
promote it. 
Solà-Morales was the general speaker and the president of the scientific committee 
of the congress663. He proposed to debate the transformations of cities at the turn of the 
century. In order to divide the complex problems and issues, he suggested five domains of 
reflection: Fluxus, Habitat, Mutations, Containers and Terrain Vague. The overall interest 
of the theoretician seemed oriented towards raising awareness of the architect’s social 
responsibility and the need to reconcile the "schizophrenia produced between the 
contemporary urban reality and the architectonic culture."664 This seemed to go hand in 
hand with the period’s concerns with globalization, postmodern tendencies, aesthetic 
obsessions, as well as deconstructivist behaviors that seemed to have shattered in this 
context architectural theory and created a fissure between practitioners and theoreticians. 
In fact, in an interview with the author, Anthony Vidler stated: 
“they (deconstructivist philosophers) are all against architecture but they don’t propose 
another architecture. Because they are anti-disciplinary, so there’s nothing there […] Either 
in terms of feminism, in terms of post-colonialism, in terms of anti-capitalism, and others… a 
whole range of theories that come out about architecture but not architectural theory. So that 
lead to some kind of gap […] then architecture took over as practice without theory…”665 
He then described Solà-Morales as a historian and architecture theoretician whose 
work is mainly attempts towards mending disciplinary fractures after various 
deconstructivist endeavors had tended to disassemble traditional ideologists and 
disciplines. Solà-Morales’s organizing of the congress and his formulation of the terrain 
vague go thus in this direction of reconditioning theory as well as multidisciplinarity in a 
productive form for the architecture profession. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
661 Wheeler, Tone. “Barcelona: UIA Conference.” Architecture Australia AA 85.5 (1996): 50. 
662 Anecdote: During the congress, Peter Eisenman and Norman Foster were escorted by guards through 
groupies. 
663 While Josep Martorell was president of the congress and Antoni Ferrer the administrator, Solà-Morales 
seemed more in control of the theoretical decisions, such as which topics were chosen, which discourses and 
what notions were to be debated. 
664 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de, Xavier Costa, and Susana Landrove, eds. “Present and Futures: Architecture in 
Cities.” XIX Congress of the International Union of Architects UIA. Barcelona: Actar, 1996. 10–23. 
665 Interview between author and Anthony Vidler in Zurich. 10 Mar. 2015.

## S. 300

297 
 
A round table “Motiu UIA” took place prior to the congress at the Colegio Oficial 
de Arquitectos de Madrid on the 20th of May 1996, where Solà-Morales officially 
presented the congress, and a session for debate and questions followed. Notes in Solà-
Morales’s archives document the event; an essay666 entitled "Sobre las Ciudades y los 
Arquitectos Actuales" published in Urbanismo included Solà-Morales’s input; Abel 
Enguita describes the debate: 
"No need to go into the description of those gaps and terrain-vague at regional level, 
where major infrastructures are communicating things that previously only existed and 
where traditional cities play a game of nodules that will continue to be, within a geographic 
and economic process that, for the most part, out of control. Faced with this phenomenon 
there are three possible answers: first is to say: this is out of our control and we will not 
intervene; The second: there is still room to do something, but we leave it to others 
(engineers, economists...); and third: we claim the competence in this construction of the 
architecture of the territory. In this last assumption, we should ask to what extent, as a 
professional group, we have a voice there or, on the contrary, whether we are going to 
renounce directly, inhibited."667 
And, evidently, Solà-Morales’s subliminal call for action in the "Terrain Vague" 
essay finds reverberations in Enguita’s and other published essays related to the UIA 1996 
Congress. Enguita later continues: 
" In the face of these new circumstances, architects must decide what role they are to 
play in the process as well as the breadth of this role. When the pace of growth was slower, 
architects were in control as the heads of construction in the building of large cities. Yet 
currently, architects are faced with an either/or dilemma: either limit themselves to specific 
building activities, letting themselves be pulled along in the wake of decisions made by other 
professionals such as engineers or politicians. or broaden their jurisdiction, defending their 
capability of tackling the issues comprehensively. The very concept of the discipline must be 
reexamined since introduction of a new facet, mutation, clashing with the traditional notion 
of architecture. Moreover, the importance of urban areas which until recently were 
unknown, such as transportation or information interchangers, is growing.”668 
The terrain vague notion was thus discussed in relation to the architects' role in 
the fast transformations of the city and the "accelerated urbanization"669 of many of the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
666 The event and publication were double-titled: “Los Temas de los Proximos congresos de architectos de 
Espana y de la UIA” and “Las Ciudades y los Arquitectos Actuales”. - Enguita, Abel. “Sobre las Ciudades y los 
Arquitectos Actuales.” Urbanismo 28: revista del Colegio Oficial de Arquitectos de Madrid (1996): 35–42. 
667 Our trans. from Spanish: “No hace falta entrar en la descripción de esos vacíos y terrain-vague a nivel 
regional, donde las grandes infraestructuras estan comunicando cosas que antes solo estaban y donde las 
ciudades tradicionales juegan un papel de nódulos que van a seguir teniendo, dentro de un proceso geografico 
y económico que, en su mayor parte, escapa de control. Ante este fenómeno hay tres posibles contestaciones: 
Ia primera es decir: esto esta fuera de nuestro control y no vamos a intervenir; Ia segunda: todavia queda 
margen para hacer algo, pero se lo dejamos a otros (ingenieros, economistas...); y Ia tercera: reclamamos una 
competencia en esa construcción de Ia arquitectura del territorio. En este ultimo supuesto, nos deberiamos 
plantear hasta que punto como colectivo profesional tenemos una voz ahi o, por el contrario, vamos a 
renunciar directamente, inhibiendonos.” – In: Enguita, Abel. “Sobre las Ciudades y los Arquitectos 
Actuales.” Urbanismo 28: revista del Colegio Oficial de Arquitectos de Madrid (1996): 35–42. 
668 Ibid. 
669 Ibid.

## S. 301

298 
western landscapes but also of the developing countries. The notion was hence established 
as one of the main topics of interest in 1996 to the contemporary urban discourse. And 
perhaps the growing interest in interdisciplinarity during this era’s architectural events 
was triggered by this change in magnitude and scale of the urban changes, causing 
architects to lose control and requestion their disciplinary thresholds and the way they 
define urban and architectural contexts. 
Berlin was undergoing changes, Paris had changed, and Shanghai was enlarging its 
interventions scales, as well as Hong Kong, Brisbane and Kuala Lumpur; all these cities 
were referenced by presenters at the UIA Congress as case studies for its main topics. In 
the dialogues preceding the congress, Solà-Morales presented two case studies illustrating 
his interest: the Bercy zone by the Seine river in Paris, and an extension of the Shanghai 
city for three million inhabitants. The first had been an obsolete industrial and transport 
zone that was transformed with minimal planning into several projects including the 
"Bibliothèque de France". The second case, in Shanghai, is a project that underwent an 
accelerated pace within eight years in which the architectural scale was largely set aside. 
The terrain vague notion was thus linked through the UIA Congress with issues of postindustrial cities, interstitial spaces and the fast changing pace and scale of urban concerns. 
The terrain vague is furthermore linked to the other chosen topics. The notion of 
Fluxus is inclusive in the last section of the "Terrain Vague" essay in which Solà-Morales 
discussed the continuum that should be inherent to any intervention in the terrain vague, 
a continuum in energy, intensity and time. Mutations and Containers are equally 
linked as they represent the catalyst and necessary form and energy producing the terrain 
vague; these can be hence considered preludes to the terrain vague, whether symptoms or 
even causes. Through the multiple mutations affecting the cities and the various 
containments, a negative of the city’s image starts to emerge. 
On another note, Solà-Morales was mainly behind the terrain vague theme and 
made sure he took charge himself of this section670. He wrote an introductory essay for the 
opening ceremony of the congress and for publication in its proceedings catalogue in 
which the notion of terrain vague was in front stage. The essay was entitled, as was the 
UIA Congress, "Present and Futures. Architecture in Cities." Solà-Morales introduced in 
the essay once again the five themes of the congress and elaborated on its importance for 
the architecture profession and theory. In this essay, the dilemma of scales between 
architectural and urban is clearly stated: 
"That architecture is one and the same substance as the city is beyond question. That the 
city is one single piece of architecture is a much more problematic assertion [...] In the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
670 When interviewed, Jordi Oliveras describes how Solà-Morales was very excited to take the reins of this 
section of the congress and proceeding publication and allocated others for the other sections. - Interview 
between author and Oliveras Jordi in Barcelona. 18 Dec. 2013.

## S. 302

299 
 
contemporary situation architecture is still the city. It forms a part of it, it materializes part of 
the setting in which urban life unfolds. However, today, more than ever, we find that the 
city is many more things than its buildings and its architectures. Transport networks, 
highways, spaces reserved for the logistics of distribution, protected natural areas, virtual 
spaces for communication and entertainment these make up the basic constituent parts of 
urban life and especially the life of the metropolis. Nowadays they not only tend to fall 
completely outside the professional work of the architect, but the traditional instruments of 
the architect, the traditional instruments of analysis and architectural practice scarcely have 
the capacity to engage with them and respond to such situations."671 
 Indeed, the terrain vague emerged in mid-1990s among a debate in which the role 
and scope of the architectural profession were already in question. The terrain vague 
comes in this context as one of the main spaces existing in the city despite, or perhaps as a 
consequence of, the control and planning forces inherent to the city. It is a recurrent 
mutation, or outcome of a mutation, that requires special attention in order to conserve 
value and benefit from this urban phenomenon of the contemporary city. Solà-Morales 
continues his essay on the same lines: 
"Translated as Waste Land in English, Terreno Baldío in Spanish, the translation fails to 
capture full richness of the original French. [...] The approach to these situations 
conventionally adopted by architecture and urban design is perfectly clear. The aim is 
always, by means of projects and investment, to re-integrate these spaces or buildings into 
the productive mesh of urban spaces of the efficient, syncopated, busy, effective city. But 
people of sensibility are reacting against these operations of renovation."672 
Hence the role of architectural interventions in the terrain vague is once again 
debated; the artistic sensibility is put in contrast to the "traditional" ordering and 
controlling of architectural input. In contrast, the author argues that in any intervention, 
it is precisely the absence and the indefinability inherent to these spaces that ought to be 
preserved: 
“it is this vacancy and absence that must be preserved at all costs, and which must 
register the difference between the federal bulldozer and a sensitive approach to these places 
of memory and ambiguity.”673 
The discourse of the terrain vague takes on a more practice-oriented tone in 1996 
within the UIA Congress and for the author. The focus is on the architect's role within 
the contemporary urban condition, the planning of cities and their architectural and nonarchitectural fragments, i.e. within the terrain vague. It had subsequently repercussions 
on how the essay was well received among practitioners and on generating inspiration 
among architects creating since these years a multitude of intervention methods and 
styles for spaces such as terrains vagues. The final point however in Solà-Morales’s 
                                                                                                                                                                        
671 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Present and Futures: Architecture in Cities.” XIX Congress of the International 
Union of Architects UIA. Ed. Xavier Costa and Susana Landrove. Barcelona: Actar, 1996. 10–23. 
672 Ibid. 
673 Ibid.

## S. 303

300 
introductory essay is set on confronting the notion of mutation to that of the terrain 
vague: 
"If the cultural categories I have proposed as a basis for understanding the new 
relationships between architecture and the great metropolis of today started with the notion 
of mutation as the most adequate approach to the phenomena of sudden transformation, the 
last of the four, terrain vague, is virtually its counterpoint, the obverse of the same 
metropolitan coin. Only by according equal attention to the values of memory and absence 
and the values of innovation will we be able to maintain confidence in a complex and plural 
urban life. The role of art, Deleuze has written, and thus the role of the art of architecture ‘is 
not to produce objects for themselves, self-referential objects, but to make itself a revelatory 
force of multiplicity and contingency’."674 
Solà-Morales thus presents the terrain vague as the counterbalance to mutations in 
the city. On the one hand, he presents mutations as a means to comprehend the fast and 
multiple urban changes. On the other hand, he presents the terrain vague as a form of 
coping with these mutations through creatively equalizing attributes of memory and 
absence. 
In brief, the terrain vague notion within the UIA Congress illustrates how the 
notion was beginning in the late 1990s to take a deeper form and a wider reception. It also 
reveals some of the directions the notion was to take in the architectural and urban fields. 
It was mainly linked to an urban identity in crisis, due to the contemporary diversity and 
plurality created by urban mutations. 
The   "Terrain   Vague"   essay   and   notion   within   Quaderns   
Barcelona’s transformations after the 1992 Olympics, culminating in some major 
event such as the UIA Congress, altered greatly the interests of the Catalan architects and 
urbanists. The Quaderns journal is one written testimony of these changes, witnessing the 
theoretical peak it reached within the 1990s. Not only was the “Terrain Vague” essay 
published in Quaderns, but the other articles surrounding this period display similarities 
with the essay and its themes, basing on it, deriving from it new directions, or simply 
being inspired by the notion and its position within the UIA Congress. 
Essays published in the Quaderns journal also divulge the array of prominent 
figures and the architectural and intellectual subjects relevant to the Catalan spheres in 
the 1990s and still nowadays; the journal has a strong theoretical standing and sway on 
the architectural, artistic and urban fields of Catalonia and Spain in general. More 
relevant to the terrain vague notion, many essays published from the 1990s to the 
beginning of the millennium, and even some contemporary ones, hold subjects related to 
                                                                                                                                                                        
674 Ibid.

## S. 304

301 
 
indefinite spaces. Looking at some essays’ titles675, one can imagine few main directions of 
interest, such as technology and architecture, architectural urbanism and finally the new 
areas of the city, i.e. the suburbs and peripheral areas, a subject intimately linked to Solà-
Morales’s understanding of the terrain vague. 
In 1996, Solà-Morales published “Terrain Vague” in Quaderns 212 edition entitled 
Tierra-Aigua/Water-Land. As described in the second chapter, this edition of the essay is 
a reedited version from the previous two676. The publication of the text followed the XIX 
UIA Congress. The ties between the journal and the author were many and therefore it 
was no surprise that "Terrain Vague" essay appears there a couple of years after its first 
edition. What is more, ties seem to link the essay with others in the same Quaderns 
edition677. The subjects are synchronized with the terrain vague notion: Maurici Pla wrote 
“Dérives, Containers, Storms, Architectures without Anchor”, while Thomas Lussi wrote 
“In No Man’s Land” and finally Toni Gironès “Passanelles Ricochets”678. When looking 
closely at this Quaderns “Editorial”, the focus is on a post-congress impression with an 
evident focus on terrains vagues and on void, not only of this number but also of the 
journal over the entire period of the 1990s: 
"In recent years, one of the main concerns of Quaderns has been to explore the semantic 
limits, but also structural, strategic and especially geographic that can highlight the new 
conceptual situations in which the classic triad "city-territory-landscape" fits, a triad now 
doomed to undergo complex movements of redefinition. In such a context, main subject of 
discussion at the XIX Congress of the International Union of Architects UIA Barcelona 96, 
the concept of void takes a significant importance, not only as absence, but above all as 
indeterminacy governing these terrains vagues, as denominated by Ignasi de Solà-Morales, 
where the absence of practices or activity is combined with the expectation of the possible, of 
the latent. Void as absence, thus, but also as promise. This ambiguity, this contrast, this 
vagueness that characterizes in general the new contemporary landscape becomes even more 
remarkable in border situations - water / land, such a strange limit, real this time."679 
                                                                                                                                                                        
675 Some examples: “’Stenosis’ where streets have no name”, “Synergism”, “Dilations”, “Suburb Corridor 
Periphery”. 
676 Description of differences between “Terrain Vague” essay versions are mentioned in the second chapter. 
677 Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua/Water-Land (1996). 
678 Ibid. 
679 Our trans. from French: “Au cours des dernières années, l'un des principaux soucis de Quaderns a été 
d'explorer les limites sémantiques, mais également structurales, stratégiques et, surtout, géographiques qui 
puissent mettre en évidence les nouvelles situations conceptuelles dans lesquelles s'inscrit le trinôme 
classique “ville-territoire-paysage”, trinôme voué aujourd'hui à subir de complexes mouvements de 
redéfinition. Dans un tel contexte, principal objet de discussion lors du XIX Congrès de l'Union 
Internationale des Architectes UIA Barcelona 96, la notion de vide prend une importance significative, non 
seulement comme absence, mais surtout en tant qu'indétermination régissant ces terrains vagues, selon la 
dénomination d'Ignasi de Solà-Morales, où l'absence d'usages ou d'activité s'allie à l’expectative du possible, 
du latent. Vide en tant qu'absence, donc, mais aussi comme promesse. Cette ambiguïté, ce contraste, ce flou 
qui caractérise en général le nouveau paysage contemporain devient plus remarquable encore dans des 
situations de bordure - eau/terre, telle une étrange limite, réelle cette fois.’’ – In : Gausa, Manuel. “Editorial.” 
Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 212 Tierra-Aigua (1996): 1–2.

## S. 305

302 
The focus is hence on redefining the correlation between urban, territory and 
landscape. This was attained through questioning the semantic limits of the fields. 
Already in this editorial, Solà-Morales is quoted as coining the term terrain vague. 
Notions of vagueness, absence and potentiality are then based on his vision of what these 
spaces entail. In this sense, Solà-Morales’s essay is also considered a canon on the topic. 
Solà-Morales’s terrain vague in this edition of Quaderns appears to also play as 
primary source of inspiration and metaphors: water depths as artificial wastelands, 
abandoned areas, harbor zones; Solà-Morales’s initial enumeration in his essay, of what 
the terrain vague could entail, is exploded here in a multitude of directions closely or 
distantly relevant to the initial premise. The re-publication of the "Terrain Vague" essay 
in this edition is justified as a representative of its importance within a post-Olympics, 
post-UIA congress, Catalan sphere hungry for inspiration, for fresh modes of intervention, 
not succumbing yet to the frown status it underwent at the beginning of the new 
Millennium. On the same lines, Pla's essay for instance exposes the new possible 
propositions when one assumes architecture projects to be primarily concerned with the 
terrain and its values, its dérives and its ambiguous limits. 
On another note, the "Terrain Vague" essay was not the only one Solà-Morales 
published within Quaderns during the mid-1990s. The author wrote another essay 
considered also pertinent: “Making Architecture Today” in Quaderns 1995. In this essay, 
Solà-Morales sends a cry for help; one that seems to precede the one in “Terrain Vague" 
which then appears as the savior. The author presents the contemporary condition of the 
architectural practice as a domain ceased in its own repetitive and safe routine, mirroring 
"globalized civilizations of worldwide communications" 680 equally succumbing to a 
hegemonic uniformity of conduct and tastes. The essay revolves around pointing out the 
crisis: 
"The current situation is one of cultural plurality in which the debates between groups 
and trends have largely lost the ability to clarify issues. Of the latest " isms " it is impossible to 
decide which to keep, due to their rapid obsolescence."681 
A suggestive note, alluding to the terrain vague notion, pivots then the essay 
towards the solution: 
"The architectures which should be of interest to us, which should be published in 
specialized journals, which we hope will be studied in architecture schools or which will 
demand of us that we take the time to visit them in situ are these. They are those which, in 
the development of programs, in the peculiarity of each urban situation, in the proposal of 
different images for working, living, representing power or communication, are capable of 
saying something full, global and yet at the same time highly individual."682 
                                                                                                                                                                        
680 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Fer Arquitectura Avui = Making Architecture Today.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i 
urbanisme 207-209 Tríptic d’arquitectura Catalana = Triptych of Catalan architecture (1995): 180–181. 
681 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. Ibid. 
682 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. Ibid.

## S. 306

303 
 
Terrain vague is one such instance in the city where local and global issues are at 
play. Once again, individuality is valued on the same level as globalization. Case specific 
architecture is advocated, one that is sensitive to nuances and new urban conditions, one 
of which is the terrain vague; an architecture disinterested in its formal presence, 
considerate of continuity and fluxus. 
Hence, Solà-Morales’s publications and involvement at the Quaderns reveal on the 
one hand his urban vision focused on the terrain vague; and consequently on the local 
and global scales, on continuity, on urban experience, fluxus, Deleuzian and Lefebvre’s 
theories. On the other hand, they show a transforming architectural and urban discourse 
concerned with globalization and with finding new definitions for emerging relations 
landscape-architecture. The terrain vague fits within these new definitions and the notion 
consequently gained momentum in Barcelona through the theoretical developments 
within Quaderns and other publications. 
The   terrain   vague   notion   and   Barcelona   
Outside Quaderns and the UIA Congress, a web of conferences, exhibitions and 
publications asserted the author and his work’s relevance within the same period 1995-
1996. Some of these are more than others linked with the terrain vague notion and 
contributed in advancing the notion beyond the essay. 
Firstly, Solà-Morales published many works related to both Barcelona and its 
terrains vagues and through which both become intertwined. The topic of Barcelona’s 
peripheries is often apparent in the professor’s work; he was implicated in writing on the 
new developments in these areas. Essays such as “Barcelona, Construir sobre lo ya 
Construido”, “Se Rompe la Baraja” in 1995, “Barcelona 1856-1999” in 1996 at the CCCB, 
one on Sabadell, another on Sant Andreu, and even an article he published in Japan 
entitled “Barcelona 92”, presented the author’s theorizing of the city’s transformations. In 
these articles, interest was mainly on urban approaches related to continuity in energy 
and flows, much similar to the final note in the “Terrain Vague” essay. Solà-Morales 
confronts Barcelona’s transformations of the last century with the changing readings of 
urban configurations and the consequent changing role of architects within these 
transformations. Secondly, the author was involved in various conferences during the 
late-1990s following similar perspectives of the city and its peripheries. He was also 
member of the jury of ETSA Vallès in 1995 along the same lines. Thirdly, the author 
published “A Vast Territory” in Anywise in 1996 similarly linked to the terrain vague 
discourse. In the essay, as in the "Terrain Vague", an array of references are presented to 
illustrate the author’s arguments. Most attention is set mainly on Solà-Morales’s

## S. 307

304 
involvement with the planning project of Marseille. Some elements of the proposed 
interventions echo the author's preaching for the new architect's position advocated in 
"Terrain Vague". He writes: 
"This calls for architectural interventions that will respect the existing elements, 
working in the direction of urban regrading. [...] The terrains on which we have worked are 
fragments of a vaster territory which we have taken into consideration. Our project is above 
all a global reflection on the territory in its entirety and on the relationship these 
interventions might establish with this. On the basis of this general engagement with images, 
numerous other considerations could be brought into play."683 
Suggestions for architectural intervention include thus new modes of circulation, 
interventions in the industrial district or the port, creation of voids overlooked by built 
space, and more importantly attention to continuity. 
Hence, these events and publications, far from being an exhaustive list, are 
nevertheless quite representative of the transformations Barcelona witnessed both 
physically and within its architectural and urban theoretical discourses, and of how these 
transformations are linked to the "Terrain Vague" essay and notion, both affected and 
affecting. 
First responses after 1996 
The impact of Solà-Morales’s terrain vague in Barcelona took on mainly three 
tangible facets traceable in the archives. As previously noted, the first was the progress 
that the Metropolis program witnessed, and the second was given by the positive and 
negative responses to the UIA Congress. Whereas the third was the change in themes of 
interest within the Catalan architectural intellectual spheres, which were reflected in the 
Quaderns journal. 
On a first note, two special editions of Quaderns entitled Forum Internacional I 
and II in 1996 were solely dedicated to the XIX UIA Congress. The array of essays within 
these two publications is very representative of correlations between the terrain vague 
notion and other notions circulating within the same period, in Barcelona but also in the 
world. Many of these articles seem to be a reception of the Congress and a reverberation. 
Their authors fall into two categories: locals to Catalonia and Spain and their relative 
topics of interest, and, equally important, essays by foreign authors whose themes are also 
compatible. Aside from presenting the UIA Congress and its discourse on the present and 
"futures" of cities, authors and presenters within both the congress and Quaderns 
defended points of view relevant to Spain, but also internationally. Some fell close to the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
683 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “A Vast Territory. Urban Superimposition: The City of Marseille.” Anywise. Ed. 
Cynthia Davidson. New York: Anyone Corporation, 1996. 144–153.

## S. 308

305 
 
terrain vague theme. For instance, Enrique Norton published "Global, Individual", 
referring to the individual condition within the global network of the city. And Toyo Ito 
wrote "The Urban Nomad", referring to the wanderer who is the dweller of the terrain 
vague. While Adriaan Geuze published "The Nomad Urbanity and the Space of Mobility", 
a theme linking the urban nomad, i.e. the terrain vague dweller, to ideas of mobility and 
fluxus. Manuel de Solà-Morales, similar to Ignasi, wrote about "The New Public 
Spaces", terrain vague being implicitly an adjacent notion. While Joan Busquets wrote 
directly on the theme: "Terrain Vague and the Potential of Restructuration". Two other 
articles fell also in the same discourse: "No Man's Lands. Inapprehensible Spaces" by 
Shane Murray and "Terrain Vague" by José Morales. Interestingly, Pietro Derossi from the 
Triennale di Milano also wrote an essay published in the same Quaderns edition entitled 
"The ‘Non-lieux’ of Consumption and Satisfaction", referring both to Marc Augé's "Nonlieux" and to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague. 
These essays could be considered some of the earliest echoes of Solà-Morales’s 
essay and his discourse on the terrain vague, proving its relevance and prominence at the 
time. One can notice recurring notions in these published essays, including Solà-Morales’s 
"Terrain Vague". One for instance is the idea of the urban nomad and the spaces where 
these undetermined urban characters wander, i.e. the no man’s land, the terrain vague 
and others. Another frequent idea was that of mobility and fluxus in relation to the 
terrain vague and other blurred urban spaces. The "Terrain Vague" essay was hence linked 
on multiple levels to the other Quaderns essays of the period. 
Some of these essays are worthy of more elaboration: mainly Juan Mur Soteras’ 
introductory essay in Quaderns Forum Internacional 1684, Manuel de Solà-Morales’s essay 
in Forum Internacional 2 and José Morales’s "Terrain Vague". The first two explain the 
UIA Congress in a fashion similar to Solà-Morales’s. Soteras, then-Dean of the Cataluña 
Order of Architects (COA), alludes to Solà-Morales’s direct involvement with the choice 
of the terrain vague as one of the five divisions before starting to explain each of these 
with the similar emphasis on architectural practice. Similarly, Manuel de Solà-Morales 
ends his essay with a reflection on interstitial space and wastelands: 
"The task of the planners is at present to give the city a new projection: making, of these 
intermediate places, non-sterile spaces that, far from being totally abandoned to advertising 
and profit, would also represent an integral part and ultimately a stimulation to the 
multifaceted urban fabric."685 
                                                                                                                                                                        
684 Mur Soteras, Joan B., and Josep Martorell. “Presentació = Présentation.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i 
urbanisme 213 Forum Internacional 1 (1996): 1–3. 
685 Our trans. from French: “C'est en cela que consiste désormais la tache des urbanistes pour donner à la ville 
une nouvelle projection: faire de ces lieux intermédiaires des espaces non-stériles qui, loin d'être totalement 
abandonnés à la publicité et au profit, représenteraient aussi une partie intégrante et finalement stimulante 
du tissu urbain multiforme.” – In: Solà-Morales, Manuel de. “Els Nous Espais Públics = Les Nouveaux Espaces 
Publics.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 214 Forum Internacional 2 (1996): 8–9.

## S. 309

306 
The author refers thus to the urbanist profession with the same connotations of 
responsibility and hopefulness regarding intervening on such spaces as the terrain vague. 
As for José Morales, he tackled first hand the subject, yet equally from a practice-based 
point of view, presenting three case studies dealing with terrains vagues686: Luc Lévesque's 
interventions in Québec Canada, Puerto Madero and Tren de la Costa which are two 
zones in Buenos Aires that had undergone many interventions through their terrains 
vagues, and finally similar interventions in the peninsula of Zorrotzaure in Bilbao by 
architects Aguilera Luque and Izquierdo. Both text and projects shared common interests 
in identity, memory, the duality form-nonform and intervening through terrains vagues 
as the better alternative to preserve these ideas. One sentence perhaps summarizes the 
rest: 
"Admittedly behind the possible occluded matters and behind the answers, it is equally 
nothing but an unstable situation, intermediate, between two times, that is in formation: that 
of our position within contemporary culture."687 
These essays shared thus an interest in developing on Solà-Morales’s terrain vague 
notion towards a practice-based direction. They also show how Solà-Morales was already 
in 1990s inspiring a new form of perceiving the urban space and its potential, and how 
this triggered a broad questioning of the architect’s role in the new urban transformations. 
Similarly, in the welcoming message to the UIA congressists by Soteras and by 
Josep Martorell, President of the UIA96 organizational committee, they described the 
theme as such: 
“The dilemma is not between the natural and the cultural, but rather between an 
obstinate civic culture and the frantic abandonment to the big ocean of the megalopolis.”688 
The attention of the congress was set on the future of big cities with an underlying 
focus on the terrain vague. In December 1994 in São Paulo, an interview regarding the 
UIA congress was already conducted with Solà-Morales in “Arquitectos do Mundo”, 
published in the Projecto journal689. He was asked to answer specific questions regarding 
the team behind the congress, the various themes and the intent. The interview reveals 
that the organization behind the congress was already in place and the terrain vague was 
already in central stage. Moreover, during the debate at the round table690 set at the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
686 Morales, José. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 214 Forum Internacional 2 (1996): 
164–171. 
687 Our trans. from French: “Il faut admettre que derrière les éventuelles choses occultées et derrière les 
réponses, ce n'est pas autre chose qu'une situation également instable, intermédiaire, entre deux temps, qui 
est en formation: celle de notre position au sein de la culture contemporaine.” – Ibid.171. 
688 Our trans. from Spanish: “El dilema no es entre lo natural y lo cultural, sino entre cultura cívica obstinada 
y el abandono frente al gran océano de las megápolis.” – Martorell, Josep. El Pais. 4 July 1996. Ignasi de Solà-
Morales Private Archive - Box 30. Article. 
689 “Arquitectos Do Mundo” in Projecto n.181 - December 1994. Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 28. 
690 File: COAM UIA Congress. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 30. Documents - Letters.

## S. 310

307 
 
COAM in May 1996 to discuss the congress and its theme, including the terrain vague, 
the architect Carlos Pezzi691 stated: 
“As for the empty areas, remember that the peripheries in Spain are still being 
mistreated since no reorganization of the space or articulation of the dispersion is raised and 
industrial disused spaces are not rearranged on a large scale, incurring its minimization 
except in Madrid and Barcelona.”692 
He addressed thus the terrain vague as a critical contemporary theme to resolve in 
Spain, especially in relation to the post-industrial zones and the peripheral massive 
unorganized constructions around the country693. In contrast, architecture theoretician 
Fernando de Teran was more interested in the international level of the terrain vague. He 
stated: 
“I think there is need to enter the description of those gaps and terrain vague at regional 
level, and where major infrastructures are communicating things that were not there before, 
and where traditional cities play a role of nodules that will continue to be, although there is a 
geographic and economic process which, for the most part, escapes control.”694 
Overall, the theme of the meeting was evident: defining the role of the architect 
within rapidly changing urban and economic conditions, where organization, control and 
a sense of proficiency were slowly being lost. The terrain vague in this context was gladly 
welcomed as a definition of one of these transformations, both as a symptom and more 
importantly as a solution. Solà-Morales staged the terrain vague as the final one of the 
UIA’s five themes and aimed to present it as potentially-charged spaces apt to produce 
innovative intervention modes on the urban fabric. In this sense, the terrain vague was 
depicted as a new urban vision, a configuration of urban spaces and a new mode of 
intervention. 
On another level, the congress was highly acclaimed, yet highly criticized. On the 
one hand, it was described by Tone Wheeler in the journal Architecture Australia as the 
“architectural Olympics”. On the other hand, many articles echoed the discomfort of the 
congressists in front of the chaos that accompanied the unexpectedly large number of 
attendees. Aside from organizational mistakes, some argued that the five key subjects of 
the UIA Congress seemed randomly chosen, failing to grasp the connections and the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
691 Dean of the C.O.A. de Andalucía Oriental. 
692 Our trans. from Spanish: “En cuanto a las zonas vacías recordar que las periferías en España todavía se 
siguen maltratando ya que no se plantea una reorganlzación del espacio o una vertebración de Ia dispersión y 
que no se reacomodan a gran escala los espacios industriales en desuso incurriendo en su minimalización, 
salvo en Madrid y Barcelona.” – File: COAM UIA Congress. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 30. 
693 A theme that would later become critical with the economic crisis that halted massive real estate 
construction sites that remained desolate new forms of terrains vagues until this day. 
694 Our trans. from Spanish: “Creo que hace falta que entre en la descripción de esos vacíos y terrain vague a 
nivel regional, y donde las grandes infrastructuras están comunicando cosas que antes no lo estaban y donde 
las cuidades tradicionales juegan un papel de nodulo que van a seguir teniendo, aunque haya un proceso 
geografico y economico que, en su mayor parte, se escapa de control.” - File: COAM UIA Congress. Ignasi de 
Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 30. Documents - Letters.

## S. 311

308 
reasoning behind the thematic choices. One main observation to the UIA criticized the 
seemingly arbitrary choice of the five themes. For instance, Ricardo Legoretta from 
Mexico argued: "I will not be contained [...] And what is a container? All architecture is a 
container."695 These arguments alluded seemingly to the difficulty at the time of grasping 
the meaning and encompassing definitions of these innovative overarching themes. And, 
while the various themes denote one wholesome mode to perceive and understand the 
contemporary challenges that architecture and urbanism faced and still face since the 
mid-1990s, they were at first glance not simple to grasp. The challenges themselves 
required clarification, i.e. the growing and shrinking cities, the transformations in urban 
flows and networks, and the undefined zones at the peripheries, as well as the 
sustainability issue. One particular letter addressed by Solà-Morales to Kamil Khan 
Mumtaz, Architect and head of the National College of Arts in Lahore, Pakistan, seems 
relevant in this regard. Mumtaz had expressed already in the first meeting for the UIA in 
Barcelona in spring 1993, his concern regarding the five themes of the congress, 
describing them as irrelevant on an international level. He complained about the 
difference between developing and developed countries and that the audience of the UIA 
Congress at the first meeting focused on developed countries. Subsequently, Solà-Morales, 
in his letter in April 1995, expressed his own concern regarding Mumtaz's text for the 
congress and his protests and previously voiced opinions696. He urged him finally to 
reconsider his approach and to be more proactive in a constructive manner within the 
UIA debates. Mumtaz nonetheless points at the importance of place and scale in the urban 
transformations of the period. It is thus only natural that the urban discussions during the 
late 1990s involved concern to include the global scale of urban concerns, including how 
the terrain vague was meant to be tackled and who it involves. 
In contrast, a letter from Juan Purcell Fricke in 1995, dean of the Faculty of 
Architecture at the Universidad Católica de Valparaíso in Chile, to Solà-Morales described 
the congress as a metaphor to the transformations affecting many contemporary cities, 
thus restoring the universal legitimacy of the congress and its themes. Due to the large 
numbers, the congress had to be relocated from the old center to “the new Barcelona, El 
Ensanche”697. Fricke described this ‘exodus’ as imitating the exodus within cities from the 
old center to the peripheries, where the terrain vague takes on a significant role in this 
displacement and in redefining this so-called new city. In this context, Fricke commended 
Solà-Morales on his great work on the congress and seemed to praise the adequacy of 
issues handled at the UIA congress, including the terrain vague. A second letter from 
Fricke in October 1995698 expressed the latter’s approval to participate within the terrain 
                                                                                                                                                                        
695 Wheeler, Tone. “Barcelona: UIA Conference.” Architecture Australia AA 85.5 (1996): 50. 
696 Letter: Solà-Morales to Kamil Khan Mumtaz, April 1995. Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 1994. 
697 Letter: Juan Fricke to Solà-Morales - October 1995. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 28. 
698 Ibid.

## S. 312

309 
 
vague section of the congress and on presenting within it his project “Ciudad Abierta” 
which was around similar topics. His reason was mainly theoretical: he considered that 
the discussion joining the 25 years project with the notion of terrain vague would push to 
question the latter from a new perspective, one that is focused on Latin America, 
considering thus the notion as equally belonging to the southern continent, even if not 
directly linked. 
In the same lines of expanding the notion’s scope, the terrain vague was 
differently interpreted by each of the presenters within this section at the UIA Congress. 
One can consider these interpretations as the first main response to the notion within the 
UIA context. Similar ideas and urban visions resurfaced as those from previous chapters: 
Deleuzian rhizomatic ideas, concerns with identity and memory, issues of scale, inbetweenness and resistance, and the like. Irena Flalová for instance wrote an essay 
claiming the terrain vague to be a case of memory, while Alberto Pérez-Gómez addressed 
the interstitial scale with the title “Spaces in-Between”699. Those who presented at the 
congress for the “Terrain Vague” section were: Zaha Hadid, Peter Eisenman, Jan Kaplicky 
(Future Systems, London), Wolf Prix (Coop Himmelblau, Berlin), Francesco Venezia, 
Wilfred Wang (Frankfurt), Herman Czech (Vienna), Brian Boigon (Toronto), Frank 
Gehry, Françoise Choay, Marina Waissman, and the moderator was Juan Busquets700. All 
these names portray how the terrain vague was intended as being a mainly Western 
theme, yet encompassing as well the international scale701. 
It is also important to mention that Solà-Morales’s endeavors in the UIA Congress 
as well as his work on the terrain vague helped bridge connections between Europe, in 
particular Barcelona, and the American spheres. The notion, aside from its specific 
definition, helped build a cross-continental collective imagery of certain spaces within 
and on the limits of the metropolis, a bridge that continued to evolve as the terrain vague 
notion developed. In this respect, an exhibit at the Fundació Antoni Tàpies in Barcelona, 
entitled “La Ciutat de la Gent" (The City of People), is representative of such exchange in 
imagery. The exhibit, by the British photographer Craigie Horsfield, portrays the city of 
Barcelona through a series of over fifty black and white photographs. It was organized to 
coincide with the 1996 UIA Congress in Barcelona. In collaboration with economists, 
anthropologists and architects, Horsfield depicted individuals and communities of the 
city, focusing on the poorer neighborhoods of Vallbona, Torre Baró and Ciutat Meridiana, 
instead of the newer areas for the Olympic Games. In this sense, the exhibit shows a 
different gaze on the city that was in line with Solà-Morales’s intentions behind the UIA 
                                                                                                                                                                        
699 Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box UIA. Documents - Letters. 
700 Ibid. 
701 We will not tackle Asian contexts and cities, they are beyond the scope of this thesis; the meaning and 
significance of terrains vagues there are quite different and would require a different research.

## S. 313

310 
congress and the terrain vague notion. It is a testimony of the seldom mentioned “other” 
on the peripheries and in the terrains vagues of post-Olympic Barcelona. 
In addition, a similar sense of interdisciplinarity as in the ANY conferences was 
witnessed through the UIA Congress and was subsequently commented on. In this spirit, 
Argentine-born artist from New York Silvia Kolbowski who also participated in the UIA 
Congress, wrote to Solà-Morales after the congress: 
“It was a very interesting context for me to work in, even though unexpected 
circumstances arose. To some extent, those circumstances are just a sign of the times in which 
architects try to be artists, artists try to be journalists, musicians try to be artists, artists try to 
be directors, directors try to be politicians, etc. Both the exhibition and the convention were 
very ambitious gestures, with all the risks involved.”702 
This interdisciplinarity, clearly present in the "Terrain Vague" essay, was 
accentuated as art and architecture were fused through the congress with designed 
installations and other presentations or performances. This approach was probably due to 
a period at the turn of the millennium fascinated by globalization and trying to redefine 
its architectural and urban disciplines’ borderlines. It thus linked the congress with the 
general concern of other groups and events, such as the Anyone Corporation and the 
Anyplace event. 
On another note, Solà-Morales promoted and disseminated the UIA and its 
themes, the terrain vague included, in some events prior to the congress. On the 3rd of 
October 1995, Solà-Morales gave for instance lectures on the terrain vague and the UIA 
Congress at the UPC under a program organized by the University of East London. Bill 
Risebero contacted him from London, inviting him to lecture at the exchange program 
between UEL and Barcelona. In a responsive letter after Solà-Morales’s lecture, Risebero 
writes: 
 "We (Bill Risebero and Chris Seaber) both believe that without your valuable input the 
conference would not have been the resounding success it was. […] Many thanks for your 
extremely authoritative contribution, which everyone greatly appreciated. Terrain Vague is 
now a live issue at UEL!"703 
Risebero thus expressed his fascination with the person of Solà-Morales and how 
his presence positively altered the impact the congress had. He more importantly 
expressed a particular interest in the terrain vague and the notion of mutations. In fact, 
the various lectures of this conference followed similar themes as the UIA Congress, 
including "Containers", "Terrain Vague" and "Mutations". The universities involved were 
many, including Delft University of Technology, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki and 
other European institutions, which provided substantial coverage of the lectures, and the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
702 Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box UIA. Documents - Letters. 
703 Letter: Bill Risebero to Solà-Morales - October 1995. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 29.

## S. 314

311 
 
UIA themes, within the European academic realm. This event propagated hence once 
more the terrain vague beyond Solà-Morales’s original input. 
The terrain vague notion benefited nonetheless greatly from the reputation of 
Solà-Morales. He had become in the mid-1990s a known and influential figure in Europe 
and internationally. An article published in El Pais in August 1996704 even presents an 
interview with Solà-Morales, treating him like a celebrity and asking about his culinary 
preferences, featuring a caricature of the architect holding what seem to be chopsticks. He 
was also selected in 1995 as one of the “Gent amb Imaginació” (Persons with Imagination) 
for the candidature of Barcelona as the European City of Culture 2001705. 
 
FIGURE 105: Gent Am Imaginació. Ignasi de Solà-Morales private archives. 1995. Article. 
As for Quaderns, the following editions of the journal kept on referring to the 
same theme or sub-themes of the terrain vague throughout the 1990s and even beginning 
of the new millennium. The titles of the Quaderns editions during 1997 are very 
exemplary of this effect: "Form and Place"(216), "Land Arch" (217), "Rethinking Mobility" 
(218). Furthermore, Solà-Morales’s interest in the non-physical forces of the city took 
resonance in Catalan writers such as Eduard Bru and Manuel Gausa. Themes in Quaderns 
during the late 1990s were clearly contemporary and focused on new territories of 
                                                                                                                                                                        
704 El Pais Article - August 1996. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 30. 
705 With a letter from Pasqual Maragall and from Ferran Mascarell, director of the candidature. (Ignasi de 
Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 32). This also shows the importance of Barcelona as a cultural hub within 
the mid and late 1990s.

## S. 315

312 
experimentation, such as the terrain vague. For instance, in Quaderns 207, Xavier Costa 
published "Non-places and the Metropolis", Joan Nogué and Jordi Roig published "The 
Site and the Memory" and a second round table and collective written work was entitled 
"The Construction of the Territory" by Eduard Bru, Oriol Nello, Josep Vilanova and 
others. The entire edition of Quaderns 210 seemed also focused on the developments of 
the new areas in and around Barcelona, including its terrains vagues, such as the Vallès 
area, the Laval and the PobleNou. A similar focus on the peripheries of Barcelona can also 
be seen in Quaderns 207-209, published in the same year. Moreover, in Quaderns 210, a 
round table was organized to discuss the changing role of the architect, under the title "La 
Figura de l'Arquitecte: Nou Marc Professional" (The Architect's figure: New Professional 
Framework), probably also resonating with Solà-Morales’s reflection on this issue. And 
lastly, Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" ended on a final note celebrating continuity. This 
continuity did not imply material permanence, but rather a network of forces and 
energies linked in time. In Quaderns 222 and 223, such ideas were reintroduced and were 
at the core of the editions: Juan Ramírez Guedes wrote “Between Open Lines”, José 
Ballesteros wrote “Temporal Sequences”, José Juan Barba “Approximations of Time-Space” 
and Javier Mozas wrote “Montage and Temporality”. As a result, the terrain vague and 
other sub-notions present in Solà-Morales’s essay were thus espoused by Quaderns and by 
the affected locals as well as internationally. 
Conclusion: from formulation to dissemination 
Hence, in this context of important western cities under transformation and some 
important events within them, the “Terrain Vague” essay developed as a theoretical 
interdisciplinary construct establishing a new set of relations between the urban fabric 
and the architect’s role. The notion propagated well through this broad exposure and 
inspired many. The terrain vague notion is thus once again established as a product of an 
author’s lifework culminating in one essay that was the synthesis of an era’s collective 
inkling. 
As Anthony Vidler notes, in an interview with the author706, both his work and 
Solà-Morales’s were tuned to culture and an interest in using it to understand the kind of 
interlocking relations among different cultural practices. He then continues by explaining 
that Solà-Morales was more interested in intellectual stimulus rather than pure theory. 
Observing the "Terrain Vague" essay, it seems thus logical that it is written in a subtle 
manifesto form and that Solà-Morales’s promoting tour around Europe and the world 
resembles vaguely a marketing touring or even a political propaganda. In this fashion, 
                                                                                                                                                                        
706 Interview between author and Anthony Vidler in Zurich. 10 Mar. 2015.

## S. 316

313 
 
there is a link between the manifesto-narrative mold in which the text is formed and the 
trajectory the essay witnessed from an experimental Triennale to the ‘architecture 
Olympics’ UIA Congress. A successful diffusion of the essay appears to have led to a 
vibrant terrain vague notion inspiring a diversity of people. 
In conclusion, the development of the terrain vague notion along Solà-Morales’s 
journey exposes in parallel the network of architects and intellectuals that were behind 
the formulation and strength of architecture theories at the end of the millennium. In 
addition, this “travelling concept”707 followed how theoretical interests shifted towards a 
concern with globalization, mobility and fluxus, but also a rising fascination with 
interdisciplinarity as a way to redefine the architectural and urban domains. Solà-
Morales’s terrain vague notion was in the middle of these transformations as one proposed 
resolution of conflicts within them, one linked to changes in scale between urban and 
architecture and with an idea of continuity in time. Accordingly, it was positively 
received within the theoretical circles of Europe and the United States, but also 
worldwide. Solà-Morales’s terrain vague was thus promulgated, since its coining, in a 
multitude of directions, first mainly in theoretical and interdisciplinary spheres, and later 
more in practice-based interventions in the urban fabric. For this period, the first direct 
receptions are chiefly tangible through records in archives and in journals republishing 
the terrain vague around the world. As for the following chapter, the reception of the 
essay spread beyond Solà-Morales’s person, as did its significance and magnitude 
worldwide.
