Toronto: University of Toronto Press, 2002.

## S. 317

314 
 
 
 
CHAPTER 5 – The Influence of the Essay and its 
Key Concept on Theory and Practice 
 
 
 
 
 
FIGURE 107: Presentation in Rosario, Argentina. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Box 30, 6 
September 1994. Photograph showing the large audience.

## S. 318

315 
 
Republications, re-presentations and influence 
The importance of the essay is its timing as a catalyst as well as synthesist of the 
thoughts of an era looking for definitions to explain its fast-changing urban environment. 
Solà-Morales made use of the momentum of the period to compose his masterpiece. The 
essay accordingly left a strong influence on artistic, architectural and urban disciplines, 
from the beginning of the new millennium onward. The projects it inspired were as 
interdisciplinary, provocative and political as the formulation of Solà-Morales’s own 
essay. By examining the essay’s influence, one can delineate how the discourse on the 
terrain vague became established in the architectural and urban fields and how it 
developed into the 21st century. 
The reverberations of Solà-Morales’s essay extend beyond the immediate context 
in which it was first received in the mid-1990s. In the years following its introduction it 
was recurrently re-presented and republished. It was translated into English, French, 
German, Spanish, Catalan, Portuguese and Japanese. It also appeared in architectural, 
urban, artistic and photographic media; the list of republications is worldwide, from 
Canada with Luc Lévesque to Australia with Julian Raxworthy. From the mid-1990s 
onward, Solà-Morales’s essay became a canonical reference for contemporary urban 
theory, especially when discussing themes of urban voids, desolate areas, peripheries, 
shrinking, sprawling, abandoned or post-industrial cities. 
Thanks to Solà-Morales’s involvement in the UIA Congress and the Triennale 
during the mid-1990s, the notion of the terrain vague was assured an international 
platform from the very beginning. Moreover, it continued to gather momentum through 
the events and publications attached to Solà-Morales’s name, and the notion was 
internationally acclaimed, across many disciplines, both in theory and architectural 
practice. Furthermore, the main cities of interest for those who were inspired by Solà-
Morales coincided with the locations in which he gave presentations of the terrain vague: 
American cities (mainly Detroit), Italian cities (mainly Milano and Rome), Canadian cities 
(mainly Quebec City and Montreal), and finally Spanish cities (mainly Barcelona and its 
satellite cities). 
Moreover, the ways in which the essay was received and considered can be 
divided into three categories of interest, or perspectives, which reflect the main themes of 
the different sections of the text708: Firstly, within the context of an interdisciplinary and 
artistic approach to architecture including photography, art and psychology; secondly, 
within an etymological and architectural framework of urban themes relevant to the 
terrain vague and the concepts of interstitiality and urban void in general; and thirdly, 
                                                                                                                                                                        
708 Mentioned in chapter two of this dissertation.

## S. 319

316 
within the sphere of architectural activism and resistance to the political and economic 
forces of the city. 
Accordingly, the influence of the essay mainly followed these themes, although of 
course an interest in the person of Solà-Morales was sparked too. Out of the multitude of 
figures who became inspired by this work, who quoted the essay and followed its ideas, 
some stand out as more relevant to mention here due to their strong attachment to the 
terrain vague theme and their significance in the development of the notion. In what 
follows, we will elaborate on some of these cases, which exemplify the above-mentioned 
categories of reception. As we cannot be exhaustive, only the main directions, which the 
influence took beyond the period in which the essay was first published, will be 
presented. 
Finally, we will also elaborate on the subject of Landscape Urbanism, whose 
protagonists were keenly inspired by Solà-Morales and whose network was considerably 
more far-reaching. In fact, this chapter is an attempt to comprehend the influence of Solà-
Morales’s "Terrain Vague" on the larger scale and lengthier timeline, beyond the first 
reception and reactions after his first lectures and original publications. Landscape 
Urbanism, in this respect, represents one main larger-scale impact that the essay had, 
aside from the case of Almarcegui within the arts, the case of Luc Lévesque within 
architecture and finally the case of Stalker within activism. 
 
Artistic and interdisciplinary itinerary 
Many indicators foretell an interdisciplinary response. Firstly, the author himself 
was a theoretician, an architect and a philosopher who used his knowledge effectively in 
his written work. Secondly, the essay proposes an entry point from photography, moving 
from there to other fields, to finally land in architecture. Thirdly, the audience of the 
essay has been quite an interdisciplinary crowd since the beginning, whether at the 
Anyplace conference, the Triennale di Milano or the Metropolis Master program. 
Consequently, many artists and photographers, such as Manolo Laguillo, Julia Schulz-
Dornburg, Martina Margini and Carole Lévesque, were inspired by the essay to capture 
the essence of the terrain vague through photographs, exhibitions and artistic 
interventions. 
Many of those who quoted Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" were interested in 
interdisciplinarity per se, or in an interdisciplinary approach towards architecture and 
other fields, mainly photography, art, filmography and finally psychology and economy. 
Some quoted Solà-Morales’s attention to certain photographers and cinematographers,

## S. 320

317 
 
such as Patrick Barron and Manuela Mariani709. They linked his interest in photography 
with a larger interest in the interdisciplinary connections between cinema, photography 
and architecture. A number of films, documentaries and artistic projects adopted the 
subject of terrain vague as their main theme and sometimes as their title, including 
Philippe Grand’s documentary Terrain Vague710, Graham Gussin’s art installation “Terrain 
Vague”711, Philipp Gallon’s video installation "Terrain Vague"712 and Ben Alper’s pigment 
prints series Terrain Vague713. An art happening entitled “Terrain Vague” took place for 
instance in Zurich, in September 2014, in which I personally contributed, in order to raise 
awareness and revive the terrain vague under the bridge at Hardplatz, and a small 
publication followed the event. Many exhibitions in Spain revolved also on the terrain 
vague or analogous themes (and still do so today), such as “Art, Dos Punts” 714 at the 
MACBA in 2013, and “Urban Peripheries, Where the City Lost Its Name”715 at the 
MUHBA in 2014. Many artists have created alternative ways to explore the potential of 
such spaces. The examples vary greatly from temporary performances, to short films and 
light installations. 
 
FIGURE 108: “Terrain Vague” a happening in Hardplatz. Zurich, September 2014. (Photograph by Author). - 
Artists and architects creating an event to raise awareness towards the forgotten area under the bridge.

711 Gussin, Graham. Terrain Vague. 2003. 50th Venice Art Biennale installation: a coach installed in a
