## S. 321

318 
 
FIGURE 109: Grand, Philippe. Terrain Vague. Switzerland, 2012. 89min film documentary on the outskirts of Geneva. 
 
The case of Lara Almarcegui 
One of the main artists to have been well inspired by Solà-Morales’s "Terrain 
Vague" is Lara Almarcegui. She is a Spanish artist based in Rotterdam. Known for her 
work at the 55th Venice Art Biennale in 2013, she has also exhibited in Dijon and 
Stockholm, and presented projects at the Liverpool Biennial in 2004, at the Museum 
Boijmans van Beuningen in Rotterdam in 2005 and at the São Paulo Biennial in 2006. 
Relation   to   Solà-­‐Morales:   an   inspired   research-­‐based   approach   
Almarcegui mentions, when interviewed, that the writings of Solà-Morales have 
been of paramount importance to her work, especially the essay “Terrain Vague”, which is 
for her an inspirational theoretical grounding that encompasses artistic, economic, 
ecological and political angles. She states: 
“Ignasi de Solà-Morales’s essay has been very important to me: “I started working with 
wastelands (terrains vagues) in the late nineties in Amsterdam, and being a young artist in an 
art school, I couldn't find references interesting enough on the subject of wastelands within 
the art context. I asked around in The Berlage master of architecture program in the 
Netherlands and a student gave me the essay by Ignasi de Solà-Morales. Since then it has

## S. 322

319 
 
been my reference. I agree a lot in his way of describing the terrain vague, especially with the 
concept of 'possibility'. I often mention that text in my lectures, and I still even now give it as 
reference to my students.”716 
The "Terrain Vague" essay thus grounds theoretically Lara Almarcegui’s artworks 
as a new mode of perceiving and intervening on the city through these spaces’ potential. 
Her work could be considered as an artistic development of what Solà-Morales described 
as a new gaze on the city through its terrains vagues. 
Similar to Solà-Morales’s call for better informed decision making, Almarcegui’s 
work is moreover often research-based interventions that vary in final form, yet start 
with a careful documentation of a terrain vague. Based on archival work, interviews and 
photographic records, her preliminary work consists of extracting essence from such 
spaces in order to then decide how, and whether or not, to intervene. The research phase 
is thus often an important and intrinsic part of the final artwork717. This approach is said 
by the artist herself718 to be inspired by Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay: “a careful 
attention to continuity”719 and potential of such spaces. 
Her installation “Materiales de construcción” (Construction Materials) for the 
Spanish Pavilion at the 55th Venice Art Biennale is for instance one such Solà-Moralesinspired project. For this installation, Almarcegui conducted a research around the city of 
Venice, going through archives and talking to citizens in order to locate and analyze the 
city’s terrains vagues. She describes: 
“My preliminary research consisted of talking to Venetian city planners and architects 
who told me about their future plans, which allowed me to locate the wastelands and empty 
plots that would be affected by those transformations. I visited those areas in order to choose 
the one I thought was the most significant.” 720 
Almarcegui then chose Sacca San Mattia to be the terrain vague included in her 
project. It seemed the most appropriate due to its complex configuration, its large size, its 
significance to the Venice region, and its materiality formed of years and years of 
compiled glass and rubble from the nearby industries. The site was in effect a terrain 
vague shaped since the 1930s by repetitive discarding of rubble. The plot of 26 hectares of 
area was the largest empty undeveloped land in Venice. Many speculative and prospective 
projects were discussed for the future of the terrain. Almarcegui’s work consisted of a 
research and documentation of the site as it was in its derelict form. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
716 Correspondence with Lara Almarcegui. Apr. 2014. 
717 Moreover, when discussing Almarcegui and other artists interested in interdisciplinary and artistic 
interventions in or about the terrain vague, we fail to mention one issue: this perspective is focused on the 
approach rather than on the message. The intent behind such interdisciplinary projects is almost always 
about bridging gaps between different disciplines, but other intents are also at stake. 
718 Correspondence with Lara Almarcegui. Apr. 2014. 
719 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 123. 
720 Almarcegui, Lara. A Guide to Sacca San Mattia, the Abandoned Island of Murano, Venice. 2013. 6.

## S. 323

320 
Almarcegui then presented her documented work in the pavilion where she also 
piled rubble and construction materials to allude to the added value of human labor. In 
this respect, the Lara Almarcegui Artbook 721 following the Biennale is mainly a 
documentation of the exhibition with some theoretical standpoints on the latter722. On the 
cover of the book, the presence of a human doing labor on her exhibition at the Venice 
Art Biennale demonstrates how her intent is not to represent a terrain vague devoid of 
humans, but rather to demonstrate that the terrain vague is intimately linked with the 
man-made intervention. Perhaps a better statement would be that the terrain vague here 
is a human production in time, not a piece of pure nature in the urban fabric. 
Almarcegui’s project developed therefore from an increased awareness toward the city, 
using its terrains vagues to reflect on the evolution of the urban fabric and the elements 
composing it. 
 
FIGURE 110: Zaya, Octavio, ed. Lara Almarcegui [Pabellón Español, 55a Exposición Internacional de Arte, La 
Biennale di Venezia]. MAEC and AECID Turner, 2013. Front cover. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
721 Zaya, Octavio, ed. Lara Almarcegui [Pabellón Español, 55a Exposición Internacional de Arte, La Biennale 
di Venezia]. s.l.: MAEC and AECID Turner, 2013. 
722 Including an article from the curator and one article particular to the terrain vague theme. - Ursprung, 
Philip. “Beyond the Terrain Vague: Following Lara Almarcegui.” Lara Almarcegui [Pabellón Español, 55a 
Exposición, La Biennale di Venezia]. Ed. Octavio Zaya. s.l.: AECID Turner, 2013. 43–59.

## S. 324

321 
 
Almarcegui’s artistic and urban projects furthermore sometimes included a second 
phase consisting of making guides to terrains vagues, based on the conducted research. 
Among these guides created by Almarcegui were portrayal of terrains vagues in the Lea 
Valley in London before the completion of the Olympic Park in 2009, in many spaces 
along the Tiber River in Rome in 2012 waiting to be filled for the Olympic Games of 
2020, as well as in the Flushing River in Queens, New York in 2010, in the city of Sharjah, 
the United Arab Emirates and of course the Sacca San Mattia of Murano. The final 
product then comprised elements of both the guide and the research transformed into an 
installation or many diverse other artistic forms, such as the use of raw materials 
expressive of the materiality of the terrain vague documented. The final products are only 
glimpses of the vast body of research she had previously collected. 
Almarcegui’s guides act in addition as an exposé of the terrain vague within the 
city, in its various shapes. They also contrast the indeterminacy, transitory form of the 
terrain vague with the organized planned city. These guides are important when 
considered as historical documents of presents that are no more existent. It is neither the 
predefined past, nor the prospect future that is documented, but merely a moment of 
moments in the ever changing present. These moments, once documented, are preserved 
in the collective memory. Their inexistence becomes thus a testimony to their potential 
and possibilities permanently lost by the city. Their erasure is a warning to the prospect 
loss of other terrains vagues, along with their particular possibilities and promises. 
Almarcegui’s guides resemble thus mini-manifestos decrying land speculation, the realestate market and orthodox urban planning that leave no room for alternative 
intervention modes on the terrain vague. Her guides also condemn the large-scale utopic 
dreams of Modernity that encouraged what is now the contemporary real-estate market 
and many urban processes that disregard alternative readings of spaces in the city. 
Approach   to   the   terrain   vague:   Urban   regeneration   
Almarcegui links through her work the notion of terrain vague with processes of 
urban regeneration. According to Octavio Zaya, the curator of the Venice Biennale 
Spanish Pavilion: 
“Almarcegui has been working at the boundary between urban regeneration and decay, 
and devising exhibition projects that render visible what escapes our attention and even our 
awareness.”723 
Almarcegui manages to instigate a discussion on the continuous cycles of urban 
renewal and degradation, with a particular attention on the ephemeral and the transient 
                                                                                                                                                                        
723 Zaya, Octavio, ed. Lara Almarcegui [Pabellón Español, 55a Exposición Internacional de Arte, La Biennale 
di Venezia]. s.l.: MAEC and AECID Turner, 2013. 2-4.

## S. 325

322 
within these practices. Her work for example could sometimes be associated with the 
transformations of Barcelona, among other cities that underwent rapid construction. The 
film of José Luis Guerin in 2001 ''En Construcción" could serve as a prologue to one of the 
aspects of her projects. The film documents the construction of a new building in the 
Raval district in Barcelona, as part of the process of regeneration of the area. During 
demolition, workers discover several ancient tombs. This triggers a reflection, not only on 
the ethical question of what to do with the remains of ancestors who lived in the area 
where now is the neighborhood, but also on the contemporary city as a ruin and the 
consequences of rapid urban transformation following the impulse given by the 1992 
Barcelona Olympics. Such ideas of transformation and systemic regeneration of regions 
are behind many of Almarcegui’s work. The artist sees potential in the terrain vague to 
counteract such top-down approaches of urban planning and construction on the urban 
environment and advocates that the urban fabric holds, as visible in the terrain vague, 
layers that should not be preemptively dismissed due to systematic development practices. 
 
FIGURE 111: Almarcegui, Lara. Construction Rubble of Secession’s Main Hall. Vienna, 2010. 
On a different note, production, construction and site are recurrent notions in 
Almarcegui’s projects. In several of her installations, piles and piles of material are laid out 
to convey on the one hand how the terrain vague is ultimately, as previously mentioned, 
a man-made process building, from raw material, meaning and significance in built and 
non-built urban forms. On the other hand, she denounces the processes of construction 
and deconstruction as parts of a vicious cycle in the lives of cities, in which the terrain 
vague is a materialization, or perhaps non-materialization, of a moment in the urban

## S. 326

323 
 
narrative. Almarcegui moreover does not perceive the terrain vague as an expectant 
empty space. Through her guides in Europe and North America, one of Almarcegui’s main 
emphases is the materiality of space. Some of her installations, such as the one at 
Secession in Austria, are an accumulation of construction material and rubble reflecting 
materials in certain buildings or terrains vagues she is addressing. In the same spirit, 
Almarcegui conceived her installation for the 55th Art Biennale Spanish Pavilion in 2013 
as a reference to construction and materiality. In this exhibit, the installation represents 
the pavilion itself. The piles of raw material accumulated by the artist within the pavilion 
are equivalent in quantity and kind to the material needed to construct the pavilion 
erected by Javier de Luque in 1922. 
 
FIGURE 112: Almarcegui, Lara. Spanish Pavilion at the Venice Art Biennale, 2013. Installation. 
Since the mid-1990s, the same period as Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague”, 
Almarcegui’s artistic practice took a turn towards understanding derelict spaces amidst 
planning and urban and architectural massive development ventures in Europe that 
prophesied the real estate and economic crises of the 2000s. Almarcegui explored such 
sites with a clear focus on their entropic character. Entropy is in fact a recurrent notion in 
the artist’s work. As Jacques Derrida describes: 
“At the origin comes ruin; ruin comes to the origin, it is what first comes and happens to 
the origin, in the beginning.”724 
                                                                                                                                                                        
724 Derrida, Jacques. Mémoires D’aveugle: L’autoportrait et Autres Ruines. Paris: Réunion des Musées 
Nationaux, 1990. 65.

## S. 327

324 
Almarcegui’s installations and guides often reunite the beginning with the end, 
where end becomes beginning; ruin precedes building, entropy reigns. 
When asked about the artworks and references that meant most to her, aside from 
Solà-Morales’s “Terrain Vague” essay, Almarcegui mentions Robert Smithson’s 1967 
photo-essay “A Tour of the Monuments of Passaic”. In fact, one can see similarities in the 
work of the two artists. Both were driven by an analytically-informed art. They both 
documented, photographed and described cities, construction sites and terrains vagues in 
industrial or post-industrial sites. Both are focused on urban erasure, dereliction and 
entropy. Moreover, Smithson’s writings on the notion of entropy and contemporary ruins 
are evidently inspirations to Almarcegui’s installations. Her work since the mid-1990s 
allowed invisible spaces to manifest themselves; spaces where meanings are inverse and 
possibilities are endless. 
Denouncing   the   political   in   the   terrain   vague      
Almarcegui’s intent was set on raising awareness of the terrain vague, without a 
necessarily highly politicized urban agenda. Nonetheless, political, economic and artistic 
schemes seem to take foreground in the artist’s work. As is in Solà-Morales’s case, she 
appears to be denouncing the loss caused by planners’ demolition of certain fragments of 
the urban fabric. Buildings and spaces live in the collective memory; they are part of the 
life of a neighborhood or a city. Their economy is therefore not only physical. The loss 
through demolition is the frame of many of the artist’s interventions; she celebrates the 
terrain vague as a valuable element within the city, as urban fragments worthy also of 
attention and consideration. As an example, in 2008, one project of Almarcegui in which 
she sheds light on the terrain vague consisted of removing the exterior wall of a house in 
ruins in the city of Taipei. The wall, as Almarcegui describes, separated the city from the 
house that was an example of many traditional houses threated by urban capitalist 
development more interested in building commercial and consumerist spaces rather than 
preserving urban history and memory. As Almarcegui explains: 
“By means of revealing sites that are hidden, the relations between environment and 
people are questioned – in past, present or future terms.” 
Invisible to the city dwellers, the value of these houses was kept almost 
deliberately insignificant. When made visible again, the artist makes their presence and 
hence their value noticeable.

## S. 328

325 
 
On   potentiality   
As noted by the curator Lars Bang Larsen725, Almarcegui’s project “Construction 
Materials of São Paulo” in 2006 demolishes the immense scale of the megalopolis and 
presents it as an abstract list, as something that is not or as something that was. Such a city 
can also weigh a billion tons, yet Almarcegui shows that it really takes shape only when 
the myriad actions of its inhabitants, in their everyday life, activate these raw ingredients 
and produce social space. As a consequence, or perhaps in reverse, Almarcegui drew 
attention to the potential inherent to the terrains vagues. 
 
FIGURE 113: Art, Dos Punts. Barcelona, 2013. - Exhibition on Barcelona and contemporary art, including Lara 
Almarcegui. 
Similarly, the artist describes in her project and terrain vague guides in the 
Netherlands: 
“…even though their existence has something to do with future, or past, urbanization 
plans that have been halted for one reason or another. Stripped of a definite purpose, the 
empty lots have enormous potential. They are free spaces where anything could happen.”726 
Then, in 2007, in another project on the Al Khan district in Sharjah, the United 
Arab Emirates, Almarcegui also creates a guide to the terrains vagues of the area, 
composed of eighty slides and a booklet. In this guide, the focus is on the present tense. A 
fishing village absorbed into the adjacent city through massive construction works 
interrupted by still terrains vagues; the scenario is perfect to denounce the development 
                                                                                                                                                                        
725 Larsen, Lars. “The Creative Time Summit: Revolutions in Public Space.” Conference: New York Public 
Library, 2009. 
726 Almarcegui, Lara. Wastelands Map Amsterdam, Guide to the Empty Sites of Amsterdam. 2000. 40 slides, 
guide and map.

## S. 329

326 
endured by the village and the contrasting potential appended in its terrains vagues. Many 
houses were emptied, some were in ruins, some destroyed. Almarcegui input is important 
as it offers through the guide a reading and hence an understanding of the chaos created 
by the ongoing construction. As Almarcegui describes in the guide: 
“The aim of the project is to describe the place as it is at the present time before it 
changes definitively; to make the rubble-strewn landscape that Al Khan has mostly become, 
into something comprehensible.”727 
More importantly, describing Al Khan district as it was in 2007 in a state of underdemolition, or under-construction, meant describing the potential it still had but was 
perhaps on the verge of losing. “A part of city that does not yet correspond to the realization 
of some plan”728, Al Khan was still open for possibilities and held a promise of freedom. 
The first, and perhaps in this case the only, respond to this promise is nature, that takes 
on the potential of the space and grows in all its diversity above the preexistent layers of 
built and landscape; ruins meet plants, wild vegetation meets gardens. The result is an 
entropic mixture of possibilities. 
 
FIGURE 114: Almarcegui, Lara. Guide to Al Khan: An Empty City in the Village of Sharjah. 2007. Guide booklet. 
Hence, Almarcegui’s interventions are well oriented towards mending with urban 
issues related to the terrain vague and architecture’s role in them. She indirectly accuses 
architects of exonerating space that would otherwise generate open possibilities if the 
public took note actively of its presence and potential. In post-industrial zones, 
Almarcegui’s line of reasoning holds most sense. As state support declines and urban and 
                                                                                                                                                                        
727 The guide was part of Almarcegui’s contribution to the Sharjah Biennial 8. - Almarcegui, Lara. Guide to 
Al Khan: An Empty City in the Village of Sharjah. 2007. 4. 
728 Ibid.

## S. 330

327 
 
infrastructure deteriorates, enhancing social injustice and economic insecurity, hope and 
potential become much needed and a call for public urban action becomes necessary. 
Uniqueness   of   the   terrain   vague      
Through a terrain vague perspective, cities and all communities could be arguably 
considered as organic as woodlands. Newer city layers are constructed on the ruins of 
others and spaces are often a collage of various fragments in time and space. The terrain 
vague therefore is almost always rich in connotations and each terrain vague and its 
context are unique. Lara Almarcegui attempts at exploring and demonstrating the 
uniqueness of these spaces through her different projects, artistic installations and 
unconventional guides of the indeterminate. As she declares about her work: 
“one wasteland has very different characteristics from the next. I try to present each site 
in as much detail as I can, zoom in a lot, try to present the uniqueness of each site.” 
The reason of the artist’s attraction for the terrains vagues lies specifically in their 
uniqueness, in their often ambiguous legal status, as well as in the fact that they are 
extremely remote and inaccessible to our everyday experience of the city, as Anthony 
Vidler would call the urban “uncanny”. Whether considered within an environmental 
preservation discourse or whether it is about the legibility of the urban and the branding 
of the city, her commemoration of the terrain vague calls into question our conception of 
the barren and the "savagery", and questions the reason why some places are considered 
more special or more natural than others. Almarcegui thus claims back the terrain vague’s 
right for its legitimate uniqueness within the urban fabric. 
Engaging   Participation   
Similar to the final section of the “Terrain Vague” essay, Almarcegui’s work could 
be considered a call for engaged participation; her artistic endeavors often hide a sense of 
undeclared manifesto. Almarcegui’s interventions are similar to contextual artists such as 
Daniel Buren. Her guides allow for instance an alternative reading of the urban landscape 
and encourage the reader to participate in a form of unauthorized tourist visits to the 
terrains vagues, witnessing anti-monumental places, uncertain and transitory. In the 
future, when perhaps the majority of the land of which she presents documentation will 
be developed or processed due to macroeconomic forces, the guides would serve to 
witness the passage of time.

## S. 331

328 
In Amsterdam, as an example, her terrain vague guides729, which she realized 
through photographs, were distributed in a gallery, the rest of the exhibition space was 
completely empty. Her aim was to encourage the visitors to leave the exhibition space and 
rather go explore themselves the terrains vagues of the city. Her purpose is not polemical 
in nature; she refuses to be the spokeswoman of any particular urban policy or any class of 
people. Her attempt is mainly to raise awareness, engage participation with the terrain 
vague and ask the public to pay attention to changes in their surroundings. 
 
FIGURE 115: Almarcegui, Lara. Guides to Terrains Vagues. Guide booklets of various exhibitions. 
The public hence is invited to take note of the disappearing terrains vagues of the 
city, as well as the constant planning and organization processes. One is also encouraged 
by Almarcegui not to remain passive and succumb to changes, but rather have a say in 
one’s own city’s transformations. As Xavier Girard, French critique and art historian, 
describes: 
“Architecture and urban planning are not the sole responsibility of governments and 
construction, but the people themselves. One begins to wonder what better fate was reserved 
for many of our architectural heritage buildings if the artists were concerned a bit more 
aggressively to raise public awareness.” 730 
                                                                                                                                                                        
729 Almarcegui, Lara. Wastelands Map Amsterdam, Guide to the Empty Sites of Amsterdam. 2000. 40 slides, 
guide and map. 
730 Girard, Xavier. “Lara Almarcegui.” La pensée de midi N° 17.1 (2006): 134–136. – Our trans. from French: 
“L’architecture et l’urbanisme ne sont pas du seul ressort des pouvoirs publics et du BTP, mais des habitants 
eux-mêmes. On se prend à songer quel sort meilleur aurait été réservé à bien des édifices de notre patrimoine 
architectural si des artistes s’étaient préoccupés un peu plus énergiquement de sensibiliser le public.”

## S. 332

329 
 
Through Almarcegui’s guide and research studies on the terrains vagues of cities, 
one is invited furthermore to measure the toll and effects of urban transformations and 
erasures on the environment and, thus, be more concerned about public decisions. 
To   intervene,   or   not   to      
Almarcegui’s aim seems to denounce, as Solà-Morales did in the essay, certain 
processes on the urban realm and shed light on forgotten urban fragments, such as the 
terrain vague. In this respect, her approach is not always either to intervene or to preserve 
the terrain vague as is; her artwork in fact often alternates between these two extremes. 
In the case of the ports of Genk and Rotterdam for example, she agreed with the 
authorities to leave certain sites as terrains vagues for twenty-five years. While in other 
projects, Almarcegui makes direct and sometimes indirect interventions; such as for the 
water structure in Phalsbourg bound to be destroyed, where the artist actively intervened 
by piling next to the fountain the equivalent of the structure in raw material of its own 
kind. Lara Almarcegui is, as Gordon Matta-Clark would call it, an “anarchitect”; she 
considers a building to be a collection of inarticulate materials, and, in parallel, an 
extraordinary landscape when it opposes a categorical use by humans. 
Along these lines, one of Almarcegui’s projects in 2008 is a book and slide 
installation in and about a terrain vague by the Danshui River in Taipei. The project is 
mainly a negotiation between Almarcegui and the localities to preserve the site as a 
terrain vague for as long as possible. Through this protection act, the space will preserve 
its qualities of indeterminacy and vagueness instead of being programmed and determined 
by planning. One could consider Lara Almarcegui’s act as an intervention of nonintervention. The artist did not simply remain passive and did not only raise awareness 
towards the terrain vague. Her approach actively, politically, physically and economically, 
modified the future of the space by hampering prospective planning. Accordingly, 
Almarcegui describes: 
“It is important to maintain terrains of ‘non-design’, because one can only feel free in 
land that has been left forgotten by town planners. In a few years’ time, once the surrounding 
terrains have become more built up, this zone will be the only remaining area of empty land 
around.”731 
Although the terrain vague remains physically untouched, its presence and 
significance within the city is altered; it is no longer an invisible site unseen by the public, 
it is now an artistic statement deliberately present within the city. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
731 Almarcegui, Lara. An Empty Terrain in the Danshui River, Taipei. 2008. slide installation, booklet.

## S. 333

330 
 
FIGURE 116: Almarcegui, Lara. A terrain vague preserved through the artist’s efforts in the Danshui River. 
Taipei, 2008. Photograph. 
In fact, rather than physical objects, installations or studies, Almarcegui’s 
interventions are better understood as processes of time. As Solà-Morales describes in his 
essay, the element of time is one of the main criteria to mind when intervening on the 
terrain vague. The significance of these indeterminate spaces is their ephemeral yet 
constant resurging presence within the city’s fabric, yet outside its productive systems. 
Almarcegui respects this timely fleeting character of the terrain vague through 
interventions that call for slowness and puts in question what is or isn’t considered 
worthy of contemplation. 
In conclusion, Almarcegui develops, as Solà-Morales incites, a link between theory 
and intervention from an interdisciplinary perspective. The focus is on deploying the 
terrain vague’s multifaceted possibilities, as advocated in the “Terrain Vague” essay. This 
approach to the terrain vague through art and interdisciplinary consideration was also 
followed by others inspired by Solà-Morales to perceive the city through the potential of 
its terrains vagues, as will be seen in what follows. The first two sections of Solà-Morales’s 
essay focused on the history of photography, art and cinema732, which facilitated and 
enabled such an approach. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
732 As described in the second and third chapters.

## S. 334

331 
 
Another   relevant   case   in   the   arts:   Antoni   Muntadas      
In a similar fashion, Solà-Morales also inspired another Spanish artist, Antoni 
Muntadas, who also adopted the topic of the terrain vague as one of his interests, although 
not as vehemently as Almarcegui. Born in Barcelona in 1941, yet residing in New York 
since 1971, Muntadas was a friend of Solà-Morales. He contributed in 1996 to the XIX 
UIA congress in Barcelona. Under the theme of mutations, he presentation an installation 
entitled “Control Room” in which the entire city is envisioned as one control room under 
surveillance. The New-York based artist was also part of the teaching board for the 
Metropolis Master Program between 1992 and 2006. 
His work focuses often on how media arbitrates our perception of reality. It is 
often as interdisciplinary as Solà-Morales’s essay or Almarcegui’s projects, focused 
however on media art and conceptual art. His work “On Translation: The Internet 
Project” in 1997 investigates for instance translation processes and how meaning is 
transformed through mediation and languages on a worldwide scale733. His work in this 
sense combines the UIA theme of mutations with the subject of terrain vague. He 
explores the transformations in perception through their mediations, similar to how the 
terrain vague is transformed through mediations on it and how one perceives it. He 
describes: 
“It is an ideal space because it is a place of union, friction and possible dialogue. They are 
terrain vague spaces that you have to search and find. This search also gives an option to 
discover other corners.”734 
In this respect, Muntadas also tackles often in his work the relation between 
public and private spaces, such as in “Proyectos Urbanos” in 2002 to 2005, “Hacia Sevilla” 
in 2008 and the “Loss of Public Space” in 2008. Muntadas’ retrospective installation 
entitled “Muntadas: Entre/Between” in 2011-12 at the Museo Nacional Centro de Arte 
Reina Sofía in Madrid is representative of his interests. The installation was divided into 
nine thematic blocks, some of which are similar to previously discussed topics related to 
the terrain vague: Public territories, Spectacle Spaces, Spheres of power, Microspaces, 
Media landscape, The construction of fear, Scopes of translation, The archive and Systems 
of art. Muntadas was often inspired by the work of Guy Debord and the Situationists, 
including ideas of nomadism, wandering and dérive, to deal with urban topics. In 
addition, similar to Solà-Morales’s call for awareness, Muntadas doesn’t perceive his work 
as an intervention, but rather a tool to understand and interpret: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
733 Further information: Magrini, Boris. “Confronting the Machine: An Enquiry into the Subversive Drives of 
Computer-Generated Art.” Degree of Doctor of Philosophy. University of Zurich, 2016. 202-204. 
734 Trans. from Spanish: “Es un espacio idóneo pues es un lugar de juntura, unión, fricción y posible diálogo. 
Son espacios terrain vague que tienes que buscar y encontrar. Esa búsqueda también da opción a descubrir 
otros rincones.” - Espejo, Bea. “Antoni Muntadas ‘Es El Momento de Creer En El Arte, de Que La Gente Se 
Lo Apropie.’” El Cultural (2011). Web.

## S. 335

332 
“It is not an intervention or an installation. I would rather speak of device. Like an 
acupuncture for transit, travel, public, flâneur...”735 
The installation even included a section entitled “Terrain Vague” reflecting on 
such spaces and their relevance to interpretations of urban spaces. His aim in this 
installation, and many of his other works, is resistive against the hegemonic ways of 
seeing, built through control and power mechanisms; He denounces the role of mass 
media in such processes. In contrast, he views the terrain vague, as demonstrated in his 
installation, as productive and active spaces of connection and free interaction. 
Another   relevant   interdisciplinary   case:   Barron   and   Mariani’s   Terrain   Vague   
Another example of ventures that developed on the interdisciplinary theme of the 
terrain vague is an editorial effort by Patrick Barron and Manuela Mariani. In 2013, 
Barron and Mariani published a book dedicated to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague entitled 
Terrain Vague: Interstices at the Edge of the Pale736. The book is a collaborative attempt to 
reposition the notion of terrain vague and such indeterminate spaces in architectural 
thought and urban planning. The many authors in the book strive to expound on Solà-
Morales’s ideas and present some cases of projects and articles interested in terrains 
vagues. 
Manuela Mariani is an architect and the co-founder of the Italian firm Interdesign. 
She is linked to Italy and the United States, as she is for instance the Assistant Director of 
Foundation Studios at the Boston Architectural College. Patrick Barron on the other hand 
is an associate professor of English at the college of liberal arts, and the director of the 
professional writing program at the University of Massachusetts, Boston. His books 
include The Selected Poetry and Prose of Andrea Zanzotto and Italian Environmental 
Literature: An Anthology. His attention shifted in more recent years from an acute 
interest in English poetry and its history towards more interdisciplinary and 
contemporary themes such as wandering and wastelands. His latest publications link, as 
Solà-Morales suggests, the theme of terrain vague to that of cinematic and photography’s 
history737. Patrick Barron also wrote himself poems about spaces such as the terrain vague 
or surrounding themes, with titles such as “Abysses”, “Derivations” and “Vagaries”. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
735 Trans. from Spanish: ““No es una intervención ni una instalación. Yo hablaría más bien de dispositivo. 
Como una acupuntura: tránsito, recorrido, público, flãneur...” - Ibid. 
736 Mariani, Manuela, and Patrick Barron. Terrain Vague: Interstices at the Edge of the Pale. New York: 
Routledge, 2013. 
737 One of his latest publications: “Cinematic Space in Rome’s Disabitato: Between Metropolis and Terrain 
Vague in the Films of Fellini, Antonioni, and Pasolini.” (Co-authored with Manuela Mariani.) 
Modernism/modernity 18.2 (2011): 309-33.

## S. 336

333 
 
Barron and Mariani’s book finds its origins in Rome in 2006 where the two 
authors organized an interdisciplinary event linking cinema and architecture. Entitled 
“Rome: Architecture in Film”, it was primarily a film retrospective that presented how 
both fields display, describe, explain and recreate forms of inhabiting and living space. At 
the core of the event, and the published article following it, are the notions of terrain 
vague and urban interstices. The authors write: 
“From vacant lots and half-built overpasses, to shantytowns and collapsing farm houses, 
commonly recognizable monuments almost never enter the screen. Confirmation of location 
by reference to the city center is consistently and carefully denied.”738 
The authors were particularly focused, as Solà-Morales was, on mediums of 
imagery, mainly cinema and photography, and how these represent, portray, delineate 
and attempt at transforming the terrain vague. 
The editors organized the chapters of the book Terrain Vague: Interstices at the 
Edge of the Pale in three sections: Locations, Traversings, and Applications. The first 
section, Locations, is a series of studies on different types of terrains vagues around the 
world, linked to the initial reason of their existence. Some are leftover of economic crises 
or post-industrial transformations such as Detroit, San Francisco and Tokyo, whereas 
some others are results of war such as Bashoura in Beirut. The aim in this first section 
seems set on connecting origin with prospective intercessions, exploring in parallel the 
contextual socio-economic and political layers specific to each terrain vague evoked. The 
second section, Traversings, evoked more the transitory and fluctuating character of the 
terrain vague through projects and studies focused on this aspect. Time is also an element 
of transition in this section, to describe for instance transformations of a site in New York. 
The third section, Applications, is, as predicted, more focused on questioning some cases 
of intervention. These range from sustainable mediations, to a care for impoverished 
urban strata, to a consideration for memory and interconnections between socioeconomic layers. The three sections of the book have in common an interest in portraying 
new and contemporary cases of interpretation of the terrain vague from the last twenty 
years, and consequently new unorthodox modes for intervening on the urban fabric in 
general. On an important note, Solà-Morales’s essay is displayed separated from the book’s 
divisions and at the beginning of the book, to present a form of “guide post, not subsumed 
under any of the subheadings,”739 for the entirety of the essays and projects that follow. 
Moreover, Barron and Mariani’s choice of whom to include in their book appear 
to be very interdisciplinary. Essays from a range of fields are included, be they 
                                                                                                                                                                        
738 Mariani, Manuela, and Patrick Barron. “Cinematic Space in Rome’s Disabitato: Between Metropolis and 
Terrain Vague in the Films of Fellini, Antonioni, and Pasolini.” Modernism/modernity 18 (2011): 309–333. 
739 Whalen, Ken. “Book Review: ‘Terrain Vague: Interstices at the Edge of the Pale.’” Space and Polity 19.2 
(2015): 210–212.

## S. 337

334 
architecture, landscape, urbanism, literature, film studies or photography, mainly fields 
already evoked by Solà-Morales when defining the terrain vague. 
The book includes a multitude of studies and urban sites around the world, such as 
Berlin, Beirut, Detroit, Paris and Tokyo. The wastelands evoked a range of diversity from 
urban to rural zones, from small interstitial scales to large metropolitan. They are either 
transport related, succumbing to natural transformation, emerging or even under the 
threat of disappearing. One of the main contributions of the book was also to demonstrate 
clearly how the terrain vague is not an empty void waiting to be filled, but rather 
dynamic and multilayered spaces. What these presented cases seem to share is a general 
resistive attitude to classical forms of perception and representation of the urban and 
architectural fields. 
Moreover, Barron pushes the notion of terrain vague forward to contemporary 
times by evoking on the one hand currently known cases of new terrains vagues 
appearing in cities, and on the other hand the sustainability and biodiversity discourse 
linked to the terrain vague: 
“Various species of both common and rare animals and plants have also discovered 
"places of their own" in terrains vagues, which, over time, if left relatively undisturbed tend 
to gain ecological diversity. […] Terrains vagues also contribute to higher urban floristic 
diversity, acting as connective habitats and providing sources of plant species to colonize 
newly vacant sites.”740 
Barron and Mariani’s book is becoming fast a reference in the terrain vague 
discourse, often quoted in publications and works on the subject. 
Architectural, interstitial and ephemeral itinerary 
Another category of the essay’s influence is more directly related to architectural 
practices. The call for action in Solà-Morales’s essay is largely directed towards architects 
who refuse to be the colonizers of space and who opt, to preserve continuity in the urban 
fabric and focus on the ‘invisible powers’ of the city. It is an attitude that strips architects, 
at least partially, of their controlling powers, and opens alternative forms of intervening 
beyond the regular client-based format. 
This approach is linked with a secondary notion, which is important to discuss at 
this point: the interstitial. Following the terrain vague, the notion of interstitiality has 
been an emergent subject both in architecture and in the sciences to define vital networks 
within larger systems. This notion became relevant in the scientific realm in 1963. It has 
                                                                                                                                                                        
740 Barron, Patrick. “Introduction At the Edge of the Pale.” Terrain Vague: Interstices at the Edge of the Pale. 
New York: Routledge, 2013. 11.

## S. 338

335 
 
been repeatedly used to designate a vital intercellular system of communication in the 
human anatomy, one that removes waste as well as nurtures tissue. In physics and 
chemistry, the term has served in reference to compounds of atoms within metals or 
networks of fluids that infiltrate pores in rocks. Therefore, a prerequisite to understanding 
interstitiality constitutes looking into the effect of science on architecture and, 
conversely, the influence of architecture on science. Moreover, much of the more current 
interest in the terrain vague is oriented towards the interstitial scale and in-between 
spaces within the city. 
As previously mentioned in the first chapter, the topic of interstitiality in the 
terrain vague was inspirational within theoretical writings741 on architecture. It was also 
adopted as an approach to architectural practices, as advocated by Peter Eisenman who 
takes the notion as one of his main driving forces742. Interstitial space is not the planned 
void, but rather the unplanned leftover, either occupied or empty. It is what escaped the 
eye of the architect or planners and lingers in temporality until an event or a program is 
injected into it, by the community or the architect. In this context, interstitiality also 
implies movement, whether this comes from the dynamics of force, intensity, changes in 
flow or actual physical motion. The terrain vague viewed through the interstitial is not a 
stable entity; its boundaries are in expansion. The notion of interstitial is also often 
discussed as if it occupied a smaller scale than terrain vague. In addition, it is a concept 
often oriented towards action within the city. A terrain vague can be experienced 
(observed and interacted with) thanks to a theoretical understanding of the notion of 
interstitiality as the event of transition through architectural limits, the inversion of 
meanings caused by this event and the intensities and extensities that are produced by the 
same token. 
On the urban scale, the role of the terrain vague, as seen from an interstitial 
perspective, changes as the contemporary city changes. Interstices become linked with a 
vision of a city formed out of a network of inner fragments where the missing pieces are 
the terrain vague. They remind us that we constantly cross limits and inhabit intervals. 
The experience of noticing and accepting the place of the in-between within the urban 
fabric is as important as understanding the white parts within a black and white picture: 
one cannot visualize the content of the picture without taking into consideration the 
white areas. Interstitial spaces therefore underpin the continuum of urban scales and offer 
social support between city, neighborhood and building. They hence rarely function with 
                                                                                                                                                                        
741 Even Patrick Barron and Manuela Mariani, as seen before, name their book Terrain Vague: Interstices at 
the Edge of the Pale. 
742 As we had begun to observe in the first chapter of this thesis. - Eisenman, Peter. “Processes of the 
Interstitial.” Written into the Void: Selected Writings, 1990-2004. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2007.

## S. 339

336 
a scale of their own, but are clearly defined on a social level rather than on the 
architectonic scale. 
Consequently, architects who were inspired by the essay often took the direction 
of temporary, ephemeral or collaborative architecture, such as the Atelier d’Architecture 
Autogérée, Raumlabor, StudioBasar, Halil Dolan and Juliette Biess. Yet one name stands 
out as more relevant to the link between the terrain vague and interstitiality: the architect 
Luc Lévesque. 
The case of Luc Lévesque 
 
FIGURE 117: Luc Lévesque visiting Solà-Morales’s replica of the German Pavilion in Barcelona. December 
2013. (Photograph by Author). 
Luc Lévesque is an architect and professor in history and theory of architectural 
practices at Université Laval in Quebec City. His body of research traces the potentiality 
of an interstitial approach to urban landscape. His aim includes mainly social issues 
related to the theme of leftover and interstitial spaces, but also urban political matters 
along with an acute interest in activism through artistic interventions.

## S. 340

337 
 
In an interview with the author 743 , Lévesque attempts to differentiate the 
interstitial from the terrain vague. He tends to describe the terrain vague as a vague space 
too large and undefined to properly consider for intervention, while he uses the term 
interstitial to define in-between spaces within the city where intervention is more 
conceivable. Furthermore, Lévesque describes in the interview how he has been 
influenced by Solà-Morales’s work ever since he attended the latter’s lecture at the 
Anyplace conference in 1994. 
Lévesque is also co-founder of the Montreal-based atelier for urban exploration, 
entitled “SYN-”, which is a group of architecturally trained artists, including Jean-Maxime 
Dufresne and Jean-François Prost, who intervene in derelict spaces and terrains vagues 
through ephemeral actions. This platform was initiated in 2000 to promote 
interdisciplinary urban interventions and public workshops. The founders consider 
terrains vagues as a constellation of sites forming a vast rhizome within the city, like a 
“hyper-building”744. In line with Solà-Morales’s intentions, the atelier seems to build on 
the link between the terrain vague and various disciplines to create new forms of urban 
interventions. The atelier is growing progressively more international. Based in Montréal, 
its work has been featured, for instance, in Chicago, Rotterdam, Dakar and Paris. 
 
FIGURE 118: SYN- Infracampus workshops at the University of Guelph. Ontario, 2013. 
 
Resistance   through   continuity 
Since the 1970s, as Lévesque argues745, all was reduced to false dualism between 
designed objects and social uses. The missing elements in that puzzle were left-over spaces 
                                                                                                                                                                        
743 Interview between author and Luc Lévesque in Montreal. 13 Nov. 2013. 
744 “Nomad territory” is what the journal Inter calls terrain vague spaces, and descriptions are available on 
their platforms. 
745 Lévesque, Luc. “La Place Publique comme Constellation Interstitielle  : Parcours Historique et 
Expérimentations.” Ed. Jona Jébrak and Julien Barbara. Collection Cahiers de l’Institut du patrimoine de

## S. 341

338 
that do not serve the political agenda yet could very well serve a social scheme, without 
the need for fixed design intent. Lévesque’s work seems to be accordingly a development 
of a wave of resistance whose one of its sources is found in Solà-Morales’s description of 
the terrain vague. Solà-Morales’s essay encourages a view of the city through its forces 
and intensities and proposes to intervene in the terrain vague by developing continuity 
through these tensions. Lévesque adopts this stance in his writings and projects, and 
encourages a view on the city through this continuity of interstitial rhizomes and 
intensities. However, unlike Almarcegui’s projects, continuity is in the foreground of 
Lévesque’s projects as he intensifies through his interventions the links with local 
communities. In this sense, Lévesque transcends, through hybrid, interdisciplinary and 
case-specific methods, the idea of continuity proposed by Solà-Morales at the end of his 
essay. 
Relation   to   Solà-­‐Morales      
Lévesque already linked in 1994 the notion of nomad territories to that of the 
terrain vague, when he requested from Solà-Morales, as aforementioned, the permission 
to publish his essay within the Inter journal’s edition about the “nomad territory”746. In a 
letter to Solà-Morales in 1994, he stated: 
"The theme of the next issue of Inter is on territoriality and nomadic territories. [...] 
Your contribution would fit perfectly into that of the Frenchman François Seigneur 
(Ephemeral Habitat in the "terrains vagues" of the 20th arrondissement of Paris) and the 
young group Big City (nomadic house in the "holes" of Montreal).”747 
For this publication, Lévesque altered the “Terrain Vague” essay to suit the 
theme748, focusing more on terrains vagues as in-between spaces within the city, rather 
than large indeterminate space from without the city’s border; Lévesque is indeed 
interested in nomad spaces within the urban fabric. Moreover, in a letter addressed to 
Solà-Morales regarding this edition of Inter, Lévesque expands on his theoretical 
intentions regarding the terrain vague: 
“This query will take the form of a questioning on the "disintegration of past great 
utopias" and on the necessity "to live" this disintegration without nostalgia for the great 
modern gestures (increasingly difficult to produce in the Western economy) - In search of 
new monumental practices, realistic, deviant and impure, producing meaning from the 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
l’UQÀM (3) [texts presented at McGill University during the 74th Congress of ACFAS, may 2006] Les temps 
de l’espace public  : construction, transformation et utilisation (2008): 145–169. 
746 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Urbanité Interstitielle.” Trans. Luc Lévesque. Inter: art actuel 61 (1995): 27–28. 
747 Our trans. from French: “La thématique du prochain numero d'Inter est sur la territorialité et les territoires 
nomades. […] Votre contribution viendrait s'insérer parfaitement à celle du Français François Seigneur (Habitat 
éphémère dans les "terrains vagues" du 20e arrondissement de Paris) et du jeune groupe Big City (maison nomade 
dans les "trous" de Montréal).” - Correspondence between Luc Lévesque and Solà-Morales, 1994-1995. Ignasi 
de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Free Files. Letters. 
748 As aforementioned in the third chapter.

## S. 342

339 
 
heterogeneous ... an "arquitectura povera"? - Projects presented, Quebecois projects related to 
this issue including the recent redevelopment of the Biosphere of Buckminster Fuller (1967 
expo) and a presentation of François Seigneur (former collaborator of Nouvel) on "nomadic 
habitat" in the urban wastelands...”749 
 
FIGURE 119: Correspondence between Luc Lévesque and Solà-Morales, 1994-1995. Ignasi de Solà-Morales 
Private Archive - Free Files. Letters. 
Similar to Solà-Morales’s interests in defying utopian urban ideals, Lévesque’s 
interests are already in 1995 oriented towards more practical and “realistic” solutions to 
the terrain vague. In another letter addressed to Solà-Morales, Lévesque asks for 
illustrations especially in relation to the Alexanderplatz project described in the "Terrain 
Vague" essay. Lévesque argues for the importance of this case study since it is the only 
architectural and tangible example in the essay. Later, attached to his publication of Solà-
Morales’s essay in Inter, the essay is accompanied with one page written by Lévesque 
explaining the various phases of Alexanderplatz from a terrain vague perspective. 
Lévesque’s formulation of the terrain vague is thus on a much more practical level; he 
confers to the notion a practice-based focus. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
749 Our trans. from French: “Cette problématique prendra la forme d'un questionnement sur "la désintegration des 
"grandes utopis" passées et sur la nécessité "d'habiter" cette désintégration sans nostalgie pour les grands gestes 
modernes (de plus en plus difficile à produire dans l'économie occidentale) -- A la recherche de nouvelles pratiques 
monumentales, réalistes, déviantes et impures produisant du sens à partir de l'hétérogène… une "arquitectura 
povera"? - Projets présentés, projets Québecois touchant à cette problématique dont le récent réaménagement de la 
Biosphère de Buckminster Fuller (1967 expo) et une présentation de François Seigneur (ancien collaborateur de 
Nouvel) sur l'"habitat nomade" dans les friches urbaines...” – Correspondence between Luc Lévesque and 
Solà-Morales, 1994-1995. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - Free Files. Letters.

## S. 343

340 
Moreover, as with Solà-Morales, Lévesque himself has a list of publications around 
similar topics such as the terrain vague and interstitial spaces. He tackled the notion of 
interstitial from various perspectives such as social, architectural, political and economic, 
often taking Montreal as case study and more than often focused on actively intervening 
on such spaces. He wrote for instance his thesis entitled “Invention d'une paysagéité de 
l'interstitiel. Contribution du Discours Architectural Contemporain: le Parcours de Peter 
D. Eisenman (1963-1988)”750. He was interested in Eisenman’s work on the interstitial. 
Similar to Solà-Morales, he linked in his thesis, since the prelude, the terrain vague to its 
cinematic and photographic connections751. Accordingly, his teaching also revolves around 
contemporary theories between architecture, landscape and urbanism. 
Developing   a   discourse   beyond   the   terrain   vague      
In the same interest of developing on the notion of the terrain vague, Lévesque 
published in 2009 an article in Territorio entitled “Towards an Interstitial Approach to 
Urban Landscape”752. In this essay, most of Lévesque’s intentions are stated and defined. 
His general approach to the interstitial and the terrain vague seems to be summarized in a 
paragraph: 
“If several conceptual directions opened up in the 50s and 60s are still pertinent today, 
the idealism that motivated them on various levels hardly seems possible any longer. Utopian 
visions for radical change have to be replaced by projects that pragmatically take on the 
paradoxes of our societies and act on existing conditions in micropolitical ways. In following 
this thread, the interstitial landscape constitutes a resource for experimentation and, in itself, 
an experiment. It calls for alternative and complementary approaches to urban intervention 
that focus less on imposing order than on inflecting the existing dynamics with a tact similar 
to the one of acupuncture.”753 
Indeed, the idea of microintervention is the running theme of the essay and 
Lévesque’s recurrent approach to the terrain vague. Lévesque first bases himself in the 
article on the ideas of anthropologist Marc Augé’s on the “non-place” to define what he 
understands as interstitial constellation. He confronts Augé’s “non-place” to architecture 
theoretician Christian Norberg-Schulz’s ‘loss of place’. He discusses then the latter’s 
argument that the idea of ‘dwelling’ is inevitably assimilated with images of protection 
and safety. In contrast, he urges: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
750 Lévesque, Luc. Invention d’une Paysagéité de l’Interstitiel: Contribution du discours architectural 
contemporain  : le parcours de Peter D. Eisenman (1963-1988). Université de Montréal, 2007. 
Indeed, Lévesque was also inspired by Peter Eisenman, who is particularly interesting in this thesis due his 
relations to Solà-Morales and being a co-founder of Any. 
751 In this respect, he evoked on several occasions Wim Wenders’s film Der Himmel über Berlin. 
752 Lévesque, Luc. “Towards an Interstitial Approach to Urban Landscape.” Ed. Franco Angeli. Territorio 
(2009): 77–82. 
753 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid.

## S. 344

341 
 
 “to give up the privileged relationship that links the dwelling with the image of 
protection associated with archetypical places; just as one must resist the temptation to 
categorize place and non-place a priori.”754 
Consequently and more importantly, he presents the notions of place and nonplace as inseparable and necessary polarities: 
“In the ‘spacing’ opened up by this polarization one will find a catalyst for new attitudes, 
new combinations of ‘dwelling’ concerned with the implications of our present condition. 
The ‘spacing’ in question involves practices, processes and temporalities. It is no longer 
enough to dream of the place or to passively criticize the non-place or generic space. It is in 
the strategic zone between these poles – the ‘mi-lieu’ – that impure and elusive hybrids are to 
be imagined, created and activated.”755 
Thus the call for action and a reverse of perspective towards the non-place is at 
hand. Lévesque proposes then to revere the terrain vague; to go beyond precodified forms 
of dwelling and seek to reinvent it within these spaces of maximum potential. 
Lévesque therefore already developed in 1995 on the theme of terrain vague by 
writing on adjacent subjects such as the nomad and the interstitial. Since the 1990s until 
now, Lévesque presented various articles on the interstitial, among which is “La Place 
Publique comme Constellation Interstitielle  : Parcours Historique et Expérimentations”756. 
In this article the notion of terrain vague and the interstitial are intimately linked. 
Another example of such articles is titled “Habitats Nomades: Derrière les Palisades”757, in 
which an attempt is at hand to raise the reader’s awareness of the political and real estate 
mechanisms that cause gentrification and the creation of terrains vagues. The author 
denounces the political agenda of Paris at the time and calls for action against putting the 
nomadic urban out of the city’s confines. This call for action was based on a strategy built 
around making the terrains vagues of Paris more visible and accessible to the lower strata 
of the population who had no longer place in the center of the city. The notion of 
nomadism is also already present when he published another article in Inter titled “De 
l’État des Lieux”758 in which the main running theme is urban nomadism and a study of an 
interdisciplinary artistic project named “Manoeuvre Nomade”. 
Then, in 1999, Lévesque published an article in Inter Journal entitled “Le Terrain 
Vague Comme Monument”759. The article, as in Solà-Morales’s essay, reintroduces the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
754 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid. 
755 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid. 
756 Lévesque, Luc. “La Place Publique comme Constellation Interstitielle  : Parcours Historique et 
Expérimentations.” Ed. Jona Jébrak and Julien Barbara. Collection Cahiers de l’Institut du patrimoine de 
l’UQÀM (3) [texts presented at McGill University during the 74th Congress of ACFAS, may 2006] Les temps 
de l’espace public  : construction, transformation et utilisation (2008): 145–169. 
757 Lévesque, Luc, François Seigneur, and Wanda B. Campbell. “Habitats Nomades  : Derrière Les Palissades.” 
Inter: art actuel 61 (1995): 29–32. 
758 Lévesque, Luc. “De l’État des Lieux.” Inter: art actuel (1995): 35-43. 
759 Trans. : “The Terrain Vague as Monument”

## S. 345

342 
terminologies and their signifiers, accentuating the paradoxical combination of ‘terrain’ 
and ‘vague’ as the main attracting point of this designation unlike other adjacent 
terminologies that refer to wastelands. As in Solà-Morales’s, flux, territory, nomadism and 
trajectory are the driving elements of the article. Moreover, as for Solà-Morales, some 
authors are of importance to the arguments: Gilles Deleuze, Félix Guattari and Paul 
Virilio760. In fact, a section of the article follows Solà-Morales’s intent on linking the 
terrain vague with Deleuze’s761 notion of rhizomes and continuity. The article is also 
focused on occidental cities, describing the development of cities from the organizational 
efforts of Pope Sixte Quint at the end of the 16th century, to Le Corbusier’s Charte 
d’Athènes and the speculative urbanization since the 1960s. Lévesque confronts these 
organizational powers to the power of the terrain vague. Once again, the terrains vagues 
are described as elements of resistance escaping the productive and organizational systems 
of the cities, “blurring the clarity of the urban figure”762. In the same lines of hope, the 
author urges for a celebration of the qualities of the terrain vague as liberating from 
preordained pictures and representations of the cities and of space. 
Lévesque moreover develops on the theme763 by advocating a link between the 
terrain vague and the resurgence of approaches towards ‘renaturalizing’ the city. The issue 
of sustainability and intervention in the terrain vague become thus intertwined. 
Moreover, Lévesque proposes the terrain vague as an interstitial space within the city, an 
in-between that confronts us from within to the idea of the frontier, “at the heart of the 
crevices that the erosion of the urban centers induce”764; an idea of frontier, but also of 
wilderness from within the city, especially in the postindustrial condition. In this sense, 
and keeping the idea of the wild and the uncontrollable in mind, Lévesque finally 
advocates the futility of attempts at preserving the terrain vague, considering those as 
unattainable horizons that are bound to constantly disappear and reemerge within the 
city’s fabric. He proposes instead to conceive the terrain vague as a rhizomatic monument, 
linked to a multitude of potential connections. The purpose of such a monument would 
be to reactivate the awareness, sensibility and the conscience of a jaded “society of 
spectacle” 765 . The final attention is thus set on raising awareness of the urban 
contemporary condition through its terrains vagues: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
760 Guy Debord was also a principal influence on Lévesque’s work in relation to notions of “non-place”, “nonsens” (fr.) and limits. He even wrote in 1994 the article ”Guy Ernest Debord : Paris 1931 – 1994” in Inter as 
Debord died that year. 
761 Lévesque’s interest in Deleuze’s thought is already visible in previous articles of his, such as in his 1992 
article in Inter “Architex : pour une pulvérisation des enclaves; essai de croisement architectonique, 
philosophique et artistique”, in which the author’s final argument is quoting Deleuze and Guattari’s book 
Thousand Plateaux. 
762 Lévesque, Luc. “Le Terrain Vague Comme Monument.” Inter: art actuel 72 (1999): 27–30. 
763 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid. 
764 Trans. from French : “au coeur des anfractuosités que signe l'érosion des centres urbains. ” – Ibid. 
765 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid.

## S. 346

343 
 
“It is a matter of immersing oneself in order to proliferate the micro-resistances that will 
put a little sand in the discursive mechanics of exclusion and ‘techno-economic progress’."766 
The intention of raising awareness of the terrain vague as an intrinsic and vital 
element of the urban fabric is thus manifested through this interdisciplinary approach. 
Lévesque’s writings on the interstitial and the terrain vague include moreover an 
architectural historical layer partially missing in Solà-Morales’s essay. While Solà-Morales 
only mentions Mies van der Rohe as a tangible example, Lévesque cites often various 
architects and urbanists as predecessors to the terrain vague mindset. He situates the 
notion within an ongoing timeline going as early as the beginning of the 20th century. He 
links it to Einstein’s theory of relativity of space-time, opens a new way of thinking space 
beyond the utopias of the time, to Giedion then Aldo van Eyck, to a new sensibility and 
an interest in looking beyond ideals, to a very site-sensitive perspective, an “interstitial 
perspective” 767. Beyond Giedion and Le Corbusier, Lévesque explains the interstitial 
within a second phase revolving around a critique of the “Modern urban space”768 at the 
end of the 1960s and throughout the 1970s, with two main references: Camillo Sitte and 
the brothers Léon and Rob Krier. As a third level, Lévesque proceeds with his history of 
the interstitial towards the second part of the 20th century, with two other references: 
Aldo Rossi and Colin Rowe769. And, in parallel with this elaborated historical storyline, 
Lévesque presents his own experimentations, within his atelier SYN- and different 
workshops, as a contemporary example on how to interpret and intervene in the terrain 
vague and the interstitial. In this respect, he often suggested modes of action and 
intervention in the terrain vague focused mainly on the idea of continuity, evidently 
inspired by Solà-Morales. 
Lévesque’s work seems often aimed at proposing architectural and urban modes of 
action on the terrain vague, by developing unconventional directions that combined art, 
architecture and urbanism, but also other fields. He is also more interested in in-between 
spaces within the city that can accommodate often-temporary interventions. In 
collaboration with his co-founded atelier “SYN-”, one project in Montreal for instance 
consisted of Ping-Pong tables installed in interstitial spaces. Another of his projects 
entailed picnic tables inserted in the terrains vagues of Paris. Similar to Solà-Morales, the 
aim was to attract attention from inhabitants to the value and possibilities inherent in 
these spaces. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
766 Our trans. from French: “Il s'agit de s'y immiscer pour faire proliférer les micro-résistances qui mettront 
un peu de sable dans les mécaniques discursives de l'exclusion et du « progrès techno-économique » à tout 
crins.” – Lévesque, Luc. Ibid. 
767 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid. 
768 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid. - Trans. from French: “Critique de ‘l’espace urbain moderne’ ” 
769 Coinciding with Solà-Morales’s period, the last two references were colleagues to Solà-Morales.

## S. 347

344 
Lévesque considers therefore the terrains vagues as a constellation of sites forming 
a vast rhizome within the city. The sociologist Andrea Brighenti, in his book Urban 
Interstices 770 , describes how Lévesque reflects on public space as an interstitial 
constellation. He also describes his approach as bridging the gap between sociologists and 
urban theorists since his methods invite urbanists to study more carefully practices 
occurring in public space. Lévesque’s agenda is thus both social, as related to the theme of 
leftover spaces, as well as political. Following Solà-Morales’s advocacy, he attempts at 
creating new pseudo-architectural forms of urban intervention. 
 
FIGURE 120: SYN-. Hypothèses d’Amarrages. Montreal, 2001-2008. Picnic tables inserted in the terrain vague. 
In   relation   to   Montreal’s   terrains   vagues   
One of Lévesque’s essays more important to the terrain vague is “Montréal, 
L’Informe Urbanité des Terrains Vagues.”771 The author first presents Montreal as the most 
appropriate city to describe the theoretical interactions behind the terrain vague. He 
defines the city through its vague and indeterminate spaces and points at its uniqueness as 
an accumulation of singularities. Basing himself on Deleuze’s thought and Montreal’s 
urban history, he puts in emphasis the accidental character of Montreal’s urbanity, where 
“accident becomes itself durable.”
772 He correlates the “savage and heteroclite 
informality”773 of the city to its active and cosmopolitan population that inhabits its 
terrains vagues occupying artistically and culturally every available vacant space and 
                                                                                                                                                                        
770 Brighenti, Andrea Mubi, ed. Urban Interstices the Aesthetics and the Politics of the in-Between. Farnham, 
Surrey, England: Ashgate Pub. Limited, 2013. 163-183. 
771 Lévesque, Luc. “Montréal, L’Informe Urbanité des Terrains Vagues.” Les Annales de la Recherche Urbaine 
85 (1999): 47–57. 
772 Ibid. - Luc Lévesque quoting Deleuze’s Francis Bacon, Logique de la Sensation. 
773 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid.

## S. 348

345 
 
corner. Lévesque argues that the violent transformations of the 1960s and 1970s 
contributed most in catalyzing an effervescent resistance against the tabula rasa, 
promoting whenever possible a reappropriation of the city and its memory. An “urban 
patchwork”774, Lévesque quotes again Deleuze to describe Montreal’s urbanity as an 
underground that repeatedly resurges, terrains vagues that constantly destabilize the 
urban status quo. The overall experience of Montreal becomes thus an urban collage of 
heterogeneous terrains vagues and interstitiality, conveying to the city its overall wellknown cosmopolitan character. 
Lévesque develops further in his essays the particular character of Montreal’s 
terrains vagues through an approach seldom adopted by others who worked on the 
notion. He proceeds by categorizing the terrains vagues of Montreal, and by doing so 
contrasts the various terminologies used to describe these: interstitial, terrain vague, 
‘friche’. He also allocates qualities to these categories: the small interstitial spaces, “dent 
creuse”775, within the cities as more ‘néfaste’ (adverse) than the terrains vagues on the 
outskirts. The terrain vague seems according to him better accepted by the general public, 
conveying a sense of romantic dérive; and when pervaded by vegetation colonies, the 
term ‘friche’ applies, which is arguably the most embraced by the local public opinion776. 
Lévesque describes Montreal’s imposing bands of terrains along railways as the best 
example of such ‘friches’, used by the neighboring communities. 
Lévesque moreover pushes further Solà-Morales’s last intentions of implied 
activism and elaborates on modes of intervening in Montreal’s terrains vagues. Coinciding 
with the same year of Solà-Morales’s essay, Lévesque argues 1994 to be the year in which 
administrative forces of Montreal became particularly interested in the terrain vague 
discourse, particularly the case of Sainte-Catherine Street, a main artery of the city 
perforated by many terrains vagues. The festive character of this street induced an 
innovative and unconventional approach towards its terrains vagues that Lévesque seems 
to applaud: 
“The advantage of limiting the layout of the site to a minimum to maximize its capacity 
for temporal variation and the great potential offered for this purpose by the fluctuating use 
of urban furniture. [...] An event-based punctuation which, by intensifying the existing 
sensitive data, confers, by the multiplicity of its variants and the random intermittence of its 
appearance, a new value to the interstice. It is thus a question here of exploiting the 
mutations of the urban substrate and the overlap of its different temporalities.” 777 
                                                                                                                                                                        
774 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid. 
775 Lévesque, Luc. Ibid. 
776 These variations in terminologies were already described in the first chapter of this thesis. 
777 Our trans. from French: “L’intérêt de limiter l’aménagement du site au minimum pour maximiser sa capacité 
de variation temporelle et la grande potentialité offerte à cet effet par l’usage fluctuant du mobilier urbain. […] 
Une ponctuation événementielle qui en intensifiant les données sensibles existantes, confère, de par la multiplicité 
de ses variantes et l’intermittence aléatoire de son apparition, une valeur nouvelle à l’interstice. Il s’agit donc ici

## S. 349

346 
Seasonal changes, cultural manifestations, artistic interventions, the accent is on 
the temporality of space and on ephemeral actions. 
Terrain   vague   experimentation   through   projects   
In the same essay, “Montréal, L’Informe Urbanité des Terrains Vagues” 778 , 
Lévesque goes once again beyond a mere call for action, where Solà-Morales’s essay 
finished. He presents examples, in art and his own work, in which his theoretical 
arguments and proposals are experimented with. One of these examples is Hypothèses 
d’Amarrages (Moorings hypothesis) project, ongoing since May 2001 in Montreal. 
Another example is the Hypothèses d’Insertions project (Insertions hypothesis) 
experimented by SYN- since 2002 in various contexts (Gatineau, 2002; Montreal, 2006, 
2008; Paris, 2007) and which continued the research begun with Hypothèses d’Amarrages 
and modified its parameters.   
 
FIGURE 121: SYN-. Hypothèses D’insertions III. Gatineau, Ottawa, 2002. Soccer tables inserted in the terrain vague. 
Lévesque’s projects within the SYN- 779 are also evoked in one of his articles 
entitled “Le Jeu comme ‘Interstice Social’”780 in which the social layer in Lévesque’s 
thinking and experiments is better affirmed. Lévesque bases his theoretical argumentation 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
d’exploiter les mutations du substrat urbain et le chevauchement de ses différentes temporalités.” – Lévesque, 
Luc. Ibid (p. 55). 
778 Lévesque, Luc. “Montréal, L’Informe Urbanité des Terrains Vagues.” Les Annales de la Recherche Urbaine 
85 (1999): 47–57. 
779 Atelier d’exploration urbaine SYN- was organized by Jean-Maxime Dufresne, Luc Lévesque et Jean-
François Prost. 
780 Lévesque, Luc. “Le Jeu comme ‘Interstice Social’, À Propos d’une Expérience d’Intervention Ludique à

## S. 350

347 
 
in the essay, and a physical intervention in the suburban neighborhood of La Chapelle in 
Paris, on the theories of Debord regarding the notion of the game, “the society of 
spectacle”, and the “construction of situations”781. His approach being thus inspired by the 
Situationist International, he evokes Attila Kotanyi and Raoul Vaneigem, members of the 
SI, to explain the idea of ‘living together’ as a condition to be constructed and 
experimented with. He then moves to more recent references with Nicolas Bourriaud and 
Benoit Goetz to argument how, by creating microcosms of games, the interstitial becomes 
the most proper space to catalyze social urban intercultural exchanges and interactions782. 
Thus basing himself on a theoretical call for using games as an urban strategy of 
intervention in the interstitial, Lévesque presents La Chapelle project he and SYN-
