contributed in 2007, based on an invitation by the interdisciplinary research group “Art, 
Architecture et Paysage”783. Following a theme of urban temporary interstices, soccer 
tables (babyfoot) were inserted in this relatively poor Parisian neighborhood with weak 
social interactions. What is most interesting in Lévesque and his colleagues’ interventions 
in the interstitial is the immediacy of reward. Positivity and active involvement is directly 
visible. Intercultural social interactions took directly place throughout this experiment in 
Paris. 
Following   Lévesque   
Lévesque was also quoted by others who wrote on the terrain vague. As an 
example, at the Val de Marne Institute of Urbanism in Paris, ethnographer Stéphane 
Tonnelat wrote his doctoral thesis entitled “Urban Interstices”784, focusing on four residual 
spaces between Paris and New York. Lévesque is already mentioned in the introductory 
note, particularly thanked for his intellectual contribution. Then in the thesis, Tonnelat 
defines the interstitial and the terrain vague by referring to Lévesque’s work on Sainte-
Catherine Street785, as an exemplary case of studying terrains vagues as urban interstices 
with their own qualities and valuable presence in the city. Tonnelat also argues that 
Lévesque’s work is one to follow for urban renewal designs through careful attention to 
the invisible uses and needs, satisfied by the terrain vague and unmet elsewhere in the 
city. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
781 Debord, Guy. Society of the Spectacle. Detroit: Black & Red, 1983. 
782 Evidently, Deleuze and Guattari also reappear as a guiding reference, as in many of Lévesque’s writing. 
The authors are here quoted in relation to their notion of molecular interactions and networking in the 
urban dwelling. 
783 Trans.: “ Art, Architecture and Landscape. ” – This research is part of the interdisciplinary research 
program "Art, Architecture and Landscape" of the French Ministries of Culture and Equipment. 
784 Tonnelat, Stéphane. “Interstices Urbains Paris - New York, Entre Contrôles et Mobilités, Quatre Espaces 
Résiduels de L’aménagement.” Université Paris XII – Val de Marne Institut d’Urbanisme de Paris - CUNY 
Graduate School, 2003. 
785 Described in Lévesque’s article “Montréal, L'Informe Urbanité des Terrains Vagues".

## S. 351

348 
Similarly, architect Kurt Ryan McGrane quoted Lévesque, as well as Solà-Morales, 
in his Master Thesis “Landscapes, Architecture”, in which the main theme, much like 
Lévesque’s thesis, is on indeterminate landscapes and the notion of folding space. He also 
attempts at defining the terrain vague through Solà-Morales’s essay and Lévesque’s 
writings, quoting the latter’s article “The ‘Terrain Vague’ as Material- Some 
Observations.” He finally states: 
“Terrain Vague embraces the interstitial. The value of interstitial spaces is through their 
ability to provide linkages. They are spaces within the city which are at the same time rural 
and urban, past and present, transportation and recreation, familiar and surprising. These 
spaces have the ability to become linkages between different physical parts of the city as well 
as provide temporal linkages of past, present, and future. Similar to the Folding concept of 
‘smoothing’, designing with the ideas of Terrain Vague in mind could produce an 
indeterminate design, capable of responding to the changing growth patterns and perceptions 
of the future city. It is flexible.”786 
The potential charged in the terrain vague seems to also excite McGrane. 
 
FIGURE 122: “Micro-Interventions”. Journal Inter 120. Spring 2015. Front cover. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
786 McGrane, Kurt Ryan. “Landscapes, Architecture.” Thesis. Montana State University - Bozeman, College of 
Arts & Architecture, 2010. 27-28.

## S. 352

349 
 
Lévesque was therefore influential, in his turn, on other theoreticians who wrote 
on the subject of terrain vague. Even José Morales in his article “Terrain Vague”787 of 1996, 
refers to Luc Lévesque’s work when discussing the practices on intermediary spaces: 
“The attempt to coordinate the porous, dissolved areas of the city and their marginal 
social conditions, often inherent in these places, is the central axis of the presentation 
presented by Luc Lévesque. Festivities, play and circulation would thus be among the 
catalysts of an authentic laboratory of urban practices. From contradictory results, the 
experiments carried out in the Saint-Roch district, Quebec's real industrial heartland until 
1960, show this alternative. The invitation is thus launched to "uncover" urban hybrid praxis, 
adapted to what this neighborhood in this marginal area is.” 788 
José Morales praises Lévesque’s approach to the terrain vague as a singular and 
ideal way of approaching such spaces 789. In the aforementioned Saint-Roch project, 
Lévesque calls for using architectural disintegration and dereliction as an urban 
integration technique, while accentuating the importance of preserving memory and 
continuity as key focus on handling the terrains vagues. Through hybrid, interdisciplinary 
and case-specific methods, Lévesque transcends the idea of continuity proposed by Ignasi 
de Solà-Morales at the end of his essay. 
Lévesque thus reflects the values of a wave of architects and urbanists who are 
similarly interested in the “attention to continuity” call in Solà-Morales’s essay. Their 
work is often a bottom-up approach exploring the concealed potential of the terrain 
vague. Their interventions in these spaces are chiefly temporary structures, ephemeral 
programs and collaborative projects. Lévesque’s interstitial as an intervention tool is just 
one of the directions that this approach can take. The following section looks at how yet 
others have followed different directions for interpreting how Solà-Morales meant to 
comprehend and intervene in the terrain vague; in these interpretations, the collaborative 
character of the architect’s role is foregrounded. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
787 Morales, José. “Terrain Vague.” Quaderns d’arquitectura i urbanisme 214 Forum Internacional 2 (1996): 
164–171. 
788 Our trans. from French: “La tentative d'accorder les zones poreuses, dissoutes, de la ville et leurs conjonctures 
sociales marginales, souvent inhérentes à ces lieux, représente l'axe central de l'exposé présenté par Luc Lévesque. 
Les festivités, le jeu, la circulation figureraient ainsi parmi les catalyseurs d'un authentique laboratoire de pratiques 
urbaines. De résultats contradictoires, les expériences menées dans le quartier de Saint-Roch, véritable Coeur 
industriel du Québec jusqu'en 1960, manifestent cette alternative. L'invitation est donc lancée de à "dé-couvrir” des 
praxis urbaines hybrides, adaptées à ce qu'est ce quartier dans cette zone marginale.” – Ibid. 
789 Moreover, similar to Lévesque and Ignasi de Solà-Morales, the author in this relies on Deleuze’s and 
Guattari’s thought to argument the rhizomatic nature of the terrains vagues within the city, an idea often 
recurring when discussing the terrains vagues as a network of ephemeral spaces within the city. – Ibid.

## S. 353

350 
Other relevant architectural case studies 
Parc   Ephémère   and   Parc   des   Festivals   
Another project in Montreal in 1995 follows a similar modus operandi. According 
to Lévesque, Gilles Bissonnette’s “Parc Éphémère” constitutes an interesting prototype of 
such interventions: 
“The lack of formality of the intervention encourages the collective appropriation which 
will spontaneously become for a time an unprecedented popular agora.”790 
In the same sprit, in 1996, another comparable project is staged by the city, the Parc des 
Festivals: 
“This modest arrangement, consisting of an immense quadrilateral of stone dust, a 
diagonal of asphalt and a minimal vegetal framework, is a suitable support to serve a "public 
place culture" which, in Montreal, as noted by the architect Jacques Rousseau, "is founded 
more in the event than in the duration". The furniture constitutes here, more than any 
permanent formalization, the key figure in a typically Montreal way of appropriating the 
transitory.”791 
Thus the proposed intervention method is by nature modest; no ostentatious 
action is requested, rather a light inflexion in modes of representation and practices. 
Spontaneity, temporality and improvisation are key triggers in the proposed intervention 
modes; the relativity of time and space takes on here another meaning. Any 
organizational intent is hence set on minimizing physical transformation, while 
maximizing potential for proactive involvement and appropriation by the various 
stakeholders, the inhabitants of the city and the community.   
                                                                                                                                                                        
790 Our trans. from French: “L’absence de formalité de l’intervention encourage l’appropriation collective qui 
deviendra spontanément pour un temps un agora populaire sans precedent.” – Lévesque, Luc. “Montréal, 
L’Informe Urbanité des Terrains Vagues.” Les Annales de la Recherche Urbaine 85 (1999): 47–57 (p.55). 
791 Our trans. from French: “Cet aménagement modeste, composé d’un immense quadrilatère de poussière de 
pierre, d’une diagonale d’asphalte et d’un encadrement végétal minimal constitue un support approprié pour servir 
une « culture de la place publique » qui, à Montréal, comme le remarquait l’architecte Jacques Rousseau, « se fonde 
plus dans l’événement que dans la durée ». Le mobilier constitue ici, beaucoup plus que toute formalisation 
pérenne, la figure clef d’une manière typiquement montréalaise de s’approprier le transitoire.” – Lévesque, Luc. 
Ibid. 55.

## S. 354

351 
 
 
 
FIGURE 123: Marché Des Possibles. Montréal, June 2014. (Photographs by Author)

## S. 355

352 
 
 
FIGURE 124: Champs Des Possibles. Montréal, July 2014. Terrain vague celebrated through art happenings 
and other events. (Photographs by Author).

## S. 356

353 
 
Carole   Lévesque   
One article in Barron and Mariani’s book Terrain Vague792 is worth of particular 
mention due to its author: architect Carole Lévesque793. Her work is often associated with 
the notion of the terrain vague, she is also interested in temporary architecture on such 
spaces as a means for alternative and exploratory discourses on the city. Her article 
“Welcome to Bashoura, or the Found City as Interstice” explores the creative potential of 
the everyday in the terrains vagues of Beirut: “where property ownership is usually obscure 
and modes of occupation are generally unscripted, nonetheless harbour established communities 
and offer an alternative reading on the transformation of cities.”794 
Carole Lévesque spent a 3-year period of observing and participating in the 
informal life of one of Beirut’s terrains vagues. The outcome was five nomadic 
infrastructures through which one can read alternatives within the urban fabric, 
improvisation in its uses and finally the emergence of unexpected narratives: 
“These infrastructures, while decidedly grounded in their social and physical conditions, 
grow to be invented places, machine-like rooms without definite locations and propose that 
within the banal, within overlooked elements and situations, lie possible re-readings of our 
cities and of our ways of inhabiting.”795 
Lévesque’s article is compelling because it projects her theoretical work towards a 
proposal for intervention. Her approach follows carefully Solà-Morales’s call for 
continuity in the last section of the “Terrain Vague” essay. The infrastructure she proposes 
is temporary, site specific, custom made to fit the social and physical conditions in which 
they are grounded. No alien or colonizing approach is visible. The intervention appears 
more anthropological in spirit than architectural as she actively immersed herself for 
three years on site in the Bashoura terrain vague in Beirut and proposed interventions 
accordingly. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
792 Mariani, Manuela, and Patrick Barron. Terrain Vague: Interstices at the Edge of the Pale. New York: 
Routledge, 2013. 
793 Not to be confused with Luc Lévesque, the Canadian professor at LAVAL. Carole Lévesque holds a 
technical degree in architecture, a degree in Design de l’Environnement, a Masters of Architecture from the 
University of British-Columbia as well as a PhD in the History and Theory of Architecture from the 
University of Montreal. 
794 Lévesque, Carole. “Welcome to Bachoura, or the Found City as Interstice.” Terrain Vague: Interstices at 
the Edge of the Pale. Ed. Patrick Barron and Manuela Mariani. New York: Routledge, 2013. 33–48. 
795 Lévesque, Carole. Ibid.

## S. 357

354 
 
FIGURE 125: Lévesque, Carole. Her office, documenting and photographing terrains vagues. Montréal, 2016. 
(Photograph by Author). 
 
Atelier   d’Architecture   Autogérée   
An approach also worthy of mentioning here is that of the interaction architectuser-inhabitant. In this mode, the architect has a significant role on the creative as well as 
the organizational level. It is an innovative creative process where roles of authorship are 
shared and thus challenged. A good example of such interference can be the project 
entitled ‘Eco-Interstice’ realized in 2006 by the French architectural firm Atelier 
d’Architecture Autogérée in East of Paris, St. Blaise area. This project is an attempt by 
architects to collaborate with the local government structures as well as the inhabitants of 
the area, the local organizations and a professional association specialized in running 
training programs in eco-construction. The aim is to explore the potential of transforming 
the intermediary space of the plot 56 into a space collectively self-managed. The structure 
has the quality of an unfinished project with the potential for further construction, “the 
construction site becoming itself a social and cultural act.”796 Such a project is situated 
more on the liminal rather than in the interstitial. As they state: 
                                                                                                                                                                        
796 Atelier d’Architecture Autogérée. “Le 56 / Eco-Interstice.” UrbanTactics. Web. 22 Aug. 2015.

## S. 358

355 
 
“The project has an important take on the notion of proximity and active borders. 
Neighborhood walls transform the boundaries of the site into interactive devices, which 
rather than separating, multiply exchange and connections.”797 
A strong interest in keeping the interstitial open for change and interpretation is 
withheld while a good establishment of the liminal is set to define the formal boundaries 
that are thus relatively fixed and ready to receive input. Moreover, liminality is 
accentuated, as the entire project seems to revolve around the notion of passage: a 
corridor of activities between buildings of a community, while still keeping the vagueness 
of the edge or the threshold.    
Arte/Cidade   
Another example of interventions on the terrain vague comes from an initiative to 
reinterpret urban space under the question ‘How to intervene in megacities?’. Arte/Cidade 
is a sequel of four urban installations that were implemented on the city of São Paulo, 
Brazil since 1994. The focus was on notions of globalization and global reconfigurations of 
economy, power and art. The projects consisted of strategic alternatives for the city’s 
global restructuring: reactivation of certain spaces in order to decentralize urban politics. 
All four of them took place in terrains vagues but from different points of view: an 
inactive slaughterhouse for ‘City without Windows’ to discuss ‘thickness’ in 1994, two 
left-out buildings for ‘City and its fluxes’ to discuss flux and changes of scale, a railway 
station and an industrial plan for ‘City and its Stories’ to discuss fragmentation and the 
post-industrial future, and a neglected industrial area for ‘East Zone’ in 2002 to debate on 
reoccupation of such spaces by informal illegal users. This condition of preexistent 
structure was inherently present in the intervention decision as a potential to approach 
the city fabric. The subjects dealt with are directly related to notions of threshold, 
transition, flux and context on one side and notions of program, quality, dwelling and 
reuse on the other. Moreover, there was a plurality of actors in these projects plus a 
variety of interpretations and points of views held around them. The outcome is a wellrounded work focusing on the broader understanding of what the terrain vague could 
entail. 
Raumlabor   
Another relevant case of analogous architectural interventions is the Berlin-based 
group of architects Raumlabor. They were for instance invited by Storefront to work on a 
project entitled ‘Spacebuster’ in New York in 2009, which opens and explores the 
possibilities of the terrain vague for collective use. It is interesting because it includes 
                                                                                                                                                                        
797 Ibid.

## S. 359

356 
construction aspects that require the presence of architects and engineers, yet it is a 
mobile project. It is an inflatable balloon-shaped space that protrudes from a van and can 
fit up to 80 people. People enter the bubble through the passenger’s door of the van 
walking through to the back down a ramp right into the inflated space. The bubble is 
supported by air pressure generated by a fan underneath the ramp. The membrane of the 
bubble is translucent so people on the inside can see schematically what’s going on 
outside and vice versa. So the membrane acts as a semi-permeable border. The architect 
and artist hence play a role of initiator: they offer a place for gathering, displace it within 
different interstices of the city accentuating thus the potential of the latter and invite 
people to come and make use of the new found space within the intermediary spaces. As a 
consequence, this mobile device creates, on the one hand, ephemeral spaces that do not 
hold formal connotations related to the surrounding, and, on the other hand, the outcome 
becomes a space within space, disregarding the relation to the limits of the external space 
and hindering the transitional entry phase to the entrance door of the van. 
 
FIGURE 126: Raumlabor. Spacebuster. New York, 2009.

## S. 360

357 
 
Architecture activism itinerary 
Solà-Morales’s essay ends on a very subtle note urging more active involvement of 
architectural practice in dealing with the terrain vague, though not necessarily implying 
physical intervention in such spaces. The essay could be considered in this respect as a 
subtle hint towards a manifesto. As a consequence, many quote Solà-Morales as an 
inspirational figure who, at least subtly, encouraged towards architectural activism, 
especially activism on an urban scale. Along similar lines, the call for action in Solà-
Morales’s essay is also sometimes interpreted as an attempt to raise awareness of the 
importance of the terrain vague in the urban fabric. Moreover, the mode of action 
proposed by Solà-Morales, i.e. the “attention to continuity”, is as vague as its 
implementation is versatile. Due to this vagueness of conclusive points, the influence of 
the essay took different directions and adopted various approaches towards ‘intervening’ 
in terrains vagues. Some simply ask questions on how to proceed beyond Solà-Morales’s 
essay, while others offer detailed accounts on how to intervene. 
The manifesto-like essay provides a tool for activists and architects to understand 
the importance of the terrain vague and to transfer this worth to the larger public. 
Inspired intervention cases and activists did not always have a tangible form; some 
reached the limits of what could even be considered an ‘intervention’, retaining only the 
idea of raising awareness as their guideline. In many cases, the outcome is politically 
charged, such as in the work of Krystallia Kamvasinou798. 
The case of Stalker 
One of the most prominent cases, and most faithful to Solà-Morales’s stance, is 
Stalker, an Italian Rome-based collective of activists, architects and researchers who 
initiated their team during the early 1990s and still intervene in terrains vagues today. 
Their aim is mainly to explore desolate spaces and unravel their potential to the public. 
They consider terrains vagues to be, as explained in their manifesto, “spaces of 
confrontation and contamination between the organic and the inorganic, between nature and 
artifice" that "constitute the built city's negative, the interstitial and the marginal, spaces 
abandoned or in the process of transformation.”799 The collective, involving architects, 
landscape architects, artists, geographers and others, was formed when radical Italian 
student movements were at their peak. The group focuses its projects on interstitial urban 
                                                                                                                                                                        
798 Dr. Krystallia Kamvasinou, a landscape architect teaching at the University of Westminster, describes, in 
an interview with the Author, how her projects often involve intervening on terrains vagues and writing on 
the politics of engaging in such spaces. One of her papers is adequately entitled “Vague parks: the politics of 
late twentieth-century urban landscapes”. 
799 Stalker. “Manifesto - Stalker through the Actual Territories.” July 2016. Web.

## S. 361

358 
spaces, migrations of non-European cultures, urban social mobility and living patterns, 
observation, mapping, site-specific interventions and new media. 
Introducing   the   founding   members      
One could identify the founding members of Stalker as the following: Lorenzo 
Romito, Francesco Careri, Giovanna Ripepi, Aldo Innocenzi, Romolo Onaviani and 
Valerio Romito. The team members were not officially affirmed. They even had at times 
differing opinions and perceptions on what the group entails. When asked in an interview 
with the author800, Romito and Fiocca were rather reluctant on mentioning who Stalker 
exactly is, or who the founders initially were. They asserted firmly however that they are 
not architects and that they do not adhere to architecture as a fixed discipline. 
Nonetheless, one of the main voices that belong to the collective and write on its 
behalf is Peter Lang. He is a professor of Architecture History and Theory in the 
Department of Architecture at the Royal Institute of Art, Stockholm. He often writes 
about the history and theory of post-war Italian architecture. He is also actively 
implicated in the urban domain as his research often tackles the public sphere with a 
focus on the informal, the derelict and disadvantaged communities. He was member of 
the Stalker since 1997. 
As for Lorenzo Romito, he is one of the main founders of the Stalker collective 
since the early 1990s. He also co-founded with the collective other endeavors such as 
Osservatorio Nomade (ON), the Primavera project and finally the Stalker Walking School. 
Having an architectural background helped him develop the collective into the reputation 
it holds now. His interests expand in interdisciplinary research and a profound interest in 
urban changes. His work and publications with Stalker are worldwide. 
Another main protagonist of Stalker in its current shape is Giulia Fiocca. She is an 
architect, a researcher and an activist interested in the urban and social contemporary 
transformations focused mainly on informal and indeterminate spaces such as terrains 
vagues, and on the underprivileged and undeclared inhabitants of such spaces. She is 
linked to Solà-Morales as she followed his Metropolis Master program in Barcelona. She is 
also involved since then with urban matters of Rome, Barcelona and Vienna. Fiocca was 
not one of the main founders of Stalker, but she has been an active and significant 
member of the collective since 2006. She was however a co-founder (with Lorenzo 
Romito) of Primavera, a conceptual project that stemmed from Stalker in 2009 in order to 
favor communal thought towards social change in Rome. She also founded the Stalker 
                                                                                                                                                                        
800 Interview between author and Giulia Fiocca and Lorenzo Romito from Stalker in Rome. Apr. 2013.

## S. 362

359 
 
Walking School with the collective and Lorenzo Romito in 2012, which demarcated the 
next step in Stalker’s evolution towards a more politically involved standing. 
Early   stages   of   the   collective   
The founding members initially came together as a group of mainly architecture 
students in the early 1990s at Roma Tre University. The group became first official in 
1994, and their earlier works between 1994 and 2002 were already quite successful and 
appraised. 
The earliest manifestation of a Stalker-like action was in 1990. Many of the future 
founders of the collective were involved in a student protest that lasted for months at the 
university La Sapienza in Rome. They were political and influential on the youth of Rome 
of the early 1990s. This movement was against the privatization of education and against 
the university system planned by ministerial reforms. The school of architecture became 
then the cradle of a nation-wide university manifestation that proved to be successful to 
stop these privatizing reforms. The main nucleus of students implicated in the movement 
took on the name La Pantera801. In consequence, between January and February 1991, 
students took over about a hundred universities across Italy, led mainly by the 
universities of human sciences and architecture in Rome, from which Stalker was 
initiated. La Pantera members were politically occupied with contemporary concerns that 
ranged from privatization issues, to the fall of the Berlin Wall to the larger debate on the 
political fate of Europe. As Peter Lang states: 
“A re-evaluation of core educational values, at a time when the postmodern architecture 
movement was beginning to fray at the seams, proved critical to building an unprecedented 
intergenerational consensus on the contemporary urban landscape.”802 
The context of the early 1990s proved to be fruitful for architects-activists that had 
the will to change the political forces on the urban realm. 
Through La Pantera, the Stalker collective had, already since its founding, a 
predefined urban, architectural and political engagement and standpoint, as well as a set 
of strategies for action that rely on the public to defy top-down planning, political 
decisions and stagnating architectural and urban thought. One of the earliest 
manifestations of La Pantera was a project in 1993 entitled “Vivilerive, For an 
Environmental Practice of the Waste”. It stemmed from an interest in a terrain vague in 
front of the Marconi industrial plant on the Tiber River. One can notice an interest in 
terrains vagues since the beginning. The space sheltered the homeless and the delinquent, 
                                                                                                                                                                        
801 Inspired by the act of a lone panther that fled the zoo of the city a week before the protests. 
802 Lang, Peter. “Stalker on Location.” Loose Space: Possibility and Diversity in Urban Life. Ed. Karen A 
Franck and Quentin Stevens. London; New York: Routledge, 2007. 193–209.

## S. 363

360 
and was desolate for years. The members of La Pantera approached the situation as they 
would do later in Stalker: they initiated a walk through the desolate spaces and crossing 
Rome’s borders until they reached a terrain vague to intervene in. They crossed borders 
and defied the limits of Rome, walking in the terrains vagues until they reached the 
devastated industrial Ostiense area of Lungotevere Marconi, where they found a perfect 
space, forgotten by the public and arrogated by local interests and authorities, to 
intervene in. They used the materials found on site, mainly window blinds illegally 
discarded by neighboring workers constructing housing blocks in the area, to create 
kilometer-long pathways through the wasteland. These actions became, a few years later, 
trademarks of Stalker: observing and analyzing the terrain vague, improvising and 
intervening based on material and information found on site, creating walks and 
pathways through terrains vagues. They were inspired by local input and utilized leftover construction materials and other insitu found materials from the area, to create 
pathways through the terrain vague. 
The style was hence already set for what will be the Stalker approach: generating 
research and observations on the terrain vague through firsthand experience, to finally 
intervene based on information, people and material derived from the place itself. The 
approach seems thus, since as early as 1993, interdisciplinary in nature, from sculptures to 
architectural elements, to festivals or even various installations. La Pantera’s endeavor 
received public recognition and a large audience that often participated actively in its 
efforts. The controversy generated by the walk and their relative research approach 
denoted their uniqueness in the urban field. Their approach and standpoint did not match 
any other group or collective existing in the 1990s. 
The name Stalker was finally announced by the founders in October 1995 for the 
first time, during a first walkabout through the terrains vagues of Rome entitled “Through 
the Actual Territories”. As Francesca Recchia poetically describes: 
“With a visionary mixture of recklessness and dedication, Stalker walked and walked 
and its walking brought back to existence those areas that a real-estate oriented, capitalist 
logic of production dismissed as useless. The first walkabout, and the Manifesto that followed 
it in 1996 set Stalker's agenda towards residual, "actual" territories in terms of care and 
affection.”803 
Stalker’s beginnings were thus already embedded with a politically involved 
approach of resistance that defied preexistent forms of planning and control in the city. In 
line with Solà-Morales’s essay on the terrain vague and his critique of the colonizing 
architects, Stalker developed in a resistive form to the predetermined roles of architecture 
and urbanism within the city’s fabric. When asked, they even reluctantly admit their 
architecture-related origins and consider themselves primarily as activists. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
803 Recchia, Francesca. “Radical Territories of Affection. The Art of Being Stalker.” Art and Activism in the 
Age of Globalization. Ed. Lieven De Cauter. NAi, 2011. 160–171.

## S. 364

361 
 
The basic characteristics of Stalker’s approach are thus already visible in the 
Vivilerive initiative. The unexpected, the improvised and the site-found opportunities are 
embraced to intervene in the terrain vague. Consonant to Almarcegui’s artistic practice, 
Stalker seizes the potential of the spaces they walk through and the serendipity of 
happenings and encounters through these spaces in order to act or at least change the 
perception and visibility of these sites to the general public. 
 
FIGURE 127: Stalker. Attraverso I Territori Attuali - Rome 5-8 October 1995. Exhibit in Rome, January 2013. 
(Photograph by Author). 
Relation   to   Tarkovsky’s   Stalker   
It was a news reporter who, in 1995, first marked Stalker with the name when he 
correlated Andrei Tarkovsky’s film Stalker, the 1979 masterpiece of the eminent Russian 
director, with the collective’s urban journeys. Comparisons were made between the 
mysterious Zone in which the film characters wandered and Rome’s terrains vagues 
where the Stalker collective made their walks. Disregarding the negative associations 
linked with the English definition of the term, the collective quickly adopted the name. 
As Peter Lang describes: 
“His laconic narrative of a Virgilian passage through unfamiliar landscapes aptly foretold 
the Roman collective's explorations inside the capital's marginal spaces.”804 
In Tarkovsky’s Stalker805, the zone appears as a paranormal depiction of a terrain 
vague, and the main protagonist can be compared to what the members of the Stalker 
                                                                                                                                                                        
804 Lang, Peter. “Stalker on Location.” Loose Space: Possibility and Diversity in Urban Life. Ed. Karen A 
Franck and Quentin Stevens. London; New York: Routledge, 2007. 193–209.

## S. 365

362 
collective would become: Expert in the Zone, sensitive to its intensities, mapping its 
invisible borderlines, communicating strangely with the different elements and 
‘inhabitants’ of the zone, and ultimately tracing a path towards “The Room” where truth 
lies. The Stalker’s approach to the terrain vague seems already delineated. 
The film itself can also be considered linked to the notion of terrain vague, as Eva 
Horn advocates in the conference “Internationale Tagung Terrain Vague” in 2006806 in 
Zurich. According to Horn, the Zone in the film is not only a terrain vague; it has its own 
evolving and interactive character and is, in effect, the main protagonist of the narrative. 
The Zone offers new possibilities of identities and nomadic living to those who enter it. 
As Stalker tells his wife, “everywhere (except the Zone) is a prison for me.”807 The Zone 
offers him the chance to be free, a refuge towards a new spirituality. While navigating the 
terrain vague, the Zone questions the fundamentals of the outer society, it questions its 
visitors’ ethics, logic, memory, even traumas. Similarly, in Solà-Morales’s essay, examining 
the terrain vague leads to a reflection on the self and the other within the urban realm 
and its boundaries. In Tarkovsky’s Zone, and later in the collective Stalker’s interactions, 
the self and the other are put under scrutiny. The collective reinvents constantly new 
forms of interacting and collaborating with those they meet along their walks, those who 
inhabit the wastelands, but also those who decide to join them on their walks and 
become, at least temporarily, Stalkers themselves. 
Walking   –   reading   –   mapping   
Stalker relies on the act of walking to cross territories. This form of approaching 
space slowly by foot allows for an unmediated experience and reading of space. Stalker’s 
walks are considered acts of creation, a form of capturing essence without a medium in 
between and without any abstraction that could obscure, put prejudice or banalize the 
experience and meaning of the spaces encountered. 
Stalker’s walks are often accompanied with a form of cognitive mapping of the 
terrains vagues they explore. As terrains vagues are often misrepresented in traditional 
maps, vaguely delineated through their edges, creating thus holes of blurriness, patches of 
grey or green, in traditional maps, Stalker’s attempt is to give sense to this vagueness. 
Similar to tapping the unconscious layers within a mind, Stalker’s maps recognize the 
intensities and extensities of the terrain vague, energies and flows that Solà-Morales 
describes through his essay. 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
805 Tarkovsky’s film may have also inspired Francesco Careri, Georgia Daskalakis, Jason Young and Charles 
Waldheim to edit the book Stalking Detroit. 
806 Horn, Eva. “Zonen. Terrain Vague in Tarkowskijs Stalker.” Internationale Tagung “Terrain Vague.” 
University of Zurich: 2006. 
807 From: Tarkovsky, Andrei. Stalker. 1980. Film.

## S. 366

363 
 
One such wandering was the project Primavera Romana that started in 2009. The 
project consisted of walking and simultaneous mapping and registering of the experiences 
and encounters made along the way. These walks were made on the terrains vagues of 
Rome, deep in the Oltrecittà808, an area made of towns and villages that were slowly 
incorporated within the limits of the expanding city. The resulting mapping is made 
available on-line and includes a lot of information on the present and the past of these 
areas, carefully documented from inhabitants’ experiences and those walking through 
these terrains vagues. Stalker mappings of the terrain vague include in fact informal 
memorial forms and is often based on oral history of those who experienced these places, 
be it once or throughout their lives.      
 
FIGURE 128: Ottaviani, Romolo - Stalker. Planisferio Roma. Rome, 1995. 
These maps are moreover not intended as a form of controlling these places. They 
constitute a learning method to create even more walks or a form of archiving these 
spaces that are often in constant transformation and are sometimes even lost in the future. 
As Lorenzo Romito explains: 
“when we go for a walk within Primavera Romana we do not decide an itinerary a 
priori, but we have a map of the area on Google map and we ask everyone to help us find 
interesting places… so people start locating and suggesting places, they add bookmarks, they 
link videos, news, comments, information. -- The map starts filling up and after the walk, it 
enriches itself with other information, it constantly changes, it becomes an index.”809 
This ‘cartography’ is not an objective form of defining space, but rather a narration 
of the terrain vague and a collective effort that binds those who go on the walks, a form of 
enriching those involved and in turn the space itself through learned interventions. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
808 Biserna, Elena. “La Primavera Romana Di Stalker. A Zonzo Con Lorenzo Romito| Digital Art, Design and 
Culture.” Digicult | Digital Art, Design and Culture. 2 May 2011. Web. 9 Apr. 2015. 
809 Biserna, Elena. Ibid.

## S. 367

364 
These walks are moreover not defined by pre-established delimitations of the 
planned space. The collective attempt at exploring places despite constraints and away 
from the given commuting routes, roads and paths. A hole in a barrier, a jump over a 
fence or a crossing through bushes represents an opportunity for a new experience and a 
new discovery within, across and towards the terrain vague. Importance is given to the 
action of traversing and perceiving as a manifestation of becoming. These wanderings 
permit to read the space differently and be actively attentive in the terrain vague to the 
new discoveries ahead. 
Following   on   the   Situationists   
Stalker’s philosophy and approach related since its founding to traditions inherited 
from the Dada movement and the readymade practices of the Dada and the Surrealists. 
Experimental movements of 1960s, including Cedric Price and the Situationist 
International, could also be considered precedents to Stalker’s approach. Its style of 
walking finds its roots, as mentioned in the second chapter, in the wandering traditions 
and dérives of Guy Debord, but also in promenadology (or strollology), the science of 
strolling founded by the Swiss sociologist Lucius Burckhardt810 in the 1980s, in Walter 
Benjamin’s flâneries, or even in the certain practices of nomadic tribes. Stalker’s approach 
can be linked to these strategies811 in which motion, geographies, psychosocial aspects and 
site-specific interventions fuse into innovative urban and architectural modes of action. 
Stalker combines these urban traditions, but turns the attention intently towards the 
suburban wastelands and the terrains vagues on the outskirts of cities. 
By this token, be it the act of walking or the subsequent innovative forms of 
mapping, Stalker proposes a reversed and innovative form of reading the city in line with 
the resistance wave mentioned in chapter two, witnessed in artistic and interdisciplinary 
urban thought since the early 20th century. As curator at Locus Sonus in Marseille, Elena 
Biserna declares: 
“If the model for the first actions by Stalker is the one of the situationist dérive, now the 
concept of walking as a form of resistance, as a relationship, sharing and re-appropriation 
modality, in a dimension more explicitly collective, seems to prevail.” 812 
Unlike more political forms of walking related to protests and such, this form of 
exploring and wandering is positive at its core: an attempt to stay open to the potentiality 
                                                                                                                                                                        
810 Burckhardt, Lucius. “Spaziergangswissenschaft.” Warum ist Landschaft schön? Berlin: Martin Schmitz 
Verlag, 2011. 257–300. 
811 Described in the first chapter. 
812 Biserna, Elena. “La Primavera Romana Di Stalker. A Zonzo Con Lorenzo Romito| Digital Art, Design and 
Culture.” Digicult | Digital Art, Design and Culture. 2 May 2011. Web. 9 Apr. 2015.

## S. 368

365 
 
of space, in accordance to how Solà-Morales encourages one to tackle the terrain vague 
without bias. 
The collective however should not be considered as the new Situationists. The 
collective in fact viewed as threat what the Situationists’ dérive had become in the 1990s: 
a cliché and a romanticized and banalized idea of urban resistance in the public mind that 
is too easily manipulated and hinders, rather than activates, the potentials and intensities 
sensed though firsthand experience in the terrain vague. 
Philosophical   stance   and   connection   to   Solà-­‐Morales      
Stalker defined their own theory partially based on Deleuze and Guattari’s Milles 
Plateaux. As Peter Lang describes: 
“Those members of Stalker who debated such issues were drawn to Deleuze and 
Guattari's rhizomatic construct of a heterogeneous universe. What slowly fell into place was 
a theory of environmental cognition drawing much of its substance from the opus Mille 
Plateaux (Deleuze and Guattari 1980).” 813 
Similar to Solà-Morales, Lang also describes in general the conditions of 
contemporary cities in a similar fashion as in the “Terrain Vague” essay: the complex 
organization and planning of the cities of today leave no room for spontaneity or 
creativity, the terrain vague remains alone a guarantee, an oasis for future potential and 
creativity. He affirms: 
“These are the spaces of transformation, the "actual territories" that encumber the more 
traditional city fabric. These are the places that ultimately need the greatest attention.”814 
 
Similar to Solà-Morales’s intent, Deleuze confronts sedentary space to nomadic 
space, an approach intently followed by both Luc Lévesque and Stalker. According to 
Deleuze, boundaries and the routes between them striate sedentary space, while the 
nomadic space is smooth, only marked by momentary points, or “strokes.”815 He links 
furthermore nomadic space to infinity and void, using the metaphors of the desert or the 
sea. The terrain vague in this sense could be considered a nomadic space or city since 
signs and marks of passage can also be recognized in its disorienting oceanic spatiality. 
Accordingly, Deleuze’s theories proved useful for Stalker both to understand a non-linear 
rhizomatic construction on the built environment and to install an equally non-linear 
approach to it. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
813 Lang, Peter. “Stalker on Location.” Loose Space: Possibility and Diversity in Urban Life. Ed. Karen A 
Franck and Quentin Stevens. London; New York: Routledge, 2007. 193–209. 
814 Ibid. 
815 Deleuze, Gilles, and Félix Guattari. A Thousand Plateaus: Capitalism and Schizophrenia. University of 
Minnesota Press, 1987. 200-205.

## S. 369

366 
The "Terrain Vague" essay and Stalker have therefore in common Deleuze’s Mille 
Plateaux as reference and theoretical underpinning. Moreover, to understand the link 
between Stalker and Solà-Morales’s terrain vague, two notions within Stalker’s manifesto 
are worth mentioning: “intensifying perception” and “perceiving the becoming”. Stalker 
considers these as necessary conditions to unveil the terrain vague. Through their 
continuous walks in the terrains vagues of Europe and the United States since 1994, 
Stalker tries to maximize one’s own presence and readiness to receive new input given by 
these territories. Stalker members argue in their manifesto that entering the terrain vague 
intensifies our perception and experience, as we move from the known urban 
surroundings to the unknown, creating thus “a sense of dislocation.”816 Our perception in 
general is altered through this alert state. In simpler words, when we actively visit and 
walk through the terrain vague, our perception and sensitivity to our urban surroundings 
are amplified. Moving helps intensify our senses through the uncertainty and the derelict 
unknown offered before us. According to Stalker, without our responsive attention, we 
cannot “perceive the becoming”817, i.e. the changing space and its signifiers as we actively 
move within it. 
In the "Terrain Vague" essay, Solà-Morales also advocates for the empowerment of 
the rhizomatic forces against the orderly conforming capitalist city. Stalker shares his 
view of a city of fragments and resistance, where potential is hidden in the non-organized 
spaces of improvised encounter. The essay provides once more a theoretical grounding for 
Stalker to understand this fragmented urban landscape and to raise awareness towards its 
potential. It also provides a theoretical platform for this group interested in otherness, 
estrangement and wandering. 
Stalker’s philosophy is as a result already since its initiation related to much of 
Solà-Morales’s intent in the “Terrain Vague” essay. Stalker’s manifesto reveals a great deal 
of interest to understand and employ contemporary urban theory and philosophy towards 
creating and justifying their own urban methods. The manifesto is divided along various 
themes that derive inspiration from other theoretical references frequently present in 
Solà-Morales’s thought: ‘fluid spaces’ by Paul Virilio is for instance an antecedent notion 
to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague, while Merleau-Ponty’s theory of the aesthetic helped 
both Stalker and Solà-Morales in their reading of cultural artifacts. 
Solà-­‐Morales   as   post-­‐action   theorization   
Reading the Stalker manifesto, one cannot but notice how perception and 
firsthand experience are appreciated over theory and dogma. That is probably the reason 
                                                                                                                                                                        
816 Stalker. Ibid. 
817 Stalker. “Manifesto - Stalker Through the Actual Territories.” July 2016. Web.

## S. 370

367 
 
behind Solà-Morales being considered a post-script thought, rather than pre-script, on 
their work. Stalker members often quote Solà-Morales when they describe the collective’s 
work and its philosophy. Francesco Careri quotes the “Terrain Vague” essay in his book 
Walkscapes818, a book meant to theorize Stalker’s endeavors and, in general, the act of 
walking in the terrain vague. The notion of terrain vague is evoked in the context of 
“Anti-walk” and “Land walk – Expansions of the field”819. On a similar note, Manuela 
Mariani and Patrick Barron’s Terrain Vague book included Francesco Careri’s article also 
entitled “Walkscapes”. They already had linked the notion of terrain vague and the film 
art and the group Stalker in an earlier written work of theirs “Cinematic Space in Rome’s 
Disabitato”820. In the article, Solà-Morales is again evoked when defining the notion of 
terrain vague, and the Stalker collective is mentioned as an example of wanderers in the 
roman terrains vagues. The collective is behind the authors’ inspiration to work on spaces 
that “come into being”: 
“A central concern is thus to study film both as a testament to a particular director’s 
artistic vision of the disabitato, as well as a historical document containing evidence of 
transformation, of loss, of gain, in short, of “the process in which space comes into being.”821 
 
FIGURE 129: Stalker Wandering through Terrains Vagues, Napoli-Pompeii, with the Students of ETH Zurich. 
2012. (Photographs by Author). 
Raising   Awareness   of   the   terrain   vague 
Similar to Luc Lévesque and Lara Almarcegui, Stalker attempts at raising 
awareness towards the potential of the terrain vague and the value of such spaces within 
                                                                                                                                                                        
818 Careri, Francesco. Walkscapes: Walking as an Aesthetic Practice. Editorial Gustavo Gili, S.L., 2002. 
819 Which are also sections of the book. Ibid. 
820 Mariani, Manuela, and Patrick Barron. “Cinematic Space in Rome’s Disabitato: Between Metropolis and 
Terrain Vague in the Films of Fellini, Antonioni, and Pasolini.” Modernism/modernity 18 (2011): 309–333. 
821 Ibid. 311 (quoting stalker).

## S. 371

368 
the urban fabric and as an intrinsic part of the city and urban life. Stalker’s work since 
1994 usually consists, as aforementioned, of collective walks where they invite all to 
actively experience the terrain vague. They then incite intervention from participants 
based on what they have unraveled through their walks. This could entail helping its 
undeclared inhabitants, such as in Campo Boario where they collaborated with the 
Kurdish community on the outskirts of Rome; or it could mean mapping the terrain vague 
such as in their walks around Naples, Sicily or Paris, or their project Olio Pu.Ro where 
they collected stories and memories of terrains vagues and turned these into a territorial 
archive.    
 
FIGURE 130: Festival of Kurdish Culture. Rome, March 2014. Poster. - Located in the Ararat center created 
with the help of Stalker. 
Using these maps, Stalker proposes a reverse reading of the city where the urban 
mass turns into an amorphous background in contrast to the vitality of the city’s marginal 
zones. Historically, the art of mapping walks had itself changed through different waves: 
Dadaists and Surrealists avoided representation by literary description, while Situationists 
did psychogeographic maps but did not want to represent the real routes, whereas Lang 
and Stalker use finally mapping as an artistic expressive tool. This science of walking and 
wandering was reinvented by Stalker as based on ideas from the 1990s, including Solà-
Morales’s terrain vague. 
 
On a similar note, Stalker proposes intriguing strategies and undertakes numerous 
projects, of which the most notable are organized walks that crisscrossed the interstitial 
urban voids of Rennes, Milan, Miami and Berlin. Through such strategies Stalker is 
adopting the Latin origin of the term ‘vague’, i.e. vagari (to wander or roam), and its 
English appropriation in the word wakerant of Germanic origin (the origin of ‘walk’), 
hence the ‘walker’. These walks, according to Helen Armstrong, 
“result, similar to the Situationists, in an abstract map based on drifting through residual 
spaces […] Using these maps, they propose a reverse reading of the city where the urban

## S. 372

369 
 
mass turns into an amorphous background in contrast to the vitality of the city’s marginal 
zones”.822 
Those abstract maps reflect spaces where one can wander through the left-over 
spaces of everyday life, randomly and often playfully drifting, even risking encounters 
with the unpleasant. Unlike Walter Benjamin’s flâneur who drifts through crowded city 
places, the voids and wastelands are Stalker’s aim. They attempt to explore and intervene 
in the terrain vague without compromising its essence. 
One of the main merits of Stalker’s endeavors is their successful depiction and 
effective demonstration of the terrain vague’s massive dimensions. The collective brought 
to the eyes of the general public the terrains vagues of Rome in all their grandeur and 
glory. Stalker’s actions are as much an alteration of perception as an alteration of the 
physical realm. When they intervene in the terrain, their intent is both to modify the way 
we perceive the terrain, as well as modifying the way this terrain exists. In their 
manifesto, they declare: 
“To intervene on a territory is not merely an act of planning but an act of creation, an 
attempt to assemble contradictions and transform them into poetic relationships.”823 
In this fashion, the act of walking is a form of rediscovering the terrain vague 
through contact with the actual state of these often forgotten ‘invisible’ spaces. This direct 
involvement includes all the contradictions and meanings of the terrain and helps thus 
develop a fresh perspective and innovative forms of perceiving the terrain. 
Stalker advocates forms of intervention that are more about raising awareness than 
physically intervening drastically on the terrain. By doing so, they promote an approach 
where the architect is invited to completely change his or her approach towards the 
terrain. What Stalker is ultimately denouncing is the utopic dreams of the Modern period, 
which sheepishly turned into nightmares and dreadful heterotopias. In their manifesto, 
the collective states: 
“The attempt at defining and controlling the entire territory, mirage of our western 
culture, just when it seemed most likely to realize itself, gives forth its first water. […] Given 
the impossibility of total human control, the cement under which the forests were covered 
has cracked open, the earth flowers in new and unpredictable forms, preparing to contest 
with its human occupants the domination of space, from the scrap-heaps and beyond.”824 
Facing those ruptures and resulting terrains vagues, the collective often 
encourages intervening through abandonment, contrary hence to Modernist planning 
strategies. In fact, Stalker’s actions are often tangent with non-intrusive forms of 
intervening on the terrain. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
822   Armstrong, Helen. “Time, Dereliction and Beauty: An Argument for ‘Landscapes of Contempt.’” IFLA 
Eastern Region Conference Proceeding Book (2006): 116–127. 
823 Stalker. “Manifesto - Stalker Through the Actual Territories.” July 2016. Web. 
824 Ibid.

## S. 373

370 
On   potentiality   
In the "Terrain Vague" essay, Solà-Morales encourages interventions that trigger 
otherwise-dormant activities and possibilities. The act of intervening is however left 
vague and open to interpretation. In compliance with this idea, Stalker proposes modes of 
intervention that put in value the potential of the terrain vague and empowers its visitors. 
The terrain vague in fact is often a space supportive of different cultural ‘others’, 
along with the diversity these bring to the otherwise conforming city. Perceiving and 
sustaining the potential of the terrains vagues thus means indirectly adopting an inclusive 
political stand towards urban and cultural diversity. This potential already existing in the 
terrain vague, Stalker’s role is thus to extract it and bring it to light for the general public, 
whether through urban interventions, social interactions, media coverage, or other tools. 
Re-­‐creating   the   urban   collective   imaginary   
The Stalker members refer to their walking practices as “Transurbance”825. The act 
itself is an actuation of territories, a recreation of an urban space and an urban collective 
imaginary. More importantly, this walking practice is a knowledge and experience 
generator. It is used by the collective to resolve issues of urban generic planification 
through reversing the focus from the built-form towards the terrain vague. 
The collective moreover focuses its interest on the terrain vague and other similar 
spaces that it designates in its manifesto as “Actual Territories”826. This denomination 
refers to the fleeting character of the terrain vague in time; ‘Actual’ refers to the process 
of becoming, or coming into being, rather than existing, in relation to Foucault’s idea of 
the “other” becoming the other. In this sense, the act of walking actuates the space; the 
terrain vague comes into being when we experience it. Actual territories are thus meant 
to be experienced, rather than represented: “The archive of experiences is the only form of 
mapping possible for these ‘Actual Territories’.”827 Similarly, Peter Lang also evokes the 
notion of terrain vague when discussing Stalker in the book Loose Space. He equates 
Stalker’s “Actual Territories” to Solà-Morales’s terrain vague; they both refer, according to 
Lang, to similar ideas: 
“These areas are not merely voids on a city planner's map, but vast quasi-parks 
stretching around the Italian capital and extending like fingers into the city proper. These 
scruffy areas are buffer zones that relieve the city's relentless expansion and urbanization.”828 
                                                                                                                                                                        
825 Stalker. “Manifesto - Stalker through the Actual Territories.” July 2016. Web. 
826 Ibid. 
827 Ibid. 
828 Lang, Peter. “Stalker on Location.” Loose Space: Possibility and Diversity in Urban Life. Ed. Karen A 
Franck and Quentin Stevens. London; New York: Routledge, 2007. 193–209.

## S. 374

371 
 
 
Participatory   and   social   engagement   
The inclusive character of Stalker’s philosophy and practices ensures that 
interdisciplinarity is sustained within the group and their events. A large social 
engagement, and sometimes an equally large public coverage, is requisite for the success 
of Stalker’s approach. The collective’s actions are also often responses to the needs, 
requests, ideas of the stakeholders implicated in Stalker’s events. These include also the 
unofficial inhabitants of the terrain vague, such as the homeless or the nomads. Stalker 
creates projects in collaboration with these communities in order to aid their situation, to 
put their difficulties in the light, or to financially support them through self-organized 
projects and spaces. 
The collective puts a strong emphasis on the act of participation. It considers its 
work as a collaborative effort between different people sharing a new experience of place 
through the terrains vagues. The strength of its projects comes from the interest and 
enthusiasm of those willing to join its walks and willing to actively participate in 
archiving experiences of the terrain vague as well as intervening on it in infinitely diverse 
approaches inspired by the place itself. 
In a similar fashion, Stalker founded in 2002 the research network Osservatorio 
Nomade (ON). The network is composed of architects, activists, researchers and artists, all 
working together through interdisciplinary and experimental techniques. Their actions 
are aimed at creating self-organized processes for the discovery and intervention in spaces 
such as terrains vagues. This interdisciplinary research initiative follows the 
abovementioned practices of wandering and mapping already established by Stalker. It is 
meant to widen the possibilities for communities to access diverse knowledge about their 
spaces and to create participative approaches towards the management of urban spaces.    
Through such endeavors, Stalker assumed an intermediary role between different 
communities and the potential hidden in their spaces. One such example is their 
involvement with the different communities of Campo Boario, including the Kurdish 
immigrants in 1999. They organized events and revitalized areas, such as repairing an 
abandoned building on the site for use as Ararat, a community center for the Kurdish 
immigrants, in the terrains vagues of Campo Boario in order to create dialogue between 
the different communities, as well as with the Roman inhabitants of nearby 
neighborhoods. Their intervention became also a political tool for change and for a better 
understanding of the diversity of the inhabitants of Rome, legal and illegal. The spaces 
and dilapidated building at Campo Boario still functions as a meeting place for 
marginalized immigrants. As Stalker states:

## S. 375

372 
“The value of empty spaces (voids inside cities, spaces on the edges of cities, 
marginalized social spaces) lies in their programmatic inspecificity, potential for people to 
take possession of the space and generate their own new, diverse uses.” 829 
The collective’s opinion follows Solà-Morales’s advocacy of potentiality in the 
terrain vague. Much is left to serendipity and an attention to “continuity”, as Solà-Morales 
would encourage. 
Moreover, the sense of collaborative effort made by Stalker reveals a large interest 
in collaborating with what the experience of the space and its protagonists can offer, 
implying, as in Almarcegui’s work, a good degree of interdisciplinarity. The outcome is as 
eclectic, diverse and fragmented as is Solà-Morales’s urban vision and his writing on the 
terrain vague. 
 
FIGURE 131: Stalker. Immaginare Corviale, Osservatorio Nomade. Corviale, Rome, 2003/2005. Stalker and 
Osservatorio Nomade wandering through the terrains vagues of Rome. 
Self-­‐organized   mini-­‐economies   
Aside from Stalker’s active approach on the terrain vague, the collective’s intent 
includes also political and economic innovative layers. They do not simply perform 
actions but they hope to encourage others and enable them to create mini-economies 
                                                                                                                                                                        
829 Lang, Peter. “Stalker on Location.” Loose Space: Possibility and Diversity in Urban Life. Ed. Karen A 
Franck and Quentin Stevens. London; New York: Routledge, 2007. 193–209.

## S. 376

373 
 
independent of Stalker’s efforts. The collective aspires for levels of cooperation that grow 
beyond its undeclared nucleus group of activists. As Romito puts it: 
 “I think mass society is over and there is the need to find other ways. I believe 
reconstruction of the social organization should come from small communities and the 
consciences of individuals and this network should grow from the bottom up.”830 
Similar to Solà-Morales’s urban vision of a network of fragments formed of 
processes on the terrains vagues, one of the collective’s main objectives is to create a 
network of solidarity and cooperation for marginalized groups. 
One example of these micro-economies is a project on nomadic agriculture called 
“Le Arance non Cadono dal Cielo.”831 The group initiated a collective harvest of citruses 
and oranges growing in Roman public gardens. It was meant to be a symbolic memorial 
act recalling the rebellion in Rosarno and the exploitation conditions from which foreign 
workers in Italy suffer. The harvest became self-organized by a group of people, and it led 
to a variety of citrus-related products and funds, which financed eventually the 
restoration of a public space in Rosarno turned into a social center for foreign workers. 
These micro-economies help marginalized groups find sanctuaries in the projects 
tackled and ensure a voice within the larger majority. More importantly, they shed the 
light on potentials hidden in space, problems that come with speed and the fast growing 
pace of urban life leaving to time to apprehend diversity in space or intensities and 
extensities of our surroundings. Stalker’s initiative is one form of approaching the 
problematic place of the terrain vague within the city and what Solà-Morales calls 
‘attention to continuity’. 
Against   colonizing   the   terrain   vague 
In later stages of Stalker’s work and in their current work, the collective lost 
interest in creating new images for the future of the city. In Lorenzo Romito’s words: 
“The time in which architects had the possibility to create new images for the future, is 
over now. We live in a world full of future images, preventing us from seeing the present. 
The only possibility of looking at the future is observing the change in our society.” 
In the last decade, Stalker’s work became less involved in artistic undertakings and 
more involved in endorsing social change through minute traces of spontaneous and 
improvised alterations. Stalker reflects on the act of ‘becoming’ as a way to perceive the 
world in its ever-changing disposition as well as our interaction with it. Walking becomes 
                                                                                                                                                                        
830 Biserna, Elena. “La Primavera Romana Di Stalker. A Zonzo Con Lorenzo Romito| Digital Art, Design and 
Culture.” Digicult | Digital Art, Design and Culture. 2 May 2011. Web. 9 Apr. 2015. 
831 Trans.: “Oranges don’t fall from the sky” – Ibid.

## S. 377

374 
in this viewpoint a political tool of semantic transformation that links the body with earth 
and that produces through affects a new cartography. 
Moreover, Solà-Morales denounces in the “Terrain Vague” essay the colonizing 
role of the architect. In response to this critique, Stalker’s philosophy defies decreeing 
individual imprints in favor of more democratic improvised decision-making processes 
that stem from all the stakeholders of a certain terrain vague rather than the Stalker’s 
members alone. As Francesca Recchia describes: 
“Stalker doesn't impose its own vision and interpretation on the urban landscapes it 
crosses, but acts as a sensor that accumulates and absorbs its sounds, smells, sensation and 
images.” 832 
This mindset towards decision-making practices is reflected in the outcomes of 
Stalker’s endeavors, be it artistic events and creations, social, economic or actual products 
worth selling. Stalker’s position as intermediaries gives thus voice to the terrain vague 
with its own intensities and stakeholders. 
In conclusion, little attention from researchers and policymakers reaches small 
spaces and terrains vagues; in contrast to the economic and spatial dominance of large 
major projects in cities. Nevertheless, according to the reflections of Stalker, the terrains 
vagues are “prepared grounds,” flexible and open, allowing the “ad hoc emergence” of 
 “performative social patterns and group alliances that eventually colonize these surfaces 
in provisional yet deeply significant ways.”833 
Stalker’s actions are in consequence both a change of perception and an alteration 
of the physical realm. When they intervene in the terrain vague, their intent is to modify 
how we perceive these spaces, as well as the way in which these exist in the city’s 
consciousness. They thus belong to a trend among activists and architects who 
contributed to the development of the terrain vague discourse along the lines of Solà-
Morales’s initial call for action. 
Large scale influence: Landscape urbanism and Stalking Detroit 
The cases of Lara Almarcegui, Luc Lévesque and Stalker illustrate how the 
influence of the "Terrain Vague" essay took different forms. Almarcegui reflects the 
artistic and interdisciplinary aspects inspired by the first part of the essay, while 
Lévesque’s approach echoes the architectural and sociological facets of the essay’s middle 
                                                                                                                                                                        
832 Recchia, Francesca. “Radical Territories of Affection. The Art of Being Stalker.” Art and Activism in the 
Age of Globalization. Ed. Lieven De Cauter. NAi, 2011. 160–171. 
833   Shane, David Grahame. “The Emergence of ‘Landscape Urbanism’ - Reflections on Stalking Detroit.” 
Harvard Design Magazine Fall2003/Winter2004: 1–8.

## S. 378

375 
 
section, and finally Stalker resonates with Solà-Morales’s call for action and activism 
developed in the last paragraphs. All three forms of influenced practices are relevant and 
noteworthy. Notwithstanding, one fourth influential case should also be mentioned at 
this stage, which conceivably has much greater presence and influence: landscape 
urbanism. 
From the late 1990s and the 2000s, all the way to the present day, this theory 
turned into an important and influential movement. It aims to revitalize urban 
conceptions and practices, and to remove landscape urbanism from its marginalized 
position. Moreover, it has adopted interdisciplinarity as a primary tool, thus following in 
the footsteps of the ANY and Solà-Morales. Originally, It was aimed at dealing with postindustrial urban territories and landscapes, though in a wider search for a new form of 
contemporary urbanism. 
Its initiation and development throughout the mid-1990s coincided with the 
period during which Solà-Morales transmitted his "Terrain Vague" essay around the 
world. The founders and original projects behind landscape urbanism were in effect very 
inspired by Solà-Morales’s work. As a consequence, the movement channeled some of the 
concepts in the "Terrain Vague" essay to extend across a larger scope. 
Solà-Morales inspired origins 
The term “Landscape Urbanism” first emerged between 1994 and 1997, the same 
period in which Solà-Morales held lectures on the terrain vague. On the one hand, 
Charles Waldheim and James Corner organized the first Landscape Urbanism conference 
in Chicago in April 1997, sponsored by the Graham Foundation, while Peter Connolly, on 
the other hand, also coined the term in 1994 in his Masters of Urban Design thesis at 
RMIT in Melbourne. As Connolly described in an interview with the author: “There was 
something in the air… we both came up with this notion at a similar time, with no direct 
connection between us.” 834 In fact, what Connolly, Corner and Waldheim seem to have 
sensed, although in different manners, was the need for a proper recognition of the 
growing field of landscape architecture and an adequate language835 to cope with its 
transformations; a similar lack of language had driven Solà-Morales a few years earlier to 
write on the terrain vague notion. 
Charles Waldheim is one of the main protagonists of the Landscape Urbanism 
movement. His is a Canadian and American architect, urban theorist, and Professor and 
Chair of Landscape Architecture at Harvard University's Graduate School of Design. His 
                                                                                                                                                                        
834 Interview between author and Peter Connolly. 26 July 2017. 
835 Peter Connolly theorized this deficiency in his thesis.

## S. 379

376 
interests are mainly on linking the fields of urbanism, landscape and architecture in order 
to defy their predetermined hierarchy and elevate landscape architecture from its 
marginal position. He was one of the most inspired by Solà-Morales’s terrain vague, since 
he promoted it as a core concern among landscape urbanists. As Waldheim himself 
explained: 
 “When I held the first conference in Chicago, we were clearly influenced by Solà-
Morales, among other people, such as Andrea Branzi in Italy, that had been thinking about 
what we can do without a master plan basis and a much more relational, time-based, kind of 
relational or ephemeral urban project.” 836 
Waldheim was reading Solà-Morales’s work from the end of the 1980s, when he 
was in graduate school, through the 1990s, when he frequently visited Spain. He had 
received funding837 to document the urban landscapes in Barcelona, between 1994 and 
1995, and he attended and even participated in the 1996 XIX UIA conference in Barcelona 
with his colleagues. In parallel, it is also worth mentioning here that Solà-Morales was 
also in contact with Peter Connolly during this same period. He had visited RMIT in 
1995, presented his "Terrain Vague" lecture there, re-published his essay in the 
university’s Kerb journal838, and even met Connolly who conducted an interview with him 
for that same edition of Kerb 839. Connolly then he wrote in the same edition a “Terrain 
vague guide” in which he tried to identify which ideas and types of spaces the terrain 
vague touches on. 
At the time, the landscape urbanism theory was developing, from the University 
of Pennsylvania and RMIT, into a well-established subdiscipline in the academic 
programs of the Harvard Graduate School of Design, MIT, the University of Toronto and 
the University of Illinois in Chicago. It also developed in Europe in the programs of the 
Catholic University in Leuven, the Oslo School of Architecture and the Architectural 
Association in London where it became fundamentally prominent. Those involved in 
writing, and often teaching, on landscape urbanism include Mohsen Mostafavi, Alan 
Berger, Stan Allen, and of course Charles Waldheim and James Corner. 
Nonetheless, even though landscape urbanism emerged and was debated in 
academic realms, it also involved various practitioners and many other projects were 
inspired by this theory. Those involved in writing more practice-oriented works on 
Landscape Urbanism included Chris Reed, Richard Weller, Christopher Gray, and 
naturally Peter Connolly. In addition, related works featured among the projects of the 
firms Field Operations (James Corner) and West 8 (Adriaan Geuze). 
                                                                                                                                                                        
836 Interview between author and Charles Waldheim. 6 July 2017. 
837 Funded research from the University of Illinois at Chicago, Institute for the Humanities Grants-In-Aid. 
838 Already briefly mentioned in the fourth chapter. - Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Kerb Journal 
of Landscape Architecture 3 (1996): 7–12. 
839 Connolly, Peter, Tim Nicholas, and Julian Raxworthy. “An Interview with Ignasi de Solà-Morales.” and 
“Terrain Vague Guide”. Kerb Journal of Landscape Architecture 3 (1996): 13–26.

## S. 380

377 
 
In consequence, landscape urbanists repeatedly sought to transform this academic 
and theoretical debate, and the resulting published literature, into a set of tools for 
practical application in the physical realm. More importantly, these tools were mainly 
aimed at addressing spaces such as infrastructural systems, roadways and chiefly terrains 
vagues. These were often marginal and large-scale zones that necessitated collaborative 
efforts, very much in line with the ideas already promoted in 1994 at the Anyplace 
conference and in Solà-Morales’s essay. 
On Stalking Detroit 
One of the first projects that initially instigated landscape urbanism was a book 
entitled Stalking Detroit840. It consisted of drawings, photographs and texts analyzing the 
urban state of Detroit in the late 1990s and at the beginning of the new millennium. 
These spaces, terrains vagues of the city, were depicted through reinvented geographies 
reflecting intensities inherent to them. The authors Charles Waldheim, Georgia(Gia) 
Daskalakis, Jason Young and Omar Perez considered the city’s terrains vagues as potential 
spaces to deter preconceived political and urban planning dominion. They valued the 
ambiguity of function and purpose as an important aspect of the terrain vague and an 
attempt at utilizing this character as an urban design strategy. 
In an interview with the author, Waldheim841 explained how he was surprised to 
notice that Detroit was not a subject of interest at the University of Michigan in Ann 
Arbor School, where he was teaching between 1991 and 1993. This caused him to become 
preoccupied with the city, which he then began to explore and research, along with 
Daskalakis, Young and Perez who were also teaching at the school during this period. The 
authors were involved with both architectural and urban concerns. Similarly in between 
academia and architectural practice, their interests included landscape interventions, as 
well as urban agriculture and ecology. 
In the book, the post-industrial city of Detroit is presented as one large terrain
