vague, an inherent outcome of wider economic and social processes: “Detroit… shaped by 
the city’s continuously and rapidly transforming economics, social, and operational conditions.”842 In 
this sense, the terrains vagues are continuously emerging spaces in the city, especially due 
its post-industrial condition, and intervening on them requires adequate ongoing 
processes in time. These spaces are then considered as the smaller spaces within the city as 
much as those within its neglected industrial sectors. More interestingly, the authors 
                                                                                                                                                                        
840 Daskalakis, Georgia, Charles Waldheim, and Jason Young. Stalking Detroit. Barcelona: Actar, 2001. 
841 Interview between author and Charles Waldheim. 6 July 2017. 
842 Daskalakis, Georgia, Charles Waldheim, and Jason Young. “Introduction: Committee Work.” Stalking 
Detroit. Barcelona: Actar, 2001. 10-11.

## S. 381

378 
describe methods of interaction with these sites that require exploring the city in similar 
fashion to the Situationists’ dérives through Paris. Stalking Detroit provides a counterexample and an alternative to post-industrial sites turned into parks. 
Connections   to   Solà-­‐Morales      
In 2001, Georgia (Gia) Daskalakis and Omar Perez were in contact with Solà-
Morales843. They had been previously his students at the UPC Barcelona and they were 
present at the XIX UIA Congress. Solà-Morales also consented on writing the introduction 
to their coming book Stalking Detroit that he considered valuable as he stated in a letter 
in 1998844. Sadly, Solà-Morales passed away that same year and a dedication in the book is 
addressed to him. 
As Waldheim affirms when interviewed845, Solà-Morales was a strong inspirational 
character for the writers of the book that ultimately resulted in founding the landscape 
urbanism movement. Indeed, many landscape urbanists were equally inspired by Solà-
Morales, including Yorgos Simeoforidis, James Corner, Edward Soja, Carlos Leite, Edward 
Chell, McGrane and Krystallia Kamvasinou. They were cognizant of his writing and their 
interests were concomitant with concepts in Solà-Morales’s essay. According to 
Waldheim846, Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay brought theoretical legitimacy to their 
work in Detroit and their interpretations of such spaces. Some, such as Kamvasinou, often 
quoted him throughout their theoretical work. Others applied his theory into practical 
approaches, such as James Corner. 
Another important correlation between Solà-Morales and Stalking Detroit is the 
XIX UIA congress of 1996 in Barcelona. This event, organized in part by Solà-Morales, 
was the first time when Waldheim, Daskalakis, Perez and Young presented their work 
that later became Stalking Detroit. Among the different venues of the congress, one 
session was dedicated to them under the theme of terrain vague, where the four of them 
gave lectures about Stalking Detroit and a panel discussion followed. They were thus all 
present at the congress that, according to Waldheim, “was a key moment in the reception of 
Solà-Morales's ideas.”847 
                                                                                                                                                                        
843 Letter: Gia Daskalakis and Omar Perez to Solà-Morales, 2001. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - 
Box 304. Detroit File. 
844 Letter: Solà-Morales to Gia Daskalakis and Omar Perez, 2001. Ignasi de Solà-Morales Private Archive - 
Box 304. Detroit File. 
845 Interview between author and Charles Waldheim. 6 July 2017. 
846 Ibid. 
847 Ibid. – Waldheim seemingly never met Solà-Morales in person, but he was certainly aware about his work 
since the late eighties.

## S. 382

379 
 
In addition, the Catalan influence is also present within the content of Stalking 
Detroit. The book mentions similar photographers cited in Solà-Morales’s essay. It 
includes for instance Jordi Bernadó’s photographs of terrains vagues. The authors wanted 
to commission new photographs of Detroit to photographers who had not seen Detroit 
beforehand and could bring a fresh outlook on the city. They searched for photographers 
who had already worked with Actar, their publishing house in Barcelona, which in fact 
had been publishing during the 1990s a series of books on cities, for instance one on 
Berlin and another on Atlanta. Therefore the team of Stalking Detroit went to the 
publishing house and asked to work with the photographers Mònica Roselló and Jordi 
Bernadó from Barcelona. The choice was important because, on the one hand, Roselló and 
Bernadó had already proficiency and experience in photographing cities, and on the other 
hand, they had the cultural sensitivity to understand the city as a cultural project due to 
their experience in Barcelona, a city under major urban transformation during this period, 
as we previously described. 
 
FIGURE 132: Daskalakis, Georgia, and Omar Perez. “Projecting/Stalking Detroit” Presentation. 1996. - Edited 
version of Synopsis for the XIX UIA Congress, Barcelona. Solà-Morales private archives.

## S. 383

380 
 
FIGURE 133: Daskalakis, Georgia, and Omar Perez. “Projecting/Stalking Detroit” Presentation. 1996. - Edited 
version of Synopsis for the XIX UIA Congress, Barcelona. Solà-Morales private archives. 
This aspect of Stalking Detroit is even more accentuated as Waldheim848 himself 
finds that the most important note in Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay is how the 
author does not refer directly to urban conditions and rather approaches the terrain vague 
notion through the history of photography. He perceives Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" 
essay as describing a specific role and status of urban photography. He also states how 
photography is an important factor, for Stalking Detroit and for such spaces, since the 
meaning of the terrain vague derives from photography. This aspect of landscape 
urbanism is accordingly already visible in their interest to understand the city through a 
variety of tools and fields of operation. 
Difference   between   Stalker   and   Stalking   Detroit   
Tarkovsky’s film Stalker revolves around a space that the antagonists call “The 
Zone”, a special terrain vague that takes the lead role in the plot. The movie inspired the 
previously mentioned group Stalker. It also inspired Stalking Detroit. The name was a 
formulation that came out of the conversations of the book’s editorial group in the mid-
1990s, and how they found in Tarkovsky's Stalker an exemplary depiction of industrial 
wastelands in the city that had been abandoned and that had maintained a certain aura. 
Then a secondary reason behind the name was, as formulated in the introduction of the 
book849, that the group’s activities on the city’s terrains vagues imitated those of a stalker, 
devoting unwanted and unrequited attention850 to Detroit, their object of affection. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
848 Ibid. 
849 Daskalakis, Georgia, Charles Waldheim, and Jason Young. “Introduction: Committee Work.” Stalking 
Detroit. Barcelona: Actar, 2001. 10–16. 
850 According to Waldheim, the inhabitants and other stakeholders in Detroit were not interested in the 
group’s atypical depictions of the city.

## S. 384

381 
 
Although the name and time period of Stalking Detroit coincided with the Stalker 
collective in Rome, it was merely a happenstance as the editorial group behind the book, 
as Waldheim admits, was not aware then of the Roman collective. More interestingly, this 
indicates, once again, how the period was ripe for such ideas and approaches on the 
terrain vague through hands-on experience. 
There are many similarities in intent between Stalker from Rome and Stalking 
Detroit and landscape urbanism. They are both interested in exploring the potential of 
spaces such as the terrain vague and at understanding urban processes in time. The 
approaches of the two movements are nonetheless very different. 
The groups behind Stalker and Stalking Detroit do not share a similar attitude 
towards urban interventions. While Stalker promotes activist and resistant urban 
positions, often promoting non-architectural intercessions, Stalking Detroit, and what 
later became landscape urbanism, see potential in carefully yet actively approaching the 
terrain vague from a design-oriented standpoint. Stalker advocates that abandoned urban 
spaces are used to their full potential once they are appreciated for their biodiversity and 
their undeclared inhabitants are encouraged to reside and operate freely in them. 
Landscape Urbanists conversely propose design strategies that explore the terrain vague 
and other spaces for new forms of urban ecology inspired by diverse environmental and 
other fluxes within the city. In Stalking Detroit, the potential of dystopian wastelands in 
Detroit is explored to propose a new hybrid form of urbanism based on dense clusters of 
urban activity, co-existing with the ecological processes of the terrain. The approach is 
neither activist nor resistant, which, as a result, separates considerably landscape 
urbanism from the Stalker collective. 
In addition, while the Stalker collective addresses its work toward a social and 
political project, the audience of landscape urbanism is mainly in the design disciplines. In 
interviews with the author, both Stan Allen and Charles Waldheim oppose considering 
landscape urbanism as a resistance or an activist movement. Waldheim’s intent is to 
fashion theoretical and projective lenses to be able to respond to the urban and social 
conditions at play, he does not however call his approach a “directly social project”851, 
even though there are often societal benefits and political implications from these 
interventions. In this respect, unlike the activist approach of Stalker, landscape urbanists 
usually prefer to work through the dominant economy to kind of insulate people from the 
worst effects of problematic systems; they try to find shifting tools from within the system 
and to fashion theoretical lenses and projective lenses that are more specifically attuned to 
the conditions they find themselves working with. Landscape urbanism seems less linked 
                                                                                                                                                                        
851 Interview between Author and Charles Waldheim. 6 July 2017.

## S. 385

382 
with ideas of resistance and more concerned with a Deleuzian language of flow, 
intensities and deterritorialisation852. 
Another difference between Stalker and landscape urbanism is the question of 
scale. While the former is interested in community-scale activism and practices of 
resistance, the founding premise of the latter is to work at a very large scale in order to 
create new forms of organization. James Corner, for instance, is especially today a 
mainstream practitioner. Even Waldheim, whose academic and theoretical affiliations 
could hint at intent for considerably changing urban systems and processes, tackles 
schemes with large-scale design-oriented strategies in mind. 
In conclusion, Stalking Detroit thus certainly influenced the development and 
reception of landscape urbanism and then the discussion about shrinking cities with 
Philipp Oswalt, who observed the issue of “shrinkage”853 at a global range in which Detroit 
was one case. This discourse helped disseminating, since the beginning of the century, the 
terrain vague notion to this international scale. 
Terrain vague-inspired working methods 
The "Terrain Vague" essay provides a useful theoretical tool for landscape 
urbanists who aspire to understand, describe or intervene in certain types of urban spaces 
and conditions. According to Waldheim: 
“The conclusion of the essay stresses how we can work in these places without totalizing 
top-down master plans, without top down solutions or modernist master plans; that produced 
a whole set of theories and ideas about temporality and flux that certainly influenced the 
work of James Corner, Stan Allen and myself, among others in the United States who 
received Solà-Morales’s theories as urban tools.” 854 
Many of the successful projects of landscape urbanists occurred, accordingly, in 
terrains vagues. For instance, Victoria Marshall and Steven Tupu’s project in 1998 for the 
Van Alen East River Competition dealt with the terrains vagues of the East River to solve 
the waste problems of New York while creating a new public space within this side of 
Brooklyn. 
Since its foundation, some of this subdiscipline’s main themes were the 
abandonment and disintegration of post-industrial cities, the large scale potential 
                                                                                                                                                                        
852 Stan Allen links ideas of resistance with Frankfurt School Marxism and the practices of negation that came 
from it, he then opposes these to the practices of landscape urbanism. 
853 Oswalt, Philipp, and Kulturstiftung des Bundes, eds. Shrinking Cities. Ostfildern-Ruit: Hatje Cantz  ; 
Distributed by D.A.P., 2005. 
854 Interview between author and Charles Waldheim. 6 July 2017.

## S. 386

383 
 
development, and the terrains vagues resulting from these phenomena. Landscape 
urbanists proposed an innovative set of tools to deal with the terrains vagues through an 
enlarged understanding of landscape; adopting methods from ecology, hydrology, 
horticulture, architecture and civil engineering to redefine both fields of urbanism and 
landscape; these methods are often focused on long-term slow processes that are 
inherently participatory and focused on the public sphere. 
On   Interdisciplinarity   
The concept of the book Stalking Detroit was based on the theory of ‘thick 
description’ by the anthropologist sociologist Clifford Geertz who argued that one could 
no longer assume to have an omniscient modernist view, yet one could not go fully 
relativist either; he called instead to analyze and “describe”855 conditions through a variety 
of different channels or media. Landscape urbanists took this approach as a mandate and 
described 1990s Detroit through film, photography, critical essays, history, new projects; 
as many different forms as they could. 
In the same spirit of collaborative and inclusive large-scale interventions, 
landscape architect Ian Thompson wrote a critique on the movement: 
“There are ideas with the Landscape Urbanism discourse which have great merit, among 
which I would include the breaking down of professional distinctions, the integration of 
ecological thinking, the foregrounding of infrastructure, the interest in the positive use of waste 
materials and the emphasis upon functionality rather than mere appearance.”856 
One of landscape urbanists’ tools was, as in the discourse on 'terrain vague', the 
use of interdisciplinary tactics on large-scale distressed sites. In an interview with the 
author, architect and theorist Stan Allen 857 described how this aspect was hugely 
important for landscape urbanism, how the movement is by definition interdisciplinary 
and how it is an essential vehicle to reshape the debates between the theory of landscape 
architecture and its practice. 
Allen describes, in addition, how landscape urbanism was well positioned to 
support such new approaches because the field of landscape architecture did not have a 
deep disciplinary history, so it could be more agile and it could position itself as the 
discipline privileged to deal with all of the complex interdisciplinarity of the problems of 
the city. As a consequence, landscape architecture was able to embrace questions of all of 
the disciplines, infrastructure, landscape hydrology, civil engineering and horticulture 
                                                                                                                                                                        
855 Geertz, Clifford. “Thick Description: Toward an Interpretive Theory of Culture.” The Interpretation of 
Cultures: Selected Essays. New York: Basic Books, 1973. 3–30. 
856 Thompson, Ian Hamilton. “Ten Tenets and Six Questions for Landscape Urbanism.” Landscape Research 
37.1 (2012): 7–26. 
857 Interview between author and Stan Allen. 30 June 2017.

## S. 387

384 
and, that architecture had a kind of uncomfortable relationship with. Most of the teams 
behind the Freshkills Park competition of 2001 to 2003, which the firm Field Operations 
won, had, for example, financial consultants, landscape architects, engineers, sociologists, 
artists and traffic planners. This interdisciplinarity represented the landscape urbanists 
approach and became as well a mainstream tendency in most urban projects since the 
beginning of the 21st century, especially in American cities. It could have originated from 
the academic contexts from which landscape urbanism emerged, and it became finally an 
integral part of contemporary urban practice. 
Political   and   economic   layers   
Another link between landscape urbanism and Solà-Morales’s interests is the 
movement’s rejection of the colonizing aspect of the architectural profession, focusing 
instead on urban landscapes and their decay that provided and still doe great potential for 
revitalization and development. Waldheim, when interviewed858, situated the terrain 
vague notion in the context of the restructuring of economy in the 1971. He considers 
Solà-Morales’s essay as necessary for urbanist architects in a capitalist system “facing the 
failure of top-down European welfare state.”859 He also defines this failure as pertaining to 
two aspects: 
“It's a failure of their (Modernist architects’) aspiration to plan the city coherently and 
completely, but it's also a failure of their social policies and political practices.”860 
In this sense, landscape urbanists were equally interested in redefining the 
landscape architecture discipline beyond the disappointments ensuing from the Modern 
Architecture ideals and the economic disarray that came almost in tandem. Waldheim 
asserts this position by describing: 
“At the end of his essay, Solà-Morales really begins to show a kind of landscape urban 
escapes. I think people didn't read the end of the essay where he really says ‘we need a 
different toolkit’.” 
On a similar note, James Corner advocated a reversal of perception of the city, in 
which the focus is on the un-built, the terrain vague, and the timely processes of the city; 
rather than the colonizing aspect of architecture, the built-form and as Solà-Morales calls 
it “the organized city”861. This inversion of perception and, consequently, of processes of 
urban design, helps reshape the terrain vague in innovative forms faithful to Solà-
Morales’s call for action: through a care for continuity and an attention to intensities 
within the city. In an analysis of São Paulo’s marginalized areas, for instance, architect 
                                                                                                                                                                        
858 Interview between author and Charles Waldheim. 6 July 2017. 
859 Ibid. 
860 Ibid. 
861 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123.

## S. 388

385 
 
Carlos Leite tackles862 these spaces in the city to denounce problems of fragmentation of 
contemporary cities and the corresponding identity crisis; the terrain vague being an 
outcome of this fragmentation. 
On   potentiality   
The landscape urbanism movement holds much potential for innovation 
consonant with Solà-Morales’s essay. In the same lines of alternative potentiality in 
thinking and experiencing the city, architect Mohsen Mostafavi, for instance, sees hope in 
the terrain vague. He suggests in his book Landscape Urbanism: a manual for the 
Machinic Landscape863 that the temporary usages on such spaces are already indicators of 
potential future practices and activities that these terrains vagues could sustain. As Stan 
Allen asserts, when interviewed: 
“What landscape urbanism shares with the terrain vague is a kind of anticipatory factor; 
the idea that the empty site is waiting for something to happen, that it's a kind of fertile 
ground for future development.”864 
According to Stan Allen, one thing that both landscape urbanism and the "Terrain 
Vague" essay share is a sense of anticipation, especially in relation to how James Corner 
interprets landscape urbanism. Indeed, Corner was interested in what Rem Koolhaas 
describes as “seeding a site”865 with potential. In that regard, landscape urbanism is linked 
to notions that the movement adopted from landscape and ecology, especially the idea 
that cities have a life in time and the critique of a master plan as a finished state and the 
promotion of approaches in which architects seed a site, without knowing in advance the 
final outcome, with new organizational potential through laying out the infrastructural 
pathways and then let the inhabitants, the other agents who operate in the city, build on 
it; an idea equally latent in the terrain vague. 
Differences   in   intent   and   approach   
Within landscape urbanism, there are also differences in approach that get the 
members and projects closer or further away from the terrain vague notion. First, James 
Corner considers the terrains vagues of all kinds, including the abandoned infrastructures 
                                                                                                                                                                        
862 Leite, Carlos. “Economic Modeling Techniques Applied to Regional and Urban Issues, ECOMOD´2006 
International Conference on Regional and Urban Modeling.” Free University of Brussels: 1-3/6/2006. 
863 Mostafavi, Mohsen, and Ciro Najle, eds. Landscape Urbanism: A Manual for the Machinic Landscape. 
London: Architectural Association, 2003. 7. 
864 Interview between author and Stan Allen. 30 June 2017. 
865 Koolhaas, Rem. “To Imagine Nothingness.” L’architecture d’Aujourd’hui Apr. 1985: LXVII.

## S. 389

386 
and ruins, as parts of a larger urban network and ecosystem866, extending both in space and 
time. Second, James Corner and Stan Allen seem to have a fairly different emphasis than 
Charles Waldheim. While Waldheim has a more theorist focus, Corner and Allen seem to 
address a more pragmatic practitioners’ focus. As Allen describes in the interview867, he 
and Corner wrote about “a kind of refusal of questions of representation and semiotics”868 
favoring instead ideas of performativity, which in turn distances a little their interests 
from the intent behind Solà-Morales’s essay where representation of urban space is, as 
aforementioned, vital. Allen’s articles for instance on field conditions869 were accordingly 
more about questions of diagrams and organization and they were operational at heart, as 
opposed to Solà-Morales's focus on meaning and on the social and political relationships 
underlying the terrain vague. Correspondingly, in the period between 1999 and 2003, 
Allen and Corner were seeking to build a practice and their theoretical work was in good 
part oriented towards problems arising from professional work. 
American   versus   European   contexts   
Landscape urbanism approaches ideas of in-betweenness and terrain vague, while 
involving infrastructure, as a new set of elements in the void-strategy equation. They 
elaborate on questions raised by Solà-Morales within the context of Anywhere and 
Anyplace, while adding an American large scale to the discourse. 
The emergence of interest in landscape, and then later in landscape urbanism, had 
in fact a strong focus, at least originally, on the American city. In an interview with the 
author 870, Stan Allen describes the "Terrain Vague" essay as very much focused on 
European cities and contrasts these with the pattern of the periphery in the United States. 
Solà-Morales’s text however does not refer solely to one specific region or urban fabric; 
the designation of terrain vague, although very much inspired as aforementioned by 
Western cities, was not exclusively location-bound. Accordingly, especially in 
Waldheim’s work, a lot of what motivated the landscape urbanism thinking from the late 
1990s was the abandonment of declining post-industrial American cities such as Detroit. 
As a result, the "Terrain Vague" essay became a useful intellectual framework in this 
North American context. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
866 Corner, James. “Landscape Urbanism.” Landscape Urbanism: A Manual for the Machinic Landscape. Ed. 
Mohsen Mostafavi and Ciro Najle. London: Architectural Association, 2003. 58–63. 
867 Interview between Author and Stan Allen. 30 June 2017. 
868 Ibid. 
869 Allen, Stan. Field Conditions Revisited. Ed. Giancarlo Valle. New York: Stan Allen Architect, 2010. 
870 Interview between Author and Stan Allen. 30 June 2017.

## S. 390

387 
 
One of the things that the essay did for landscape urbanists is that it gave them a 
kind of European historical legitimacy to thinking about and working on places that were 
not the center. As Waldheim states in an interview with the author: 
“The essay was very important especially for those of us in North America. It was really 
useful to have a continental European figure of a certain caliber and a certain visibility to 
state how these places matter; it gave us quite a lot of confidence here to look at these places. 
Only some Europeans were taking that kind of subjects as seriously.”871 
Solà-Morales’s essay was thus valuable as a theoretical base for those interested in 
the terrain vague. In this fashion, he joins others from Continental Europe who inspired, 
through the 1990s, the landscape urbanism movement to develop an interest in 
wastelands, but also in a kind of alternative architectural, urban and landscape approach. 
As Waldheim describes: 
“By weakness I mean both in a literal sense but also in the political sense: what you can 
do after the larger hegemonic forces have failed. By weakness I mean a whole intellectual 
tradition that I associate with Branzi and others, such as when Rem Koolhaas asks “Whatever 
Happened to Urbanism”. What are we to do in these places now that top-down master 
planning has failed. I think Solà-Morales is questioning how we can deal with these places 
without totalizing control.”872 
For Waldheim and others in the landscape urbanism movement, Solà-Morales 
belonged thus to a few European architects and theoreticians, including Rem Koolhaas 
and the Italian architect Andrea Branzi, who were arguing for a political position, often 
embedded in their projects, regarding how to make urbanism in the wake of architectural 
and economic hindrances. 
Reactions   and   interpretations   
When Stalking Detroit was first presented at the XIX UIA Congress in Barcelona 
in 1996, the reactions were mostly disapproving. As Waldheim recalls: 
“I remember the audience was very unhappy with us, the European audience was 
generally enraged by what we presented about Detroit, because we were basically using 
continental urban theories to explain a situation that they viewed as kind of a crime on both 
ethical and moral terms.”873 
Stalking Detroit in this sense brought to light the failure of the urban capitalist 
system. It was also one of the very first times that the case of 1990s-Detroit was presented 
to a European context. The premise of the work was to present the city in a coherent way 
faithful to its status quo at the time. They presented photographs, designs and 
documentation that stirred unease. The audience at the congress, according to Waldheim, 
seemed enraged that no one was held accountable for the abandoned city. The reactions 
                                                                                                                                                                        
871 Interview between Author and Charles Waldheim. 6 July 2017. 
872 Ibid. 
873 Ibid.

## S. 391

388 
could have been especially displeased in Barcelona since the city, in contrast to Detroit, 
was in the mid-1990s an exemplary case of success after the urban reforms done for the 
1992 Olympic games. The projects proposed by Stalking Detroit did also little to ease 
concerns; the approach seemed radical for the time. A running intent was to leave the 
most abandoned areas of the city, fence them off and allow them to return to nature. Such 
schemes were deemed a social and political failure and even “fatalist and nihilist”874. 
From this period onwards, the Landscape Urbanists’ approaches on the interstitial 
and the terrain vague raised at times controversial reactions. David Shane, for instance, 
accuses the movement’s practice of being inadequate to the major problems of justice and 
equity within the city: 
“The problem is that the small scale, bottom-up, and eco-friendly moves advocated by 
Stalking Detroit do not address fundamental issues of social justice and equity that are also 
part of the foundations of a true urbanity.”875 
 
Moreover, at the first conference on Landscape Urbanism at the University of 
Pennsylvania in April 2002, the Dean Garry Hack questioned the interstitial strategies and 
the small-scale interventions of the participants, asking: “Hyperurbanization: Places of 
Landscape Architecture?”876 He identified a fundamental hindrance in this practice as 
being irrelevant beyond the American scale. He denounces the landscape urbanists’ 
approach as irrelevant for European suburbs in which the “city of spectacle”877 is still 
prevalent. He accuses moreover the approaches in Stalking Detroit as amnesiac and 
oblivious to the preexistent structures and morphological models in the city. 
Similarly, the establishment of landscape urbanism, around the United States and 
then in Europe, provoked at times worldwide reactions critical too of the movement. 
Practitioners and academicians from Australia and New Zealand were particularly 
concerned as Peter Connolly, Julian Raxworthy and Tim Nicholas were equally immersed 
in these concepts of urban landscapes and terrains vagues. Connolly and his colleagues, 
were already in the early mid-1990s beginning to address such spaces and concerns 
within their work. 
In the first year of their masters, they looked at the notion of typology in 
architecture and at how it was relevant to landscape architecture. They also looked at 
initial ideas of open systems, largely inspired by Deleuze and Guattari. They were, in 
parallel, doing big mapping exercises on the whole of Melbourne, as well as relating what 
they were finding on the ground with what they could understand from the maps. They 
                                                                                                                                                                        
874 Ibid. 
875 Shane, David Grahame. “The Emergence of ‘Landscape Urbanism’ - Reflections on Stalking Detroit.” 
Harvard Design Magazine Fall2003/ Winter2004: 7. 
876 Ibid. 5. 
877 Ibid, referring to: Debord, Guy. Society of the Spectacle. Detroit: Black & Red, 1983.

## S. 392

389 
 
had an intense relation between a seminar on theory and a mapping project, which then 
went on to be a design project and a book called Technique. This generated a lot of 
thinking about urban space. As an outcome, they were associating ideas of the terrain 
vague and landscape with notions like inflection, catastrophe and especially aesthetics and 
experience on the ground. 
In this respect, although the understanding of the concept of terrain vague was 
similar on the theoretical level, the ideas and approaches that stemmed from the Royal 
Melbourne Institute of Technology and the Victoria University of Wellington were rather 
different from those of landscape urbanism. One could even argue that Peter Connolly’s 
approach was, and still is, more faithful to the terrain vague than that of landscape 
urbanists. Similarly to Stalker in Rome, Connolly tackles the terrain vague through handson experience878. The landscape architect puts a lot of emphasis on fieldwork and looking 
at real examples. He generally invites his students to submerge themselves in the terrain 
vague before deciding to intervene on it. He states: “I take students out on-site for one 
hour each week, we walk around and we'll just talk about landscape and they learn 
theory and techniques in the field.”879 In this regard, Connolly’s interest is landscape 
architectural design techniques but he doesn’t consider his work to be under landscape 
urbanism. 
As in Solà-Morales’s work, Connolly also relies frequently on the theories of Gilles 
Deleuze and Félix Guattari to understand the urban fabric; he even argues, in his doctoral 
thesis 880 , that landscape urbanism misunderstands Deleuzian concepts. According to 
Connolly, landscape urbanism misinterprets the philosophers’ concepts of effects and 
affects, as well as aesthetics. Affect in Deleuze’s thought belongs to the anonymous 
involuntary workings of the world. Urban landscapes, including the terrain vague, 
involve effects as well as affects, which is an idea that Solà-Morales also evoked, as already 
mentioned in previous chapters. Connolly finds thus a discrepancy between the terrain 
vague notion and landscape urbanists’ methods. Connolly adds moreover that landscape 
urbanism does not address the idea of “expression” as the type of knowledge that allows 
understanding affect beyond the limits of typology and program. 
Connolly has been criticizing landscape urbanism almost since it started because 
he understood it as a form of urbanism of landscape that is too architecturally driven. In 
addition, he argues in his thesis that there were important aesthetic questions, in relation 
to landscape, which got pushed aside due to the rise of landscape urbanism and they 
didn’t reappear since. He believes that some practices in architecture lead architects to 
                                                                                                                                                                        
878 Interview between Author and Peter Connolly. 26 July 2017. 
879 Interview between Author and Peter Connolly. 26 July 2017. 
880 Connolly, Peter. “An Affirmative Open Systems Conception of How to Design Landscape.” PhD in Urban 
Design Thesis. School of Architecture and Design, RMIT University, 1994.

## S. 393

390 
pay little attention to affects and aesthetics of landscape, which he considers quite 
important. By “aesthetics”, however, Connolly does not mean “beauty”, but rather the 
anonymous workings of the landscape. 
Finally, Connolly addresses Deleuze’s ideas on time and fluxus. He criticizes once 
more landscape urbanism as not properly and accurately dealing with open systems, 
affects or time in a way faithful to Deleuze and Guattari’s original theories. Landscape 
urbanists do not engage with affects and with engaging themselves in the middle, a 
Deleuzian theory already described in the first chapter. They thus mistake movement and 
change over time with what the world really does. 
By the same token, a critic of landscape urbanism is that the movement adopts 
dubiously philosophical and theoretical concepts, especially from Deleuze and Derrida, 
which only confuses practitioners and alienates a majority of the professional audience. 
As Ian Thompson describes: 
“Unhelpful imagery and obscurantist language that Landscape Urbanism ought to dump; 
Larding the case for Landscape Urbanism with Deleuzian and Derridean references was a 
mistake, since it was done principally to impress an academic elite, and it has even left large 
sections of its intended audience bemused.”881 
Likewise, Christophe Girot, in an interview with the author882, describes landscape 
urbanism of today as lacking some of the ideological ground it initially adopted. 
Nonetheless, one can in turn argue that it is precisely this amnesia to preexisting 
urban models that gives the movement its chief strength. As urban theoretician David 
Shane states: 
“Its greatest strength lies in a determination not to accept the readymade formulas of 
urban design, whether “New Urbanist” or “Generic” urbanist megaforms à la Koolhaas. 
Landscape Urbanists want to continue the search for a new basis of a performative urbanism 
that emerges from the bottom up, geared to the technological and ecological realities of the 
postindustrial world.”883 
One of the movement’s strongest points is thus their openness to innovation and 
creative collaborative and interdisciplinary amalgamations unbound by former urban 
cases or hefty theoretical ideologies. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
881 Thompson, Ian Hamilton. “Ten Tenets and Six Questions for Landscape Urbanism.” Landscape Research 
37.1 (2012): 7–26. 
882 Interview between Author and Christophe Girot in Zurich. 11 Apr. 2013. 
883 Shane, David Grahame. “The Emergence of ‘Landscape Urbanism’ - Reflections on Stalking Detroit.” 
Harvard Design Magazine Fall2003/ Winter2004: 5-7.

## S. 394

391 
 
Inverted modes of perception and intervention 
Thus, in summary, Solà-Morales’s essay offers another mode of perceiving the 
urban fabric and intervening in it. The author suggests viewing the city as an assemblage 
of fragments, of which the terrain vague is an essential element. On a general note, the 
development of the terrain vague notion reflects some of the changes witnessed in the 
architectural field in general and in turn affects it. Architects turning towards smaller 
scale and interstitial projects, the melting of the field into an interdisciplinary fusion, and 
the changing role of architects as organizers and collaborators rather than masters of the 
situation, are all visible transformations in the influence of the terrain vague notion. 
Drawing conclusions from the various influenced cases, the notion of potential 
seems to be the running attraction of Solà-Morales’s essay. Its impact is visible on how 
and how much the text was consequently praised and adopted, and this happened on two 
levels: variations in interpretations and usages of the essay, and the recurrence of 'vague' 
connotations. The combination of a timely theme, an inspiring essay, an interdisciplinary 
formulation and a powerful author-theoretician thus proved to be a great recipe for 
establishing the terrain vague discourse. 
What we can see, moreover, reflected in the case studies are the underlying 
characteristic elements of the terrain vague as described in the essay: territoriality, 
vagueness, wandering, potential, resistance, intensities, and continuity. The outcome is an 
amalgam of traits set forth by the essay mixed with the experience and observations 
related to the various cases, all of which finally portray a rounded vision of the 
complexity inherent in the terrain vague. The diversity of directions taken in the 
reception of Solà-Morales’s essay also evokes the potential for wide-ranging 
interpretations of how to intervene, or not, in the terrain vague. One can imagine a 
grayscale884 stretching from merely reflecting on the subject, to raising awareness of these 
spaces in the city, to extensive intervention in them. 
From the different case studies afore-presented, one can conclude that the 
specificity of terrains vagues lies in the way they link different logics (spatial and social) 
that often seem separated. In the different case studied, we witnessed how various 
attitudes towards the terrain vague shape the creative process and the final outcome of the 
project. Form, function and materiality, which often compose the guidelines of 
architecture, turn, in this context, into secondary layers; the terrain vague becomes the 
notion through which we discuss these criteria. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
884 We use the word grayscale because the range of approaches towards the terrain vague should not include 
black and white sides. There is no positive direction in intervention modes.

## S. 395

392 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
CONCLUSION

## S. 396

393 
 
The essay, a notion’s traveling medium 
 “[The terrain vague is] a process of unfolding, of differentiation, and of a quest for 
information that is often not quantifiable and escapes the logic of analysis systems of 
traditional urbanism.” 
 
 Ignasi de Sola-Morales, ‘Terrain Vague’ in Anyplace 
 
Through this research, we were able to realize how a notion, the terrain vague, 
thrived through the medium of an essay, Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague", since the mid-
1990s when it was first written. We also observed how approaches and reflections on 
terrains vagues have become increasingly significant in the urban thought of the last 
twenty years. This seems to substantiate how the terrain vague notion evolved in marked 
urban approaches towards a discourse larger than a mere idea. Such a notion, which 
appears to combine seemingly antagonist meanings of both vagueness and territoriality, 
and of both fear and hope, proves to hold a strong potential for urban change. It is capable 
of responding to multiple issues, questions and problems posed by the contemporary 
urban fabric composed of fragmented diversified substances. 
As both Solà-Morales’s essay and the notion thrived in concert, they were jointly 
analyzed, and the correlation between the two was deemed worthy of special 
consideration. As a consequence, a good part of this thesis was to understand the 
connections between the historical political urban and architectural contexts of the 
terrain vague notion on the one hand, and the contexts of the essay and its author on the 
other. From the notion’s history line, to the author’s trajectory and writings, to the urban 
transformations of the 1990s, many lines thus intersect through one essay that is 
accordingly the central object and case study of the thesis. 
The purpose of this research was to verify the hypothesis that Solà-Morales’s 
“Terrain Vague” essay is a political statement and a hidden manifesto whose formulation, 
presentation and diffusion led to its status as base reference and to the vibrancy of the 
terrain vague discourse from the mid-1990s onward. Behind this hypothesis, some main 
questions arose regarding the position of the essay within the terrain vague discourse: was 
the interest in the notion triggered by the successful diffusion of the essay? Was it 
triggered by the essay’s inherent qualities, its inspirational writing and message? Can Solà-
Morales be considered the inventor of the terrain vague discourse? And finally, what was 
the inspiration behind writing the essay? 
The approach was similar to examining ripples when a pebble drops in water, i.e. 
the study started with an analysis of the core essay, then the inquiry was extended to 
understand its many references, next the focus enlarged even more to include the various 
contexts linked to the essay, its author and the terrain vague notion. The scope was finally 
broadened once more to observe the various reverberations Solà-Morales’s essay

## S. 397

394 
generated from the 1990s onward and the main directions that the effects of the essay and 
terrain vague notion took that could still be compelling for the present-day concerns of 
the urban and architectural fields. 
Recapitulation of the chapters 
In order to respond to the aforesaid questions and hypothesis, we needed 
principally to demystify the general fascination with Solà-Morales’s essay and the terrain 
vague notion. We hence tackled both in parallel, linking the two hither and thither. Since 
not much can be found written around the history of neither the essay nor the notion, 
despite the renown, a major part of the thesis was to reconstruct the essay’s chronology 
and its contexts, as well as the various contexts behind the terrain vague notion. We thus 
disclosed how the notion belonged to a historical line of urban and architectural exertions 
since as early as the 19th century. Another good part of this research was a thorough 
analysis of Solà-Morales’s essay, treating it as a core case study as another thesis would 
treat an architectural project or an architect’s profile. 
Accordingly, the first chapter of the thesis entailed situating the terrain vague 
notion within a theoretical frame before tackling the essay; it presents a recount of the 
historical development line of the notion before Solà-Morales’s essay. This chapter also 
includes an initial cultural and urban contextualization of the essay and a relevant 
account of its author. As the term “terrain vague” in particular was not expressly used in 
the architectural and urban fields before the essay, we attempted at closing gaps by 
following the various connotations associated with the discourse. We also explored the 
architectural practice of urban resistance against the hegemony of the orderly city, which 
the terrain vague notion appears to be following. A spectrum of terminologies was thus 
uncovered around the notion. The first chapter hence relates to the different nuances of 
the terrain vague including territoriality and vagueness, but also interstitiality, resistance, 
decay, ruins, borderlines and potentiality. The thesis thus begins with a journey into a 
‘prehistory’ of the terrain vague notion, before the birth of the centerpiece, i.e. the 
"Terrain Vague" essay. 
The second and key chapter then focuses solely on analyzing Solà-Morales’s text. 
The essay was asserted as the birth of the notion of terrain vague for the architectural and 
urban fields through Solà-Morales’s various interdisciplinary viewpoints. The mechanisms 
behind the elaborate essay were explored. Whether through targeting various audiences, a 
highly evocative, polarized and emotional writing style, a fragmented multilayered 
presentation of the notion, or a conclusive call for action aimed at architecture and urban 
practitioners, a simple rendering of an urban notion became, through the author’s 
manipulation, the principal manual on the terrain vague.

## S. 398

395 
 
The analysis of Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" revealed furthermore that this text 
embraces more than contemplation on the terrain vague subject. A series of references in 
the essay creates a network of reflections and connotations, from which distinct imagery 
begins to elucidate what the terrain vague could be or incorporate, as much in their 
theoretical delineations as in their visual materializations. Accordingly, the third chapter 
was an analysis of what the terrain vague represents through the collection of theoretical 
and photographic references in the essay. We could thus deduce that the terrain vague is 
not a response to a specific case, but a mode of conceiving a variety of contemporary 
urban spaces and situations, from leftover spaces to interstitial areas to post-industrial 
remains. By this token, Solà-Morales was able to create a collective imagery and a 
theoretical baseline around his coining of the terrain vague. This had positive and more 
assertive repercussions on how the notion was received and how the essay became a 
central reference to the discourse and to what the terrain vague could signify. 
Bearing in mind the inherent strength of the essay, Solà-Morales sought to present 
and to publish it in various contexts worldwide. This journey in turn affected the course 
of the notion and the development of the essay beyond its first draft written in 1993. The 
fourth chapter was hence a broadening of the scope beyond the text and its references. 
We realized in this manner how the essay’s various publications and presentations 
contributed in two ways to the renown the terrain vague notion received. On the one 
hand, it helped the notion develop into a prominent discourse on contemporary urban 
spaces. The essay and its presentations incorporated some circulating ideas and urban 
impressions of the time. On the other hand, the multiple publications of the "Terrain 
Vague" essay helped in diffusing the notion to a multitude of places and cities and to a 
variety of people of many disciplines. In return, many were inspired by the essay, which 
triggered an equally diverse array of reactions, interpretations and interventions of 
terrains vagues. On a parallel note, Solà-Morales’s position and contexts greatly affected 
how the essay was received. Firstly, in the mid-1990s, Solà-Morales experimented initially 
with the notion within the Metropolis Master program in Barcelona, which triggered a 
first interdisciplinary reaction within Barcelona beyond the local interest in urban spaces 
that had already begun in the city since its transformations prior to the 1992 Olympic 
Games. Secondly, still in 1993, Solà-Morales introduced the notion to the academic circles 
of the United States via Princeton University, who were already interested during this 
phase in concepts such as ruins, decay and urban spaces through the classes and works of 
different professors including Alan Colquhoun, Georges Teyssot and Alessandra Ponte. 
The notion triggered also interest there among students who adopted the notion in their 
theses, but also among the teachers and theoreticians who requested other presentations 
of Solà-Morales. In return, Solà-Morales’s interactions with the theoreticians at Princeton 
and other circles in the United States helped the author concretize his ideas around the 
terrain vague and finally write during this year his "Terrain Vague" essay.

## S. 399

396 
Thirdly, in 1994 and 1995, Solà-Morales alternated presentations and publications 
of his essay between a seminar at the Triennale di Milano and the Anyplace conference of 
the Anyone Corporation at the CCA in Montreal. The former was an experimental 
platform to test his ideas while the latter was the official launching of what would 
become his most treasured publication and his most celebrated notion and contribution to 
the field. The Triennale di Milano was a pertinent context to present the notion due to 
the city’s post-industrial history filled with terrains vagues, and due to the Triennale 
event being equally experimental and interdisciplinary as was Solà-Morales’s essay. In 
parallel, the Anyone Corporation was of great importance to Solà-Morales. His 
involvement in the corporation propelled his name and work worldwide. The terrain 
vague notion thus benefited from this wide-reaching limelight through Solà-Morales’s 
presentation and publications within the Anyplace conference. A sizeable international 
crowd attended the lectures. Solà-Morales presented the notion in a form as 
interdisciplinary as the intent of the Anyplace conference. His most recognizable 
presentation of the essay came moreover devoid of images or displays, following the 
requests of the organizers. The presentation, as seen in videos, was moreover edging the 
dramatic as Solà-Morales read carefully and slowly each word of his essay in a semiprivate lecture broadcasted on screens to the wider audience of the conference. The 
notion was in this manner presented through the power of words, which consolidated the 
link between the essay and the notion. The essay was also published within the Anyplace 
proceedings with only one accompanying image. This provides one explanation as to why 
the "Terrain Vague" essay is always mentioned and often quoted, word by word, 
whenever the terrain vague notion is discussed in present time. On a different note, the 
city of Montreal also seemed an adequate context in which to present the notion. The 
city’s history and contemporary project have often displayed interest in the city’s diverse 
terrains vagues. From Luc Lévesque to Village Ephémère, the terrain vague after Solà-
Morales’s presentation still is an ongoing special subject to locals. And finally, beyond 
1995, Solà-Morales brought back the terrain vague to his city of origin, Barcelona. 
Through a series of republications within Quaderns and through organizing the XIX UIA 
Congress in 1996, with the terrain vague being one of its main themes, the author thus 
solidified the importance of the terrain vague notion to the world. 
Accordingly, the terrain vague notion was reflecting the context of the 1990s 
already prone to accepting such concepts on alternative forms of understanding and 
intervening on the urban fabric. Transformations for instance of post-industrial cities, of 
sprawling cities and of shrinking cities, instigated interest in such ideas in reaction to the 
major changes of the time occurring within cities and on the peripheries. Be it Milano, 
Montreal or Barcelona, Solà-Morales’s "Terrain Vague" essay was thus well received.

## S. 400

397 
 
On an equally important note, the position of Solà-Morales as a theoretician, 
philosopher and architect within various prominent intellectual scenes seems to have also 
secured a strong reception of the notion. Solà-Morales’s archives alone reveal how the 
interaction between the theoreticians, artists, scholarly figures and architects of the 1990s 
established a semi-closed circle of friends, colleagues and acquaintances that could even 
be described as a powerful theoretical ‘exclusive cluster’. One could seemingly link many, 
if not most, of the important architectural and urban theories of this period to this tacit 
group of intellectuals. From the Triennale di Milano, to the CCA, Princeton, the UIA 
Congress and the Anyone Corporation, the theoretical domain still maintained during this 
phase a strong lingering, though withering, position within the architectural and urban 
professions. The terrain vague notion thus belonged to this theoretical realm that still 
sustained a strong influence and an effect on architectural, urban and artistic 
interventions within the late 1990s and even today. This context at the end of the 
millennium was thus important to understand the terrain vague notion, comparable to 
how other adjacent notions also belonged to different contexts such as "dérive" within the 
Situationists’ framework or "flâneur" for Walter Benjamin’s wanderings or "drosscape" for 
Alan Berger’s American cities. 
The "Terrain Vague" essay and the associated notions were thus reinforced by a 
variety of supports linked to Solà-Morales: a strong network of influential figures, 
important theoretical and interdisciplinary contexts, relevant transformations in urban 
settings within prominent cities, an array of intellectual events, presentations and 
publications, as well as a timeframe theoretically predisposed to such notions, and finally 
an audience prone to positively accede to such ideas of alternative urban approaches. 
One could state at this point that the terrain vague notion was on the one hand 
empowered by the inherent strength and intensity of Solà-Morales’s essay and the array 
of collective imagery created by his references, as observed in the second and third 
chapter. One could also find this empowerment, on the other hand, within the contexts 
and chronology that the essay and notion insinuated from the 1960s onward as observed 
in the first chapter, and that they beheld from the 1990s onward, as exposed in the fourth 
and fifth chapters. 
As a result, the influence of the "Terrain Vague" essay and notion described in the 
fifth chapter reveal many links with the directions and connotations observed in the 
previous chapters. First, similar connotations as those of the array of terminologies are 
again apparent. Second, the different sections of the essay are reflected in the reactions. 
Third, the influence of the essay and the resulting interventions show an array of 
approaches and interpretations as versatile as the array of terrains vagues included in the 
essay and its written and visual references. Fourth, the influence of the essay reflects the

## S. 401

398 
interdisciplinary interests of the period, and evidently, some of those who were strongly 
inspired by Solà-Morales’s essay were also involved in or at least present at one or more of 
the events in which Solà-Morales presented the "Terrain Vague". Many of those inspired 
by the essay were also of the same cities in which the author presented his work and in 
which he was influential, i.e. mainly Barcelona, Montreal, Milano, but also Berlin, Rome, 
and Melbourne. 
The example cases described in the fifth chapter are evidently not exhaustive in 
portraying the reception and influence of the essay and notion. They show nonetheless an 
array of what intervening on the terrain vague implied, and what it could still mean in 
the contemporary city as many of those described in the cases initiated their work in the 
1990s but are still ongoing nowadays. 
Finally, as one of the most problematic characteristics of terrains vagues is the 
common inability to define and categorize them, this research, as Solà-Morales’s essay, is 
an attempt to locate the terrain vague within architectural theoretical and professional 
discourses of the 1990s onward, as well as its present-day relevance. All chapters, 
including the fifth, were devised with this framing intent in mind. 
Solà-Morales’s impact: a synthesizing and catalyst moment 
In view of the previous, the "Terrain Vague" essay is revealed as both a synthesist 
of an era’s theoretical and urban transformations and its proclivity towards such notions, 
as well as a catalyst for interpretations and interventions in the terrain vague and onto the 
city. Solà-Morales seems to have gathered within one essay a thousand and one nights of 
notions and impressions pertinent to the last decades at the end of the millennium and to 
an emerging type of urban spaces that caught the attention of his contemporaries. 
Through his coining and the way he sheathed the terrain vague notion and presented it to 
the world, Solà-Morales seems to have struck a nerve in urban and architectural practices. 
Moreover, the double innuendo within his essay, alluding to a narrative tale on the one 
hand and a hidden manifesto on the other, reflects this oscillation between a synthesizing 
narrative of a period and a catalyzing call for action and for raising awareness. 
A multifaceted synthesis 
As aforementioned, the strength of Solà-Morales’s terrain vague is in part due to 
its synthesizing of a period’s issues into one essay and notion. In return, this synthesis of 
the conditions and issues of a period seems to be Solà-Morales’s method to ground and 
embed the terrain vague within a context. The coined term is thus turned into a central

## S. 402

399 
 
notion by rooting it in the 1990s; and Solà-Morales’s act of coining of the terrain vague 
term was key in producing this synthesis. 
The essay was the travelling medium for the terrain vague notion to be presented 
and propagated, but the medium itself has also proven significant in its own right as a 
theoretical construct. The position of the essay as architectural writing, the form in which 
it is written and how it was published reveal how it was a synthesist of the 1990s era. 
Firstly, the essay reflects the urban and historical contexts of late 20th century, an 
era conscious of the damages caused by the World Wars, the Cold War, the fall of the 
Berlin Wall and the collapse of the USSR. Solà-Morales refers indirectly in the essay to 
this historical layer through the list of photographic references that portray, in word and 
visually, how the terrain vague notion arose from this context. Solà-Morales hints at this 
setting being a trigger of interest towards such indefinite spaces emerging, in the city and 
on its outskirts, from political and urban traumatic incidents such as a war, a postindustrial history or a diplomatic tension. On a more particular note, Solà-Morales’s essay 
seems to address some urban transformations on cities of the period: the peripheries of a 
post-Olympic Barcelona, a central mega-terrain vague in the heart of Berlin, a 
deteriorating Detroit city-turned-terrain vague, a post-industrial Montreal with 
cosmopolitan varieties of terrains vagues or even Rotterdam’s and Milano’s post-industrial 
gentrification. The "Terrain Vague" essay also alluded to the economic changes of the 
period as Solà-Morales seemed to stand against the economic hegemony of Late 
Capitalism. He addressed moreover separately each of these city-cases throughout his 
work from the 1980s to the beginning of the 2000s885. 
Secondly, Solà-Morales’s essay found resonance in a diverse and interdisciplinary 
multitude of interested people because it unerringly portrayed the urban social and 
psychological conditions of dwelling in the contemporary, mainly Western, city of the 
1990s. From the feeling of alienation to the need for freedom and escape, Solà-Morales 
successfully married these emotions with the political and economic mood of the cities 
afore-described, and more importantly with the condition of experiencing and dwelling 
in the terrain vague. 
Thirdly, the essay could also be considered a synthesist of the period’s 
architectural and urban concerns. On the one hand, Solà-Morales’s work during the 1990s 
seems to belong to an undeclared group of theoreticians and architects who resisted the 
wave of the self-proclaimed ‘postmodernists’ that propagated after Portoghesi’s Venice 
Biennale of 1980 celebrating this wave. The essay could be considered one proposed 
                                                                                                                                                                        
885 As described in the chapters, Solà-Morales’s work should be understood as a continuum where one essay 
feeds into another through the last two decades of the century.

## S. 403

400 
alternative approach to the urban against the postmodern celebration of architectural 
built forms and aesthetics. 
On the other hand, the essay reflects some of the transformations that the 
architectural and urban profession witnessed at the end of the millennium, be it on a 
theoretical level or for practitioners. The role of architects in urban transformations, and 
the scope of this role, was the core point debated in prominent discussions of the period 
and within publications and conferences, including the XIX UIA Congress of 1996. In 
parallel, architecture theory was slowly losing ground due to analogous causes: a drastic 
change in scale of urban concerns due to globalization and the resulting problems of some 
cities in sprawl and other shrinking cities, as well as a massive profusion and diffusion of 
theoretical production devoid of genuine monitoring. Predecessors to Solà-Morales in 
thinking the city through its indeterminate spaces had moreover instigated the challenge: 
they left a city in paralysis in face of the current abundance of theoretical writings. Solà-
Morales’s essay was written nonetheless in a phase where architecture theory, as 
aforesaid, seemed to still attract a focused international interest, especially attributable to 
such collaborations as the Anyone Corporation. The "Terrain Vague" essay thus benefited 
from this seemingly remaining boost of theoretical might in the face of challenges 
overpowering the role of the architect. The terrain vague notion was accordingly 
presented by Solà-Morales as a form of opposition to this loss of power and to these 
changes. He saw in the terrain vague both the consequence and the resolution of such 
conflicts. 
Fourth, Solà-Morales’s presentation of the terrain vague notion also echoed the 
period’s interdisciplinary interests. In the face of the changes inflicting the architecture 
profession, many turned (and still do) to interdisciplinarity as a solution to mend the fast 
pace of urban growth and transformations. The Anyone Corporation, and the Anyplace 
conference, was one such group; but also the Triennale di Milano worked in this 
direction, as well as Quaderns and other journals and collectives. And Solà-Morales’s 
adoption of sociology, psychology, photography and other fields to define urban spaces 
such as terrains vagues fit perfectly this reflection about the limits of the architecture’s 
profession. 
As a result, Solà-Morales’s essay embedded the notion in the context of the 1990s 
on many layers. This created a base for the creation of a terrain vague discourse. The 
notion could be hence imagined as the point of convergence of many aspects linked to the 
social political economic and urban contexts and architectural and urban transformations 
of the period from the 1990s onward. The notion could almost also be considered as a 
ripple effect from concerns regarding globalization, interdisciplinarity, a changing role of 
architects and a fast pace of urban transformations at the end of the millennium.

## S. 404

401 
 
Moreover, the notion is at the crosspoint of different continuing theoretical lines. 
First, Solà-Morales’s publications and lectures over two decades between the 1980s and 
1990s could be put under one umbrella that define a new mode of perceiving the urban 
fabric. The terrain vague takes forefront stage in this interpretation as Solà-Morales 
employs many philosophical and theoretical ideas to determine an experimental approach 
to the urban as a collection of fragments that should be treated through their different 
uniqueness. 
Second, the terrain vague notion and essay, as mentioned on various occasions 
throughout the thesis, seem to indirectly allude to adjacent concepts and affiliated ideas 
previously tackled since at least the 1940s, but mainly since the 1960s in the urban and 
architectural field. The most prominent notion among these is the notion of resistance 
through acts of wandering and attention to continuity. It is a major line connecting the 
terrain vague notion to a stretch of approaches from the Surrealists to the Situationists and 
finally to Lara Almarcegui, Luc Lévesque and the Stalker collective. The terrain vague 
notion is thus a synthesis as well of this stream of thought since the 1940s onward and a 
continuation of this form of interpreting and intervening the urban fabric. In this sense, 
Solà-Morales’s formulation of his essay in a ‘tale-like’ narrative construct could be 
affiliated with this approach in which the gradual experience and narrative of urban 
spaces and of the terrain vague are the core interest and legitimacy of the method. 
All these devices helped therefore the notion be grounded on many layers and 
allowed the terrain vague to be close to a ‘representative’ phenomenon of the urban 
transformations and architectural professional changes at the end of the millennium. We 
realized henceforth through the first three chapters that a growing interest in approaches 
such as the terrain vague developed through the last decades of the 20th century. In 
contrast, by freeing itself from the constraint of having to decide between one or the 
other of the existing urbanist dogmas – i.e. a dense city or a spreading city, a historic city 
or a modern one, a city center or a peripheral city, a sprawling city, and the like - the 
terrain vague approach seems fundamental in this new era of sustainable development, 
because it proposes an innovative urban approach made up of various substances: 
programs, landscapes, transport systems and architecture inputs. Then the terrains vagues 
become, in this continuous and heterogeneous urbanity, the guarantors of a continuity, 
and reveal the strong identity of places. In this respect, the "Terrain Vague" essay and 
notion, besides being synthesists of an era and of its architectural approaches and changes, 
could also be considered catalysts of urban and architectural devices from the 1990s 
onward, as described in what follows.

## S. 405

402 
A catalyst of visions and mediations 
The synthesizing character of Solà-Morales’s essay and its coined notion had 
repercussions on modes of intervening on the urban fabric, be it in the 1990s or presentday. The terrain vague can be hence equally considered a catalyst of changes in 
perception and intervention due to the essay and its author, its contexts and histories, as 
much as due to the notion bringing answers and triggering development. Once again, the 
essay and the notion are hard to separate; each possesses qualities that helped the terrain 
vague become a catalyst of change. 
Firstly, the essay possesses appropriate inherent qualities, as described in the first, 
second and third chapters. It was written in a way to incite emotions towards our urban 
surrounding and the terrain vague potential, and to stimulate the imagination of the 
reader through various conceptions. Its style could also be assimilated to a hidden 
manifesto ending with a call for action and for intervention in the terrain vague. 
Secondly, as observed in the fourth and fifth chapters, the essay and author’s contexts 
played also a particular role in turning the notion into the influential discourse it held 
from the 1990s until nowadays. The terrain vague was effectively a “travelling concept”886 
through the worldwide journey of the essay; its influence was hence relational to this 
diffusion. More importantly, it was the same powerful contexts of the Anyone 
Corporation, the UIA Congress and the Triennale di Milano that assured an attentive 
interdisciplinary audience still in tune with the importance of theoretical works. The 
essay thus inspired a large, diverse and international circle of theoreticians and 
professionals. 
Thirdly, the terrain vague notion was inherently and of its own accord powerful 
as a catalyst considering the context of the architecture profession at the end of the 
millennium. Facing the aforementioned urban concerns of the period, the terrain vague 
represented a promise of hope and potential for positive change. The terrain vague was 
thus as much referring to particular urban spaces within and without the city, as it was a 
representation of resolution of contemporary conflicts in relation to the transforming 
urban fabric and to the changing architectural profession. Moreover, the notion being 
fundamentally interdisciplinary triggered inspiration beyond the architectural discipline, 
prompting the latter to shift towards more collaborative methods. 
As a result, both essay and notion profited from their role as synthesists of a 
period’s contexts in order to equally become catalysts of transformations and resolutions. 
Solà-Morales thus helped, through his coining of the terrain vague term, label and pin 
                                                                                                                                                                        
886 Referring once again to Mieke Bal’s concept. – Bal, Mieke. Travelling Concepts in the Humanities: A 
Rough Guide. Toronto: University of Toronto Press, 2002.

## S. 406

403 
 
down the notion and made it tangible and usable in the theoretical realm as well as for 
architecture and urbanism practices. 
Implications   for   the   architectural   profession   
An important aspect to mention here is how the terrain vague notion and essay go 
beyond a mere idea and present an urban vision based on the indeterminate in urban 
dwelling. Following Henri Lefebvre’s ideas in Critique of Everyday Life 887, static 
explanations of urban life are insufficient; an interdisciplinary and intricate mode is 
needed to understand everyday life in the city. Lefebvre moreover advocates for an open 
dialogue between cultural representations, everyday life and the construction of the city. 
Solà-Morales’s notion of terrain vague provides accordingly an interdisciplinary tool to 
enable this dialogue and to reassert the importance allocated by Lefebvre to 
representations of space. The terrain vague has hence proven to be, as instigated in the 
introduction of this thesis, a perfect urban moment in which physical presence, 
representation and direct experience are thoroughly reevaluated towards new creative 
forms of urbanity befitting of contemporary transforming cities. 
Subsequently, the triggered change introduced by Solà-Morales’s terrain vague 
had two facets. Firstly, it had implications on how one perceives urban space and in 
particular the relevance of the terrain vague to the city and its inhabitants. Solà-Morales 
promoted a vision of the urban fabric as being composed of a diverse network of 
intensities and fragments where the experience of urban spaces, especially that of terrains 
vagues, plays a major role in valorizing city dwelling. In this urban vision, the meaning, 
place and value of terrains vagues shifted to a more central standing in the urban 
discourse after Solà-Morales’s essay. The author elevated in this fashion the notion’s 
position in the architectural discourse beyond a mere type of space. 
Secondly, there were implications in relation to architectural and urban 
interventions. Those who were inspired by the terrain vague notion shifted their 
attention to alternative forms of approaching urban spaces. One could detect a reversal in 
these mediations from the built to the non-built and from the orderly city and its 
architectural objects and aesthetics to the terrain vague. Solà-Morales thus generated an 
inverse approach: the terrain vague becoming, instead of planned urban spaces, the 
strategy of comprehending the urban and mediating on it. This thesis research 
subsequently uncovers how the terrain vague is more than an urban space type and how it 
incorporates an alternative mode of perceiving and intervening on the urban fabric 
through an act of inversion of emphasis. This perspective for urbanism focused on the 
                                                                                                                                                                        
887 Lefebvre, Henri. Critique of Everyday Life. London  ; New York: Verso, 1991.

## S. 407

404 
terrain vague presents new potential urban strategies capable of bringing innovative 
answers to the urban concerns of the contemporary city. Be they Lara Almarcegui, Carole 
Lévesque, the collective Stalker, Village Ephémère or other interdisciplinary groups 
engaged in the terrain vague, the intent is often towards finding creative forms of urban 
intervention. 
Moreover, as theoretical and architectural works often quoted diametrically Solà-
Morales’s essay, inspired interventions in the terrain vague often followed the essay’s 
sections and inferences. Correspondingly, many interventions followed either an artistic 
interdisciplinary approach focused on art and photography as in the first sections of the 
essay, a social etymological approach focused on socially redefining urban indeterminate 
spaces as in the middle sections of the essay, or finally an activism call for change and 
awareness approach focused on the idea of an urban continuity corresponding to the last 
sections of the essay. 
Towards   a   continuum   within   the   grayscale   
The modes of interventions inspired by the essay could as well be laid out on a 
grayscale oscillating between two extremes: full action and construction on the terrain 
vague on one side, and nil physical intervention or construction on the other. We refer to 
it as a grayscale because we consider all the interventions within this array as belonging to 
gray tones between these two poles, devoid of positive or negative judgment. Neither full 
construction nor lack of construction is deemed here as categorically detrimental 
approaches. The criteria for evaluating these intervention modes lay instead in their 
adeptness to preserve continuity and intensities. Solà-Morales in fact does not advocate 
for one form of intervention over another, instead he encourages all interventions that 
ensure continuity in time and intensities and fluxus in the city, ideas most probably 
inspired by Deleuzian and Benjaminian urban philosophies. 
On this grayscale, the first most prominent type of approach within the essay’s 
influenced reactions is the ‘interdisciplinary approach’, which gets closest to the ‘nointervention’ extreme. The second most noticeable approach mode is the focus on the 
interstitial scale of the terrain vague and on minute architectural interventions, which 
gets closer to the ‘construction’ extreme. As for the third mode, the activist approach, this 
seems the most-balanced within the illustrated interventions, standing almost equally 
between the two poles since the physical intervention in the terrain vague is tailored in 
this approach to each urban case in particular. This last approach is important since it 
seems to be the closest to preserving Solà-Morales’s idea of urban continuity. In this 
fashion, Solà-Morales’s essay was thus one attempt at facing the sweeping waves of 
globalization and the profusion of architectural theory. The grayscale thus offers an array

## S. 408

405 
 
of solutions following Solà-Morales’s ideas in the face of urban and architectural problems 
simmering since the 1990s. Contemporary approaches on the terrain vague integrate 
moreover various protagonists from different domains, including the landscape, 
infrastructure, social and environmental sciences as well as urbanism or architecture. 
Accordingly, on the larger scale, the terrain vague helps resolve problems affecting the 
architectural profession at the end of the millennium. It represented the potential to 
transform the role of architects on the urban domain. In this viewpoint, architects are 
chiefly attentive to case specific spaces, acting more on collaborative and participatory 
terms with other urban stakeholders. 
The idea of resistance previously associated with the terrain vague resurfaces here
