as a provocation for architectural activism, calling for awareness of the value of terrains 
vagues and towards the potential hidden in such spaces and in the communities, contexts 
and histories surrounding them. A similar sense of resistance is also noticeable across the 
various influenced reactions to the essay: an opposition to the hegemony of the orderly 
city, and to the growing power of the image, the ‘star-architecture’, and the ‘postmodern’ 
wave. Correspondingly, the terrain vague notion was most welcome on account of the 
fresh potential and promise it generates. 
Relevance to the 21st century 
On a personal note, a comment made by Almarcegui during our interview affected 
me beyond the confines of this dissertation: 
“Descampados have existed in my imaginaire since I can remember; as many people of 
my generation, I used to play in the descampados. In the 1970s most of the middle class 
moved to the peripheries, which where not completely constructed, so we played a lot in the 
wastelands; My oldest memory of a great wasteland near my house: a tree and an old sofa, me 
jumping from the branch of a tree to an old sofa, again and again. Another memory is of a 
storm in a wasteland and some friends taking shelter and playing in a house under 
construction. I think these are memories we all share, isn’t it?”888 
 As a matter of fact, it is not. These are memories that we do not all share, which is 
unfortunate. My own interest in the terrain vague stemmed from memories shared by my 
own family, my mother and my aunts, describing how they used to often play in the 
terrains vagues around Beirut, rolling down its hills to reach the beach, back when the 
city was not destroyed by civil war and real estate speculative development. Around 
twenty-five years later, my own memories of terrains vagues were in comparison scarce. I 
remember for instance an old abandoned two-story house and its backyard in front of our 
nine-story building, in which I always imagined a witch living with her dog. Ten years 
afterwards, I fail to see how my young brother’s childhood had any recollection of any 
                                                                                                                                                                        
888 Correspondence between Author and Lara Almarcegui. Apr. 2017.

## S. 409

406 
imaginary world built around a terrain vague, since that old house, and many many 
others, were replaced by high-rise buildings. 
This dissertation was written in the aim of finding alternatives to how we perceive 
the city and its spaces and to give the terrain vague a comprehensive historical and 
theoretical grounding in order to highlight its hidden manifesto and transform the 
“Terrain Vague” essay into a tool for future urban implementations. In the same route of 
raising awareness, I believe that this approach is limited in presenting accurate options for 
design and implementation, yet opens the doors to understanding the terrain vague as 
spaces that require transformations in time in which the architect is not the sole 
mastermind. 
On a contemporary level, although the 1990s are less than thirty years ago, the 
period in which the "Terrain Vague" essay was written and presented is evidently 
different from present day. To our interest, architectural and urban concerns at the turn 
of the century seemed still anticipatory of the changes that were to be brought. A mix of 
anxiety and hope accompanied ideas of globalization, of cities in expansion and shrinking 
cities. At the beginning of the new millennium and nowadays, these concerns 
materialized into more concrete issues and the general focus shifted in part beyond 
Western cities. The terrain vague notion transformed as the scale grew larger. Attention 
shifted further to developing countries889 and to the international scale of globalization. 
Yet massive urban construction projects in Western cities, especially in Europe, lessened. 
And in parallel, the control of architects of the last century ebbed as political and real 
estate powers gained ground. The economic crisis of 2008 additionally debilitated the 
profession in the last decade. Consequently, architects still have to come to terms with 
these losses in power and with landscape and infrastructure becoming more and more 
significant with the rise of interest in sustainability. 
Nonetheless, the terrain vague still seems to be a present-day notion that has 
resonance among theoreticians and practitioners. At any rate, many of those involved 
with the terrain vague subject during the 1990s are still active and operational on such 
ideas. The relevance of the terrain vague notion grew beyond Solà-Morales’s essay and his 
period and contexts. And perhaps the terrain vague notion holds even more water now 
since the need to pin down the architectural profession grew stronger as architects keep 
losing decisive power on the urban stage with the growing scale of globalization and the 
growing might of economic powers on the urban fabric. 
                                                                                                                                                                        
889 This thesis reflected on the subject of terrain vague being mainly relevant in Western contexts and cities. 
One form of further developing on this research is to change the scope of focus toward developing countries 
and how the terrain vague notion could be, or not, relevant to other regions, such as the prominent terrains 
vagues around Shanghai or the Middle Eastern wastelands described in Almarcegui’s guides.

## S. 410

407 
 
As described in the fifth chapter, and as witnessed in the various interviews 
conducted throughout this research, the terrain vague notion still seems contemporary, 
although concerns are at times raised regarding the contemporary scale of terrains vagues. 
The potential and message of hope that Solà-Morales links to the terrain vague seem still 
inspirational for the contemporary architectural practice and valid for the urban concerns 
of the contemporary city. Moreover, the sub-notions and imagery presented in Solà-
Morales’s essay still seem to address the contemporary city. Photographers such as Manolo 
Laguillo still capture the terrain vague of the contemporary. Julia Schulz-Dornburg even 
photographed the new Spanish terrains vagues emerging between 2008 and 2012 out of 
the economic crisis. Moreover, Solà-Morales’s Deleuzian urban vision of fragments and 
networks took a more tangible meaning with the development of the internet and social 
networks affecting the urban space. Even his advocated ideas on ‘otherness’ take a 
contemporary shape with the masses of refugees inhabiting the terrains vagues in cities, 
on the outskirts or other borderline areas. 
Nonetheless, one could detect changes in approaches on the terrain vague between 
the period of the 1990s and nowadays. Within the grayscale of possible mediations, the 
contemporary wave of interest in the terrain vague notion seems more oriented towards 
smaller interventions in such spaces, in contrast with the large urban interventions 
attempted within the 1990s. Architects seem to be currently using the ‘interstitial’ 
dimension of the terrain vague as an innovative form of perceiving the fragmented 21st 
century urban frame and as a new opportunity to interfere on the latter through 
interdisciplinary interventions and changing formally established roles of authorship. In 
this fashion, interstitiality becomes an opportunity for recreation rather than deletion. 
Whether Almarcegui, Stalker or Lévesque, the interest seems focused on accentuating the 
ephemerality, fragility and temporality of the terrain vague, and that through raising 
awareness towards the potential of such spaces, rather than fully consuming this 
potential. More often than not, the idea of resistance against late capitalism, the market 
and against the need for urban and space consumption remains in the background. 
Perhaps this is the contemporary fascination with the terrain vague: an open-ended 
alternative for intervention beyond the dissolving borders of the architectural and urban 
professions, and beyond the limits of a collapsing financial and real-estate market 
overawed by economic crisis. As a consequence, Solà-Morales’s coining of the terrain 
vague notion is vital for the hope and potential it promises in face of a profession losing its 
power and an urban fabric in constant unmanageable expansion. Such a positioning of the 
subject helps understand whether and how the role of architect is altered by this 
contextual positioning of new modes of understanding not through mainstream creative 
processes but through interdisciplinary interventions in interstitial spaces, brown fields 
and other terrains vagues.

## S. 411

408 
The notion of ‘creative process’ is therefore contested when an architect interferes 
on the terrain vague. The duality between recreation and erasure is not a process owned 
by the authority of the architect. The work on the terrain vague is thus, contemporarily, a 
work of collaboration and resistance; it is a political act. However, that is maybe why it is 
a mode inherent to the capitalist system itself and to the city, the reverse coin of the same 
system. It brings change and possibly collapse to the normative systems, but it also cannot 
be made solely by one author. Hence, to consume such spaces and to make them clear, 
useful or signifying eradicates the quality and the capacity of such spaces. 
Nonetheless, despite the differences between the 1990s and contemporarily, the 
grayscale still offers nowadays an array of possible intervention styles on the terrain vague 
and consequently on the city in the broader sense of ‘intervention’. And it seems in this 
regard that Solà-Morales’s call for both action and continuity is finally a call to find a 
balance within this grayscale. The approaches that come closest to his ideas and essay are 
thus those who seek a balance between action and non-action. Accordingly, those who 
were most inspired by the "Terrain Vague" essay fall well in the center of this grayscale, 
such as many, if not most, of those described in the fifth chapter. 
Effectiveness   of   a   theoretical   tool   at   the   end   of   the   millennium      
In conclusion, we attempted throughout this thesis to understand what really lies 
behind the terrain vague notion. We aimed at comprehending and solidifying the notion 
by uncovering its contexts, its hidden connotations, and the way it was formulated, 
presented to the world and ultimately received by it. The terrain vague notion being 
repeatedly used but never fully explored, this research sheds light on the many aspects 
this notion has to offer beyond a kind of urban space. 
Through this research, the terrain vague notion had proven to be a key to 
understanding the interactions and contexts of the 1990s, the transformations that 
occurred on the theoretical level and on the urban level during this phase, and how the 
approaches to the terrain vague from the 1990s onwards can perhaps lead to positively 
and effectively dealing with some contemporary urban and architectural issues. 
Accordingly, Solà-Morales’s essay and the terrain vague notion were thus synthesists of a 
period but they continue to be catalysts of change within this era and beyond. In this 
respect, there seems to be new directions offered to architects via approaching the terrain 
vague. 
This thesis thus provides a useful base from which theoreticians and architects can 
produce new modes of perception and interventions from a terrain vague perspective. In 
other words, it helps opening up a larger perspective on how to understand the terrain

## S. 412

409 
 
vague, how to perceive such spaces within the urban fabric and how to potentially 
intervene, or not, on such spaces from a context-aware standpoint, including the subnotions, connotations, imagery and intervention styles presented. 
From the different case studies presented, one can conclude that the specificity of 
terrains vagues is in the way they link various spheres, scales and logics, spatial and social, 
that often seem separated. New city developments and autoregulated planning start to 
enclose a great deal of architectonic systems that multiply circulation and access ways in 
order to multiply possibilities of relations between inhabitants. What is thus significant is 
the relation between individuals, the relation to the other, the social and urban models at 
hand, the link between places of constraint, places of choice, those imposed, or those that 
link, or supporters of links, and likewise the rupture of such links. Accordingly, one can 
argue that we cannot feel the city and these links unless through the terrain vague. We 
feel intensities and extensities, deviations in sensual experiences, rather than the numbing 
of planned urban spaces. That is how we sense the city; the sensual is in the terrain vague, 
where the metamorphosis and the osmosis occur. According to Solà-Morales, employing 
Deleuze’s terms, architecture will always be on the side of forms, the figurative, the 
optical, “while the divided individual of the contemporary city would look for forces 
instead of the forms.” 890 
Finally, awareness of changes in connections and intensities thus becomes a key 
factor. Being conscious of one's actions and accountabilities are the vanguard tools to 
reshape the urban fabric through the terrain vague. Intervention, perception and 
reception become intimately linked with raising awareness, consciousness and 
participation. We return henceforth to the idea of transfer of knowledge and 
responsibility. Contemporary urban thought should occupy itself with the cultivation of 
two main skills: on the one hand the ability to readjust one's course and adaptively adhere 
to a constant rapidly changing environment, and on the other hand the dexterity of 
creating informal open cooperations that expand our horizon beyond our disciplinary 
limitations and traditional processes of creation. Only then would we be able to accept 
our shifting roles as coordinators, activists or awareness promoters rather than our 
stereotyped role as architectural masterminds and decision-makers on the urban fabric 
and the terrain vague. 
 
                                                                                                                                                                        
890 Solà-Morales, Ignasi de. “Terrain Vague.” Anyplace. Ed. Cynthia Davidson. Cambridge, Massachusetts: 
The MIT Press; Anyone Corporation, 1995. 118–123.
